div.head: Commentary Notes
div.note:
1 Dramatis Personæ]
This edn.; the Quartos and the Folios have no list. Rowe (ed. 1709) first lists the characters, giving male, followed by female, followed by supernatural characters. Theobald (ed. 1733) adds Philostrate and the characters of the interlude; Capell (ed. 1768) corrects the designation for Hippolita from Princess to Queen, adds Attendants upon Theseus and Hippolita, and assigns to each clown the role played in the interlude; White (ed. 1858), alone, adds the Presenter of the Interlude; and Brooks (ed. 1979) first adds A Fairy, in Titania’s service. Wilson (ed. 1924) is the first major editor to list characters in order of appearance, the convention preferred in this edition. A few recent editors present groups of characters according to plot lines, as in Mowat & Werstine (ed. 1993).

2 Theseus, Duke]
Capell (ed. 1768, 1:65) assumes the name to be from Plutarch’s Life of Theseus; Grey (1754, 1:40–1) suggests both name and title derive primarily from Chaucer’s KnT (see n. 26). Yuasa (1994, p. 77): According to NCD, Theseus is derived from the Greek tithemi, meaning to put or to set, and it refers to his function as the great orderer or settler. In this play, too, he is asked to settle a very difficult question between love and justice. However, I think his name also suggests the Greek theos and Latin deus, both meaning god. Granville-Barker (ed. 1924, pp. xxv–vi) represents the attitude of many earlier commentators: Duke Theseus has no closer relation to his historical namesake than has Oberon to Louis Quatorze. But some recent critics interpret Sh.’s character and more generally the play through associations with the historical and mythical figure; e.g. Riklin (1968, pp. 282–3), Pearson (1974, passim), Levith (1978, p. 23), Montrose (1983, pp. 74–5). See also .

3 Hippolita, Queene of the Amazons]
Drawn from Plutarch (Lives, ed. 1595, pp. 14–15; see ) or from Chaucer (KnT 866–78; see ); see also n. 20–23. Moberly (ed. 1881): According to Greek legends the Amazons lived on the river Thermodon, near Trebizond, and were attacked there first by Hercules, who wished to carry off the queen’s girdle, then by Theseus, who led captive another queen—Antiope. On this the female warriors undertook, as Plutarch says, a march neither trifling nor feminine; inasmuch as it ended in the very heart of Athens, where Theseus defeated them after a terrible conflict.…According to Plutarch, the wife of Theseus was the Antiope above mentioned, the same who was called Hippolyta by the poet Cleodemus; and by her mediation a truce was made between Athens and her countrywomen. Shakspere, on the contrary, distinguishes the two ladies, and apparently intends to make Hippolyta the leader of the just defeated invasion. Brown (1987, p. 32) perceives ambiguity in her name and title, suggesting tension between higher and lower states of love: As queen of the Amazons she was by definition a votary of Diana, but the name [Hippolyta] itself means looser of horses, and horses are a standard Platonist symbol for the passions. Yuasa (1994, p. 77): Hippolyta is a compound of the Greek hippos, meaning horse, and luo, meaning to release. I think there is a good balance between the ruling Theseus and the liberating Hippolyta. Carter (2006, para. 23), ignoring Chaucer: Hippolyta was an Amazon queen (and thereby is not simply an invented back formation of Hippolytus as Louis Montrose suggests [1996, p. 148]), but neither the wife of Theseus nor the mother of Hippolytus. Shakespeare seems to have perversely chosen her name in a deliberate intertextual use of myth to recall yet more instances of incest, filicide and suicide.

4 Philostrate]
From Chaucer, KnT 1428; see n. 15. Larry D. Benson (ed. The Riverside Chaucer 3rd ed., Boston, 1987, p. 832) states: Chaucer took the name Philostrate from Boccaccio’s Filostrato.…Philostrato, from the Greek, means army lover, but Boccaccio, and probably Chaucer, took it to mean overthrown by love. Yuasa (1994, p. 78) derives the name from Greek philos, meaning loving, and strategia, meaning strategy. This is a very appropriate name for a man who needs a great deal of ingenuity and dexterity.

5 Egeus]
Usually the name of the father of Theseus; e.g. Chaucer, KnT, Plutarch (Ægeus), Golding’s Ovid (Aegeus): see , , . Melchiori (1994, pp. 199–200) suggests the Athenian names are typical of current Elizabethan romance literature, and that Egeus undoubtedly recalls Egeon in Err. (It.). Yuasa (1994, p. 78) considers it important that the ending of his name relates him to Theseus and Demetrius.

6 Hermia]
Wölffel (1852, p. 127): As Hermes (messenger of the gods) reconciles the heavenly with the earthly, and brings gentle mores and spiritual beauty to a rough mankind, so the name Hermia implies a certain charm which comes from heaven and which outshines sensual beauty, which is as inaccessible to the common mind as the sky is invisible to the eye that turns towards the earth (Ger.). Wigston (1884, p. 313) also connects Hermia’s name with Hermes, claiming a derivation from the Greek ερμενεια or ερμηνεὐω, to interpret, explain, expound; in accordance with his Platonic reading of the play, Hermia’s name signifies that interpretation, which is the bringing to light of the spiritual creative archetype. See also Blits (2003, pp. 78–9) at n. 709–12. Tobin (1979, p. 154): Hermia was Aristotle’s mistress and often was paired with another Helena, the whore of Simon Magus. Nashe in Saffron-walden mentions Hermia and changes the paramour of Simon Magus to Silenes,…(SW, 111). McKerrow notes in his gloss on this passage, the name of Simon’s companion is generally given as Helena but Selen is found in certain editions of Irenaeus [Works of Thomas Nashe, rpt. Oxford, 1958, 4:357]. Such a pairing of the names of two notoriously promiscuous women may have prompted Shakespeare to repeat the conjunction in the names of his Athenian ladies, themselves the victims of male promiscuity. See also Thiselton (1903, p. 54). Nosworthy (1982, p. 104), citing Plutarch: The mention of Hermes, Hermus and the city of Hermione affords as good an explanation as any for the name of Hermia. Davies (1986, p. 124): Her name relates her to the Hermetic mysteries of the cult of the goddess Isis, popularised through Apuleius’ The Golden Asse. Brown (1987, p. 31): The fact that Hermia’s name derives from a male god and Demetrius’s from a goddess both dissociates the individuality of character from the name and offers yet another example of the all-pervasive harmony of opposites in the play. Holland (ed. 1994, p. 133): The name is uncommon. It is possibly derived from Hermione, daughter of Helen of Troy [see Od. 4.16], and hence linked to Helena; Ovid juxtaposes the names in Ars Amatoria, Scilicet Hermionem Helenae praeponere posses (2.699, Would you be able to prefer Hermione to Helen?)—a teasing comment in view of the choices made by Demetrius and Lysander; he quotes 768 as in effect an answer to Ovid’s question. See also Herbert (1977, p. 19). To Hunter (2003, p. 8) the name suggests hermaphrodite.

7 Lysander]
Cooper (1565): A kynge of Lacedemonia.…When it was layde to his charge that he had broken the league that in Miletus he had sworne vnto, Tushe (sayeth he) as chyldren are to be trayned and deceiued with apples and triffles, so are men to be beguyled with othes. Wright (ed. 1877) suggests the name is taken from North’s Plutarch. So too Johnson (ed. 1894, p. 103), Wilson (ed. 1924, p. 102), Brooks (ed. 1979), etc. Proescholdt (1878, pp. 12–13) suggests that Chaucer’s Emetrius and Licurge (KnT 2128–86; see ) by a somewhat convoluted path led Sh. to change the first to the more familiar Demetrius, and the second, via Plutarch, to Lysander. Herbert (1977, pp. 20–22) is prompted by Chaucer’s opposing knights Emetrius and Ligurge to consider the fateful dispute between Menelaus and Paris. Noting that Paris’s alternative name in the Greek epic is Alexandros, who by several characters in LLL (5.2.563–83; 2516–37) is called Alisander, and believing that this sounds close to Lysander, he pursues similarities between Shakespeare’s lover and the Homeric hero. Riehle (1990, p. 175): The name Lysander…occurs not only in Plutarch but also in Solinus’s Collectanea [ed. T. Mommsen, Berlin, 1895, p. 29; tr. Golding, 1587, E3v]. This comment occurs among several examples of parallels suggesting Shakespeare knew this work. (Sh. could also have found Lysander in, for example, Aelian, Demosthenes, or Xenophon, all available in 16th-c. English translation.) Yuasa (1994, p. 78) derives Lysander from Greek luo (to release) and andros (men), seeing Lysander as a liberating agent. See also Wigston (1884, p. 314), Brown (1987, p. 31). Holland (ed. 1994, p. 133): A version of Alexander (another name for the Paris of Troy who stole Menelaus’ Helen as Lysander will, in a way, attempt to steal Helena from Demetrius…), perhaps also linked to Leander, Hero’s lover [see n. 1999–2000]. Nathaniel and others pronounce the name of Alexander the Great as Alisaunder in LLL [5.2.564–86 (2514–38)]. Blits (2003, p. 12): Shakespeare gives the two young lovers the names of two principal enemies of Athens, both of whom defeated Athens by imitating it. The Spartan Lysander defeated Athens at sea in the last battle of the Peloponnesian War, while the Macedonian Demetrius, son of Antigonus, conquered Athens in the guise of saving it following Alexander’s death.

8 Helena]
Wölffel (1852, p. 127): Since Homer’s time the name Helena has symbolized the charm of earthly beauty. Wigston (1884, pp. 313–14): Helena etymologically signifies alluring; and, as allied to the name Helen, stands typical of that external beauty which is phenomenal and sensible. Johnson (ed. 1894, p. 103): Helena is called the daughter of Nedar, of whom nothing further is known [but see n. 116 Nedars]. It may be noted that the fair Helen, wife of Menelaus, on whose account the Trojan War began, was in her childhood carried away by Theseus and his friend Pirithous for her great beauty [see (Hellen)]. See also Nosworthy (1982, p. 104). Knight (1958, p. 199): The study of Shakespeare’s names raises some queer questions.…Why are Shakespeare’s two man-chasing women, in [MND and AWW], both called Helena? Herbert (1977, pp. 19–20) connects Helena with Helen of Troy, partly through the slight similarity of sound between Nedar and Tyndar, putative father of Leda, Helen’s mother. Levith (1978, p. 24): A chorus of women is suggested by the alliterated names in Coriolanus: Valeria, Virgilia, Volumnia. Another is to be found in [MND]: Hippolyta, Hermia, and Helena. Tobin (1979, p. 154): The irony in the play of an unloved Helen…has always prompted a smile in audiences and readers. See also Tobin on Hermia, above. Donaldson (1985, pp. 39–40): Sh. went so far as to blur the distinction he made between them by assigning them both trisyllabic names beginning with H, ending with a, and accented on the first syllable, so that either could, by simple substitution, grace a sonnet originally addressed to the other. Brown (1987, p. 31): Helena is the name of the woman who was both an inspiring beauty—the face that launch’d a thousand ships—and a demonic paramour who tempted great men to their doom. Snyder (1988, pp. 71–2), noting the association with Helen of Troy, suggests that the Helenas of [AWW and MND] are linked by the name chosen for them, a name that ironically contradicts its prototype and thus underlines their peculiar situation as subject, the locus of active desire, rather than the usual woman’s part as pursued object. Roberts (1991, pp. 145–6) argues that in the Renaissance Helen of Troy comes…to represent all three stages of the female trinity: a virgin to be fought over, a wife who betrays, and a bringer of death.…St. Helena, mother of the Emperor Constantine, also has a curiously ambiguous history.…Helens share an association with Hel, the Norse goddess of the underworld who provided early English theologians with a convenient translation of the Greek Hades.…It is a short step from Hell to Helen; she cites Sh. punning on Helen/hell in MND 622 and Tro. 4.1.58 (2233), and claims that in MND, AWW and Tro. Sh. specifically linked the Helen with threatening and aggressive sexuality. Yuasa (1994, p. 78): Helena comes from Helen, whose etymological meaning is the bright one. There is some irony in her name, because we get the impression that she is not so beautiful as Hermia. He also suggests a link with St. Helen, Constantine’s mother, on the grounds that she is said to have been repudiated by her husband.

9 Demetrius]
Walker (1860, 2:32): Emetrius king of Inde, the ally of Arcita in Chaucer [see ], is perhaps Demetrius, robbed of his initial letter by a slip of some earlier romancer’s pen; Lettsom in Walker (1860, 2:32) adds: Possibly the Bactrian Demetrius, called rex Indorum by Justin. See also Proescholdt (1878, p. 12) above, under Lysander. Wright (ed. 1877) suggests the name is taken from Plutarch (see ), as do many others. Wigston (1884, pp. 314–15): The name meaning of, or belonging to Demeter or Ceres—Mother Earth, signifies that (p. 315) Demetrius is…the phenomenal or material aesthetic instinct of Nature. Johnson (ed. 1894, p. 103): Demetrius was the name of several kings and orators. It occurs in North’s Plutarch and in the New Testament (Acts xix.24; III. John 12). Shakespeare was possibly thinking of its derivation, one devoted or belonging to Demeter or Ceres, the goddess of agriculture and rural life. See also Richer (1974, p. 8) (Fr.), Davies (1986, p. 128), Brown (1987, pp. 30–1). Brooks (ed. 1979, p. 3): A M[aster] Demetrius is mentioned in the Nashe/Harvey controversy (Nashe, Have with you to Saffron-Walden, 1596; Works, III. 125). Yuasa (1994, p. 78), deriving Demetrius from Demeter, the name of the Greek earth goddess, sees Demetrius as a binding agent. See also Blits above, DP n. 7.
For discussion of why the name is vile, see n. 761–2.

10–15 Peeter…Tayler]
Wilson (ed. 1924, p. 102): Commentators have remarked that Bottom takes his name from the bottom or core of the skein upon which the weaver’s yarn is wound; but they have not noticed that most of the other clowns have technical names likewise. Sewell (1960, pp. 129–30): The names are not merely technical terms. They have that quality of association which goes with poetry, and the associations bear with them not merely the mechanical history of things artificial…but also that other history, of things natural.…They imply the great unity of natural history, plants and trees, animals, man as body and mind, the arts. For Taylor’s (1973, pp. 192–207) argument that the names of Bottom and his fellow actors relate to those of Alençon and his retainers, see . Levith (1978, p. 19): As well as occupations, the rude mechanicals…are named for intended physical features. Franke (1979, pp. 284–94) explores sexually suggestive connotations of each of the mechanicals’ names. Parker (Name, 2003, p. 9): It is well known that these names suggest sexual conjunction or joining in matrimony along with their artisanal functions; see also Idem (1996, pp. 94–5). Wells (2004, p. 37): Recent explorations of the sex lives of Shakespeare’s mechanicals…are fantasies released in their author’s minds by the texts; he suggests they should be presented as fiction, not as criticism. See also n. 266–8.

10 Peeter Quince, the Carpenter]
Most frequently Peeter, but Peter at 324, 1740. Johnson (ed. 1894, p. 103): He derives his name presumably from the bitter yellow fruit so-called, which is used in making preserves. See also n. 277. Furness (ed. 1895, DP n. 6): Bell (iii, 182, note) letting the cart, as Lear’s Fool says, draw the horse, asserts that Shakespeare adopted this name from the old German comedy Peter Squenz. Acheson (1922, p. 193) believes Peter Quince parodies the name of the author of a ballad Penelope’s Complaint (1596), Peter Colse, which he argues is a pseudonym for Matthew Roydon. Wilson (ed. 1924, p. 102): Quince is simply a spelling of quines or quoins, i.e. wedge-shaped blocks of wood used for building purposes, and therefore appropriately connected with a carpenter. Knight (1958, p. 171): Even with low-comedy characters significances may be submerged or complex.…Quince [is] traditionally played as a small and wizened old man, for one whose office is to correct the illiteracy of others [896–914], though his scholarly equipment is meagre and his talk scarcely as acid as the name might imply. Martz (1971, p. 65): I take Quince…to be a pun on quince, the pear-shaped fruit whose seeds (of comedy?) are used in medicine and the arts and quoin, a cornerstone or, more generally, a wedge-shaped block put to various uses, and also on coin, money as signifying value. Snodgrass (1975, p. 226) believes his name echoes Penis Cunt. See also Hartman (1983, p. 357). Barkan (1986, p. 267): Peter Quince may be the linear carpenter who tries to hammer together the play. Waddington (1988, pp. 14–15) connects Quince’s name to the fruit, showing that in painting (Bordone), and emblems (Alciati and Ripa) it is a symbol of fecundity in marriage, a notion perhaps deriving from Solon, as indicated in Plutarch’s Life of Solon. His Christian name perhaps links him with (p. 15) the one apostle known to have been married. Webb (1991, p. 145): Sh. in fructifying the name may have remembered the marriage assoc[iation], for Peter is the organizer of the entertainment for the nuptials of Hippolyta and Theseus. Sh.’s only other reference to quinces is in the marriage preparations in Rom. 4.4.3 (2542). See also Langley (1991, p. 126) and Parker (2001, pp. 53–4; (Peter), 2003, pp. 39–54).

11 Snugge, the Ioyner]
Johnson (ed. 1894, p. 104): He seems to have gone through life with only a surname, but its suggestion of comfortable self-complacency is admirably suited to his character. Wilson (ed. 1924, p. 102): Snug means compact, close-fitting, tight—a good name for a joiner. Knight (1958, p. 171): Snug for a home-repairing joiner. Sewell (1960, p. 130): Snug suggests a bodily, almost animal, warmth, as do the words snuggle or nestle. Evans (ed. 1974): Snug’s name suggests the expert joining of pieces of wood by a maker of fine furniture. Franke (1979, pp. 282–5) suggests that Snug’s (p. 283) name and trade in themselves…contained a pun. Citing Jaques’s connecting to join in marriage with to join in carpentry (AYL 3.3.84–9 [1690–4]), he argues that in MND (p. 284) join…refer[s]…to the consummation of marriage or, perhaps, simply to copulation. The nomen agentis would then lend itself to sexual punning. The name Snug, however, might reinforce this, as it has…a substantival [meaning] which is…sexually suggestive; he refers to OED Snug n1 1, Snag n1 1a. P. Parker (1987, p. 122) stresses this name’s importance as part of a pervasive network of discursive and rhetorical joining and misjoining. Idem (1996, pp. 88–95) further explores aspects of joinery in discourse and matrimony. See also Wiles (1993, p. 61), n. 852–6.

12 Nick Bottom, the Weauer]
(Halliwell (1841, p. 20): There is a connection between this name and the trade, which the obsoleteness of the term has caused to escape the commentators. A ball of thread wound upon any cylindrical body was formerly called A Bottom of Thread. Halliwell also claims (ed. 1856): Nicholas was either a favorite Christian name for a weaver, or a generic appellation for a person of that trade. Ebblewhite (1886, p. 24) explores a heraldic meaning: The fusil…is sometimes blazoned as a spindle. It is also called a bottom in the quaint blazon of the coat of Badland. Furness (ed. 1895, DP n. 8) notes that Sh. uses the word as a verb in TGV 3.2.51–3 (1496–8).
Knight (1958, p. 171) compares with Pompey Bum, the name Bum being explicitly interpreted in terms of bestiality at [MM 2.1.217–19 (664–6)].…Bottom’s name, apart from suggesting a bottom of thread, for a weaver, is similarly meaningful. He is a stupid, earthy, man, and his union with Titania comically symbolic, the more so for his ass’s head. Sewell (1960, pp. 130, 132–3): Bottom carries its obvious bodily reference and also the trade meaning. Finding Theseus, Ariadne, and the labyrinth present or implied in MND, and noting that OED (Bottom, n. 15a) quotes Ralegh’s History of the World (He received from her [Ariadne] a bottome of threde), she asks: (p. 133) Is Bottom the weaver the clew to this play and what it says about postlogic? L’Hôte (ed. 1964, pp. 8–9), in discussing the problem of translating the mechanicals’ names, reveals the different ways Bottom’s name has been interpreted, from Lefond (the bottom, end, depths, core, nub, etc.), to Lanavette (the spindle), to Mesfesses (my buttocks) (Fr.). Wells (ed. 1967, p. 129): As a weaver’s term, a bottom was the object on which thread was wound. Bottom as posterior is not recorded till late in the eighteenth century, but the name Mistress Frigbottom in Thomas Dekker’s The Shoemaker’s Holiday (1600) shows that this sense existed in Shakespeare’s time. Colman (1974, p. 28): The modern sense of bottom as buttocks is never so much as glanced at in the Dream, and it does not appear in the quite generous lists of synonyms for the rump given in the Italian and French dictionaries of John Florio and Randle Cotgrave (1598, 1611). The OED’s earliest record of bottom’s being written in this modern sense is dated 1794–96. See also Williams (1994, p. 135), who defines bottom as pubic region, confirming posteriors as an 18th-c. meaning. Levith (1978, pp. 19, 23, 77): The intended physical feature for which Bottom is named is his broad seat. (P. 23): Bottom, the fabulous ass-man, is found at the center of a forest-labyrinth containing some lost young people. Invited to get to the bottom of this most rare vision…Shakespeare’s audience need clutch only at the thread of the meaning of Bottom’s name—a weaver’s spool, which Theseus used to defeat the Minotaur. (P. 77): The name Bottom is also a pun on ass, an association reinforced by the visual image of Bottom’s head. The world of [MND] is topsy-turvy; things are turned around, bottoms are up.
Stroup (1978, p. 80), quoting the Geneva Bible (1557) version of I Cor 2:6–10, maintains that the phrase the bottom of Goddes secretes in this passage is a source, if not the source, of Bottom’s name. Willson (1979, p. 408) attacks Stroup’s argument, asserting that rather than engaging in the treacherous adventure of source-hunting scholars need to consider the connotative richness of Shakespeare’s comic names. Bottom is located at the bottom of the play’s social ladder…but his words and actions often puncture the romaticized posturings of his betters.…By providing a bottom (here in the sense of standard or measure) of earthy directness, if not always sense, Nick the weaver helps to return the mood of the play…to…palatable and plausible reality.
Seeking a double entendre in Bottom the weaver parallel to that he sees in Snug the joiner, Franke (1979, p. 288) appeals to the history of English phonology to claim that a pun on weaver/wiver was possible at this time; he further asserts that the shape of a bottom of thread lent itself to phalllic associations. See also Parker (1996, pp. 94–5). Clayton (1979, p. 28) muses on the tag Fundamentum non habet, and on the anecdote in which Johnson, having excited laughter for saying a certain woman had a bottom of good sense, responds by calling her fundamentally sensible (Boswell’s Life of Johnson, OSA New ed. 1953, p. 1141); Clayton continues: Latin etymology linked fundus, the bottom of a thing in Elyot, with fundamentum, and in English fundament is well-founded in anatomy as in architecture. Juvenal 3.108 links a definite fundus with a disputed chamber-pot. And Turner’s Herbal (2.166 [ed. 1562]) says of the Chaste Tree, the Agnus Castus, The sede layd to wyth water swageth the payne of the nicks or ryvinge of the fundament.
Barkan (1986, p. 267): Bottom is the weaver of a seamless fabric of reality and dream, of art and life, the incarnation of Arachne. Patterson (1988, pp. 35–7) suggests the ass’s head distinguishes itself from comic props and animal masks in general, and becomes part of a complex structural pun.…The name Bottom refers not only to the bottom of the social hierarchy as the play represents it, but also to the bottom of the body when seated, literally the social ass or arse; in support of this suggestion, she calls on Puck (424–5), modern critics, Stephen Batman and Apuleius. Webb (1988, p. 6): Sh. makes no unquestionable pun, ass/arse, nor does arse itself appear anywhere in his works; he adds (1989, p. 7): Perhaps something may be gleaned from uses of ass with associations more naturally attaching to the posterior, but he warns that the examples he gives may be chance collocations, while in some…the words are far separated. See also n. 1743.
Peltrault (1989, pp. 156–65 ): Bottom is well named, as he represents the lowest scale of Athenians. His name also evokes (p. 157) ces joyeuse paires de l’anatomie humaine (those joyous pairs of the human anatomy i.e. the buttocks) celebrated by the carnivalesque. When Puck gives him an ass-head, to English ears the bottom becomes the top. As weaver he also has a complex rapport with legends of Theseus, both of Ariadne and the labyrinth, and also the descent to hell with Pirithous and subsequent rescue from his chained seat by Hercules. Thus Nick Bottom constitutes a nickname enirely appropriate for Theseus, of whom he is the grotesque double (Fr.). Empson (1994, pp. 243–4), conceding that bottom is not applied to a part of the body until the later 18th c., quotes OED meanings the base on which a thing stands, an abyss, a valley, an alluvial hollow, the most remote or inmost part, the foundation, the soil in which a plant grows, the essence or reality of something, financial stability; all these are at least seventeenth century. Clearly, Bottom is a fundamental character, well fitted to confer with nature upon the bases of life. He notes (p. 244) Ven. 235–7, sweet bottom grass in Venus’s description of her body, and discusses possible ass/arse pronunciation and puns. Holland (ed. 1994, p. 147) on Bottom as core of weaver’s skein cites TGV 3.2.53 (1498), Shr. 4.3.136–7 (2119–20) and Caxton’s Aeneid (Ariadne and Theseus): No one has yet proved convincingly that the word bottom could at this date refer to a person’s behind; if it could, then the transformation into an ass (arse) would seem almost a literalizing of Bottom’s name. Yuasa (1994, p. 79): Bottom’s name is not merely derogatory, for it can mean…the foundation or basis upon which our society rests. Smyth (1995, pp. 619–20, 638–9) plays with the notion of Bottom as numerical fundament.
Parker (1996, pp. 95–6): Bottom the weaver…recalls in his name the phallic shape of the bottom, or core, on which a weaver’s yarn was wound, as well as the weaving of generation in which the couples of this marriage play are finally knit [1706]. She notes that Ovid’s tale of Pyramus and Thisbe is told by weavers, finding this particularly appropriate for Bottom the weaver and for the weaver Fates, as well as for the thread of discourse. Witte (1999, pp. 25–39) explores significances of the connection between Bottom’s name and his trade (p. 35): Bottom’s name also connotes the silkworm’s cocoon and alludes to his role within a complex pre-industrial system of fabrication soon to be conditioned by market forces. Parker (Name, 2003, pp. 10–29) examines especially the bodily associations of bottom, the weaver’s bottom, nick and ass (considering the pun on ace as well as arse). The multiple meanings of the name include (p. 28) the upward metamorphosis of a Bottom that is at once a temporary translation in the social scale that is quickly undone (as in the game of hazard evoked in Sir Thomas More); metamorphosis upward in a phallic rising and a sexual consummation between Titania and an ass (simultaneously evocative of union with the fabled Fairy Queen and of the coupling of human and bestial that produced the Minotaur of the labyrinth), and the joining of low and high that is suggested by the Bottom of God’s secrets.…None of these meanings—including the possibility that Bottom could designate the latter end of the body or the posterior—eliminates or necessarily excludes the other.
Wilder (2008, pp. 46–7), noting that different SPs identify Bottom by his trade as weaver, his theatrical function as clown, and his role as actor (Pyramus), sees also a Protean doubleness…within each of the names. See also n. 1742.

13 Francis Flute, the Bellowes mender]
Johnson (ed. 1894, p. 104): No doubt so called because flutes and bellows are both wind instruments. Wilson (ed. 1924, p. 102): He would of course have to repair fluted church-organs as well as the domestic bellows. Brooks (ed. 1979) adds: He is, moreover, a lackbeard with a fluty treble. Knight (1958, p. 171): Flute, ironically, for the bellows-mender, who is to speak as small as you will [312] as Thisbe. Sewell (1960, p. 130): Flute the bellows-mender suggests by that combination of woodwind and bellows the moving breath which is life itself,…a life which is also capable of translation into music. Clayton (1979, pp. 13, 16–17, 19, 21, 26): Erudite musings connect fistula with the actor’s flute and with the pipe in Ovid’s tale of Pyramus and Thisbe; flute-players with Demetrius and Lysander; bellows with Pyramus’s sighs; biforis used for flutes connects with the moon at the casement window and with masks in depictions of Dionysiac drama. In Ovid’s Fasti 6 (p. 21) masked men dress as flute-girls and flutes act like bellows. (P. 26): Flute is the natural image of the player, the word persona links flute and mask. Parker (1996, p. 95), drawing on Franke (1979, pp. 288–9): Francis Flute, the bellows mender, combines the flute that is slang for the male member, the belly linked etymologically with bellow, and the suggestions of the sexually liberal or promiscuous in Francis or frank. See also Wiles (1993, pp. 59–60). Williams (1994, p. 519), defining flute as penis: The figure of sex as music-making draws in suitably shaped instruments for genital use. Wells (2004, p. 34): There is nothing in the dialogue to suggest that the timorous Francis is lewdly inclined, nor is any joke made about his name. Greenblatt (ed. 1997, p. 819): As Flute’s name suggests, domestic bellows whistle through holes when needing repair.

14 Tom Snowt, the Tinker]
Johnson (ed. 1894, p. 104): He was perhaps a trifle long-nosed. The name is at any rate suggestive of folly. See also Levith (1978, p. 19), but Kukowski (1991, p. 87): Sh. never uses snout of a person’s appearance. Wilson (ed. 1924, p. 102): Snout means nozzle or spout [OED, n.1 4], which suggests the tinker’s trade in mending kettles; but OED does not give spout as a synonym for snout. Knight (1958, p. 171): Snout, jingling with spout, for a tinker. Sewell (1960, p. 130): Snout has obvious animal connections, and was used in Shakespeare’s time for fish and bird as well as beast. Wiles (1993, p. 59) believes the meaning nozzle for Snout’s name has phallic implications; see n. 2008. See also Franke (1979, p. 289); Parker (1996, p. 95).

15 Robin Starueling, the Tayler]
Johnson (ed. 1894, p. 104): He was of course a lean and hungry creature, the ninth part of a man; he quotes (1H4 2.4.244–7 [1202–5]), you Starueling,…You Tailors yard, etc. Wilson (ed. 1924, p. 102): The only non-technical name among [the mechanicals], though it is apt enough, alluding as it does to the proverbial leanness of tailors, of whom it took nine to make a man [Tilley T23]. Sewell (1960, p. 130): The name Starveling by its image of thinness brings in the body and food, and the word was used not only for human beings but also for animals or plants. Toliver (1971, p. 92) adds that he has the name of a bird; see . Foakes (ed. 1984, p. 57): Starveling points to the proverbial image of a tailor…, as well as being appropriate to John Sincklo, the actor in Shakespeare’s company who played thin men’s parts; see Greg [1955], pp. 114–15 and nn. Sasaki (1986, p. 89) thinks his name suggests sterility and death. Patterson (1989, p. 56): It is not implausible to see an allusion to a time of hardship…in the name of…Robin Starveling. Cf. the Sokols (2000, p. 274). Parker (1996, p. 95), following Franke (1979, p. 289–90): Starveling the tailor, whose craft evokes tails, is linked to the joke about the gossip who falls on her back and cries tailor [422–8], and his thinness, it is suggested, explains why, within this predominantly phallic mode of naming, he takes only female parts. See also Taylor (2003 Name, p. 9).

16 Robin goodfellow, the Puck]
OED: See Robin1 and goodfellow.…1.1 A sportive and capricious elf or goblin believed to haunt the English country-side in the 16–17th centuries; also called Hobgoblin or Puck. OED, Robin1: OF. Robin, a dim. or familiar form of the personal name Robert. OED, good-fellow n.: 1.1 An agreeable or jovial companion; esp. a boon companion, a convivial person, a reveller. OED, puck, pook: n.1: Forms: α. 1 púca, 3–4 (9 Sc.) puke, 4–7 pouke, 5 powke, 6 pooke, 9 pook, pouk. β. 6–7 pucke, 7– puck. γ. 9 dial. poake. [OE. púca = ON. púki a mischievous demon. Cf. W. pwca, pwci, Ir. púca (pooka). Cf. poker n.2, pug n.2. The ulterior history of the name and the question whether it was originally Teutonic or Celtic, is unsettled.] An evil, malicious, or mischievous spirit or demon of popular superstition. a. Treated as a unique being, and in Middle Eng. (the pouke) commonly identified with the biblical devil; from the 16th c. (with capital P) the name of a fancied mischievous or tricksy goblin or sprite, called also Robin Goodfellow and Hobgoblin. (In this last sense commonly Puck.) See also n. 410.
Scott (1802, 1:lxxxiv, 2:212–13) describes the Bogle or Goblin; a freakish spirit, who delights rather to perplex and frighten mankind, than either to serve, or seriously to hurt them. This is the Esprit Follet of the French; and Puck, or Robin Goodfellow, though enlisted by Shakespeare among the fairy band of Oberon, properly belongs to this class of phantoms; at 2:12 he describes him as the merry Goblin. Halliwell (1845, p. vii): The author of Tarlton’s News out of Purgatory, printed in 1590, assures us that Robin Goodfellow was famosed in every old wives chronicle for his mad merry pranks. Thoms (1847, pp. 981–3, 1054–5): Though Puck is now only applied to designate the merry wanderer of the night, it was originally a name applied to the whole race of fairies, and not to any individual sprite; he describes the names and characteristics of comparable sprites in Friesland, Jutland and Germany. Reiterating the generic nature of the appellation Puck, he explores its etymology. On Hobgoblin (p. 1055), Grimm…refers to the practice of bestowing upon these domestic sprites Christian names, in their diminutive forms.…Hob, although very generally used to signify a clown or countryman,…is also the familiar or diminutive form of Robert and Robin, so that Hobgoblin is equivalent to Robin the Goblin—i.e. Robin Goodfellow. See also Dyer (1883; 1996, pp. 5–8). Latham (1930, pp. 219–263) traces the history of Robin Goodfellow, noting which of his characteristics Sh. retained, and which he modified. Robin Goodfellow (p. 253) owed his literary career and his classification as a fairy, as a puck, and as Hobgoblin, to Shakespeare who put him on the stage for the first time in his history as Puck, in company with the diminutive fairies. See also Briggs (1959, esp. pp. 15, 45), and Holland (ed. 1994, pp. 35–49).
Chambers (ed. 1897, p. 97): In the stage-directions of the Qq. Ff. Puck and Robin-Goodfellow or Robin are used indifferently [Q1 Puck(e) 627, 718, 1133, 2153; Robin goodfellow 373, 1021, 1559; Robin 888, 1465], both often occurring in the same scene. There is no need to assume with Mr. Fleay that this points to a revision of the play.…In the text, Robin is always used [Q1 1396, 1560–1, 1596, 2222; 404] except…where he calls himself an honest Puck [2215], and the Puck [2219], and…where the fairy speaks of sweet Puck [410] as one of his names; Oberon also addresses him as gentle Pucke [524, 1579]. On variation in naming as sign of revision, see Wilson (ed. 1924, p. 103).
McKerrow (1933; 1964, p. 112): Rowe has in all cases Puck, and is indeed responsible for the introduction of Puck as a proper name. Shakespeare would, I think, have said that his name was Robin, or Robin the Puck, Puck being really a common noun equivalent to goblin or sprite. Mowat (1994, p. 317): Rowe’s decision to call Robin Goodfellow Puck, while historically decisive, was also very strange. Gentle Puck, as the dialogue of the play teaches us, is what one calls Robin when one wants to get his good will, but it is not his name. See Tilley (1950, R147), where the name Robin Goodfellow is treated as proverbial. Snyder (1999, pp. 33–4) traces the editorial tradition whereby the generic label Puck became substituted for the proper name Robin. Brooks (ed. 1979, pp. xxiv–v) believes speech prefix variations can readily be understood as corresponding each to the aspect of the character then uppermost in Shakespeare’s mind as folkloric prankster or Oberon’s minister. Clayton (1999, p. 83) praises Brooks’s plausible and intelligent interpretation, and is himself inclined to see (the) Puck as having the darker nature, Robin as the more sociable. But the distribution of names…does not seem to support the distinction (or Brooks’s). For variation in speech prefixes as caused by type shortage rather than having interpretive significance, see Kennedy (1998, pp. 178–90).
Bell (1852–1864) exhaustively traces the names and kin of Sh.’s Puck through (title page) the superstitions of all nations, in a work that casts more light on folkslore than on MND’s character. Chambers (ed. 1897, pp. 165–6) provides from various counties and countries a brief list that might be increased indefinitely of the name’s wide affinities. Parry-Jones (1960, pp. 56–7) quotes a note by Rev. Henry Thomas Payne, Archbishop of Carmarthen 1829–32, who describes a ritual at a Welsh well, (p. 57) called cwm-Pwca, or the Hobgoblins Dingle.…Shakespeare’s Puck is evidently grounded upon the Welsh name—He is in fact the same. Morén (2000, pp. 16–23) connects Puck with the Scandinavian (p. 19) milkstealing puke and thence suggests various puns in Rabelaisian-Bakhtinian style.
Yuasa (1994, p. 79), dubiously: Puck is believed to mean either to blow or to poke. [Cf. OED Puck (v.) Now only dial. (chiefly Ir.) trans. To hit or strike; to butt.] In either case the etymology indicates wickedness, but Shakespeare identifies him with Robin Goodfellow, so that he becomes a good-natured fairy although he retains some of his original mischievousness. In a different context, Morris (1958, pp. 601–3) details literary and botanic phallic associations with the name Robin. Hartman (1983, p. 368), pursuing an oedipal argument, anachronistically believes Robin to be an androgenous name.

18 Oberon, King of the Fairies]
Douce (1807, 1:183): The name of Oberon as king of the fairies, must have been exceedingly well known from the romance of Huon of Bourdeaux, in which this Oberon makes a very conspicuous figure. First noted by Steevens (ed. 1778, 3:126); Bell (1787, p. 98), quoting Henley, adds the name may be further traced to the Histories of Ogier of Denmark, and Isaiah the sorrowful. Johnson (ed. 1765, 8:2h7v) connects MND’s fairies with Spenser’s FQ (see ), as does Keightley (1833, 2:126–7), but without specifying that Oberon appears by name in FQ 2.10.76. Malone (in Boswell, ed. 1821, 2:337) notes that Oberon (Auberon) had appeared in the Entertainment at Elvetham in 1591. Knight (ed. 1839): The name of Oberon, King of Fairies, is found in Greene’s James the IVth.; noted also by Steevens (ed. 1778, 3:31). On Oberon as king of shadows see Baynes (1880) at n. 1388. Duffy (1972, p. 119 n.) suggests the name could come from a contraction of the name of a dwarf king in a Welsh romance.
Drake (1817, 2:337 n.): Oberon, or, more properly Auberon has been derived, by some antiquaries, from l’aube du jour; and Mab his Queen, from amabilis, so that lucidity and amiability, their characteristics, as delineated by Shakspeare, may be traced in their names. Keightley (1833, 1:59, 2:6 n.): The name Oberon is only Elberich slightly altered. (2:6 n.): From the usual change of l into u (as al, au, col, cou, &c.), in the French language, Elberich or Alberich (derived from Alp, Alf) becomes Auberich; and ich not being a French termination, the usual one of on was substituted, and so it became Auberon, or Oberon; a much more likely origin than that from L’aube du jour, which is entitled to no more attention than that of Mab from amabilis. Undeterred, Morley (1893, p. 234): Oberon (Auberon, Alberon) has his name from aube, the white light of dawn (which is from albus, white). Knight (1958, p. 184): The sound of…Oberon, with its o’s exerting the effect found in Morocco and Othello, certainly suits the powers of a fearsome and dusky-featured deity from the furthest steppe of India [444]. Wright (1968, p. 19): In romance mythology Oberon was an eastern fairy; a figure of grace associated with the divine love which preserves marriage and orders the universe. Levith (1978, p. 76): The Latin oberro, I wander about or go astray, may be the root of the name. (Oberrare also means blunder, err, mistake.) Brown (1987, pp. 28–33) suggests (p. 33) the name Oberon puns on over, as ruler of nature and the ruler over the others in MND, and also (p. 27) as, with Titania, occupying the highest, most spiritual position on the scale of love. Yuasa (1994, p. 79): Oberon is related to Aubray, the French form of the Old German Alberic, which is a compound of alfi, meaning elf, and ric, meaning ruler, but according to the Dictionary of Mythology, Folklore and Symbols [Gertrude Jobes, 1962, p. 1192], Oberon is also a Masculine name from the Frankish, meaning the obedient.
Cambillard (1939, p. 121) claims that astrologically Oberon = Sun (Fr.). Asimov (1970, p.28): Oberon and Titania are both in the heavens now. In 1787 the astronomer William Herschel so named the two outermost satellites of Uranus.

19 Tytania, Queene of the Fairies]
Keightley (1833, 2:127 n.) notes that King James (Daemonologie, 1597, p. 73) calls Diana the queen of the phairie and that Ovid calls Diana Titania. Baynes (1880, p. 101) points out that other goddesses were also styled Titania by Ovid, and explores the significance of the classical associations; see . Earlier, Lloyd (in Singer, ed. 1856, 2:432) claimed that Ovid provided no help in explaining the mythological fitness of the adoption of the name, since it depends upon the analogy between the mythology of Faërie and of the classic Titans; Titania, Titanis, or Titaia, are the titles of the feminine members of the family of Ge and Uranus, of Earth and Heaven, a leading group in the primeval mythology of the Greeks, and which is placed, in the Theogony of Hesiod especially, with some qualification, in broad contrast to the family of Kronus.…The general outlines of the Titans blend away into physical symbolism, or, still farther, into mere physical energies and elemental powers. It is in this respect that, however contrasted in scale, they combine with the elemental mythology of elves and fairies. Briggs (1959, pp. 24, 44–5): Sh. followed [Scottish] tradition in making Titania Queen of the Fairies instead of Queen Mab. She also explores Titania’s connection with Diana, the classical goddess (see ).
Morley (1893, p. 234): Titania…was named…. after Titan, the Sun God, brother of Helios, a name associated with titha, the Sanskrit word for fire. See also Knight (1958, p. 184). Root (1903, p. 52): It is Diana as goddess of the groves who gives her name Titania to the queen of fairies in [MND].…It has been noticed that in Golding’s translation the name Titania does not occur. Cambillard (1939, pp. 120–121) identifies Titania as the moon partly through the moon’s alternate names: Titania like Lucina takes an interest in maternity; the moon causes the disorder in the earth pale in her anger [479], as Titania is also in revolt and angry; Theseus says that he will celebrate his marriage at the change of the moon and Titania says she won’t leave the wood until the day after Theseus’s marriage; and Titania’s revolt causes disasters just as the moon in its last quarter is reputed to be particularly pernicious (Fr.). Davenport (1949, p. 525), noting that Circe is called Titania in the story of Picus in Met. 14.320 ff., draws parallels between Titania and Circe. White (1970, p. 47): Titania may be…the daughter of any Titan.…There is no reason to believe…that she is Artemis or Diana. Diana was known for her chastity; Titania is a married woman, in love with an ass. Titania is not a huntress; nor does she preside over marriages. But she is more than a parent and original of the human mortals; she is the queen of those parents. If she is the queen of the gods or queen of the unreal world of fairies, she can only be Hera or Juno. McPeek (1972, p. 74 & n. 10): Titania’s name is a patronymic of Diana.…Apuleius makes it clear that Diana is only one of the names and aspects of Queen Isis (XI.5; 117). That Shakespeare was aware of the manifold nature of Diana is evident from his association of his fairies with the triple Hecate [2166–7]. Wiles (1993, p. 4): Noting that in Spenser [FQ 7.6.4–11] a Titaness…tries to drive [Cynthia] from her throne in the moon, argues the name Titania defines a chthonic, anti-Olympian persona for Shakespeare’s fairy queen. Yuasa (1994, p. 79): Titania comes from Titan which is usually believed to have been derived from the Greek titaino, meaning to stretch, but NCD also suggests tio, meaning to honour, as a possible source. Since Titania is a patronymic applied to Diana, I think the name is meant to be a eulogy. However, I cannot help feeling that the name is somewhat incongruous with the Queen of the Fairies. When she becomes too Titanic, and forgets the virtue of obedience, Oberon is no longer the ruler. Hackett (1997, p. 15) notes that the Circean reference is reversed: instead of a female seductress who exploits men’s sexual desires in order to enthrall, humiliate and bestialize them, we see in Shakespeare’s Titania a woman with magical powers who is herself enthralled and humiliated by her passion for a beast.
Richer (1974, pp. 10–11) dividing the name TIT -ANIA, plays with semantic possibilities of each part to arrive at the meaning Titled she-ass (Fr.).

20–4 Pease-blossome…Mustard-seede]
Wood (ed. 1902, p. 79): The names of the fairy queen’s attendants are expressive of their delicacy and beauty. Sidgwick (1908, pp. 38–9) The names of the four attendant fairies, Peaseblossom, Cobweb, Moth, and Mustard-seed, are Shakespeare’s invention, chosen perhaps to typify grace, lightness, speed, and smallness. It should be noted that in The Fairy Queen (1692) the actors playing the mechanicals doubled as the fairies; see also Ringler (1968, p. 133).
Reynolds & Sawyer (1959, pp. 513–21): Because each of these fairy servants represents an item used in household remedies…, it seems possible that the four fairies find their origin in folk medicine.…Bottom’s greetings to Peaseblossom and Mustardseed play upon the medicinal uses of the pea and mustard plants.…The fourth fairy Moth, or Mote, is linked to the other fairies through the use of the moth in household remedies; the method of preparation [(p. 521) steamed alive] may offer a possible, or partial, explanation why this character was never developed. (P. 518): Cobwebs were placed on a cut to staunch the flow of blood. Bottom’s greeting to Peaseblossom introduces a quibble on the plant cycle. Are the pod and the seed parent to the blossom, or is it the other way about with the blossom and the plant proving parent to the seed? Applying Halliwell’s (1847; 1855, 2:610) comment on William’s wooing a peascod instead of Audrey (AYL 2.4.51–2 [832–3]) to MND, they expound: The juice from the flower might augur a strange and wonderful love; from the first well-filled peascod the rustic lovers could read the future of their love. The juice of the flower could expunge the very love it engendered; and so the common pea: if either lover were jilted, the pain of the lost love was eased by rubbing the whole body with the hay of the pea plant. The play on peasecod and codpiece, cod meaning testicle as well as bag of seeds suggests a much more physical love. Bottom’s words to Mustardseed (1010–14) recognize (p. 519) the two most common uses of mustardseed—its medicinal use in a plaster or poultice…and its table use as a condiment to be served on beef,…[and] also play upon the minute size of the mustardseed in contrast to its strength—both its biting pungency and amazing fertility. (P. 520): The common night moth had both an internal and external medical use: because of its caustic properties it was made into a plaster to treat old sores and was also used to prepare a diuretic.
Briggs (1959, p. 23) quotes Nashe (Terrors of the Night, 1594, ed. R.B. McKerrow, Oxford, 1958, 1:347) on the diminutiveness of fairies, and cites a passage where he suggests playfully that mustard contains a devil,…which may have caught Shakespeare’s quick eye when he was naming Mustardseed. White (1970, p. 56): It is a strange kingdom that Titania rules. Of the four fairies who are named, one, Moth, does not speak, and yet she is the only one who is, nominally, animate. Cobweb has to do with sophistry, and had so to do for an Elizabethan audience. Mustardseed has to do with the kingdom of heaven.
Brooks (ed. 1979): Almost certainly Shakespeare had in mind not moth but mote, of which moth is a regular Elizabethan and Shakespearean spelling:…Mote matches Cobweb for insubstantiality, and has a virtually decisive link with Mustardseed through Scripture, minuteness being the point of the Gospel references to each. In LLL, [4.3.159–60 (1498–9)], the reference is explicit to the contrast in Matthew vii. 3–5, Luke vi. 41 f., between the tiny mote and the huge beam.…A grain of mustardseed…indeed is the least of all seeds (Matthew xiii. 31–2); cf. Mark iv. 31. White (ed. 1858) in support of his decision to print Mote rather than Moth gives a number of parallels, including a passage from Randolph’s Amyntas where the word is applied to a fairy [1.3; 1638, O3v]. The interpretation, though not the textual emendation, is supported by e.g. Doran (ed. 1959, 979–80) and Clemen (ed. 1963). Recognizing the possibility of Mote but unwilling to abandon Moth are e.g. Wells (ed. 1967, p. 143): But the association with Cobweb may support the traditional spelling with the modern meaning; Foakes (ed. 1984, pp. 84–5): Mote may be what Shakespeare intended, a minute particle matching the mustard-seed,…but Moth also fits, as a tiny winged creature. Mowat & Werstine (ed. 1993), printing Mote: Each of these names indicates something very tiny or otherwise hard to see.…Mote: speck (Since the words mote and moth were pronounced the same way, and since the character’s name is spelled moth in the early printings of this play, the character’s name might mean, instead, a small flying insect). Holland (ed. 1994, p. 186), also printing Mote: Sh.’s unequivocal references to moths are uncomplimentary [MV 2.9.79 (1192); Oth. 1.3.256 (606)].…Motes and cobwebs are similarly insubstantial; motes and mustardseeds are equivalently minute in the Bible.
Hughes (1993, p. 222) discusses Philostratus’s Imagines and what he says about bees as a possible means of understanding the diminutiveness of the fairies.
Wiles (1993, p. 122): The names of the fairies suggest sexual attributes.…Cobweb—used to staunch bleeding—…Peaseblossom—a flower which will grow into a phallic peascod—…Mustardseed—the tiny potent seed which grows into a huge tree. Hackett (2003, pp. 344–5) discusses the sexual connotations of the names of the four fairies.
See also nn. 996–1015, 1000–1, 1003, 1004, 1005, 1010–14, Sources and Analogues , Criticism .

0.1–0.3 A…dreame]
First comment on the title stems from the apparent contradiction between the season it announces, and the season of the action indicated by Theseus’s reference to Maying, which Johnson notes with his usual terseness (see n. 1625). Farmer (in Steevens ed. 1778; also ed. 1773, Appendix 2, 10:2O8v) compares the similar discrepancy in WT, where the latter part of the play is at the season of sheep-shearing; neither title seems intended to denote the precise time of the action. Malone (ed. 1790, at 1625) imagines the title suggested by the time it was first introduced on the stage, which was probably at Midsummer. A Dream for the entertainment of a Midsummer-night; he compares TN and WT. Gentleman (ed. 1774) looks further to the relationship of the title to the nature of the play: By introducing the word Dream as part of the Title, he evidently meant to prepare for a rambling incoherence of plot, and he certainly chose Midsummer for the season, to accommodate the favourite children of his romantic fancy, his Fairies. Coleridge (c. 1818; 1960, 1:90, 109, 37, 206), more sympathetically, is convinced that Shakespeare availed himself of the title of the play in his own mind [as] a dream throughout. (P. 109): He refers to the exquisite significance of the titles MND, AYL, and WT, where (p. 37) the total effect is produced by a co-ordination of the characters, by a wreath of flowers; (p. 206) these plays have one idea which gives the tone to each…not seldom implied in the titles, which deserve to be mentioned as an idioticon of Shakespeare’s.
Drake (1817, 1:328–34) finds the title appropriate because of customs and superstitions attached to midsummer eve, such as gathering fern seed to go invisible, which was possible only on that night, and was (329) under the peculiar protection of the Queen of Faery, seeing spirits while sitting, fasting, at the church porch, (331) observance…by rejoicings, spells, and charms, and seeing a future husband or wife. For these reasons a play so titled may have been chosen for exhibition on that evening. Moreover, Sh. (333) might have been influenced in his choice of the fanciful machinery of this play, by the recollection of the proverb attached to the season; an adage founded on the common opinion, that the brain, being heated by the intensity of the sun’s rays, was more susceptible of those flights of imagination which border on insanity, than at any other period of the year; he cites Steevens (in Reed ed. 1813, 5:359) glossing TN 3.4.56 (1576), Olivia’s exclamation on Malvolio’s midsummer madness: ’Tis midsummer moon with you, is a proverb in Ray’s Collection; signifying, you are mad. Halliwell (1841, p. 3) also quotes Heywood’s Epigrammes on Proverbes (1567), As mad as a March hare; where madness compares, Are not Midsummer hares as mad as March hares? Harness (ed. 1825; 1830, at 1625): I presume that to this play the name was given, from its light and fantastic events, being such as might be supposed to float on the imagination during the visions of a summer night. Daniel (ed. 1828, p. 5): It is strictly a midsummer night’s dream—a fairy vision that may be supposed to pass before the mind during that luxuriant and romantic season. Spalding (1840, p. 479): MND is what its title indicates—a dream over which broods the magical dimness of a summer night, half hiding and half revealing scenes where nature slumbers in her most luxuriant beauty. But it is also the dream of a poet—such a dream as no poet save one ever dreamt. Halliwell (1841, pp. 2–3), quoting Coleridge, speculates that the first idea of the play was conceived on Midsummer Night, possibly on the Midsummer Night that according to Aubrey Sh. spent at Grendon in Buckinghamshire. Hunter (1845, 1:282): At the sight of such a title we naturally ask—Who is the dreamer? The poet, any of the characters of the drama, or the spectators? The answer seems to be that there is much in this beautiful sport of imagination which was fit only to be regarded as a dream by the persons whom the fairies illuded: and that, as a whole, it comes before the spectators under the notion of a dream.…By calling it a dream he obviated the objection to its incongruities, since it is of the nature of a dream that things heterogeneous are brought together in fantastical confusion. Strachey (1854, p. 677): The title…is the clue to the whole play. By its help you may trace a coherent design and execution from first to last.…None of your gabble about unbridled imagination and irregular genius; the fault is in ourselves if we do not discover that here, as elsewhere, Shakespeare’s intellect was the master of his poetic faculty, and subject to the strict laws of reason even when most it seems to wander at its own sweet will.…Sleep and dreams are facts, phenomena of our actual human existence. Lloyd (in Singer ed. 1856, 2:423–4): The proper scene of the Dramatic Dream is by the light of a midsummer moon,…The climate and the season both are fitting for fairy spiritings. Midsummer-night, St. John’s Eve, was the traditional occasion of spiritual liberty. Then the upper and the under worlds were nearer than at any other season, and the transition of spiritual natures from one to the other had freest course. Gervinus (1863, 1:262–3, 268): The piece is called a Midsummer-Night’s Dream; the Epilogue expresses satisfaction, if the spectator will regard the piece as a dream; as in a dream, time and locality are obliterated. The many allusions to dreams (263) have suggested…the idea that the poet had intentionally aimed at letting the piece glide by as a dream. But the poet’s purpose, which has…given a name to the piece, is that it is (268) designed to be treated as a dream; not merely in outer form and colour, but also in inner signification. The errors of that blind intoxication of the senses, which form the main point of the piece, appear to us to be an allegorical picture of the errors of a life of dreams. Dowden (1875, p. 72): MND is, as its name implies, a phantasmagory; a mask of shadows full of marvel, surprises, splendour, and grotesqueness. Ulrici (1868–9; tr. 1876, 2:76): The title intimates Sh.’s intention and view in [the play’s] composition.…Human life appears conceived as a fantastic midsummer night’s dream. As in a dream, the airy picture flits past our minds with the quickness of wit; the remotest regions, the strangest and most motley figures mix with one another, and, in form and composition, make an exceedingly curious medley; as in a dream they thwart, embarrass and disembarrass one another in turn, and—owing to their constant change of character and wavering feelings and passions—vanish, like the figures of a dream, into an uncertain chiaro-scuro. Moyse (1879, p. 12): The title at once suggests that we have something rare before us….The light of the play reveals the aptness and significance of the name very clearly. The poet is the dreamer. His mind gives itself up entirely to a waking reverie or a sleeping dream. Ellacombe (1880–6, pp. 71–2): The name marks the season, and there is a profusion of flowers to mark it too. It may seem strange to us to have Apricocks at the end of June, but in speaking of the seasons of Shakspere and others, it should be remembered that their days were twelve days later than ours of the same names. Rolfe (1889, pp. 184–5): The title does not refer to the time of the action.…The word midsummer does not occur in the text of the play. In choosing the title Shakespeare not improbably had in mind the many superstitions connected with Midsummer Eve. He also mentions proverbs, as in TN and Heywood, and the suggestion that the title refers to the time of first production. Korner (1891, pp. 17–18), returning to the apparent contradiction in time of title and action, argues that Sh. is harmonizing the dates of the summer solstice and the first of May by reference to solar myths. Verity (ed. 1893, pp. xv–viii) argues that the title refers to the date of presentation, not the action of the play, and is (p. xvi) composed…to harmonise with the associations of the festival. (P. xvii): Witches and spirits…were peculiarly active then, and strange things happened. He compares MND with Jonson’s masque The Satyr, finding it significant that both great dramatists should each have written a work in which Midsummer-night is associated so pointedly with the denizens of fairy-land. (P. xviii): [It is t]o be judged as a dream.…We do not expect logical coherence in a dream. We must not look for it in the fairy fantasies of a dream-comedy. Furness (ed. 1895, pp. v–vi): To the inheritors of the English tongue the potent sway of fairies on Midsummer Eve is familiar. The very title is in itself a charm, and frames our minds to accept without question any delusion of the night; and this it is which shields it from criticism. He comments on German critics’ difficulties in reconciling title and time of action, (p. vi) reflected in the various translations of the title into German. Chambers (ed. 1897, p. 23): Taking perhaps a hint from Lyly, Shakespeare invites us to consider the whole thing as a dream. This is the significance of the title. It is life seen through a glass darkly; such a vision of life as a man might have on Midsummer Night, the one season of the year around which Elizabethan superstition gathered most closely, when herbs were believed to have their especial virtues, and strange beings to be abroad. And yet it is not all a dream, or, if a dream, it is one which passes very easily into actual life. Courthope (1903, 4:92–3) suggests that Sh. supplied an answer to prosy criticism of the title’s inaccuracy in the Epilogue, (93) implying that the essence of the comedy lies in the fairy machinery, which explains the course of affairs to the spectators, but not to the actors. Lefranc (1919, 2:117–19) claims that the title contains a clear allusion to the Whitsun plays, and to the kinds of public festivities—tableaux, parades, dramatic representations, morris dancers, etc.—held on Midsummer Eve, especially as documented at Chester (Fr.). Chambrun (1927, p. 54): The name, indeed, excuses all absurdities, for who, when dreaming, is obliged to respect logic or historical truth? The author gives himself over to fantasy.
Kittredge (ed. 1939, pp. ix–x): The title of the play has no reference to the time of the action, which all takes place about May Day; but it indicates (or strongly suggests) that the nocturnal adventures of the lovers, the transformation of Bottom, and the whole fairy machinery may be regarded as a phantasmagoria.…Midsummer Eve, June 23 (the Vigil of St. John the Baptist), was traditionally a magic time.…The skepticism of Theseus [1794–5] is whimsically confirmed by Puck [2207–2210].
De La Mare (1940, pp. 270, 275): It is called a dream, it is in the nature of a dream, and, as if in a dream, we may submit ourselves to its sweet influences and fantastic enchantments. He suggests Sh. (p. 275) borrowed [the title] from a play already in being.
Schanzer (1951, pp. 233–4) argues that both Walpurgisnacht (the eve of May-day) and Midsummer Night (St. John’s Eve) are particularly well fitted to provide the time-setting for the supernatural events in the wood. Besides the connections with witchcraft and magic, Midsummer Night is also associated with flower magic, and with various superstitions connected with maidens’ dreams on that night. Furthermore the proverbial midsummer madness links with what he considers the main theme of the play, (p. 234) the ridiculing of a certain kind of madness, of love-madness. Barber (1959, pp. 119–24) sees MND shaped by May games and pageantry. He argues (p. 120): People went Maying at various times; Theseus’s reference to Maying (1653–5) can be thought of as happening on a midsummer night, even on Midsummer Eve itself, so that its accidents are complicated by the delusions of a magic time. (P. 123): The humor of the play relates superstition, magic and passionate delusions as fancy’s images. The actual title emphasizes a sceptical attitude by calling the comedy a dream. It seems unlikely that the title’s characterization of the dream, a midsummer night’s dream, implies associations with the specific customs of Midsummer Eve. But Clemen (ed. 1963, pp. xxiv–v): MND is clearly related to the practices of midsummer night, the night before June 24, which was the date of St. John the Baptist’s festival and hence connected with merrymaking, various superstitions and folk customs, dances, pageants, and revels. More than any other night in the year, midsummer night suggested enchantment and witchcraft, something which Shakespeare has superbly embodied in his fairy world. To an Elizabethan audience, moreover, the play’s title would have immediately called to mind the so-called midsummer madness, which was a state of mind marked by a heightened readiness to believe in the delusions of the imagination that were thought to befall the minds of men after days of great summer heat. Thus, by means of his highly suggestive title, Shakespeare has firmly planted the dreamlike action of his drama in the popular beliefs and customs of his time. Furthermore, the title gives theatergoers and readers a clue as to how the work should be understood—namely, as an unrealistic creation of the imagination, a series of dream images containing all the contradictions and inconsistencies that dreams normally possess, but containing too their symbolic content. Indeed, the dreamlike character of what takes place is repeatedly alluded to. In Puck’s epilogue, for instance, the audience themselves are explicitly addressed: [quotes 2211–13]. In short, the play’s title makes significant allusion to the nature and meaning of the work, though it makes no reference to the period of time during which the events of the drama occur. In fact, the action takes place between April 29 and May 1. Kermode (2000, p. 61): Sh.’s title alludes to the licence of St. John’s Eve, the right to make love in the woods, out of the control of the city, in the realm of nature and not of civil order.
Riklin (1968, p. 281) accounts for the apparent contradiction between May, a time of upsurging life forces, with vegetation and its gods and spirits, and Midsummer when the year passes its zenith,…a time of good harvest, fruitfulness and maturity, by understanding the connection in the sense of a condensation, if we use Freud’s terminology, which yields a play which, being deeply rooted in popular sentiment and experience, therefore affords insight into the soul. Pyle (1969, pp. 1–2) compares WT and Lyly’s Endymion, the Man in the Moon, where in both the intention of the title is of predisposing the playgoer to excuse, as in keeping with the matter of the play, anything in the means or method used. In his prologue, Lyly (p. 2) leaves it open to the audience to receive his play as nothing more than light and fanciful entertainment; but by seeming to disclaim contemporary application he puts it in their minds to look for a sophisticated application as well.…A Midsummer Night’s Dream is a parallel case, its title ostensibly implying a weak and idle theme, / No more yielding but a dream [2211–12], and thereby hinting perhaps at a grace beyond the reach of reason. Salingar (1974, p. 13): The association between comedy and festivity and magic was, of course, latent in popular tradition, in the ancient tradition of rejoicing so as to usher in a fresh season and also purging away evil so as to begin the year anew or warding off the threatening spirits set loose at a turning point of the year. But, among the Elizabethans, Shakespeare seems to have been exceptionally sensitive to this tradition, as is suggested by the very choice of such titles as [MND and TN]. True, he makes free with the calendar (which was not exacting, however, where popular customs were concerned): he brings the rite of May into his midsummer comedy and describes the behaviour of Malvolio and Sir Andrew in his Christmas play as midsummer madness and then as matter for a May morning; the title of the play stands for a mood rather than a date. (Similarly, there is little more than a marginal overlap between title and plot in Chapman’s comedy, May-Day.) What counts in these Shakespearean titles is the suggestion of a festive but yet critical passage of time, when the characters are swept out of their previous selves and brought into a fresh harmony with a natural order and sequence in life. Pagnini (1976, p. 102; tr. 1999, p. 33): Midsummer day symbolically represents the passage from innocence and pubescence, which are the metaphoric and mythical characteristics of springtime, to the physiological potency and maturity—and fertility—of summertime. This is, indeed, the central theme of the play.…The title of the play itself refers not to Midsummer day but to Midsummer night, with an entire series of connotative implications. Night is an evident symbol of irrationality, blindness, error, and dream. It should also be noted…that the dream is the space where the two worlds of reality and irreality meet and blend together. See also Hunter (John Lyly, 1962, p. 329) on the confusion of dream and reality in this play; Foakes (ed. 1984, n. 1584), concerning the fearce vexation of a dreame.
Morris (1985, p. 237): We are told by the title to consider the happenings [of the chief action] merely a dream; thereby we are forcibly informed that the duration of young love is no more than the ephemeral joy of a happy night-vision, beheld on the shortest night of the year.…The festivities on Midsummer Eve are the last great burst of life, of joy and high spirits, before the long winter draws on. Laroque (1991, pp. 217, 228–30): Sh. played on the similarities between the festivities of May Day and those of Midsummer, deliberately confusing them as if they were more or less equivalent. Similarly, calendrical references to the moon, and to day and night, are muddled and apparently (p. 229) nonsensical. (P. 230): The festivities are diluted by being extended into time that fades away in the distance (if they began on May Day, it will be Trinity Sunday by the time they are over).…While the reference to dreams in the title is in itself enough to tip the misunderstandings and intrigues of the forest world into an atmosphere of unreality, the muddle over the calendary reference points and the incompletion of the festival increase still further the doubts raised in the spectator’s mind. The festival itself is perceived either as a chimerical dream or as stranded somewhere between the extremes of postponement and promise and, as such, is rejected as fantasy.…In this play, it is not a matter of liberating festivity from the constraints of the winter calendar or of allowing it to develop freely in the more flexible framework of the pastoral celebrations of summer. The point is to use festivity as a means of abolishing continuous time altogether. In this respect, the festival is the exact equivalent of sleep: a non-time in which awareness of duration and of existence is wiped out.
Wiles (1993, pp. 73, 104, 138): The title has proved an enigma for critics who have focussed upon the play’s overt references to May Day.…The conventions of the epithalamium show how central the idea of midsummer was in the symbolic representation of marriage. While May Day was associated with youth, growth and desire, midsummer was associated with transition. Just as midsummer is the most important turning point in the solar cycle, so marriage is the most important turning point in the human life cycle. (P. 104): The play merits the title A Midsummer Night’s Dream because of the broad structural way in which it anticipates—or conjures up in a dream—a moment of transition. There is some significance, perhaps, in the fact that maidens on midsummer night dreamed of the man they were to love.…Midsummer Night is also relevant to the play because of its association with what Olivia terms midsummer madness. (P. 138): Since Shakespeare’s play only refers in oblique ways to midsummer, his choice of title can be explained as a homage to Spenser’s influential poem [Epithalamion].

4–265 Now…againe]
Bradbrook (1951, p. 155): The opening scene, daylight and Athens, maintains a formal style which sets the death penalty hanging over Hermia and the treachery of Helena in their proper perspective. Kermode (2000, pp. 60–1): The opening scene is masterly, establishing not only the narrative but the thematic interest. Lysander is accused of corrupting the fantasy of Hermia [40]: disorders of the fantasy or imagination are a main topic of the play. When Hermia complains that her father cannot see Lysander with her eyes, Theseus tells her she is required to subordinate her eyes to her father’s judgement [65–6].…Lysander and Hermia alone complain of the misfortunes of love: [159].…Now poor fancy’s followers [165] decide to elope. Helena complains of her ill fortune, for Demetrius prefers Hermia’s eyes to hers. The emphasis is always on the eye as the source of love, or rather of doting:…[117–18, 239]. Doting is a disordered condition of the imagination,…and it originates in an eye uncontrolled by judgement.…[Quotes 244–52].…Helena’s remarks about the blind Cupid are traditional, love being irresponsible, a disease of the eye. But there is a contrary neo-Platonic interpretation of blind Cupid, namely that love transcends even sight,…at work in the play.

4–14 Now…solemnities]
Schanzer (Moon, 1955, p. 242): The opening lines…establish the dominant atmosphere…of moonlight, love, and dreams.

4–9 Now…reuenewe]
Scragg (1996, p. 75): The imagery here, while conveying the strength of Theseus’ desires and his enforced submission to the temporal process, defines authority in the world of the play as female, rather than male. The old moon, explicitly female, is seen as frustrating the happiness of the Duke, and is compared to other authority figures in atypical ascendency over masculine dependents. Cf. n. 6–14.

4 Hippolita…hower]
Irving & Marshall (ed. 1888): Shakespeare followed Chaucer, who himself followed the Theseida of Boccaccio, in making Hippolyta (properly Hippolyté), and not her sister Antiope, the wife of Theseus. See .

5 apase]
Johnson (1755, Apace 1): Quick; speedily. Schmidt (1874) adds: In speaking of approaching time, almost = soon. Associated with the urgency of desire in Juliet’s speech of longing for the amorous rights of her marriage, Gallop apace you fierie footed steedes (Rom. 3.2.8 [1645]).

5 fower…daies]
For discussion of the time of the action of the play, see . Granville-Barker (ed. 1924, pp. xv–xvi) sums up the main modern attitude: The duration of the action is planned for four days. This is…so emphatically stated that we are obviously meant to remark it. And the dramatic worth of the matter lies, of course, in the need for giving Hermia some clearly stated and limited time in which to make choice of her fate.…Thereafter the matter loses its importance for him, and he becomes…correspondingly careless about its regulation. The adventures in the wood are in effect the adventures of one night. When Theseus wakes the lovers, he states that the four days are up—and who is there that would contradict such a benevolent hero?
The emphasis on fower here and at 10, 11 may intentionally call to mind the Pythagorean tetrad, which was the archetypal idea in the mind of the creating deity (Heninger (1977, p. 107), who from it created the body of the world…and it was harmonized by proportion, and therefore has the spirit of friendship (Plato, Timaeus 32, tr. Jowett, 1892, 3:451). Heninger (1974, p. 377) suggests that Sh. sometimes structured his work according to cosmic patterns, and that in MND only when the opposites are reconciled and all of the couples are sorted out in appropriate pairs can the fairies dance at the wedding. Cf. 1486.

5 happy]
Schmidt (1874, 3): Favourable, propitious (OED, 3). Cf. 1773. Scott (ed. 1898): By the figure of Hypallage, or of Transferred Epithet, the happiness of the expectant lovers is transferred to the time through which they are passing.

6–14 An…solemnities]
Hunter (1845, 1:287): Shakespeare, like Homer, may sometimes slumber: for, as the old moon had still four nights to run, it is quite clear that at the time Hippolyta speaks of there would be no moon, either full-orbed or like to a silver bow [12]. Neil (ed. 1878): Why should Shakespeare, and his readers too, not slumber and enjoy the sweet privilege of having the moon arranged to suit the prospects and hopes of the future in a midsummer night’s dream?
Thomas (1971, pp. 297, 620) cites several authorities which show that people…allowed the phases of the moon to determine their timing of various activities. The medieval Church had inveighed against the practice of only celebrating marriages…when the moon was waxing. There were those who thought it imprudent to marry under a waning moon. Wilson (1993, p. 14) quotes Scot, The Discouerie of Witchcraft 9.2 (1584, n5r), who appears to approve the observation that the increase of the moone maketh plants and liuing creatures frutefull:…decaieng in the wane. Halliwell (ed. 1856, at 13) quotes from the old romance of Sir Eglamour of Artois (which he edited in 1844) where the marriage is arranged In the spryngynge of the mone (1161, p. 169). The reference by Moberly (ed. 1881) to the Germanic belief that victory could not be won if an action was fought before the new moon (Caesar, The Gallic War, 1.50) may be appropriate to the hero and his warrior love.
Hackett (1997, p. 12), quoting Wilson’s (1993, p. 14) reference to the moon as governess of menstruation and tides: It may not be too fanciful to suggest that the delay of the wedding is related to the coinciding of the unpropitious waning of the moon with Hippolyta’s own menstrual period.

6 me thinks]
OED (Methinks, impers. v.): It seems to me. For impersonal verbs with no expressed subject but a pronoun in the dative, see Franz §627. See also Blake 4.4.1.

6–9 how…reuenewe]
Whalley (1748, p. 55) compares Horace’s Epistles, 1.1.21–3, for which Halliwell (1841, p. 17) gives Thomas Drant’s translation [Horace his arte of Poetrie, pistles, and Satyrs Englished, 1567, B8v]: Slowe seames the yeare vnto the warde which houlden downe must be, In custodie of stepdame straite: Slowe slydes the time to me. Colman (1768, 2:164) translates Terence’s Hecyra 4.595–6 [The Mother-in-Law], where the stepmother says: ’Tis now my chief concern to make my age Easy to all, that no one may regret My lengthen’d life, nor languish for my death; he comments: This idea of the long life of a Step-Mother being odious to her family is applied in a very beautiful and uncommon manner by Shakespeare. Capell (1783, 2.3:100): Dowagers that are long-liv’d wither-out estates with a witness, when their jointures are too large, and what remains too little for the heir’s’ [sic] proper supportance: whose impatience to bury them, must (in that case) be of the strongest degree. Mowat & Werstine (ed. 1993): In the same way that a stepmother or a widow with rights in her dead husband’s property (1) makes a young heir wait to inherit it, or (2) wastes it, or (3) has a claim on the young man’s income until she dies. The Sokols (2000, dowager/widow, p. 93) quote these lines to illustrate a social issue…that overlapped with legal matters in the possible resentment on the part of a younger generation of some widows’ control of extensive property.
