Weiss (1971, p. 78): A daylight man, he [Theseus] resents the moon, finds it no more than an obstacle.…It is amusing and right that he should translate the moon into terms of money, inheritance, activity. Hopkins (1998, p. 25): Theseus insistently perceives all the blocking figures to their marriage as female.…He alludes…to the moon…, a stepdame and a dowager. Hippolyta, in marked contrast, concurs in imaging the moon as female, but views it as a symbol of empowerment, a representation of the bow [12] which was once her weapon. Theseus’ assumptions are even more remarkable in a play where the blocking figures are in fact uniformly male. Leggatt (1999, p. 47): The moon…will haunt the language of this play; it makes its first appearance as a malignant old woman, against whom Theseus feels powerless. Blits (2003, p. 19): Theseus’s first simile reminds us that his stepmother was Medea, who, according to ancient legend, sought not merely to wither out his revenue, but to kill him during a celebration in his honor; see .

6–7 oh…waues]
Sitwell (1948, p. 189): Long stretches of moonlight are given by the vowels…; first, internal assonances, then a wonderful fading dissonance (Moone wanes); and with that, we move from the moonlight into shadow. Schanzer (Moon, 1955, p. 242): Indicates, I think, a night-setting for the scene.

6 Moone]
Moone or its compounds is found 52 times in the play, the most uses in all Sh.’s plays, the next highest in frequency being Tmp. with 12. Spurgeon (1931, p. 151): The influence and presence of the moon is felt throughout, largely through the imagery. See . For speculation on the dates of new moons in the 1590’s, their bearing on the play as enacted to celebrate a wedding, and lunar contradictions as evidence of an earlier draft, see , .

7 waues]
Q2 emends to wanes and all editors, probably rightly, follow. See textual n. But Wood (1966, p. 129) finds, in Kyd’s Spanish Tragedy: Phoebus wauing to the western deepe (1.2.83). OED (Wave, v. 5b): Of the sun: To decline. Obs. rare, quoting only Kyd’s use. In his edition Ross (1968, p. 11) states of the play’s copy text O1 that The printing is extremely careful and that the more experienced of the two compositors set the portion of the text containing wauing, but he changes the word to waning. The line was adapted in Wily Beguiled (1602): When Phebus waues vnto the westerne deepe (252), though Greg lists waues among doubtful readings in the Malone Society edition (1912, p. viii). Another example of n/u confusion is at 1598 where the compositor puts fine for fiue. See also 2054 (wane).
Taylor (1971, p. 135): Waues may mean to die away in waves, or carry the legal sense of waive,…suggesting desertion and foresaking [sic]; both meanings are unattested by OED.

7 lingers]
Johnson (1755), quoting this instance: To protract; to draw out to length. Out of use. Capell (1783 [1774], 1:Glossary): Make linger (OED 7a Obs.). For more on the forms of transitive verbs see Abbott §290; Franz §630(c).

7 desires]
Schmidt (1874, 2): Especially sensual affection, lust, appetite. Pearson (1974, p. 292): A significant word in Theseus’ history: desires and lust were more likely to be equated in the Renaissance mind than were desires and love. See also n. 76, and cf. 1494.

8 Like to]
For this common construction see OED, Like, adj. 1a. See Franz §532; Blake 5.4.3. See 12, 1236, 1401.

8 Stepdame]
Schmidt (1875) Stepmother.

8 dowager]
Johnson (1755, 1), quoting this instance: A widow with a jointure [i.e. an estate willed to her by her deceased husband]. OED (n.), quoting the instance at 167: A woman whose husband is dead and who is in the enjoyment of some title or some property that has come to her from him.

9 withering out]
OED (Wither, v.2 7 fig.), quoting this instance first: To cause to decline, decay, or waste. Much of the commentary rejects Warburton’s (ed. 1747) emendation wintering on and praises the original. Heath (1765, p. 41): The metaphor appears to me extremely apposite. Harbage (1963, pp. 105–6) finds the verb multiply suggestive: the anticipated legacy is withering away, the young heir is withering as he waits, and the stepdame or dowager is out-withering both. Lewis (1969, p. 252): Reuenewe and withering…had bawdy connotations. Theseus may be mocking the young man whose sexual prowess is diminished as he is forced to postpone the means to give it full sway. Cf. Partridge (1968, revenue). Walter (ed. 1964): As th was sometimes pronounced d [see Cercignani (1981, pp. 330–1)] there may be a glance at widow. See conj., . Cf. n. 86.

9 reuenewe]
Cawdrey (1604): Rents comming in (OED 3, quoting 168). Seymour (1805, 1:41) accents the first syllable (cf. 168, where the second is accented). See Franz §74.

10–14 Fower…solemnities]
Interpretations of the tone and import of these lines differ sharply. Robinson (1968, p. 384): In the repetitive syntax Hippolyta here captures the sense of moving through ordinary days and nights into transcendent life and time. In the one fine simile that images the moon as heavenly silver bow she symbolizes the translation of the old ways of war into the celebration of nature and love. Time shades into dream, and a war instrument is metamorphosed into a celestial symbol of beauty and new life. McGuire (1985, pp. 1–3) finds this speech ambiguous, and, following his lead, Holland (ed. 1994): It could be an attempt to reassure Theseus that the wedding day they both long for will soon come or an expression of anxiety that the wedding day which will cost the defeated Amazon the last vestiges of her independent selfhood will come only too soon. Leggatt (1999, pp. 47–8): The issue…is not just impatience versus patience but male desire against female control.…This is…Hippolyta’s last speech in the scene…. The silence into which she lapses…reminds us that she is not just a bride but a captive.

10–11 Fower…time]
Rudd (1979, p. 186) compares Theseus’s lamenting the slow passage of time till their marriage with Pyramus and Thisbe in Ovid finding that the day passes too slowly after they have decided to elope. He parallels Hippolyta’s comforting words here with Ovid, Met. 4.91–2, which he translates as: The day quickly steeps itself in the water and from the same water night emerges. See . The duke’s impatience emerges again at 1826–32, 1837–8.

10 steepe]
Johnson (1755), quoting this instance: To soak;…to imbue; to dip. Kittredge (ed. 1939): Become absorbed. Lever (ed. 1961): Plunge into night. The idea is of day plunging into night’s darkness as if into deep water. See OED, v.1 1c, quoting Spenser (Shep. Cal. March, 116), stouping Phebus steepes his face; and 3a, To bathe in slumber (quoted by Brooks, ed. 1979). Cf. Chapman, The Shadow of Night (1594), 376–7 (Poems, ed. Bartlett, 1941, p. 28): No pen can anything eternall wright, That is not steept in humor of the Night (quoted by OED, 2b), and Jonson, Epicœne (1616), 3.5.48–9: Those delights [of the marriage-bed] are to be steep’d in the humor and silence of the night.

10 night:]
The compositor of Q2 apparently mistook Q1’s colon for an s, probably influenced by the plurals (daies and themselues) earlier in this line and by nights in the next line, and many editors followed. See textual n.

11 Fower]
See n. 5.

11 dreame away]
OED (v.2 8), quoting this instance first: To dream away: to pass or spend in dreaming. Scott (ed. 1898): The figure is Hypallage rather than Personification—the dreaming being transferrred from the happy lovers to the time of their dreams. Further, the term dream (according to a familiar metaphorical application) connotes the rapidity of time’s advance: (cf. [154]). Crooks (ed. 1906) compares 1838, the lazy time. Garber (1974, p. 75): Hippolyta uses dream here to mean make to seem unreal. She is suggesting an illusion, the compression of an objective span, four days and nights, into a subjective experience, short as any dream [154]. See also 1720 (dreame). Winter (2003, pp. 95–6), construing away as towards or into non-existence (OED 5), connects this phrase and weare away (1828) as examples of how Sh. plays with the audience’s perception of time (Fr.).

12–13 Moone…bowe…heauen]
Walter (ed. 1964): An apt simile for Hippolyta, the Amazon, whose symbol was the crescent moon. Wiles (1993, p. 49) cites Spenser, FQ 5.5.3, Seneca, Hippolytus 387–403, Vergil, Aen. 1.490, and others to show Amazons bore a crescent-shaped shield, and suggests that here an emblem of the moon was displayed to the audience as part of Hippolyta’s costume.

12 like to]
See n. 8.

12 siluer bowe]
Theobald (1909, p. 199) compares Homer’s description of Apollo’s bow: And dreadful was the clang of the silver bow (Iliad 1.49). Purdon (1974, p. 184) sees the bow as one of the iconological attributes of Diana, goddess of the Moon and chastity. Carlsen (1988, p. 98): We are left rather in doubt whether that silver bow be that of chaste, cool Diana or Cupid’s which inflicts love’s wound upon human beings. Webb (1989, Bow): Sometimes representative of woman, because of her supposed pliancy; also of the vulva or vagina. When the labia are closed the narrowness of the aperture and its curve are reminiscent of the shape, while an arrow (penis) is fitted, ready for shooting, suggesting that here it is used symbolically,…at the approaching wedding of Theseus and Hippolyta. Hackett (1997, p. 13): in Hippolyta’s image of the bow for the new moon…Diana’s bow, figuring female sexual autonomy and virginal intactness, is overlaid with Cupid’s bow and the bow of the Petrarchan lover, figuring male desire and penetration, a blurring of associations which aptly represents the fact that the change of the moon will mark the Amazon’s transmutation from absolute virgin queen to wifely queen consort. This mention of a bow links up with Cupid’s bowe and arrowe (180–1 and 536–42), Bottom’s bowstrings (371), Robin’s allusion to the arrow, from the Tartars bowe (1124), and perhaps Cupids archery (1126).

13 Now]
Collier (ed. 1842): Then the moon, which is now bent in heaven like a silver bow, shall behold the night of our solemnities. Page (ed. 1897) elaborates: And then the moon, now bent in heaven like a silver crescent, will (when in a more advanced stage, being four days older) look down (brightly on) the night of our solemnities. Others favoring QqFf’s Now include Scott (ed. 1898), Porter & Clarke (ed. 1903), Thiselton (1903, pp. 11–12), Wood (1966, p. 129), Herbert (1977, p. 172, n. 7), Wiles (1993, p. 166). From the first word of the play, immediacy of action is stressed by the repetition of Now (68 times).
However, Rowe’s (ed. 1709) emendation to New has been accepted by almost all editors (see textual n.) Knight (ed. 1842) points out that Theseus’s old Moone (7) would contradict Hippolyta’s simile, which is more appropriate to a crescent moon. Dyce (1844, p. 44): Now for new is one of the commonest misprints. See also n. 6–14. Commentators are further puzzled to reconcile these lines, the deadline Theseus gives Hermia (92), and other references to moonlight or lack of it, with a notional time scheme for the action (see ).

13 bent]
Brooks (ed. 1979): A bow bent is ready either to be strung, or to let fly the arrow. In a context like the present, it is an archetype of fruitful union: the woman draws the man but follows him; together they project the child (cf. quiverful for a family with many children [Ps. 127:5]). Calderwood (1992, pp. 77–8): Applied to the frustrated Theseus,…the bent bow suggests the centaur, a familiar figure for lust.…At the same time, the bent bow evokes Hippolyta’s past by figuring as a symbol of Diana in her lunar aspect. Cf. n. 371 for the erotic use of bent bow by Apuleius. See also 1170.

14 solemnities]
Johnson (1755, 3), quoting this instance: Awful ceremony or procession. Schmidt (1875, 1): Ceremony performed (especially the celebration of nuptials);…and hence, festivity (OED 1,2). Scott (ed. 1898): Formal ceremonies…, not…the playful festivity or merriment immediately mentioned by Theseus. Porter & Clarke (ed. 1903) compare Chaucer, KnT 868, 870, and 2700–2. Cuningham (ed. 1905) compares MerT 1709. See , , . Moisan (1987, pp. 36, 41–5) explores the illusory nature of the order implied by solemnity in KnT and MND. Mowat (1989, p. 339) notes that Golding uses the word solemnitie in its Chaucerian, ceremonial sense in connection with Theseus (7.547; see ). See 1655, 1710, 2151.

15 Philostrate]
As Grey (1754, 1:40) first notes, this is the name assumed by Arcite in Chaucer’s KnT (1428; see ). Irving & Marshall (ed. 1888): Pronounced as a trisyllable.

16–28 Stirre…complaint]
Brooks (ed. 1979) compares the incursion of Egeus’s problems upon Theseus’s celebration with the intrusion of sorrow in the joy of the hero’s reception in Golding’s Ovid 7.575–80 (see ), and with the interruption of Theseus’s festive homecoming by the Theban widows (Chaucer, KnT 893–951; see ).

16 Stirre vp]
Johnson (1755, 4): To incite; to animate; to instigate. OED (16d): To rouse to action. See 1402.

16 merriments]
OED (n. 1): A brief comic dramatic entertainment; (2): Mirth, fun; a festivity. See also n. 1171.

17 peart]
OED: A variant of Pert a., with lengthened vowel, found already in 15th c., and formerly occurring in all senses; still widely used in the dialects, and sometimes as a literary archaism or localism in senses no longer expressed by pert. All editions follow F1’s spelling pert (see textual n.), which Johnson (1755, 1), quoting this instance, defines: Lively; brisk; smart. See OED, Pert 6a, also quoting this instance; however, OED devotes a subsection 6, (β), to instances of peart and its variant spellings, quoting in the heading Elworthy (1899, p. 461), who stresses the separateness of peart: In [pert] there is a decided implication of impudence, and it is anything but a word of praise, whereas Peart conveys the impression of sprightly liveliness, of a joyous, healthy, fresh, happy condition. Thirlby (MS 1747–53) compares Tmp. 4.1.58 (1716), pertly; Schmidt (1875) quotes Sh.’s only other use of pert in LLL 5.2.272 (2191).

17 nimble]
Cotgrave (1611, Agile): Nimble, agile, actiue; quicke, prompt;…of a readie stirring. Schmidt (1875): Light, quick and lively in motion (OED 3a, b).

17 spirit]
Animating power (OED 7a), possibly suggesting personification. Schmidt (1875, 5): That which pervades and tempers the whole mind. Johnson (1755, 19): In the old poets spirit was commonly a monosyllable. See Abbott §463. For the pronunciation see Kökeritz (1953, pp. 212–13). See 375, 590, 1026.

18–19 Turne…pomp]
Cf. the measures taken To driue away the dull Melancholy from the betrothal festivities of Red Crosse and Una (FQ 1.12.38.8). Cf. Per. 1.2.2 (225), The sad companion dull eyde melancholie.

18 Turne…forth]
Drive out, send away (cf. OED 25). Only use in Sh. OED does not list Turn forth, and gives only one other usage (Milton, Comus 222, under Lining and Sable, but the sense of the verb is different). Cf. Johnson (1755, 28): To drive by violence; to expel (all examples with out). Schmidt (1875, Turn, 1g): With away, off, out, and similar words, = to discard; (Forth (adv. 1): Off, away, abroad.

18 melancholy]
Johnson (1755, 3): A gloomy, pensive, discontented temper. Schmidt (1875) adds depression of spirits, sadness.

19 The pale companion]
Grey (1754, 1:41) quotes Anon. whose emendation That (see textual n.) is proposed in order to differentiate Melancholy from the moon, another pale companion [which] was to be witness to the marriage pomp; in support Grey cites TGV 4.2.100 (1722), pale queene of night, and 1H4 1.3.202 (526), the palefac’d moone.

19 companion]
Johnson (1755, 3): A familiar term of contempt; a fellow (OED, n.1 4), quoting 2H4 2.4.123 (1150), scuruy companion. But Porter & Clarke (ed. 1903): The sense of the passage hardly seems to require this meaning, for the word was quite as frequently used in Shakespeare’s day to mean one who accompanies another [OED, n.1 1a].…What Theseus objects to is having melancholy accompany their festivities. See also 232 (companions).

19 pomp]
Schmidt (1875, 2): A festival procession; a feast (OED 2). White (ed. 1858) objects to the practice in modern editions [see textual n.] to mark Philostrate’s exit here. Theseus surely did not mean that Philostrate should then rush out incontinent, and begin on the moment to awake the pert and nimble spirit of mirth in the Athenian youth. But Furness (ed. 1895): Philostrate must leave at once, if he is the double of Egeus. See .

20–3 Hyppolita…reueling]
Grey (1754, 1:41): Alluding to Theseus’s conquering the Amazons, and carrying away Hippolita (by others call’d Antiope. See Plutarch’s life of Theseus) their queen. Express’d by Chaucer, Knight’s Tale [859–88]. Young (1966, p. 17) finds Theseus here very close in behavior and ideals to Plutarch’s founder of a city and establisher of communal feasts and games, and to Chaucer’s wise and pleasure-loving ruler who seeks resolution of conflict through ceremony. Olson (1957, p. 103): Theseus, King of Order, has come to rule an all-too-passionate queen. The duke appears to announce the date of the coming marriage; presumably his undisciplined desires will end when the new moon, Chaste Cynthia, replaces the old stepdame whom Renaissance classicists would have recognized as distraught Hecate. There is to be a season of ceremony and pageantry,…[but s]uch stable conditions could not long remain. If they did, there would be no play. But the set presentation of Hippolita and Theseus and their marriage plan states the ethic which is to govern the rest of the work. Farrow (ed. 1972): Theseus fought and mastered the Amazon queen in battle. Now he can be loving and generous for she is in the right place for an Elizabethan wife: only when the man rules the woman, his natural inferior, can marriage be happy, and in this play Theseus and Hippolyta enjoy an ideal relationship. Holland (ed. 1994, p. 131): Some Renaissance writers saw the Amazons as victorious; see, for example, Anthony Gibson’s comment Hippolita dissipated the troops of great Theseus, dismounting himself in the flight, yet afterward (on mere grace) made him her husband (A Woman’s Worth (1599), fol.5r).
Nuttall (2000, p. 52): Instead of making Theseus say I won you by violence but now I will seek to gain your trust by a loving devotion, Shakespeare makes him say, I won you with my sword, but now we will proceed in joyous triumph. The second half of the antithesis actually fails to achieve a fully antithetical status. Triumph remains, obstinately, an arrogant, masculine word. It carries the idea of military victory into the new world of marriage. This opinion ignores Theseus’s vow to wed her in another key and distorts the meaning of triumph (see n. 23). But focussing on 20–21, Belsey (1985, p. 189) has a reservation similar to Nuttall’s: The text here proposes a parallel where we might expect an antithesis. Such doubts are given an extreme interpretation by Traversi (1968, 1:140): This amounts to a recognition that love is compatible with the cruel and the irrational, as indeed much in the coming action will confirm; and by Gohlke (1980, p. 171): These lines, in which the sword may be the metaphoric equivalent of the phallus, in which love may be either generated or secured by hostility, and in which the two partners take up sadistic and masochistic postures in relation to each other, are not irrelevant to the concerns of the play. They may be seen to reverberate in the exaggerated submission of Helena to Demetrius, in the humiliation of Titania by Oberon, in the penetration by violence of the language of love. However, Blits (2003, p. 21) argues: He may have won the warrior queen’s love precisely by doing her injuries, by displaying his heroic virtue. On the other hand, Theseus may be speaking hopefully, proleptically, or even euphemistically and apologetically. Hippolyta may be his war prize, whose love he hopes yet to win despite his having done her injuries. McAlindon (2003, p. 163) sees the notion of universal discordia concors, which underpinned the ubiquitous metaphor of love’s creative or delightful war, literalized in these lines.

20–3 iniuries]
Scott (ed. 1898): May refer either to the defeat sustained by Hippolyta or to the earlier wrong inflicted on the Amazons in the carrying off of their queen Antiopa.

22 key]
Johnson (1755, 5), quoting this instance: (In musick.) Is a certain tone whereto every composition, whether long or short, ought to be fitted…Harris (OED, n.1 7, quoting 1233 as first instance). Schmidt (1874, 2): Metaphorically = tone. Casey (1994, p. 37) sees this metaphor as the first movement from disharmony to harmony in the several love relationships in the play. Cf. Godfrey’s (1993, p. 179) comment on musical puns at n. 246–7.

23 pompe]
Schmidt (1875, 1): Magnificence, splendour (OED 1).

23 with triumph]
Thomas (1587, Triumphus): A triumphe, a solemne pomp or shew at the returne of a Captaine, for a victorie that he hath gotten (OED 1). Malone (ed. 1790) quotes the explanation for triumphs in Milton’s L’Allegro (l. 120) from Warton (1785, p. 56) : Shews, such as masks, revels, &c. (OED 4); in the same note (p. 57) Warton quotes 22–3 of MND, followed by 3H6 5.7.42–4 (3213–15). Steevens (in Reed, ed. 1803) quotes a pamphlet of 1581 to show that the phrase means with all high, great, & statelie dooings (OED 3). Doran (ed. 1959): (1) Magnificently, with public festivities, (2) victoriously.

23 reueling]
OED (Revel, n.1 2): An occasion or course of merry-making or noisy festivity, with dancing, games, masking, acting, or other forms of lively entertainment. White (1793; 1853, p. 29) defends his conjecture revelry (see textual n.): There is scarcely a scene in this play which does not conclude with a rhyming couplet. Furness (ed. 1895) argues that revelry may not rhyme with key, since key was probably pronounced kay. It is hard to see how the Q1 reading could be a compositorial error.

25 and Helena]
See textual n. Thiselton (1903, p. 12) asserts: The Folio is certainly right in omitting Helena—whom the Quartos include—among the entrants. Had she been present, Lysander’s reference to her [115–19] had been other from what we find it. Wilson (ed. 1924): She has nothing to say before [192] where Q. gives her a second entry; but her entry here…may have been necessary in the original draft. Brooks (ed. 1979): Helena’s premature inclusion (Qq) comes no doubt from Shakespeare’s foul papers. Sometimes he seems to have noted (in a massed entry) everyone he expects to need in a scene, anticipating actual points of entrance. Andrews (ed. 1993) suggests she may be a silent observer of the exchanges that follow. She could exit at 137 to re-enter at 191. Some productions have successfully included her in the opening scene, e.g. Folger Theatre Oct/Nov 2006 (dir. J. Banno) which showed her as a horrified spectator, consoled by Hermia who crossed to the side stage to embrace her.

26 Theseus]
Walker (1860, 2:147) says the Shn. pronunciation is trisyllabic, but Chambers (ed. 1897, p. 190) finds only two places where this is true, at 451 and 1834. Midline—here and at 447, 515, 1034, 1607, 1610, and 1793—it is disyllabic. See Kökeritz (1959, p. 94), Dobson (1968, §387).

26 duke]
Grey (1754, 1:41–2) explains Duke as the title for Theseus in Chaucer, KnT 860, 893, etc. (see ); in a footnote he draws on Plutarch who tells how Theseus turned the city of Athens into a commonwealth, and was the first, who…parted with the regal power. See . Steevens (ed. 1773) gives examples of the use of Duke from Chaucer to Stanyhurst’s translation of Vergil in 1582, later (ed. 1793) calling this a misapplication of a modern title. But Douce (1807, 1:179): Duke is a legitimate use of the word in its primitive Latin sense of leader.…Shakspeare might have found Duke Theseus in the book of Troy, or in Turbervile’s Ovid’s epistles. See the argument to that of Phædra to Hippolytus.

27 Egeus]
Delius (ed. 1859): In Sh. [Egeus is] trisyllabic, accented on the second syllable (Ger.). See 124, 132, 1656. For possible sources and associations of the name, see .

27 Whats…thee]
A common expression; Schmidt (1875, News): = what is the matter with you? what would you have with me? See 1304.

28–53 Full…case]
For Gildon’s (1710) praise of this scene, see . Blits (2003, p. 22), citing Aristotle, The Art of Rhetoric, 3.14–19: His speech…contains the four principal parts of a classical forensic speech: (1) an introduction (prooimion) ([28–9]), (2) a narration (diêgêsis) ([30–35]), (3) a proof (pistis) ([36–46]), and (4) a conclusion (epilogos) ([47–53]).

28 vexation]
Schmidt (1875, 2e): Anger, mortification. OED (3), quoting this instance: The state of being distressed, esp. by something causing annoyance, irritation, or disappointment. See 1584.

28 complaint]
Johnson (1755, 4), quoting this instance: Remonstrance against; information against. Egeus is not merely using the word in this general sense, but rather in the legal sense of a grievance laid before a…judicial authority…for purposes of prosecution or of redress (OED, n. 4b).

29 my…my]
Blits (2003, pp. 24, 50): My is, by far, his favorite word. Although he has just seven speeches, Egeus uses my eighteen times and mine another three times. [In accordance with Q1, Blits counts only acts 1 and 4.] On Egeus’s possessiveness and possible jealousy, see also .

29 childe]
Johnson (1755, 4): A girl child, quoting WT 3.3.70 (1511–12) (OED 8b). See 35, 37.

29 Hermia]
The name is pronounced mostly as a disyllable (35 times), but as a trisyllable here and at 113, 568, 572, 768, 1180, 1342, and 1697. For the accented final -a see Cercignani (1981, pp. 301–2).

30, 33 Stand…Lisander.]
Printed as SD’s in Qq and Ff. Settle (ed. 1692) was the first to treat these lines as dialogue and most editors follow. See textual n. Furness (ed. 1895): These lines are clearly part of the text, but being in the imperative mood, so familiar in stage-copies, the compositor mistook them for stage-directions, and set them up accordingly. Wilson (ed. 1924) believes these speeches were probably written as separate half-lines in the original MS, to denote deliberate utterance…, and thus led the compositor astray. Wells & Taylor (1987, p. 280): Though QF has been defended as a stage direction [citing Honigmann (1976, p. 124)], it produces either two incomplete lines or (if rearranged) a defective caesura; Shakespeare’s other texts provide no parallel for such directions; and the compositor usually uses roman for names in stage directions. Compare Thysby stand forth [894]. At least two recent editions, Tucker & Holden (ed. 1990) and Andrews (ed. 1993), adopt the QF reading, with the former explaining: We have found that the Folio stage instruction works well in performance with the lovers stepping forward before Theseus on a gesture from Egeus.

35–44 This…heart]
Characterized by Pope (ed. 1725, 6:4G2v) as Courtship, describ’d. Gentleman (ed. 1774): This Egeus, though he seems a harsh peremptory parent, yet he gives a much more sensible account of the means which have allured his daughter, than Brabantio in Othello, who talks of witching drugs and charms [1.1.171–3 (188–90), 1.3.60–4 (397–401)].

35 man…bewitcht]
Many editors agree with Q1, but many, bothered by the seeming hypermetricality of the line, follow F2 and omit man, while some adopt witch’d with Theobald (ed. 1733). See textual n. Malone (ed. 1790) was the first to defend F2: The emendation was made for the sake of the metre, by the editor of the second folio. It is very probable that the compositor caught the word man from the line above. Boswell (ed. 1821), though he omits man in the text, argues for the Q1 reading: A redundant syllable, at the commencement of a verse, perpetually occurs in our old dramatists. Knight (ed. 1839) follows Q1 because if man is omitted…the emphatic repetition of Egeus is in consequence destroyed. Furness (ed. 1895) agrees: It is needful to retain man as an antithesis to man in line 32;…reducing hath to ’th; This man ’th bewitch’d. However, Sprague (ed. 1896) omits man claiming that the antithetic emphasis is not on man in [32] and man in [35], but on this man and this. Watt (ed. 1906) and Verity (ed. 1912) keep Q1 as their text but make bewitcht one syllable—the first scanning b’witcht and the second ’witch’d, adding prefixes are often dropped. See Abbott §460.

35 bewitcht]
Florio (1598, Infrogiare): To besot or bewitch one with flatteries and faire words. Anon. (ed. 1887): To affect by witchcraft; to gain power over a person by fascination. Hoeniger (ed. Per., 1963) compares Simonides’s accusation of Pericles—Thou hast bewitcht my daughter (2.5.49 [998]). The Sokols (2000, p. 424): In a number of Shakespearian contexts false witchcraft accusations arise palpably from the deluded wishes or other misguided emotions of accusers, as in MND 1.1.27–45 [35–53], OTH 1.2.63–75 [281–93] and 1.3.60–4 [397–401],…WT 4.4.422–4 [2265–7]. These accusations are thus given psychologically plausible motives, excluding the supernatural. Yet the exposure of twisted motivations is always unemphasized, leaving the error of the false accusers implicit.

35 bosome]
Florio (1598, Seno): A bosome,…the minde or inward and priuiest part of a mans hart or affections (OED 6a, quoting 229). Cf. n. 760.

35 childe]
See n. 29.

36–46 Thou…harshnesse]
Punctuation affects meaning here. See textual nn. at 40 and 43. In Q1, in Steevens (ed. 1773), and in Ridley (ed. 1934), Egeus charges Lysander of three love crimes in three clauses, each containing the accusatorial thou hast (in the third clause, hast thou): (36–37) he has given her rimes etc.; (38–40) he has sung and stolne her fancy; (41–46) he has filcht her heart, with bracelets etc. and with cunning, thus making her stubborn rather than obedient. These three editions put a heavy stop after phantasie, thus attributing the stealing of Hermia’s fancy to his singing. Others put a comma or nothing after phantasie, transferring the theft to the gifts of bracelets etc., and making stolne govern 40–43. A strong stop after 43 then sets off 44–6, implying that all Lysander’s previous actions are summed up in cunning.

36 Thou, thou]
Abbott (§231): Thou in Shakespeare’s time was…the pronoun of…contempt or anger on some occasions. OED (pers. pron. 1b): Used in apposition to and preceding a n. in the vocative: in reproach or contempt often emphasized by being placed or repeated after the n., quoting 1978, and Tmp. 3.2.45 (1397): Thou lyest, thou iesting Monkey thou. Walter (ed. 1964): Egeus chokes with wrath.…Scornfully emphatic. See n. 1066, 1068. See also n. 1976, 1978. Gould (1884, p. 15) alone: The second thou spoils the metre and should be omitted. See conj., . But see the next note below, on giuen, which solves the metrical difficulty; and compare the repetitiousness of Egeus’s speech at 1649–52 and 1679–84.

36 giuen]
Monosyllabic. On the softening of medial v see Abbott §466. See 1069. See also n. 156 (heauen).

36 rimes]
OED (n.1 3), quoting this instance: A riming poem or piece of riming verse. Thirlby (MS 1725–33) hesitantly conjectures charms. Deighton (ed. 1891): As though the rhymes were a love-potion. Harrison (ed. 1937): Love spells. Kittredge (ed. 1939): Love poems—with an allusion to the use of rhyme in magic charms.

37 childe]
See n. 29.

38–41 Thou…conceites]
Cf. Chaucer’s Eneas wooing Dido (LGW 1273–5; see ), and Proteus’s advice to Thurio on tangling Silvia’s desires (TGV 3.2.68–84 [1513–29]).

39 faining…faining]
An alternate spelling of feigning, OED (ppl. a.): 2. Dissembling, deceitful, quoting faining loue; 4. Of the voice, quoting faining voice, and referring to Feign (v. 12.a.): To sing softly, to hum an air. See also OED, Fain v1, Hence faining ppl. a., gladsome, affectionate; also, longing, wistful. Many editors follow Rowe (ed. 1709) in printing feigning. See textual n. Halliwell (ed. 1856): Mr. Hunter [1845, 1:287–8] proposes to retain [faining], in the sense, I presume, of wishing fondly, as in Spenser’s Hymn on Love [216],—fairer than fairest, in his faining eye. The probability, however, is that Egeus intended to imply that the love of Lysander was assumed and deceptive. See also Thiselton (1903 p. 12). Furness (ed. 1895) disagrees: [Lysander’s] love was sincere.…[T]he original word is here correct,…in its not unusual sense of loving, longing, yearning.…love-sick. So also Watt (ed. 1906): Egeus would prefer to consider Lysander’s passion insincere; but the general sense is that Lysander has succeeded owing to an unfair use of the methods calculated to appeal to sentiment. Horwood (ed. 1939): Egeus attempts both to deride and disparage Lysander’s love by his quibble on feigning. Walter (ed. 1964): The repetition and quibble point the sarcasm. Foakes (ed. 1984): Quibbling on a now obsolete sense of feigning (= singing softly) and on the more usual meaning (= deceitful, sham), as well as on faining…meaning affectionate, wistful.

40 stolne…phantasie]
Warburton (ed. 1747): The expression is elegant and pretty. It alludes to the taking the impression of a Key in Wax, in order to have another made to unlock a Cabinet. Idem (MS 1747–79): Fancy is the Key, and whoever had that is easily master of all, the Owner possesses. Delius (ed. 1859): The picture of Lysander himself which he illicitly impressed upon Hermia’s imagination and affection (Ger.). Hunter (ed. 1870): Stealthily obtained influence over her fancy by means of bracelets, &c. Most commentators agree with Wright (ed. 1877): Secretly stamped his image on her imagination. But Cowling (ed. 1922) objects: But is not the meaning rather: And hast stolen kisses (the imprint of her delusion in love)? It is difficult to see how a wooer could steal an imaginative image which is impressed on the mind of his lover. Knowles (privately): As often, Sh. mixes his metaphors. The idea that the feeling of love is an impression placed into wax as by a seal-ring is common in Sh.: see TGV 2.4.201–2 (856–7), TN 2.2.29–30 (685–6), AWW 1.3.133 (456). That impression in Hermia’s mind has not been stolen away, as Cowling objects, but the occasions of the impressing were acquired, and the means applied, surreptitiously, thereby filching her heart and with it her obedience to Egeus.

40 phantasie]
Johnson (1755, Fantasy, 1): Fancy; imagination; the power of imagining.…3. Humour; inclination (OED, Fantasy 4a, 7). Phantasy or fantasy, from a Greek root meaning literally a making visible, together with its shortened form fancy, is used most often in MND to mean imagination, or love, though there may be overtones of several other meanings, such as: the faculty by which the mind perceives objects; spectral or illusory appearance; caprice; opinion; inclination or desire (see OED, Fantasy Etym., 1–3c, 4–7; Fancy 1–4, 6–8). The words’ meanings, both in the exercise of the imagination, and in amorous inclination, could be interpreted as good or bad, in the first leading either to monstrous distortion of sense perceptions or to visionary revelation of truth, and in the second either to lust or true love. Hughes (1990, p. 392): In the sixteenth century, a strong association developed between fantasy and sexual desire. It was most marked in the use of fancy,…[T]he two words, though…often used as synonyms, were sufficiently differentiated…to allow for ambiguity and wordplay. Because…writers used imagination as a synonym for fantasy and with equal frequency, the association with desire colored the whole concept. Cf. Brooks (ed. 1979, p. cxxxiii n. 5 and p. cxxxvi n. 3). See 127, 165, 541, 639, 1119, 1688, 1797, 1816 and nn. See also .

41–2 bracelets…sweete meates]
Young (1966, pp. 81–3), describing the stylistic trait of profusion in the play as achieved by listing, as Egeus does here, comments: This is not merely the garrulity of an old man. Every character in the play has the same habit. Maguin (2002, pp. 195–6) quotes the lines as an example of asyndeton, juxtaposing elements of speech that should normally be coordinated, and of copia verborum, (p. 197) (Lat. abundance of speech), based on repetition and variation. An ideal of rhetoric expounded by Erasmus.

41 bracelets…haire]
Halliwell (ed. 1856): The custom of lovers presenting each other with bracelets of hair was common in Shakespeare’s time, giving several examples. Cf. John Donne’s The Relique, l. 6: A bracelet of bright haire.

41 gawdes]
Gildon (1710, Glossary): Bawbles, gawdy things.… Hanmer (ed. 1744, 6:Glossary): A toy, a trifle. Capell (1783 [1774], 1:Glossary): Toys, Nick-knacks; any small Ornaments us’d by Women (OED, Gaud n.2 2). See 1692.

41 conceites]
Cotgrave (1611, Gentilesses): Prettie conceits, deuises, knacks, feats, trickes. Dyce (ed. 1864, 9:Glossary): A fanciful gewgaw. See OED 9a, citing this use.

42 Knackes]
Johnson (1755): A little machine; a petty contrivance; a toy, quoting Shr. 4.3.67 (2051), A knacke, a toy, a tricke. Most define the term simply as toy, while others add the notion of trickiness or deception, combining OED, n.2 1 and 3. Brooks (ed. 1979) refers to Dowland’s song Fine knacks for ladies (1600) where true love confers real value to the trifles. See E. H. Fellowes, English Madrigal Verse 1588–1632, rev. by Sternfeld & Greer (Oxford, 1967, pp. 471–2).

42 nosegaies]
Cotgrave (1611, Bouquet): A nosegay, or posie of flowers (OED 1a).

42 sweete meates]
Florio (1598, Murone): A kind of conserue, or preserue, or sweete meate; (Zuccherini): A kind of sweete meate made of sugar like marchpane, of which they make of diuers formes and fashions, as of beasts and birds (OED Sweetmeat, 1).

42 messengers]
Kittredge (ed. 1939): In apposition with bracelets, rings, etc. Such things convey love’s messages to the lady. See also 1026.

43 preuailement]
Johnson (1755, 4), quoting this instance: Prevalence, defined as Superiority; influence; predominance. Gollancz (ed. 1894, Glossary): Weight, sway. Kittredge (ed. 1939): Persuasive force; efficacy. OED (n.), quoting this instance first: Obs. rare. The action or fact of prevailing, influencing, or gaining ascendancy.

43 vnhardened]
Johnson (1755), quoting this instance: Not confirmed; not made hard. Hunter (ed. 1870): Immature. Wright (ed. 1877): Tender, and capable of receiving impressions; inexperienced. Gollancz (ed. 1894, Glossary), followed by many: Impressionable. Sprague (ed. 1896) wonders whether this word and the images of 56–8 were suggested by impression (40). OED ppl. a., quotes this instance first.

44 cunning]
Doran (ed. 1959), suggesting a link with 35: Expert knowledge (OED 3); expanded by Black (1965, p. 29): Expertness verging on witchcraft (OED, 4, 5). See 1153.

44 filcht]
OED (v. 1): To steal, esp. things of small value; to pilfer. Occas. in weaker sense: To take away surreptitiously. Winter (2003, p. 90) notes that this word reduces Lysander to the level of a common thief, who has stolne (40) Egeus’s property (she is mine 50) (Fr.). See also 174, 226, 1189–92, 1316–17 and nn.

45 due to]
OED (Due a. 5a): Owing by right of circumstance or condition; that ought to be given or rendered; proper to be conferred, granted, or inflicted. a. with to. See 164, 1241.

46 stubborne]
Johnson (1755, 1): Obstinate; contumacious. Schmidt (1875, 2) adds, headstrong.

46 harshnesse]
Johnson (1755, 4): Peevishness. Schmidt (1874): Roughness, want of tenderness. Collier (ed. 1853) alone emends to hardness (see textual n.) because he finds it more in accordance with the rest of the sentence (Idem, 1853, p. 98); however, in his 1858 ed. he reverts to Q1, calling the change unnecessary, and very possibly only a variation made by a performer. Wright (ed. 1877) compares Lr. 2.4.171–2 (1455–6). Deighton (ed. 1891): Sullen obstinacy against my will; harshness is more generally used of the rough treatment of a superior, quoting Tmp. 3.1.7–9 (1242–4). Bates (ed. 1895): Is it possible that Hermia inherited this quality? Evans (ed. 1974): Discordance [OED], i.e. disobedience.

47 Be it so]
Hunter (ed. 1870): If it be so that. The Clarkes (1879, p. 349): Supposing that, if it prove that. For so meaning provided that or if see Abbott §133; Franz §565; Blake 5.3.2.3 (c). See 1349, 1903. Rodway (ed. 1969) contrasts the weighty dignity of Theseus’s heavily stressed lines at 54–60 with Egeus’s line here, which has no strong stresses and thus seems a gabble, and makes him sound tetchy and garrulous.

47 Grace]
OED (16b): A courtesy-title now only given to a duke, a duchess, or an archbishop. Formerly used in addressing a king or queen.

48 marry with]
Johnson (ed. 1894): The added preposition is not common, comparing Ham. 3.4.29 (2410). But OED (v. 6a): To wed…. Const. with (formerly very common). See Abbott §194; Franz §535 Anm. 1.

49–52 I…lawe]
Warburton (ed. 1747): By a Law of Solon’s, Parents had the absolute power of life and death over their children. So it suited the poet’s purpose well enough to suppose the Athenians had it before.—Or perhaps he neither thought nor knew any thing of the matter. As a source for this law, Wright (ed. 1877) gives Sextus Empiricus; see Outlines of Pyrrhonism, tr. R. G. Bury, 1933 (3.211–12; 1:467): Solon gave the Athenians the law concerning things immune, by which he allowed each man to slay his own child; but with us the laws forbid the slaying of children. The Roman lawgivers also ordain that the children are subjects and slaves of their fathers, and that power over the children’s property belongs to the fathers and not the children, until the children have obtained their freedom like bought slaves; but this custom is rejected by others as being despotic.
Moberly (ed. 1881): In Timon of Athens the old Athenian [1.1.109+1 (137)] threatens his daughter only with disinheriting if she marries against his will [1.1.136–9 (169–72)]. The notion that a girl so acting would be condemned to death or monastic seclusion suits the more romantic turn of the present play, but answers nothing in Greek customs. Hemingway (1911, p. 80): Egeus is not unlike Capulet, and makes similar speeches, less brutal to be sure…, but no less tyrannical. Hemingway also quotes Rom. 3.5.191 (2238): And you be mine, ile giue you to my friend. Frye (1957, pp. 166, 183–4): We notice how often the action of a Shakespearean comedy begins with some absurd, cruel, or irrational law: the law of killing Syracusans in [Err.], the law of compulsory marriage in [MND],…and the like, which the action of the comedy then evades or breaks; it is an idiotic law, part of the stumbling and blinded follies of the world of experience. See also Idem (Natural Perspective, 1965, pp. 52–3). Brooks (ed. 1979) quotes Brooke’s Romeus (1562; ed. Bullough, 1957, 1:336, 1952–5): By lawe…if children did rebell, The parentes had the power, of…sodayn death. Anderson (1987, p. 48): That Egeus intends vengeance rather than justice is shown by the fact that he omits the third legal alternative, which is, however, immediately supplied by Theseus [74–5].
However, Berry (Northrop Frye, 1972, p. 35), objecting against Frye above: The death penalty, which Egeus invokes, is certainly on the statute book; but it is in practice commuted to a kind of imprisonment. The law itself is perfectly defensible, and is in fact defended by the courteous and rational Theseus. The notion that parents ought to approve the marriage of their children is of long standing and is even today not quite extinct. The action of [MND] (which suggests the fragility of subjective passion) makes it retrospectively plain that Egeus has a case. It is certain that a sizeable proportion of Shakespeare’s original audience would have thoroughly approved Egeus’s stand…and Shakespeare does not reject this point of view.

49 priuiledge]
Johnson (1755, 2), quoting this instance: Publick right. Deighton (ed. 1891): Custom. See also 599, 1102.

50 dispose of]
Johnson (1755, 8), quoting this instance: To put into the hands of another. Deighton (ed. 1891): Do as I please with her (OED, v. 8a).

51–3 Which…case]
Leoni (ed. 1818) reduces to the dramatic brevity of an opera libretto: Però Demetrio,—o morte.

53 Immediatly…case]
Steevens (ed. 1793): Shakspeare is grievously suspected of having been placed, while a boy, in an attorney’s office. The line before us has an undoubted smack of legal common-place. Poetry disclaims it. Rushton (1858, p. 38): Contrary to the form of the statute in that case made and provided is the allegation which concludes indictments for offences which are contrary to the statute. Campbell (1859, pp. 48–9), probably independently, also quotes part of the legal formula, which, he says, shows Sh.’s familiarity with legal phraseology…whether he ever were an attorney’s clerk or not.

53 Immediatly]
Thirlby (MS 1725–33): Especially. Schmidt (1874, 1): Directly, expressly. Brooks (ed. 1979): As provided, without respite or reprieve (cf. OED [adv. 2], with nothing intervening: here, between sentence and execution). See also 811.

53 prouided]
OED (ppl. a. 5): It being provided, stipulated, or arranged (that): used chiefly in legal and formal statements.

54 Be aduis’d]
Dyce (ed. 1864, 9:Glossary): Aware, cautious, circumspect (OED, ppl. a. 2). Hudson (ed. 1880): Old language for bethink yourself; that is, deliberate or consider (OED, Advise v. 5, 6). But Schmidt (1874, 2): Take my advice, be ruled by me, take heed (OED 8). For the imperative passive, see Blake 4.2.9 (d). Compare Capulet warning Juliet of a similar choice: Lay hand on heart, aduise, bethinke your selfe [Rom. Q1 H1v; cf. 3.5.190–5 (2237–42)].

55–9 To you…it]
Pope (ed. 1725, 6:4G2v) characterizes the passage as Child, the Duty it owes a Father. Johnson (ed. 1765): You owe to your father a being which he may at pleasure continue or destroy. Moberly (ed. 1881): Your father is to you a kind of creator, who has of course power over his work, as the potter or modeller has over the clay or wax which he forms.
Reactions to Theseus’s assertion include: Gentleman (ed. 1774): This mediatorial interference is friendly, but upon exceeding unphilosophical, and rigid, not to say unnatural principles. Griffith (1775, p. 16): When I said that the duty of a child was natural, I did not mean to invest the parent with an authority which was not so. Baildon (ed. Tit., 1904, pp. lxv–lxvi): I should say that Shakespeare’s own position was, that while a daughter had the right to refuse an unwelcome suitor, she was wrong to marry the favoured one in defiance of her father’s wishes and commands.…Theseus put the case for the father very strongly, though he obviously here, as in the Knight’s tale of Chaucer, has great sympathy with the lovers. See KnT 1760–1819. Aronson (1972, p. 205) notes: Throughout this absurd encounter between a tyrannical father and his daughter, no mother is ever mentioned. Montrose (1983, p. 70): Theseus represents paternity as a cultural act, an art: the father is a demiurge or homo faber, who composes, in-forms, imprints himself upon, what is merely inchoate matter.…[His] lecture on the shaping of a daughter is a fantasy of male parthenogenesis. Boehrer (2004, p. 103) sees in these lines a peculiarly artificial figuration of the organic creativity of parenthood. The last three comments view procreation from a 20th–21st-c. stance, but Egeus and Theseus would agree that A father is the well and head of begetting and gendring, and that In the male are vertues formall and shaping, and working, and in the female materiall suffering and passiue (Batman vppon Bartholome, 1584, 6.15.75v, 6.13.74v). See also nn. 57–8, 1222–46.

56 compos’d]
Schmidt (1874, 1a): To constitute, to form. OED (v. 1b): To fashion, frame (the human body, etc.).

56 yea]
OED (adv. 3, 4): Used to introduce a statement…stronger or more emphatic than that immediately preceding: = indeed; and more: = yes 4. Often practically coinciding with nay adv.1 5, which however properly expresses the contrast in degree between the statements, etc., whereas yea expresses their identity in substance.

57–8 forme…imprinted]
Halliwell (ed. 1856) quotes Jonson’s Magnetick Lady, fol. ed. [Workes, 1640, 2:26]: A soft, tender, delicate rib of mans flesh, That he may worke like waxe, and print upon [2.6.19–20 (6:539)]. See Schmidt (1875, 1) for wax as a substance used to make impressions of things, and cf. Tilley (W136): Soft Wax will take any impression. Fairchild (1937, p. 67): The idea of beauty becomes more distinct in connection with figures of wax than with any other in sculpture. Bate (1990, p. 75) compares these lines and related images of impression and imprint in Son. 112.1 and 77.3, with Golding’s Ovid 15.188–92; see . Calderwood (1992, p. 7): Theseus’ version of Hermia’s conception apparently comes…from the patristic view that man is to woman as spirit is to matter. Following Aristotle, Aquinas held the woman’s role in reproduction to be entirely passive [cf. Summa Theologica 1.92.1; tr. New York, 1947, 1:466]. Leggatt (1999, p. 49): The image Theseus uses is not organic but mechanical, the printing of a form on inert matter. He seems to forget that there was at some stage of this process a pregnant woman. Turner (1999, p. 96): The image of the wax seal is superficially a symbol of absolute authority,…But…the young people…escape into the night,…the preconscious world of dream. It is as if the wax resisted the impression of the seal and insisted on taking its own shape. James (2004, p. 66), possibly taking forme here in the same sense as in Rom. 3.3.126 (1943), a forme of waxe, relates the image to Ovid’s tale of Pygmalion, where it describes the sensation felt by the sculptor-turned-lover when the ivory statue becomes flesh as malleable as softened wax (Met. 10.283–6).

58 and…power]
Delius (ed. 1859): And within whose power it is. For ellipsis of it is see Abbott §403; Blake 6.3.2.1.

59 figure]
Schmidt (1874, 2): An image framed by any kind of art (OED 10).

59 disfigure]
Johnson (1755), quoting this instance: To change any thing to a worse form; to deform; to mangle (OED 1). Grinfield (MS 1834–63, p. 254): Efface. Wright (ed. 1877): Obliterate. Holland (ed. 1994): Theseus is outlining a conventional but still brutal view of the father’s power; disfigure…suggests maiming. See also nn. 871, 1815.

62 In himselfe]
Neil (ed. 1878): Like the Latin per se; ie intrinsically, essentially (OED, Per, prep. 9).

63 in this kinde]
Hunter (ed. 1870): In a matter of this nature (OED, Kind, n. 13a). Wright (ed. 1877); comparing AYL 2.1.27 (634): In this respect (OED 8). Verity (ed. 1893): In a matter of this kind (marriage); or in this respect, viz. that he wants your father’s approval (voice). Chambers (ed. 1897): I.e. not as a man, but as a husband. See 1885, 2015.

63 wanting…voice,]
Verity (ed. 1893): Strictly wanting has nothing to agree with. Thiselton (1903, p. 13), supporting Qq’s comma against F1’s unique period, cites Abbott §378 on the construction of participle without noun. See Franz §663; Blake 4.3.8 (b). See 1812 (imagining). Freeman (ed. 1998), noting that F1’s period means that all three lines 62–4 are stopped: Perhaps as if Theseus were taking great care to point out each step of the case against Hermia.

63 voice]
OED (n. 10c): Support or approval in a suit or petition, quoting only this and two other Shn. instances. Compare Capulet promising Paris his consent, and faire according voyce [Rom. 1.2.19 (266)] if he woos Juliet successfully.

64 The…worthier]
Gentleman (ed. 1774): The thought in this line, if should had been used instead of must, would have been thoroughly just and pleasing.

65–70 I would…thoughts]
Grinfield (MS 1834–63, p. 255): Specimen of Shakspeare’s refined Elegance in thought & diction; and also (what is more) his discernment of female feeling. Traversi (1968, 1:141): Her plea is one uttered in exclusive attention to her own impulses. Berry (Explorations, 1972, p. 91): Theseus and Hermia’s crucial exchange introduces the eye motif which is the true iterative image of the play. Leggatt (1974, p. 92): When Hermia and Egeus look at Lysander, they see two different people, for she sees with the eyes of love, he with the eyes of cantankerous old age, obsessed with its own authority. Cf. the exchange between Speed and Valentine on seeing with love or judgment (TGV 2.1.66–73 [459–66]).

65–6 I…looke]
Brooks (ed. 1979, p. xlvi) notes the combination of rhetorical figures antimetabole (repetition of words in inverted order) and anadiplosis (giving the word which ends one clause the first, or near the first, position in the next.) Of antimetabole Puttenham (1589; 1936, p. 208) says it is a figure which takes a couple of words to play with in a verse, and by making them to chaunge and shift one into others place they do very pretily exchange and shift the sence.

65 would]
Johnson (1755, 8), quoting this instance: I wish, or I pray (OED, Will, v.1 35). See Abbott §329; Franz §619; Blake 4.3.7.6 (d). This meaning is very common. See 97, 214, 394, 1299, 1674, 1928. Cf. n. 431.

66 your…looke]
Scott (ed. 1898): Her eyes must be merely instrumental, enabling her to appreciate the husband chosen for her by her father, not organs by which she is able to choose for herself. Mowat (1989, p. 342) finds this an unmoved and cool response in contrast with the reaction of Chaucer’s Theseus to the grieving queens (KnT 952–3; see ).

66 iudgement]
Cotgrave (1611, Iugement): Aduice, conceit, opinion; also, capacitie, discretion, vnderstanding, insight into matters. Cf. OED 7a, 8b. Schmidt (1874, 5): Discernment, good sense.

66 looke.]
The comma in Q1 may be accidental, or may have been intended to indicate an interruption. See 1670, 1678 (lawe), 1915 and nn. Cf. the semicolons at 145, 147, 149. See also .

68 I know…bould]
Warburton (ed. 1747): It was the Opinion of the Ancients, that when a person did or said any thing that exceeded his common faculties of performance, that he did it by the Assistance of some God. So here she insinuates, that it was Love that enabled her to plead his cause. Rudd (1979, p. 186): This sounds like an unconscious echo of Ovid’s Thisbe: audacem faciebat amor [4.96]—a phrase rendered by Golding as Love made her bold (see ). Wikander (1993, p. 28), questionably seeing an unconscious pun on maid and the bawdy sense of bold, considers this revealing in retrospect, as we recall Hermia’s subsequent portrayal as the more prudish of the two girls.

69 Nor…modesty]
Delius (ed. 1859): In how far my feminine dignity could be affected by it, and suffer from it (Ger.). Wright (ed. 1877): Nor how much it may affect my modesty. Furness (ed. 1895): Is it not rather, how much it may affect my reputation for modesty? Black (1965, p. 29): Affect the propriety of my conduct. See also n. 709.

69 concerne]
Cotgrave (1611, Concerner): To concerne, touch, import; appertaine, or belong vnto. Warburton (MS 1747–79): For affect (OED, v. 3). Moberly (ed. 1881): Be consistent with. Verity (ed. 1893): Suit, befit.

69 modesty]
Cotgrave (1611, Modestie): Humilitie, bashfulnesse, maidenlinesse. OED (3a): Womanly propriety of behaviour; scrupulous chastity of thought, speech, and conduct (in men or women). Lobban (ed. 1913): Womanly good name. See 593 and n. 709.

70 presence]
Minsheu (1617): The Chamber of Presence in a Princes Court. Johnson (1755, 3), quoting this instance: State of being in the view of a superior. Schmidt (1875, 2): Abstr. pro concr., = persons of rank assembled in a place, noble company. Deighton (ed. 1891): In the presence of one so exalted as my sovereign. Holland (ed. 1994) Either a place prepared for a ceremonial presence (OED 2c), suggesting the location of the scene, or the group of people assembled with Theseus (OED 3). Cf. 752, 1103, 2168.

70 plead…thoughts]
Minsheu (1617, Pleade): To argue in the Law. In the transitive more usually governing cause, case or matter (OED, Plead, v. 5). Thirlby (MS 1747–53) suggests but rejects the emendation cause. Wright (ed. 1877): To utter my thoughts by way of plea or argument. Brooks (ed. 1979): Submit them as a plea; no exact equivalent in OED; earlier than its first example of urge as a plea. See OED, v. 7c and 7b.

71–2 knowe The worst]
Proverbial. Tilley (W915): To know the Worst is good.

71 I may knowe]
Abbott §369: The Subjunctive after verbs of command and entreaty is especially common. See Franz §647; Blake 4.3.2.1 (c). See 389, 648, 1118, 1404.

72 may befall]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Can possibly befall; citing Abbott §307: May originally meant to be able. See the etymological note in OED, May v.1. See Franz §604; Blake 4.3.7.9 (a). See 171 (may I marry), 538 (might), 1794 (may beleeue). Kittredge (ed. 1939): Can (according to law).

74–87 Either…blessednesse]
Heraud (1865, p. 172): Glorious simplicity of the olden time, when such things could be said without offence to modesty! Van Laan (1978, p. 54): Theseus is to use the categorical, either-or rhetoric of this speech twice more before the scene ends [96–9, 126–30]. Fully symptomatic, this rhetoric effectively epitomizes the inflexibility of both the laws of the state and their executors. Holland (ed. 1994): Theseus reveals an alternative to death not mentioned by Egeus. Does Egeus know of this and has concealed it? Or is it news to him? Theseus’ palliation of the choice alters the mood of the scene.
Commentary elicited by Theseus’s speech on the debate between marriage and virginity, on Sh.’s attitude to Catholicism and the religious life, and on possible allusion to Queen Elizabeth is usually prompted by specific lines, and so recorded below.
However, some comment on the passage generally. Wilkes (1877 [1876], p. 111) refers to it as a reverent allusion to the Roman Catholic religion, remarking that a threatened imprisonment of Hermia for life in a state prison would have been fully adequate to all the necessities of the scene, instead of bringing in a nunnery…but Shakespeare evidently wanted to patronize the Catholic religion. Wilson (ed. 1924): These are remarkable lines; evidently written with Queen Elizabeth in mind. But phrases like barren sister and withering on the virgin thorn would surely have sounded harsh in her ears. But Rowse (1963, p. 205): No-one has perceived that this is a reference to the young Earl [Southampton] who would not marry. Everyone has seen, absurdly, a reference to Queen Elizabeth, where a sense of tact should have told them that such words could not possibly have been uttered in her presence. Clark (1931, p. 326) finds that the passage fairly represents Shakespeare’s thoughts on nuns and convents, in being eloquent of his wonder at their self-denial and his admiration of their asceticism. Duffy (1972, p. 147) finds the speech very reminiscent of the end of the masque of Diana, not given but published, from the Kenilworth revels, exhorting Elizabeth to marry. Kirsch (1981, p. 85) finds the kind of response intended to be evoked here and by Isabella’s habit in MM equivocal at best.…Theseus’ thought is a modulation of the parable of the talents [Matt. 25:14–30]. Levi (1988, p. 139): The girl is threatened with death or a nunnery, the nun’s life being so magically described that Shakespeare only just manages to make wedded happiness more attractive. Interpreting Theseus’s alternative as punitive rather than merciful, Treadwell (1991, p. 25) suggests: What this law really attacks is Hermia’s fertility.…Theseus’s language is full of images of infertility; and Boehrer (2004, p. 102) claims: The choice here—reconfiguring Egeus’s original ultimatum of marriage or death [49–53]—takes shape primarily as a contrast between cross-gendered fecundity and homosocial childlessness, which serves as the genealogical and sexual equivalent of execution.

74 dy the death]
Johnson’s dictum (ed. 1765, 1:311) on MM 2.4.165 (1179), citing also MND, This seems to be a solemn phrase for death inflicted by law, is quoted by OED, Die v.1 2c. Steevens (ed. 1773) calls it a scriptural expression. Tyrwhitt (in Malone, ed. 1790, 2:58) objects: The phrase is a good phrase, as Shallow says, but I do not conceive it to be either of legal or scriptural origin. Chaucer uses it frequently. [Quotes CT, GP 605; also KnT 1220, see ]. It seems to have been originally a mistaken translation of the French La Mort. On the, expressing notoriety, Deighton (ed. 1891) cites Abbott §92; see also Franz §262 and Spevack (ed. Ant., 1990, p. 276, n. 2856); also Blake 3.3.4.2.2 (d). On scriptural instances, Eaton ([1860], p. 209) cites Mark 7:10 and Ecclus. 14:17. Rolfe (ed. 1877) and Wright (ed. 1877) add Matt. 15:4. Noble (1935, p. 160) notes the text in Matthew deals with conduct to parents as does Theseus here but since the phrase occurs also in the Old Testament too much should not be made of the point. Shaheen (1993, pp. 93–4): The expression…[is] used to translate the Hebrew phrase mōt tamūt, dying thou shalt die; he provides many references to its use in the Old and New Testaments, and lists the forms it is given in Tudor translations of the Bible.

74 abiure]
Johnson (1755, 1), quoting this instance: To cast off upon oath, to swear not to do something.

76–7 question…blood]
Johnson (in Steevens, ed. 1773): Bring your youth to the question. Consider your youth. Wood (ed. 1806): Reflect on the desires attendant on youth. Tyrrell (ed. 1850): Question your youth, examine your nature whether you can bear this enforced sequestration from liberty, and the exercise of natural affection. Phillips (ed. 1969): Theseus asks Hermia to take into consideration her own lively and eager temperament.

76 desires]
Johnson (1755): Wish; eagerness to obtain and enjoy (OED 1). Possibly also, in conjunction with blood in the next line, with overtones of OED (2): Physical or sensual appetite. Hunter (ed. 1870): Natural affections. See also n. 7.

77 blood]
Johnson (1755, 11): Temper of mind; state of the passions. OED (5): The supposed seat of emotion, passion; (6): The supposed seat of animal or sensual passion; hence, the fleshly nature of man. The definition of Sanguis in Cooper (1565), generatiō. somtime life: strength (OED 4 Obs.) suggests how the word links with the imagery of fecundity and sterility in Theseus’s speech. Cf. also Bartholomaeus Anglicus: Blood is the seate of the soule,…and is the perfecter of youth,…and keepeth and saueth the heart and spirits, and maketh them glad, and waketh loue (Batman vppon Bartholome, 1582, F6vb). See 83, and cf. 145, 1071, 1098, 1120.

78–9 Whether…can]
Verity (ed. 1893): Dependent on the verbs in [77].

78 Whether]
Abbott §466: Frequently written or pronounced whe’r or where. See also Franz §726. Settle (ed. 1692) avoids the contraction by emending the text, perhaps influenced by the jingle of you yeelde, which Pope (ed. 1725) also changes though without altering the number of syllables (see textual n.). See 970, 1104.

79 liuery]
Cotgrave (1611, Livrée): Ones cloth, colours, or deuice in colours worn by his seruants, or others (OED 2a); (La Livrée des Chanoines): Their liuerie, or corrodie; their stipend, exhibition, dailie allowance in victualls, or money (OED 1a). The use of the word in English to refer to the dress or provision of a member of a religious order is unusual. The Clarkes (ed. 1864): Here means more than garb, or professional attire; it has the force of state, condition, life to be led. Andrews (ed. 1989): Theseus depicts the nun’s Liv’ry (habit in two senses) as if it, too, meant a kind of death: barren, faint, cold, fruitless, and solitary. See also 488.

79 Nunne]
Gentleman (ed. 1774) doubts whether the title of Nun could be properly applied to one of Diana’s votaries. Douce (1807, 1.180) finds the term as whimsical an anachronism as any in Shakespeare, but Tyrrell (ed. 1850) believes the anachronism rectified by the reference to Diana’s altar at 98. Rolfe (ed. 1877) quotes Ven. 752 as similarly anachronistic; see also n. 82. Wright (ed. 1877) is the first to point to a parallel use by North in Plutarch’s Life of Theseus; see . See OED, n.1 1b, for other examples of the word applied to a votaress of a pagan deity.

80–2 For…Moone]
Fripp (1930, p. 115) quotes these lines as exemplifying the coldness of Ovid’s Diana: Austere, morbid in her chastity, visiting death on young Actaeon because he happened to catch sight of her in her bath (see ). Cf. Diana’s reaction to Callisto’s pregnancy (Met. 2.463–4, Golding 2.576–7; see ).

80 For aye]
OED (ay, aye, adv. 3a): For ever, to all eternity. See 99, 1428.

80 in shady]
For omission of the after prepositions in adverbial phrases see Abbott §90; Franz §267; Blake 3.3.4.2.2 (h).

80 shady]
This word, first recorded by OED in any sense in 1579, probably means here Shaded, protected by shade (OED 2) rather than dark as in Schmidt (1875) or Dark, obscure as in Page (ed. 1897), comparing Luc. 881. Cf. Johnson (1755): 1. Full of shade; mildly gloomy; 2. Secure from the glare of light; or sultriness of heat.

80 cloyster]
The preceding line probably justifies Schmidt (1874) and OED (2) in understanding monastery or convent here rather than simply enclosed space (OED, 1), but cf. TGV 1.3.2 (303). Seymour (1805, 1:41): The poet was very good to make a christian of Theseus. Buccola (2003, p. 163) finds pointed Catholic reference in the use of this word and sister (81).

80 mew’d]
Johnson (1755, v. 1), quoting this instance: To shut up; to confine; to imprison; to inclose. Dyce (ed. 1864, 9:Glossary) quotes Randle Holme’s The Academy of Armory [(1688, 2.9.24)]: Mew, is the place (whether it be abroad, or in the house) in which the Hawk is put, during the time she casts, or doth change her Feathers. Compare Gremio protesting Baptista’s plan to sequester Bianca, Why will you mew her vp [Shr. 1.1.87 (391)]. In William Warner’s Albions England, Curan is thwarted in love because his Saint was kept in Mewe (1586, p. 83).

81 barraine]
Johnson (1755, Barren, adj. 1): Without the quality of producing its kind, not prolifick (OED, Barren, adj. 1, quoting this instance). See also n. 1035. Brooks (ed. 1979) compares the sterilis iuventus of Seneca, Hippolytus 479.

81 sister]
Florio (1598, Sorella): A sister. Also a nunne. See also n. 80 (cloyster).

82 Chaunting…Moone]
Delius (ed. 1859): Hermia is seen here as a nun who addresses her cold devotion to the childless moon goddess who represents virginal chastity (Ger.). Deighton (ed. 1891): With languid monotony offering up hymns of praise to that chaste-cold divinity, the moon. The faint hymns are in contrast with the fervid devotion offered to divinities from whom some warm return of favour might be expected; the moon (personified as Diana, the goddess of chastity) making no return of love to her devotees. For fruitless in this sense, and for an illustration of the passage generally, cp. [Ven. 751–5], Therefore despite of fruitless chastity, Love-lacking vestals, and self-loving nuns, That on the earth would breed a scarcity And barren dearth of daughters and of sons, Be prodigal.

82 faint]
Hunter (ed. 1870): Without fervour. Schmidt (1874, 4): Cold, without zeal. Rolfe (ed. 1877): Without feeling or fervour. Furness (ed. 1895), objecting to Rolfe: But is such an imputation of insincerity, almost of hypocrisy, in keeping with the dignified seriousness of the Duke’s adjuration? May it not be that midnight hymns chanted by nuns within a convent’s walls must always sound faint to the ears of men outside? Sprague (ed. 1896): May not the faintness of the sound be the effect of feebleness due to penance, or self-mortification, fasting, and vigils? Scott (ed. 1898): Perhaps the poet means to suggest the difficulty of keeping up so dull a life, the tendency to faint or fail in the performance of this worship. Porter & Clarke (ed. 1903): Theseus is really trying to make Hermia realize how dull and undesirable such a life would be, and presents it from his point of view. The hymns would necessarily seem faint to him and therefore half-hearted on her part. Watt (ed. 1906): The hymns chanted by a fasting nun, without being insincere, might well be fainter in sound than the passionate songs of natural youth.

82 colde]
Brooks (ed. 1979): Sexless, devoid of sensual heat (earlier than OED’s [adj. 7c] first instance, 1597, of this figurative sense). See also OED (6a): In the physiology of the Middle Ages, and down to 17th c. cold and hot (in association with dry and moist) applied to the complexion of things,…Thus,…phlegm, Winter, Venus, and the Moon were moist and cold. See also 533, 1476.

82 fruitlesse Moone]
Chambers (ed. 1897): Diana, in one of her aspects, was the moon-goddess, which explains the cold fruitless moon. Kittredge (ed. 1939): Diana, who has no offspring, being a maiden goddess and the patroness of maidens. Cook (1976, p. 19): A faint irony is already present: the Diana of Theseus’ praise is served by a chastity quite alien to the moon of this play, which will not be cold and fruitless. See also n. 1412.

83–4 Thrise…pilgrimage]
Verity (ed. 1893): Probably a compliment to Queen Elizabeth, the Maiden Queen; cf. the more marked reference in [532–41].

83–4 so…To]
For omission of as in relatival constructions, see Abbott §281; Franz §650, Anm. 1. See also 1669–70.

83 Thrise blessed]
OED (Thrice 3b): Used vaguely or hyperbolically: Very, highly, greatly, extremely (cf. L. ter). Schmidt (1875) gives many examples of the use of thrice by Sh. before adjectives and participles. Theobald (1909, pp. 48–9) considers its use as an intensative…a peculiarity of Shakespeare’s style. Jankowski (2000, p. 137), unconvincingly, claims the phrase clearly echoes Jerome’s belief that consecrated virgins reap a hundredfold reward in heaven, roughly three times that of the thirtyfold reward granted to the married; cf. Jerome, Adversus Jovinianum 2.22 and Letter 22, 15.

83 that…bloode]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Who attain such a mastery over their natural inclinations. For blood, see n. 77.

83 master]
Johnson (1755, v. 2), quoting this instance: To conquer; to overpower; to subdue.

83 there]
Variant form of their; OED, poss. pron. A. Illustration of Forms. (β)

84 vndergoe]
Dyce (ed. 1864, 9:Glossary): To endure with firmness (OED 5). Schmidt (1875, 3): To take upon one’s self, to undertake, to perform (cf. OED 8).

84 maiden pilgrimage]
Warburton (MS 1747–79): Pilgrimage for Penance: the species for the genus; but here with as little propriety in the thought, as of authority in the use: For this Pilgrimage was perpetual confinement in a Cloister. Wright (ed. 1877): A course of life passed in virginity. This sense of pilgrimage is in accordance with the usage of scripture. Compare Genesis xlvii.9: The days of the years of my pilgrimage are an hundred and thirty years. And see [AYL 3.2.130 (1328)] (OED 1c). Deighton (ed. 1891): As to submit themselves to a pilgrimage through life uncheered by the joys of love, adding as examples R2 2.1.154 (801) and Heb. 11:13. Noble (1935, p. 160) also refers to Ps. 119:54. Disparaging Shn. usages of pilgrimage are noted by Johnson (1755, 2): Shakespeare used it for time irksomely spent, improperly; and by Schmidt (1875, 2): Any long and weary journey.

85–7 But…blessednesse]
Some commentary arises in reaction to proposed emendation (see n. 85 below). Hunter (1845, 1.288): The virgin is thrice blessed, as respects the heaven for which she prepares herself; but, looking only to the present world, the other is the happier lot. White (1854, pp. 208–9): Theseus has just described a religious state which makes those who adopt it thrice blessed. He then speaks of another state, which confers another kind of happiness. Each ensures happiness, but happiness of a different nature. When he discriminates between the two, he speaks, not of their degree of happiness, but of their kind. The latter is a happiness earthlier, that is, more consistent with the natural instincts of the human heart, than the former. Delius (ed. 1859), although noting that Capell’s emendation may have been what Sh. wanted to say, comments that the rose distilled into rosewater kept its fragrance and is therefore happier on earth than the one that withers on the stalk and lives and dies in untouched holiness; as is often the case with Sh., the image and the object are condensed into a metaphor (Ger.). Furness (ed. 1895): Theseus’s meaning is clear, however much we may disagree with the sentiment, that in an earthly sense the married woman is happier than the spinster.
More general commentary includes Gentleman (ed. 1774): This declaration of Theseus preferring those who fulfil the laws of nature to those who live in unprofitable abstraction, is truly sensible, and the simile perfectly agreeable. Heraud (1865, pp. 172–3): He makes a slight concession to the elder superstition, but immediately recovers it in favour of natural impulse:…Still the prominent thought! recurring—still recurring—never to be absent from the mind of the anti-celibate poet. Ebsworth (1878, pp. 284–5), who asserts that the passage shows Theseus’s calm wisdom, devoid of sectarian bigotry, and also the recognition, befitting Shakespeare’s noble nature, of the high, saintly compensations found in such a removed life. Harbage (1952, p. 233): For sexual abstinence as an ideal in itself, for votresses of Diana or brides of the Church, we find no advocacy in Shakespeare. Chastity, in this sense, is viewed only as respectable eccentricity.…The blessedness in these lines appears like a fate worse than death, and Hermia is spared for earthlier happiness. Harbage (1963, p. 107): The rose distilled…is the woman fulfilled in the birth of a child. Cunningly, the rose undistilled is associated with the virgin thorn. Thakur (1966, p. 30), over-ingeniously: The Rose distilled or the quintessential Rose may be the one Rose in which the white and the red are united, and thus refer to Queen Elizabeth. Thorne, in speaking on the stage, can well be spoken throne with a special intention. Holland (ed. 1994): Marriage, for Theseus, distils and refines the essence of the maiden’s sweetness;…His speech awkwardly balances praise of the nun’s state and a male disapproval of female virginity.
Guillory (1983, p. 88) applies the metaphor to style, seeing in the lines a locus for the debate between chastity and plenitude as alternative poetic principles.
Various parallels have been adduced. In illustration of the frequent use of the rose as image of virginity resulting in beauty unenjoyed, Dodd (1752, 1:74) quotes Milton’s Comus, 737–44. On beauty preserved for use like distilled flowers, Malone (1780, 1:114) comments This is a thought in which Shakespeare seems to have much delighted, and quotes Son. 5.9–14, 6.1–4, and 54.10–14. Massey (1866, pp. 36–9, in Rollins, ed. Son. 1944, 2:119–20) finds the thought derived from Sidney’s Arcadia (ed. Evans, 1977, p. 461). Warton (1785, p. 222), annotating Milton’s Comus (743–4), quotes MND 85–7 with Malone’s comment, and adds a parallel from Lyly’s Midas (1592), 2.1.110–12 (3:126): You be all yong, and faire, endeuor all to be wise & vertuous, that when, like roses, you shal fall from the stalke, you may be gathered & put to the still. Steevens (ed. 1793) repeats the quotation from Lyly and adds: This image however, must have been generally obvious, as in Shakspeare’s time the distillation of rose water was a common process in all families. Brooks (ed. 1979) quotes the same image used by Lyly in Euphues (1:234) and in Sapho and Phao 2.1.109–10 (2:383). Anders (1904, p. 288): In Florio’s Second Frutes, 1591, p. 183 [i.e. 187], sig. Aa2 [Bb2], we have a passage which throws light on this: beauty…is also as you say, a flower sweet, on the stalk, but sweeter in the still. In Florio’s parallel Italian text, the metaphor is applied to La donna, not to la bellezza. Halliwell (ed. 1856) is the first to quote Erasmus’s colloquy Proci et Puellae, quoted in part also by Walker (1860, 3:46): Ego rosam existimo feliciorem, quae marcescit in hominis manu, delectans interim et oculos et nares, quam quae senescit in frutice (Opera omnia, ordo 1, tom. 3 [1972], p. 285). Ebsworth (1878, p. 383) quotes N. Bailey’s translation (1725), and mentions but has not seen Nicholas Leigh’s 1568 version A Modest Meane to Mariage, which translates the passage: Certes in mine opinion the rose is the happiest, and commeth to the better ende, which withereth and dieth in the hande of man, delighting in the meane while both the eies and the nosethrils, than thother which withereth on the bush; and continues for there must it needes wither also at length (B7r-v). Adams (1878, p. 404) quotes Thomas Heywood’s translation from Pleasant Dialogues and Drammas (1637). Thiselton (1903, p. 13) extends the quotation to include the passage about women who (in Leigh’s translation) before they were maried, were pale colored, faint, and as it were pined away, who by the friendly felowship of an husband, haue waxed so faire, and welfauoured, that you would think they neuer came to the flower of their beautie till then. The passage in Gager’s Meleager (performed 1581/2, printed 1592/3) which Boas (1914, p. 169) claims anticipates famous lines in MND is drawn from Erasmus, as Reyher (1947, p. xii) notes. Kittredge (ed. 1939) points out that Erasmus is closely translated by Greene, Mamillia, 1583 (2:43): Whether doest thou think the ruddy Rose, which withereth in the hand of a man, delighting both sight and smelling, more happie than that which fadeth on the stalke without profit?

85 earthlyer happy]
Seymour (1805, 1:41): This anomalous comparison of an adverb is not singular. See [Tmp. 2.1.21 (696)] You have taken it wiselier than I meant. For Sh.’s free use of adjectives as adverbs, see Abbott §1. Schmidt (1874, 1) defines earthly as pertaining to the earth, with examples often paired with heavenly, and cites this phrase as using The comparative adverbially. OED quotes 85–87 under Blessedness and Distil v., but under Earthly gives no instance of the comparative of the adverb, and only one instance in 1858 of the comparative of the adjective (1b). For emendations and conjectures see textual n. and . Earlier happy, adopted by Rowe (ed. 1714) and considered by Pope (ed. 1725) and (Johnson (ed. 1765), is characterized by Pye (1807, p. 46) as an emendation made at the expence only of two letters and common sense. The support of Knight (ed. 1839) for earthly happier is opposed by Hunter (1845, 1:288): The objections to this are, (1) that it is against authority; (2) that nothing is gained by it; (3) that if there is any difference in the meaning it is a deterioration, not an improvement; and (4) that it spoils the melody.

86 withering]
Webb (1991, pp. 136–7), comparing AWW 1.1.160–3 (164–8) where virginity is likened to a withered pear: Becoming parched, shrivelled up: in the human parallel of senescence, because of the absence of vital spirits, of rich blood…which are generated by sexual activity. See also n. 9.

86 virgin thorne]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Virgin belongs to rose rather than thorn:…for thorn, = tree or shrub armed with thorns, cp. [1H4 1.3.175–6 (498–9)], To put down Richard, that sweet lovely rose, And plant this thorn, this canker, Bolingbroke. Scott (ed. 1898): The sentence and its metaphors are…somewhat mixed. The virgin thorn belongs to the rose; thorny virginity to the maiden. The first phrase is in reality meaningless and is useful only as suggesting the second, i.e., as suggesting the hardships of celibacy. The thorn belongs, of course, not to a shrub or tree, but to the rose bush. For virgin used attributively as an adjective, meaning Not yet touched, handled, or employed for any purpose etc., see OED 16b, quoting this instance first.

87 Growes]
Sitwell (1948, p. 189) comments on the beauty given by the echo (almost at opposite ends of lines divided from each other) of a rhyme with rose.
