87 single blessednesse]
Johnson (1755, Blessedness, 2), quoting this instance: Sanctity. OED (b), quoting this instance first: Used by Shakes. to express divine blessing accorded to a life of celibacy; hence (more or less jocularly), the unmarried state. Noble (1935, p. 160): For the blessed lot of celibacy see 1 Cor. vii. 1–26. Brooks (ed. 1979): Celibacy, with the divine blessing it carries; but single had also the sense of feeble, attenuated [unattested in OED].

88–91 So…souerainty]
Griffith (1775, p. 16), continuing the sentence quoted at 55–9: I cannot blame Hermia, therefore, upon the severe laws of Athens being declared to her, for the chaste and spirited resolution she frames to herself on that occasion. The Clarkes (ed. 1864): In this, and Hermia’s preceding speech, there is just that composure of utterance and calm self-possession in the announcement of a strong inward conviction, which comes to women in the midst of perturbation from external causes, when they are both noble-minded and gentle-natured. The fine way in which the poet manifests this is well worth the study of those who sneer at strong-mindedness; ignorantly confounding it with unfeminine boldness. It is noteworthy also—as a study of Shakespeare’s perfect mode of delineating a womanly character—the way in which he makes Hermia begin by a few diffidently uttered words; then warm into two energetically assertive speeches; and lastly subside into resolutely maintained silence, having said her say and meaning to abide by it. Traversi (1968, 1:141): Hermia clings to her decision with an obstinacy equal to that of her father. Leggatt (1999, pp. 49–50): The echoing of my lord and his lordship shows that in getting as much control over her own life as the law allows she is defying Demetrius and Theseus together, as well as Egeus. What is at stake for her in her desire for Lysander is not just her love for this one man but her freedom to make her own choice.

88 So…die]
Traub (1998, p. 138): Hermia’s appropriation of Theseus’s rhetoric…resourcefully asserts the possibility of growth and life within a religious sisterhood.

89–91 Ere…souerainty]
Porter & Clarke (ed. 1903) suggest an implied allusion, then readily understood, to the letters patent issued by Queen Elizabeth in 1584…to Sir Walter Raleigh, granting him sovereignty in her name over the virgin lands of the New World, which he reached in the same year, and named in honour of the Virgin Queen [see Raleigh Trevelyan, Sir Walter Raleigh, 2002, pp. 67, 70–1, 77–8].

89 virgin Patent]
Thirlby (MS 1733–47): Privilege[:] virgin patent is apt to convey an odd image. Johnson (1755), quoting this instance: A writ confirming some exclusive right or privilege. Grinfield (MS 1834–63, p. 255): Treasure. Wright (ed. 1877): My privilege of virginity and the liberty that belongs to it.…The word is derived from the literæ patentes, or letters patent, which conveyed the privilege. Verity (ed. 1893) cites Schmidt (1875, pp. 1415–17) on this type of compound noun and adds: Thus here virgin defines the character of the patent: it is a patent consisting in virginity. Chambers (ed. 1897): Almost any relation between two substantive-ideas can be expressed in Elizabethan English by making one of them an adjective of the other. Brooks (ed. 1979): Entitlement to virginity, citing OED, Patent, n. 5, where this is the first instance. Foakes (ed. 1984): Right to my virginity. Suhamy (1989, p. 186) objects that Brooks’s gloss is unclear, capable of being interpreted as referring to the husband’s rights and to the fetishism of defloration, and that this impression is strengthened by Foakes’s displacement of the possessive (Fr.).

90 his]
Verity (ed. 1893): Emphatic; it contains the antecedent of whose. See Abbott §218; Franz §321. Cf. 1369.

90 Lordshippe]
Minsheu (1617, Lordship): Or Signiorie…Vide Iurisdiction. Theobald (MS. 1723–33): I.e. Controul, Subjection. Knight (ed. 1839): Authority. The word dominion in our present translation of the Bible (Romans, [6:9]) is lordship in Wickliffe’s translation. Citing MND and AWW 5.3.156 (2874), Schmidt (1874, 1): State of husband, conjugal right and duty…(or in both passages = sway, sovereignty?). Wright (ed. 1877): Power, authority; especially used of the authority of a husband. The Clarkes (1879, p. 566): Marital dominion. Anon (ed. 1887): Not used in the modern sneering sense, but simply to indicate the authority of a husband over a wife.

90 whose vnwished]
The metre requires that vnwished be trisyllabic. Early editors adopt F2’s insertion of to, and read unwish’d. But Malone (ed. 1790, 10:577): The change was certainly made by the editor of the second folio from his ignorance of Shakspeare’s phraseology. The addition also awkwardly echoes Vnto, and weakens the emphasis on vnwished. Delius (ed. 1859): Sh. constructs the relative clause as if he expected to (that would precede whose) to be repeated at the end; he left it out as if it was implied by whose, an oversight found frequently in his work (Ger.). On the omission of the preposition, see Abbott §201; Franz §542. Hunter (ed. 1870): The omission of the preposition to after a verb of giving is quite common; and in the expression to give whose yoke sovereignty, we may call yoke the indirect object of the verb.

90 vnwished]
Johnson (1755), quoting this instance: Not sought; not desired. OED (ppl. a. 1b), quoting this instance: Unwanted, unwelcome, unpleasing.

90 yoake]
Schmidt (1875, 1) lists this instance under Emblem of servitude and slavery, a definition derived from Johnson (1755, 2); but Johnson’s third definition, A chain; a link; a bond, under which he gives the phrase yoke of marriage might suggest less violence in Hermia’s tone. For earlier more positive connotations of the figurative use of yoke in relation to marriage (albeit with ironic overtones) see Chaucer, ClT 113–15: That blisful yok Of soveraynetee, noght of servyse, Which that men clepe spousaille or wedlok. OED (n.1 8b): In reference to marriage, combining the ideas of union or co-operation and subjection or restraint. Rosenblum (1981, p. 359) notes that two illustrations of Matrimonio in Ripa’s Iconologia incorporate a yoke (cf. ed. intro. E. Mandowsky, Hildesheim, 1970, pp. 305–7). The image is common in 16th-c. poetry, including references to Love’s or Cupid’s yoke. See also n. 468.

91 My soule]
OED (Soul 3a): The seat of the emotions, feelings, or sentiments; the emotional part of man’s nature. See also 117, 183, 560, 1256. Deighton (ed. 1891): Emphatic; I from the bottom of my soul.

91 souerainty]
Minsheu (1617, Soueraintie): Vi. Supremacie, & Principalship.

92 Take…pawse]
Delius (ed. 1859): Take time to think, to reconsider (postpone) your decision (Ger.). Deighton (ed. 1891): Take time to reflect on the matter. OED, Take, v. 28, quoting this instance; Pause, v.1 1a.

92 by]
On by indicating time (= at), see Blake 5.4.2 By (e).

92 newe moone]
See n. 13.

93–4 The…fellowshippe]
Deighton (ed. 1891): The day on which Hippolyta and I are to ratify by marriage a bond of everlasting partnership in love, comparing TN 5.1.160–1 (2322–3). Furness (ed. 1895) and Cuningham (ed. 1905) comment on the legal phraseology. Barber (1959, p. 133), citing 40 and 55–9, draws attention to a developing imagery of wax and to the metaphor of sealing as procreation.…The consummation in prospect with marriage is envisaged as a melting into a new form and a new meaning. Porter & Clarke (ed. 1903): A high ideal of marriage, not only ahead of Greek society, but ahead of Shakespeare’s time. Farrow (ed. 1972): Fellowship implies that they will be friends rather than master and slave (though Theseus by conquering Hippolyta has already won back man’s natural place as woman’s superior). Andrews (ed. 1993): Theseus portrays marriage as a contract between equals who devoutly wish to be yoked together. Holland (ed. 1994): The image of sealing a bond is strongly legalistic, suggesting a business agreement as much as a marriage. See also n. 1169 (seale).

94 fellowshippe]
OED (1a): Partnership;…Also, in political sense, alliance; (3b): Intimate personal converse; (3c): Sexual intercourse. OED (Fellow, n. 4a): Consort, spouse, husband or wife. Possibly echoing the marriage service; see n. 1192.

95 Vpon…dye]
Hunter (ed. 1870): The order is, Prepare either to die upon thay day. [Cf. MV 1.1.121 (130)], That you to-day promised to tell me of; where to-day is adverbial to tell.

95 Vpon that day]
Johnson (ed. 1894): These words sum up the two preceding lines, which contain an unfinished sentence.

96 For…will]
Deighton (ed. 1891): As a punishment for disobedience.

97 Or…would]
Delius (ed. 1859): He would refers to your father (Ger.).

97 would]
See n. 65.

98 Dianaes altar]
Verity (ed. 1893): The Latin goddess identified with the Greek goddess Artemis; a very critical person might object that the Greek Theseus should not be made to use the Latin name. Watt (ed. 1906): Until comparatively lately the Greek gods were always known by their Latin names. Artemis would have been an unknown word to the audience at the Globe. Porter & Clarke (ed. 1903) claim that in no religious cult was Diana or Artemis worshipped as a virgin by virgins, with the possible exception of ancient rules in the Arcadian Orchemenos, and suggest that Sh.’s mingling of the idea of Diana as the virgin goddess attended by virgin nymphs with the austerity of medieval monasticism was partly prompted by Emelye’s prayer to Diana in Chaucer, KnT 2329–30; see . But cf. Ovid, Met. 2.409–65 or 3.163–181 (Golding 2.504–77, 3.192–213; see , ). Knowles (privately) refers also to the virgin nymphs attendant on Diana in Lyly’s Gallathea. Hassel (2005, p. 13): The phrase…refers through eponym to the deity’s predominant characteristic.…[S]wearing on Diana’s altar means promising chastity, flying from it means committing to a sexual life.

98 protest]
Cawdrey (1604): To affirme, and declare openly. Schmidt (1875, 2): To promise solemnly, to vow (OED, v. 4, quoting this instance).

99 For aye]
See n. 80.

99 austeritie]
Florio (1598, austerità): Seueritie, crueltie, strictness. Bullokar (1616): Sharpnesse, hard vsage. OED (3a), quoting this instance first: Severe self-discipline or self-restraint; moral strictness, rigorous abstinence, asceticism.

99 austeritie…life]
Deighton (ed. 1891): A hendiadys for the austerity of a life of singleness, such as was led by those who devoted themselves to religious seclusion.

100 sweete]
Knowles (ed. AYL, 1977, p. 17 n. 63): A common epithet in vocative phrases, equivalent to the current dear. Rylands (1928, p. 104): This is Shakespeare’s favourite adjective, his darling word, applied indiscriminately (or so it seems to us) to all subjects.…Besides the elementary and sensuous use, the word means lovely, dear, bitter sweet; citing sweet hay (1545) among the more curious uses, he claims Shakespeare is not commonplace and conventional in his affection for the word. See OED, adj. 8. See 233, 410, 697, 712, 738, 1274, 1292, 1537, 1542, 1590, 1766, 1778, 1884, 1896, 1976, 1978, 2074, 2150, addressing (besides Hermia) Helena, Puck, Demetrius, Lysander, Bottom, Titania, Hippolyta, Wall, Moon, and the courtiers. See also n. 1545.

101 crazed]
Johnson (1755, To Craze, 1), quoting this instance: To break; to crush; to weaken. Rann (ed. 1787): Feeble, infirm. Schmidt (1874, 1): Invalid. OED (ppl. a. 3), quoting this instance first: Impaired, damaged, unsound. Holland (ed. 1994): Flawed claim, but crazèd also suggests mad. In support of reading razed (see conj., ), Wilson (1873, pp. 242–3) notes: The decision of Theseus has just been given, by which all claim or title of Lysander to Hermia’s hand is erased. The word razed repeatedly occurs in this sense in the dramas.

101 title]
Johnson (1755, 5): A claim of right. Evans (ed. 1974): Claim to possession. Cf. OED 6, 7a, c.

101 certaine]
Minsheu (1617): Sure, Manifest. OED (a. 3): Established as a truth to be absolutely received;…indubitable. See also 529, 534, 1313, 1929.

101 right]
Cotgrave (1611, Droict): A right or title vnto,…; a mans due; that which either he hath, or should haue. Johnson (1755, n. 3): Just claim (OED, n.1 10a, quoting this instance). See also 106, 114.

103 Let…him]
Steevens (in Reed, ed. 1803) compares Lr. 1.1.129 (137): Let pride, which she cals plainnesse, marry her.

103 Hermias]
Thirlby (MS 1733–47) conjectures Hermia: He has her love already; withdrawn MS 1747–53. Tyrwhitt (1766, p. 50) conjectures a comma erroneously read as terminal s. For the sole adoption, see textual n.

104–7 Scornefull…Demetrius]
Blits (2003, p. 28): Egeus…answers [Lysander] in the form of a full syllogism, while emphasizing legal terminology. Reason and law, in his view, go together. Boehrer (2004, pp. 99, 105), arguing (questionably) that the play foregrounds various sorts of same-sex attachments: Egeus’s attitude provides a concise instance of the masculine homosocial ethic.…From this perspective, marriage exists primarily as a vehicle for the formulation of masculine same-sex attachments, which receive secondary figuration through the exchange of women: hence the primacy of Egeus’s love for Demetrius, and…[his] insistence upon his absolute authority over Hermia and upon her concomitant status as a part of his estate.

105 my…render]
Deighton (ed. 1891): My love, the affection I bear to him: render, give; as often in Shakespeare without any idea of giving in return, or giving back (OED 7); see also n. 563.

106 my…her]
Deighton (ed. 1891): The right in her which as a parent I possess. Thirlby (MS 1725–33) compares Lady Percy speaking to Hotspur of my rights of thee (1H4 2.3.45 [893]). See n. 101.

107 estate vnto]
Capell (1783 [1774], 1:Glossary), citing also AYL 5.2.11 (2419): Settle as an estate (OED, v. 3, quoting this instance first). Rolfe (ed. 1877): Used by Shakespeare as a verb elsewhere only in [Tmp. 4.1.856 (1743–4)] (followed by on) and in [AYL 5.2.11 (2419)] (followed by upon). The Clarkes (1879, p. 550): Confer, bestow. Furness (ed. 1895): If Shakespeare elsewhere discloses the lawyer, he betrays the layman here. A lawyer would, instinctively almost, say estate upon or on. Cuningham (ed. 1905): But is this not hypercriticism? Surely unto is merely a reminiscence of the habendum clause of a conveyance. Gill (ed. 1981): The phrase is a legal one, continuing the idea that Hermia is a mere possession.

108–19 I…man]
Shaw (1895, p. 44): It should be clear to any stage manager that Lysander’s speech…should be spoken privately and not publicly to Theseus. Brooks (ed. 1979): Shaw…for once misjudges a stage effect, insisting that Lysander address these representations to the Duke apart. They are his public self-justification and counter-charge against Demetrius in what is a form of trial-scene. Holland (ed. 1994): It is not clear whether Lysander’s speech is directed to Theseus or, possibly, Egeus. Concurring, Raman (2001, pp. 260–1) asserts that this ambiguity foreshadows the projected break between civil law and paternal authority by pointing to a potential disjunction between the symbolic and the real father. Knowles (privately): There wouldn’t be any ambiguity on stage; the actor would have to decide whom to address. Egeus is probably beyond rational appeal from scornful Lysander, Theseus is the lawgiver and judge in the scene, My Lord sounds like Theseus (cf. 31, 88), and Theseus, not Egeus, replies.
Scragg (1988, p. 11): The heavily end-stopped lines, and regular rhythm…sustain, rather than diminish, the audience’s awareness of artifice, and hence frustrate dramatic involvement. That is not to say, of course, that the execution is flawed. The style is designed to promote dramatic distance, to allow us to enjoy what happens to Lysander rather than becoming implicated in his experience.

108 deriu’d]
Johnson (1755, 2), quoting this instance: To descend from (OED, ppl. a. a, quoting this instance first).

109 well possest]
Valpy (ed. 1833): Have as ample possessions (OED, Well-possessed, quoting this instance only). For the use of the passive see Abbott §295; Franz §631; Blake 4.2.7 (a).

110–11 My…Demetrius]
Deighton (ed. 1891): In that which I owe to fortune, I am in all respects the equal, if not the superior, of Demetrius.

110 fortunes]
OED (n. 5): One’s condition or standing in life; often absol. a prosperous condition,…Also pl. Some early editors (see textual n.) read this as an abbreviation for fortune is, and Wells (ed. 1967) admits the possibility of such a reading. Later, discussing the destruction of ambiguity necessitated by the modern use of the apostrophe in this word and at 988 (in Glowe-wormes, where either one or more could be meant), Wells (1984, p. 9) observes that if we are experiencing the [play] in the theatre…the ambiguity, unimpeded by the constraints of typography, may work effortlessly on our receptive consciousness.

110 as…rankt]
Kittredge (ed. 1939): Of as good a rank; as high in position and prosperity (OED, Rank, v.1 3a).

111 vantage]
Johnson (1755, 2): Superiority (OED 3a).

112 which is more]
Watt (ed. 1906): What is more important (OED, Which 11. pron.). For discussion of the phrase, see Blake 3.3.2.6 (d).

113 belou’d]
OED (Belove, v. 3), quoting this instance: To love. Now only in pass.

113 of]
Johnson (1755, 7): By. For a history of this usage, see OED (Prep. 15). See 788–9, 795, 797.

113 beautious]
Johnson (1755, Beauteous): This word is chiefly poetical. Scott (ed. 1898): More poetic than the hackneyed beautiful,…also sometimes more suitable for verse since it can be used as a disyllable. Spevack (ed. 1990, Ant., p. 124 n. 1196): For the suffix, added to words of French or Latin origin, see Franz §131.

113 Hermia]
For the pronunciation see n. 29.

114 prosecute]
Johnson (1755, 1), quoting this instance: To persue; to continue endeavours after any thing (OED 1a).

115 auouch]
Johnson (1755, 1): To affirm; to maintain; to declare peremptorily (OED 7a). Cf. Golding’s Ovid 13.380, His deed avouched to his face.

115 to his heade]
Wright (ed. 1877): Before his face, openly and unreservedly. See OED, Head, n.1 38a, where this instance and the one in Ado 5.1.62 (2146), to thy head, antedate the first quotation given there (MM 4.3.142 [2233], dated 1603). Craig (in Cuningham, ed. 1905) compares Golding’s Ovid 13.245, I…accused him to his head. See Franz §522.

116 Made loue to]
Johnson (1755, Love 3), quoting this instance: Courtship. Andrews (ed. 1989): The modern meaning of the phrase is inapplicable here. Lysander is saying that Demetrius wooed Helena. See OED, Love, n.1 7g, quoting this instance.

116 Nedars]
Walker (1860, 2:30): Perhaps a mistake of the printer’s for Nestor. But Dyce (ed. 1864): Very unlikely. Herbert (1977, p. 19) suggests an echo of Helen’s patronymic name, Tyndaris; cf. Met. 15.233, which Golding (15.255) translates Helen. Foakes (ed. 1984): The name was invented to fit the line. Hawkes (1992, pp. 13, 15): The nearest version of [Nedar] in Ancient Greece exists as Neda, the name of a river, and, presumably before that, of a nymph.…An old Neda could easily be Helena’s mother, and Nedar is an anagram of Arden…the family name of Shakespeare’s mother. Earlier, Westenholz (1896. p. 135) points out the anagram of Arden, but in pursuit of a different theory (see ). Morse (2003, p. 56), partly on the basis of Egeus’s reference to Nedar as old at 1651, rejects the theory that Nedar could be Helena’s mother. Not only would it be an indecorous reference to a woman of evidently high social status, not only are mothers absent, but the point of obedience and disobedience is patriarchal. Altimont (2007, pp. 275–6): A more convincing and intriguing origin for Nedar is to be found in Hebrew, where it exists as a verb meaning was missing; was absent, as an adjective meaning missing, absent, and as a noun meaning absentee. He suggests the name is an amusing commentary on a character whose absence from the stage means Helena has no champion against Demetrius’s defection or Egeus’s claims; he also cites as possibly relevant to the themes of constancy and choice in marriage a Hebrew noun neder (plural form nedareem or nedarim)…meaning pledge, vow, and the Nedarim Tractate of the Babylonian Talmud. See conj., .

117 soule]
See n. 91.

117–18 and she…Idolatry]
Traversi (1968, 1:142–3): Lysander, whilst speaking…in the defense of his own interest,…is ready to dismiss the love of others, as in the case of Helena, as so much irrational excess: [quoting 117–18], from which we are left to conclude that love, blind to its own partiality, is characteristically clear-sighted in relation to that of others. He goes on to examine the theme of doting in the play (1:143–53).

117, 118 dotes]
OED (v.1 3): To be infatuatedly fond of, to bestow excessive love or fondness on or upon; to be foolishly in love. See 239, 244, 548, 1024, 1558, 1693. See also 1562 (dotage). Reyher (1947, p. 59) compares Lyly, Endimion 3.4.61–2 (3:48) where the most fond lover is chastised: You doted then, not loved. Brooks (ed. 1979) adds the contrasting of louing and doting in Rom. 2.3.81–2 (1088–9). Richmond (2002, p. 150): To dote on someone…is to be obsessively concerned with them sexually, to the point of self-abandonment, even of self-destruction.…This passion will be quite independent of the subject’s virtue or monstrosity, as in Titania’s fixation on the ass-headed Bottom. The word in this sense occurs notably more frequently in this play than in any other Shn. work (ten times, followed by Ado and Oth. with five). See also Kermode (2000, p. 59) on dote and dotes.

118 Deuoutly]
Johnson (1755): Piously; with ardent devotion; religiously.

118 Idolatry]
OED (n. 2): Immoderate attachment to or veneration for any person or thing; admiration savouring of adoration. Hassel (2005, p. 168) notes Sh. always uses the word metaphorically.

119 spotted]
Johnson (in Steevens, ed. 1773): As spotless is innocent, so spotted is wicked. Caldecott (MS 1813–33): Not merely the opposite of spotless or innocent; but, as shifting & variable, stained, of no one or uniform colour. OED (2b fig.): Morally stained or blemished.
Brownlow (1987, p. 27) suspects Sh. may have picked up the word spotted from Southwell’s poem St. Peter’s Complaint (circulating in MS…1592) where it is twice used in a moral and religious sense. He notes the word occurs with this meaning four times in three of Sh.’s early works: Luc. 721 and 1172, R2 3.2.134 (1493), and here. Thirlby (MS 1733–47) compares Lr. 5.3.139 (3093), toad-spotted. Cf. also Sidney, Arcadia (ed. Evans, 1977, p. 165), where Clitophon’s device has the motto Rather dead than spotted; on the source and dissemination of this motto see the edition of the New Arcadia by V. Skretkowicz (1987, pp. 524–5). See also 660. See conj., .

120 so]
= As. See Abbott (§275); Franz §580. See OED, So, adv. and conj. 39f.

121 to haue spoke]
On the perfect infinitive expressing something which did not happen, after verbs of intending, wishing, hoping etc., see Abbott §360; Franz §657; Blake 4.3.1.2. See 1676–7, 1682.

121 spoke]
On the curtailed form of past participles see Abbott §343; Franz §168; Blake 4.2.4.1 (c) and 4.2.5 (a). See 186–7, 688 (forgot), 1136 (mistooke), 1227 (chid), 1228 (forgot), 1347 (chid), 1388 (mistooke), 1895 (broke), 1917 (rid), 2126 (shore), 2141 (writ).

122 But…affaires]
Tillyard (1944; 1946, p. 228) thinks this admission shows the sense of obligation to his subjects…which Theseus possesses. Pearson (1974, p. 293) however thinks this shows his injustice: Theseus’ desires [7] for Hippolyta have caused him to neglect his duties as ruler. He does not demand that justice be performed in the fulfilling of Demetrius’ vows [to Helena].

122 ouer full]
OED (Overfull), quoting this instance: Too full. Deighton (ed. 1891): Too much occupied.

122 selfe affaires]
Delius (ed. 1859): My own business (Ger.). On compounds with self see Abbott §20. See OED, Self- 5, quoting this instance only.

123 loose]
Variant form of lose. See 585, 1280, 2098. The meaning here is forget. See OED, Lose, v.1 5d, quoting this instance.

123–35 But…selues]
Brooks (ed. 1979) : The preservation of the lawes [North’s Plutarch; see ] was an especial care of Theseus’.…Using the ducal we [129], he pronounces sentence accordingly; but suspends it [126–30]. Personally, he is dissatisfied with it, he sees it troubling Hippolyta [131], and by private suasion will try to talk Egeus and Demetrius out of their insistence [123–5, 132–5]. Comparison with Solinus in Err. [1.1.1–158 (5–161)]…helps to confirm this interpretation, and as with Solinus, in the rift between the Duke’s constitutional and his humane personal judgement, Shakespeare is preparing for his part in the dénouement.

125 schooling]
Johnson (1755, School v. a. 2), quoting this instance: To teach with superiority; to tutor. Delius (ed. 1859): Reprimand (Ger.). Schmidt (1875, School vb. 2), quoting also 1H4 3.1.188 (1722), I am schoold, adds: To set to rights. OED (vbl. n.1 2), quoting this instance: Admonition, reproof, scolding. Some later commentators, beginning with Verity (ed. 1893), soften the meaning to instructions (referring to 133–5) or, as Gill (ed. 1981), advice.

126–30 For…life]
Quoting these lines, Longo (1980, pp. 17–18): The setting…takes on the aura of a formal ceremonial ritual of trial at court.…In this ritual the Duke is symbolic of the governing force of reason, of the law; he is a splendid example of that force which Neitzsche characterized as Apollonian.

126–7 For…will]
Wallace (1997, p. 115): The military metaphor with which Theseus addresses Hermia intrudes itself strangely here into his speaking: the daughter should arm herself for a battle that can only end with her defeat at the hands of her father. For Theseus, the Hermia-Egeus conflict is clearly analogous to the battle he has just won…against the Amazon queen.

126 For]
Johnson (1755, 2): With respect to; with regard to (OED, prep. A. 26a). See Abbott §149; Franz §483; Blake 5.4.2 For (d).

126 looke]
Johnson (1755, 5): To take care; to watch. OED (v. 3b): To take care, make sure, see (that or how something is done; also with omission of that). Now arch. See 648, 1118, 1404.

126 arme]
Schmidt (1874, 1a), citing this instance: To furnish with weapons of offence or defense (OED, v.1 2e, quoting this instance first; see also 2b). Cf. 534.

126 your selfe]
Andrews (ed. 1989): Normally spelled as two words in the Elizabethan period (as is my self), when the self was treated somewhat more objectively than the modern spelling of myself and yourself allows. It was not until the 1740’s and the next two decades that my self and your self (selues) were spelled as one word in editions of MND. But himself (six uses) and themselues (five uses) are always one word, from Q1 on. There are no uses in MND of herself or itself. See Franz §310. For my and your self see 135, 216, 230, 283, 326, 594, 840, 1338, 1523, 2045.

127 To…will]
Andrews (ed. 1989) suggests that the alliteration focusses the contending forces, i.e. Hermia’s fancies and her father’s will. Wilson (1969, p. 79) finds Theseus’s position on the extreme right, calling it a doctrine against which Robin Goodfellow had argued with some passion in Tell-Troth’s New Year’s Gift (1593). See Robin’s Inuectiue against Ielosy where he says that the first cause [of jealousy]…is a constrained loue, when as parentes do by compulsion coople two bodies…. (New Shakspere Society Publications, Series VI, Shakspere’s England, Nos. 2–3, ed. F. J. Furnivall, 1876, p. 5). See Shulman, .

127 fit]
Johnson (1755, 1): To accommodate to any thing; to suit one thing to another (OED, v.1 7, quoting this instance).

127 fancies]
Johnson (1755, 5), quoting this instance: Inclination; liking; fondness. Moberly (ed. 1881): Affections. Deighton (ed. 1891): Fanciful desires. Kittredge (ed. 1939): Thoughts of love. Perhaps also Capricious preference (OED, n. 8a). See n. 40 (phantasie). See conj., .

128 Law…Athens]
White (1911, p. 81): Under Draco’s laws, death in Athens was appointed for almost all offenses. Those convicted of idleness or the theft of a cabbage or an apple were made to suffer the same penalties as criminals who committed sacrilege or murder. See North’s Plutarch, 1595, p. 95. See also n. 1704–6.

129 Which…extenuate]
Theobald (MS 1723–33) compares Err. without citing a place, but he is probably referring to the Duke in 1.1.4 (8) who, like Theseus, may not infringe our Lawes, despite his sympathy for the grieving father Haplesse Egeon (1.1.140–9 [143–52]). Verity (ed. 1912) compares the Duke in MV who, similarly, cannot denie the course of law (3.3.26 [1714]). Cf. also Angelo, the Duke’s surrogate, who may not extenuate Claudio’s offence, for he must dye (MM 2.1.27 [478], 31 [482]). Cf. n. 1704–6.

129 we]
Kittredge (ed. 1939): Speaking officially, Theseus uses the royal we instead of I.

129 extenuate]
Thirlby (MS 1733–47): Lessen penalty. The Clarkes (ed. 1864): Mitigate in severity, diminish in force (OED, v. 4b, quoting this instance).

131 Come…loue]
Warburton (ed. 1747): Hippolita had not said one single word all this while. Had a modern poet had the teaching of her, we should have found her the busiest amongst them; and, without doubt, the Lovers might have expected a more equitable decision. But Shakespear knew better what he was about; and observed decorum. Thaler (1929, pp. 26–7), quoting Warburton: The critical principle here would seem to be that ladies should be seen but not heard—but Shakspere…did not fancy such unfair discrimination between the sexes. He observed something more than decorum; namely, consistency of characterization. The bouncing Amazon [445] is easily bored, from first to last, by other people’s concerns (see also ). Latimer (1886, p. 97): She was simply vexed at having her lord’s time and attention drawn away from her by a matter of business. But some think Hippolyta sympathizes with the lovers. Bates (ed. 1895): Is it possible that the captive Queen of the Amazons has not approved of these proceedings and that her cheer is less sprightly than Theseus expects? Brooks (ed. 1979): She is downcast at the ill-omen, intruding upon the joyous preparations for her wedding, of love threatened with death or a compelled celibacy. And cf. the compunction of Chaucer’s Hippolyta at Theseus’ harsh sentence (thereupon rescinded) on the lovers in the [KnT 1747–60]. See . Wallace (1997, pp. 115–16) suggests that Theseus’s question is provoked by a midline look from Hippolita which can be as eloquent as an actor, actress, or director cares to make it.…It offers us, at the very least, the opportunity to register the resistant personhood of Hippolita on the space of the stage. But Moberly (ed. 1881): A lover-like enquiry, as it was long since he had spoken to her, and he wishes to be assured she is none the worse. Chambers (ed. 1897): He whispers a tender word.

131 my]
OED (poss. a. 2c): Prefixed (without intervening adj.) to the name of the person addressed: (a) poet. as a latinism, expressing intimate friendship (obs. or arch.); (b) in the language of fervid affection. This instance antedates the first quotation in OED (1732). Schmidt (1875): Expressing tenderness, quoting 1591, My Oberon. See n. 1793.

131 loue]
See n. 93.

131 what cheare]
Schmidt (1874, 1): = How is it with you? (OED, n.1 3b). See 2092.

132–5 Demetrius…selues]
Wilson (ed. 1924) finds these lines a repetition, in other words, of 123–5, a sign of a later revision, when Hippolyta was added to this scene. See .

132 Demetrius…along]
Andrews (ed. 1993) compares Rom. 1.1.99–102 (101–4) where another quarrel ends with the ruler arranging private conferences designed to restore harmony to his city.

132 goe along]
Thirlby (MS 1747–53): I.e. with me. See Abbott §30, quoting this instance. See OED, Along, C. adv. 4, quoting this instance first.

133–5 I…selues]
Black (1965, p. 30): We hear no more of this business; it merely gets Theseus, Egeus, and Demetrius off the stage.

133 businesse]
OED (n. 15a), quoting 1436: A particular matter demanding attention. Seymour (1805, 1:42): A trisyllable.

134 Against]
Johnson (1755, 8): In provision for; in expectation, quoting Ham. 1.1.158 (157). Delius (ed. 1859): Concerning (Ger.). See OED, prep. 19. See Abbott §142; Franz §460 (= in expectation of), §§557, 643 (= until); Blake 5.3.2.4 (b). See 1122, 1872.

134 our]
Wallace (1997, pp. 115–16): The royal plural leaves it in doubt whether our includes her nuptial. But cf. 4, our nuptiall hower, addressing Hippolyta and probably including her.

134 nuptiall]
Johnson (1755, Nuptials): Marriage.…It is in Shakespeare singular, but contrarily to use. Schmidt (1875) gives the two exceptions—Per. 5.3.80 (2335), and Oth. 2.2.7 (1104) (the quartos have the plural, the folios the singular). OED (B. n. 1. [sing.]), quotes this instance first. See Franz §192. See 1872.

135 Of…selues]
Miriam Joseph (1947, pp. 54–5) gives this as an example of anastrophe, or unusual word order, one of various forms of departure from the ordinary order, which frequently confer both emphasis and distinction. Cf. WT 3.2.242 (1434).

135 nerely]
Johnson (1755, 2): Closely; pressingly. OED (Nearly, adv. 3): In a special degree or manner; particularly. Delius (ed. 1859) and most others: Nearly modifies concerns (Ger.). Doran (ed. 1959) alone: Modifies confer. McDonald (ed. 2000): May modify confer or concerns. See Abbott §421 on the transposition of adverbs, where he quotes this instance. All editions from F4 to Johnson (ed. 1765) and from Malone (ed. 1790) on drop the comma before nerely, which makes it easier to refer the adverb to conferre.

135 your selues]
See n. 126 (your selfe).

136 With…desire]
Schmidt (1874, Desire, 1): I.e. obediently and gladly. Deighton (ed. 1891): With dutiful eagerness. Kittredge (ed. 1939): With zealous devotion.

136 desire]
Thirlby (MS 1747–53): To serve you. Gill (ed. 1981): Willingness.

137–265 How…againe]
A few early editors, beginning with Pope (ed. 1725), start a new scene here. See textual n. Later Fleay (1879, p. 56) too proposed a break: At line [138]…a new scene must begin.…This scene….is clearly in a street. But Chambers (ed. 1897): The interview between the lovers…is carefully led up to in what precedes. Theseus’ commands to Egeus and Demetrius to accompany him have no significance in the story: they are only the playwright’s rather crude device to clear the stage for Lysander and Hermia. Moreover the Manet Lysander and Hermia of the F1 stage-direction disposes of Fleay’s view. Wilson (ed. 1924): Mr. Chambers mistakes Fleay’s argument, which is that the two sections were separate scenes in the original draft of the play but had been fused (by the playwright’s rather crude device) in the course of revision.…Q[1]. reads Exeunt at [136] which suggests the end of a scene: F. adds [its SD] by way of showing that the scene continues. Phillips (ed. 1969) suggests a compromise: if different parts or levels of [the] stage are used, and Hermia and Lysander descend a few steps,…the audience can readily believe that they are now in the street or a public courtyard.

138–41 How…eyes]
Discussing the artificiality of the language here, Belsey (1993, pp. 182–3): [T]he poetic image of the roses in her cheeks legitimates the conceit that follows: the roses are short of water, which Hermia is about to supply. The exchange has the effect of distancing the threat to Hermia, and putting before the audience instead what is delicate, lyrical, and witty in romance.

138–9 How…fast]
Walter (ed. 1964): An odd question after what has happened to Hermia. Perhaps it is a display of tenderness or of encouragement. Cf. Friar Laurence’s description of the deathlike effects of the potion on Juliet: The roses in thy lips and cheekes shall fade [Rom. 4.1.99 (2394)].

138 How now]
Schmidt (1875, Now): Used to introduce an address, especially one expressive of surprise or curiosity:…More frequently how now…= what is the matter? OED (How, adv. 4b): Ellipt. for How is it now? Often used interjectionally. arch. Cf. 375.

139–41 How…eyes]
In A gorgious Gallery (1578, p. O2v) Thisbie sees herself as a fading tree that cannot bloom, and is overwhelmed by tears because she is to be parted from her lover.

139 How chance]
Abbott (§37), quoting this instance: How may it chance…that (OED, Chance, v. 5). See Franz §627; Blake 4.4.1 (e). See 2105.

139 roses]
OED (n.1 9), quoting this instance first: Chiefly pl. The fresh pink or ruddy hue of the complexion, esp. in young women.

139 fade]
Schmidt (1874, 1): To lose the colour of life, to wither (OED, v.1 1).

140–1 Belike…eyes]
Scott (ed. 1898) condemns these lines as an Italianate conceit illustrating the age’s tendency to far-fetched similes, illustrations or ideas.

140 Belike]
Commentators use sometimes the one and sometimes the other of the definitions given by Johnson (1755, 1, 2): 1. Probably; likely; perhaps (OED, A. adv.). 2. Used in a sense of irony; as, we are to suppose. Schmidt (1874): As it seems, it should seem, I suppose. See Blake 5.1.3.5 (vi).

141 Beteeme…eyes]
Furness (ed. 1895): The tempest of Hermia’s eyes could readily pour down the rain to revive the roses in her cheeks.

141 Beteeme]
Gildon (1710, Glossary): To bring forth, or breed. Pope (ed. 1725): Pour down upon ’em. Johnson (ed. 1765): Give them, bestow upon them. The word is used by Spenser; Steevens (ed. 1778) supplies FQ 2.8.19.6. Johnson adds Pope’s definition in his Appendix (8:2H6v). Most give either of these meanings, or both. OED (v.1 2a) agrees with Johnson, quoting this instance first: To vouchsafe, accord, grant, concede. (Const. To beteem a person a thing, a thing to a possessor). For Pope’s meaning Pour OED (v.2 Obs. rare1) gives only one instance, from 1618. Other attempts at definition are unattested by OED. Becket (1815, 1:265): Cause them to grow or flourish. Singer (ed. 1826): Afford or deign to allow, influenced by Sh.’s use in Ham. 1.2.141 (325); but OED (v.1 2b) terms this meaning rare and quotes only Ham. Evans (ed. 1887) cites Skeat, Concise Etymological Dictionary (1880, Teem), beteem.…To think fit, hence to permit, to allow, where both MND and Ham. are cited; but OED (Teem, v.3 Obs. rare1) quotes only an instance from 1593. Hudson (ed. 1851) compares the use in Golding’s Ovid (10.163) as meaning permit, afford, and Moberly (ed. 1881) quotes the same defining it as condescend. See conj., .

142–52 Eigh…it]
Macdonald (1992, p. 39): This remarkable little duet is not really calculated to make us sympathize with lovers’ travails, for it formally exemplifies in its artificial and stagey way the very constraint it otherwise protests. We may even suspect that these lovers are rather enjoying their plight, indulging themselves in their own sense of suffering and affliction. By the logic implied here, if the course of love were to run smoothly, then it would not be true love, and thus the lovers actually derive a sense of their own authenticity from the very obstacles that stand in their way. Cartwright (1862, p. 160) contesting the theory that these lines refer to Sh.’s unfortunate marriage: The story of his domestic infelicity was first started…by Oldys misinterpreting one of the Sonnets. He is probably referring to a note to the two opening lines of Son. 93 by Malone (1780, 1:653), who quotes Oldys as saying that these lines seem to have been addressed by Shakespeare to his wife on suspicion of her infidelity. See Rollins, ed. Son., 1944, 1:231 and 2:256.

142 Eigh me]
OED (Ay, int. 2, giving eigh as a variant form): Ay me = Alas! ah me!—an ejaculation of regret, sorrow, pity. Pronounced as in day, and not to be confused with aye, pronounced eye, for which see n. 617. Cf. the Latin interjection ei mihi (ah me, woe is me) as in Vergil, Aeneid 2.274, Tibullus 3.6.33, etc. Cf. T. Heywood’s Loves maistresse: Ay-me, that any wench were heere, whose name is Ayme (1636, E3r). See 802.
F2’s emendation Hermia (see textual n.) is praised by a few, beginning with Hunter (1845, 1:288): It appears to me to have a point and pathos even beyond what the passage, as usually printed, possesses. Hermia! for aught.… A skilful actor might give great effect to the name. White (ed. 1858): The exclamation [Eigh me] is unsuited to Lysander and to his speech; and I believe that it was an error of the press, or of the transcribers, for the proper name, and that its absence in the folio is the result of its erasure in the quarto stage copy, the interlineation of the correct word having been omitted by accident. Thiselton (1903, pp. 14–15): [The Qq reading] seems out of place, for Lysander is clearly trying to comfort Hermia, and not to make her more sorrowful by displaying his own grief. Hermia of the second Folio is certainly an improvement. But after all we need not quarrel with the occasional occurrence of a line of only four accents: see [Abbott §505], (who, however, states that such lines are very rare). But most agree with Halliwell (ed. 1856): The authority of both the quartos support[s] the present lection, and is of course to be preferred to that of [F2].

142 for]
OED (prep. 23a): In spite of. See Abbott §154; Franz §484; Blake 5.4.2 For (j).

142 aught]
Johnson (1755): Any thing. OED (n.2 [pron.] A): Anything whatever; anything. In interrogative, negative, and conditional sentences. See Franz §356; Blake 3.3.2.7 (i). Cf. 1099.

143 here]
Variant form of hear (OED). See 1703.

143 tale or history]
Johnson (1755, Tale 1; 2): 1. A narrative; a story. Commonly a slight or petty account of some trifling or fabulous incident.…2. Oral relation, quoting 142–4. He defines history as A narration of events and facts. Black (1965, p. 31) sums it up neatly: In fiction or in fact. See OED, Tale, n. 5a. See 422. See also n. 1158.

144–59 The…confusion]
Characterized by Pope (ed. 1725, 6:4H1v) as Love, the crosses of it. Theobald (ed. 1733, at 146) is the first of several commentators to call attention to Ven. 1135 where Venus begins her six stanzas cursing Love. Newton (1749, 2:285–6), in a note on PL 10.888–908, compares Lysander’s lines (omitting Hermia’s interjections) with the complaints which Adam makes of the disasters of love. Knight (ed. 1839): The passage in Paradise Lost, in which Milton has imitated this famous passage of Shakspere, is conceived in a very different spirit.…Milton has dramatically forgotten that man is not the only sufferer. Latham ([1892], p. 402): There is a forced artificiality [in this exchange], which is worked out like a kind of duet between the lovers, in a fashion which real feeling never follows. But Shaw (1895, p. 44): Shakespeare makes the two star-crossed lovers speak in alternate lines with an effect which sets the whole scene throbbing with their absorption in one another. [Quoting 144–52] With a Hermia who knew how to breathe out these parentheses, the duet would be an exquisite one; but Mr. Daly…cuts out the whole antiphony, and leaves Lysander to deliver a long lecture without interruption from the lady. Fender (1968, pp. 38–9): The artificiality of this passage suits it well to the several formal definitions of romantic love which Act 1 provides for the audience; the balanced periods support the idea that love imposes a kind of symmetry by pairing off individuals; the antitheses remind us that the match is often unequal. The formality also expresses Lysander’s and Hermia’s attempt to buttress themselves against the unknown trials of love awaiting them. Weld (1975, p. 195) considers the emphasis on transiency odd…in a play written for a wedding celebration, but it is part of the general recognition of the evanescent and illusory nature of earthly pleasure which is one of the premises of the action. Brunel (ed. 1974, p. 38) finds the lyricism of the love duet provokes some suspicion through its very excess. The situation is tragic, but the treatment is parodic (Fr.). Hibbard (Love, 1978, pp. 152–3) sees reflected in it the real-life situations of marriage contracts in Sh.’s day.
Sitwell (1948, pp. 186–9) analyzes the rhetorical devices and their effects in this great passage concluding: Never has there been a more magical use of sound to convey meaning than in this play.

144 The…smoothe]
Staunton (ed. 1857) compares Blurt, Master Constable (1602; attributed by him to Middleton, but by Berger to Dekker), I never heard Of any true affection, but t’was nipt With care, (ed. Thomas L. Berger, 1979, 3.1.39–41). ODEP gives Lysander’s line as a proverb, quoting this instance first, and one from 1836. Dent (C716.1) lists as a proverb, but comments (p. 8): Unlike all other citations for this play, this was almost surely not proverbial in Shakespeare’s day. Neil (ed. 1878): This line, in Elizabeth’s time, when the queen sought to prevent the marriage of her courtiers and favourites, and often punished those who married without her consent asked and given, must have had a peculiar force and applicability.

145–50 But…eyes]
Chambers (ed. 1897, p. 195) points out the special use of end-stopped lines, in which a rapid dialogue is carried on, by each speaker confining what he has to say within the limits of a single line. This is the stichomuthia [stychomythia] of Greek tragedy, and, whether rhymed or unrhymed, has a lyrical antiphonic effect, alluding to this passage, 206–14, and 738–41. Greenblatt (ed. 1997, p. 806): The effect [of the stychomythia] here is to convey the lovers’ mutual anguish, tinging it slightly perhaps with a gently ironic distance that evaporates in the poignant lament that follows [151–9]. Dash (1997, p. 78): Stylistically, those clipped single lines suggest the exasperation and intensity of the young woman we are to meet later on in the forest. Moreover, the patter, or duet, of these two lovers complements the long heavy speeches of Egeus and Theseus, taking us back into the comic, romantic world of the play. See also n. 206–14.

145, 147, 149 bloud;…yeares;…friends;]
Thiselton (1903, p. 15) observes that the semicolons here mark more distinctively Hermia’s exclamations as interruptions than the full stops in the corresponding positions in the Folio. See n. 66.

145 bloud]
Johnson (1755, 4, 6): 4. Descent; lineage.…6. Birth; high extraction (OED, Blood, n. 9a). Verity (ed. 1893): Birth; the two persons are not of the same social rank. Cf. Perdita’s fears in WT 4.4.17 (1817). See also n. 77.

146–51 O…choyce]
Rushton (1909, p. 94): In this passage Shakespeare introduces Anaphora, or the Figure of Report, and Ecphonisis, or the Outcry, for Hermia begins with O, the first, third, and fifth lines, and Lysander begins with Or the second, fourth, and sixth lines. These terms are from Puttenham, (1589; 1936, pp. 198 and 212–13).

146, 148, 150 O…loue.…O…young.…O…eyes!]
Gildon (1710, 7:318) first omits these lines of Hermia, and so do Newton (1749, see n. 144–59 above), Dodd (1752, 1:76), and, in their acting versions, Garrick (ed. 1755), Reynolds (ed. 1816), Planché (ed. 1840), Kean (ed. 1856), and Daly (ed. 1888); see . Coleridge (1960 [1811], 1:90): It would be a great improvement if these two lines [146, 148] were omitted. The third O Hell! [150] would then become a beauty, and most natural. Halliwell (1841, p. 70) would omit all Hermia’s interpolations, but changes his mind later. Most editors keep the interjections, and a few defend them. Gentleman (ed. 1774): The intersection of these single lines has a pretty effect in reading, and must have a better on the stage. Capell (1783, 2.3:100) admits that Hermia’s interjectory lines have an odd appearance at first; and are condemn’d by some criticks, as blemishes of a passage whose latter part rises to a pitch of sublimity…[that was] a pitch above comedy; and…to qualify…the speech’s over-formality…[Sh. breaks it] by the lady’s light remarks on some parts of it. He points to 184–7 and 219–20 as further examples of Hermia’s banter. Halliwell (ed. 1856): In some alterations of this play, the interruptions of Hermia are omitted, an arrangement which unquestionably increases the effect of Lysander’s speech, and might be adopted in representation, were it not that the author evidently intended both the speakers should join in passionately lamenting the difficulties encountered in the path of love. It must also be remembered that it was the occasional practice of Shakespeare and Milton to introduce parenthetical interruptions into the midst of the most eloquent passages. It may be that the lines are omitted because they seem too bold, too unladylike, for a young woman.

146 crosse]
Johnson (1755, 5), quoting the instance at 163: Any thing that thwarts or obstructs; misfortune; hindrance; vexation; opposition; misadventure; trial of patience (OED 10b). Wright (ed. 1877) adds Trial; from the figurative use of the word in Scripture. See Matthew [10:38] (OED 10a). Deighton (ed. 1891) also sees a Christian connotation here, but most take the word in the more general sense. Scott (ed. 1898): Suggests something perverse, contrary to nature. See also n. 163.

146 inthrald]
Obs. var. of Enthral; OED (1): To enslave, bring into bondage. Schmidt (1874, 2): Reduced to the servitude of love. Cf. 957.

146 loue]
Hunter (ed. 1870): One of too high rank to be enslaved by love for one of low degree. Similarly, Wright (1968, p. 23): Too high in social class to be the servant of love. So also Gill (ed. 1981), Andrews (ed. 1993).
Most editors follow Theobald (ed. 1733) in emending to low (see textual n.), which provides an antithesis analogous to 148. This was first conjectured by Thirlby (MS 1725–33), who later (MS 1747–53) disapproves its adoption. Malone (ed. 1790) cites Ven. 1139 in support of the emendation. Brooks (ed. 1979) believes it confirmed by a passage in Lyly, Euphues and his England, Surius on the choyce of a wife: my friends are unwilling I should match so low, not knowing that love thinketh the Juniper shrub to be as high as the tal Oke [2:219, lines 24, 5–7].

147 misgraffed]
Capell (1783 [1774], 1:Glossary): Graffed, or grafted, wrong or amiss. Morgan (1876, p. 155 n.): Unequally matched. See Franz §61. OED (pa. pple.) quotes this instance only.

147 respect]
Schmidt (1875, 4a), quoting this instance: = with regard to (OED 4a). The Clarkes (1879, p. 627) observe that in all five uses in this play respect has the meaning regard or consideration, rather than the more usually assigned one of reverence or deference, citing this instance, 170 (a verb), 588, 603, and 1888.

148 O spight]
Schmidt (1875, 3): Vexation, mortification. Sh. uses this interjection three times in MND, once in Ham., ô cursed spight (1.5.188 [885]), and in other plays, but OED gives no listing of the phrase as an interjection. Deighton (ed. 1891): O sad misfortune, and Doran (ed. 1959) at 2078, Malice of fortune, combine OED’s Spite 2a and 2c to come perhaps closest to the meaning. Kittredge (ed. 1939) suggests plague (unattested in OED). See 1170, 2078, 1221, 1464.

148 ingag’d]
Johnson (1755, Engage 7): To bind by appointment or contract. See OED, Engage, v. 4a and 7a, where this instance antedates the first quotation given in both places (1603 and 1642).

149 Or…friends]
Deighton (ed. 1891): A matter of negotiation between the friends, or relatives, not a matter of love between the principal parties. Wright (1968, p. 23): It depended upon the choice made by their relatives or guardians, the regular meaning of friends in such contexts. Holland (ed. 1994): Particularly those whose control over a minor’s property gives them the right to make decisions about his/her marriage (see OED, sb. 3). The notion that an individual’s marriage ought not to depend on the choice of others is obviously central to this scene.
F1’s merit (see textual n.) has elicited comment. Knight (ed. 1839): The alteration in the folio was certainly not an accidental one; but we hesitate to adopt the reading, the meaning of which is more recondite than that of friends. The choice of merit is opposed to the sympathy in choice;—the merit of the suitor recommends itself to another’s eye, but not to the person beloved. Brooks (ed. 1979, pp. 154–5) argues ingeniously that merit is Shn., comparing Tro. 1.3.348–9 (815–16): choice…makes merit her election, and claiming that merit is taken from prompt-copy and is Shakespeare’s expression, but that he revised it to friends in order to make the idea plain to the audience. Even less convincingly, Taylor (1981, p. 333) argues that F1’s merit arose from a compositor misunderstanding a marginal annotation, and substituting merit for friends instead of for else it, as the annotator intended.

150 O hell]
Schmidt (1874, 2): A state of extreme torture and misery, citing also 220, 622 and 1170. Noyes (ed. 1908): Hell! to Hermia meant no more than Bother! to a woman of to-day. Black (1965, p. 31): Torment. Hermia is not swearing. See also 220, 622.

151 a…choyce]
Johnson (1755, Sympathy), quoting this instance: Fellowfeeling; mutual sensiblility; the quality of being affected by the affection of another. OED (n. 2): Agreement, accord, harmony. Kittredge (ed. 1939): Harmony, correspondence (in rank, age, etc.) between the two that have chosen each other. Mowat & Werstine (ed. 1993): If the lovers were suitably matched.

153–9 Making…confusion]
Carroll (1985, p. 148): This sounds so apocalyptic in its Ecce [beholde (157)], so much like a description of human life, so much like Bede’s famous description of the bird’s brief flight through the lighted hall and back into darkness [Ecclesiastical History, 2.13 (Loeb ed. 1:283–5)], that it might stand for all human experience in the play. The jaws of darkness threaten everything, but the metaphor of the jaws reminds us specifically of the monstrous which lurks nearby and within, however comic it might seem. The reach of Lysander’s description seems even larger when we begin to hear the code words which link it to the rest of the play—shadow, dream, heaven and earth, darkness, quick bright things, and confusion.
Wilson (ed. 1924) claims that in an earlier version 160 followed 152, the semicolon of this line marking an interruption (as in 145, 147, and 149), and that 153–9 were a marginal addition made in revision. See .

153 momentany…sound]
A striking reference to the ephemeral nature of the vibrations of the air by which any thing is made audible. See OED, Sound, n.3 1a. Thiselton (1903, p. 16), alone: I.e. transient as a swoon, comparing F1’s unique reading sound for swoune at 809. OED (Sound, n.4 1): A swoon. Very common c1530–1650.

153 momentany]
Johnson (ed. 1765) follows F1’s momentarie in his text but has a note, Momentany. Which is the old and proper word. Most follow Q1 (see textual n.), but Knight (ed. 1839) prefers F1 because momentary occurs in four [actually seven] other passages in our poet’s dramas; and this is a solitary example of the use of momentany, and that only in the quartos. Dyce (ed. 1864, 9:Glossary): Lasting for a moment. OED (Momentany, a.): Very common in the 16th and 17th c.

154 Swift…dreame]
Grey (1754, 1:43): Man’s life is compared to a shadow and a dream, in several passages of scripture, but he gives no examples. Halliwell (ed. 1856) quotes Job 8:9 for shadow and 20:9 for dream, and adds Chaucer, MerT 1315, giftes of fortune, that passen as a schadow on a wal. See . See also ShipT 9. Scott (ed. 1898) compares 1 Chron. 29:15 and Ps. 73:19, 20. Dent compares S262.1: To flee like a Shadow. See OED (Swift, a. 3), quoting this instance: Passing quickly, and Shadow, n. 4c. See also Tmp. 4.1.156–8 (1827–9).

155–9 Briefe…confusion]
Gildon (1710, 7:318): The Simile of Lightning is a perfect Hypotiposis [defined by Puttenham (1589; 1936, p. 238) as a description of things as if they were truly before our eyes] and the Epiphonema [a striking or witty closing to a poem, etc.; ibid., pp. 216–17] in the last Line concludes the Topic beautifully.

155 Briefe…lightning]
Proverbial (Tilley L279).

155 collied]
Johnson (1755, Colly), quoting this instance: To grime with coal; to smut with coal. OED (ppl. a.), quoting this instance first: Rendered colly; begrimed; blackened; darkened, murky. Pope (ed. 1725): Black. Hanmer (ed. 1744, 6:Glossary) adds: Sooty. Capell (1783 [1774], 1:Glossary): Darken’d with Clouds. Holbrook (2003, p. 86): Collied has its primary meaning coal-black, but also summons up the proverbially dirty trade of coal-carters and miners. That mingling of social levels preserves Shakespeare’s verse from cliché and monotony. Singer (ed. 1826) compares Sh.’s only other use in Oth. 2.3.206 (1326), hauing my best iudgement collied [F1; coold in Qq].

156 spleene]
Hanmer (ed. 1744, 6:Glossary): A sudden start, a hasty motion, a momentary quickness. Warburton (ed. 1747): Shakespear always hurried on by the grandeur and multitude of his Ideas assumes, every now and then, an uncommon licence in the use of his words.…Thus wanting here to express the ideas—of a sudden, or—in a trice, he uses the word Spleen; which, partially considered, signifying a sudden hasty fit is enough for him, and he never troubles himself about the further…signification of the word. OED (n. 7a), quoting this instance: A fit of temper; a passion. Also transf. exc. arch. Cf. also 4a: A sudden impulse; a whim or caprice. Thirlby (MS 1725–33) compares Jn. 5.7.50 (2660), and (MS 1747–53) Jn. 2.1.448 (763). See conj., .

156 vnfolds]
Johnson (1755, 4): To display; to set to view (OED, v.1 3, quoting this instance). See also 221 (vnfould).

156 heauen]
Monosyllabic in Q1 except at 13. On the softening of medial v see Abbott §466; Kökeritz (1953, p. 260); see also Cercignani (1981, pp. 326–7). See 220, 622, 1496, 1805 (bis). See also n. 36 (giuen).

157–8 And…vp]
Many commentators, starting with Thirlby (MS 1733–47), compare Rom. 2.2.119–20 (919–20). Halliwell (ed. 1856) quotes a passage from Sharpham’s play The Fleire (1607), 2.1.5–6: …momentarie sports, which like to lightning appeares, and vanisheth ere one can say tis come (ed. Petter, 1986, p. 261). See also n. 2128.

158 iawes]
OED (Jaw, n.1 5 fig.): The seizing action or capacity of any devouring agency, as death, time, etc. Rylands (1928, pp. 236–7): In the earlier plays [Sh.] makes not a little play with physical words, instancing among other examples this word.

158 it]
I.e. the lightning.

159 So…confusion]
Chambers (ed. 1897): Note the pathos of the final line, moving slowly and dying away without a stress.

159 quicke]
Parsons (1953, pp. 64–5) argues this is adjectival and, with bright, refers to lightning: Lysander’s four examples of quickness are applied, not to the length of the course of true love, which varies in each case, but to the speed with which war, death or sickness terminates it, when one of their number lays siege to it. But Wells (ed. 1967): This may be taken either as an adjective (alive, vital) or an adverb (quickly).

159 bright]
Rylands (1928, p. 94): The poetic word for alive, symbolical, as the rose, of beauty and vitality.

159 confusion]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Ruin (OED 1). See also n. 1631–2. On the disyllabic pronunciation of -ion, see Dobson (1968, §§276 and 292); Cercignani (1981, pp. 296 and 308–9). See Abbott §479; Franz §66. See 19, 232, 491, 1224, 1237, 1257, 1411, 1412, 1631, 1632, 1809.

160 euer]
Johnson (1755, 2): At all times (OED, adv. 2a). Hunter (ed. 1870): In every instance.

160 crost]
Johnson (1755, 6): To thwart;…to obstruct; to hinder (OED, Cross, v. 14a). See 494. Cuningham (ed. 1905) compares crosse (163).

161–3 It…crosse]
Richmond (2000, p. 113): Hermia echoes sentiments of the Knight’s Tale when she acknowledges destiny and advocates a Chaucerian response, [quotes 162–3]. A way of patience in adversity is…salient in medieval Christendom and appears frequently in Chaucer’s poetry. Cf. KnT 1084–6. See .

161 It…destiny]
Deighton (ed. 1891): It (sc. that they should be thwarted) is a decree firmly established by destiny. Wilson (ed. 1924), tendentiously: A very poor line of verse, which we take to be a piece of patchwork designed to cover a rent in the text. The clumsy duplication of then in [160 and 162] strongly suggests a cut, possibly of some half-dozen lines to balance the insertion of [153–9].

161 edict]
Johnson (1755): A proclamation of command or prohibition; a law promulgated, accenting the former syllable. Delius (ed. 1859): Sh. stresses edict now on the first, now on the second syllable (Ger). While there is disagreement about the accentuation of the word elsewhere, almost all accent the latter syllable here. See Abbott §490. OED (in the etymological note): In 16th and 17th c. accented on the last syllable. Only Deighton (ed. 1891) accentuates the former syllable.

161 destiny]
Gentleman (ed. 1774): We love not mention of destiny unless to refute so prejudicial and uncomfortable a notion; the idea here inculcated, especially respecting Love, is too much encouraged both among young and old.

162 let…patience]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Teach ourselves, thus tried, to endure with calmness. Shaheen (1993, p. 94): Patience under trial is mentioned at James 5.10–11.

162 triall]
Kittredge (ed. 1939): Hard lot.…The trial is personified. Phillips (ed. 1969): Sufferings.

162 patience]
Seymour (1805, 1:42): A trisyllable. See Abbott §479; Cercignani (1981, pp. 294, 309). See 1573. But at 1089 and 1186 it is disyllabic.

163 crosse]
Most of the few who comment accord this use the same meaning as Johnson (1755, 5) quoted at 146, but Schmidt (1874, 1): The ensign of the Christian religion…used as the emblem of human suffering. Suhamy (2002, p. 228) sees a pun on both meanings. Scott (ed. 1898) quotes Matt. 16:24. Noble (1935, p. 161) suggests an echo of the same phrase in Matt. 10:38. Shaheen (1993, p. 94) notes parallel references at 3H6 4.4.20 (2324) and R3 3.1.4–5 (1576–7).

164–5 As…followers]
Thiselton (1903, p. 17) sees a reflection of the sufferings of love’s servants in the temple of Venus (Chaucer, KnT, 1918–23; see ).

164 As dewe]
Hunter (ed. 1870): As much pertaining. Schmidt (1874, Due 3): Belonging. See n. 45 (due to).

164 thoughts]
Delius (ed. 1859): Melancholy thoughts, ruminations (Ger.). Evans (ed. 1974): Melancholy moods. Cf. OED (Thought1 5a): Anxiety,…grief, sorrow, trouble, care, vexation.

165 Fancies]
Almost all who comment follow Malone (ed. 1790): Love (OED, Fancy 8b). Foakes (ed. 1984) adds: But the word was synonymous with fantasy or imagination, and more commonly meant capricious or arbitrary preference (OED 8a). See also nn. 40, 127.

166 perswasion]
OED (n. 1b): Argument, quoting Chaucer’s House of Fame, 2.872: A good persuasion. Others offer similar meanings. Johnson (1755, 2): Opinion. Wright (ed. 1877): Conviction. Deighton (ed. 1891): Doctrine. Lobban (ed. 1913): Sentiment.

166 therefore heare mee]
Moberly (ed. 1881), wryly: Lovers’ logic—we must resign ourselves to what is ordered for us; therefore let us take flight and end it. Castelain (ed. 1943): After having politely approved Hermia’s resignation, Lysander immediately proposes a less passive solution, which Hermia accepts without hesitation (Fr.).

166 Hermia]
Disyllabic. See n. 29. On the shortening of polysyllabic names at the end of a line, see Abbott §469. Cf. n. 1357.

167–73 I haue…vs]
White (1911, p. 79) claims that, if sufficiently distant: Her home would furnish a haven, beyond the jurisdiction of the law of Athens.…For…the free states of Greece were merely cities with their districts, and each district possessed its own constitution and had exclusive management of its own concerns.

167–73 dowager]
Leggatt (1999, p. 50): We glimpse, beyond the borders of the play, an alternative power. This time the dowager is not the sterile, oppressive figure of Theseus’s opening description of the moon [7–9], and her childlessness is not mere barrenness but frees her for acts of charity. In every respect she offers a counter to the authority of Theseus. But her house lies beyond the borders of the play: the lovers never get there. See n. 8.

168 reuenew]
See n. 9. Hudson (ed. 1880): Here the accent is on the second syllable.

169–70 From…sonne]
A few editors find these lines transposed. See textual n. Thirlby (MS 1725–33) argues against his own conjectured transposition by noting it would result in a clash of and she [168] with And she [170]. Keightley (1867, p. 130): Common sense dictates the transposition made here.…The third line [170], it is evident, had been an addition made by the poet in the margin. Hudson (ed. 1880): The [Q1 reading] manifestly upsets the proper order and sequence of the thoughts.…Such transpositions are uncommonly frequent in this play. Wells & Taylor (1987, p. 280): These are the last two lines on a Q1 page (A4), and so in bibliographical context correspond to two lines apparently transposed in Quarto Lear. However, Brooks (ed. 1979): The Q1 order of the lines is illogical, but that is not enough to justify Johnson’s belief that it is corrupt. It neither obscures the sense, nor contravenes the way people speak.

169 remote]
OED (adj. 2a): Far away, far off, distant from. Most editors (see textual n.) keep the Q1 reading, as Malone (ed. 1790) does, even though he earlier supports F1 (1780, p. 114): Remov’d, which is the reading of the folio, was, I believe, the author’s word, quoting Ham. 1.4.61 (648). Knight (ed. 1839) quotes AYL 3.2.341–2 (1529–30) to aid his adoption of the F1 reading. In support of Q1 Deighton (ed. 1891) quotes LLL 5.2.795–6 (2755–6). The first instance of removed in the sense of remote from in OED (3) is dated 1617. The only use of the word in MND is at 806, in a different sense.

169 leagues]
Defined roughly as from one to four miles. Cotgrave (1611, Lieuë): A League; two miles. Les grandes lieuës, comme celles d’Allemaigne; containe verie neere four of our miles. Les moyennes lieuës, comme celles de Dauphiné, ou de Languedoc; are about three of our miles. Les petites lieuës, comme celles d’Italie; are little other then our miles. Florio (1611, Lega): A french league, that is, two english miles by land, and three by sea. Johnson (1755, 2): A measure of length, containing three miles. Schmidt (1874) and OED (n.1) agree with Johnson. See 175, 551.

170 respectes]
Johnson (1755, 1): To regard (OED 2). The Clarkes (ed. 1864) add considers. See n. 147.

171 may]
See n. 72.

172 sharpe]
Johnson (1755, 8), quoting this instance: Severe; quick to punish; cruel; severely rigid (OED, a. 5c).

173 If…mee]
The if means supposing that (OED, 1a [γ]), or, almost, since, and the phrase is commonly used in this way; cf. Shr. 4.3.50 (2032), 1H4 2.4.283 (1238), JC 5.3.14 (2494), etc. Thiselton (1903, p. 18) seems to misinterpret the meaning of the clause in his comment in n. 180–9.

174–5 Steale…towne]
Neil (ed. 1878) compares Golding’s Ovid 4.106, 108, To steale out of their fathers house…to meete without the towne. See . Taylor (Ovid’s Myths, 2004, p. 52) finds the echo of Golding ominous, and notes that the Elizabethan moralist finds the myth a piteous tale showing a headdie and frenticke young love doomed to end in wo and payn (Verse Epistle 109-10; see ). See also n. 362.

174 Steale]
Winter (2003, p. 90), quoting OED, v.1 9a: To depart or withdraw secretly or surreptitiously and 1a: To take away dishonestly (portable property, cattle, etc.) belonging to another; esp. to do this secretly or unobserved, notes the double meaning here of an escape from Athens which was a prison to the lovers, and the stealing of Egeus’s property, his daughter (see 106) (Fr.). See 226. See also n. 44.

175 wood]
Lees (ed. 1902): Perhaps suggested by the grove faste ther bisyde [Chaucer, KnT, 1478]. Doran (1974, pp. 55–6): [The wood is] a Pyramus and Thisbe wood, a place of rendezvous for the eloping lovers.…In the second place, the wood is a Knight’s Tale wood,…a May wood, rich in association with spring, with love, with seasonal festival, with poetry.…Finally, it is a fairy-haunted wood.

175 league]
Halliwell (ed. 1856): This wood is called, in the next scene, the Palace Wood, and is there described as being a mile without the town [362]. It appears that Shakespeare, in this and other instances, made a league and a mile synonymous. The league was certainly variously estimated. See n. 169.

175 without]
Johnson (1755, 4, 7): 4. Beyond; 7. On the outside of (OED, B. prep. 1a). See 362, 1678.

177 To…May]
For these observances, commentators on MND often quote Stubbes, who describes the wholesale exodus from Parish, Towne, and village…to the woods and groues…where they spend all the night in pleasant pastimes, returning with branches of trees and the gaily decked May-pole, activities presided over by Sathan prince of Hell and resulting in extraordinary losses of maidenheads (The Anatomie of Abuses, 1583, ed. M. J. Kidnie, 2002, pp. 209–10). The celebration of the burgeoning of nature in May by gathering flowers, speeding out to the greene Wood, and bringing home Lady Flora attended by a fayre flocke of Faeries is more enthusiastically hymned by Palinode in Spenser’s Shep. Cal., but such iouysaunce is rebuked by Piers as doing the Devil’s work (Maye, 1–43). Other frequently quoted less polemic sources of information on May rituals include Stow (A Survey of London, 1598, 1603, ed. C. L. Kingsford, 1908, rpt. 1971, 1:98–9), who describes the participation of the citizens of London of all estates (1:98); Strutt (1801, pp. 261–7), who draws on a manuscript describing the Maytime activities of Henry VII and gentlemen of his court to show that May-games might continue through the month and into June (pp. 264–5); Brand (1848–9; 1970, 1:212–98); Drake (1817, 1:152–74). Modern studies of the importance of May games and other festivities in Sh. include Barber (1959, esp. pp. 18–24, 119–20) and Laroque (1991, esp. pp. 111–19, 214–17).

177 obseruance]
Johnson (1755, 1), quoting this instance: Respect; ceremonial reverence (OED 1b). Knight (ed. 1839) notes: The very expression, to do observance, in connexion with the rites of May, occurs twice in Chaucer’s Knight’s Tale, quoting 1033–45 and 1497–1500 (see ). Chaucer’s association of May, the lovers, woods, Theseus and hunting is echoed in MND; cf. 1625, 1653–5.

177 to]
Halliwell (ed. 1856), defending Q1’s reading (see textual n.): The expression…was a common one applied to the observances of May morning, adding Tr. 2.112 to Knight’s (ed. 1839) two Chaucerian instances, KnT 1033–1045, 1497–1500 (see ). Adopting F1’s emendation for, White (ed. 1858) claims Sh.’s derivation of the phrase from Chaucer is a reason why he would not have repeated it letter for letter.

179 My…Lysander]
Brown (ed. 1996): Hermia completes Lysander’s line, suggesting her eagerness and instant agreement.

180–9 I…thee]
For Warburton’s (ed. 1747) condemnation of Hermia’s behavior, and Heath’s (1765) rebuttal, see . Thirlby (MS 1747–53) commented on Warburton’s proposal: Ridiculous to the last degree. Johnson (ed. 1765) thought the redistribution of lines (see textual n.) judicious, but not necessary. Thiselton (1903, p. 18): Warburton’s strictures upon her oaths may be easily disposed of, if we realise that Lysander’s If thou lov’st me [173] is rankling somewhat in Hermia’s breast. A half-playful resentment of the insinuation of a doubt on this point underlies her whole speech, and is strongly brought out by the parenthesis [187]. Traversi (1968, 1:144): The essentially dual nature of passion, compounded of simplicity and deception, could hardly be more strongly affirmed. Halio (1990, p. 140): Hermia may be merely teasing her lover,…[b]ut teasing always contains a barb, and…the allusions to male infidelity are ominous.…In any event, the rhetoric of the first eight lines is neatly undercut by the final couplet [188–9], whose jingling and prosaic simplicity collapses the soaring quality of what precedes it. Schmidgall (1990, pp. 78–9) points to the highly figured rhetoric of Troilus (Tro. 3.2.173–83 [1806–16]), remarking that earlier…Hermia indulged in the same verbal fioratura in a comic context which Benjamin Britten turned into a stately little duet. See . Brown (ed. 1996): The rhymed couplets…accentuate the effect. But Gentleman (ed. 1774): There could not be a prettier or more fervent set of oaths coined in the amorous stile than those uttered by Hermia with so much affectionate delicacy; but we wish the Author had not here, nor on any other occasion, changed pleasing emphatic blank verse for unpleasing unnatural rhimes.

180–3 I…loues]
Brooks (ed. 1979, p. xlv): In LLL, Rom., R2 and MND, Sh. is still patterning his diction in verse by salient use of the rhetorical schemes.…Hermia’s vow…has a series of phrases beginning identically (anaphora), and three of them of the same length (isocolon).

180–1 I…heade]
Gardette (1989, p. 85; 2002, p. 31) Hermia invokes the Ovidian Cupid, who was not blind, and who chooses by the eyes (cf. 65 and 150), while Helena at 249 invokes blind Cupid from the later tradition (Fr.).

180 I…bowe]
Wright (ed. 1877) compares Ven. 581, by Cupid’s bow. Hemingway (1911, p. 80) compares Rom. 2.1.17–21 (767–71). Clark (1930, pp. 422–3) compares Lyly, Campaspe 3.5.63–5 (2:343): Cupid payd; He stakes his Quiuer, Bow, & Arrows, His Mothers doues.

181 By…heade]
Gildon (1710, 7:316): Shakespear was acquainted with the Fables of Antiquity very well: That some of the Arrows of Cupid are pointed with Lead, and the others with Gold, he found in Ovid [Met. 1.468–72, Golding 1.565–8; see ]. Steevens (ed. 1793): So, in Sidney’s Arcadia [ed. Evans, 1977, p. 309], arrowes two, and tipt with gold or lead. Grinfield (MS 1834–63, p. 258): Gold wins love at times. Wright (ed. 1877): Compare [TN 1.1.34–6 (41–3)]. Cuningham (ed. 1905) compares Marlowe’s Hero and Leander 1.161, Loves arrow with the golden head (2:435), which Brooks (ed. 1979) sees Sh. as echoing. Kittredge (ed. 1939): For elaborate treatment of Cupid’s arrows see Lyly, Sapho and Phao, [5.1.2; 2:410–11]. Bate (1993, pp. 133–4 ): The context of this allusion is Apollo’s love for Daphne (see ); he discusses the relevance of this myth to the situations of both Hermia and Helena in their relationships with Lysander and Demetrius, especially in the light of Helena’s self-identification with Daphne (see nn. 609–13, 610). See also n. 12.

182–265 By…againe]
Chambers (ed. 1897): From this point the lyricism of the scene is enhanced by the use of rhyme. Knowles (privately) instead of lyricism proposes artificiality, suggesting self-delusion.

182–3 By…loues]
The reason for Singer’s (ed. 1856) transposing of these lines is given below (n. 183, that). He is followed only by Hudson (ed. 1880). See textual n.

182 simplicitie…doues]
Proverbial. Dent (D573): As loving (…simple) as a Dove. Thirlby (MS 1733–47): Harmless as doves [Matt. 10:16]. Walter (ed. 1964) quotes the Rheims version: Simple as doves, showing it is close to the Vulgate’s simplices sicut columbae, and suggesting Hermia is perhaps playfully ironic. But Wright (1968, p. 24) quotes Fraunce’s The Third Part of the Countesse of Pembrokes Yuychurch. Entitled Amintas Dale: [Venus’s] Doues are wanton…being meanes to procure loue and lust [1592; ed. 1975, p. 115]. Brooks (ed. 1979): In love, simplicity is the contrary of duplicity: mating for life, doves typified fidelity. For the doves who draw Venus’s chariot commentators refer to Ovid, Met. 14.597; Ven. 153, 1190; Rom. 2.5.7 (1317). See n. 345. See also 611, 769, n. 2116.

182 simplicitie]
Johnson (1755, 1): Plainness; artlessness (OED 3a). Schmidt (1875, 2), quoting this instance and 1901, adds: Innocence. Kittredge (ed. 1939): Pure sincerity. Doran (ed. 1959): Guilelessness.

182 Venus]
For the genitive of proper nouns ending in -s see Franz §199; Blake 3.2.1.2 (a). See 299, 326, 484, 515, 782, 1034, 1607.

183 that]
A few commentators are puzzled by the vagueness of the reference of that. Thirlby (MS 1747–53) asks What is that? Singer (ed. 1856) believes the allusion is to the golden arrow of Cupid (in Ovid, Met., 1.470) and so transposes lines 182–3. However Keightley (1867, p. 131): The allusion is most probably to the Cestus of Venus, which Root (1903, p. 115) finds in Martial 6.13, but more fully described in Iliad 14.214[–21]. Brooks (ed. 1979): Venus’ girdle, which endows with irresistible beauty anyone to whom she lends it. Nicholson (1885, p. 244): By that refers to simplicity (182), i.e. by truth, or true friendship.

183 knitteth]
Johnson (1755, 3), quoting this instance: To join; to unite (OED 7 fig. a). Applied to marriage by Chaucer, MerT 1391; see . Brooks (ed. 1979): Probably a traditional expression, quoting from OED Hampole’s Pricke of Conscience (1340)—body and saul togyder knyt. See nn. 699, 1994. See also 1706.

183 prospers]
Johnson (1755): To make happy; to favour, quoting Lr. 3.7.92 (2169). OED (2): To cause to flourish.

184–5 And…seene]
Steevens (MS 1773–8): Shakespeare had forgot, Theseus perform’d his exploits before the Trojan war, and so long before the death of Dido. Wright (ed. 1877): See Virgil, Aeneid, 4.584, &c. Steevens pointed out the anachronism.…But Shakespeare’s Hermia lived in the latter part of the sixteenth century and was contemporary with Nick Bottom the weaver. Anon (ed. 1886): Dido…stabbed herself on the top of a funeral pyre when the pious but paltry hero had sailed away from Carthage in obedience to the gods. Root (1903, p. 57) alludes to Aen. 5.1–[7]. Cowling (ed. 1922) quotes uritur infelix Dido (Aen. 4.68: Unhappy Dido burns), referring to the flames of love (see OED, burn, v1 1b). Partridge (1968, burn v. 2) notes the pun. Brooks (ed. 1979): It is in Virgil (iv. 584 ff.) that before she immolates herself on the pyre Dido actually sees Aeneas sailing away, and not in [Marlowe’s Dido]…, or Chaucer’s Legend of Good Women. Holland (ed. 1994): Hardly an emblem of something that prospers loves [183]. Cf. Shr. 1.1.154 (457) and 2H6 3.2.116–19 (1816–19).

184 Carthage queene]
On noun-compounds, where the first noun is used adjectivally, see Abbott §§22, 430; Franz §202; Blake 3.3.3.1 (c). See 226, 1229 (childhood innocence). Wright (ed. 1877) finds the same phrase in Marlowe’s Dido 4.4.29, 132 and 158 (1:43, 46, 47), and Beaumont and Fletcher’s The Maid’s Tragedy 2.2.33 (2:60). See also Peele, The Tale of Troy (1589; Works, 1:202, l. 483).

185–7 When…spoke]
For Chaucer’s views of the unreliability of men’s oaths, and of the false Troian’s treatment of Dido, see LGW 1254–66 (see ). Cf. also n. 257–9, and Cooper (1565) at Lysander in DP.

186–7 By…spoke]
Brooks (ed. 1979): Tender teasing, like Jessica’s accusing Lorenzo of many vows of faith, And ne’er a true one [MV 5.1.19–20 (2428–9)]. Foakes (ed. 1984): Hermia’s teasing anticipates the breaking of his vows by Lysander.

186–7 broke…spoke]
On the curtailed form of past participles see n. 121 (spoke).

188 that same]
OED (Same, a. A. 5): Appended redundantly to a demonstrative (this…that) usually expressing some degree of irritation or contempt, sometimes playful familiarity. See Franz §317; Blake 3.3.4.5 (d). See 490, 1011, 1568, 1872, 1956, 1963.

188 place…appointed]
Possibly echoing the place where Pyramus and Thisbe by foreappointment met (Golding 4.104; see ). Cf. A gorgious Gallery (1578, O3r, O4v), the fore appoynted place, th’appointed place; and La conusaunce damours (1528?, b1v), the appointed place.

190 Keepe promise]
Brooks (ed. 1979) finds a slight echo of Hero and Leander (2. 96). In A gorgious Gallery (1578, O4r) Thisbie is mindefull of her loue, and promis lately past Of fresh new loue as she sets out to meet Pyramus under the cloake of night. See also n. 1975.

191 Enter Helena]
See n. 25.

192 God…Helena]
