God cause fair Helena to prosper. Schmidt (1875, Speed, vb. 2b): To assist, to guard, to favour; used only in expressing wishes. Cf. OED, Speed, v. 6a, b. See also OED God-speed, and God, n. 8. The insertion of a comma (see textual n.) can be taken to change noun and verb to adjective and noun; hence the gloss by Neil (ed. 1878): Good speed or success.

192 faire]
Doran (ed. 1959): Beautiful (blonde only by implication, because a light complexion was the standard of beauty) (OED, adj. 1a and 6). Blits (2003, p. 36): A courteous form of address which Theseus has already used several times when speaking to Hermia and Hippolyta [4, 54, 76, 126]; he cites also 850, 1630, 1702.

192 whither away]
Schmidt (1875, 2): = where are you going? Thirlby (MS 1733–47), citing TGV 3.1.51 (1120), where the question implies haste: Blind admirers will say perhaps that she was going fast & therefore the verse runs. Perhaps they will take note of her long leggs too. Winter (2003, p. 93) notes that Helena, here apparently fleeing (or crossing the stage away from them), by telling Demetrius of Hermia’s flight (260) illustrates the game of flight and pursuit that animates the heart of the action (Fr.).

193–4 Call…happy faire]
Girard (1991, p. 42): Helena is just as pretty as Hermia and she knows it, but it is no comfort to her. K. Hall (1995, pp. 180–1): Helena’s somewhat overwrought punning reveals a very real problem.…No two women who are the objects of one man’s attention can be fair at the same time. When one is fair or desired, the other is almost as a matter of course other or dark.

193 That faire]
Deighton (ed. 1891): That title of fair which you give me. Cf. Franz §358.

194 your faire]
See textual n. The F reading you, frequent in the 18th c., was dismissed by Steevens (ed. 1778). White (ed. 1858) and Furness (ed. 1895) defend it, as does Thiselton (1903, p. 18): The antithesis between you faire and me faire strongly favours the Folio reading.

194 faire]
Knight (ed. 1839): Used as a substantive for beauty (OED, n.2 4). Cf. AYL 3.2.95 (1293). For adjectives frequently used for nouns see Abbott §5; Franz §358.

194 happy faire]
Johnson (1755, Fair n.1): A beauty; elliptically a fair woman (OED, n.2 2, quoting this instance). Deighton (ed. 1891): O beauty fortunate in attracting the love of Demetrius! Thiselton (1903, p. 18): In O happie faire, faire may stand both for fairness and fair one. Holland (ed. 1994): Both fortunate beauty and a lucky, beautiful person.

195 loadstarres]
Johnson (1755), quoting this instance: [More properly…lodestar…] The polestar; the cynosure; the leading or guiding star. OED (n. 2. fig.), quoting this instance: A guiding star; that on which one’s attention or hopes are fixed. Grey (1754, 1:43): A high compliment, and ’tis used as such by Chaucer, Skelton, Sir Philip Sidney, Spenser, and others; he quotes examples from these authors. Steevens (ed. 1778) quotes Kyd, The Spanish Tragedy 3.10.106: Led by the loadstar of her heav’nly looks, and Peele, The Battle of Alcazar 1.1.104: The loadstar and the honour of our line. Further examples from plays and poems are given by Halliwell (ed. 1856) and Brooks (ed. 1979). See also n. 749.

195 aire]
Florio (1598, Aere): A tune, a sound, a note, or an ayre of musicke or any ditty. OED (n.1 A. IV. 18), quoting this instance first: Connected succession of musical sounds;…melody. See 201.

196 More]
The Clarkes (ed. 1864): Is must be understood before more. In Shakespeare’s style of elliptical construction, he makes the plural verb are [195] do service for the singular verb is in the second clause of the sentence.

196 tunable]
Johnson (1755), quoting 1645–6: Harmonious; musical (OED 1. arch.). Verity (ed. 1893): In Elizabethan E[nglish] the termination -able, now commonly passive, was often active =-ful. See Abbott §3; Franz §124; Blake 3.3.3.4. See 1645.

196 larke…eare]
Deighton (ed. 1891): For the omission of the article before lark, see Abbott §83. See also Franz §267; Blake 3.3.4.2.2 (h) and 3.3.4.3.2 (b). Harting (1871; 1965, pp. 133, 135): With the labourer whose avocation takes him across the fields at early dawn, the lark is always an especial favourite;…Perhaps no bird has received more notice at [Sh.’s] hands.

197 When…appeare]
Praised by Strindberg (1909; 1966, p. 230) as a line without an image making an abstract time concrete and vivid:…But this is the English landscape in a verse; I saw it in Gravesend one May day: there were fields and hedges, wheat and hawthorn, and it’s in Dickens’ novels!

197 hauthorne]
OED (Hawthorn, n. 1): A thorny shrub or small tree…extensively used for forming hedges; the White-thorn. It bears white, and, in some varieties, red or pink blossom (called may). Ellacombe (1878, pp. 84–5): Under its many names of Albespeine, Whitethorn, Haythorn or Hawthorn, May, and Quickset, this tree has ever been a favourite with all lovers of the country.…But not only for its beauty…—it is essentially the May tree, the tree that tells that winter is really past, and summer has fairly begun. He quotes Spenser, Shep. Cal., Maye, 6–14, which Brooks (ed. 1979) suggests is here echoed by Sh. See also n. 817.

198 catching]
Johnson (1755, To Catch, v.n.), quoting this instance: To be contagious; to spread infection (OED, ppl. a. 2, quoting this instance first). Bucknill (1860, p. 89): The general contagiousness of disease was a prevalent opinion in the old time. Andrews (ed. 1989): Helena then goes on to play on other meanings of catch: (a) to hear, register [199], (b) to capture and make mine [200], and (c) to sing a catch or round of music [201]. Cf. OED, v. III; VIII 34, 35, 36, and n.1 14.

198 were…so]
Depending on the text adopted in 199, this is conditional: If favour were so; or expressing a wish: Would that favour were so.

198 fauour]
Hanmer (ed. 1744, 6:Glossary): Countenance, visage. Steevens (ed. 1778): Feature, countenance; (ed. 1793): I.e. her beauty. Rann (ed. 1787): Resemblance in features, air, look, speech, gesture. Halliwell (1841, p. 72): Favour is not here used…in the sense of countenance, but evidently in the common acceptation of the term…, i.e., favour in the eyes of Demetrius. Wright (ed. 1877): Outward appearance, aspect; with a play upon the other meaning of the word; he compares Rosaline’s similar quibble in LLL 5.2.32–3 (1920–1). Cf. OED, n. 1a, 8, 9a–c.

199–201 Your…melody]
Most debate concerns whether Your words I catch should be read with what precedes or follows, and whether it should be emended (see textual n., and conj., ). Q2’s omission of the comma after Hermia encourages a heavier pause after catch, linking the clause to the preceding line, and possibly prompting F2’s emendation of I to Ide. This leads to the widely adopted emendation Yours would I catch, first printed by Hanmer (ed. 1744), but claimed by Thirlby (MS 1747–53): He had it from me. This reading is defended as certain (Capell, 1783, 2.3:101) and as absolutely necessary from the context (Singer, ed. 1856); and supported by Wilson (ed. 1924): Cf. wood misprinted word LLL 4.3.244; and Brooks (ed. 1979): Q1’s error originated, no doubt, in misreading woud as word (u as two-stemmed r). Most editors who follow either F2 or Hanmer’s emended text also take 199 as end-stopped, reading ere I goe with what precedes.
Caldecott (MS 1813–33) considered restoring Q1, interpreting: I am gratified by your compliment & thankful for your good wishes. I catch this pleasing sound as I should like your excellent Qualities. Knight (ed. 1839) prints Your’s would I but defends Q1, suggesting in a note that placing parentheses around Your words I catch, fair Hermia gives an intelligible meaning.…It is in the repetition of the word fair that Helena catches the words of Hermia; but she would also catch her voice, her intonation, and her expression, as well as her words. Moving the parenthesis to follow catch, he prints this text in his 1842 edition. Collier (ed. 1842) also prints Your words I catch, but completes the preceding line with an exclamation point, and introduces a semicolon after Hermia: The meaning is, that Helena only catches the words and not the voice of Hermia. In his 1858 edition Collier recants: I am now convinced that Sir Thomas Hanmer was right.…Helena means, I would fain catch your favour, or appearance. Sprague (ed. 1896) in his text substantially follows Knight, but interprets according to Collier’s punctuation: Sickness is contagious, Oh that beauty, too, were so!—I already catch your words, fair Hermia—Oh that, ere I go, my ear might catch your voice (and so reproduce it, the speaking voice); my eye the beauty of yours; my tongue your tongue’s sweet music (the singing voice)! Wells & Taylor (1987, p. 280) substantially follow Collier (ed. 1842), interpreting: If we assume that ere I go belongs to the following rather than the preceding sentence, then Your words I catch makes the same sort of sense as sickness is catching: a statement of fact, followed by a wish, the two linked by a pun on catch. Also following Collier’s punctuation, Holland (ed. 1994): Helena is complaining that Hermia’s words are the only thing she catches from her. She goes on to enumerate what she would like to catch before she goes.

200–1 My…melody]
Kittredge (ed. 1939): My ear should catch the tone of your voice; my eye should learn to look with the glances that your eye gives; my tongue should speak with your tongue’s music, to which my ear had been attuned. Hollindale (1992, p. 50): In these two lines there are three statements. The first of them expresses approving receptivity: Helena’s sense of hearing will take in the love-charming quality of Hermia’s voice. The third expresses the desire to emulate and copy: Helena’s voice will now become, in her envious fantasy, like Hermia’s voice. Between the two, pivotally balanced, is the reference to eyes. Where does it fit into the thought-pattern? Will Helena admire Hermia’s eyes as an observer, or will her own eyes magically replicate Hermia’s? The balanced placing of the balanced phrase permits us to read or hear it in several ways—as a choice, as a confusion, or as an item in the cumulative dialogue between subjective and objective experience. See also n. 198.
Thirlby’s prediction (MS 1733–47): Some ass will perhaps read hair - locks was partly fulfilled by Lettsom (in Dyce ed. 1864), who conjectures hair…hair. For the ensuing debate, see Furness (ed. 1895).

200–1 eye…melody]
Furness (ed. 1895): I cannot believe that to Elizabethan ears the rhyme here was imperfect.…[C]ompare the Fairy’s song…, where the music is marred if the rhyme be not perfect. See 664–5. On the pronunciation of eye and -y which produces this rhyme, see Cercignani (1981, pp. 248–9, 305).

202 bated]
Florio (1598, Deffalcare): To bate,…to deduct. Rann (ed. 1787): Excepted. Cf. OED (v.2 6): To deduct, subtract; but more exactly, (7): To omit, leave out of count, except. Obs. [First instance is dated a 1611].

203 ile]
Thirlby (MS 1725–33): Forte [perhaps] I’d & vel [possibly] I’ld. Mr Theobald has it (see textual n.). Singer (ed. 1856): The old copies have Ile, a misprint for I’ld, i.e. I would. The same error seems to have occurred in a future passage, (see 552). Dyce (ed. 1864): I cannot but think that the frequent confusion of Ile and Ide is a misprint, not an idiom. W. N. Lettsom. But it certainly appears that our ancestors frequently used will where we now use would; e.g. [Err 1.2.85–6 (250–1); Ado 5.1.277–8 (2362–3)].

203 translated]
OED (v. 4), quoting the instance at 1054: To change in form, appearance, or substance; to transmute; to transform. Steevens (ed. 1778): To translate, in our author, sometimes signifies to change, to transform. Becket (1815. 1:266) demurs: To translate is no doubt to change, to remove. So we talk of translating a bishop to another see; but to translate, is certainly not to transform,…since to transform can signify nothing but to metamorphose, to change the external shape. Similarly Delius (ed. 1859): Steevens here explains to translate as to change; more accurately it is equivalent to to transfer, to bequeath. If Helena possessed the whole world she would transfer it, with the exception of Demetrius, to Hermia (Ger.). But Schmidt (1875, 1): I would give the rest, to be changed to you, i.e. if, in return, I were you. Deighton (ed. 1891) quotes 935–6 and Ham. 3.1.110–13 (1766–8): For the power of Beautie, will sooner transforme Honestie from what it is,…then the force of Honestie can translate Beautie into his likenesse.
Phillips (ed. 1969): Thus early the transformation (or hallucination) theme, which is going to involve the four lovers and—with more comic effect—Bottom, is introduced. Girard (1979, p. 191): Desire speaks here, and it is desire for another’s being. Helena would like to be translated, metamorphosed into Hermia, because Hermia enjoys the love of Demetrius. Evans (1985, p. 75): A series of transformations in a world of shadows and darkness is designated translation, a synonym for metaphor in Elizabethan English (see OED, Translation 4). Holland (ed. 1994): Helena, unlike Bottom, positively desires translation but, by the end of the play, this metamorphosis will prove unnecessary. In the wood Helena will be translated into Hermia, as the object of both Demetrius’ and Lysander’s desire. See also nn. 935–6, 1054, 247 (transpose), 1579 (transformed), 1750 (transported), 1815 (transfigur’d).

204–12 O, teach…hateth mee]
Davis (1996, p. 11) points to the figure Puttenham (1589; 1936, p. 206) calls the Crosse-couple, because it takes me two contrary words, and tieth them as it were in a paire of couples, and so makes them agree like good fellowes, and sees a second level, where Helena’s distress is multiplied by seeing itself in the mirror of Hermia’s effortless and unwilling victory. And the two levels contrapuntally, indeed ironically, rhyme in together, as only true love should.

204 looke]
Both how she directs her sight (looks upon others) and how she appears to those looking at her. Cf. OED, v. I.1 and III.

204 Art]
OED (I): Skill; but the unusual capitalization indicates more is implied. Wilson (ed. 1924, Glosssary): Magic art. Doran (ed. 1959): Carries suggestion of magic art; cf. [35, 44]. Cf. OED 1 and 11a. Brooks (ed. 1979): Address, studied conduct, referring to OED 13; both this meaning and OED 14 carry implications of trickery or cunning.

205 You…heart]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Make his heart move in whatever direction you please.

205 sway]
Johnson (1755, 3), quoting 770–1: To govern; to rule; to overpower; to influence (OED 9b, quoting this instance and 770). Phillips (ed. 1969) suggests an allusion to the moon, governess of floods (478), which sways the motion of the sea.

205 motion]
Thomas (1587, Affectus): Affection, disposition: motion: passion (OED 9a. Obs.). Doran (ed. 1959): Impulses, affection.

206–14 I…mine]
Chambers (ed. 1897): The antithetical structure should be observed, not only in the stichomuthia or balance of line against line, but also within the lines themselves. Phillips (ed. 1969): It is worth considering whether the rhymes make [the stychomythia] any more effective, dramatically, than the passage at [144–50]. Dash (1997, p. 79), also referring to 144–50: The repetition of the pattern, comic in its shift of topic, also defines and contrasts the young women. Helena pines for Demetrius, Hermia would most willingly relinquish him. Cf. in a context similar to this, Err. 4.2.7–16 (1110–22), and in a tragic context 1H6 4.5.18–21 (2131–4), 34–47 (2147–60). See n. 145–50.

206, 209, 211 I frowne…follows me]
Brooks (ed. 1979), citing Tilley (S150): Scorn at first makes afterlove the more (TGV 3.1.95 [1164]), quotes Lyly, Euphues and his England: But so extreame are the passions of loue, that the more thou seekest to quench them by disdayne, the greater flame thou encreasest by desire (2:132).

206 still]
OED (adv. 3a): Continually, constantly. Obs. exc. poet. Abbott §69; Franz §420; Blake 5.1.3.3. See 225, 972, 1386, 1457, 1999. But possibly also as Schmidt (1875, 4): Even after all that has happened or been said; nevertheless, all the same (OED, adv. 6a). See 765.

207–10 O that your…mooue]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Would that my warmest welcome and my most earnest prayers could effect that which is the prompt result of your disdain and maledictions!

209 I…curses]
St. John (1908, p. 159): Even in this part of the dialogue the shrew peeps out. Cf. 1334–5, 1360.

210 mooue]
Schmidt (1875, 4): To excite, to rouse, to awaken (OED 7a, 9a).

213 His…mine]
See textual nn. Q1’s additional syllable apparently prompted the Q2 substitution of none for no fault, but this change deprives Helena’s response of the necessary referent. The further change of folly to fault requires adding a syllable (either oh or fair) before Helena. Dyce (ed. 1864) cures the metrical problem in Q1 by reading Helen, pointing out that she is so addressed at 221, and elsewhere [799, 1162, 1197, 1199, 1279, 1685]. Holland (ed. 1994): Q1 could be left unemended if Helena is elided into is. Furness (ed. 1895) interprets the Q1 and Q2F texts: If we assume that Hermia is trying to comfort her dear friend with assurances of her enduring love, then there is a charm in this asseveration, in the Folio, that she does not share in Demetrius’s folly, which gives hate for love, but that she returns love for love; and her words become sympathetic and caressing. But if we adopt the text of Q1, Hermia’s words have a faint tinge of acerbity (which, it must be confessed, is not altogether out of character), as though she were defending herself from some unkind imputation, and wished to close the discussion (which would also be not unnatural). Most editors follow Q1.

214 None…beauty]
Deighton (ed. 1891): I grant that all you can be blamed for is your beauty. Scott (ed. 1898): None (pertains to you) but your beauty; or, as an emendation, a semi-colon might be inserted after none. Phillips (ed. 1969): By no fault of yours—unless your beauty counts as a fault.

215–16 shall…will]
Kittredge (ed. 1939): These words express a promise—shall in the third person, will in the first.

216 my selfe]
See n. 126 (your selfe).

216 fly]
I.e. fly from. For prepositions omitted after verbs of motion see Abbott §198. See n. 625 (fly…seeke), and 752. OED (v.1 11a): = FLEE 1.

217–20 Before…hell]
Interpretative comment varies according to whether my loue is taken as referring to Lysander, to Hermia’s love for Lysander, or his for her; and whether graces is taken as referring to Lysander’s personal qualities, or heavenly gifts. Thirlby (MS 1747–53): The meaning is plain. But it is very odd and liable to ridicule. Johnson (ed. 1765): Perhaps every reader may not discover the propriety of these lines. Hermia is willing to comfort Helena, and to avoid all appearance of triumph over her. She therefore bids her not to consider the power of pleasing, as an advantage to be much envied or much desired, since Hermia, whom she considers as possessing it in the supreme degree, has found no other effect of it than the loss of happiness. The Clarkes (ed. 1864) suspect a misprint of Lysander for Demetrius, but interpret the passage as it stands: Hermia wishing to console Helena—who attributes to her friend greater powers of attraction than herself—says that ere she won Lysander’s love she was at peace; but that now the dread of separation destroys her happiness, and therefore the power of gaining affection may not be so enviable as is supposed. Deighton (ed. 1891): How powerful must be the graces of my beloved one, seeing that they have made Athens a place of torture to me; i.e. since so long as she remained in it she could not marry Lysander.…Johnson, however, seems to take my love as = the love which I feel. Dey (1899, p. 454) objects against Johnson: While misery may love company, I believe we may understand Hermia merely as assuring Helena that she will soon leave her a clear field.…Power of pleasing is not identical with my love (capacity for loving). Phillips (ed. 1969): Athens is a hell to her because she is tormented by an apparently hopeless love and an intolerable choice. Foakes (ed. 1984): At first sight, [Lysander] might be thought an error for Demetrius, who has made Hermia wretched, but her paradox is that Lysander’s graces in his love, by provoking the jealousy of Demetrius and her father’s anger, have made Athens hell for her. Girard (1991, pp. 76): Hermia equates the graces that dwell in her love with hell itself, and confirms that the most singular and characteristic aspect of her passion is the suffering that it causes her; this suffering testifies to the splendidly romantic nature of her current love affair. Suhamy (2002, p. 252): A curious statement, made bitterly blasphemous by the ironical usage of the word graces. It is not very clear whether the phrase my love refers to the feeling of love itself or to the person of Lysander; the second interpretation seems more probable, owing to the presence of the pronoun he in the next line, but the overall significance amounts to the same idea,—love makes people unhappy. Knowles (privately): Just romantic hyperbole? I used to love Athens (it seemed a heaven), but by contrast with my greater love for the graces of Lysander, it now seems so completely unattractive (a hell) that I leave it without hesitation.

217–18 Before…Athens]
Abbott (§425) comments on the emphatic adverbial expression preceding the transposition of verb and subject in these lines.

219 graces]
Kittredge (ed. 1939): Delightful qualities. Walter (ed. 1964): (a) attractive qualities, (b) divine influences. Cf. OED 1a, 2a, 11b, e. Cf. nn. 744, 1998, 2069, 2182.

220 That…hell]
Dyce (1844, pp. 44–5), in support of Q1’s repeated a, compares Milton PL 1.254–5. The Q2F reading into hell requires a disyllabic pronunciation of heauen, unusual in MND (see n. 156). See textual n.

221 mindes]
OED (n.1 II): Thought; purpose, intention.

221 vnfould]
Johnson (1755, 2), quoting this instance: To tell; to declare. OED (v.1 2): To disclose or reveal…; to explain. See also n. 156.

222–3 To…glasse]
Greenfield (1968, p. 241) cites the proverb moonshine in the water (Tilley M1128; see also LLL 5.2.208 [2107]): The proverbial implication of folly in seeking moonshine helps to unify two seemingly diverse elements in the play, the lovers and their illusions and the mechanicals and theirs (see 864). Wright (ed. 1877): There is a discrepancy here in point of time. At the opening of the play there are four days before the new moon. See nn. 6, 236. Smidt (1986, p. 131): This may pass as a poetic way of indicating the trysting-time rather than a meteorological forecast, especially since it is complemented by Hermia’s parting words till morrow deep midnight [236].…The young couples…at no time make the slightest reference to the moon as actually shining.…But they do complain of darkness: [740; 1204; 1461]. It is important that the lovers should be seen, or imagined, as fumbling in darkness, literally and figuratively. They are, however, allowed a little light from the stars from time to time [1084; 1214–15; 1397]. Blits (2003, p. 189): During the scenes in the woods the fairies had the benefit of moonlight, but the lovers did not; [e.g. 434, 517, 1017]. See also n. 2107–9.

222 Phœbe]
Florio (1598, Phebea): The Moone so called because she is Phebus sister. OED (Phoebe1 1), quoting this instance: The name of Artemis or Diana as goddess of the moon; the moon personified. poet. Root (1903, p. 52): Allusions to Diana as the moon-goddess, or oftener by metonymy as the moon itself,…are largely confined to [Sh.’s] earlier works.…In this aspect, and in this aspect alone in the authentic plays, she receives her alternate names, Phoebe and Cynthia. Brooks (ed. 1979) quotes Abraham Fraunce: In heauen she is called Luna,…in the woods Diana, vnder the earth Hecate or Proserpina (The Third Part of the Countess of Pembroke’s Yvychurch, ed. Snare, 1975, p. 108).

223 in…glasse]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Mirrored in the water. Cf. Zelmane’s verses Over these brooks in Sidney’s Arcadia: In watery glass my watery eyes I see (ed. Evans, 1977, p. 326). Both these instances antedate OED’s first example (1605) of glass poetically applied to water as a mirror (n.1 8c).

223 watry]
OED (Watery 5), quoting this instance: Chiefly poet. or rhet. of natural features, as the sea and rivers. See also 539, 1017; all three uses are connected with the moon.

224 Decking…grasse]
Thirlby (MS 1747–53): I suppose Beholding her visage in her glass put him in mind of dress & ornament…& perhaps particularly of a pearl necklace or some other ornament of pearl, but by ill luck it would not fit the moon & so is bestowd upon the grass. For dewdrops or tears as pearls, see OED, Pearl, n.1 5. See also n. 384–5, and 1568–9.

224 Decking]
OED (Deck, v. 2a): To cover with what beautifies; to array, attire, adorn.

224 bladed]
Johnson (1755), quoting this instance: Having blades or spires. OED (ppl. a. 2), quoting this instance first: Having, producing, or abounding in blades.

225 flights]
Wells & Taylor (1987, p. 280) argue unconvincingly for slights = sleights, tricks. (See textual n.)

225 still]
See n. 206.

226 Athens]
This may be a genitive, and deserve an apostrophe (see n. 182, Venus), or it may be a noun used adjectivally (see n. 184).

226 gates…steale]
Taylor (1991, p. 496) compares Golding’s Ovid 4.106, 114–5 (see ). See n. 174. Citing Dante, Inferno 3.1, 11 and Vergil, Aeneid 6.127, Sasaki (1986, pp. 69–70) claims: The gate image implies the entrance to the Underworld.…These gates stand on the boundary which divides the city of Athens under the waning moon from the night world of the wood of interlunation. Leggatt (2004, p. 195): Implies a walled city. To break out of Athens is to break out of an enclosure.…[I]n Pyramus and Thisbe we encounter another wall,…One wall pens lovers in, in a city whose laws frustrate their desires; the other wall separates lovers from each other. This being a comedy, both walls disappear in the course of the action. Cf. nn. 2134–5, 2153.

226 deuis’d]
Johnson (1755): To consider; to contrive; to lay plans; to form schemes (OED 5c). Deighton (ed. 1891): Planned. Evans (ed. 1974): Decided.

227–9 And…sweld]
Wells (ed. 1967): The reference to the girlhood friendship of Hermia and Helena looks forward to the scene of their quarrel, to which it is an ironic background.

228 faint Primrose beddes]
Steevens (ed. 1793): Whether the epithet faint has reference to the colour or smell of primroses, let the reader determine. Schmidt (1874, 1), listing it with Peculiar expressions and hyphenating primrose-beds, questions: so called because flowers are the emblems of weakness? or for having a faint smell? Irving & Marshall (ed. 1888) opt for colour rather than odour: Shakespeare uses pale and faint together more than once. Compare [Jn. 5.7.21 (2627)]…. The pale colour of the primrose suggests the idea of faintness; the lighter coloured flowers look as if, in their struggle with the cold of early spring, they had grown weak and faint. Similarly Deighton (ed. 1891): Beds of pale primroses, as they are called in [WT 4.4.122 (1936); Cym. 4.2.221 (2531)], and Wilson (ed. 1924, Glossary): Prob. pale not faint-scented; but Kittredge (ed. 1939): Delicately perfumed (with a suggestion of languor). Cf. Ven. 151–2, where Venus lies on a Primrose banke whose flowers are forcelesse. Cf. n. 82 (faint).
Delius (ed. 1859) takes faint as relating proleptically to the composite primrose-beds: a bed for the weary analogous to lazie Bed [Tro. 1.3.147 (607)], or naked bed [Ven. 397]. Wright (ed. 1877) supports Delius, citing also AYL 2.7.132 (1109), two weake euils. Deighton (ed. 1891) objects: But the picture of Helena and Hermia lying out in the meadow is one that does not at all necessarily infer weariness, and the interpretation seems a very forced one.

228 Primrose]
On the origin and application of the name, see OED, n. (a.), Note at end of entry. Ellacombe (1878, p. 178): As it brightens up our hedgerows after the dulness of winter,…it is the very emblem of cheerfulness. Yet it is very curious to note what entirely different ideas it suggested to our forefathers. To them the Primrose seems always to have brought associations of sadness, or even worse. Cairncross (ed. 2H6, 1957) commenting on 2H6 3.2.60–63 (1760–3) and citing 224, 228; Ham. 1.3.41, 50 (504, 513): Shakespeare constantly associates the primrose, as here, with liquid tears, pearl, or dew. Webb (1991, pp. 45, 143) associates the flower with love-sickness, and its green-yellow colour with forlorn or uncertain love; cf. TN 2.4.112–15 (1001–4) and WT 4.4.122–5 (1936–9).

229–32 Emptying…companions]
Theobald’s widely adopted emendations (see textual nn.) are supported by considerations of rhyme, misreadings, and parallel usages. Theobald (ed. 1733): This whole Scene is strictly in Rhyme; and that it deviates in these two Couplets, I am persuaded, is owing to the Ignorance of the first, and the inaccuracy of the later, Editors: I have therefore ventur’d to restore the Rhymes, as I make no Doubt but the Poet first gave them. Sweet was easily corrupted into swell’d, because That made an Antithesis to Emptying: and strange Companions our Editors thought was plain English; but stranger Companies, a little quaint and unintelligible. To support his claim that Sh. used Companies, to signify Companions, Theobald quotes H5 1.1.55 (96), but Taylor (ed. H5, 1982, p. 97) gives: Companies different groups of associates or followers;…not, as usually glossed, companions. For the latter OED offers no evidence. The examples given by Schmidt (1874) in support of glossing Company (subst., 3) as companions, and Brooks’s (ed. 1979) citation of OED, n. 1, are unconvincing. Cf. 19 and n. Heath (1765, p. 44) in support of Theobald’s emendations quotes Ps. 55:14–15 in the translation of the Great Bible, which is also used in the Psalter and the Book of Common Prayer, claiming: The poet seems to have had in his eye the following passage…But it was even thou, my companion, my guide, and mine own familiar friend. We took sweet counsel together, and walked in the house of God as friends. Heath does not perceive irony in citing this psalm, which laments that foes are less to be feared than the treachery of an apparent friend, a point emphasized in the metrical form of verse 14: But that it was my fellow deare, which frendship did pretend: And didst my secret counsell heare, as my familiar frend.
Among those emphatically endorsing the emendations are Malone (1780, 1:115; Capell (1783, 2.3:101–2); Knight (ed. 1839); Dyce (ed. 1857): More certain emendations were never made; White (ed. 1858): Both corrections…are justified by sense and rhyme. Paleographical support is adduced by Walker (1860, 2:66) and Wilson (ed. 1924) citing sweld/sweete as an example of the common confusion of final d and e; Brooks (ed. 1979) elaborates: Q1 sweld is an easy misreading, ld for te, of swete; To misread companies (or cōpāies) as companiōs (or cōpāiōs) was easy; and as a metrical corollary, stranger would naturally be misread strange. The emendation is confirmed by the rhyme.
Not all editors agree. Steevens spent nearly 30 years wrestling with the question: in his review of Heath (1765, p. 167), he objects to Mr. Theobald’s restoring those four lines to rhime, for two very plain but substantial reasons; first, because they are unrithmetized in the old editions; secondly, because they make better sense without the rhimes; in ed. 1773, which prints the emendations, he suggests that Warburton’s retention of Q1 (in ed. 1747) is justifiable, on the analogy of a stuff’d bosom in Macbeth; in edd. 1778 and 1785 he restores the Q1 readings, but in ed. 1793 finally capitulates, adopting the emendations in deference to the opinions of other criticks. Caldecott (MS 1813–33), reading Emptying…swel’d: To seek new friends and stranger companions and glossing emptying them of their loads of secrets & heaps of advice while bursting almost with an eagerness to impart;…new kinds of friends, society, to which you are less accustomed, finds that Theobald’s readings cannot be necessary but for the rhyme; & for that neither in itself or in our author’s poetics is there any necessity. Collier (ed. 1842) defends QF by noting other parts of the scene are not in rhyme, and Halliwell (ed. 1856) by claiming that Theobald was sacrificing the sense to the ear, the participle emptying corroborating the old reading swell’d, and the comparative, as applied to companions or companies, being pointless. Cf. Bacon, Of Friendship: A principal fruit of friendship is the ease and discharge of the fulness and swellings of the heart, which passions of all kinds do cause and induce (Works, ed. Spedding et al., 1870 rpt. 1968, 6:437). Andrews (ed. 1993): Hermia’s phrasing looks forward to [499–513].

229 bosomes]
See n. 35. Associated meanings are suggested by the note in Johnson (1755): Bosom, in composition, implies intimacy; confidence; a fondness. See also 694, 701, 702.

229 counsell]
Johnson (1755, 5): Secrecy; the secrets intrusted in consulting (OED 5b). Schmidt (1874, 4): Secret and inmost thought. The Clarkes (1879, p. 544): Interchanged confidence. See 1225, 1343.

230 my selfe]
See n. 126 (your selfe).

232 companions]
For the pronunciation see n. 159.

233 playfellow]
Phillips (ed. 1969): This word suggests (as does were wont in [228], which implies continuity) that Helena’s and Hermia’s excursions to the woods go back to childhood or near it. The girls are, of course still very young. Cf. OED, citing this instance: Usually said of children or young people.

235 Keepe word]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Keep your promise of meeting me. Wright (ed. 1877) compares Lysander’s Keepe promise, 190. See also 1298–1300, and OED, Word, n. 8, quoting both instances.

235–6 starue…foode]
Thirlby (MS 1733–47) cites TGV 2.7.15–17 (990–2): Oh, know’st thou not, his looks are my soules food? Pitty the dearth that I haue pined in, By longing for that food so long a time. Noyes (ed. 1908), quoting MV 3.2.63–8 (1409–13): The idea that love is fed by gazing seems to be a favorite thought with Shakespeare.

236 till…midnight]
Blackstone (in Malone, 1780, 1:115): Shakespeare has a little forgotten himself. It appears from [5–14] that to-morrow night would be within three nights of the new moon, when there is no moonshine at all, much less at deep midnight. The same oversight occurs in [222]. See nn. 6–14, 222–3. Pye (1807, p. 47) mocks Blackstone’s objection as immaterial, since: A lover might contrive to be near enough his mistress to see her in a clear night.

236 deepe]
OED (a. 12c): Used of the intense stage of night, when nature is plunged in darkness.

237–8 Exit…my Hermia.]
Following Dyce (ed. 1857), most editors move Lysander’s words to Hermia up to precede the SD, leaving his farewell to Helena on a separate line. Holland (ed. 1994): Q1’s placement…leaves Lysander talking to Hermia’s departing back after she has left—possible but unlikely.

239 As…you]
Gill (ed. 1981): May Demetrius love you as much as you love him. Miriam Joseph (1947, p. 58), quoting this line: Zeugma, one verb serving a number of clauses, is a favorite with Shakespeare.

239 dote]
For the optative use of the subjunctive without let or may Rolfe (ed. 1877) cites Abbott §365, who says that this construction gives great vigour to the Shakespearian line. See n. 117, 118.

240 How…be]
Holzknecht & McLure (ed. 1937): How much more happy some can be than others.

240 happie]
Fortunate, lucky; as well as content, pleased; see OED, a. 2a, 4a. Cf. 5, 26, 85, 194, 745.

240 ore]
OED (Over, prep. 9a), quoting this instance: Above or beyond…; in preference to. Phillips (ed. 1969): By comparison with.

240 othersome]
OED (a. and pron. Now arch. or dial. Etym.): [The two words OTHER a. and SOME pron. or a., formerly often improperly written as one. Usually as correlative to some.]…pron. Some others. See Franz §354; Blake 3.3.2.7 (j).

241–9 Through…blinde]
Cf. Julia puzzling over the rival attractions of Silvia (TGV 4.4.183–96 [2001–14]).

242 But…that]
Deighton (ed. 1891): But that is of no avail; more commonly in the sense of that does not matter, is of no consequence.

243 will not knowe]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Obstinately refuses to know.

243 doe]
Used for emphasis, or for metrical reasons. See Blake 4.3.7.4 (a).

244–5 And…qualities]
Farrow (ed. 1972): Just as Demetrius is in the wrong to be violently attracted to Hermia, so am I to admire him. Helena knows she’s a fool but can’t help it.

244 doting…eyes]
Demetrius is particularly susceptible to eyes: cf. 256–7, 1163.

244 doting]
See n. 117, 118.

245 admiring of]
Cawdrey (1604, Admire): Maruell at, or be in loue with. Verity (ed. 1893): I take admiring as a present participle and of as the redundant preposition found in Elizabethan E[nglish] with many verbs. Scott (ed. 1898): The construction of the line is parallel to that of the preceding; and therefore admiring and doting must be parsed in the same way. They are participles with conjunctive force. On the adjectival use of the participle as a qualifier after the noun, see Blake 4.3.8.(b), quoting 1812–13. Abbott §178 notes that verbal nouns are naturally followed by of, but that in constructions such as this the verbal is treated as a present participle, and means in the act of. See 1233.

245 qualities]
Doran (ed. 1959): Gifts, parts. Cf. OED, n. 1c, quoting as its first instance Tro. 4.4.76 (2466).

246–55 Things…where]
Gildon (1710, 7:319): Whether these Reflections are not too just for one in Helena’s Condition to make, I leave to the Judicious, but as they are here divested of all Persons they are admirable.

246–7 Things…dignitie]
Green (1870, pp. 348–9) sees a parallel with the emblem Perpolit incultum paulatim tempus amorem,—that by degrees time puts the finish, or perfectness to uncultivated love, which is illustrated in Emblemata Amatoria by a Cupid watching a bear lick her whelp into shape; see Henkel & Schöne (1976, pp. 442–3). Olson (1957, p. 105): At one level, these lines express the will of such infatuates as the queen of fairy to delude themselves. At another, they represent a perversion by Helena of the belief that Love moves always to impress its form upon the base material of Chaos. Cf. Chaucer, KnT 2987–93 (see ), and Boece 2. Metrum 8. Schalkwyk (1986, p. 58) suggests: There are two ways of reading the lines.…Either such transposition is perverse, distorting the true nature of the perceived objects,…and so should be avoided entirely, or it is a quality of the mind that is to be welcomed, for it is what makes love, and by that token society, possible at all. Godfrey (1993, p. 179) perceives a sequence of musical puns: on the instrument, a bass viol; on quantity as duration of notes; and transpose as shifting key (see n. 22). Blake (7.1.1.5) quotes these lines to illustrate the transposition of the direct object to the beginning of a clause.

246 Things…vile]
Kittredge (ed. 1939): Even things that are low and worthless. Of course Helena does not apply these words to Demetrius’s qualities.

246 vile]
Thomas (1587, vilis): Of no value or account, litle worth or set by. Cf. OED, etym. and A. adj. 5. Schmidt (1875): In general used as a vague term of contempt, = base, bad, abject, villainous. See 685, 762, 1290, 1931, 1942. See also 2003 and n. 2090 (vilde).

246 holding no quantitie]
Johnson (in Steevens, ed. 1773): Quality seems a word more suitable to the sense than quantity, but either may serve. Steevens (ed. 1793) compares Ham. 3.2.167 (2036), quoted OED, Quantity 6 for the Obs., rare meaning proportion. Schmidt (1875, Quantity 2): Bearing no proportion to what they are estimated at by love. But Chambers (ed. 1897): Here used, I think, in the sense of large quantity, just as it has the exactly opposite sense in [Shr. 4.3.111 (2097) thou Ragge, thou quantitie, thou remnant]. There seems no point in explaining it by proportion as in [Ham. 3.2.167 (2036)]. Caldecott (MS 1813–33): Having no body or weight. Light in estimation. Hallam (1839, 2:389), claiming (doubtfully) a Latinism, glosses value. Hudson (ed. 1880), parallelling (doubtfully) Ham. Q2 I3r (3.4.74–6): Here quantity appears to have the sense of strength, virtue, or efficacy. Doran (ed. 1959): I.e. without dimension, therefore shapeless, unlovely.

247 Loue…dignitie]
Barkan (1986, pp. 260–1): She is reflecting upon the psychological metamorphoses induced by passion. When in retrospect the statement is applied to Bottom…it points to the interconnections of physical and psychological transformation.

247 transpose]
OED (v. 1), quoting this instance: To transform, transmute. Obs. See n. 203 (translated).

247 forme]
Johnson (1755, 4): Beauty; elegance of appearance (OED 1e Obs.). Neil (ed. 1878): A scholastic term. Cf. OED, n. 4. Philos. a. The essential determinant principle of a thing. Phillips (ed. 1969): What is decent and fitting; mannerliness (as in good form).

248–55 Loue…where]
Christopher Smart (1756; in Vickers 1974–81, 4:204): Shakespear had this picture from Propertius, [2.12.1–12]. Thomson (1952, p. 33) thinks it unlikely that Shakespeare read Propertius, but does not note that his description appears in Renaissance florilegia (e.g. Mirabellius, Polyanthea, Venice, 1507, under Amor cupidineus, d1v). Root (1903, p. 48): Except for the quality of blindness all these attributes are to be found in Ovid. Malone (in Halliwell, ed. 1856): It is remarkable that no trace of such a notion [of blindness] has been found in any ancient Latin or Greek poet; nor has it been ascertained at what period or by whom this delineation of the god of love was first given. Crane (1920, pp. 278–9 n.) cites this passage among references for the frequent debate over why Love is depicted as blind, young, naked, and armed with the bow. Cowling (ed. 1922): A medieval addition to mythology. Cf. [Chaucer, KnT 1965; see ]. Panofsky (1939; 1962, pp. 95–128) explores the history and associations of the Blind Cupid and the Seeing Cupid, citing this passage as an example of how Renaissance authors sometimes paraphrased the strictures of medieval mythographers (pp. 122–3 n.): The content…is still identical with that of the old moralizations, although its spirit is sweetly sad and emotional instead of depreciating and rationalistic. Kermode (1961, p. 215) notes that Helena is making a traditional complaint against the blind Cupid, but calls attention also to the contrary interpretation of blind Love; that it is a higher power than sight; indeed, above intellect. Amor…sine oculis dicitur, quia est supra intellectus [Pico della Mirandola, quoted by Wind, 1958, p. 56]. See also Fulton (1976, pp. 285–7). See also n. 248.
Reyher (1947, p. 58) compares the titular shepherd’s diatribe against love in Greene’s Menaphon (6:39). Brunel (ed. 1974, p. 40) compares Son. 137, Thou blinde foole loue. Brooks (ed. 1979) finds verbal echoes of the conversation on Love and Reason in Montemayor’s Diana (ed. 1968, pp. 156–7; see also pp. xlviii, 435); see .

248 Loue…minde]
Collins (1904, p. 59): Euripides says precisely the same thing of love in a passage which is, in other respects, remarkably parallel to the passage where this line occurs; speaking of its power of transforming defects into beauties, he adds, οὐ γὰρ ὀφθαλμὸς τὸ ταῦτα κρῖνον, άλλὰ νοῦς. (For it is not the eye that judges these things but the mind.) (Frag., ex Incert. Trag., clii [Tragicorum graecorum fragmenta, ed. Nauck, 1964, Frag. 909.6; ed. Snell, vol. 5 Euripides, ed. Kannicht, 2004, Frag. 545.12]). Anderson (1927, pp. 124–5): The imagination inclines either to sense or to reason; it communicates with the affections of the heart. Love may therefore be irrational, following sense, or rational, following reason. There appears in literature, accordingly, a frequent contrast…between love of the heart or mind, on the one hand, and love of the senses—desire, fancy, or frenzy—on the other.…The contrast is stated clearly in two of Shakespeare’s earliest plays: [in this line, and Rom. 2.3.67–8 (1074–5)]. It is implied in the well known lyric, Tell me where is fancy bred [MV 3.2.63–72 (1409–18)]. Wells (ed. 1967): Love is prompted not by the objective evidence of the senses, but by the fancies of the mind. Foakes (ed. 1984) cites Tilley (L517) Love is without reason and compares Lysander’s claims at 770–3 (see n.). Edwards (1986, p. 93): What Helena means by the mind is what is imagined and not real, the fancy whose obsessions cloud, distort, and deny the truth that is seen by the eye. This is the world of the dark-woman sonnets: O me! what eyes hath love put in my head Which have no correspondence with true sight! (148). The pejorative implications of Edwards’s interpretation and of the glosses of Wells and Foakes are somewhat contradicted by neoplatonic readings which elevate the divine perception of the intellect above the corporal senses. Wind (1958, pp. 61–2) hears a mocking echo of the mystic phraseology of Proclus’s Commentary on the Timaeus and the fragment of an Orphic hymn in his breast guarding eyeless, swift love. He also quotes from Beroaldus’s Commentary on Apuleius: For Plato writes in the Symposium that the eyes of the mind begin to see clearly when the eyes of the body begin to fail. Cody (1969, p. 130) parallels Ficino, ii. 1344 (In Convivium) and Castiglione’s Il libro del cortigiano, bellezza…che si vede con gli occhi della mente, translated by Hoby beautie that is seene with the eyes of the minde (printed by John Wolfe, 1588, 2O8r).

250 Nor…taste]
Q1’s punctuation ties the meaning to the rhyming couplet, but Delius (ed. 1859) points out that 250 expands on 248, and that 251 should rather be taken with 252–3. Deighton (ed. 1891): Nor has Love’s mind the smallest flavour of the critical faculty. Nevo (Transformations, 1980, p. 113): We can make no sense of the logic of Helena’s Nor hath unless we see that it precludes a neo-Platonic mind, for it cannot be said of intellect that it hath not of any judgement taste. Nor, however, can the denigratory medieval view of love as sensual appetite, a lust of the eyes, be made to sort with Helena’s conception of love’s capacity to transpose to form and dignity. Cf. also Sonnet CXIII: Since I left you, mine eye is in my mind.

250 taste]
Johnson (1755, 5): Intellectual relish or discernment (OED, n.1 6 Obs.). Schmidt (1875, 3): A flavour of something extraneous to the thing itself; a relish, a tang, a tincture. Kittredge (ed. 1939): A touch, a trace. Phillips (ed. 1969): Experience; also = a minute amount, and appropriate here in either sense. Cf. OED, n.1 3c. fig.

251 figure]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Symbolize. Cf. OED, v. 6.

251 vnheedy]
Johnson (1755), quoting this instance: Precipitate; sudden. OED (a. Now rare), quoting first E. K. Gloss to Spenser’s Shep. Cal. April 26, and this instance second: Unheedful. Schmidt (1875): Inattentive, inconsiderate. Rolfe (ed. 1877): Unheeding. [Abbott §450]. It is used by S. nowhere else. Gill (ed. 1981): Thoughtless. On the effect of the many adjectives in -y in this play see , Gordon (1928, p. 274). Cf. 1406 (Batty).

252–3 therefore…beguil’d]
Deighton (ed. 1891): For this reason that in making his choice as to whom he should wound with his arrows, he is often led astray.

253 beguil’d]
Wright (ed. 1877): Deceived. So in Genesis iii. 13: The serpent beguiled me, and I did eat. Cf. OED, v. 1. Wood (ed. 1902): Cheated (OED, v. 3).

254 As…forsweare]
Phillips (ed. 1969): As boys trying to be witty contradict themselves for the fun of it.

254 waggish]
OED (1), quoting this instance first: Playfully mischievous. Florio (1598, Frascarie): Knaueries, youthly waggish tricks. Cf. 1488, and Rosalind’s complaint against that blinde rascally boy, AYL 4.1.213 (2119).

254 game]
Almost all follow Johnson (ed. 1765): Not contentious play, but sport, jest (OED 2a, quoting this instance).

255 is periur’d]
Gill (ed. 1981): Breaks his promises (OED, v. 1d).

256 eyen]
Archaic plural of eye. Steevens (ed. 1773): Common both in Chaucer and Spenser. Delius (ed. 1859), noting that this form is used by Sh. mostly though not exclusively for rhyme, cites Shr. 5.1.117 (2498). Wilson (ed. 1924, Glossary): Rarely used by the Elizabethans except for rhyming purposes. See 754, 1163, 1979. Franz §189b, Anm. 2.

257–9 Hee…melt]
Capell (1783, 2.3:101), emending So to Lo on the analogy of Helena’s exclamation at 1219: To be deliver’d with emphasis, and meaning—this man of hail-like oaths; when he came to feel a little heat from Hermia, What follow’d then? why, Lo, he dissolv’d, for so it must be read. However, the same meaning is supplied by So: OED (conj. 10a): Accordingly, consequently. Blake (5.1.3.5 [xxii]): Thus, thereupon. See also Abbott §66. Capell may also be mistaken in paraphrasing some as little; see OED (a1 4a), quoting this instance: A certain…amount…freq. implying not little, considerable.
Knowles (privately) compares TGV 2.4.192, 200 (847, 855) and 3.2.6–10 (1451–5). On men’s faithless oaths, see also n. 185–7.

257 hayld]
OED (Hail, v.1 2), quoting this instance: Throw or send down…like hail in a storm. Walter (ed. 1964): (a) showered, (b) called (heaven to witness). The figure is continued in the next two lines.

258 haile]
OED (n.1 3) cites this instance first for the figurative use.

259 dissolued]
Wilson (ed. 1924, Glossary): (a) break faith or troth, (b) melt (OED 10a, 15).

260 I…flight]
Gildon (1710, 7:314) attributes Helena’s action to Dotage on her Lover. For Coleridge’s (c. 1818; 1960, 1:90) strictures on Helena’s ungrateful treachery, together with some rebuttals of his opinion, see . Even less excusably, Proteus betrays his friend Valentine’s elopement to the Duke (TGV 3.1.4 f. [1073 f.]) See also n. 192.

260 goe tell]
For the omission of to from the infinitive, especially after go, see Abbott §349; Franz §650; Blake 4.3.1. See 384, and also n. 514.

262 intelligence]
Deighton (ed. 1891): This information which I shall communicate to him. See OED, n. 7a; possibly also with overtones of OED (n. 5b): Applied to the communication of spies, secret or private agents, etc.

263–5 If…againe]
Gentleman (ed. 1774): Helena here manifests that childish degree of amorous weakness, which takes the foolishest steps to gratify even in part its own fond feelings. Phillips (ed. 1969): She means to make Demetrius take her to the wood with him, and is so desperate to have this time with him that she will betray her friends for it. Although the four lovers are not drawn in any depth, Helena is a consistent picture of infatuation.

263 If…expense]
Debate over whether the expense is charged to Demetrius or to Helena, and over the meaning of dear, is sometimes resolved by accepting double meanings. Steevens (ed. 1793): It will cost him much, (be a severe constraint on his feelings,) to make even so slight a return for my communication. Caldecott (MS 1813–33): Taking such trouble will prove a delightful expenditure to me. Tyrrell (ed. 1850): If I have thanks I shall have bought them dearly, i.e. by treachery. Objecting to Collier’s emendation (see textual n.), Lettsom (1853, pp. 195–6): So far from saying that Demetrius’s thanks will be any recompense for what she proposes doing, Helena says the very reverse, that they will be a severe aggravation of her pain. A dear expense here means a painful purchase, a bitter bargain. If I have thanks, the sacrifice which I make in giving Demetrius this information will be doubly distressing to me. Of course she would much rather that Demetrius, her old lover, did not thank her for setting him on the traces of his new mistress. Scott (ed. 1898): She betrays a friend, which involves loss of honour—a dear expense indeed. Cf. the opening of Sonnet 129. Gill (ed. 1981): (i) For Demetrius, it will be an effort (and thus expensive) merely to give Helena thanks for the information, but (ii) a word of thanks from Demetrius will be precious (dear) to Helena, even though (iii) she has paid a high price for it (in betraying her friend).

264–5 But…againe]
Halliwell (ed. 1856) compares the disguised Felismena’s reaction to her former lover’s request that she serve as go-between to his new beloved: But bicause she had him before her eies, whom she loved more then her-selfe, the content, that she had by onely seeing him, was a sufficient remedie of the paine (Montemayor, Diana, ed. 1968, p. 96). Delius (ed. 1859): Helena finds it a painful joy that she will see Demetrius on his way to the woods and back and that she will accompany him in his pursuit of Hermia (Ger.). Deighton (ed. 1891): But in this manner I mean to requite the pain I shall thus give myself, to wit, by enjoying the sight of him on the way there and back.

264 enrich]
Walter (ed. 1964): A continuation of the image in dear expense, but with the paradoxical turn that the loss is gain.

265 To haue]
By having. On the gerundive use of the infinitive, expressing purpose, see Abbott §356; Franz §655; Blake 4.3.1.1 (g, iii). See also 379, 530, 594, 596, 623, and 1178.

266–371 Enter…bowstrings]
Johnson (ed. 1765): In this Scene Shakespeare takes advantage of his knowledge of the theatre, to ridicule the prejudices and competitions of the Players. Bottom, who is generally acknowledged the principal Actor, declares his inclination to be for a tyrant, for a part of fury, tumult, and noise, such as every young man pants to perform when he first steps upon the Stage. The same Bottom, who seems bred in a tiring-room, has another histrionical passion. He is for engrossing every part, and would exclude his inferiors from all possibility of distinction. He is therefore desirous to play Pyramus, Thisbe and the Lyon at the same time. Staunton (ed. 1857) suggests Sh. was referring to the plays and pageants exhibited by the trading companies of Coventry, which were celebrated down to his own time, and which he might very probably have witnessed. Responding, Furness (ed. 1895): This is not impossible, especially in view of the fact…that midsummer eve was especially chosen as the occasion for a showe or watche, performed by various companies of handicraftsmen. Heare we maye note that ye showe or watche, on midsomer eaue, called midsomer showe, yearely now vsed within ye Citti of Chester, was vsed in ye tyme of those whitson playes & before, so says David Rogers, in 1609, Harl. MS, 1944, quoted by F. J. Furnivall in Appendix to Forewords of The Digby Mysteries, p. xxiii, New Sh. Soc. [1882]. Verity (ed. 1893): The whole scene is imitated closely in [TNK 3.5] (a scene written, no doubt, by Fletcher), where the morris-dancers have a final rehearsal of the piece they are to enact before Theseus and Hippolyta.

266–75 Enter…night]
Forey (1998, p. 323), comparing the festivities for Theseus’s wedding with those in Ovid for his escape from death, suggests that Golding’s translation of vulgus (Met. 7.432) as the meaner sort and every common clowne (7.548; see ) may well have prompted Shakespeare’s introduction of the mechanicals in this context. Cf. n. 14.

266–8 Enter…Tayler]
For commentary on the names and trades of the mechanicals see DP. Douce (1807, 2:285), discussing anachronism in Sh.: In the dramatis personæ of many of his plays we find a medley of ancient and modern names that is often extremely ridiculous. At Ephesus we meet with Pinch, a schoolmaster [Err. 4.4.39 SD, etc. (1321–2)];…and at Athens with Snug, Bottom, Snout, Quince, &c. Brooks (ed. 1979): In North, the artificers are the most numerous and least well off of the three classes of Athenians.…Cf. [1869]f.; see . Cf. 2H6 4.2.30 SD (2350–1): Enter Cade, Dicke Butcher [Dicke the Butcher at 4.2.25 (2344)], Smith the Weauer, and a Sawyer.

269 company]
OED (n. 6b): A party of players, a theatrical corps. See 2136–7.

270 You were best]
Hunter (ed. 1870): It were best for you (OED, Be 19). See Abbott §§230, 352; Franz §§293, 650 (b). See 351–2.

270 generally]
All who comment agree with Thirlby (MS 1733–47) that this is an affected blunder, but differ slightly in defining what Bottom means. Delius (ed. 1859): Nominally. Hunter (ed. 1870): Absurdly for individually. Wright (ed. 1877): Particularly, severally. Verity (ed. 1893): Separately. Chambers (ed. 1897), of muddled word usage: Very likely it was part of the dramatic method of Will Kempe, who was the chief comic actor of the Lord Chamberlain’s company up to 1599. Possibly an example of cacozelia, the ignorant misuse of words, which Miriam Joseph (1947, pp. 75–7), quoting 849, 895 and 1552, calls characteristic of Bottom. See also n. 849, defect.
Halliwell (1849, p. 130) argues that in act iv., sc. i [presumably 1515–52, 1727–45] Bottom is a clever humourist, and although, as throughout the play, exhibiting a consciousness of superiority, yet he is without his former absurdities. Is it quite certain that his wrongly-applied phrases in the second scene of the first act are not intended to proceed from his whimsical humour?

271 scrippe]
Grey (1754, 1:45), quoting Chaucer, Tr. 2.1130: Formerly used in the same sense with script, and signified a scrip of paper, or any manner of writing (cf. OED, n.3 1, first recorded 1617). Foakes (ed. 1984): Bottom’s mistake for script, meaning what is written down (OED, Script, n.1 1). Cf. n. 270 (generally). Although in sixteen dictionaries from 1530 to 1611 collected in EMEDD the use of scrip is only as a shepheards pouch, poke, or scrip [Thomas, 1587, Pera] (OED, n.1), it is possible that Sh. intended and his audience perceived no blunder. Thirlby (MS 1725–33) calls attention to the parallel use of scrowle at 272 and 283, and Steevens (ed. 1778) quotes Heywood’s Hobson assuring the Queen, Ile take thine owne word without skrip or scrowle (The second part of Queen Elizabeths troubles [running title If you know not me, you know no body], 1606, HP).

272 scrowle]
Johnson (1755, Scroll), quoting this instance: A writing wrapped up. (OED, Scroll, n. 2b, quoting the instance at 283). Neil (ed. 1878) cites Isa. 34:4.

273 fit]
In addition to meaning qualified; proper (Johnson, 1755, 1) or properly qualified (OED, a. 4), the word may have a specifically theatrical connotation: see n. 328.

273–4 Enterlude]
Bullokar (1616, Interlude): A Play, a Comedie. Johnson (1755, Interlude): Something plaid at the intervals of festivity; a farce (OED, Interlude 1a). See also n. 1956.

274 his]
White (ed. 1858, 291–2) believes the singular possessive to be in conformity to the usage of educated persons in Shakespeare’s day. Marshall (1982, p. 550) reads it as a telling mistake of Peter Quince, which serves to call attention to a possible discrepancy in the emotions of the betrothed couple in the earlier scene. Q1’s comma after Duke, abandoned in later editions, suggests that & the Dutches, could be spoken as a belated parenthesis.

275 day…night]
Wright (ed. 1877) compares Rom. 1.3.21 (373–4), On Lammas-eve at night. Thiselton (1903, pp. 21–2) quotes five uses of the phrase including four from Lyly’s play titles, such as Endimion (1591) and Gallathea (1592), on Newyeeres day at Night. Brooks (ed. 1979): Normal idiom…. But here the context of comic language brings out a slight absurdity in day at night.

276 Peeter]
Wilson (ed. 1924): This spelling of Peter occurs six times in present text [here and at 282, 305, 321, 819, 1729], but not apparently elsewhere in the canon. It is perhaps a compositor’s idiosyncrasy.

276–7 treats on]
Johnson (1755, 2): To discourse on. Black (1965, p. 33): Is about. OED ( v. 2a): To deal with some matter in speech or writing; to discourse.…Const. of, formerly also on, upon. See Abbott §180; Franz §500; Blake 5.4.2 On (f).

277 grow]
OED ( v. 11c), quoting this instance: To come by degrees to, upon; to arrive at, draw to (an agreement, conclusion, point, etc.). Also with on. Heath (1765, pp. 44–5): The expression [has] some propriety and humour in the mouth of a weaver, whose piece is continually growing on or encreasing till it is terminated in the last thread. Johnson (in Steevens, ed. 1773), apparently thinking of the fruit called quince: Grow is used, in allusion to his name, Quince. Steevens (ed. 1778): The phrase has no reference to the name of Quince; he quotes several examples of the same kind of expression. See Schmidt (1874, Grow, 6) for other Shn. examples. Holland (ed. 1994): Bottom may be punning on Quince’s name and the shape of quoins. Cf. 1817 (growes to).

278 to]
F1’s addition of on (see textual n.) may have resulted not from authorial or editorial revision but from an eyeslip, the compositor erroneously catching the preposition from the phrase treats on, which, in Q1 and Q2, is almost directly above.

278 a point]
OED (n.1 A. 30), quoting this instance: A conclusion, completion, culmination, end, period. See also n. 774. F4 puts appoint, to which Settle (ed. 1692) adds the Parts; see textual n. Warner (in Steevens, ed. 1778) suggests the same reading, explaining that is, appoint what parts each actor is to perform.

279 Mary]
Nares (1822; 1905, Marry): An asseveration confirmed by the name of the Virgin Mary. Kittredge (ed. 1939): Always used lightly, with little or no thought of its meaning and without profane intent. See OED, Mary 1b, and Marry int. See Franz §255; Blake 5.2.5, 8.3.3, 8.3.5. See 903, 2140. See also n. 825 (Berlakin).

279–80 our…Thisby]
Steevens (ed. 1778), anticipated by Thirlby (MS 1733–47): This is very probably a burlesque on the title page of Cambyses: A lamentable Tragedie, mixed full of pleasant Mirth, containing, The Life of Cambises King of Percia, &c. By Tho. Preston, [c. 1569]. Malone (in Steevens, ed. 1778) adds Lyly’s Campaspe (1584), intituled a Tragical Comedy, but later withdrew it, possibly because the phrase tragical Comedie is from the running title, not the title-page (see 2:304). The Prologue to Edwardes’s Damon and Pythias makes a point of the new denomination: we haue it termed, a Tragicall Commedie (1571). Wright (ed. 1877): A new Tragicall Comedie of Apius and Virginia…By R. B.…1575. Scott (ed. 1898): Sh. may here be satirizing the vague use of the term; or he may simply be exhibiting the rudeness of men who could find mirth in a sad story.…[I]n Bacon, Essays 18, Comedy seems to be the general term for Drama. Wilson (ed. 1924): Shakespeare was fond of laughing at the conventional classification of drama. Cf. Polonius’ famous list [Ham. 2.2.396–9 (1444–7)]. Probably the playbills of the age provided him with occasions for such laughter; or the title-pages of his own quartos…, e.g. The most excellent and lamentable Tragedie of Romeo and Juliet [Q2], The comicall History of the Merchant of Venice, [not the title-page, but the running title] etc. Taylor (1990, p. 62), noting that Cooper’s Thesaurus (1565) translates Fabula as A fable: a tale: an interlude or comedie, claims that this is a joke against Quince’s poor understanding of Ovid’s vulgaris fabula non est (Met. 4.53; Golding 4.64, see ). Richmond (2002, p. 111), citing Shr. Ind. 2.137–41 (291–5), notes comedy was then a generic term covering all performances, as comedians covered all actors (as in la Comédie française).
Halliwell (ed. 1856, at 1853–4) notes the ubiquity of versions of the Pyramus and Thisbe story, and suggests Sh. probably used Chaucer and Golding (see ). Holland (ed. 1994, pp. 90–2) notes that the only parallel he has found to performance by workers of a classical play is Julia’s claim to have played Ariadne, passioning For Thesus [sic in F1] periury (TGV 4.4.167–8 [1986–7]): Hence Bottom and his fellows are trespassing into an area of performance usually the exclusive preserve of university students and comparable groups of educated amateur actors or the preserve of educated authors. Their trespass is part of the comedy: nothing like a bunch of workers attempting their own adaptation of a play on Pyramus and Thisbe had ever been seen in England, let alone Theseus’ Athens. See also Stern (2000, pp. 29–31).

281–2 A…merry]
Steevens (ed. 1785): This is designed as a ridicule on the titles of our ancient moralities and interludes. Thus Skelton’s Magnificence is called a goodly interlude and a mery. Halliwell (ed. 1856) adds: A new interlude and a mery of the nature of the iiij. elements, declarynge many proper poynts of philosophy naturall, and of dyvers straunge landys [by John Rastell, 1517].

281 peece of worke]
Knight (ed. 1839): Bottom and Sly both speak of a theatrical representation as they would of a piece of cloth or a pair of shoes. Sly says of the play, ’Tis a very excellent piece of work [Shr. 1.1.253 (563)]. The expression is frequent in Sh., but Hamlet is the only other character who uses it to refer to a play; see Ham. 3.2.46–7 (1896) and 3.2.240–1 (2108). Cf. OED, Piece 7a.

283 scrowle]
See n. 272.

283 Masters]
OED (n.1 21b): A term of respect or politeness…. pl. = Sirs, gentlemen. See 359, 840, 919 (bis), 1762, 1775.

283 spreade your selues]
Steevens (ed. 1793): I.e. stand separately, not in a group, but so that you may be distinctly seen, and called over. Phillips (ed. 1969): Spread out—a nonsense, when there are only six of them. Bottom has a talent for unnecessary and chaotic organisation. Holland (ed. 1994): Bottom is probably objecting to their crowding round Quince but the sight of five people spreading out across the stage when all will answer the roll-call is comic. OED (v. 7 refl. a): To extend, expand, etc.

283 your selues]
See n. 126 (your selfe).

284–5 Nick…Weauer]
For some of Bottom’s weaving connections with Ovid see the DP.

286–7 Readie…proceede]
The Clarkes (ed. 1864): Bully Bottom, the true type of all conceited players—amateur and professional—after having first taken upon himself to dictate to the manager how he shall conduct the affair by beginning by the enumeration of the actors, no sooner hears himself named, than he interrupts the enumeration he has proposed by desiring to have his own part first cast, and then they may proceed; though he even goes on to discuss his especial part at length, before he gives them an opportunity of proceeding.

286 part]
Johnson (1755, 8), quoting the instance at 329: Character appropriated in a play.

288 set downe]
Johnson (1755, 37): To put in writing, quoting Ham. 3.2.38–40 (1886–8). OED (Set, v.1 143e [a]), quoting this instance: To put down in writing; to enter in a catalogue or account…to record.

290 A…tyrant]
Verity (ed. 1893): It is characteristic of Bottom to assume that he has been chosen to play the chief part. All through he shows himself the leading spirit of our company [269]. Phillips (ed. 1969): Both [were] stock types on the Elizabethan stage. Foster (1975–6, pp. 191–2): Bottom…knows that characters in a drama are either lovers, or tyrants, nothing else.…Bottom appears also to know that in order to change a tragedy into a comedy one has to transform the tyrants to lovers.…Indeed, this is the fundamental problem at all levels of the play, turning tyrants into lovers. See 296, 302.

291 gallant]
In F1 compositor C’s gallantly corrects the grammar of Qq (see textual n.) but a second reason for his adverbialization may have been to lengthen the text and fill in space because of miscalculation in his casting off. See n. 341 (if ).
Johnson (1755, 2): Brave; high spirited; daring; magnanimous. OED (Gallant, a. A. 5a), placing this instance alone, after the others: Chivalrously brave, full of noble daring. OED (Gallantly, adv. 3) quotes F1’s gallantly as the first instance. Collier (ed. 1842): The folio improves the grammar, but renders the sense less characteristic. Responding, White (ed. 1858): On the contrary it makes the speech quite unsuited to good Peter Quince, who always speaks correctly. But Abbott (§1): Adjectives are freely used as Adverbs. See Franz §241. See also 1636.

293 aske]
Johnson (1755, 5): To require, as physically necessary (OED 23). Hunter (ed. 1870) compares R2 2.1.159 (806), etc.

293 the]
For the preceding a verbal see Abbott §93; Franz §667; Blake 4.3.8.1 (b).

294 Audience]
Schmidt (1874, 2): Persons present, witnesses:…Especially the spectators in a playhouse (unattested in OED). Gurr (1987, p. 90), maintains that from the 14th c. on audience tended to hold the judicial connotation of a hearing, in both the king’s court and in lesser lawcourts; in LLL 5.1.138 (1869) and in MND, the royal playgoers are quite properly called the audience, in a combination of their judicial and playgoing functions. For the term auditor see n. 892.

294 looke to]
Johnson (1755, 21), quoting the instance at 843–4: To watch; to take care of. OED ( v. 21d): In the imperative or in injunctive contexts: To direct one’s solicitude to (something) as endangered or needing improvement. See also n. 843–4.

295–301 I wil…loftie]
Smith (1999, p. 281): With their intellect the audience may consign Bottom to the bottom of the Great Chain of (Social) Being, but with their ears they hear a voice that compels attention, first in booming [u] and [o:] (I wil mooue stormes: I will condole, in some measure), then in roaring [r] and blustery [ʃ]: he quotes 296–301 (To…loftie), italicising all the r’s and sh’s.

295 mooue stormes]
OED (Storm, n. 3b): A tumultuous rush (of sound, tears, etc.); a vehement utterance (of words), quoting from a 1602 translation (usually ascribed to Sir Edward Dymock) of Guarini’s Pastor Fido, M2v: [Loue],…foe to Reason,…dost mooue A thousand stormes of sighes, of teares, of plaintes. The Italian was available to Sh. in John Wolfe’s ed. (1591, H4r). A few editors, beginning with Settle (ed. 1692), emend to stones (see textual n.), but Thirlby (MS 1733–47) refers to 141, Hermia’s the tempest of mine eyes, which Bottom may be echoing, and Singer (1853, p. 28) paraphrases: I will move storms of passion in them [his audience], let them look to their eyes. Most agree with Holland (ed. 1994): The emendation is attractive but unnecessary.

295 condole]
Halliwell (ed. 1856): To lament or bewail (OED 1 Obs.). All commentators agree, except two. Schmidt (1874), quoting the only other Shn. use, by Pistol in H5 2.1.127 (625): To mourn.…Used blunderingly by Bottom [295 and 302–3]. Wright (ed. 1877): Bottom of course blunders, but it is impossible to say what word he intended to employ.

295 in some measure]
Hunter (ed. 1870): In a considerable degree (OED, Some, a. 4a). Bottom is, as usual, boasting. But Page (ed. 1897): To some degree or purpose. This is an ironically modest account of the great things Bottom intends to achieve (OED, Measure, n. 14b, quoting this instance).

296 To…yet,]
Staunton (ed. 1857) objects to the emended punctuation (see textual nn.) stating that yet means now as in 301. Wright (ed. 1877): Q1’s may be the right punctuation: yet in this unemphatic position being used in the sense of however. I.e. As to what is left however, or As to the remainder now. Cf. 2H6 1.4.63 [692] and 2.2.43 [1004]. Thiselton (1903, p. 22) compares 1897. Most from Theobald (ed. 1733) on repunctuate, moving the stop to follow rest and reading yet with the next clause. Hunter (ed. 1870) understands To the rest as Go on to the others. Brooks (ed. 1979) claims that yet is needed to introduce [Bottom’s] renewed hankering after a display in a different hero-role. Deighton (ed. 1891): To the rest seems to me nothing more than a stage direction that has crept into the text. It is so treated by Settle (ed. 1692), enclosing it in square brackets and italicizing.

296–300 I…Fates]
Bottom characterizes roles similar to that of Ercles in two parallel phrases, or…rocks. He is then inspired to develop a speech of the type suitable to such tyrannical figures; raging rocks suggest shiuering shocks, and after a brief hesitation locks presents itself, producing prison gates, which may be considered the outcome of Fates, while in keeping with the loftie style a vulgarized classical image and a garbled proverb fill out the necessary parallel rhymes to shocks and locks. Some exemplars have been suggested for the kind of rant that Bottom is emulating and Sh. burlesquing. Grey (1754, 1:46) quotes two ridiculous passages from two wretched poems cited by Persius in his Satires (1.92–5, 99–101). Halliwell (1841, p. 22) instances two passages from Studley’s translation of Seneca’s Hercules Œtæus (Seneca His Tenne Tragedies, ed. Thomas Newton, 1581 [1927, 2:193, ll. 1–4, and 2:210, ll. 18–20]) corresponding to lines 1–4 and 458–9 in Seneca. He arranges the fourteeners of the translation into eight lines of eight and six. Bottom’s effort has been appreciated. Gentleman (ed. 1774): This small scrap of jingling exclamation has great merit in its stile; its affected loftiness, alliteration and strained figures are admirably in the Burlesque strain. Chesterton (1904, p. 625): This passage is exceedingly good poetical diction with a real throb and swell in it, and if it is slightly and almost imperceptibly deficient in the matter of sense, it is certainly every bit as sensible as a good many other rhetorical speeches in Shakespeare put into the mouths of kings and lovers.…The style of the thing, though deliberately bombastic and ludicrous, is quite literary, the alliteration falls like wave upon wave, and the whole verse, like a billow mounts higher and higher before it crashes.
Theobald (ed. 1733) puts a punctuation mark, a dash, after split, lightens the punctuation after rocks, and indicates 298–300 (the…Fates) as verse by quotation marks, because he believes the lines to be either a Quotation from some fustian old Play, which I have not been able to trace; or if not a direct Quotation, a Ridicule on some bombast Rants, very near resembling it. Most editors follow, usually from Johnson, ed. 1765) on setting the lines as verse. The punctuation changes were anticipated by Settle (ed. 1692), the lineation by Leveridge (ed. 1716) and Thirlby (MS 1725–33).

296–7 my…rarely]
Boughner (1954, pp. 141–2) compares the ranting tyrant Herod of the miracle plays: Sh.’s audience was clearly expected to respond to the traditional manner of Herod, but a Herod now masquerading in the lion skin of Hercules. Economou (1990, p. 248): Bottom’s affinity for Ercles’ vein, a tyrant’s vein [302] recalls that earlier country bumpkin with a flair for the dramatic, the Herod-playing would-be lover, Absolon of Chaucer’s Miller’s Tale [3384]. See also .

296 humour]
OED (n. 6b), quoting this instance first: An inclination or disposition for some specified action, etc.. Paster (2003, p. 50): When Bottom announces to his fellow theatrical aspirants: my chief humour is for a tyrant…[h]e means both the strong desire he feels for such a role at that particular moment and the predilection for dominance that might lead him to choose it.

296 tyrant]
Harrison (1933, p. 85) suggests that Edward Alleyn is here being satirized, and later (ed. 1937) adds that he was the great tragedian and leader of the rival company, the Admiral’s men,…famous for furious and tyrannical parts. See n. 290.

297 Ercles]
Delius (ed. 1859): A mutilation of Hercules (Ger.). OED: Bottom’s pronunciation of Hercules. Malone (in Boswell, ed. 1821, at 302): A play called Hercules, written by Martin Slaughter [Slater], a comedian, was exhibited in 1595. Halliwell (ed. 1856) notes the play is mentioned in Henslowe’s Diary, where both parts are listed as new in 1595, the first premiering on May 7 and the second on May 23; both plays were high money earners, being repeated on May 27 and 28 respectively, and frequently throughout the rest of the year (ed. Foakes & Rickert, pp. 28–34; see also p. 89). Halliwell gives other allusions to this Herculean type, among them one from Sidney’s Arcadia: With the voice of one that playeth Hercules in a play but never had his fancy in his head (ed. Evans, 1977, p. 142). Koeppel (1911, p. 190) notes that Studley uses the shortened form Hercles in both Hercules Œtæus and Medea, as does Heywood in Hercules Furens (Ger.).

297 rarely]
OED (adv. 3), quoting this instance first: Unusually or remarkably well; finely, splendidly, beautifully.

297 to…Cat]
OED (Tear, v.1 1d), quoting this instance first: To play the part of a roistering hero; to rant and bluster: cf. tear-cat in TEAR-2. Dent (C171.1) records this as a proverb but his examples all postdate MND. Warburton (ed. 1747), defending his emendation of Cat to Cap: As a ranting whore was called a tear-sheet [Doll Tearsheet in 2H4], so a ranting bully was called a tear-cap. Dissenting, Edwards (1748, p. 18; 1765, p. 52) defends the original phrase as a burlesque upon Hercules’s killing a lion. Steevens (ed. 1773) gives examples of Tear-cat and tear a cat in three plays: Day’s Isle of Gulls (1606), Marston’s Histriomastix (1610), and Dekker and Middleton’s Roaring Girl (1611). Keightley (1867, p. 425): An expression of ranting violence of which the origin is merely conjectural. For some gruesome suggestions for its origin, see e.g. Moberly (ed. 1881) and Littledale (1916, 1:517). Sharpe (1935, p. 65) parallels the Cambridge play Silvanus, performed in January 1597, where the actor, supposedly mad, actually twists the necks of two cats upon the stage; see (Boas, 1914, p. 302). See textual n. and .

298 split]
OED (v. II. intr. 8; ed. 1933, †7): As predicate to all: To go to pieces. Obs.; OED cites but does not quote MND, thus avoiding the textual question, though the definition supports Theobald’s (ed. 1733) placing of a stop after split (see n. 296–300). OED quotes other examples from Greene, Beaumont and Fletcher, and Middleton. Cf. Cotgrave (1611, Mettre): Mettre tout par escuelles. To make all split; the same phrase at Escuelle is rendered (As we say) to throw the house out at windowes. Bottom appears to be wavering between the intr. use, and OED (I. trans. 1): Of storms, rocks, etc.: To break up (a ship); to cause to part asunder.

298 raging rocks]
Thirlby (MS 1747–53) and others conjecture ragged. See . Halliwell (ed. 1856) believes raging to be Bottom’s blunder for ragged rocks in Isa. 2:21. Rolfe (ed. 1877) compares Whitney’s Emblemes (1586), [ed. H. Green, 1866, p. 129, ll. 1 and 14], The raging Sea [repeated in l. 11]…shakes the shore, and ragged rockes doth rente. Cf. also Golding’s Ovid 6.93, ragged rock, and 2H6 3.2.97–8 (1797–8): The splitting Rockes…with their ragged sides. See also n. 270 (generally).

298 and]
The first objection to Farmer’s emendation of and to with (see textual n.) came from Holt White (1793; 1853, p. 30): Surely the nonsense was designed, followed by Halliwell (ed. 1856): And is…intentional, on the part of the author.

299 prison gates]
Walter (ed. 1964): Possibly the gates of Hades. The myth of Hercules tells how for his twelfth labour he descended to Hades to capture the three-headed dog Cerberus. He found Theseus bound to a rock and tore him free.

299–300 and…farre]
Cf. Chaucer, KnT 1493: And firie Phebus riseth vp so bright; see .

299 Phibbus carre]
Delius (ed. 1859): A distortion of Phoebus (Ger.). OED (Car, n.1 1b): Applied also to the fabled Chariot of…the sun. Halliwell (1841, p. 22) compares Studley’s translation of Seneca’s Hercules Œtæus, which includes Phoebus Carre (Seneca His Tenne Tragedies, ed. Thomas Newton, 1581 [1927, 2:193, l. 2]). Cuningham (ed. 1905) quotes Ant. 4.8.29 (2683), Phœbus Carre. Craig (in Ibid.) suggests a reference to the description of the chariot of the sun in Golding’s Ovid 2.144–8; see . Kranendonk (1932, p. 211 n.) compares Spenser, FQ 1.2.1.7, Phœbus fiery carre. Lawrence (1920, p. 826), unconvincingly: The lines gird at the lost play of Phaeton for which Henslowe, on January [15], 1597–8, records a payment of £4 to Thomas Dekker, its author [Henslowe’s Diary, ed. R. A. Foakes and R. T. Rickert (1961), p. 86].
