Sillars (1978, p. 125), anticipated by L’Hôte (ed. 1964, pp. 21–2), suggests Phibbus may represent Phoebe’s and so form part of a major strand of imagery of the moon and its power over fate. Cf. Ham. 3.2.155 (2024), Phebus cart. For the genitive of proper nouns ending in -s see n. 182 (Venus).

300 make & marre]
OED (Make, v.1 46b): Proverbial phrase. to make or mar: to cause either the complete success or the ruin of (a person or thing). See Tilley (M48). The antithesis is frequently used by Sh., mostly in a comic context or involving clownish characters; cf. Rom. 2.4.115–16 (1216), LLL 4.3.189 (1532–3), AYL 1.1.29–31 (32–4), Oth. 5.1.4 (3086), Ant. 5.2.276 (3527–8), Tim. 4.2.41 (1591); the closest to MND is Mac. 2.3.32 (774). Bottom’s usage is garbled in his substituting and for or (one may either make or mar, but usually not both at the same time), and in making the supernatural powers the subjects rather than the agents of the process. Antony’s use of Making, and marring (Ant. 3.11.65 [2095]) may be an allusion to a game of the same name, according to Spevack (ed. Ant., 1990, p. 204). See also n. 270 (generally).

300 Fates]
Neil (ed. 1878, at 2124): Clotho (Spinner), Lachesis (Allotter), and Atropos (Unchangeable)—daughters of Night, who over-rule the destinies of men. Fripp (1930, p. 125), citing 2084: The Parcae, the Three Sister Fates, who weave the destinies of men, make decrees which the gods cannot break, and record all that happens on tables of brass and iron, secure against thunder, lightning and ruin, and eternal; he quotes Ovid, Met. 8.452–3, 15.780–1, 807–9. Apparently Bottom is driven by alliteration to the nonsensical (not to mention dangerous) epithet foolish. See OED, Fate, n. 2, quoting the instance at 2000 first. See also 2000, 2084, 2124, 2127, and Dramatis personae p. 8 n. Parker (1996, pp. 95-6).

301 loftie]
Johnson (1755, 2): Sublime; elevated in sentiment (OED 2c).

301 Now…Players]
Chambers (ed. 1897): Bottom’s self-importance, and the way in which he divides the company into (1) Himself, (2) The rest of the players, are delicious.

302 Ercles vaine]
Johnson (1755, 9): Strain; quality. Schmidt (1875, Vein 3), quoting this instance and 1H4 2.4.387 (1344), King Cambyses vaine, adds: Style, manner of speech or action (OED, Vein 12a). OED (Ercles): Ercles vein is a stock quotation. To illustrate this bombastic style Malone (in Boswell, ed. 1821): In Green’s Groats-worth of Wit, 1592, a player who is introduced says: The twelve labours of Hercules have I terribly thundered on the stage [Works, ed. Grosart, 1881–6, 12:131]. Glossing Cleopatra’s taunting phrase this Herculean Roman (Ant. 1.3.84 [402]), Spevack (ed. Ant., 1990, p. 47) quotes J.H. Walter (ed. Ant., 1969): A glance…at the popular stage figure who raged in Ercles vein. Brooks (ed. 1979): Cf.…Herod’s rant [Ham. 3.2.14 (1862)], and Pilates voys [Chaucer, MilT, Prologue 3124], the traditional style…of mediaeval and early Elizabethan stage-tyrants. For the genitive of proper nouns ending in -s see n. 182 (Venus). See n. 297. See also 1105.

302 tyrants]
See n. 290.

302–3 condoling]
OED (ppl. a.), quoting this instance first: That condoles; expressing sympathy in sorrow. The Clarkes (ed. 1864): That the speaker thinks this is a word expressive of the most affecting pathos, is evident from his twice using it in the course of his speech. Kittredge (ed. 1939): Bottom means pathetic. See also n. 295 (condole).

306 take…you]
Johnson (1755, Take, upon 112): To appropriate to; to assume. OED (Take, v. 16a): To assume…the part or character of. to take on oneself, to put on.

307 wandring knight]
Thirlby (MS 1725–33): I.e. Knight errant. Johnson (1755, Knight Errant): A wandering knight; one who went about in quest of adventures. Craig (in Cuningham, ed. 1905) compares 1H4 1.2.15–16 (129): Phœbus, he, that wandering knight so fair. Brooks (ed. 1979): Flute (unmanly treble and all) hopes for a hero’s part like those in Sir Clyomon, Knight of the Golden Shield…and Clamydes the White Knight (1570), referring to his introduction, p. lxxxviii.

309 Nay]
Johnson (1755, 1): No; an adverb of negation (OED 1a). Often a response to a question or a request. See 695, 950, 963, 1380, 1472. Cf. nn. 847, 1348.

309 faith]
Used interjectionally as a mild oath (OED, n. 12b). See 1318.

309–10 I…co&mmacron;ing]
Halliwell (1841, pp. 73–4), comparing Tophas’s claims about his incipient beard in Lyly’s Endimion (5.2.17–22; 3:68): Flute is proud of his approaching signs of manhood, which he calls a beard.…It is scarcely necessary to observe that the female characters were at this period performed by boys. Traub (2001, p. 141): Despite the fact that adolescent boys and young men performed all female parts in Shakespeare’s company, only twice is a male character cross-dressed—and both Flute’s theatrical portrayal of Thisbe in [MND] and Falstaff’s unwilling female impersonation in [Wiv.] make them the comic butt of a joke. Shakespeare depicts male characters as uncomfortable descending into femininity.

311–12 Thats…will]
Many comment on various aspects of young males playing female roles before the Restoration. Johnson (ed. 1765): This passage shews how the want of women on the old Stage was supplied. If they had not a young man who could perform the part with a face that might pass for feminine, the character was acted in a mask, which was at that time a part of a Lady’s dress so much in use that it did [not (correction made in the 1773 ed.)] give any unusual appearance to the Scene: and he that could modulate his voice in a female tone might play the woman very successfully. See also Furness (ed. 1895). Drake (1817, 2:221): Italy and France long preceded us in the introduction of women on the theatric boards; for Coryate writing from Venice in 1608, and describing one of the theatres of that city, says, .…I observed certain things that I never saw before; for I saw women act, a thing that I never saw before. [Coryate’s Crudities, 1611, p. 247]. Halliwell (ed. 1856) has a long account of the subject.

311 all one]
Johnson (1755, One n. 4 ): The same thing, quoting AYL 3.5.133 (1907). Schmidt (1875, One 2): = the same (OED, One, 13b; All adv. 5b). Knowles (privately): The sense is, more accurately, it’s no matter, there’s no difficulty, don’t worry.

311 Maske]
Linthicum (1936, pp. 271–2): Stowe tells us that Woman’s maskes…were first devised and used in Italy by Courtezans, and from thence brought into France,…and from thence they came into England about the time of the Massacre of Paris [1572]. [Stowe’s Annales…cont’d by Edmund Howes, 1631, p. 1038.] But English women were wearing masks in 1554…and [they] are alluded to in Mary Magdalene, 1566 [1558]. OED distinguishes between a mask worn for disguise (Mask, n.3 1a) as for Flute here, and for protection (1b), quoting TGV 4.4.153 (1972), her Sun-expelling Masque. Foakes (ed. 1984): Autolycus carries Masks for faces among his pedlar’s ware in WT 4.4.221 [2047].

312 small]
Cotgrave (1611, Claironner): To squeake, squeale; speake shrilly, smally, or with an effeminate voice. Halliwell (ed. 1856): Low, soft, feminine. Wright (ed. 1877): In as thin and clear a voice. Phillips (ed. 1969): Piping. Evans (ed. 1974): High-pitched. Douce (1807, 1:52) points out that when Slender says she has browne haire, and speakes small like a woman [Wiv. 1.1.47–8 (48–9)], the real humour…consist[s] in Slender’s characterizing Ann Page by a property belonging to himself, and which renders him ridiculous. The audience would naturally smile at hearing him deliver the speech in an effeminate tone of voice. This observation may be applied to Flute. Delius (ed. 1859) compares Bottom’s little voice [314]. Other parallels include: White (ed. 1858) Ant. 5.2.219–20 (3462–3), of Cleopatra’s fears of being mimicked in a Roman triumph; Wright (ed. 1877) Chaucer, MilT. 3360, of Absolon, the would-be courtly lover; Cuningham (ed. 1905) Lr. 5.3.273–4 (3236–7), of Cordelia; Craig (in ibid.) Golding’s Ovid 2.465, of Cygnus, and North’s Plutarch (ed. 1899) 9:111, of Cleopatra; Brooks (ed. 1979) Chaucer, GP 688, of the Pardoner.

313 And]
Johnson (1755, 2): And sometimes…seems a contraction of and if. OED (conj.1 C. 1a): If. See Abbott §101; Franz §564; Blake 5.1.1 (iv). See 337, 1768. See also nn. 345, 808, 1996.

314 monstrous]
Johnson (1755): Exceedingly; very much. A cant term. OED (a. 8b), quoting this instance first: Quasi-adv. in the sense: Exceedingly, wonderfully, mighty. Johnson (ed. 1894): Minute. Cf. [2023: The smallest monstrous mouse]. Or…tremendously. Walter (ed. 1964): (a) enormously, (b) unnaturally, (c) extremely. For the adjective used adverbially, see Franz §241, and as an intensifier §382b. See Blake 5.1.3.1 (iii). Cf. AWW 2.1.184 (795). Forey (1998, p. 327) notes that monstruous or monstrous is a favorite word of Golding’s (eg. To the Reader 18, 1.548, 4.67; see , , ). See also 918.

314 monstrous little]
Chambers (ed. 1897): Another comic Oxymoron. Miriam Joseph (1947, p. 135): Synoeciosis, a composition of contraries, stimulates attention by the seeming incompatibility of the terms it unites. Also known as contrapositum, conjunctio, commistio; see Sonnino (1968, p. 61). See 2023.

314 Thisne, Thisne]
Hanmer (ed. 1744) emends to Thisby, Thisby. See textual n. Clark & Wright (ed. 1863): It may be questioned whether the true reading is not thisne, thisne; that is, in this manner, a meaning which thissen has in several dialects. OED (Thissen adv.): In this way or manner.…(Some so understand Bottom’s Thisne in [MND 313], but the earliest quotation is from before 1652. A handful follow this conjecture, but most agree either with Schmidt (1875): Bottom’s blunder for Thisbe, or Watt (ed. 1906): [Bottom] may be speaking as Pyramus and using a pet name. White (ed. 1883) alone emends to Listen, listen. See also n. 270 (generally).

315–16 my…deare]
For the transposition of adjectives see Abbott §419. Kranendonk (1932, p. 215) compares Spenser, FQ 3.6.54.6–7, her louer deare, Her louer deare, her dearest Marinell, noting the archaic habit of reversing noun and adjective. See 898.

320–3 Robin…mother]
Seng (1967, p. 152): Tailors are continually ridiculed in Renaissance literature for lacking manliness; so…Robin Starveling…is assigned to play the role of Thisby’s mother in the rustics’ play.

322–6 Robin…Thisbies father]
Theobald (ed. 1733) attributes the absence of the lovers’ parents from the interlude later performed to Sh.’s double Forgetfulness. Capell (1783, 2.3:102) asserts Sh. showed good judgment in surprising the audience with unexpected changes. Cf. Maginn (1837, p. 372 n.). Steevens (ed. 1778): No part of what is rehearsed is afterwards repeated, when the piece is acted before Theseus. Furnivall (Player, [1879], p. 428): The change [of the casting of his tailor for Thisby’s mother to Moonshine] was due to Quince’s second thoughts and Bottom’s suggestion at the rehearsal, [857–72]. Wilson (ed. 1924) explains the changes according to his general theory of successive revisions of MND. Kittredge (ed. 1939): The rôles of the three parents were omitted when the interlude was revised to meet the criticisms of Bottom and others. See [821–883]. Wells (ed. 1967): A realistic explanation should not be sought. Petronella (1978, p. 6): To give Tom Snout the role of Wall in place of the originally assigned part of Pyramus’s father is to make obvious the idea that the hard, unchangeable wall is no more an obstacle than is Pyramus’s father himself, who symbolizes any father (Egeus, the father of Hermia, included) given to obstructing young love. And to give Robin Starveling the role of Moonshine in place of his original assignment (Thisbe’s mother) is to underscore in a play that is filled with symbolic moonlight (suggestive of fruitfulness and change) that productive change will have its say. Speaking of both the Interlude and Hamlet’s Mousetrap, Hodgdon (1991, p. 18): The resulting performances [of both] depend on a view of the play as a flexible, unfixed entity, open to a process of adaptation and cocreation in the theater as well as in the mind. Stern (2000, pp. 33–4) discusses Quince’s role as manager or devysor, and notes that the manager had the power to alter the play even if he were not its author, and that there are parallels in Turnop in John a Kent, and in a Mr. Gibson in the records for Kent for the making of revisions privately, as evidently Quince has done between rehearsal and performance. See also n. 895–917.

326 Pyramus]
For the genitive of proper nouns ending in -s see n. 182 (Venus).

326 my selfe]
For the spelling, see n. 126 (your selfe).

327 Lyons]
In Ovid it is a lioness who frightens Thisbe, but Pyramus sees only the beast’s spoor, and apostrophizes the male animal (see Golding 4.131 and 139, , and Chaucer LGW 829 and 842, ). Anon (1900, n.p.): The adoption of the male brute undoubtedly afforded opportunities for wild fun, for boisterous uproarious mirth. van Emden (1975, p. 201) notes that A gorgious Gallery (1578, O4v) in conformity with the whole P. et T. tradition, makes the creature masculine.

328 fitted]
OED (Fit, v.1 6a): To make fit or suitable; to make ready, prepare. Const. for, to.… Deighton (ed. 1891): The cast of the play is now complete. Verity (ed. 1912): Suitably arranged. Kittredge (ed. 1939): Fitted out, well equipped. Although OED does not assign a special dramatic sense to fit (273) or fitted (328, 1862), these words may be playhouse terms, meaning suitable for a role, and cast with suitable actors for the various parts. Thirlby (MS 1733–47) refers to Shr. Ind. 1.86–7 (96–7), but sure that part Was aptly fitted, and naturally perform’d. See also Jonson in Bartholmew Fayre 5.3.137 (6:121), All fitted, and in Epicoene 5.3.2–3 (5:256), I have fitted my Diuine, & my Canonist. See 273, 1862.

329–46 Haue…Nightingale]
As an example of the practitioners of new modes of criticism who fall into a polemical style of criticism that becomes either impenetrable or absurd Foakes (1994, pp. 69–70) quotes 329–46, and comments: This delightful episode, in which Bottom instantly changes his heroic roar for the men [296–300] into a cooing roar for the ladies, is treated by poststructuralist critics as exemplifying a deep anxiety about the instability of human identity, quoting Kavanagh (1985, p. 154): This dialogue functions as a kind of internal commentary on Shakespearean ideological practice. The problematic of proto-professional ideological production denied autonomous political weight in a society struggling to preserve the hegemony of an aristocratic class-ideology is here displayed in order to be ridiculed. Shakespeare’s artisans pose the issues quite clearly in their discussion: for us to assert an effective ability to manipulate their sense of reality…would be an unacceptable usurpation of ideological power, possibly punishable by death. Cf. also the comments in n. 339–43.

329 part]
Besides meaning role, as at 286, 297, etc., part also means the words assigned to or spoken by an actor in such a character; hence, a written or printed copy of these (OED, n. 9a. Theatr.). Stern (2004, p. 123): The part did not relate strongly to the play in narrative terms, for the full story is not clear from looking at a part. Each actor received what he was to say and a cue; to him, the play will have consisted of his role and the few words that led into it. See 360. See also n. 913.

329–30 if it bee]
Abbott (§298): Be is often used (after though, if, and other words that often take the subjunctive) without having the full force of the subjunctive. See Blake 4.2.2.1. See 1361.

329 it]
Thiselton (1903, p. 23) alone: [The omission of it in F1] is probably intentional: see Abbott 399, 400, 402, and 404. See textual n.

330 am…studie]
OED (Study, n. 6b), quoting this instance first: Theatr. The action of committing to memory one’s part in a play. Hence, to have or be a quick, slow, etc. study, to be quick, slow, etc. in learning by heart. Thirlby (MS 1725–33) compares Ham. 2.2.540–3 (1580–2). Steevens (ed. 1778): Study is still the cant term used in a theatre for getting any nonsense by rote. Malone (in Boswell, ed. 1821): Steevens wrote this note to vex Garrick, with whom he had quarrelled. Study is no more a cant term than any other word of art, nor is it applied necessarily to nonsense.

331–2 You…roaring]
Grinfield (MS 1834–63, p. 262): An apt hit for extempore ranters. Verity (ed. 1893): Really the lion has eight lines to speak when the interlude is performed in Act V. But that was one of the changes made after the rehearsal in Act III. sc. I.

331 extempore]
Johnson (1755, 1), quoting this instance: Without premeditation; suddenly; readily; without any previous care or preparation (OED, adv. 1). F1’s unique reading extemporie is not recorded in OED as a variant form: it may be a compositorial error, or it may represent a blunder by Quince as he tries to employ a sophisticated expression.

333 Let…to]
Irving & Marshall (ed. 1888): This touch is a masterly piece of characterization. In making Bottom anxious to play nearly every part of the cast, Shakespeare was not satirizing professional actors so much as the rude and vulgar amateurs, who represented the characters in the Interludes and Entertainments which were performed at village festivals, or in the homes of the nobility. Wedgwood (1964, p. 190), as evidence of Sh.’s continuing appeal, cites Edward Norgate, who, writing to his cousin from Berwick over two days in June of 1639 while serving with King Charles I’s army, complains: Mr. Weckherlin, who plays Pyramus and Thisbe and the Lion too, has sent away two packets and left my letter behind, so that I am fain to add to the heap to make amends for the delay. (Calendar of State Papers, Domestic Series, 1639, p. 272). Phillips (ed. 1969): Bottom’s sublime self-confidence and naïve longing to hog the limelight are among his most engaging traits. He is like the small child who wants to be Robin Hood and the Sheriff of Nottingham. See 313, and Johnson (ed. 1765) at 266–371.

333 that]
Abbott (§283): So before that is very frequently omitted. See Franz §571; Blake 5.3.2.1 (c). See 335, 338, 400, 646.

333–4 I…good]
OED (Heart, n. 54g), quoting this instance first: To do one’s heart good: to make one feel better, gladdened, strengthened. Proverbial according to Dent (G320.1), but not in Tilley.

335–6 I…againe]
The Clarkes (ed. 1864): Not only does Bottom propose to play every part himself, but he anticipates the applause, and encores his own roar. Webb (1996, p. 109) notes the emphasis given to the lion’s attribute of terrifying roaring (cf. 2H6 3.1.19 [1313]).

335 that]
See n. 333.

337–9 And…all]
For a similar event in 1594 that Sh. may be glancing at here, see Malone’s (ed. 1790) note at 841–2.

337 And]
If; see n. 313.

337 terribly]
Evans (ed. 1974): Terrifyingly. Andrews (ed. 1989): Quince means terrifyingly, but the more usual meaning [badly] is unintentionally applicable as well. See OED (adv. 2a), first instance 1604: Badly.

338 fright…Ladies]
Verity (ed. 1893): Perhaps this was or became a proverbial joke; he refers to Jonson’s Masque of Augurs (1622) where performing bears are to be allowed if they would…not fright the ladies (Works 7:634), and to a similar passage in TNK 3.5.34–5 (1624–5).

338 that]
See n. 333.

339 shrike]
OED ( v.) lists this separately but with the same meaning as shriek. Honigmann (1965, p. 88): Shrike…is a Shakespearian spelling.

339–43 and…hang vs]
Plasse (1992, p. 34): That the mechanicals should mention three times in five lines the possibility of being hanged as a result of their performance suggests that their own corporeal vulnerability before an audience which has the power to express such a fatal sort of disapproval is just as much on their minds as the possibility that they might fright the ladies. The mechanicals’ urgent desire to please their royal audience and their fear of punishment, moreover, reflect the marginal, suspect, and legally precarious position occupied by Elizabethan actors. Mangan (1996, p. 166) agrees, but does not quite believe that the death penalty would actually be incurred.…Yet the actors’ fears may not have sounded totally misplaced to an Elizabethan audience, who would have been aware…that plays on the London stage which caused offence to those in power would call down upon dramatists and actors the wrath of the Privy Council. On the other hand could Sh.’s exaggeration of their fear represent an attempt at humor in this comedy? See n. 329–46.

339 were]
Page (ed. 1897): Used conditionally for would be, comparing Mac. 1.7.1 (475), If it were done. See Abbott §301; Franz §173; Blake 4.2.9 (c).

340 euery…sonne]
Proverbial (Tilley M1202). OED (Mother’s son 1) citing this instance: A man. Chiefly in phr., every mother’s son. See 884. Montrose (1995, p. 69), unconvincingly: Like their companion Bottom in his liaison with Titania, the mechanicals are collectively presented in a childlike relationship to their social superiors. They characterise themselves, upon two occasions, as every mother’s son [here and at 884]; however, they hope to be made men by the patronage of their lord, Duke Theseus. Montrose may be misreading made men. See n. 1764–5.

341 if]
On F1’s compositor’s addition of that as a means of filling space, in the same line that he expands the normal SP for Bot. to Bottome., see n. 291.

341 you should]
Thirlby (MS 1733–47): Why you? why not we? Idem (MS 1747–53): I. See conj., .

342 fright…wits]
Proverbial; Dent compares Tilley (W583).

342 they]
Johnson (ed. 1894), alone, though Foakes (ed. 1984) seems to concur with this interpretation (see n. 343, discretion): i.e., those in authority, not the ladies.

343 no…but]
For but in the sense of except following a negative comparative (no more) see Abbott §127; Franz §225. See OED, But, C. conj. 5. See 1583–4, 2212.

343 discretion]
Schmidt (1874, 2): Becoming regard, consideration. Barnett (1887, p. 13): Option. Deighton (ed. 1891): Choice. Page (ed. 1897): Used in the sense of consideration, with an unconsciously grotesque reference to the meaning of good sense or right judgment. Foakes (ed. 1984): Bottom means they would have no choice (discretion = the power of a court of justice to decide a punishment [see OED, n. 4b]), but his confused way of putting it suggests they would have no better judgement or discrimination than to hang them. See also n. 2033.

343–4 aggrauate]
Thirlby (MS 1725–33) is the first to compare Mrs Quickly’s similar blunder in 2H4 2.4.162 (1184). Halliwell (ed. 1856) compares Jonson’s Cynthia’s Revels 2.4.51 (4:78): You did so aggravate the jest withal. Compare also The Case Is Altered 5.6.16 (3:174). Delius (ed. 1859): Bottom mistakes the word: he means mellow, soften (Ger.). Barnett (1887, p. 13): Accommodate, or diminish. Irving & Marshall (ed. 1888): Moderate. Johnson (ed. 1894): In Bottom’s language, make lower than usual. Wood (ed. 1902): Perhaps he means attenuate, make thin. Gill (ed. 1981): Make worse; Bottom really wants a word like moderate (= control). See also n. 270 (generally).

344, 345 roare you]
Roar for you. OED (You, pron. 7): Used with no definite meaning as indirect object (ethical dative), quoting the instance at 344 first, but, quoting F1, omitting the second you (see textual n.). See Abbott §220; Franz §294; Blake 3.3.2.1 (a). See n. 345 (you). See also 829.

345 sucking doue]
OED (Sucking, ppl. a. 2b), quoting this instance first: Of a bird: That is still with its mother. Now chiefly in sucking dove, echoed from [345]. Delius (ed. 1859): Having in mind a sucking lamb, he really means a young dove (Ger.). Bailey (1866, 2:198–9): Utterly nonsensical…. The blunder…may be rectified by striking out a single letter from dove, leaving…doe, comparing 2H6 3.1.71 (1365), sucking Lambe, or harmlesse Doue. Johnson (ed. 1894): A portmanteau phrase, suggesting the combined gentleness of the dove and the sucking-lamb. Cuningham (ed. 1905): Must refer to the manner in which young doves are fed. Craig (in Ibid.) compares AYL 1.2.91–4 (255–60), Heere comes Monsieur the Beu. Ros. With his mouth full of newes. Cel. Which he will put on vs, as Pigeons feed their young, and remarks: i.e. with bill placed inside bill.…Hence the young pigeon alone looks like a sucking animal. Smith (1976, pp. 152–3): Shakespeare…recalled a slip made by a professional actor on a public stage; he quotes Robert Chamberlain’s A New Booke of Mistakes (1637, D2r), where among other actors’ blunders there is Like to the harmlesse Lambe, or sucking Dove (cf. 2H6 3.1.71 [1365]), and continues: What more natural than that Shakespeare should recall a slip made by an actor in his own play and put it to use in creating his immortal Bottom? See also n. 270 (generally). Brooks (ed. 1979): Bottom confuses the sitting dove (Columbae…cum sedent, Erasmus, Adagia) and the sucking lamb [1 Sam. 7:9]; he cites also Tilley, D572, 573; L33, 34. Shaheen (1993, p. 94) cites also Matt. 10:16 (see n. 182, and cf. 611), and refers to Tilley L35 and L37 for variations on sucking lamb.

345 you]
White (ed. 1858) alone explains F1’s omission of the pronoun as not an error: This form of the expression having occurred immediately before, its modification gives a colloquial ease to the dialogue which could hardly be the result of accident. See textual n. See n. 344, 345.

345 and]
Steevens (ed. 1793): As if. See OED, conj.1 C. 3, quoting this instance. See Franz §564; Blake 5.1.1 (iv). Thirlby (MS 1733–47) cites 2H4 2.4.108–9 (1134–5), and twere an aspen leafe, and Tro. 1.2.126 (281), and twere a clowd in Autumne. See also n. 313.

345 any]
Johnson (1755, 2): Whatsoever (OED, a. 1). Page (ed. 1897): Observe the intensive force of this word: any nightingale whatever, comparing Cym. 1.4.61 (374), any, the rarest of our Ladies. See Franz §352.

345–6 Nightingale]
Spenser declares the reputation of the bird in Shep. Cal. November 25, The Nightingale is souereigne of song. See n. 664 (Philomele).

347 You…Piramus]
Holland (ed. 1994): In many productions, Quince’s abrupt statement leads Bottom to throw down his part and start to walk out in a display of theatrical temperament at which Quince has to woo and flatter him back.

348 sweete fac’t]
Halliwell (ed. 1856): Good-looking, quoting Err. 5.1.419 (1910), a sweet-fac’d youth. Craig (in Cuningham, ed. 1905) cites the same phrase from Marlowe’s The Jew of Malta 4.2.40 (1:315). See OED, Sweet, a. C. 1c, quoting this instance first.

348 proper]
OED (a. A. 9): Of goodly appearance, good-looking, handsome.

348–9 as…day]
Proverbial (Tilley S967). See OED, Summer’s day b, quoting this instance first. However, ODEP gives Lyly, Mother Bombie (1594) 1.3.44–5 (3:178) as preceding MND.

348 shall]
May. See Abbott §315; Franz §609.

349 gentlemanlike]
Harrison (ed. 1937): This compliment, above all others, fetches Bottom. Muir (1954, p. 145) suggests the influence of A gorgious Gallery (1578, P2r), where the dying Pyramus is called The Gentilman. See also 982, where Titania calls Bottom this gentleman.

350 must needes]
Johnson (1755, Needs): Necessarily; by compulsion; indispensably. OED (Needs, adv.): Of necessity, necessarily. c. Directly following the vb., in must needs. See Abbott §25; Franz §237. See 1143.

351 beard]
See n. 354–7.

351–2 were I best]
See n. 270.

353 what you will]
Proverbial (Dent W280.5). See OED, What, C. I. pron. 4a (a): Anything that, quoting the title Twelfe Night, Or what you will.

354–7 I…yellow]
Johnson (ed. 1765): Here Bottom again discovers a true genius for the Stage by his solicitude for propriety of dress, and his deliberation which beard to chuse among many beards, all unnatural. Steevens (ed. 1773) refers to his note (ed. 1773, 2:106, n. 8) on MM 4.2.175 (2041) for this custom of wearing coloured beards, where he says: It was the custom to die beards, quoting these lines from MND, and comparing Lording Barry’s Ram Alley (1611), What colourd beard comes next by the window? A black man’s, I think. I think, a red; for that is most in fashion. (The text is slightly abbreviated. See 1611, sig. B3r; ed. C. E. Jones, 1952, ll. 216–18.) Gentleman (ed. 1774): This circumstantial enumeration of beards, seems to be a satirical stroke at the fashionable attachment in Shakespeare’s days to that superfluous ornament of the face much venerated in some nations at this time. Halliwell (ed. 1856, at 351) gives many contemporary instances of the popularity of coloured beards. The Clarkes (ed. 1864): It was the custom to dye the beard of various colours; and Bottom’s imagination runs wild in fancying the most flaringly unfit ones for the purpose. But his beard, and his looks, and his appearance, are the great point to be debated. Moberly (ed. 1881): The beards of which Bottom speaks are not the right valances for a stage-lover, seeing that a little yellow beard is the same as a Caine-colourd beard [Wiv. 1.4.23 (420)], a brown one would be the Judas colour [AYL 3.4.8 (1718)]; and in general the colours here mentioned [are] those of the villain of the play. Wilson (ed. 1924): Bottom, the weaver, rattles off the names of various dyes familiar to him in his craft. Foakes (ed. 1984): Bottom has to be clean-shaven, for later he acquires an ass’s beard and becomes hairy about the face [1534]. Donaldson (1985, p. 13), quotes Chaucer’s description of Sir Thopas’s beard (Thop. 730–1), and comments: This bright saffron bushiness may have influenced Bottom’s consideration of the appropriate beard to assume for the role of Pyramus. Holland (ed. 1994) calls attention to Cambridge College inventories of play costumes [which] listed numerous beards of different colours, in Alan H. Nelson, ed., Records of Early English Drama: Cambridge (Toronto, 1989), 1:171, etc. For general information about theatrical beards see Dessen & Thomson (1999, pp. 22–3).

354 discharge]
Johnson (1755, 9): To perform; to execute. Wright (ed. 1877): It appears to have been a technical word belonging to the stage. It is not, however, given in OED in this special sense, but see v. 11: To acquit oneself of, fulfil, execute, perform (a charge, office, duty, trust, function, etc.), quoting 2008. Thirlby (MS 1733–47) compares Cor. 3.2.105–6 (2211–12) and Tmp. 2.1.254 (948). See 1754, 2008, 2143.

354–6 your…your]
Johnson (1755, 2): Used in an indeterminate sense, quoting MM 4.2.43–7 (1896–1900). OED (poss. pron. 5b), quoting the instance at 843: Used with no definite meaning, or vaguely implying that you know of, corresponding to the ethical dative you (you 7). Bottom’s repetition of your five times is like Lepidus’s in Ant. 2.7.26–7 (1363–4) about which Abbott §221 says: It indicates a colloquial vulgarity which suits the character of Lepidus; he compares Ham. 4.3.21–4 (2686–9). Blake (3.3.4.5 [c]): It is particularly used by fools. Wales (1995, pp. 310, 313, 315), designating your generalizing YOUR YOUR2: YOUR 2 generic statements or gnomic propositions are undoubtedly more vivid or immediate, more colloquial than those with the articles.…It is perhaps significant that the earliest examples I found in Shakespeare (7 in A Midsummer Night’s Dream, 1595) are used by Bottom the comic weaver. In many plays of the period YOUR 2 is associated with ordinary skilled and unskilled trades like the apprentice, carrier, nurse, captain and simply the Clown: the notoriously slowwitted workman or rustic. See 358, 843, 1544.

354 straw colour]
Johnson (1755, Strawcoloured), quoting this instance: Of a light yellow. See OED Straw colour, quoting this instance. Linthicum (1936, p. 45): The yellow of straw was fashionable in England by 1578, as many wardrobe accounts, including Queen Elizabeth’s, testify.

355 Orange tawnie]
OED (a. A.), quoting the instance at 943 first: Of a dull yellowish brown colour; tan-coloured or brownish-yellow with a tinge of orange. Linthicum (1936, p. 45): Orange-tawny, known in England by 1523, was a gay colour, usually associated in the drama with courtiers.

355 purple in graine]
Johnson (1755, Grain 11): Died or stained substance, quoting Spenser, Epithalamion 228, crimson dy’d in grain. Schmidt (1875, Purple): 1) Violet…2) Red; used of blood. Idem (1874, Grain, 5): in g. = innate, natural, not factitious (used of colours). Wright (ed. 1877): The dye obtained from the kermes,…an insect. Barnett (1887, p. 14): In grain came finally to mean a fast or fixed colour. Verity (ed. 1893): Red; cf. mustachio-purple-hued = having red mustaches [1H4 2.1.75 (710–11)]. Foakes (ed. 1984) neatly sums up: Fast-dyed crimson. Purple was still associated with red, and especially the colour of blood, as in the purple blood of 3H6 2.5.99 [1237], and it is an absurd colour for a beard. Donaldson (1985, p. 13) compares Chaucer’s Sir Thopas, whose scarlet in grayn [727] complexion may have suggested purple in graine. See . See 544, 985, 1125.

356 french…colour]
OED (French crown), quoting this instance: The English name for the French coin called ecu, escu. Wright (ed. 1877): The colour of the gold coin of that name.

356–7 perfit]
OED (Perfect, a. B. 5d), quoting this instance first: Entire, unqualified; pure, unmixed, unalloyed. See also the headnote on Spelling and Etymology, and A. Illustration of Forms 3. Andrews (ed. 1989): Bottom’s pronunciation of perfect. Mowat & Werstine (ed. 1993): Since perfect became the preferred spelling around 1590, it is possible that the old form was deliberately chosen for Bottom—as again at [368–9]. A few modern editors have begun to restore the Qq spelling: see textual n. See also 1162.

358 your]
For the indeterminate use, see n. 354–6.

358 french…haire]
Cf. the hair-heir pun in Err. 3.2.122–4 (913–15) where the allusion to the French civil war of the 1580s and 1590s over an heir combines with an allusion to the French disease and its destruction of hair; see Foakes (ed. Err., 1962).

358 french crownes]
OED (French crown, b): Punningly, with reference to the baldness produced by the French disease. Theobald (ed. 1740), commenting on MM 1.2.52 (145): Not the piece of mony so call’d, but that Venereal Scab, which among the Surgeons is stil’d Corona Veneris [literally, the crown of Venus or love; medically, the Term for syphilitic blotches on the forehead, which often extend around it like a crown (OED, Corona 6b)].… Where these Eruptions are, the Skull is carious, and the Party becomes bald. Bucknill (1860, p. 89): [In MM, French crown] has been supposed to refer to a venereal scab, probably rupia; but in the retort which Quince makes upon Bottom’s catalogue of beards, the loss of hair from the crown of the head, also a symptom of the morbus Gallicus, appears to be referred to. Dyce (ed. 1864, 9:Glossary) quotes also H5 4.1.225–7 (2074–6). Anon. (1774, p. 99) objects to the anachronism of the rabble of low mechanics; who if they are supposed to be English have no business at Athens, and if Athenians have no business to talk of French crowns.

359 bare fac’t]
Johnson (1755, 1, 2): 1. With the face naked; not masked, quoting this instance. 2. Shameless; unreserved; without concealment; without disguise. OED (Barefaced, a.1a, b), quoting this instance first: 1. With the face uncovered: hence a. with no hair on the face, beardless, whiskerless, also fig.; b. without mask or vizard. Clemen (ed. 1963) accepts the first meaning, bald, and as a second suggests brazen, for which see OED (2).

359 maisters]
See n. 283.

359–63 here…rehearse]
Stern (2000, p. 31): The preparation of Pyramus and Thisbe is telescoped in time—the performers have only a day to learn their parts, and actually hold just one, fragmentary rehearsal (though there is also reference to a rehearsal in front of the Master of the Revels [ie. Philostrate; see 1864–5] that, logically, cannot have taken place at any time). But in other respects the rehearsal process mirrors the standard for higher-class provincial production. The first thing to happen is that the parts are distributed to be learnt separately at home…. Quince calls for a single general group rehearsal to be held after the parts have been committed to memory—and then the performance takes place.

360 parts]
See n. 329.

360–1 I…you]
Verity (ed. 1893): He begins with the strongest, and ends with the weakest, verb: a type of anti-climax time-honoured as a joke. Bottom echoes this somewhat at 850–1.

360 am to]
Kittredge (ed. 1939): Must. Doran (ed. 1959): Have to. See OED, To, prep. B. 11b (a). Cf. 1775.

361 con]
Johnson (1755, 2), quoting this instance: To study; to commit to memory (OED, v.1 3). See 1877.

362 palace wood]
Deighton (ed. 1891): The wood in which the palace stands. OED (Palace, n.1 6a): Of or belonging to…a palace.

362 a…towne]
Wilson (ed. 1924): A careless echo of [175]. Rudd (1979, p. 187) considers the echo of Golding (4.108; see ) relevant since Bottom and Flute are the stage Pyramus and Thisbe, and finds the Dukes oke a suitably sturdy substitute for Ovid’s mulberry tree. See nn. 174–5; 370.

362 a mile]
Wright (ed. 1877): In [175] it is a league. Verity (ed. 1893) adds about 3 miles, but see n. 175 on the variations in length of this term.

362 without]
See n. 175.

364 dogd]
Johnson (1755): To hunt as a dog, insidiously and indefatigably (OED, Dog, v. 1a). Black (1965, p. 35): Pestered.

364–5 deuises]
Schmidt (1874, 2): Plan, scheme, citing this instance (OED 6). Deighton (ed. 1891): Plans for playing. Cf. 1847.

365 draw]
Johnson (1755, v. a. 34), quoting this instance: To compose; to form in writing (OED, v. 63a).

365 bill]
OED (n.3 5a): A written list or catalogue, an inventory.

365–6 properties]
Steevens (ed. 1773): Whatever little articles are wanted in a play for the actors, according to their respective parts, dresses excepted (OED, n. Property 3, quoting this instance). Halliwell (ed. 1856): The assertion of Steevens, that dresses were not included amongst properties, is disproved by a passage in [Jasper] Mayne’s Citye Match, 1639, where apparel is spoken of as suited to be sold for properties.Ayscough (ed. 1784) refers to Wiv. 4.4.78–9 (2204–5): Go get vs properties And tricking for our Fayries. Knight (ed. 1839) quotes a list of properties from Richard Brome’s The Antipodes, 1640, from Collier’s History of English Dramatic Poetry (3 vols, 1831), 3:356 n.
Brooks (ed. 1979) observes that this is one of Quince’s duties as bookholder. Chambers (1923, 2:540): The book-holder…prompted the speeches, surveyed the entrances and exits, and saw to the readiness of the properties. Also known as book-keeper; see Gurr & Ichikawa (2000, p. 40 and passim).

368 obscenely]
Many agree with Thirlby (MS 1725–33): A blunder for obscurely, who compares LLL 4.1.143 (1140). Some follow Grey (1754, 1.47): I should have imagined that Shakespeare wrote more obscurely, had I not met with the following distinction in Mr. Tho. Randolph’s play, intitled, The Muses Looking-glass. Act 4. sc.2. [Kataplectus is asked if he would speak obscenely.] Kataplectus. Obscœnum est, quod intra scænam agi non opportuit. (Poetical and Dramatic Works of Thomas Randolph, ed. W. C. Hazlitt, London, 1875, 2 vols., 1:244.) Rann (ed. 1787): Privately, and with less restraint. Neil (ed. 1878): Perhaps a joke is intended for unstagely, privately, quoting Randolph, and translating: That is obscene which is not fit to be represented on the scene. Furness (ed. 1895), after quoting Grey and Randolph: The point is scarcely worth noting, but I think that scænam is here used not as on the stage, but merely as in public, and the whole phrase is only an ordinary definition of obscenum. Zukofsky (1963; 1987, p. 48) examines the word in the context of Wittgenstein’s proposition 2.014, Objects contain the possibility of all states of affairs (Tractatus Logico-Philosophicus, 1918): The pun is on scene heard as (while ob says against, therefore not) seen, and so rehearsed it may well strike the mind courageously that the Aristotelian final cause, or for the sake of what, or what for of it is: Take pains; be perfect; adieu. [368–9]. Brooks (ed. 1979): Bottom may have felt an appropriateness in obscenely: the obscene is that which should not be done unless in private, and the rendezvous is to ensure that the rehearsal will be in private. Irving & Marshall (ed. 1888) gloss as Thirlby above, but in the endnotes suggest for both LLL and here, Seemly with which many recent commentators agree. Other suggestions include: Chambers (ed. 1897): Unseen; Thiselton (1903, p. 24): Unseenly; Wilson (ed. 1924, Glossary): Openly, clearly, so as to be seen; Kittredge (ed. 1936, Glossary): Conveniently; Idem (ed. 1939): Dramatically; Foakes (ed. 1984): Fitly. Halliwell (1841, p. 75) sums up Bottom’s misuse of language: Like Mrs. Malaprop…if he reprehends anything in this world, it is the use of his oracular tongue, and a nice derangement of epitaphs [Sheridan, The Rivals (1775), 3.3.31–2, Dramatic Works, ed. Cecil Price, 1973, 1:110]. See also n. 270 (generally).

368 coragiously]
Cotgrave (1611, Courageusement): Couragiously, stoutly, valiantly, boldly, forwardly, hardily; confidently, with great assurednesse. Deighton (ed. 1891), questionably: Without fear of interruption. Kittredge (ed. 1939): With spirit (OED, Courage, n. 3). There may be a sexual equivoque here, not intended by Bottom: see Williams (1994, pp. 318–19) where courage means sexual desire.

368–9 Take…adieu]
Collier (1853, p. 100): The words…seem to belong to [Quince], as the manager of the play, rather than to Bottom. See textual n. Dyce (ed. 1857) finds the speech suitable for Bottom, comparing euery man looke ore his part (1782–3), but later (ed. 1864) he follows Collier and assigns the lines to Quince. Clarkes (ed. 1864): Surely [these words] are peculiarly characteristic of Bottom’s style of interfering, dictating, and taking upon himself even the manager’s own prerogative of ordering his actors.

368–9 perfit]
Phillips (ed. 1969): I.e. word-perfect. See OED, Perfect, a. 2c (Got by heart). Franke (1979, p. 287) compares 2 Cor. 13:11: Finally, brethren, farewell. Be perfect. See also n. 356–7, and cf. n. 1162.

370 Dukes oke]
Halliwell (ed. 1856): The conjecture is perhaps a whimsical one, but the localities here mentioned, the Palace Wood and the Duke’s Oak, bear some appearance of being derived from English sources, and in a certain degree support an opinion that they were either taken from an older drama, or were names familiar to Shakespeare as belonging to real places in some part of his own country. Cuningham (ed. 1905) compares Herne’s oak in [Wiv. 5.3.14 (2460–1)], etc. Craig (in ibid.): It was in Shakespeare’s day in Warwickshire, and it still is the custom in many parts of England to give such names to large oaks: was this in Shakespeare’s mind here? See n. 362.

371 holde…bowstrings]
Hanmer (ed. 1744): A proverbial phrase signifying, without fail, or, in all events. Schmidt (1874, Bow-string): = Come what come may. Thiselton (1903, p. 24), taking hold to signify a handshake, and cut bowstrings as meaning much the same as disable us, interprets: Here’s my hand on it; I’ll answer for the appointment being kept. Otherwise, never more count us as actors.
Commentators seek the origin of the expression in proverb lore, archery, or fiddle playing. Thirlby (MS 1733–47): Ray’s Proverbs [(1678) p.] 57: Hold, or cut codpiece-point [Tilley (C502)]. Cited also in Steevens (ed. 1778) and (believing himself to be the first to mention it) Staunton (ed. 1857). Warburton (ed. 1747): This proverbial phrase came originally from the Camp. When a Rendezvous was appointed, the militia Soldiers would frequently make excuse for not keeping word that their bowstrings were broke, i.e. their arms unserviceable. Hence when one would give another absolute assurance of meeting him, he would say proverbially—hold or cut bow-strings—i.e. whether the bowstring held or broke. For cut is used as a neuter, like the verb frets. Capell (1783, 2.3:102): That character’s phrase…was born in the days of archery: when a party was made at butts, assurance of meeting was given in the words of that phrase; the sense of the person using them, being—that he would hold or keep promise, or they might cut his bowstrings, demolish him for an archer: from which particular usage, the phrase had an easy transition among the vulgar to that general application which Bottom makes of it. So Hudson (ed. 1851): I’ll be there, rain or shine. Steevens (ed. 1778): [Warburton’s] interpretation is very ingenious, but somewhat disputable. The excuse made by the militia soldiers is a mere supposition, without proof; and it is well known that while bows were in use, no archer ever entered the field without a supply of strings in his pocket; whence originated the proverb, to have two strings to one’s bow. In The Country Girl, a comedy by T. B. [Anthony Brewer] 1647, is the following threat to a fiddler: —fiddler, strike, I’ll strike you else, and cut your begging bowstrings [D3v]. The bowstrings in both these instances may only mean the strings which make part of the bow with which musical instruments of several kinds are struck. The propriety of the allusion I cannot satisfactorily explain. Steevens in Malone (1780, 1:115) quotes Shirley’s The Ball, 1639: Have you devices to jeer the rest? Luc. All the regiment on ’em, or I’ll break my bowstrings [2.2.127–9] (The Plays and Poems of George Chapman: The Comedies, ed. T. M. Parrott, 1914, p. 556). Idem (ed. 1790): To cut the bowstring, when bows were in use, was probably a common practice of those who bore enmity to the archer. He hath twice or thrice cut Cupid’s bowstring, (says Don Pedro in [Ado 3.2.10–12 (1217–19)]) and the little hangman dare not shoot at him. Becket (1815, 1:268): Perhaps it is used proverbially for to mar [referring to 1751], to destroy. Keep all to this, or we shall have our bow-strings cut—all will be spoilt. We shall not be able to play. Bow-strings are here the strings of the fidler’s bow. Cuningham (ed. 1905): In very wet weather the bow-string, if not protected, would be more liable to fray and snap asunder.…The English archers at Creçy protected their strings during the thunderstorm which preceded the battle, while the Genoese bowmen did not. Petersen (1983, p. 131) suggests the meaning was similar in its time to the casual American expression fish or cut bait which the Dictionary of American Slang defines as: A request…that another act, obtain a result, or bring something to a successful conclusion soon, or else stop trying and give someone else the chance to do so. Godshalk (1995, p. 316): Retreating archers cut the strings of their discarded bows in order to keep the enemy from using the bows against their former owners.…. He means, Either be present at the rehearsal, or quit the troupe.
The expression may glance at Homer: If you do not meet at the oak, then Zeus will cut your bowstrings as he did Teucer’s in Iliad 15.461–seq. which prevented him from killing Hector at that time; i.e. keep promise or merit the wrath of the gods. There is also possibly a reference to a passage in Apuleius’s The Golden Ass, where Lucius begs Fotis to help him: Thus when I had well replenished my selfe with wine, and was now readie vnto Venerie not onely in minde but also in bodie, I remoued my clothes, and (showinge to Fotis my great impaciencie) I said, O my sweete harte take pitie vpon me and helpe me: for as you see, I am now prepared vnto the battaile which you your selfe did appointe, for after that I felte the first arrow of cruell Cupide within my brest, I bent my bowe very stronge, and now feare (because it is bended so harde) least the stringe should breake…[1566, Fiiir; Loeb ed., 2.16]. The words bowe and stringe (arcum and nervus in Latin) are metaphors for penis. Lucius pleads for help, or else disaster. Bottom’s phrase, though he is unconscious of its Apuleian implications, connects with a strand of imagery in Act 1, from the Moone, like to a siluer bowe Now bent (12–13), to Cupids strongest bowe,…arrowe, with the golden heade (180–1). For discussion of the imagery of the bow and bowstring as sexual metaphors see J. N. Adams, The Latin Sexual Vocabulary, 1982, pp. 21–2, and Williams (1994, pp. 138–9).

373 a Fairie]
Dessen & Thomson (1999, p. 87): Calls for these supernatural figures give little indication of their appearance. For the doubling of Theseus with this Fairy see .

373–4 at one…another]
See textual n. Collier (ed. 1842) adopts Capell’s (ed. 1768) emendation of Q1’s SD, explaining that the scene is obviously laid in a wood but that the characters are said to enter at different doors in Q1 because the wood is supposed. Dyce (1844, p. 45) objects: The doors refer to the actual stage-locality, not to the scene supposed to be represented. Dessen (1984, pp. 27–28) discusses the distinction between SDs that are theatrical signals and fictional signals. Dessen & Thomson (1999, pp. 73–4, door) give examples from hundreds of entrances/exits at one door/at another door. See also n. 432–3.

373–4 Robin goodfellow]
For the nature, role, and sources and analogues of this character, see DP, and , , , . Wells (ed. 1967) for a description of the character’s possible appearance quotes a stage direction to Act 4 of Grim the Collier (c.1600; ed. Farmer, 1912, I2r): Enter Robin Good fellow in a suite of Leather close to his body, his Face and Hands coloured russet-colour, with a Flayle.

note.p: Brooks (ed. 1979): Cf. Gallathea, I. ii [2:434–5], a dialogue where, to further the exposition, Lyly has Cupid (a mischief-maker, like Puck) interrogate a Nimph of Diana about herself and her mistress, who, she indicates, is at odds with his mother Venus.

div.note:
375–85 How…eare]
Jourdain (MS c. 1860) reassigns 375 to the Fairy, and 376–8 to Robin. See conj., . He may be reflecting a performance tradition, since one of the settings of all or some of these lines listed by Gooch & Thatcher (1991, #10383–4) is called Puck’s Song (#10413). Chambers (ed. 1897) assumes lines 376–83 constitute a song (see n. 376–7 below). Noble (1923, p. 54) warns against vocal settings (see ). Sitwell (1948, p. 189) refers to the passage as the first Fairy Song. Holzknecht (1950, p. 262): It is sung by Queen Titania’s dainty little harbinger and sets the mood for what follows. Also still belatedly in tune with 19th-c. balletic treatment of MND, Gerstner-Hirzel (1957, p. 65) details dance steps for the actors, taking choreographic hints from the meters for these and the following lines, concluding: After savors [383] the little elf turns with a sudden jump or pirouette. Griffiths (ed. 1996, p. 114) describes other 19th- and 20th-c. balletic and operatic performances by the First Fairy.
Without recommending a song and dance treatment, Strindberg (1909; 1966, p. 229) recognizes the musical force of the verse: Mendelssohn’s overture, which opens with the wonderful motif of the fairies, was probably inspired by the fairy’s stanza in the first scene of Act II:…The anapests…over hill, over dale are those of the overture so one could sing them according to the notes. But critics have differed on the nature of the rhythm. Coleridge (1960 [1811], 1:91) hears amphimacers. Guest (1838, 1:170–2, 190–2) describes a rising four-beat verse, which occurs so often in the fairy dialect of the sixteenth century, as to form one of its most characteristic features. Ness (1941, p. 129) calls it mostly trochaic tetrameter, a metre normally reserved by Shakespeare for the speech of supernatural figures. See also Wright (1988, p. 114). On meter, see also .

375 How…you]
Most read this as a tetrameter line of seven syllables, pronouncing spirit and whither monosyllabically as Thirlby (MS 1733–47): spir’t we’r. See n. 17 (spirit). See Abbott §§463, 466, and 504. A few differ. Furness (ed. 1895): To read this line rhythmically we must, according to Walker…and Abbott (§466), contract spirit into sprite, and whither into whi’er.…I am not sure, however, that the ear is not quite as well satisfied with the line as it stands. Cuningham (ed. 1905) alters the text to fair spirit, arguing: It is not by any means certain that this line is not pure blank verse, and that a word has not dropped out before spirit. Cf. [192] God speed, fair Helena! Whither away? It is noteworthy that Puck uses only plain blank verse in this scene; and it is not till [719] that he adopts a lyric measure. Brooks (ed. 1979): Pedantic prosodists have boggled over this freely rhythmical line. Yet it scans as three trochees and a dactyl. Knowles (privately) reads it as a pentameter lacking the initial syllable, pointing out that How now before a vocative is usually accented on the first syllable, though at the beginning of the line it is more usually preceded by an unaccented Why (e.g., Rom. 1.5.60 [633]).

375 How now]
Schmidt (1875, Now): Sometimes = How do you do? at other times = what is the matter? or what are you doing? OED (adv. 4b.): Ellipt. for How is it now? Franz (§250) lists this under interjections of astonishment, surprise (etc.). Blake (8.2.3): A relatively informal greeting. See 1026. Cf. n. 138.

375 spirit]
OED (3a): A supernatural, incorporeal, rational being or personality, usually regarded as imperceptible at ordinary times to the human senses, but capable of becoming visible at pleasure, and freq. conceived as troublesome, terrifying, or hostile to mankind. Hassel (2005, p. 337) divides into evil and good groups, with a third group identified by Oberon’s spirits of another sort (1429), which reside outside the religious sphere. In MND the word, together with the alternate sprite or spright, is used of the supernatural population of fairies, elves or goblins (see 386, 403, 971, 978, 1026, 1429, 2177; see also OED, Spright, 2). The introduction of supernatural beings has not been prepared for in the preceding scenes. Robin’s greeting and the ensuing dialogue clarify whatever indications may have been given by costume. See also nn. 1421–8, 1423–4.

376–9 Ouer…greene]
Pope (ed. 1725) first relines these four lines as eight and all editors follow (see textual n.), though Sitwell (1948, pp. 189–90) objects because the pause at the end of each line makes the fairy move slowly.…The fairy was in a hurry.

376–7 Ouer…fire]
Thirlby (MS 1725–33) compares Drayton’s Nimphidia, The Court of Fayrie, ll. 309–11: Thorough Brake, thorough Brier, Thorough Muck, thorough Mier, Thorough Water, thorough Fier, (3:134). Quoted by Johnson (ed. 1765); to which Steevens (ed. 1773) adds: Printed in 1593 (withdrawn in ed. 1778). Correctly dated 1627 by Halliwell (ed. 1856). Idem (1879, p. 6): [Sh.] all but literally quotes [Spenser, FQ 6.8.32.1]: Through hils and dales, through bushes and through breres, arguing that MND was composed some time after January the 20th, 1595–6, on which day the Second Part of the Faerie Queene was entered on the books of the Stationers’ Company. Chambers (ed. 1897): The argument is thin, for (a) the two passages may well be independent, and (b) if either is an imitator, it may be Spenser. There is no reason why the song should not have reached Ireland before 1596. Holland (ed. 1994): Shakespeare might have seen Spenser’s lines in manuscript or, more probably, the lines could simply be conventional.

376–7 thorough…thorough]
Johnson (1755, prep.):The word through extended into two syllables. Not just an alternative Elizabethan spelling, as Brooks (ed. 1979) claims, but a parallel development of the fully stressed Old English form: see the full etymological note in OED, Thorough, prep. For the early editors who shorten thorough to through to make the lines decasyllabic see textual n. The same spelling is at 481.

376 brier]
OED (n.1 2), quoting the instance at 922: Brier-bushes collectively; (n.1 1): A prickly, thorny bush or shrub in general; formerly including the bramble, but now usually confined to wild rose bushes. For examples of various uses see 907, 922, 1051, etc.

377 parke]
Johnson (1755): A piece of ground inclosed and stored with wild beasts of chase (OED, n. 1a).

377 pale]
Schmidt (1875, 1): An enclosure (especially of a park) (OED, n.1 3).

377 flood]
OED (n. 3), quoting this instance: Water as opposed to land, often contrasted with field and fire. See also n. 478.

378 I…sphere]
Winter (2003, p. 92–3) notes that the fairies belong to the realm of the impalpable, having no other mode of movement but unending flight, a paradoxical wandering always swift and fleeting, imperceptible [insaissisable], invisible. The queen of the fairies herself chooses to seek explanation from Oberon while engaged in this characteristic movement (see 1617) (Fr.). See also n. 1554. Warton (1785, p. 263) compares this line with the boast of Milton’s Spirit in Comus, [I] can soar as soon To the corners of the Moon (1016–17). Cf. 1615–16.

378 swifter…sphere]
Furnivall (On Puck’s, [1879], pp. 431–2): At the date of the Dream, the Ptolemaic system was believd in, and the moon and all the planets and stars were supposd to be fixt in hollow crystalline spheres or globes. These spheres were supposd to be swung bodily round the earth in 24 hours by the top sphere, the primum mobile, thus making an entire revolution in one day and night (OED, sphere, n. 2a); taking the 19th-c. figure of the length of the moon’s orbit, he calculates the speed indicated to be about 20 miles a second, but in view of Puck’s claim for orbiting the earth at 552–3, reduces the orbital length and hence the speed to 10 1/2 or even 8 miles a second. In a footnote he disclaims Sh.’s interest in such calculations, but enjoys the joke of doing sums about Puck. Empson (1979, p. 6; 1994, pp. 208–9) also calculates the fairy’s speed, not as a jest, but arguing for Sh.’s acquaintance with Copernican theory. For more on fairy speed see nn. 552–3, 1615–16. For other references to the spheres see 1084, and cf. nn. 529, 754.

378 Moons]
Finding the meter defective many seek to squeeze an extra foot from moons. See textual n. and conj., . Thirlby (MS 1733–47) conjectures moones, which Steevens (MS 1773–8) adopts, calling it the Saxon genitive case, and (ed. 1778) comparing Spenser, FQ 3.1.15.5, whales bone. See also Rann (ed. 1787) and Wright (ed. 1877). Wilson (ed. 1924) defending moonës: The line is crowded in Q[1]. and the compositor would have every inducement to omit the e. Steevens (MS 1793–1800) conjectures moony from Sidney, Arcadia (ed. Evans, 1977, p. 478), which White (ed. 1858) adopts. Guest (1838, 1:294–5) favors the monosyllabic pronunciation: The flow of Shakespeare’s line is quite in keeping with the peculiar rhythm which he has devoted to his fairies. Collier (ed. 1842): All that seems required for the measure is to dwell a little longer than usual upon the monosyllable moon’s. Abbott §484 and Franz §722 both disyllabize Moons, but Abbott says his scansion is not to be represented as necessary. Dobson (1968, §312 n. 2) identifies moones and nightes (1614) as disyllables. Knowles (privately): No dissyllable is needed; the slight pause needed to separate the contiguous s’s serves (metrically) as an unaccented syllable. For other struggles with alleged missing syllables see nn. 429, 630 (I…blowes), 1228, 1485, 1548–9, 1614 (nights).

379 to dew]
By dewing. On this use of the infinitive, see n. 265.

379 orbs]
OED (n.1 1), quoting this instance first: A circle, a ring. Johnson (ed. 1765): The circles supposed to be made by the Fairies on the ground, whose verdure proceeds from the Fairy’s care to water them. Capell (1783, 2.3:103): Those natural phænomena seen in meadows of rings rising in them, generally in the night and in summer time; adding that the Idle-headed eld [Wiv. 4.4.36 (2158)]…thought them the work of fairies, dancing there by night in a round [516], roundel [651], or ringlet [461]; and a fairy circle or ring is the name they are still call’d by. He then gives a scientific explanation of them. Delius (ed. 1859) compares Wiv. 5.5.65–8 (2547–50), and Wright (ed. 1877) Tmp. 5.1.37 (1988). Furness (ed. 1895) alludes to Brand (1848–9; 1970, 2:480–3), and Dyer, (1883; 1966, pp. 15–16), among others.

380 cowslippes]
Johnson (1755): A species of Primrose [primula veris], quoting Tmp. 5.1.89 (2046). Idem (ed. 1765): The cowslip was a favourite among the fairies. OED (1a): A well-known wild plant in pastures and grassy banks, blossoming in spring, with drooping umbels of fragrant yellow flowers. See 385, 2120.

380–1 tall…coats]
In support of his emendations (see textual n.) Collier (1853, p. 100): Cowslips are never tall, and…the crimson spots are not in their coats, or on the petals, but at the bottom of the calix. Singer (1853, pp. 28–9): A most mischievous piece of meddling with a fine passage full of fancy. He argues that a cowslip would be well designated by a fairy as tall; most agree, except Collier (ed. 1858), who, though he restores the original Q1 text, maintains his position.

380 Pensioners]
OED (n. 2b), quoting this instance: A member of a body-guard, an attendant, a retainer. Malone (ed. 1790): The golden-coated cowslips were chosen by the author as pensioners to the Fairy Queen, the dress of the Band of Gentlemen Pensioners being in the time of Queen Elizabeth very splendid, and (as we learn from [Francis] Osborne) the tallest and handsomest men being generally chosen by her for that office. See [Historical Memoires on the Reigns of Queen Elizabeth, and King James, 1658, pp. 39–40]. The allusion was pointed out by Mr. Steevens. Thirlby (MS 1733–47) draws attention to the only other use in Sh., Wiv. 2.2.77 (845), where pensioners outshine the best and fairest courtiers, even earls.

380 bee]
For be used with a plural, see Abbott §300, Blake 4.2.2.1. Here and at 1139 it is needed for the rhyme. See also 382.

381 spottes]
Thirlby (MS 1733–47) compares Cym. 2.2.38–9 (945–6), A mole Cinque-spotted: Like the Crimson drops I’ th’bottome of a Cowslippe. Rydén (1978, p. 25): A remarkable example of Shakespeare’s sense of botanical detail is his noticing of the orange(-red) patch at the base of each lobe of the corolla (especially characteristic of the cowslip).

382 fauours]
Johnson (1755, 7; 8): 7. Something given by a lady to be worn; 8. Any thing worn openly as a token. Dyce (ed. 1864, 9:Glossary): A love-token (OED, n. 7a). Wright (ed. 1877) compares LLL 5.2.130–1 (2022–3).

383 freckles]
Johnson (1755, 2), quoting this instance: Any small spot or discoloration (OED, n. 2, quoting this instance). Thirlby (MS 1733–47) compares H5 5.2.49 (3036).

383 liue…sauours]
Hunter (ed. 1870): Their scent resides.

384–5 I…eare]
Thirlby (MS 1733–47): In Q[ueen] Bess’s time men wore pearls in their ears. Halliwell (ed. 1856) says the lines derive from this custom and from the notion that the dew-drop was the commencing form of the pearl. Cf. Batman vppon Bartholome (1582): The Margarite, or Orient perle, is gendered of the dew (lib. 16 cap. 62, f. 262v, 2Y4v). OED (Pearl, n.1 1a) quotes Mandeville (c. 1400, ed. 1839, xiv. 158): The fyn Perl congeleth and wexeth gret of the dew of heuene. Wright (ed. 1877) gives allusions to the wearing of jewels in the ear both by men and women from Rom. 1.5.46 (619), Marlowe, and Jonson. Steevens (ed. 1778) found this line imitated in the anonymous The Wisdom of Doctor Dodypoll (1600, Malone Society, 1964, 1089–90); see . See also 224 and 1568–9.

384 I…here]
Hanmer (ed. 1744) makes the tetrameter line pentameter by adding and there (see textual n.) but most agree with Brooks (ed. 1979): Metrically, a transition: in four beats, like [378–83]; iambic, like [385] ff.

384 goe seeke]
For the omission of to from the infinitive after go see n. 260.

386 Lobbe]
OED (Lob, n.2 2), quoting this instance: A country bumpkin: a clown, lout. Hanmer (ed. 1744, 6:Glossary): A lubber, a looby. Warton (1762, 1:120 n.) quoting from Milton’s L’Allegro the drudging Goblin (105) and the Lubbar-fiend (110): The Goblin is Shakespeare’s Robin Goodfellow; he also mentions the same traditionary being in Beaumont’s The Knight of the Burning Pestle 3.3.425 (1:58), Lob-lie-by-the-fire. Steevens (ed. 1778) and Halliwell (ed. 1856) give more instances. Johnson (ed. 1765): Lob, lubber, looby, lobcock, all denote both inactivity of body and dulness of mind. Collier (ed. 1842): The Fairy, by the word lob, reproaches Puck with heaviness, compared with his own lightness. Thoms (1847, p. 1055): Dr. Johnson’s observation…is in the present case altogether misplaced. For here the name Lob is doubtless a well-established fairy epithet. Tyrrell (ed. 1850), also objecting to Johnson: I cannot see how any of these meanings can be applied to the lively and active spirit, Puck. Probably it has reference to his supposed clumsy grotesqueness of appearance. Hunter (ed. 1870): suggests that while the word denoted a clown, implying, usually, a clumsy, awkward fellow…here, Shakspeare means fool or jester of the fairy court. Irving & Marshall (ed. 1888): The Fairy may mean the word in the sense of urchin, mischievous lad; or she may use it as a term of contempt, Puck being of more clumsy make than the other Fairies, and being looked down upon by the Queen Titania’s attendants as a clownish fellow. Briggs (1959, pp. 24, 88) says that like the Brownie, Lob is sometimes regarded as of full mortal size, or even more than human size. Foakes (ed. 1984): Was he [Robin] played by an adult actor? Or is it simply a fairy joke? Holland (ed. 1994) also suggests an adult actor, perhaps larger than the Fairy.
Keightley (1850, pp. 318–19) tentatively connects lob etymologically with the verb to leap and so wishes to suppose Lob to be a sportive spirit; less clearly, he also associates it with a spider. Even more questionably Barnett (1887, p. 14): From the context it rather seems to be used as a term of endearment. May it not be from lobe, the flap of the ear? (Lobbe is a 16th-c. spelling of lobe, and eare concludes the fairy’s preceding line.) See also n. 410, Hobgoblin, and , .

387 Elues]
Johnson (1755, Elf 1), quoting 2175–8: A wandering spirit, supposed to be seen in wild unfrequented places. Wright (ed. 1877): Fairies. Wells (ed. 1967): Fairy boys. (That is, presumably, Cobweb, Peaseblossom, Moth, and Mustardseed.) Foakes (ed. 1984): By Shakespeare’s time synonymous with fairies, and used of both sexes, though originally elves were male imps; but perhaps anticipating…Peaseblossom and the rest [at 979]. OED (Elf, n.1 1a): In mod. literature, elf is a mere synonym of fairy.…Originally elf was masculine, elven feminine, but in the 13th and 14th c. the two seem to have been used indifferently of both sexes. In mod. use elf chiefly, though not always, denotes a male fairy. See 400, 655, 992, 2177.

387 anon]
OED (adv. 4; 5) gives the earlier meaning as (4) Straightway, at once, instantly, but adds that the word was (5) gradually misused…to express: Soon, in a short time, in a little while, which is probably Sh.’s meaning here and elsewhere in the play, except at 1397.

388 Reuels]
OED (n.1 2): An occasion or course of merry-making or noisy festivity, with dancing, games, masking, acting, or other forms of lively entertainment. For Queen Elizabeth’s Office of the Revels and its Master, see n. 1830. Milton reduces his fallen angels to faerie elves, Whose midnight revels…some belated peasant sees (PL 1.781–3).

389 come]
For the use of the subjunctive, see n. 71.

390–401 For…there]
Smidt (1986, p. 123): It is in fact remarkable that the boy nowhere actually appears in the play in spite of the importance attributed to him in terms of plot. Mahood (1992, p. 6): A few [characters who are neither heard nor seen] have so haunted the imagination of directors that for centuries they have figured on stage as mutes: Jane Shore…in [R3], Romeo’s…Rosaline, and the Indian Boy. Williams (Moonlight, 1997, p. 24) argues that the boy’s presence…seems very likely because he is called Titania’s attendant [391], and he is the focal point of the central argument between the fairy king and queen. But he admits that Neither the stage directions nor the texts of the Folio or Quarto indicate his presence. The Indian boy appears on stage in some later productions from Purcell (1692) on: see ibid., passim pp. 47, 54, 86–7, 116.

390 passing]
Johnson (1755, 2), quoting this instance: Used adverbially to enforce the meaning of another word. Exceeding. Clark & Wright (ed. 1864, Glossary): Surpassingly, exceedingly (OED, adv. a). For the form of this intensifier see Franz §§241 (c), 387, and Blake 5.1.3.1 (iii), both quoting this instance.

390 fell]
Hanmer (ed. 1744, 6:Glossary): Fierce, cruel, but, more accurately, OED (a. 3), quoting this instance last: Hot, angry, enraged, virulent. See 2027, 2083.

390 wrath]
OED, quoting this instance, and noting (rime hath): (Var. of wroth a.…) Wroth, angry, irate; deeply resentful.

391 Because that]
For that as a conjunctional affix see Abbott §287; Franz §546; Blake 5.3.2.4 (ii, a).

392 A…king]
Thirlby (MS 1725–33) compares Robin’s version of the origin of the changeling with Titania’s account at 499–513. Brooks (ed. 1979) finds Robin’s version not incompatible with the story of his mother, below [499] ff.; Shakespeare may or may not already have conceived that. Holland (ed. 1994) agrees that the stories are not incompatible…since the boy’s father might reasonably imagine the boy to have been stol’n by Titania.
The tale of the Indian boy suggests a number of analogues. Wigston (1884, p. 319) compares the Indian Bacchus, who, at times, can hardly be distinguished from Eros. Fripp (1928, p. 593): Ascanius is stolen by fairies in Marlowe’s Dido (5.1.214–15; 1:55). Cambillard (1939, p. 121): The child is a symbol of Mercury [the alchemical metal]. (Fr.) Olson (1957, p. 112): The battle which makes all the trouble concerns a very elusive changeling boy who was carried into the otherworld by the fairies. In like manner, Ganymede was taken by Jove,…commonly thought to be a parable of the capture of man’s rational soul by the love of God. Wright (1968, pp. 27–8): Such an interpretation is perhaps more in keeping with the symbolism of a masque, but it does suggest that Oberon, who was traditionally associated with divine love,…acts to regain his own rather than out of perverse caprice. Oberon of course does not require the precise justification of this mythological gloss: quite simply Titania has abandoned her proper wifely and queenly allegiance, and until Oberon has displaced the changeling and regained his rightful place in her affections, the order of nature will not be restored. Cf. n. 396–400. Cody (1969, p. 136): The quarrel of the fairy king and queen over [quotes 392] echoes the Olympian quarrel in the Metamorphoses over Semele’s infant son, as well as the jealousy of Apollo over Coronis and the Thessalian youth [Ovid, Met. 3.259–315; 2.542–7, 596–630]. On the changeling’s origin and the dispute over him, see also nn. 495, 499–513, 512–13.

392 boy stollen,]
Q1’s punctuation suggests that Titania stole the boy, while the readings of Q2 and Theobald (ed. 1733) are ambiguous (see textual n.), and tend to leave her blameless. Knowles (privately): Not quite blameless if she’s receiving stolen goods.

392 stollen]
The word stollen is always a monosyllable in the verse of this play, whether spelt as here and in 440, 1074, or as stolne in 40, 570, etc.

392 Indian king]
Phillips (ed. 1969): Although Oberon and Titania seem very English fairies, Shakespeare has taken over a part of the tradition found in Huon of Burdeaux that Oberon’s kingdom was in the east.…The association with the east adds to the mystery of the fairy people. Hendricks (1996, p. 43), unconvincingly: The figurative evocation of India localizes Shakespeare’s characterization of the fairies in [MND] and marks the play’s complicity in the racialist ideologies being created by early modern England’s participation in imperialism. Williams (Moonlight, 1997, p. 24): Shakespeare’s use of an Indian boy might derive in particular from [Spenser, FQ], where the founder of Oberon’s royal line Was Elfin: him all India obayd [2.10.72.5]. But his choice no doubt echoes the larger matter of English interest in India in the 1590s; the British East India Company was established in 1600. The Vaughans (1991, p. 45), perversely, consider the allusion is to North American Indians, rather than to an East Indian potentate.

393 changeling]
Johnson (1755): (From change; the word arises from an odd superstitious opinion, that the fairies steal away children, and put others that are ugly and stupid in their places.) 1. A child left or taken in the place of another, quoting Spenser, FQ 1.10.65.8–9 for the child left, and this instance for the child taken. Idem (ed. 1765): Commonly used for the child supposed to be left by the fairies, but here for the child taken away (OED 3, citing 393 and quoting 495). Ritson (1783, p. 42): It is here properly used, and in its common acceptation; that is, for the child got in exchange. Dr. Johnson will recollect that a Fairy is now speaking. Thirlby (MS 1725–33) mentions Drayton’s Nimphidia, then (in MS 1733–47) quotes ll. 73–80 (Works, ed. Hebel, 1961, 3:127) on changelings, and cites WT 3.3.118 (1558). Neil (ed. 1878) cites 1H4 1.1.86–90 (89–93). Marshall (1982, pp. 568–9): The changeling comes to represent all of the characters in the play who are traded or fought over as property. It also shows us that the other characters are changelings in the sense that the play’s plot revolves around their exchanges: their substitutions and their interchangeability. Drawing on Puttenham, who names one of the figures of exchange, Hipallage, or the Changeling (1589; 1936, p. 171), Marshall further discusses the character in rhetorical terms: Born of this mirror of metaphors [in 500–10], destined to be switched, substituted, and exchanged, the changeling is also a trope for tropes. P. Parker (1987, p. 122): The link [to rhetorical figures of disorder] becomes even more evocative when we note that Puttenham actually compares this figure to the disorder represented by the changeling of fayries. Puttenham (1589; ed. 1936, p. 173) chooses the English name because it is easier for our Ladies and pretie mistresses in Court to remember, since they hear Nurses saying that the Fayries vse to steale the fairest children out of their cradles, and put other ill fauoured in their places, which they called changelings, or Elfs; so, if ye mark, doeth our Poet, or maker play with his wordes, vsing a wrong construction for a right, and an absurd for a sensible, by manner of exchange. Hackett (1997, p. 5): The word changeling could also have the wider sense of anyone who changes either their nature or their allegiances, [instancing Beatrice-Joanna switching between three male partners in Middleton and Rowley’s The Changeling, 1622]. In this sense, Demetrius, Lysander and even Titania herself are changelings.
White (ed. 1858): [A] trisyllable. (See Abbott §487, quoting this instance). The other two uses in the play, at 495, 1574, are disyllabic.

394 And…childe]
A few commentators speculate on the reason for the quarrel over the changeling. Holland (1933, p. 4) finds a parallel to the struggle of Titania and Oberon for the child in the struggle between Elizabeth and Philip over Mary’s child James. Foakes (ed. 1984, p. 29), improbably: Is the child’s father conceivably Oberon, which would explain Oberon’s jealousy, and make him a fairy child? See 436. Ignoring the unambiguous account of the changeling’s maternity at 499–513, Weisstein (2000, p. 316): Perhaps her [Titania’s] own illegitimate offspring.

394 would haue]
See n. 65.

395 Knight…traine]
Deighton (ed. 1891): As leader of his retinue of attendants. Thirlby (MS 1725–33) refers to 496 Henchman.

395 trace]
Caldecott (MS 1813–33): I.e. traverse, range (OED, v.1 5a). Wright (ed. 1877): Wander through. Furness (ed. 1895): There is an intimation here of hunting, of tracing the tracks of game.

396–400 But…square]
Calderwood (1965, p. 511): The point seems to be that Titania is violating natural order in two ways: by making the changeling child all her joy at the expense of the proper object of her joy, Oberon, and by refusing to let the boy pass from a feminine into a masculine world where, if natural growth is to have its way, he belongs. Knowles (privately) objects: All her joy is common hyperbole (as Lr. 1.1.82–3 [88–9] shows), there is no hint that she has transferred her amorous affection wholly from Oberon to a child (rather than that she is punishing Oberon for his attempt to deprive her of his ward), and proper masculine upbringing is nowhere alluded to.

396–7 But…ioy]
On Titania’s relations with the changeling, see also n. 512–13 and , .

396 perforce]
Johnson (1755), quoting this instance: By violence; violently (OED 1a). Rolfe (ed. 1877): By force. Evans (ed. 1974): Forcibly. This seems too strong. Schmidt (1875, 4), quoting also 957: At any rate (German: durchaus [= absolutely, at all costs]) (OED 1b). Cf. 786, 1113.

396 withhoulds]
Schmidt (1875), citing this instance: To keep back instead of granting. OED (v. 3), quoting this instance: To detain; keep in bondage, in custody, or under control.

397 Crownes…flowers]
Wright (1968, p. 28): As she does Bottom at [1566–7]. Greenwood (2002, pp. 22–6, 30) discusses these episodes and the image of Hiems’ odorous chaplet (485–6) in terms of the symbolism of wreaths and garlands in classical and medieval literature, which can (p. 23) shift from positive acclaim of love and glory to negative reproach by which inadequacy is laughed at.

397 ioy]
Johnson (1755, 4): A term of fondness, quoting Lr. 1.1.82 (88) (OED, n. 3b, quoting the instance at 1514 first).

398–400 And…square]
Thirlby (MS 1725–33) compares 457–62.

398 groue]
Johnson (1755): A walk covered by trees meeting above. OED (1), quoting the instance at 1431: A small wood; a group of trees affording shade or forming avenues or walks, occurring naturally or planted for a special purpose. See 522, 624, 640, 1027, 1431, 1636.

398 greene]
See n. 379.

399 fountaine]
OED (1a): A spring or source of water issuing from the earth and collecting in a basin, natural or artificial. Now arch. or poet. exc. fig. Fountains are prominent features of pastoral fiction; see e.g. in Montemayor’s Diana (ed. 1968) the natural fountain of the Sicamours (pp. 24, 51–9) and the artificial fountain of Felicia’s temple (p. 133). Cf. AYL 4.1.153–4 (2062–3).

399 spangled]
Johnson (1755, To spangle), quoting this instance: To besprinkle with spangles or shining bodies (OED 1). Deighton (ed. 1891): The stars in heaven resembling the spangles (small disks of bright metal) worn as ornaments on dresses, bridles, etc., quoting Spenser, FQ 4.11.45.5, With glittering spangs, that did like starres appeare. Cf. Shr. 4.5.31 (2329).

399 starlight sheene]
Until 1859 starlight was usually taken as a noun and sheene as an adjective, most following for the latter Johnson (ed. 1765): Shining, bright, gay. The glosses of Gildon (1710, Glossary): Shine, and Hanmer (ed. 1744, 6:Glossary): Clear, bright; also, brightness, lustre, allow ambiguity. The suggestion of Delius (ed. 1859) that here sheene is used substantively as in Ham. 3.2.157 (2026), And thirtie dosen Moones with borrowed sheene [Q2], dominated commentary for the next century. Wright (ed. 1877) quotes Milton’s substantive use in Comus 1003, spangled sheen. OED, while allowing Starlight as both n. and adj., quotes this line for the noun (1a). Disagreement still persists among the few later editors who gloss, e.g. Clemen (ed. 1963): Brightly shining starlight; Andrews (ed. 1989): Luster of starlight.

400 But]
I.e., except (Abbott §120; Franz §567 and Blake 5.3.1 (b, ii) both quote 1174–5).

400 square]
