Cotgrave (1611, Desaccorder): To discord, or disaccord; to iarre, differ, disagree, dissent, square, be repugnant; (Se quarrer): To strout, or square it, looke big on’t, carrie his armes a kemboll braggadochio-like. Most commentators follow Gildon (1710, Glossary): To quarrel (OED 8b). Peck (1740, pp. 223–4): [Sh.] uses square in both senses…to express an agreement, citing WT 5.1.52 (2786), Tit. 3.2.31 (1483), and, erroneously, Ant. 2.1.45 (670); and to express a quarrel, citing Tit. 2.1.100 (663) and 2.1.124 (687), and Ant. 3.13.41 (2199). He offers some alternatives: see conj., . Collier (ed. 1842): Quarrel. [referring to n. 9, 2:190] To square, is now to take the first position for boxing. Following this hint, the Clarkes (ed. 1864): It appears to mean prepare for fighting, show pugnacious intentions, rather than actually to fight. The first instances of this latter meaning in OED are in the early 1800’s (Square v. 3e). Craig (in Cuningham, ed. 1905) quotes North’s Plutarch: Hannibal hearing of their iarre and squaring together, sought straight opportunitie to make their discord finely to serue his turne (1597, Life of Fabius, p. 198, R3v).

400 that]
For the omission of so, see n. 333.

400 Elues]
See n. 387.

401 acorne cups]
Beisly (1864, p. 45): The acorn is well known to be the fruit of the oak, and the cup which holds it is of a beautiful shape. The poet could not have chosen a more suitable object for fairy elves to hide in. Skeat (1891, p. 180), on the tininess of fairies, quotes an old Danish ballad describing a troll as No bigger than an ant, and also Drayton’s Nymphidia ll. 361–8 (Works, ed. Hebel, 1961, 3:136), where the idea of creeping into a nut is seen again.

402–14 Either…night]
Gentleman (ed. 1774): This description of Puck’s mischievous qualification, so much believed by the credulous, is well introduced, and happily supported; it fully satisfies us on the subject; yet in the next speech our luxurious bard improves upon the imagery, and pours forth a fresh flood of fancy. Anon (1844, p. 69) parallels Thomas Nashe’s description of such merry prankes (see ). Fender (1968, p. 15): This exchange sounds about as natural as the beginning of a music-hall routine (with Fairy playing the straight man); it is crude, intrusive exposition written largely in end-stopped lines—reminiscent (or so it seems at first) of the early Shakespeare of Henry VI. But there is an important difference: in [MND] certain passages in which the dialogue seems unrealistic are used deliberately, for specific effects.…[Here] the formal style suits the way his character is presented: he is not allowed to develop into an individual with his own unique style. Instead he is described—in the first instance by someone else—according to certain traditional modes of behaviour.

402 Either]
Apparently pronouncing this as a disyllable, which makes the line hypermetrical, Pope (ed. 1725), substituted Or. See textual n. However, as Malone (ed. 1790, 10:578) observes when Pope makes the same substitution at 811, Sh. frequently employs either and other similar words, as monosyllables. Cf. Abbott §466, who includes either among words in which th is softened though to what degree it is impossible to say.

402 shape]
Possibly suggesting the theatrical usage, OED (n.1 8a): A part, or character impersonated; the make-up and costume suited to a particular part. Obs.

402 making]
OED (vbl. n.1 6c): Of persons: Bodily form or appearance; build, make. Obs. Hunter’s (ed. 1870) claim that this is a stage term denoting the characteristic effect of dress, &c. is unattested in OED.

403 that…sprite]
Warton (1785, p. 236), compares Comus, 846, that shrewd medling elfe. See n. 375 (spirit).

403 shrewde]
Johnson (1755, 1): Having the qualities of a shrew; malicious; troublesome; mischievous (OED, a. 1a, quoting this instance). See 1359. The spelling shrew’d in some editions (see textual n.) may hint at the origin of the adjective in the past participle of the verb shrew (= To curse) or in the noun for the animal (OED, loc. cit., etymological note).

403 knauish]
Johnson (1755, 2), quoting the instance at 1488: Waggish; mischievous (OED, a. 2, quoting this instance and 1488). These are the only uses in MND, thereby serving to connect Robin with Cupid; see further nn. 1387, 1488.

404 hee]
Abbott §224: He and she are used for man and woman.…This makes more natural the use of he that, with the third person of the verb, in [404–5] (OED, pers. pron. 4a, quoting this instance). See also Franz §290; Blake 3.3.1.3 (c).

405–9 frights…Misselead]
Some, from Mason (1785, p. 69) to Halliwell (ed. 1856) find that the grammar is here defective. But others, from Thirlby (MS 1725–33) on, agree with Wright (ed. 1877): The first verb frights is of course governed by he which immediately precedes. The others are in agreement with you, (he gives two biblical examples). See Abbott §415; Franz §678, quoting 404–7, and Wright; Blake 6.1.1.6 (d), quoting 404–6. See textual nn. 405, 406–9.

405 Villageree]
Johnson (1755), quoting this instance: District of villages. Delius (ed. 1859): Villagery, the inhabitants of a village (Ger.). Wright (ed. 1877): Peasantry. Johnson defines it as a district of villages, but it denotes rather a collection of villagers. OED (Villagery), quoting this instance first: Villages collectively. Now chiefly as an echo of the Shaksperian passage. Most agree with Delius. Trisyllabic (Abbott §468; Franz §50).

406–8 Skim…barme]
Johnson (ed. 1765, Appendix, 8:2H7) quotes Samuel Harsnett’s Declaration of Egregious Popish Impostures (1603, S3v): And if that the bowle of curds, & creame were not duly set out for Robin-goodfellow the Frier, & Sisse the dairy-maide,…why then, either the pottage was burnt to next day in the pot, or the cheese would not curdle, or the butter would not come, or the ale in the fat [vat] would neuer have good head. He attributes the note to Warton (1762, 1:120), but quotes directly and more fully from Harsnett himself. Steevens (ed. 1773) adds: Reginald Scot gives the same account of this frolicksome spirit, in his Discovery of Witchcraft (see ).

406–7 Skim…cherne]
Johnson (ed. 1765) suggests transposing the lines, finding the sense confused. Later, in Steevens (ed. 1773) he explains: The mention of the mill [querne] seems out of place, for she is not now telling the good but the evil that he does but concludes there is no necessity of alteration. See textual n. and n. 406 (querne). Malone (ed. 1790): Perhaps the construction is—and sometimes make the breathless housewife labour in the quern, and bootless churn. Ritson (in Steevens ed. 1793): We must understand both [the skimming and the grinding] to be mischievous pranks. He skims the milk, when it ought not to be skimmed…and grinds the corn, when it is not wanted; at the same time perhaps throwing the flour about the house. Malone (in Boswell, ed. 1821): The charge against Puck is not that he skims the milk at an improper time, but that he steals the cream. Jonson says the same of Mab in his Entertainment at Althorpe [53–6 (7:122)]. Deighton (ed. 1891): Skim the cream from the milk and drink it up. But Phillips (ed. 1969): The skimming is ambiguous—perhaps he does it to help, but perhaps he does it to eat the cream.

406 sometimes]
See n. 408.

406 querne]
Gildon (1710, Glossary): Churn. Hanmer (ed. 1744, 6:Glossary) adds Also a mill. Johnson (1755), quoting this instance: A handmill. Capell (1783 [1774], 1:Glossary): Churn or Chern, the Vessel in which they make Butter. Halliwell (ed. 1856): Robin works at the hand-mill, sometimes with the desire to reward industry by assisting to grind the corn, sometimes in mere wantonness, regardless of whether the corn is in the quern or not, quoting various authors. Delius (ed. 1859), however, interprets querne as churn or kern = Butter churn. Puck labours mischievously in such a butter churn, so that the housewife churns in vain (Ger.). Moberly (ed. 1881) agrees, suggesting that perhaps Sh. did not accurately distinguish quern and churn…, and that the labour in the quern is the malignant exertion which hinders the butter from coming. Ridley (ed. 1934): Properly a hand-mill for corn; but its sudden introduction into the middle of dairy operations is odd, and one wonders whether it is not in fact some form of churn. OED (Quern1): A simple apparatus for grinding corn…also, a small hand-mill, and (Kirn, Sc. & North. dial., n.1 1): A churn.

407 bootlesse]
Johnson (1755, 1), quoting the instance at 612–13: Useless; unprofitable; unavailing; without advantage (OED, a.1 3). Morén (2000, pp. 23–7) argues that because of the ambiguous syntax bootlesse can refer to you (i.e. Puck) which suggests a barefoot devilish pouke, and that this introduces a sequence of punning innuendos.

408 sometime]
OED (adv. 1a): At one time or another, with the possibility of recurrence or repetition; now and then; occasionally, = sometimes 1. Common in the 16th and 17th centuries, now rare or Obs. See Franz §237, quoting this instance and 406. In MND the singular form is used ten times, the plural twice. Cf. 634.

408 beare no barme]
Phillips (ed. 1969): Fail to ferment. Knight (ed. 1839): Holland, in his translation of Pliny [Natural historie (1601) 2.145], speaks of the froth, or barm, that riseth from these ales or beers. The froth is either that arising during fermentation, which is yeast (OED, n.2 1a), or the head on poured ale (OED, n.2 2). Morén (2000, p. 25) suggests puns on beare/bare and barme/bosom; however, the latest instance given in OED for barm meaning bosom (n.1) is 1513.

409 Misselead…harme]
Thirlby (MS 1747–53): Head ’em into bogs or ditches or the like. Ignis fatuus. Jack’o’lantern. Halliwell (ed. 1856) compares Milton, PL 9.640, Misleads th’ amaz’d Night-wanderer from his way, first noted by H. J. Todd, Poetical Works of Milton, 1801. Verity (ed. 1893): Mislead, with false fire, referring to 924. Andrews (ed. 1993): The Fairy’s phrasing…anticipates the role Robin will play with the Night Wanderers who are coming to the wood. For the proverbial status of this characteristic, see Tilley R147, who quotes Tyndale (1531, The exposition of the fyrste Epistle of seynt Ihon, p. 139): The scripture…is become a maze unto them, in which they wander as in a mist, or (as we say) led by Robin Goodfellow, that they cannot come to the right way.

409 nightwanderers]
Brooks (ed. 1979): This compound (and cf. Puck as wanderer of the night [414], and the Fairy’s wandering, swifter than the moon’s sphere [375, 378],) is from Marlowe, Hero and Leander, 1.107 (2:434)…, and Seneca, Oedipus, l. 254. Cf. Ven. 825, night-wand’rers.

410 Hobgoblin]
Cotgrave (1611, Gobelin): Hob-goblin, Robin-good-fellow, Bug. OED (n. 1), quoting this instance: A mischievous, tricksy imp or sprite; another name for Puck or Robin Goodfellow. Briggs (1959, p. 15): Hobgoblins are rough, hairy spirits, which do domestic chores, work about farms, guard treasure, keep an eye on the servants, and generally act as guardian spirits of the home. Useful as they are, they are easily offended and often mischievous.

410 sweete]
Tyrwhitt (in Steevens ed. 1778): The epithet is by no means superfluous; as Puck alone was far from being an endearing appellation. It signified nothing better than fiend, or devil. Sidgwick (1908, p. 38) notes the euphemistic tendency to call powerful spirits by propitiatory names. Just as the Greeks called the Furies Eumenides, the benevolent ones, so is Robin called Good-fellow. See 2215 (honest Puck). See n. 100.

410 Puck]
Johnson (1755), quoting the instance at 1579: [perhaps the same with pug.] Some sprite among the fairies, common in romances. For OED (n.1), which quotes the instances at 410, 2215, 2219, see DP. Steevens (ed. 1793) notes that Golding calls Chymæra a pooke (9.766). See Bell (1852–64).

412–13 Are…aright]
For attempts to mend the meter see textual n. at 413, and conj., . Most, however, agree with Abbott §506: Lines with four accents, where there is an interruption in the line, are not uncommon.…A syllable or foot may be supplied by a gesture, etc., quoting this instance. Bates (ed. 1895): The pause was probably intentional…, to give Puck time to strike an impressive attitude.

413–14 Thou…night]
The mislineation in Qq is corrected by F1: see textual n., and .

415–26 I…loffe]
Starkie (1916, pp. 220–1), discussing Sh.’s wit and humor in Aristotelian terms of the pleasure derived from the clever use of metaphor allied with misleading the hearer, quotes this speech as the most complete example of the way in which in a sense, every metaphor, every jest, is a deception (see The Art of Rhetoric 3.10.1–8, esp. 3.10.6). Hunter (Shakespeare, 1962, pp. 16–17): As jester to Oberon, he shares some features with Shakespeare’s later jesters, Touchstone and Feste—detachment from the problems of those around him and attachment to his own off-beat conception of wit [quoting 1142–5]. Holland (ed. 1994): Robin’s description of himself focuses on his shape-changing, a metamorphic ability he displays throughout Dream; see also . Bicks (2003, p. 31): Each trick is an attempt to stop up the channels of female speech by negotiating inside and around female bodies: Puck ventriloquizes the neighing of the female colt, drowns out the drinking gossip, and interrupts the aunt’s sad tale by toppling her. His spying presence at an all-female gathering and his muffling of the gossip’s speech are fantasies of male intrusion and censorship. See also n. 923–4.

416 beane-fed]
Thirlby (MS 1747–53): f[orte] quia lascivior [perhaps hence more wanton]. Deighton (ed. 1891): Fed on beans, and so lusty and frolicsome; cp. the slang expression of the present day, full of beans, in the same sense. Thiselton (1903, p. 26) compares Cym. 2.5.16 (1354), full Acorn’d Boare.

416 beguile]
Johnson (1755, 1; 2): 1. To delude; to cheat.…2. To deceive (OED 1).

417 Neyghing…fole]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Assuming the form of a young filly and neighing like it. Thoms (1847, p. 982) gives examples of fairies assuming the form of a horse.

417 filly fole]
On composite words such as she-bear, man-child, filly-foal used to express gender see Franz §213, quoting this instance and defining the phrase as = female colt.

417 filly]
Johnson (1755, 2), quoting this instance: A young mare, opposed to a colt or young horse (OED 1). The f in filly in Q1 is a worn piece of type and was mistaken for a long s by the compositor of Q2 (who made the same error at 1438 and 1564). See textual n. Halliwell (ed. 1856): Silly is probably the right reading, in the sense of simple. Thiselton (1903, p. 26): Whether we should adopt the reading of [Q1] (filly for silly) will depend upon whether in contemporary usage foal was restricted to the female. As I am not aware of any such restriction, I incline to the [Q1] reading.

417 fole]
Johnson (1755, Foal): The offspring of a mare…. The custom now is to use colt for a young horse, and foal for a young mare (OED Foal n. 1).

418 gossippes bole]
In his comment on line 100 of L’Allegro in Milton’s Poems (1785) Warton gives the recipe for this beverage as ale, nutmeg, sugar, toast, and roasted crabs or apples. It was called LAMBS-WOOL. Wright (ed. 1877): Originally a christening cup; for a gossip or godsib was properly a sponsor. Hence, from signifying those who were associated in the festivities of a christening, it came to denote generally those who were accustomed to make merry together; he compares Err. 5.1.406 (1895), Gossips feast. Delius (ed. 1859) compares Sh.’s other use of the phrase in Rom. 3.5.174 (2219).

418 gossippes]
Johnson (1755, 1, 2): 1. One who answers for the child in baptism; which degenerated into 2. A tippling companion, quoting this instance. OED (n. 2b), quoting this instance first: Applied to a woman’s female friends invited to be present at a birth. Brooks (ed. 1979): A tattling woman. Bicks (2003, p. 28): What began as an ungendered term connoting spiritual affinity degenerated in the sixteenth century into a solely feminine and primarily derogatory label, with the midwife at the nexus of the shift.

419 very]
Johnson (1755, 1): True; real (OED, a. 3a). See Abbott §16; Blake 3.3.3.6 (b). Similar uses are at 1757, 2032.

419 rosted crabbe]
Pope (ed. 1725): Crab apple. Thirlby (MS 1725–33) compares LLL 5.2.925 (2892). Ellacombe (1878, p. 15): The Crab was held in far more esteem in the sixteenth century than it is with us. The roasted fruit served with hot ale…was a favourite Christmas dish.

419–20 crabbe…bob]
The Clarkes (1879, p. 673) quote these lines as a sample of an imperfectly rhyming couplet, along with 644–5 and 2095. Cercignani (1981, pp. 113–14), however, identifies these lines, and 2095 (pappe…hoppe), as rhyming, with bob pronounced as bab.

420 bob]
Johnson (1755), quoting this instance: To play backward and forward; to play loosely against any thing. OED (v.3 1): To move up and down like a buoyant body in water, quoting 1623 Cockeram III, Tantalus…hath Apples bobbing at his nose. Deighton (ed. 1891): Jump up; thus spilling the ale as she is about to drink it. Brooks (ed. 1979): Knock, as he bobs up and down in the liquid.

421 dewlop]
Johnson (1755, Dewlap 1, 2): 1. The flesh that hangs down from the throat of oxen [OED 1a].…2. It is used in Shakespeare for a lip flaccid with age, in contempt, quoting this instance. Schmidt (1874): Hanging breast. OED (1b), quoting this instance first: [Applied] humorously to pendulous folds of flesh about the human throat. Holland (ed. 1994) notes that this is OED’s first instance of its transfer from cattle to humans, and referring to Theseus’s deawlapt hounds (1643), suggests that this link metamorphoses the observed immediacy of this old woman’s throat into an Ovidian image. The term may mean lap; see OED, Lap, n.1 2b, and cf. Ham. 3.2.112–19 (1966–72). Cf. also Cotgrave (1611, Landie): The deaw-lap in a womans Priuities, and Minsheu (1599, Crica de muger): The dew-lap in the priuie part of a maide or virgin. Robin may be pouring the ale into her lap rather than just on her throat.

422–6 The…loffe]
Douce (1807, 1:182) quotes from Margaret Cavendish, Duchess of Newcastle’s The Queen of Fairies (Poems, 1653): The goodwife sad squats down upon a stool, Not at all thinking it was Hob the fool, And frowning sits, then Hob gives her a slip, And down she falls, whereby she hurts her hip.

422 wisest Aunt]
Steevens (ed. 1778): Procuress, comparing WT 4.3.11 (1679), and adding, The wisest aunt may mean the most sentimental bawd (OED, Aunt, n. 3 for bawd and prostitute, quoting first Middleton, 1607). Most, however, agree with Ritson (1783, p. 43): But this conjecture is much too wanton, and injurious to the word aunt, which, in this place at least, certainly means no other than an innocent old woman (OED, n. 2, quoting this instance only). Heron (1785, pp. 109–10): [Steevens’ note] is truly Warburtonian, as the expression taken in its direct sense is much more humorous. Such notes make one sick. We shall by and bye be informed that, when Hamlet says Mother, he means capital bawd, because Mother Needham’s character is well known. Schmidt (1874, Aunt): Term for an old gossip, quoting this instance only; Idem (1875, Wise 1): A w[ise] woman = a woman skilled in hidden arts, as fortune-telling, palmistry etc., comparing this instance. He is ignored. Verity (ed. 1893): Wisest; said ironically. Furness (ed. 1895): The most sedate old woman. Brooks (ed. 1979): The greatest wiseacre among the cronies.

422 saddest tale]
Thirlby (MS 1725–33): Gravest. Schmidt (1875, Sad, 1): Sorrowful, melancholy. Wright (ed. 1877): Most serious. Evans (ed. 1974): Soberest. Caldecott (MS 1813–33) compares Mac. 3.4.64–5 (1334–5). Drake (1817, 1:268) quotes WT 2.1.25 (618), A sad Tale’s best for Winter. Brooks (ed. 1979) compares Lyly, Endimion 3.4.7–9 (3:47). See also n. 143.

423 three foote]
OED (a. a), quoting this instance first: = three-footed.

424 bumme]
Johnson (1755, 1), quoting this instance: The buttocks; the part on which we sit. OED (Bum, n.1 1) issues a warning, Not in polite use, then quotes Johnson’s definition, adding the posteriors, and citing this instance. Editors make various substitutions for the awkward word. Bowdler (ed. 1807): , and followed by Planché (ed. [1840]), the Clarkes (ed. 1864), Verity (ed. 1893), Lawler (ed. 1909); Reynolds (ed. 1816): seat followed by Kean (ed. [1856]); De Wickedé (ed. 1875): when; Chambers (ed. 1897): , then; Lyde (ed. 1897): —— explaining, The pause completes the verse.

425 tailour cryes]
Johnson (ed. 1765): The custom of crying tailor at a sudden fall backwards, I think I remember to have observed. He that slips beside his chair falls as a taylor squats upon his board. The Oxford Editor [Hanmer] and Dr. Warburton after him, read and rails, or cries [see textual n.], plausibly, but I believe not rightly. Besides, the trick of the fairy is represented as producing rather merriment than anger. Malone (ed. 1790, 10:577): This phrase perhaps originated in a pun. Your tail is now on the ground. See Camden’s Remaines, 1614. Proverbs. Between two stools the tayle goeth to the ground [p. 304] (Tilley S900). ODEP, under B, quotes the earliest instance from Seneca (the Elder), Controversia 3.189, Duabus sellis sedit. The proverb was popular in France: cf. Cotgrave (1611, Demeuré): Il est demeuré entre deux selles à terre. Betweene two stooles his tayle is come to ground. Halliwell (ed. 1856): The expression is probably one of contempt, equivalent to thief, and possibly a corruption of the older word taylard, which occurs in the romance of Richard Cœur de Lion,…The Elizabethan use of the term, as one of contempt, appears to be confirmed by the following passage in [Cornu-copiae,] Pasquil’s Nightcap, 1612, [by William Fennor]—Theeving is now an occupation made, Though men the name of tailor doe it give [p. 1, sig. B1]. Others giving examples of tailors’ bad repute include Cuningham (ed. 1905), Watt (ed. 1906), and Lobban (ed. 1913). Deighton (ed. 1891): Probably [the expression] would be used in an angry tone to the person who had been clumsy enough to upset her, as we still say a regular tailor of a bungling fellow; and cobbler and botcher of a clumsy workman. Harrison (ed. 1937) recounts a tale of a drunken widow who had a stool pulled from her in 1595 which may be a topical jest alluded to here.
Among the wilder speculations, Jourdain’s (MS c. 1860) has some support (see following n.): The meaning of this is breaks wind. To hide the noise she coughs and cries Tailor to exempt her from paying forfit. Others do not impress. A. H. (1887, p. 42): One often sees that a sudden tumble eventuates in the rent of a necessary garment, so tailor is indicative of a summons for assistance. Warren (1887, p. 264): A. H.’s interpretation is untenable, because a sudden fall backwards will not split petticoats as it will trousers. Furness (ed. 1895): The slight substitution of an e for an o in the word tailor will show that, as boys in swimming take a header, the wisest Aunt was subjected to the opposite. Hulme (1958, p. 722): Tailor I suggest could have for Shakespeare’s contemporaries the sense penis or vagina (OED, Tail, n.1 5c). Idem (1962, p. 99): In Puck’s speech the sense posterior seems to be required (OED, Tail, n.1 5a). Lee (1975, p. 57): As she falls, at least three events occur simultaneously: (a) her tale or story is interrupted or broken, (b) her tail (buttocks) seems broken—hence the need for a mender of both tale and tail; and (c) her position on the ground, with legs in the air (in the person of the miming Puck), can only be interpreted as an invitation to the sexual act. And her cry of tailor is a call that echoes the Elizabethan obscenity associated with that tradesman. Andrews (ed. 1993): [S]ince tail was a common term for the male member…Tailor could also refer to a lustful man. See conj., .

425 falles…coffe]
For the interpretation that she coughs to conceal a fart, see Jourdain (MS c. 1860) above. Morén (2000, pp. 30–1) notes Erasmus’s advice for a boy who needs to fart in public: He muste disguise the sounde by coughyng (The ciuilitie of childehode, 1560, B5r). See also n. 425–6. Deighton (ed. 1891): Is seized with a fit of coughing. Hulme (1958, p. 724): It may well be that a proverb recorded by Ray in 1678 [p. 82] was current in Shakespeare’s time: His tail will catch the chin-cough (whooping-cough). Spoken of one that sits on the ground (Tilley T12). No earlier instance is given by Tilley or ODEP.

425–6 coffe…loffe]
On the use of variants with f for original h…in final position, see Cercignani (1981, p. 342). On the variation of au to ŏ in loffe, see Dobson (1968) §87 n. 1; Cercignani (1981, p. 211). OED records coffe and loffe as variant spellings of cough and laugh, but gives no examples of the first and quotes only 426 (v. 1a) for the second. Honigmann (1965, p. 118) cites coffe as a Shn. spelling; the only other occurrence in Sh. is in the 1622 Q Oth. 4.2.29 (2721). The spelling loffe occurs in Sh. only here. Capell (1783, 2.3:104): A rustic sounding of—laugh; to whose spelling all the elder editions assimilate—cough, and it’s sound should incline to it. Halliwell (ed. 1856): The ancient pronunciation of the word. The only other instances of the verb in the play have the normal spelling—laughing (409), and laught (504). Possibly the variants are used for the sake of a bilingual pun: Italian Loffa (pl. loffe) means a fizle, a fiste, a close fart (Florio, 1598); cf. Morén (n. 425). Brooks (ed. 1979), although modernizing the spelling of coffe (see textual n.): The out-of-the-way forms, loffe…waxen for wax, increase (archaic inflexion), and neeze for sneeze (unique in Shakespeare), have evidently been chosen to suit the episode described. See also n. 427.

426 Quire]
Johnson (1755, 1): A body of singers; a chorus (OED, Choir, quire, n. 6, quoting this instance). Caldecott (MS 1813–33): The gossip chorus. Dyce (ed. 1864, 9:Glossary): A company, an assembly. Deighton (ed. 1891): Another spelling of choir. Foakes (ed. 1984): They laugh in consort like a chorus.

426 hould…loffe]
Hoadly (MS post 1734) compares Milton, L’Allegro (32), And Laughter holding both his sides.

426 hould]
Since the twelve other uses of hold (holde, holdst, etc.) in the play are spelled with o and not ou, this unique form may indicate a different pronunciation, in keeping with the other aberrations in the speech. There is a variant spelling at 2063.

427 waxen…myrth]
The Clarkes (ed. 1864): Increase in their fun; grow louder in their jollity.

427 waxen]
Johnson (ed. 1765): [E]ncrease, as the moon waxes. Rann (ed. 1787): Yexen—hick up (OED, Yex 2). Farmer (in Steevens ed. 1793): Probably corrupted from yoxen, or yexen. Yoxe Saxon. to hiccup. Steevens adds: The meaning of the passage before us will then be, that the objects of Puck’s waggery laughed till their laughter ended in a yex or hiccup. It should be remembered, in support of this conjecture, that Puck is at present speaking with an affectation of ancient phraseology. Though many cite Farmer, only one editor adopts his conjecture. See textual n. Abbott §332: [The old form -en] is rarely used by Shakespeare, and only archaically. OED (Waxen, v.1), quoting this instance, suggests this form may sometimes be due to intentional grammatical archaism. See Franz §187 and Blake 4.2.2 (d). Bradley (1916, 2:552) objects: But the frequent use of waxen by Arthur Golding (whose language is not otherwise archaistic) seems to indicate that it was apprehended rather as a poetical synonym of waxe than as an inflected form of that verb.

427 neeze]
Rann (ed. 1787): Sneeze. Verplanck (ed. 1845) sees it as the antiquated spelling,…retained as late as our common version of the Bible. OED: Now north. dial. and Sc. According to OED, sneeze is a late developing form, which may have been felt as a strengthened form of neeze. Both forms were in use in the 16th and 17th centuries. Wright (ed. 1877): Cotgrave gives both forms, Esternuer. To neeze, or sneeze. See also n. 425–6.

428 A…there]
Morén (2000, p. 31), questionably, paraphrases: A more sexually wanton whore was never seduced there. He gives various analogies for sexual innuendo of each word.

428 wasted]
Johnson (1755, Waste 5): To spend; to consume (OED, v. 8). Gordon (ed. 1910) adds: Not necessarily without profit, comparing AYL 2.4.94–5 (881–2). Mahood (1984, p. 128) on MV 3.4.12 (1738): With no hint of an unfavourable meaning, but with a suggestion of making time pass quickly, quoting Tmp. 5.1.303–5 (2300–2).

429 But…Oberon]
Many find this line lacking a syllable and offer emendations, or suggestions. See textual n. and conj., . Johnson (ed. 1765), explaining the reading of the early eds.: The word Fairy or Faery, was sometimes of three syllables, as often in Spenser. Seymour (1805, 1:43): As Dr. Johnson observes, sometimes a trisyllable with our old writers; but never, I believe, with the accent as here placed on the second syllable Făēry; he proposes adding for before here. Wilson (ed. 1924): The fact that Q[1]. never uses this spelling elsewhere is strong evidence that Shakespeare intended the trisyllabic pronunciation here. But Q1 uses the very close spelling Faiery (498, 1419) and Faieries (1554) where the words are clearly disyllables. Abbott §484 gives many examples of words with oo which are disyllabized, including this instance. Furness (ed. 1895): No change is absolutely necessary. The break in the line affords, I think, sufficient pause to fill up the metre. Brooks (ed. 1979): The marshal-like cry of room! fills the place of a beat and a light syllable.Arguing for their adoption of make room, Wells & Taylor (1987, p. 281): Sh. nowhere else treats room as disyllabic. Moreover, although Shakespeare uses the imperative (Room!) on three occasions, elsewhere it always occurs at the beginning of a sentence rather than inside it; opting to follow Pope (ed. 1725), they list three other uses of make room: Jn. 1.1.255 (268), MV 4.1.16 (1921), Ado 2.1.84–5 (491–2). Most editors accept the Q1 reading. See also n. 378.

429 roome]
OED (n.1 3. To make room. b): To make way, yield place.…with imperative suppressed. Schmidt (1875, 1) lists four other instances where Sh. uses room elliptically: LLL 5.2.697 (2653); JC 3.2.166 (1703), 3.2.168 (1705), 5.4.16 (2621).

430–1, 434–5 And…gon. | Ob.…Tytania]
In these two places F1 put Qq’s single verse line as two lines in order to fill in space, because the compositor had erred in casting off for sig. N2vb where these lines occur. See textual nn.

431 Would]
Schmidt (1875, Will II, 1): I would optatively, followed by a subjunctive.…I omitted, citing this instance. OED (Will, v.1 36), quoting 2051: With ellipsis of 1st pers. pron. as an expression of longing = I wish, O that. See also Franz §306. Cf. n. 65.

432–3 Enter…hers]
Munkelt (1987, p. 254): The conventional use of the stage doors for the symmetrical entry of separate groups makes the dispute of the two characters visual. She finds the visual image ironically contradicted by the conversion of the normal Well met to Ill met, and sees significance in the parallel SD at 373–4: This double entry of a follower each of Titania and Oberon not only anticipates their quarrel on a lower level but also seems to depict it less seriously in the chiasmus of the two parties: The fairy uses Oberon’s, Puck enters by Titania’s door, perhaps foreshadowing a reconciliation. On the entrance from different doors, see n. 373–4. On traine, see n. 395.

434–6 Ill…hence]
Wells (ed. 1967): The entrance of Titania and Oberon is marked by a change from rhyming [couplets] to blank verse.

434–5 Ill…Tytania]
Warren (1983, p. 9): Few characters in Shakespeare have such an arresting opening line as this.…The dramatic vigour of this fairy quarrel requires emphasis…, because much influential modern criticism does not take the fairies at face value, as characters in a play, but as a metaphor for something quite different.

434 Ill met]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Instead of being pleased to encounter her at the usual time for the meeting of fairies, Oberon is now vexed, and reverses the ordinary salutation, well met! (OED, Meet, v. 4b). Sh. uses well met often, but ill met only here. Cf. 1702. See also n. 814.

434 moonelight]
See n. 222–3.

435 proud]
Schmidt (1875, 4): Selfish, cold, unkind (unattested in OED). Miller (1940, p. 67), finding Schmidt’s definition inadequate…in this context, sees an implication of another common meaning, which Dr. Johnson [1755, 8] expresses in the phrase, salacious: eager for the male (OED 8). Johnson (1755, 3 and 4) could also apply: 3. Arrogant; haughty; impatient (OED 1); 4. Daring; presumptuous (OED 4).

436 Qu.]
Steevens (ed. 1778): As to the Fairy Queen, (says Mr. Warton in his Observations on Spenser [1762, 1:58]) considered apart from the race of fairies, the notion of such an imaginary personage was very common. Chaucer, in his Rime of Sir Thopas, mentions her [788–816], together with a Fairy land [WBT 857–62]. See , . The apparent exception to the designation of characters in this play by their personal names is explained by the compositor’s type-shortage. See Kennedy (1998, pp. 186–7), and .

436 What…Oberon]
OED (What, B.1. int. 2a): An exclamation of surprise or astonishment (sometimes mixed with indignation): usually followed by a question. Scott (ed. 1898): Equivalent to: Who is this?—Oh it is Oberon the jealous king. See also 806, 807, 892, 1301, 1303, 1316, 1320, 1355, 1537, 2082, 2116.

436 Iealous]
Johnson (1755, 1): Suspicious in love. Doran (ed. 1959): Envious (OED 4b). Miller (1940, pp. 68–9), arguing that Oberon is suspicious of Titania’s relations with the changeling and also eagerly desirous to regain her love, cites OED 2: Ardently amorous; covetous of the love of another. See also OED, a. 4: Troubled by the belief, suspicion, or fear that the good which one desires for oneself has been or may be diverted to another;… a. in love or affection, esp. in sexual love:… distrustful of the faithfulness of wife, husband, or lover.

436 Fairy skippe]
Editors from 1691 to the present have been divided over whether to emend Fairy to Fairies (see textual n.); the question is important in bearing upon who is being addressed, and upon the staging. There are four candidates for a single character addressed: (1) Capell (1783, 2.3:104): The Fairy spoke to by his Queen…is her leading fairy, her gentleman-usher, whose moving-off had been a signal for all the rest of the train; no need then of—Fairies, the unauthoriz’d alteration of the third modern [Theobald, ed. 1733] and followers. A similar notion is elaborated by Nicholson (1870, p. 56), who envisages the lilliputian potentates providing an antic and mirthful imitation of ceremonies of state, so that Titania addresses a sportive usher, or it may be officer of the guard leading the procession, comparable to the one going in front of Oberon’s triumphal entry in Jonson’s masque (7:351). (2) That the Fairy could be the one who had been speaking to Robin is dismissed by Dyce (ed. 1857), but asserted by Thiselton (1903, p. 27): Titania bids this Fairy to waste no more time in the company of Robin, but to proceed with the function of dewing her orbs. (3) Collier (ed. 1842) supposes that Oberon might be addressed, but according to Dyce (ed. 1857) this cannot be reconciled with the line which immediately follows,—I have forsworn his bed and company. (4) Andrews (ed. 1989) suggests Titania may be addressing herself.
Among those favoring the plural, even if relying on imagined MSS in support of their opinion, Collier (ed. 1842): There seems no reason why she should speak to a particular fairy, when surrounded by her whole train. The Rev. W. Harness suggests to me that the proper reading is Fairies, keep hence, and that the error has arisen from the person who copied the play writing by his ear, and not by his eye. Dyce (ed. 1857): The mistake…was probably occasioned by Fairy occurring a little above; Fairies is parallelled by the command Fairies away (520); Fairy sent is a similar error (see n. 1575); and Fairy is unlikely to be an archaic plural when Sh. uses fairies elsewhere [520, 974, 2166]. Walker (1860, 1:265): There is no comma after Fairy in the folio…; probably, therefore, it stood in the MS. Fairies skip hence; which might very easily be corrupted into Fairie, were it only through the justling of the two s’s. Keightley (1867, pp. 51–2): In speaking and reading we often slur over, elide or suppress the final consonant if the following word begin with a consonant;…The most usual case is when…the first word ends and the next begins with the same consonant. Despite Dyce’s objection to the archaism, Hunter (ed. 1870) takes Fairy as plural, quoting Chaucer’s Wife of Bath’s Tale 859–61 (see ).

436 skippe]
Finding the word undignified Collier (ed. 1842) is attracted by Harness’s suggested keep. Dyce (ed. 1857), comparing 1614 and 2205, conjectures trip. (See conj., ). Nicholson (1870, p. 56): The movements would be brisk and gambolling, perhaps made even to dance music. Brissenden (1972, p. 89): Dancing was one of the main occupations of the Elizabethan fairies, and their principal means of getting from one place to another. They do not walk or run. They skip [436], they hop, they gambol [983], they trip away [2205]. One seventeenth-century commentator, Robert Kirk, remarks irresistibly on their paroxisms of antic corybantic jollity [An Essay of the…invisible People, Edinburgh, 1815, p. 8].

437 I…company]
A drastic action; cf. Gloster’s reaction to the possibility of his wife’s dishonorable conduct: I banish her my Bed, and Companie (2H6 2.1.193 (949).

437 forsworne]
Schmidt (1874, Forswear, 1a): To refuse or renounce upon oath, to swear that one will have nothing to do with a person or thing.

438 wanton]
OED (a. and n. Etym.): Literally undisciplined. Several meanings of the adjective given in OED might be suggested here: 1a: Ungoverned, rebellious; 2: Lascivious,…Also, in milder sense, given to amorous dalliance; 3: Sportive; 4: Spoiled, petulant; 6a: Capricious. For the noun here, Schmidt (1875, 1) favors One apt to play or dally (cf. OED, n. 2). See also OED (n. 1): A person, esp. a child, spoiled by over-indulgence and excessive leniency. See also nn. 474, 505. Deighton (ed. 1891): Alluding to her love of Theseus. Scott (ed. 1898): Referring to her love for the changeling boy. Kittredge (ed. 1939): Not unchaste woman, but in the general sense of headstrong creature. Miller (1940, p. 66–9) argues that she is unchaste in her relations with the changeling: see n. 512–13. Wright & Lamar (ed. 1958): Oberon calls Titania an undisciplined creature because she stubbornly refuses to do as he wishes. Wright (1968, p. 28): Irresponsible, with an overtone of loose, woman. Foakes (ed. 1984): Rebel. Properly, a spoiled child, someone who is unmanageable.

438–9 Am…Lady]
Moberly (ed. 1881): When you make me your one lady, you shall be my one lord. Deighton (ed. 1891): If, as you say, you are my lord, I ought to be your lady, but that I cannot be since you have been vowing love to Phillida, and therefore you cannot be my lord. Ward (1999, p. 209): When Oberon demands that Titania respect his patriarchal authority, he is told that such respect must be earned by performance, and that his own negligent magistracy disqualifies any such claims.

438–9 Lord…Lady]
Brooks (ed. 1979): Husband, with his authority; wife, with her claim to his devotion (OED, Lord 4, Lady 7). Holland (ed. 1994): Husband and wife, domination and submission. Dubrow (1999, p. 144), unpersuasively: Why do critics…tend to assume that Titania is Oberon’s lawful wedded wife? His reference to himself as her lord certainly lends itself to a range of glosses other than that one, and if there are no clocks in the forest, neither, presumably, are there many churches. Cf. n. 90 (Lordshippe).

439–56 but…iealousie]
Lloyd (in Singer, ed. 1856, pp. 431–2) finds a parallel with the exchange between Chaucer’s Pluto and Proserpina; see , . Irving & Marshall (ed. 1888): Shakespeare does not seem to have had any legendary authority for Oberon’s flirtations. Do not these lines rather militate against the idea of Oberon and Titania being such very diminutive people? Could a mannikin hope to impress the amorous Phillida? Again, Oberon’s retort on Titania seems to imply that she was capable of inspiring a passion in that prototype of all Don Juans, Theseus. Perhaps these fairies were supposed to possess the power of assuming the human shape and size; or, what is more likely, to Shakespeare they were so entirely creatures of the imagination that they never assumed to his mind’s eye any concrete form. Furness (ed. 1895): In the first place, if we must resort to a prosaic interpretation, Marshall’s query is answered by the fact that Oberon assumed the shape of Corin; in the second place, one of the strokes of humour in this whole scene, between atomies who can creep into acorn-cups, and for whom the waxen thigh of a bee affords an ample torch, lies in the assumption by them of human powers and of superhuman importance. Not only is Titania jealous of the bouncing Amazon, but this their quarrel influences the moon in the sky, changes the seasons, and affects disastrously the whole human race. There is a touch of the same humour, but deeply coarsened, in the scandal which Gulliver’s conduct started when he was at the court of [Lilliput]. Fripp (1930, p. 116): These strange amours, which in Ovid have still their mythological origin and significance clinging to them, telling of the relations and transmutations of natural forces, were treated as fairy-lore by the Old Masters [cites Burton, The Anatomy of Melancholy 1.2.1.2]; and as such they are handled by Shakespeare, who presents the quarrels and infidelities of Oberon and Titania as those of spirit-beings below the human.

439–43 but…Phillida]
Warburton (ed. 1747, at 1849) sees this as a compliment to Sh.’s friend Spenser, intimating that the pastorals of that poet were so sweet, that it was a superior being under the disguise of a mortal who composed them.

440 When…land]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Occasions on which you have secretly left the fairy land, your proper domain.

440 hast]
Defending F1’s wast (see textual n.), Keightley (1857, p. 263) alone argues: The critics give a wrong sense to the words of Titania, whose meaning is that Oberon did so once, while they would make her say that such was his habit. On the use of be or have with intransitive verbs of motion, see Abbott §295, Franz §631 and Blake 4.2.7 (a), and compare 1074 and 1681 against 570.

440 stollen]
On the monosyllabic or elided pronunciation, see n. 392.

441–3 shape of Corin…Phillida]
For these pastoral names, Neil (ed. 1878) cites Horace Odes 2.4.14 [Phyllis/Phyllidis], and England’s Helicon (1600) [for] several poems relating to Phillida and Coridon quoting lines from Breton’s Phillida and Coridon [ed. Rollins, 1935, 1:27–8] which Venezky (1951, p. 142) also cites from its context in the entertainment of Queen Elizabeth at Elvetham in 1591. Vergil’s Corydon and Phyllida (Eclogues 2.1; 3.78) are cited in Anon (ed. 1886). Cuningham (ed. 1905) first notes the poem in Tottel’s Miscellany (1557) entitled Harpelus complaynt of Phillidaes loue bestowed on Corin, who loued her not and denied him, that loued her (ed. Rollins, rev. ed. 1966, 1:132; the poem also appears in England’s Helicon, ed. cit., 1:40–3). A pastorall of phillyda & Choryn was presented and enacted before her maiestie by her highnes servauntes at Greenwich in the evening of December 26, 1584 (Feuillerat, 1908, p. 365).
Sitwell (1948, p. 191): There is a strange darkening and concentration, changing of shape, from Corin to the sound of Corne. Bate (1993, p. 137): Oberon imitates Jupiter in his way of metamorphosing himself for the purposes of seduction; (e.g. in Golding’s Ovid 2.526–41; see ). Holland (ed. 1994) unconvincingly claims that Oberon is accused of wasting his time as amateur rustic poet and musician rather than of making love to Phillida.

442 pipes of corne]
Johnson (1755, Corn-pipe): A pipe made by slitting the joint of a green stalk of corn. Ritson (in Steevens MS 1793–1800) notes Chaucer’s pipes made of grene corn (HF 1224). Rann (ed. 1787) glosses oaten, the more usual pipe of pastoral literature. Wright (ed. 1877) compares When Shepheards pipe on Oaten strawes (LLL 5.2.903 [2869]). Sprague (ed. 1896) suggests Sh. might have had in mind the oaten pipe (avena) of Vergil Ecl. 1.2). Fripp (1930, p. 106) compares also Ovid Met. 1.677 (Golding 1.842, see ). For corne, see n. 469.

442 versing]
Johnson (1755, To Verse, v. a.), quoting this instance: To tell in verse; to relate poetically (OED, v.1 2, quoting this instance). Hunter (ed. 1870): Reciting love verses. Gill (ed. 1981): Singing love songs. On the changing of nouns and adjectives into verbs (usually transitive) see Abbott §290, quoting this instance. Cf. 487 (childing).

443–7 Why…wedded]
Deighton (ed. 1891): What brings you here all the way from the plains of India, except that, etc., i.e. your only reason for having taken this long journey is your desire to be present at the marriage of your mistress, Hippolyta.

444 Come…India]
Halliwell (1841, pp. 26–7), possibly influenced by the juxtaposition of essays on Pygmies and fairies by Ritson (1831), conjectures that the location was suggested by the abode of the Pygmies, in the vttermost mountaines of Indie (Batman vppon Bartholome, 1582, 18.86, Addition). Hunt (1844, p. 180): Shakspeare understood the charm of remoteness in poetry, as he did everything else. Oberon has been dancing on the sunny steeps looking towards Cathay, where the Chineses drive Their cany waggons light [Milton, PL 3.438–9]. Hendricks (1996, pp. 45–8) demonstrates that Oberon is linked to the exotic East, commonly referred to generically as India, in Huon of Burdeaux and Greene’s The Scottish Historie of James the fourth, and notes (p. 47) that the ancestor of Spenser’s Oberon all India obayd (FQ 2.10.72.5).

444 steppe]
Most editors favor the reading of Q2F steepe (see textual n.). As a variant spelling of Step (OED, n1, esp. 7a), Q1 is defended by Capell (1783, 2.3:104): Step…is of more authority and also of more simplicity than a word the moderns have follow’d; and by Onions (1911; 1966): Prob[ably] to be read furthest step (= utmost limit of travel or exploration, the furthest one has been). Schmidt (1875) glosses Steppe as a vast uninhabited plain (?), although White (ed. 1858) had already pointed out that the word was not known in Shakespeare’s day (OED, first example 1671). This line is quoted, giving Q1 as an alternate spelling, under OED, Steep, n. 1a: The declivity or slope of a mountain, hill, cliff; a steep or precipitous place. Without authority adduced, Hunter (ed. 1870): The word steep has reference to the curvature of the earth.

445 forsooth]
Cotgrave (1611, Voire dea): (Ironically) yes forsooth, yea ywis. Johnson (1755, 1), quoting 1255–7: In truth; certainly; very well. It is used almost always in an ironical or contemptuous sense (OED, adv. 1a, b).

445 bounsing]
Only Shn. usage (TNK 3.5.26 is probably by Fletcher). Johnson (1755, To Bounce, 4), quoting this instance: To be bold, or strong. OED (ppl. a.): In various senses of the verb relating to…vigorous or ungainly movement. Often…big rather than elegant or graceful. (In many of the quotations the exact shade of meaning is doubtful). The usage in Cotgrave (1611, Foüet) has a strong sexual connotation: Il n’a pas le foüet pour mener cette trompe. He is not sufficient for so bouncing a wench. Schmidt (1874, Bounce, vb., 2): To be noisy, to bully, to swagger:…(or is it = stout, plump?) Page (ed. 1897): Strapping, strong, stout. Titania probably means to convey chiefly the idea of unfeminine, masculine. Compare the next line. Chaucer…calls Ipolita…the hardy quene [KnT 882]. See .

445 Amason]
Hippolyta is Queen of a race of female warriors alleged to have lived in Scythia. Cf. OED 1, 2, 3; see DP; see .

446 Your…loue]
Chambers (ed. 1897, p. 191) comments on this line and 459: When the third foot is a pyrrhic, the rest of the line is divided into two equal parts, and thus a markedly antithetic rhythm is…produced. Phillips (ed. 1969): Love between Hippolyta and any being who can be equated with Oberon does not appear in the usual legends.

446 buskind]
OED (ppl. a. 1): Shod or covered with buskins [half-boots], quoting this use first. Rolfe (ed. 1877): Neither this word nor buskin is found elsewhere in Sh. Deighton (ed. 1891): A boot reaching to the middle of the leg. It was worn in war, the chase, etc., and by tragic actors in heroic characters, with very high heels serving to add stateliness to the figure. Thomson (1952, p. 78): Perhaps in Shakespeare’s mind some memory was stirring of the huntress Diana or the Carthaginian girls who had the custom when hunting to bind their legs high up with a buskin, adding in a footnote, Alte suras vincire cothurno, Aeneid, I. 337—a passage Shakespeare appears to have known. Cf. also Golding 3.197; see . The buskins provided for the Amazons in the mask presented at court in the Christmas season of 1578–9 were of oringe cullor velvet; buskins and pumpes for nymphes & shepherdes in 1584–5 cost twenty-five shillings (Feuillerat, 1908, pp. 286, 371).

446 mistresse]
Johnson (1755): 5. A woman beloved and courted; 7. A whore; a concubine. Scott (ed. 1898), questionably: Sweetheart, without any bad association such as the word bears now (OED 10a). See also OED (11): A woman who illicitly occupies the place of a wife.

447 Theseus]
Disyllabic: see n. 26.

447 must]
Abbott §314: Sometimes used by Shakespeare to mean no more than definite futurity, like our is to in He is to be here to-morrow, citing this instance and MV 4.1.182 (2093) as being without the notion of compulsion. Dey (1901, p. 481) objects to both examples: Is there not an idea of compulsion in must as here used? (cf. OED, v.1 3b). Blake (4.3.7.10) finds the rather weaker sense of necessity verging towards futurity approaching certain futurity in this instance. See also Franz §607. See 1024.

448 To…prosperitie]
Deighton (ed. 1891): The presence of benevolent fairies at births, christenings, weddings, was supposed to bring good fortune, as that of malevolent ones to bring misfortunes. See n. 2193–8. Farrow (ed. 1972): To bless them with happy marriage and fine children.

449–55 How…Antiopa]
Black (1965, p. 37) believes: The real joke in this bickering is that neither Theseus nor Hippolyta is aware of their tiny adorers’ existence; but cf. 441–3. Blits (2003, pp. 58–9): As seems true of the fairies’ powers in general, her ability to affect people may be unknown to those she affects.

449 for shame]
Schmidt (1875, Shame, subst. 1): = blush for yourself, be ashamed (OED, Shame, n. 13b).

450 Glaunce…Hippolita]
Hint censoriously that I have won Hippolyta’s favor (see Schmidt, 1874 Glance, vb. 1b, subst. 2, and Credit, subst. 2; cf. OED, Credit, n. 6). Andrews (ed. 1989): Object to my favour. McDonald (ed. 2000): Mention my standing with Hippolyta. But Evans (ed. 1974): Cast aspersion on my good name by accusing me with Hippolyta (cf. OED, Credit, n. 5).

451 Theseus]
Trisyllabic: see n. 26.

452–5 Didst…Antiopa]
For Ruskin’s exculpation of Theseus’s amours, see . Connecting Renaissance allegorizations of Theseus’s labyrinth with the image here implied, Sasaki (1986, p. 77) suggests that Theseus could have been seen as a symbol of lust rather than of rational order. See .

452 not thou]
F1’s smoothing of the sound and rhythm by reversing the order of these words was adopted without comment by most editors for more than 300 years. The Q1 reading has held sway since post 1959. See textual n.

452 glimmering]
Johnson (1755, Glimmer 1): To shine faintly. OED (ppl. a. 1) quotes this instance, but refers for the meaning to the verb, where either 1. To shine brightly, to glitter or 2. To give a faint or intermittent light could apply. See 1084, 2175. Steevens (ed. 1778): Faintly illuminated by stars. Warburton (ed. 1747) attempted to defend his emendation (see textual n.): The meaning is, She conducted him in the appearance of fire through the dark night; but was mocked by Heath (1765, p. 45): To represent the Queen of Fairies herself as supplying the place of a Jack o’ the lanthorn makes a most burlesque contrast with that dignity of character with which the poet hath cloathed her, and is indeed perfectly ridiculous. Cf. n. 923–4.

453–5 Perigenia…Antiopa]
The stories of all these women are told in Plutarch’s Life of Theseus; see , . Ridley (ed. 1934): The parentage of Theseus’ various lovers and the circumstances of their relations with him are here of small importance. But see etc.

453 Perigenia]
Theobald (1726, pp. 159–60), discussing an editor’s duty to correct corruptions in the text, first calls attention to the alteration of the name from its form in Greek (see textual n.), and then restates his opinions (ed. 1733): Thus all the Editors, either not knowing, or not attending to, the History of this Lady, have falsely call’d her: but our Author, who diligently perus’d Plutarch, and glean’d from him, where his Subject would admit, knew, from the Life of Theseus, that her name was Perigyné; (or Periguné) by whom Theseus had his Son Melanippus. She was the daughter of Sinnis a cruel Robber, and Tormentor of Passengers in the Isthmus. Plutarch and Athenæus are both express in the Circumstance of Theseus ravishing her: and the Former of them adds (as Diod. Siculus, Apollodorus and Pausanias, likewise tell us;) that he killed her Father into the Bargain. I corrected this Mistake of the Name in my SHAKESPEARE restor’d; and Mr. Pope has vouchsafed to correct from Me in his last Edition. For Plutarch, see . Upton (1746, pp. 284, 286) gives as a rule that Sh. alters proper names according to the English pronunciation, and says of this example: Her he calls very poetically Perigenia. Capell (1783, 2.3:104): An alteration of Perigouna (the harsh word of the Poet’s Plutarch) made with elegance. Malone (ed. 1790): The alteration was probably intentional, for the sake of harmony. Furness (ed. 1895): Malone thinks that Shakespeare changed the name for the sake of rhythm, but the rhythm remains the same with either spelling. Wilson (ed. 1924): A combined minim and o:e misprint…. The Q. error is so much in the run of the ordinary misprints in the Qq. that it is almost certainly due to the compositor rather than to careless transcription by Shakespeare from North. Sisson (1956, 1:126): If the compositor was capable of the error, so may Shakespeare well have been. The spelling Perigenia seems natural enough, even if unscholarly, on the model of Iphigenia, for example.

453 rauished]
The forced violation implied (see OED, v. 2a, b) contradicts Plutarch’s account; see Sources . White (ed. 1883): She willingly bore a son…to Theseus.

454 Eagles]
The compositors of QqF apparently failed to recognize this as a proper name, setting it in roman instead of italic. Most 18th- and 19th-c. editors follow Rowe (ed. 1709) in printing Ægle (or Æglé), but Capell (1783, 2.3:104) objects that this is not it’s sound in pronouncing, and prints (ed. 1768) Egle. Commenting on Eagles Dyce (1844, p. 46) claims: In Shakespeare’s time it was not uncommon to use the genitive of proper names for the nominative. Hunter (1845, pp. 289–90): In fact Shakespeare wrote Ægles, as he found it in North’s Plutarch:—and they blame him much also for that he so lightly forsook his wife Ariadne for the love of Ægles, the daughter of Panopeus. See . Furness (ed. 1895), noting the frequency with which the final s was added, not only to proper names, but to all words, objects against Dyce: If it be the genitive case in Eagles, why should this solitary genitive be surrounded by the nominative forms of Peregenia, Ariadne, and Antiopa? We need some other cause than inflection, I think, to explain this sibilant tendency, be it in some peculiar flourish in writing, or be it in some delicate phonetic demand, which our modern ears have lost. Brooks (ed. 1979): Eagles (Q1) is the compositor’s vulgarization.

455 Ariadne]
The best known of these victims of Theseus’s infidelity; cf. TGV 4.4.167–8 (1986–7). Chaucer ends her legend: But thus this false louer can begile His trew loue, the diuel quit him his while (LGW 2226–7; see ). Bate (1993, p. 120), linking these allusions in MND and TGV to Chaucer’s main source, Ovid’s Her. 10, notes that they occur in contexts which undermine the illusion that these are just happy comedies of fulfilled love. Root (1903, p. 41) observes the name may have been taken from North’s Plutarch, where Ariadne is described as being fallen in fansie with Theseus (see ).

455 Antiopa]
Wright (ed. 1877): Antiopa, according to some, was the name of the Amazon queen, and the mother of Hippolytus. See North’s Plutarch, p. 14. See .

456–92 These…originall]
Warburton (ed. 1747): There are not many passages in Shakespear of which one can be certain he has borrowed from the Ancients; but this is one of the few that, I think, will admit of no dispute. Our Author’s admirable description of the miseries of the Country being plainly an imitation of that which Ovid draws, as consequent on the grief of Ceres, for the loss of her daughter [quotes Met. 5.474–5, 477–84; cf. Golding’s translation 5.590–604, see ]. Dodd (1752, 1:79) also sees prodigious similarity between the two passages, but Heath (1765, p. 46) finds the claim without foundation. Bate (1993, p. 141 n.), comparing Golding’s version: As with other aspects of the play’s Ovidianism, the imitatio is marked by copiousness—thus Golding’s the corne was killed in the blade is embroidered into the green corn | Hath rotted ere his youth attained a beard—and by translatio into the vernacular, as when Shakespeare adds in such distinctively English elements as the nine men’s morris filled up with mud. Others recognizing parallels with this passage of Ovid include Leoni (ed. 1818), Halliwell (ed. 1856), Wright (1968, p. 30), Brooks (ed. 1979, p. lxxxvi).
Various other passages, both in Ovid and elsewhere, are adduced as parallels. Staton (1963, p. 169): Such effects of divine bickering are common in Ovid. One passage, which describes Juno’s retribution on Aegina for Jupiter’s infidelity, is in Golding’s translation very close to Shakespeare: [quotes 7.678–93; see ]. The points which Shakespeare and Ovid have in common here and which occur in the same order in both passages are: (1) the winds blow contagious fogs over the land; (2) the rivers become infected; (3) the plowman and the ox are affected; (4) the sheep die of murrain. The passages are close enough that I should call the one the probable source of the other. Henning (1969, p. 485) notes that Scot’s description of the power over storms imputed to witches is marginally attributed to Ovid (Met. 7), containing Medea’s incantation (see , ). Brooks (ed. 1979) compares also Ovid’s description of Deucalion’s flood (Golding 1.331–64; see ). Rudd (1979, pp. 180–1), noting the generally Ovidian nature of Sh.’s treatment of Oberon and Titania as quasi-Olympian, and that when they are at odds, the effect is felt in the world of nature, comments (p. 238, n. 17): Such phenomena are frequently a sign of divine displeasure in Ovid, Seneca, and elsewhere. Dodd (1752, 1:79–80) cites the description of Erictho’s power in Lucan (Pharsalia, 6.521 ff.); he quotes Seneca’s Medea 759–66 and refers to the description of the plague at the beginning of Oedipus (35–51, 133–90), quoting part of the version of Dryden and Lee (1679, B1r; the authors claim to have followed Sophocles, but appear to be echoing MND). Brooks (ed. 1979, pp. lxiii, 139–41, 144–5) also believes the Senecan passages influenced the speech. Miglior (1993, p. 258) calls attention to the Hymnus Homeri ad Cererem, from which he feels both the Ovidian description of Ceres’ wrath and the description of the plague in Seneca’s Oedipus derive (It.). Bradbrook (1951, pp. 159, 203 n. 24) finds some recollection of Vergil’s Sixth Eclogue. Hankins (1978, pp. 39–41), Rhoads (1985, pp. 76–8) and Taylor (Plato’s, 2004, pp. 276–7) note the close similarity of Eryximachus’s speech on the harmonious power of love in Plato’s Symposium (tr. Jowett, 1892, 1:557–8) and suggest possible avenues of influence. Scragg (1982, pp. 73–4) finds impressive verbal correspondences with Tyterus’ description (I.i.13–34 [2:432–3]) of the havoc brought about by Neptune’s resentment against humanity in Lyly’s Gallathea. For further literary precedents in these lines, see nn. 463–7, 466–7, 467, 474–5, 478–9, 478, 482–6, 482–3, 482, 483, 484–6, 485, 486–8, 490–2, 492.
Other critics look to experience rather than authority for the source of the speech. Steevens (ed. 1773, at 476): The confusion of seasons here described, is no more than a poetical account of the weather, which happened in England about the time when this play was first published; later (ed. 1793) he supports this belief by quoting a description of that time’s inclement weather from Churchyard’s Charitie, 1595, E1v; in the dedicatory epistle Churchyard tells the earl of Essex that he is writing on the coldnes of charitie, bicause a great noble man, told me this last wet sommer, The weather was too colde for Poets. Blakeway (in Boswell, ed. 1821, 5:342): Strype…has printed an extract from one of Dr. J. King’s Lectures, preached at York, in which that divine reminds his hearers of the various signs of God’s wrath, with which England was visited in 1593 and 1594 (if I understand the extract aright,)—as, storms, pestilence, dearth, and unseasonable weather. Of the last, he says, Remember that the spring (that year when the plague broke out) was very unkind, by means of the abundance of rains that fell. Our July hath been like to a February; our June even as an April: so that the air must needs be infected.… Then, having spoken of the three successive years of scarcity, he adds, And see, whether the Lord doth not threaten us much more, by sending such unseasonable weather, and storms of rain among us: which if we will observe, and compare it with that which is past, we may say that the course of nature is very much inverted. Our years are turned upside down. Our summers are no summers: our harvests are no harvests: our seed-times are no seed-times. For a great space of time scant any day hath been seen that it hath not rained upon us [Annals, 1824, rpt. c. 1968, 4:294–5]. Halliwell (1841, pp. 6–8) quotes descriptions of the cold and extremely wet summer and autumn of 1594 from Simon Forman (MS. Ashm. 384) and from Stow (Annales, 1615, p. 768). On the application of observations of the weather to the question of dating the play, see . Brooks (ed. 1979, p. xxxvii n. 4): Why should not fact supply Shakespeare with part of his picture of cosmic disorder, while literature heightened that part and supplied the rest?
The poetry is highly praised, though with some difference of opinion over its dramatic viability. Gildon (1710, 7:317): Titania’s Description…is very fine. Gentleman (ed. 1774): This speech throughout is so obviously beautiful, it possesses such elegance, and is so chaste both in idea and versification, that, though too long for repetition, we know not which lines might be spared. Duff (1770, p. 162): The speech…is altogether poetical and picturesque. Keats (in White, 1987, p. 105) finds it exquisitely rich and luxurious…The thing is a piece of profound verdure. By verdure, Keats may have in mind a tapestry richly ornamented with vegetation; see OED 3, and Britannica Online, Verdure Tapestry. For Maginn’s (1837, p. 376) praise of the ornate poetry of the fairy scenes, see ; he continues: [T]he homely consequences resulting from the fairy quarrel, [quotes 468–72] and so on, are ostentatiously contrasted with misfortunes more metaphorically related: [quotes 480–6]. Halliday (1954, p. 94) calls it the sonnet poetry with a difference, praising the greater naturalism of rhythm and description; see . Barber (1959, p. 147): Part of the delight of this poetry is that we can enjoy without agitation imaginative action of the highest order.…[T]he phrase [the human mortals] crystallizes what has been achieved in imaginative distance and freedom. But Titania is as far off from us as we are from her. Wells (ed. 1967, p. 24): The speech has, of course, great poetic merit…But to dismiss it as irrelevant, or as mere decoration, is to fail to give proper allowance to the nature of poetic drama. Slights (1988, pp. 262–3) invoke[s] the principle of indeterminacy to interpret this perplexing set-piece of visionary verse.
Some commentators have seen comic absurdity in the speech. The Clarkes (ed. 1864): Titania’s attributing to her own and her fairy consort’s right regal little differences the calamities of universal Nature forms a fine satire upon the dogmatical assumptions with which the origin of such calamities has been ascribed time out of mind. Schelling (1927, p. 15): It tells of the dreadful effects of an inverted year, of floods, desolated country, dying flocks and general rural disaster, and all because Titania, that mite of a fairy queen, has quarrelled with Oberon, her fairy husband. Here is the passage which will give you Shakespeare’s own ideas of comets, portents and the like. He is laughing at you over his little pigmies, the fairies, and wondering if we human pigmies are any bigger, any wiser, any less hopelessly self-centered. Barber (1959, p. 146): The factitiousness of the causes Titania lays out for the weather is gently mocked by the repeated therefore’s.
Others view it more portentously. Thaler (1925, p. 678) parallels with it the description of the affliction visited upon man…in Paradise Lost [10.651–66 677–96, 736] upon man’s tasting the apple. Bradbrook (1951, p. 159): The whole anthropomorphic world of the Ovidian romance…is embodied in the magnificent speech of Titania which places the fairies in control of the whole natural scene, and evokes so richly and powerfully Nature’s waste fertility, all to disorder wandering; she also hears in it (p. 160) the voice of Venus Genetrix. Young (1966, p. 172): Titania’s [concept of nature] is mutable, Montaigne’s new reality, the languishing and wavering dance that surrounds man and limits his understanding. Wells (ed. 1967, p. 25): Titania’s speech is without any comic tinge, though…it is rendered appropriate to a comedy by its generalized, somewhat distancing style. It presents seriously the danger of disaster when control is lost and the malevolent forces of nature gain the upper hand. Weiss (1971, p. 84): Titania speaks in formal, leisurely vein of the consequences of their quarrel: a natural perversion of nature in the chain of being. McFarland (1972, p. 84): It is…an account of paradise itself, though of paradise in a state of delicate and temporary desolation. Garber (1974, pp. 70–1) calls it visionary landscape, because it is a projection of the subconscious state of mind upon the external state of terrain and climate.…[W]hat was formerly a fairy place, locus amoenus, is now all disordered.…Suggestions of the fall from paradise are unmistakable, yet the ostensible cause (possession of the Indian page boy) seems insufficient. Here we are directly confronted with the terrifying and inexplicable world of the irrational unconscious.…The overwhelming impression is that of sterility, and this is important because it is the opposite of fruitful creation and change. Vlasopolos (1978, p. 27): It establishes a cause-and-effect relationship between disrupted sexuality and universal disorder. Titania herself concludes that the discord, a breach of natural harmony, has engendered an unnatural progeny of evils. Chaudhuri (1990, p. 26): Such chaos is brought about not by any profound challenge to the moral order but by the shallow private caprice of ethically inconsequential fairies. Hackett (2003, p. 346): In a highly Ovidian play, it is an Ovidian-style aetiological myth, explaining natural phenomena by means of a quarrel between tempestuous gods.…Throughout, the pattern of correspondence works in two directions simultaneously: Titania and Oberon have caused sterility and monstrosity in nature, but this sterility and monstrosity are in turn images of the fairy couple’s marital and parental failure. Blits (2003, p. 60) objects that there is no evidence in the play of any of the natural disasters Titania describes: Her narrative…seems fictitious even within the fiction of Shakespeare’s play.

456 These…iealousie]
Deighton (ed. 1891): All these stories about my intercourse with Theseus are mere calumnious inventions due to jealousy. Holland (ed. 1994): Is Titania lying? Theseus’ involvement with these women is amply documented in Plutarch though he of course says nothing about Titania’s involvement.

456 forgeries]
OED (2): Fictitious invention. Now only poet. Formerly also with more reproachful sense: †Deception, lying; a fraudulent artifice.

457–62 neuer…disturbd]
Abbott §347: The simple present is [here] incorrectly combined with the complete present. But the two verbs are so far apart that they may almost be regarded as belonging to different sentences, especially as but may be regarded as semi-adversative.

457–62 the]
Warburton (ed. 1747) supports Hanmer’s emendation (see textual n.): We should read that. For it appears to have been some years since the quarrel first began. Thirlby (MS 1747–53): But what is the antecedent to this that? I suppose he’ll say we must supply…when we quarell’d. Heath (1765, p. 46) objects: How long before [the quarrel over the changeling] had happened is no where, except in this place, said.

457–62 middle…spring]
Thirlby (MS 1725–33): Does he mean the beginning of midsummer which tho’ it sound odly may have a meaning. Thirlby (MS 1747–53): I think this was the night before May Day. Steevens (ed. 1773), comparing day-spring: Our author seems to mean the beginning of middle or mid summer (OED, Spring, n1 5a, quoting this instance). Henley (in Steevens, ed. 1785) argues for the season when trees put forth their second, or as they are frequently called their midsummer shoots. Korner (1891, p. 17) claims this phrase seems distinctly to indicate a harmony between the two dates of May-morn and Midsummer. (See .) Thiselton (1903, p. 28): Possibly the previous May-day may have been meant. May-day appears to have been regarded variously as the beginning of Spring and the beginning of Summer.

458–60 Met…Sea]
Arnold (1866, p. 122) finds here the sheer, inimitable Celtic note of aërialness and magic in Sh.’s handling of nature.

459 By…brooke]
On the rhythm, see n. 446.

459 paued fountaine]
Johnson (ed. 1765): A fountain laid round the edge with stone. Steevens (ed. 1778): Perhaps paved at the bottom. So, Lord Bacon in his Essay on Gardens: As for the other kind of fountaine, which we may call a bathing-poole, it may admit much curiosity and beauty…. As that the bottom be finely paved…the sides likewise, &c. [ed. Kiernan, 2000, p. 143]. Henley (in Steevens, ed. 1785): The epithet seems here intended to mean no more that that the beds of these fountains were covered with pebbles in opposition to those of the rushy brooks which are oozy. Irving & Marshall (ed. 1888): But were not springs, in retired spots, often paved with small bricks in order to prevent the water soaking away? Sprague (ed. 1896): Would fairies haunt an artificially paved fountain?

459 rushie]
OED (a1, 2), quoting this instance: Full of rushes. Greenblatt (ed. 1997): Fringed with reeds. See n. 251.

460 in…Sea]
Caldecott (MS 1813–33): i.e. in or upon the edge or verge of the seashore.

460 in]
Schmidt (1874): Not seldom, in a local sense, = on (OED, prep. 1, 2). See Franz §502; Blake 5.4.2 In (c). Pope’s correction to on (see textual n.), opposed by Caldecott (MS 1813–33), abandoned by Delius (ed. 1859), and dismissed by Halliwell (ed. 1856) as unnecessary, was still defended by Lettsom (in Dyce, ed. 1864) by an appeal to LC 39: Vpon whose weeping margent she was set. Cf. 465.

460 beached]
Johnson (1755): Exposed to the waves, quoting the beached verge of the salt flood (Tim. 5.1.216 [2461]). Schmidt (1874): Formed by a flat strand. OED (1), quoting this instance first: Having a beach; prob. also, in early use, Covered with a beach or shingle.

460 margent]
OED: Now arch. and poet. = MARGIN. Foakes (ed. 1984): The usual form of margin in Shakespeare’s age, and the one he always used. See Franz §61.

461–2 To…sport]
Brissenden (1981, p. 42): The first mention of dance in the play comes after all four groups of characters have been introduced…and the theme of disorder has been related to each of them.…It is precisely appropriate that this quarrelsome couple and their attendants are the characters…most concerned with dancing for dancing was one of the main occupations of the Elizabethan fairies. See also Idem, 1972, p. 89, and n. 516.

461 To…winde]
Miriam Joseph (1947, p. 112) gives this as an example of synecdoche, a trope which heightens meaning by substituting genus for species, species for genus, part for whole, whole for part.

461 ringlets]
OED (2), quoting this instance first: A circular dance or course; a circle of dancers; a fairy-ring. Douce (1807, 1:184): An allusion to what the country people call fairy rings, which they suppose to be the tracks of the dances of those diminutive beings. Furness (ed. 1895) envisages fairies tossing their gossamer ringlets in the wind, but Wells (ed. 1967) points out the meaning lock of hair is not recorded before 1667.

461 to…winde]
Thirlby (MS 1725–33): I.e. to the musick of the whistling wind.

462 brawles…sport]
OED (n.1 1): A noisy turbulent quarrel; with a possible pun on (n.3 2): A kind of French dance. Whiter (1794, pp. 102–4): The quarrels of Oberon with Titania were denominated by the word brawls from its affinity in one sense with the sport and the ringlet dances, which these brawls or quarrels had interrupted. Brawl is a species of dance…in which several persons danced together in a ring.…There is another passage in our Author, where brawls and sports are connected as in the instance before us, accompanied even by the same idea of interruption, which the one had received from the other. Thou say’st, his sports were hinder’d by thy brawls [Err. 5.1.77 (1546)].…I am disposed however to imagine…that the excellence of this dance consisted in its intricacy, and that the performers displayed their dexterity by mingling with another company of dancers, and preserving their own figure distinct and separate amidst all the mazes of apparent interruption. He cites also LLL 3.1.8–10 (779–80) where there is a similar pun on brawl and brawling. Baskervill (1929; 1965, pp. 10, 347–9) lists the brawl among courtly dances, and details its movements; he notes (p. 348) that it is particularly rich in mimetic and dramtic features, and in illustration of its variety of form refers to stanza 62 of Sir John Davies’ Orchestra, where Love compounds a thousand brawls from the seven motions found in nature. Drawing on a tendentious analysis of the above-mentioned passage in LLL and another in Marston’s The Malcontent 4.2.1–12, Howard (1993, p. 333) argues: Oberon’s noisy brawl…represents a covert cultural assault on Titania. He imports tricky French customs…that cloak sexuality and self-promotion in the guise of civility; he replaces shared popular festivity with an aggressive performance and transforms dancing from a communal ritual into a conquest.

462 hast disturbd]
Blake (4.3.7.1), quoting this instance: The perfect may also indicate repetition.

463–7 Therefore…Continents]
Shaheen (1993, p. 95), comparing the homily concernyng good ordre and obedience, to rulers and magistrates (Certayne Sermons, 1547, R1r-v), Tro. 1.3.78–137 (537–97), and similar passages in 16th- and 17th-c. writings: Sh. was writing in a well-known tradition. That tradition—discord and insubordination produce chaos—was probably best expounded in the homilies. If the reference exists, it undercuts Titania’s complaint, since the sermon propounds the moste excellent and perfect ordre of the obedience of wife to husband.

463–5 Therefore…fogges]
Hollindale (1992, p. 74): This is a powerful image of disregarded music. The piping winds are a harmonious equivalent in the world of nature to the human arts of ordered, pleasing sound, turned to disorder by dismissal and neglect. Knowles (privately): The focus is not on disregarded music but on disregarded wind and other related natural forces, which soon take their revenge.

463 pyping…vaine]
Wright (ed. 1877): Because we could not dance to them. See Matthew 11:17: We have piped unto you, and ye have not danced. Shaheen (1993, p. 95), comparing also Luke 7:32: Perhaps an analogy rather than a reference, but the similarities…seem to be more than coincidence. Brooks (ed. 1979) also compares Spenser, Shep. Cal., June 30–1.

463 pyping]
OED (ppl. a. 1b): Characterized by piping, 1.e. the music of the pastoral pipe; (2a): Sounding shrilly; whistling. Cf. Thomas (1587, Sibilus): A whistling…in Poets the blowing of windes: also piping (Ovid [Met. 13.785]). Hunter (ed. 1870): Having piped.

464–5 As…fogges]
Wright (ed. 1877) compares Lr. 2.4.166–7 (1449–50): Infect her Beauty, You Fen-suck’d Fogges, drawne by the powerfull Sunne.

465 Contagious]
Thomas (1550, Pestilentioso): Pestilent or contagiouse. Florio (1598, Insalùbre): Vnholsome, vnhealthfull, noisome, contagious (OED, a. †5).

465–6 which…Hath]
For the use of a singular verb following the relative, see Abbott §247; cf. 1120 (costs).

465 in]
See n. 460.

466–7 euery…Continents]
Roberts (1983, pp. 111–12), quoting KnT 2991–3 (see ): The jealous quarrel of Titania and Oberon has broken love’s chain and thus the elements are no longer held in certayn bonds.

466 pelting]
The word caused editorial uneasiness as early as F1, which emends to petty (see textual n., and conj. ). Johnson (1755): This word in Shakespeare signifies, I know not why, mean; paltry; pitiful; (ed. 1765): The meaning is plainly, despicable, mean, sorry, wretched; but as it is a word without any reasonable etymology, I should be glad to dismiss it for petty, yet it is undoubtedly right. The Clarkes (ed. 1864) defend it as probably the poet’s word, inasmuch as it combines the idea of headlong course with insignificance, possibly having in mind the meaning violent, passionate (OED, ppl. a. 2) or, anachronistically, To move at a vigorous and rapid pace (OED, v1, 7). Furness (ed. 1895) prefers F1’s petty since there is a tinge of contempt in the pelting of the Qq, which is here needless; insignificance is all-sufficient. Sh. uses it together with petty in MM 2.2.112 (869) where both adjectives are clearly derogatory; see also R2 2.1.60 (701), Tro. 4.5.267 (2840), Lr. 2.3.18 (1269). Golding uses it for Ovid’s parva in describing the cottage of Philemon and Baucis, and translates stabula (dwelling like a stable, hut), to which Tereus dragged Philomele, as pelting graunge (8.804–5, 6.663; see , ).

466 proude]
OED (a. 7c), quoting this instance: Of the sea or a stream: Swelling, swollen, high, strong, in flood. Also, in antithesis to pelting, OED (1a): Arrogant, overweening, and (4): Presumptuous. Halliwell (ed. 1856): Often used without any reference to the state of the mind; but simply as implying exuberance or overfulness (cf. OED 7d). Also possibly with an overtone of OED (8a): Sensually excited; Ellis (1973, p. 81): Proud in the sense of lustful…is used metaphorically of rivers in [MND], with a pretty pun on continence. Cf. n. 435.

467 they haue]
