Wright (ed. 1877): The plural follows loosely as representing the collection of individual rivers. Deighton (ed. 1891) compares Lr. 2.2.75–6 (1148–9): smooth euery passion That…rebell. See Franz §353. Cf. Abbott §12; OED, Every, a. (quasi-pron.) 1a.

467 ouerborne…Continents]
Johnson (ed. 1765): Born down the banks that contained them. So in (Lr. 3.2.57–8 [1710–11]): Close pent guilts Rive their concealing continents. Brooks (ed. 1979), citing Sir Thomas More (ll. 39–40 in Evans, ed. 1974, p. 1688); Ham. 4.5.100 (2839); Cor. 3.1.247–9 (1973–5): Waters rising over their continents, the bounds which should contain them, are Shakespeare’s recurrent image for anarchical insubordination. See also R2 3.2.106–10 (1464–8).

467 Continents]
OED (n. 1a), quoting this instance: That which contains or holds. Now rare or arch. Cf. Ant. 4.14.40 (2873). Hallam (1839, 2:389) finds evidence of Sh.’s knowledge of Latin in the term, believing it derived from the continente ripa of Horace (Odes 2.18, 22). Baldwin (1944, 2:505) objects: But in Horace the expression appears certainly to mean mainland or continental shores. Ellis (1973, p. 81) finds a pun on continence, i.e. Self-restraint in the matter of sexual appetite (OED 2).

468–70 The oxe…bearde]
Sitwell (1948, p. 192): A wonderful use of falling dissonances, adding strangeness, and coming like a faint breath of cooler air, gone in a moment, in this summer night.

468 The…vaine]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Laboured in dragging the plough; the yoke was the curved piece of wood put upon the neck of oxen and attached by traces to the plough.

469 lost his sweat]
Verity (ed. 1893): Expended his toil in vain.

469–70 greene…bearde]
Malone (1783, p. 10): So, in our author’s 12th Sonnet: And summer’s green all girded up in sheaves, Borne on the bier with white and bristly beard.

469 corne]
Usually wheat, or possibly rye, barley, or oats (not maize). See OED, n.1 3a. See also n. 442.

470 his]
The usual neuter as well as masculine possessive pronoun until the 17th c.; see OED, His (poss. pron., 3rd sing. masc. and neut.) and Its (poss. pron.). See Franz §320; Abbott §228. See 1204, 1409.

470 bearde]
Delius (ed. 1859) points out the double meaning: Beard, as a sign of manhood, and awns (beardlike bristles, beard) of the corn ears as a sign of ripeness (Ger.).

472 crowes]
Harting (1871; 1965, p. 110): The Carrion-Crow…has no objection to carrion and offal of all kinds. Should a sheep die in the field, the crows of the neighbourhood are sure to be attracted to it. Seager (1896; 1971, p. 77) quotes Bartholomaeus Anglicus on crows’ habit of eating vnclene meates and venymous (De proprietatibus rerum, 1535, 12.9).

472 fatted]
Wright (ed. 1877): Fattened. Compare (Ham. 2.2.579–80 [1619–20]): I should have fatted all the region kites With this slave’s offal.

472 murrion]
Obsolete form of murrain. Steevens (ed. 1778): The murrain is the plague in cattle. It is here used by Shakespeare as an adjective (OED 5a).

473 nine mens Morris]
OED (n2): Obs. exc. Hist. = MEREL 2. Chiefly nine men’s (peg) morris; this instance is the first given, with no other until 1709. The definitions and examples given in OED for MEREL apply to the board game. The sport mentioned here is variously likened to chess (Warburton, ed. 1747), draughts (or checkers) (Alchorne, in Steevens, ed. 1778), gobang (Moberly, ed. 1881), hopscotch (Kittredge, ed. 1936, Glossary), or noughts and crosses (Brooks, ed. 1979). Steevens (ed. 1778) prints three descriptions and a diagram: In that part of Warwickshire where Shakespeare was educated, and the neighbouring parts of Northamptonshire, the shepherds and other boys dig up the turf with their knives to represent a sort of imperfect chess-board. It consists of a square, sometimes only a foot diameter, sometimes three or four yards. Within this is another square, every side of which is parallel to the external square; and these squares are joined by lines drawn from each corner of both squares, and the middle of each line. One party, or player, has wooden pegs, the other stones, which, they move in such a manner as to take up each other’s men as they are called, and the area of the inner square is called the Pound, in which the men taken up are impounded. These figures are by the country people called Nine Men’s Morris, or Merrils, and are so called, because each party has nine men. These figures are always cut upon the turf or leys, as they are called, or upon the grass at the end of ploughed lands, and in rainy seasons never fail to be choaked up with mud. James.…A game still play’d by the shepherds, cowkeepers, &c. in the midland counties, as follows: A figure is made on the ground…by cutting out the turf; and two persons take each nine stones, which they place by turns in the angles, and afterwards more alternately, as at chess or draughts. He who can place three in a strait line, may then take off any one of his adversary’s, where he pleases, till one, having lost all his men, loses the game. [diagram]. Alchorne. In Cotgrave’s Dictionary, under the article Merelles, is the following explanation. Le Jeu des Merelles. The boyish game called Merils, or fivepenny morris; played here most commonly with stones, but in France with pawns, or men made on purpose, and termed merelles. The pawns or figures of men used in the game might originally be black, and hence called morris, or merelles, as we yet term a black cherry a morello, and a small black cherry a merry, perhaps from Maurus a Moor, or rather from morum a mulberry. Tollet Other descriptions of the game are given in Strutt (1801, pp. 236–7), and Staunton (ed. 1857).
Some have made a connection with Morris-dancing (OED, Morris, n.1). Capell (1783, 2.3:105): The place where a morris had been perform’d by nine men: and the mazes spoken of afterwards, should be—mazes of their forming, or of some other dancers, in their summer gambols on greens. Dodd (1780, 1:219): A kind of labyrinthal maze cut in the grass—wide enough for the nine dancers to pass, without incommoding each other. The dancers are generally dressed fantastically; have different departments in the dance, and are usually nine. Though the custom is almost antiquated, I have seen some of them, within this little time, in Bedfordshire; they travel the country, and levy contributions on the villagers. Douce (1807, 1:184) explains the name morris for merrils as probably a corruption suggested by the sort of dance which in the progress of the game the counters performed.
Emending to mortice, (see ), an anonymous editor (in Johnson, ed. 1771, addenda 1:12) asserts: The mortice is the frame on which the pins or men, are placed, in the game of nine-pins. That this mortice, or frame, which often has cavities for the pins, might be filled with mud, is easily conceived; but that any kind of rural chess should be so circumstanced is impossible.

474–5 And…vndistinguishable]
Steevens (ed. 1793): This alludes to a sport still followed by boys; i.e. what is now called running the figure of eight. The Clarkes (ed. 1864): It should rather seem to refer to the traces left by the feet of those who engage in rustic sports and dances on the village green.

474 queint]
Rann (ed. 1787): Nice, exact, curious (cf. OED, Quaint, 4, 7). Brooks (ed. 1979): Ingeniously elaborated (OED 3). See also n. 657.

474–5 Mazes…vndistinguishable]
Lynch (1999, pp. 120–1) links expressions of confusion and doubt in the mazed world (488), the amazed or doubting lovers (1247, 1385, 1671, 1712), and the awakening Bottom (1734–40) with the similar states of mind of Chaucer’s dream-vision narrators, especially in HF, BD, and LGW. See .

474 Mazes]
Stukeley (1724, pp. 91–3), cited Thirlby (MS 1725–33): I have frequently found these places call’d Julian’s bower, both at roman towns and others, but especially very common in Lincolnshire,…They are generally upon open green places by the side of roads or rivers, upon meadows and the like near a town.…[V]ery often they are call’d Troy town. [W]hat generally appears at present is no more than a circular work made of banks of earth in the fashion of a maze or labyrinth, and the boys to this day divert themselves with running in it one after another, which leads them by many windings quite thro’ and back again. Stukeley speculates that these are remnants of the Roman military games described with specific reference to the Cretan labyrinth by (for example) Claudian (Panegyric on the Sixth Consulship of the Emperor Honorius, 621–39) and Vergil (Aeneid, 5.583–95). Halliwell (ed. 1856): Several mazes of the kind here alluded to are still preserved, having been kept up from time immemorial. On the top of Catherine-Hill, Winchester,…was a very perplexed and winding path running in a very small space over a great deal of ground, called a Miz-Maze. The senior boys obliged the juniors to tread it, to prevent the figure from being lost, and I believe it is still retained. Wright (ed. 1877) quotes Steevens (see n. 474–5), adding: But I have seen very much more complicated figures upon village greens, and such as might strictly be called mazes and labyrinths. Matthews (1922, pp. 71–99) gives descriptions and diagrams of many English turf mazes; he speculates on their origin and purpose, quoting MND as evidence that they were, from Tudor times onwards, used for recreational purposes (p. 94). See also Bord (1976, esp. pp. 13, 44–58) and Doob (1990, esp. pp. 113–17). Phillips (ed. 1969): It may mean no more than the network of little paths crossing the village green, leading for example towards the church or the school.
The word is regularly coupled with labyrinth, and has strong classical associations. Florio (1598, Labirinto): A laberinth or place full of intricate windings and turnings, made in such wise that whosoeuer came into it coulde not get out againe without a perfect guide, or without a thred directing him, the clew or bottom whereof should be left at the entrie, wee call it a maze. Cotgrave (1611, Dedalé): Intricate, perplexed; Dedalian, Maze-like; (Dedaler): To make a Maze, or Laberinth. Florio (1598) also connects both words with A thicket or brake of Iuniper (Ginepraio), and A grove of holmes (Leccétto). Sh. puns on the association in Ven. 684, describing the hare’s doublings as like a laberinth to amaze his foes. See also nn. 488, 1247, 1671.
The associations with Theseus’s encounter with the Daedalian labyrinth containing the minotaur, or with various moralized meanings, occurred to Ruskin (Ariadne Florentina 1875, Works, 22:461–2), and have been explored by (among others) Ormerod (1978, pp. 47–8), Evans (1980, pp. 165 ff.), Lecercle (1989, pp. 143–9), Holland (1994, p. 150), Lynch (1999, pp. 119 ff.). See e.g. , , .

474 wanton greene]
Hunter (ed. 1870): The green where sports were carried on. Compare the similar metonymy in [R3 1.4.39 (875)], The wandering air.

474 wanton]
Johnson (1755, 7), quoting this instance but attributing it to Milton: Not regular; turned fortuitously. OED (a. 7a), quoting this instance first: Profuse in growth, luxuriant, rank. poet. Cf. Johnson (1755, 6) and Florio (1598, Mórbido): Wanton, fruitfull, ranke, proude in growing. Deighton (ed. 1891): Playful; a transferred epithet properly applicable to those who by their playing formed the mazes. Warton (1785, p. 101) compares Milton Arcades 46–7, where the genius of the wood claims the Jove-given power to curl the grove With Ringlets quaint, and wanton windings wove. This suggests that Milton saw no immoral connotations in quaint and wanton. See also nn. 438, 505.

475 For…tread]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Owing to their not being trodden (cf. OED, Tread, n. 4a).

475 tread]
Gardette (1989, p. 93) suggests that a syllepsis on tread/thread evokes the story of Ariadne and the labyrinth (Ovid Met. 8.172–3; Golding 8.230–4, see ) (Fr.); for the pronunciation he cites Kökeritz (1953, pp. 320–1), where the closest similar instance given is Hotspur’s roan/throne wordplay (1H4 2.3.70 [919]) (Fr.).

475 vndistinguishable]
Johnson (1755, 1), quoting this instance and 1712–13: Not to be distinctly seen. OED (1), quoting this instance first: Incapable of being made out or discerned. Rolfe (ed. 1877): Used by S. only here and in [1712]. Girard (1991, p. 55): The four seasons are undergoing the same process of undifferentiation as the four lovers. See n. 1712.

476 humane]
The usual 16th- and 17th-c. spelling of human. OED (2a): Of the nature of humans; (3a): As distinguished from God or superhuman beings; pertaining to the sphere or faculties of mankind (with implication of limitation or inferiority). See 774.

476 humane mortals]
Steevens (ed. 1778): Shakespeare might have employ’d this epithet, which, at first sight, appears redundant, to mark the difference between men and fairies. Fairies were not human, but they were yet subject to mortality. Ritson (1783, p. 43) objected: Oberon, Titania, and Puck never dye;…the oldest woman in England never heard of the death of a Fairy. Human mortals is, notwithstanding, evidently put in opposition to Fairies, who partook of a middle nature between men and spirits. Puck in a subsequent scene says, Lord, what fools these mortals be [1139]. Heron (1785, p. 110) specified: That fairies were subject to mortality, I will venture to say there is no evidence in any creed of popular superstition. Reed (ed. 1785) put in evidence against Ritson the genealogy of the fairies in Spenser’s FQ 2.10.70–6, where fairy kings including Oberon die. Ritson (1788, pp. 11–14) at sarcastic length attacked Reed because Spenser’s fairies by virtue of their allegorical function are dissimilar from Sh.’s and moreover the fairies of Spenser’s master Ariosto are immortal; he dismisses the mortal fairy king Oberon of Huon of Burdeaux since this is a foolish Romance; and as evidence that Sh. has expressly represented his Fairies immortal cites lines 993, 1388, 1429, 2207. Steevens (MS 1793–1800) calls attention to Chapman’s use of the phrase Mortall humanes in his translation of the Homeric hymn To Earth the Mother of All (Chapman’s Homer, ed. Allardyce Nicoll, 1956, 2:598 l.11); he also quotes Burton’s Anatomy of Melancholy (1.2.1.2; ed. 1932, 1:185): post prolixum tempus moriuntur omnes, adding i.e. aerial & familiar spirits &c were all mortal. Douce (1807, 1:185–6) points out that Burton is not speaking of fairies, but spirits, devils, and angels; in opposition to Steevens, he calls the phrase merely a pleonasm, and defines it as simply the language of a fairy speaking of men.
Other interpretations include: Seymour (1805, 1:44): I cannot think that any distinction is meant between men and fairies, but between mankind and the rest of perishable nature. Knight (ed. 1839): Whether Titania were, or were not, subject to death, she employed the language of poetry in speaking of human mortals, without reference to the conditions of fairy existence. Tyrrell (ed. 1850): This term is probably given to distinguish men or women from those fairies who are described by some writers also as being mortal. Thus Kirk, in his Essay on Fairies [1815, p. 8], expressly tells us that the Scottish fairies are mortal. The Clarkes (ed. 1864): A fairy-queen expression for the huge material beings of mankind, in contradistinction to the dainty proportions and conditions of her own race. Neil (ed. 1878): It is not a pleonasm, but a happily chosen epizeuxis, or the intensifying union of two similar terms. Deighton (ed. 1891): Though Titania and her elves were immortal, some fairies were mortal, and the expression is probably meant to contrast human beings who were mortal with fairies that were so too. Cuningham (ed. 1905): It is simply a compassionate epithet on Titania’s part, and indicates her pity for mortals who are only human, i.e. who are subject to the ills and weaknesses of humanity. Theobald (1909, pp. 199–200), giving examples from Homer and Hesiod: This expression…is very classical and corresponds with the phrase mortal man. See also Barber (1959, p. 147) at n. 456–92. On the general question of whether fairies were immortal, cf. Briggs (1967, pp. 142, 154–5), offering a variety of opinions. Hale (1998–9, p. 200): Logically [the phrase] should be distinguishing us humans of the audience in one direction from non-human mortals—the animals.…And it distinguishes us in the other direction from human immortals…namely these fairies—transcendental unlike us, but petty and flighty like us. The net result is a quick sense of a well-populated though tiny universe, to have such parallel existences and consciousnesses in it. See also 954 and n., 993, 1139, 1621.

476–8 want…Therefore]
Dissatisfaction with the meaning and logical development of this passage led to a plethora of emendation and conjectural emendation in lineation, punctuation, and text (especially in the latter part of 476); see textual n. and conj., .

476–8 want…heere]
The QF text is defended by Capell (1783, 2:3.104), interpreting heere as referring to place, not time: Their winter here is—their accustom’d winter, in a country thus afflicted: and what that accustom’d winter was, appears from the next line; to wit, a winter enliven’d with mirth, and distinguish’d with grateful hymns to their deities; which omitting now, (as if angry with their gods, to whom they falsely impute the calamities above-mention’d) Therefore &c. Others accepting QF include Malone (ed. 1790): Their winter may mean those sports with which country people are wont to beguile a winter’s evening, at the season of Christmas. Hunter (ed. 1870): Have no distinct winter season here. Baker (ed. 1896), inserting a comma: The human mortals want, (are in need, see [465–75]) their winter (being) here (here at this unaccustomed time). Doran (ed. 1959): Lack winter as well as other seasons (?), would like winter now as more tolerable than the present summer (?)
Thirlby (MS 1725–33) suggests wonted cheer. Theobald (ed. 1733): The concluding Word is, certainly, a very dragging Expletive: and tho’ I have not ventur’d to displace it, I scarce believe it genuine. I once suspected it should be—want their Winter Chear;—i.e. their Jollity, usual Merry-makings at that Season. Cheer was adopted by Hanmer, and either adopted or praised by many subsequent editors. In the latter 20th c. it has become established, mostly through the strong advocacy of Brooks (ed. 1979), arguing: (1) Q1 seems corrupt, here being (as Dyce observed) defensible only by straining; (2) h for ch is an easy misreading; (3) cheer fits the context, (4) introducing expressions (hymns and carols) of the cheer in question; (5) it postulates for want the sense (be without) most frequent in Shakespeare; (6) it was probably suggested to him from the November eclogue of The Shepheardes Calender.
Other early conjectures won few adherents. Warburton (ed. 1747; in Theobald, ed. 1733) referring to rural rejoicing after harvest, suggests HERYED, i.e. praised, celebrated. An anonymous conjector in Caribbeana (1741, 2.75) claimed here = Herr, Heer, or Herus, signifying Lord, Prince, Master, Chief, Leader…i.e. the human mortals want their Princess or Encourager of their Winter Revels; similarly Steevens (1765, pp. 167–8): Here signifies a master. The meaning is, that the human mortals…wanted their landlord, who used to make them merry at Christmas. Johnson (ed. 1765), proposing wonted year, offers a reorganization of the speech to satisfy his ideas of logical development, but confesses: I know not what credit the reader will give to this emendation, which I do not much credit myself.
Other emendations of want, winter, or heere appealed only to those proposing them, except gear (Brae conj., in Clark & Wright, ed. 1863), defended by Wilson (ed. 1924): Gear (=clothes) fits the context excellently. The summer is so cold, says Titania in effect, that the humans need their winter clothes.
The relationship of ideas in the passage, called into question by Johnson’s radical reorganization, is addressed by Steevens (ed. 1778): The repeated adverb therefore, throughout this speech, I suppose to have constant reference to the first time when it is used.—All these irregularities of season, happened in consequence of the disagreement between the king and queen of the fairies, and not in consequence of each other; and by Malone (in Steevens, ed. 1778, 3:127): [478] &c. has no connection with [477]. It does not refer to the omission of hymns or carols, but of the fairy rites, which were disturbed in consequence of Oberon’s quarrel with Titania. Wood (ed. 1806) reorganizes the grammatical construction, emending want to wants and reading the preceding lines as a description of depopulation: Winter here, (i.e. in the country near Athens, for all the actors are from Athens itself,) wants his mortals to bless the night with hymn and carol. It appears to me that, by using this construction, and recurring at each therefore, to the fairies’ quarrel as the cause of these disorders, Titania’s speech becomes much more intelligible than it is in the common reading. Q2Ff’s use of a comma instead of a period (see textual n.) allowed Morehead (1814, pp. 28–9) to propose breaking the thought after want, a reading supported by Knight (ed. 1839) as indicating the climax of the ills previously described, and allowing Therefore of 478 to introduce another clause in the catalogue of evils. Similarly influenced by Q2Ff punctuation, Moberly (ed. 1881) claims 476–7 must be either spurious or transposed from their proper place. Shakspere would not have so clumsily anticipated the point of the season’s confusion, which is presently to begin as a new subject with [482]; not to mention that the words here and now suggest that the action of the play is supposed to be in winter (whereas, as we presently see, violets and wild roses are out), and that the punctuation as it stands connects the therefore of [478] with what immediately precedes, instead of with Oberon and Titania’s quarrels. Chambers (ed. 1897) believes commentators to have been misled by Q2Ff’s comma at the end of 476, and by the assumption that hymns and carols belong necessarily to winter nights. I prefer to put a full stop after [476], and begin a new period with [477]. Then I should explain the passage as follows:—In [463–476] Titania describes the inclement summer due to the revenge of the winds. She concludes: The summer is so bad, that men wish it were winter. Then she begins again: Not only have we offended the winds, but we have neglected the hymns and carols due from us to the moon. Therefore she too is wrathful, and does her part to spoil the weather.

477 hymne or carroll]
Warburton (in Theobald, ed. 1733): (Notwithstanding the Impropriety of the Sentiment) since Christianity, this season, on Account of the Birth of the Author of our Faith, is particularly devoted to Festivity: and to this Custom, be assur’d, Hymn or Carol blest alludes. Johnson (ed. 1765): Though Shakespear is no great chronologer in his dramas, I think he has never so mingled true and false religion, as to give us reason for believing that he would make the moon incensed for the omission of our carols. I therefore imagine him to have meant heathen rites of adoration. Malone (in Steevens, ed. 1778, 3:127): Those sports with which country people are wont to beguile the winter’s evening. Douce (1807, 2:288), listing anachronisms: Carols, inasmuch as they are applicable to songs in general, and, in an antiquated sense, to dances, may be doubtful, though the allusion was in all probability to Christmas carols. Halliwell (ed. 1856): The term carol, now used solely for a lively devotional ballad sung at Christmas, was formerly employed in a more general sense. It seems here to be applied to any kind of religious song. Chambers (ed. 1897): The explanation of the hymns and carols, as addressed to the moon, may be supported from Chanting faint hymns to the cold fruitless moon [82]. The nuns, nymphs, and fairies are all treated as in some ways identical by Shakespeare in the play. Cuningham (ed. 1905) denies any such reference. Wilson (ed. 1924) claims the allusion is not to Christmas carols…, but to the songs and dances of May-time or the summer wakes, semi-religious merry-makings that lasted all night. Cf. OED, Hymn, 2a; Carol, 3b, quoting this instance. See also n. 82.

478–9 Moone…anger]
Malone (in Steevens, ed. 1778, 3:127): The moon is with peculiar propriety represented as incensed at the cessation…of those sports which have always been reputed to be celebrated by her light. Brooks (ed. 1979) compares Seneca’s Oedipus.

478 Moone…floods]
Thirlby (MS 1733–47) compares Tmp. 5.1.270 (2265), where Caliban’s mother is credited with being able to take over the moon’s characteristic powers: [She] could controle the Moone; make flowes, and ebs. Douce (1807, 1:187–8): In Newton’s Direction for the health of magistrates and students, 1574, 12mo, we are told that the moone is ladie of moysture [x2r]; and in [Ham. 1.1.118; Q2 b2v] she is called the moist star. In Bartholomæus De propriet. rerum, by Batman, [1582, 8.29], the moon is described to be mother of all humours, minister and lady of the sea. But in Lydgate’s prologue to his Storie of Thebes, there are two lines which Shakspeare seems closely to have imitated; Of Lucina the moone, moist and pale, That many showre fro heaven made availe [ed. Robert R. Edwards, 2001, ll. 7–8; see also Chaucer, Woorkes, 1561, 3R4r]. Halliwell (ed. 1856): In Walter Gray’s Almanacke and Prognostication for the year 1591, the moon is called the chief governesse of fluddes and streames [C2r]. Brooks (ed. 1979): By extension from causing tides and (supposedly) the menstrual flow. In Hero and Leander, I. 107, 111, in a passage Shakespeare recalled at [409 and 1420], that pale and watrie planet over-rules the flood [Works, ed. Gill, 1987, 1:191].

478 gouernesse]
Johnson (1755, 1), quoting this instance: A female invested with authority. OED (1a): Formerly often applied to the presiding or ruling goddess (of a department of nature, art, etc.).

478 floods]
OED (1): The flowing on of the tide. But also, as in n. 377, water generally, including streams. See also 503, 1424.

479 Pale]
For a pale moon as unpropitious Dyer (1883; 1966, p. 69) compares R2 2.4.10 (1294). Furness (ed. 1895): Because it can shine but dimly through the contagious fogs.

479 washes]
OED (7a), quoting this instance: To inundate or saturate with water. Rann (ed. 1787): Impregnates with unwholsome moisture. Halliwell (ed. 1856) quotes Batman vppon Bartholome, 1582, 8.29, fol. 133va: The Moone gathereth deawe in the aire, for she printeth the vertue of hir moysture in the aire, and chaungeth the ayre in a manner that is vnseene, & breedeth and gendereth deaw in the vtter part thereof.

480 Rheumaticke diseases]
Johnson (1755, Rheumatick), quoting this instance: Proceeding from rheum or a peccant watry humour (OED 4a, quoting this instance). Malone (ed. 1790, 10:578): Signified in Shakspeare’s time, not what we now call rheumatism, but distillations from the head, catarrhs, &c. Wright (ed. 1877) quotes Malone, adding: It would be more correct to say that the term included all this in addition to what is now understood by it.…The accent is on the first syllable, as in [Ven. 135]. See Abbott §492; Franz §74.

480 abound]
OED (v1, 1), quoting this instance: Prevail widely.

481 thorough]
For the disyllabic form, see n. 376–7.

481 distemperature]
Johnson (1755, 1), quoting this instance: Intemperateness; excess of heat or cold, or other qualities (OED 5). Steevens (ed. 1778): Perturbation of the elements. Rann (ed. 1787): Irregularity, confusion. Malone (ed. 1790): The perturbed state in which the king and queen had lived for some time past. The Clarkes (ed. 1864): Here used for mutual displeasure, disagreement, disturbance. Tyrrell (ed. 1850): On account of this intemperate quarrel and violence between Oberon and Titania, the functions of nature are deranged and the seasons alter. Furness (ed. 1895): May mean the anger of the moon. Doran (ed. 1959): Disorder in the natural constitution. Wells (ed. 1967): Both ill-humour, discomposure and bad weather. See OED 1, 3; possibly also (4): Trans. and fig. Derangement, disturbance, disorder (of society, state, etc.) On alteration of the seasons, see also n. 456–92.

482–8 hoary…Liueries]
Martindale (1990, pp. 74–5), drawing a parallel also noted by Bush (1932, p. 111): A surreal vision of confusion in the seasons, a version of the impossibility topos (adyneton), [is] a figure common in classical poetry.…The lines have their background in Spenser and Ovid/Golding (the four seasons in the palace of the Sun, Metamorphoses II.27–30 [Golding 32–9, see ]; and in Pythagoras’ speech, XV.199–213 [Golding 221–37, see ]), with a further possible link with Medea’s alteration of the seasons in Metamorphoses VII.…[T]he lines oscillate between a personification of aspects of nature, with erotic overtones, involving a weird but vigorous union of youth and age, and a description, of some precision, of a phenomenon in nature. Similarly the masque-like figure of Hiems as an old man covered in ice but wearing a garland of roses partly dissolves into a description of a wintry scene…. The trick is typical of Ovid; in XI.157ff. [Golding 176–8; see ] Mount Tmolus is at one moment a fully anthropomorphized god, at the next the mountain itself with nodding trees. See also the unseasonable weather caused by Flora’s resentment, Fasti 5.321–4. See also n. 484–6.

482–6 hoary…set]
Howarth (1961, p. 92): Look at that from one angle and it is Ovid in English; look at it from another, and it is no such thing but medieval personification.

482–3 hoary…rose]
Edgecombe (2001, p. 177), drawing on a sexual meaning of lappe (cf. n. 421): Although Shakespeare means us primarily to think of the rose’s receptive lap as a hollow iced with hoarfrost, he also invites us to imagine ancient men (personifications of winter like Chaucer’s January)…copulating with young rosy women, the metonyms of May. The idea is to some extent duplicated in the image of Hiems’s chaplet of summer flowers. Cf. MerT 1738, 2042–52; see , . Berowne uses a similar image for unseasonable behavior contrary to the course of nature (LLL 1.1.105–7 [114–16]).

482 hoary headed frosts]
Brooks (ed. 1979): Echoing Shepheardes Calender, [December, 135], My head besprent with hoary frost, emphasized by E. K.’s gloss. Gill (ed. 1981): Anything affected by frosts is white (hoary) on the surface.

482 hoary]
Johnson (1755, 3), quoting this instance: White with frost. OED (1a): Of the hair, head, or beard: Grey or white with age. There seems little reason for Q2’s change to hoared (see textual n.), which OED defines as Made or grown hoary, and at (3) quotes this instance as in Q2.

483 Fall…lappe]
Steevens (ed. 1778): To have snow in the lap of June, is an expression used in [Dekker’s] Northward Hoe, 1607 [4.1.132; 2:451], and Shakespeare himself in Coriolanus [mistakenly for Tim. 4.3.385–6 (2025–6)], talks of the consecrated snow that lies on Dian’s lap: and Spenser in his Faery Queen [2.2.6.5], has: And fills with flow’rs fair Flora’s painted lap. Caldecott (MS 1813–33): This lubberly trick, common in our author’s time, which Gallants had of laying their heads in lady’s laps, furnished the metaphor in the verse. The general custom is not any where censured by our author: but on the contrary some of his best characters either practise, or invite to, it; as Hamlet & Mortimer’s wife [Ham. 3.2.112–14 (1966–8); 1H4 3.1.212 (1755)].

483 fresh lappe]
Deighton (ed. 1891): The blooming calix, or cup.

484–6 on…set]
The image of a chaplet on the chin has been defended. Capell (1783, 2.3:105) was struck with some distant remembrance of the incana barba of a Silenus or some such person having a chaplet put on it by nymphs that are playing with him; and thought it possible, that that passage (which he cannot recover) might have produc’d chin in the place alluded to. In Ovid, Met. 11.91, Phrygian rustics bind Silenus with wreaths (vinctum coronis; Thomas (1587): Corona: A crowne, a chaplet, a garland). Vergil has incrinis incanaque menta (locks and hoary chin) at Aen. 6.809 and barbas incanaque menta (beard and hoary chin) at Georgics 3.311, in both of which incana may be seen as a transferred epithet (hypallage), and menta as a poetic plural often used for beard. Weston (in Steevens, ed. 1785) attributes it to the influence of Vergil’s description of Mount Atlas, quoting Aen. 4.250–1. Malone (ed. 1790) supplies Phaer’s translation of the Vergilian lines: And from his hoary beard adowne, The streames of waters fall, with yse and frost his face doth frowne (1584, F5); he adds (ed. 1790, 10:578) that he believes the singular image to have been suggested by the description of Winter in Golding’s Ovid (2.39; see ). Verplanck (ed. 1845): The image of the snowy beard of Winter, as well as his icy crown, being wreathed with sweet summer buds, is sufficiently clear, as well as poetical, and suits the personification.…[It is] an image which has, I believe, been used by ancient poets, and certainly by modern painters. Halliwell (ed. 1856): The author evidently intended a grotesque contrast. Thiselton (1903, p. 29): There is nothing out of harmony with the context, if we imagine the chaplet encircling the face, its upper arc resting on Hiem’s crown, its lower on his chin. Thiselton (1904, p. 16) quoting Aeneid 4.216–17, where Paris has a Maeonian band propping his chin and essenced locks (tr. Fairclough, Loeb ed.): Why should not the Chaplet have had a chin-band of the same composition as itself? No commentator has drawn attention to the alliterative link chinne and Chaplet.
But emendations were very early proposed and adopted (see textual n., and conj. ). Thin conjectured by Whaley (in Thirlby, MS 1733–47) is now the widely accepted reading for chinne. Tyrwhitt (in Steevens, ed. 1778): I.e. thin-hair’d. Steevens (ed. 1785) cites Cordelia speaking of Lear’s thin helm (Lr. 4.7.35 [2783+4]) and (in Reed, ed. 1803) the White Bear[d]es who arm’d their thin and hairelesse Scalps against Richard (R2 3.2.112 [1470]). Walker (1860, 2:274): The corruption of t into c is frequent in old books. Wilson (ed. 1924): The error was particularly likely to happen before h; but in any case Shakespeare’s t and c were often dangerously alike; he refers the reader to Plates V and VI in Shakespeare’s Hand in The Play of Sir Thomas More (1923). Fripp (1930, p. 124 n. 4) claims that Ovid’s use of spoliata (stripped, deprived) describing Winter’s hair (Met. 15.213) confirms the amended reading thin for chin. Brooks (ed. 1979) develops the suggestion: Golding’s Winter has shirle thinne heare as whyght as snowe [15.235] (cf. thin and icy); E.K.’s gloss on Colin’s frosty hairs calls them scattered.
Cuningham (ed. 1905) is the only modern commentator to favor the early suggestion chill (see conj. ): The double epithet…of chill and icy is not out of place or merely tautological;…The point of the epithets seems to lie rather in the coldness of old Hiems’ scalp than in its want of covering.

484 Hyems]
Latin hiems, f. OED: Winter; esp. in poet. personification. Martindale (1990, p. 75) notes that Hiems is a female, not a male figure in Ovid (2.30); in Golding’s expanded translation (2.36–9, see ) Winter is male. Delius (ed. 1859) quotes LLL 5.2.891 (2855). The insertion of an apostrophe before the s by Rowe (ed. 1709) and others (see textual n.) led Neil (ed. 1878) and Sprague (ed. 1896) to see a connection with Sanskrit hima, snow. For the genitive see n. 182.

485 An…buddes]
Compare 1567.

485 odorous]
OED, quoting this instance: Sweet-smelling, fragrant. See also 895–6 and nn.

485 Chaplet]
Johnson (1755, 1), quoting this instance: A garland or wreath to be worn about the head (OED, 1). Brooks (ed. 1979) sees a reminiscence of Spenser, Shep. Cal. November, 115: Coloured chaplets.

486 as in mockery]
Scott (ed. 1898): The flowers of summer on the head of winter, or the emblems of youth placed on old age, strike the observer as incongruous and mocking. Gill (ed. 1981): As though making fun of the old man.

486–8 Spring…Liueries]
Theobald (1909, p. 240), ignoring the confusion of the seasons in these lines: This closely resembles the passage in Lucan’s address to Piso,—Nature herself is subject to change, and variously shapes her course, And unfolds the year by means of the changing aspect of vegetation. (Ad Pisonem, 133.)

487 childing]
Rann (ed. 1787): Fruitful, bearing fruit (OED, ppl. a. arch., 2. fig. a, quoting this instance first). For the changing of nouns into verbs, see n. 442 (versing).

487 childing Autumne]
Warburton (ed. 1747): It is an old word which signified teeming, bearing fruit.…This is the proper epithet of Autumn. Steevens (ed. 1773): The pregnant autumn, frugifer autumnus. Cf. Horace Odes 4.7.11: Pomifer autumnus fruges effuderit. Holt White (1785, p. 278): Childing is also an old term in botany, when a small flower grows out of a large one, The childing autumn, i.e. producing flowers on those of summer; adding unseasonably in Steevens (ed. 1793), which Wright (ed. 1877) contradicts: It means the autumn which seasonably produces its own fruits. It is the change of seasons which makes it abnormal. Knight (ed. 1839) notes the parallel with Son. 97: The teeming Autumne big with ritch increase. Abbott §290: Autumn producing fruits as it were children. F4’s chiding (see textual n.) is supported by Capell (1783, 2.3:105), not seeing how the epithet angry could well have presented itself to the Poet, if chiding had not preceded; and by White (ed. 1858): In allusion to the lowering skies and harsh winds of Autumn. Similar reasons led Herr (1879, pp. 92–3) and Noyes (1892, pp. 531–3) to support chilling or churlish (see conj., ).

488 wonted Liueries]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Usual dress. Cf. n. 79.

488–9 the…is which]
Mowat & Werstine (ed. 1993): The bewildered world can no longer distinguish one season from another according to the produce (increase) normally brought forth in each.

488 mazed]
OED (Maze, v, 3): Bewilder, confuse. Often with some notion of a figurative maze or labyrinth. Wright (ed. 1877) compares 1H6 4.2.47 (1998). Berry (1984, pp. 148–9) comments on the popularity of mazes and knot gardens which often had central bowers, as Titania’s bower is central to the mazed world, and on the use of the maze both to reflect and create a special state of mind. Richman (1990, pp. 98–9), noting Titania’s earlier reference to mazes (474): The world in its confusion has become literally and figuratively a maze, a labyrinth in which no right path can be found. But the word takes on also its second sense of amazed, that is, astonished, struck with wonder by the alterations. The speech in performance will stand or fall on the actress’s ability to convince the audience of her character’s astonishment and shame that she and Oberon are damaging the natural world. See also n. 474–5.

489 increase]
Thirlby (MS 1725–33): Products. Johnson (ed. 1765): Produce. Malone (1780, 1:116) quotes Son. 97; see n. 487. Malone (ed. 1790): Scriptural: Then shall the earth bring forth her increase [Ps. 67:6]. See also Heath (1765, p. 48), who refers obliquely to this psalm as daily repeated in the evening service of the Common Prayer. Cf. Scot, . Wright (ed. 1877): Products or fruits, which formerly distinguished the seasons (cf. OED 7a). But Brooks (ed. 1979), possibly drawing on OED 1a or 4: Aggrandizement. The stress on the second syllable was normal until the 1700s; see OED.

490–2 And…originall]
Birch (1848, p. 212) sees here an indication that the fairies’ religion approaches anthropomorphism. Brooks (ed. 1979) compares Seneca’s Oedipus, where the hero acknowledges that all things feel the plague that he has brought about (ll. 36–185). Girard (1980, p. 118): Titania makes it clear that the real problem has nothing to do with nature; it is rooted in the rivalry between herself…and Oberon.…The quarrel occurs because of an insignificant pretext, and is an example of pure prestige rivalry. Hopkins (2003, p. 200): The conjunction of evils, parents and original points unmistakably to the introduction of original sin through the evil deed of our first parents, Adam and Eve. It thus introduces a note of foreboding on a scale suitable to the Fall of Man.…[I]t paradoxically also lightens the tone, for there can of course be no such thing as a Christian tragedy.

490–1 And…dissention]
On the lineation, see .

490 progeny]
Johnson (1755): Offspring; race; generation. OED (2. fig.): Issue, result.

491 our…our]
Brown (ed. 1996): Titania’s two ours signal that she has shifted from accusing Oberon to taking full responsibility for her part in the feud. Her aim is to restore harmony. But he refuses to accept the mutual guilt she implies.

491 debate]
OED (n.1 1a): Strife, quarrelling, wrangling. Obs. or arch. Deighton (ed. 1891): Used in a much stronger sense than at present, and more in accordance with the literal meaning of the verb, sc. to beat down.

491 dissention]
Four syllables; see n. 159.

492 Parents and originall]
McPeek (1972, p. 75) suggests a reminiscence of the claim of Apuleius’s Venus, En rerum naturae prisca parens, en elementorum origo initialis (4.30), translated by Adlington, Beholde the originall parent of all these elementes (see ).

492 Parents]
OED (4. fig. a), quoting this instance first: Source, cause.

492 originall]
Johnson (1755, 2): Fountain; source; that which gives beginning or existence. OED (n. 2a): = ORIGIN n. 2.; an originator, author. Now rare or arch. Deighton (ed. 1891): First cause.

493 Doe…in you]
Clayton (1999, p. 71) quotes this line as the heading to an argument that Oberon’s mildly phrased dialogue indicates that he is in the right concerning the quarrel over the changeling.

493 amend]
OED (v. 2): Correct, rectify. arch. Morse (2003, p. 55): A word associated with spiritual renewal, restoration, as in amendment of life. See also 2016, 2218, 2222 and nn.

493 it]
Deighton (ed. 1891): The present state of things between us.

493 it lyes in you]
Wright (ed. 1877), comparing Son. 101.10: It is in your power (OED, Lie, v1. B. 12c).

494 Why…Oberon]
Deighton (ed. 1891): What reason is there (i.e. there is no reason) that compels Titania to thwart him she once loved so fondly? said coaxingly.

494 crosse]
See n. 160.

495 I…boy]
Bell (1861, 2:207) claims: In Oberon and Titania’s contest for the child…we have the contest of Jupiter and Juno for the infant Hercules. See also n. 542–5. Campbell (in Campbell, Rothschild, & Vaughan ed. 1965, p. 10) argues that the entertainment at Elvetham in 1591 was part of the Earl of Hertford’s campaign to have his son by Lady Catherine Grey re-established as legitimate, and hence heir to the throne: If the play contains clear references to these events, the little changeling boy…is Hertford’s son. Titania, or the Queen, would be the legal guardian of the child, although he should belong to Oberon’s (King Henry VIII’s) train because in his will he had designated him as his heir.

495 Changeling]
See n. 393.

496 Henchman]
Pope (ed. 1725): Or Usher. Thirlby (MS 1725–33): Rather page, I think. Grey (1754, 1:50): A Henchman was a page of honour. ’Twas an ancient office which was dissolv’d by queen Elizabeth in the year 1565. Johnson (1755), quoting this instance: A page; an attendant. Obsolete. Steevens (ed. 1773): The office might be abolished at court, but probably remained in the city; (ed. 1778): Skinner derives the word from Hine A.S. quasi domesticus famulus. Spelman from Hengstman, equi curator. Blackstone (in Malone, 1780, 1:116): Quasi haunch-man. One that goes behind another. Pedisequus. Douce (1807, 1:189): Although in more modern times the pages or henchmen might have walked on foot, it is very certain that they were originally horsemen. OED, quoting MND among many instances at 1b, discusses the history of the office and the derivation of the word, agreeing with Douce that it comes from hengest, hengst, a male horse. Lecercle (1989, p. 149) argues that Hengst meaning both stallion and gelding refers to both knight and child, and that in species less noble than horses corresponds to jackass: Henchman is thus literally a jackass-man, i.e. Bottom with an ass’s head,…Bottom is not different from the changeling, he is a repetition, a Doppelgänger.

497–513 Set…him]
For the dramatic purpose of this passage, see Hunter in n. 524–45. Sitwell (1948, p. 192): There is a marvel of air-thin texture, woven upon the loom of the moonlight and shadow. Halliday (1954, p. 95), praising the assonance and rhythm: It is not dramatic poetry, but it is narrative poetry such as had never before been heard in English. Barber (1959, pp. 136–7): [This speech] can serve as an epitome of the metamorphic action of the play.…[I]t is a glimpse of women who gossip alone, apart from men and feeling now no need of them, rejoicing in their own special part of life’s power.…The passage conveys a wanton joy in achieved sexuality, in fertility; and a gay acceptance of the waxing of the body…The mother, having laughed to see the ship a woman with child, imitates it so as to go the other way about and herself become a ship. But cf. n. 504–10. Everett (1961, p. 321): The world of feminine loyalty that Titania clings to is even more interesting [than that of Hermia and Helena described in 1230–43], in the rich and strange sensuousness with which it is presented. Marshall (1982, p. 568): When Titania insists to Oberon that the fairyland buys not the child of me [498], she is perpetuating rather than rejecting terms that inscribe people in a system of economic relations. Her monologue pictures the boy as merchandise. Montrose (1983, p. 72): Titania represents her bond to her votaress as one that is rooted in an experience of female fecundity, an experience for which men must seek merely mercantile compensations. P. Parker (1987, p. 19), quoting Barber: [The votaress] figures by metonymy much of what happens to the female—as to a temporary dilation and delay—in A Midsummer Night’s Dream, which begins with the deferral of consummation, deviates into a topsy-turvy middle space of female rebellion, and ends with the proper marital hierarchy.…What, then, in Barber’s phrase, might have seemed an autonomous female-centered world turns out to have been only a dilative detour en route to that end [of re-establishing patriarchy]. Slights (1988, p. 261), taking issue with Marshall and Montrose: The passage captures the pleasure the two women take in gently mocking the world of marine commerce with the richer joys of sorority and pregnancy. Mahood (1992, p. 7): The Indian Boy’s mother, who of that boy did die, is made so vivid by the poetry…that she becomes a significant part of a play which is as much concerned with the perils of marriage as it is with its joys. Dubrow (1994, p. 162), commenting on the imagery of material treasure, and on commodities, in terms of orphaned children and the wardship system: The text is indubitably concerned with guardianship and its self-serving rewards, but it raises these questions within a scene whose language primarily and predominantly celebrates the relationship between Titania and her votaress.…The point is neither that the lyricism of the speech is a mere screen concealing the competitive acquisitiveness of protocapitalism nor that the casting of guardianship in mercantile terms is an irrelevant and untroubling detail in an image of joyful communion; rather, conflicting tones and levels coexist in uneasy and unresolved suspension; the argument is elaborated in Dubrow (1999, pp. 147–9). Raman (2001, p. 244): Titania’s description…cunningly instrumentalizes the discourse of mercantile colonialism to justify her ownership of the child. See also n. 508–11 and .

497 Set…rest]
OED (Rest, n.1 9b), quoting this instance first: Assure, decide finally. Golding adds the phrase to Ovid in translating Scylla’s longing for her father’s lock of hair (8.80; Golding 8.100), and Picus’s rejection of Circe (14.378; Golding 14.432, see ); the priority given by OED to MND is an example of the frequent privileging of Sh. in that work. See also Sidney’s Arcadia, where Amphialus responds ironically to the Forsaken Knight’s challenge, herein set your heart at rest, you shall not be forsaken (ed. Evans, 1977, p. 535). Thirlby (MS 1733–47) cites Ray’s Proverbs [1737, p. 207]. See Tilley (H327). Deighton (ed. 1891): Said sarcastically; do not for a moment allow yourself to be agitated by any hope on the subject; make up your mind that such a thing is quite out of the question. Brown (ed. 1996): Titania completes Oberon’s verse line, suggesting that she does not pause before responding.

498 The…mee]
Deighton (ed. 1891): The whole fairyland is an insufficient bribe to tempt me to give up the boy. The emendation of The to Thy conjectured by Thirlby (MS 1733–47) on the analogy of thy Fairy kingdome (520) and attributed by Collier (1853, p. 101) to his old corrector (see textual n.) is considered by Furness (ed. 1895) a trivial and harmless improvement. The possible distinction between the whole of fairyland and that share of it which Oberon can claim is ignored by Clemen (ed. 1963): i.e., even your whole domain could not buy.

498 of]
Gill (ed. 1981): From (OED 10a). See Abbott §166; Franz §509; Blake 5.4.2 Of (a).

499–513 His…him]
Wilson (ed. 1924): It has been supposed by most commentators that the account which Titania now gives of the boy’s birth…conflicts with the changeling story. On the contrary, it develops it. The mother, concubine of an Indian king, dies in child-bed; and Titania, for love of the mother, steals the child. Conceivably this changeling story may have been more prominent in the version of 1594 or of 1592. Agreeing that Titania would still have to resort to kidnapping, Phillips (ed. 1969) adds: There seems to have been a predictable mixture of selfishness in her unselfish-sounding action. See also nn. 392, 495, 512–13.

499 Votresse]
OED, quoting this instance and 540: Var. of votaress, after forms like enchantress, protectress. OED (Votaress): A female votary, esp. a woman devoted to a religious life or to a special saint. Sh.’s only other use is the trisyllabic form in Per. 4.Chor.4.

499 Votresse…order]
Hunter (ed. 1870): A votaress of Diana, like Titania; he refers to Per., where Thaisa takes on vestal livery at Diana’s temple, becoming her votaress (3.4.10 [1363], 3.4.13 [1367], 4.Chor.4 [1374]). Porter & Clarke (ed. 1903): Possibly Titania meant they were both worshipers of Diana, who was the ancient goddess of motherhood as well as the medieval goddess of fairies. Others believe that Titania is here being equated with Diana, which Wilson (ed. 1924) finds problematical in view of the pregnancy. Schanzer (Moon, 1955, pp. 241–2) acknowledges the frequent identification of Diana with the queen of the fairies, but agrees that it seems perverse to give to Diana a human votaress who is with child; he suggests a topical allusion to some unidentified incident concerning Queen Elizabeth and one of her ladies-in-waiting. Staton (1963, pp. 170–1) finds parallels with the story of Diana’s nymph Callisto, whether in Ovid (Met. 2.401–507; cf. Golding 2.512–613, see ) or Renaissance versions; Witte (1996, p. 56 n. 34) notes that Titania’s reaction to the pregnancy is opposite to Diana’s (Fr.). Phillips (ed. 1969) objects to the equation of Titania and Diana: In spite of the correspondence of names the aloof goddess of chastity cannot really be equated with the rash wanton Titania.

500–10 And…marchandise]
Venezky (1951, p. 142; Plate VIII): In the Elvetham water show, gold-breasted India, who accompanies Nereus, sends as a gift to Elizabeth the ship whose swollen sails can be seen in the illustration. See also , and Wilson (1980, pp. 96–118).

500 spiced]
OED (ppl. a., 3), quoting this as the only example before 1881: Fragrant, aromatic; spice-laden. Cf. Cotgrave (1611, Aromatizé): Perfumed, aromatized, spiced.

500 Indian]
Halliwell (1841, p. 76) quotes Batman vppon Bartholome, 1582, Bk. 15, fol. 252r, Of India: As the riuers there are very many, so are they very great, through whose watry ouerflowing it commeth to passe, that in the moyst grounde, the force of the Sunne approaching, ingendreth or bringeth foorth all things in great quantitie: and seemeth almost to fill the whole worlde, with Spice and Precious stoanes. Of which, it aboundeth more than all other Countreyes of the worlde. Webb (1989, p. 63) notes erotic associations with INDIA, and contrasts Sh.’s moral treatment: Sh.…places India in line with fertility, motherhood, embuing surrounding nature with procreative richness. Desai (1998, p. 3) argues for a reference to the rivalry between England and Portugal for the spice trade.

501 Full]
OED (C. adv. 1c): Very, exceedingly. See Franz §379; Blake 5.1.3.1 (iii), both quoting 1420. See 1420, 1991. See also n. 949.

501 gossipt]
Johnson (1755, To Gossip, 1), quoting this instance: To chat; to prate; to be merry. OED (v. 2): To act as a gossip, or familiar acquaintance; be a boon-companion. See also n. 418.

502 Neptunes]
Root (1903, p. 91), citing this line and Ham. 1.1.119 (Q2 B2v), 3.2.156 (2025); Mac. 2.2.57 (721); Per. 3. Chor. 45: In a few instances,…Neptune is spoken of as owning the sea. See 1433.

502 yellow sands]
Tollet (MS 1740–79, p. 60) followed by Rolfe (ed. 1877) compares Ariel’s song, Come vnto these yellow sands (Tmp. 1.2.375 [520]). Fripp (1930, pp. 100–1) hears an echo of fulvae arenae, a favourite phrase of Ovid’s (e.g. Met. 2.865, 9.36, 11.355). Brooks (ed. 1979) believes Sh. here and in Tmp. recalls Marlowe’s Hero and Leander, where all is whist…Save…the sea playing on yellow sand (1.346–7; 2:440).

503 Marking…flood]
Knight (1932, p. 145): As we watch Titania and her loved friend laughing at the traders on the flood, imitating their voyage on the waters of life, we see fairyland laughing at storm-tossed mortality.

503 Marking]
Cotgrave (1611, Marquer): To marke, note;…to heed, regard, obserue, take especiall notice of (OED 13). See 542, 1631. See also nn. 949, 1611.

503 embarked…flood]
Wright (ed. 1877): The merchants embarked upon the sea. For this position of the participle see [Tim. 4.2.13 (1560)]: A dedicated beggar to the air. Cf. Abbott §419a.

503 embarked]
OED (ppl. a.), quoting this line and Ven. 818 as the only examples: That has gone or been put on board ship.

503 traders]
Schmidt (1875): Persons engaged in trade and commerce. So also Wright, ed. 1877. Delius (ed. 1859), on the analogy of Tmp. 2.1.5 (679), The Masters of some Merchant, understands trading ships. So also Ridley (ed. 1934, Glossary) and others, including Foakes (ed. 1984): The sense is unambiguous, but is first recorded in OED in 1712. By contrast, the sense trading vessel, merchantman for merchant is amply documented in 16th-c. usage (OED, 4).

503 flood]
See n. 377.

504–10 When…marchandise]
Taylor (2002, pp. 38–9) sees an important distinction between what [the votaress] does and what the ships do. The distinction is between imitation and translation, simile and metaphor, likeness and identity.…In imitating the ships, the votaress, always conscious of her act of imitation, retains her own identity, remains herself;…the sails lose their identity as sails and become pregnant women.…[T]he language of Titania…makes the distinction, of critical importance throughout the play, between something remaining itself and something becoming something else.

504 When]
Verity (ed. 1893): At which times (OED 6a).

504–5 sailes…winde]
Craig (ed. 1903): We have the same conceit in Chapman’s continuation of Marlowe’s Hero and Leander:—And all her fleet of spirits [sprites] came swelling in With child of sail [325–6; 2:466]. Cf. also Beaumont and Fletcher’s The Maid’s Tragedy: Rather the wind but courts the pregnant sails (2.2.19; noted by T. W. Craik, ed. 1988). Jones (1951, pp. 280–3) discusses beliefs in the possibility of conception from the wind, citing e.g. Ovid, Fasti 5.195–202, where Chloris/Flora is ravished by Zephyrus; quoting MND, he notes (p. 283): In more modern times this ancient belief is found only in the form of poetic analogy.… Not only have the life-bringing powers been ascribed to the outer air, usually in the form of wind, but this has been extensively identified with the principle of life and creation altogether.

504 sailes]
For the neuter noun taken as feminine see Franz §206.

505 bigge bellied]
Johnson (1755, Bigbellied), quoting this instance: Pregnant; with child; great with young (OED, Big, a. B.2a).

505 wanton winde]
Malone (in Boswell, ed. 1821): Why the wind was termed wanton we may learn from Othello [4.2.78 (2774)]: The bawdy wind that kisseth all it meets. Again, in The Merchant of Venice [2.6.16 (912)]: Hugg’d and embraced by the strumpet wind.

505 wanton]
Holzknecht & McClure (ed. 1937): (a) sportive, (b) lascivious (Johnson, 1755, 1, 3; OED 3 and 2b, quoting this instance). Doran (ed. 1959): Sportive, amorous. Andrews (ed. 1989): Erotically playful. See also nn. 438, 474.

506 swimming]
Johnson (1755, To Swim, v. n. 4), quoting this instance: To glide along with a smooth or dizzy motion. OED (ppl. a. 3), quoting this instance first: Of the carriage of the body: characterized by a smooth waving motion. Obs. Halliwell (ed. 1856): Ben Jonson, in his Cynthia’s revels [2.4.55; 4:78], mentions the swim as a technical phrase for a particular movement in dancing; quoted in OED, Swim, n. 2. Baker (ed. 1896): Undulating. Greenblatt (ed. 1997): As though gliding through the waves.

506 gate]
OED (n.2 7), referring to Gait (n.1): Bearing or carriage while moving. See 2150. Cf. n. 2200.

507–8 Following…imitate]
Warburton (in Theobald, ed. 1733): Following What? She did not follow the Ship whose Motion she imitated; for That sail’d on the Water, She on the Land. And if by following, we are to understand, copying; it is a mere Pleonasm, that Meaning being included in the Word imitate. From Circumstamces in the Context, there is great Reason to think our Author wrote, follying, i.e. wantoning, in Sport and Gaiety; so the old Writers used Follity for Foolishness; and Both Words are from, and in the Sense of folâtrer, to play the Wanton. And this admirably agrees with the Action, for which she is here commended, with the Context. (Follity and to folly are not recorded in OED). Warburton’s difficulties and emendation were derided by Heath (1765, pp. 48–9), and dismissed by Johnson (ed. 1765): The foregoing Note is very ingenious, but since follying is a word of which I know not any example, and the Fairy’s favourite might, without much licentiousness of language, be said to follow a ship that sailed in the direction of the coast, I think there is no sufficient reason for adopting it. The coinage of new words is a violent remedy, not to be used but in the last necessity. Despite these strictures, Johnson used Warburton’s emendation in quoting 506–8 (see n. 506). Kenrick (1765, pp. 17–19) traces out the image of a ship appearing to follow its wind-filled fore-sail, arguing that the line should be repunctuated to make womb the object of Following, a suggestion favored by several editors (see textual n.) but condemned by Mason (1798, p. 18) as ridiculous, Wright (ed. 1877) as worse than unnecessary, and Furness (ed. 1895) as repulsive. Delius (ed. 1859): Following = following as an example, imitating; it has the preceding which as an object, and this which refers to the whole preceding sentence to see the sails conceive etc (Ger.). For other proposed changes, see conj., .

507 rich with]
Schmidt (1875, 1): Enriched by. OED (Rich, 3b fig.): Wealthy in. See also n. 510, and 598, 1256. Collier (1853, p. 101), claiming the MS correction ripe, suggests the word rich had perhaps been caught from [510].

508–11 saile…dye]
Leggatt (1999, p. 52): They joke about the votaress’s parody of the male activity of trade; but the power and wealth her pregnancy embodies are also fatal to her. Women die giving life. Men (as in The Merchant of Venice) risk their merchandise; women risk everything. Edwards (1997, p. 180) observes that the image carries forward, making her death a shipwreck, and the child a rescued treasure.

510 rich with]
OED (a. 3a): Wealthy, amply provided with. Deighton (ed. 1891): Qualifying she in [506]. Compare n. 507.

511 she…mortall]
Golding uses this phrase of the death of Semele, when Jove removed the child Bacchus from her womb to sustain him himself (3.387–92; see )

511 of]
Abbott §168: As a consequence of. See Franz §518 Anm. 2.

511–12 dye,…boy:]
Wilson (ed. 1924) believes the end punctuation to be transposed.

512–13 And…him]
Burnand (MS 1888–1917): And what is to become of him as he grows up to be a man? Chambers (ed. 1897), citing MV 5.1.193–4 (2617–18): A succession of lines which all begin in the same way is much in Shakespeare’s earlier manner. Miller (1940, p. 69), flatly but unauthoritatively: A lie. Titania was in love with the changeling, the loved boy [396].…[S]he treats the boy precisely as she treats Bottom when enamoured of him; he compares 396–7 with 1566–7. Cody (1969, p. 135): A good case can be made for taking the implied tenor of her relations with the changeling and with Bottom to be Bacchic. See also nn. 397, 438. Traub (1998, p. 142): With the repetition of the line And for her sake, and her later reiteration that she will not part with the boy, Not for thy fairy kingdom [520], Titania sounds a note of female loyalty, of the primacy, strength, and endurance of female bonds.

512 reare]
OED (v.1 9b), quoting this instance first: Bring up, foster.

513 part with]
Johnson (1755, To Part, v. n. 6), quoting this instance: To Part with. To quit; to resign; to lose; to be separated from. Franz (§535): To part with and to part from are used by Shakespeare in the sense of to separate from, take leave of a person. He does not differentiate, as observed in modern usage, between with, which expresses the position of a subject vis à vis the act of separation (he parted with his footman, i.e. he dismissed his footman) and he parted with his friend, i.e. he took leave from his friend.)…However, Shakespeare is familiar with parting with in the sense of giving away [OED 6c(b)] (Ger.). See also Blake 4.3.10 on phrasal verbs, and the way Sh. plays with the possible double meanings of prepositional extensions.

514 How…stay]
Phillips (ed. 1969): Oberon’s rapid change of subject suggests that he means to resort to some other means than argument.

514 stay]
Johnson (1755, n.s. 1): Continuance in a place; forbearance of departure (OED, n.3 6). Doran (ed. 1959): A noun, object of intend. But Wright (ed. 1877) and Rolfe (ed. 1877), citing Abbott §349, read it as a verb; so also Franz §650, and Gill (ed. 1981): Do you intend to stay? See n. 260.

514–15 stay…day]
Ness (1941, p. 128), commenting on speech-link rhyme here and 1282–3: There is no strong justification for these rhymes. In a play as full of rhyme as this one they might easily have slipped in without the deliberate intention of the playwright.

515 Theseus]
Disyllabic: see n. 26. For the genitive see n. 182.

516 If…Round]
Halliwell (ed. 1856): The fairy poetry of the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries contains frequent allusions to the dancing in the round or ring.…At an earlier period, Chaucer mentions the dancing of the Fairy Queen and her followers, [WBT 860–1; see ]. Golding uses the phrase to describe the devotion of the wood nymphs to the sacred oak of Ceres: oftentimes with hand in hand they danced in a round About the Trunk (8.934–5). Reed (in Steevens ed. 1785, at 651] quotes Sir John Davies’ Orchestra, 1622 (first pub. 1596): Thus when at first Love had them marshalled As earst he did the shapelesse masse of things, He taught them rounds and winding Heyes to tread (Poems, ed. Krueger, 1975, p. 107). Craig (ed. 1903): See North, Plutarch’s Lives (Life of Aristides), ed. 1595, p. 349, the daunce of the children which daunced in a round at Athens. Kittredge (ed. 1939): Cf. Ben Jonson’s Althorp Entertainment, 1603 (A Satyre, Workes, 1616, pp. 871, 872): There came tripping vp the lawne, a beuy of Faeries, attending on Mab their Queene, who falling into an artificiall ring, that was there cut in the park, began to dance a round [7:121–2]. Howard (1993, p. 331): The round danced by Titania and the fairies is an emblem of connection: each dancer facing in toward the others, their linked hands miming inclusion and continuity. This is the dance of an oral tradition.…Oberon, in contrast, has withdrawn from popular culture and not only refuses to dance but also impedes the dancers.
Andrews (ed. 1989) proposes reading patiently as a four-syllable word, with the first and third syllables stressed, in order to regularize the iambic pentameter; others may prefer to hear a dancing measure in an irregular four-stress line: trochee, iamb, and two anapests.

516 patiently]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Without displaying ill humour such as you have of late so often displayed.

516 Round]
Schmidt (1875, subst. 2): A dance in a circle (OED, n.1 11a). Reed (in Malone, ed. 1790, at 651): Rounds or roundels were like the present country dances. See also nn. 461–2, 651, 921.

517 Moonelight Reuelles]
Cf. Wiv. 5.5.38 (2520). See also nn. 23, 222–3.

518 spare]
OED (v.1 7b), quoting this instance: To avoid, shun, keep clear of. Now rare.

518 haunts]
Schmidt (1874, 1): A place much frequented. Possibly with overtones of the meaning of the verb, to be troubled by manifestations of spirits: see nn. 918, 1027.

520 Not…away]
Early editors were troubled by what they perceived as irregular metre (see textual n.). On Pope’s substitution of Elves Thirlby (MS 1733–47) comments: I think no living author would like to have such liberties taken with his writings. Steevens (ed. 1793), omitting Fairy on the advice of Dr. Farmer, and reading Fairies as a trisyllable, is mocked by Knight (ed. 1839) and Furness (ed. 1895). As Knowles (privately) points out, the unaccented syllables -ies, a- in the last foot are elided.

520 Fairies away]
Nicholson (1870, p. 56): While king and queen have been parleying and wrangling, the attendant courtiers and maids of honour have been frisking, intermingling, flirting, renewing acquaintance,…It is her thus scattered train that Titania recals with Fairies away! Besides, her majesty is in a pet, and will not that one remain behind or be out of her ordered place. See n. 436.

520–1 away…stay]
An example of the common practice of exit rhyme, as Ness (1941, p. 128) points out. For other examples of exit or cue rhymes, see 675–6, 900–1, 925–6, 1023–4, 1383–4+1, 1901–2, 2151–2.

521 We…Exeunt]
Gentleman (ed. 1774): This scene of altercation is conducted with becoming dignity of stile and purity of expression; points not easily attended to, where so rough a foundation is laid for quarrel, as that turbulent passion Jealousy.

521 chide]
Schmidt (1874, 2): To scold, to quarrel (OED 1b). OED 1a: To contend with loud and angry altercation; to brawl, wrangle. Obs. Cf. 1068, 1227, 1245, 1347, 1636.

522–3 Well…iniury]
Nelson (1988, p. 92): Oberon’s expressed motive for casting a spell on Titania…is not the restoring of order but revenge. But see n. 624–5.

522 goe thy way]
Depart (OED, Go, v. 21b; Way, n.1 7b). Blake observes that the phrase often expresses annoyance (8.3.4). The Clarkes (1879, p. 402): Go along with thee, pursue thine own way. Thirlby (MS 1733–47) compares H8 2.4.134 (1496), Goe thy wayes Kate. It is a common biblical phrase: see Mark 10:52, Luke 17:19, etc. See 1475.

522 Thou…groue]
For omission of go see Schmidt (1875, Shall 8), quoting this instance, and OED, Shall v. 24. For further discussion of ellipses of the verb after such auxiliaries as will, is, shall, and must when followed by a preposition of motion see Abbott §405; Franz §621, quoting this instance; Blake 6.3.2.3. Other examples are at 736, 1416, 1481, 1533, 1630, 2187.

523 iniury]
OED (1; 2): 1. Wrongful action or treatment; violation or infringement of another’s rights; suffering or mischief wilfully and unjustly inflicted.…2.…Insult. Deighton (ed. 1891): Slight, contemptuous treatment, comparing 3H6 4.1.107 (2137). See 1173.

524–45 My…idlenesse]
Rowe (ed. 1709, 1:viii): Queen Elizabeth had several of his Plays Acted before her, and without doubt gave him many gracious Marks of her Favour: It is that Maiden Princess plainly, whom he intends by [quotes 535]. And that whole Passage is a Compliment very properly brought in, and very handsomly apply’d to her. Steevens (ed. 1778): It was no uncommon thing to introduce a compliment to queen Elizabeth in the body of a play. So, again in Tancred and Sigismunda [corrected in Errata to Gismunda], 1592 [by Robert Wilmot, 1591–2]: There lives a virgin, one without compare, Who of all graces hath her heavenly share; In whose renown, and for whose happy days, Let us record this Pæan of her praise. Cantant. Halliwell (ed. 1856) adds a further example from the anonymous (sometimes attributed to Kyd) Soliman and Perseda (1590, 5.5.36–48; ed. J. Murray, 1991). Bond (1902, 3:14, 98–9), following Halpin (1843, see ), believes the allegory…is largely suggested by Lyly’s Endymion. Herpich (1905, pp. 425–6) parallels Richard Barnfield’s poem Cynthia ll. 136–44, where there is a similar compliment to the maiden Queene who reigns In Westerne world (see Complete Poems, ed. George Klawitter, 1990, p. 121).
That the speech refers to Elizabeth continues to be generally accepted, but as Furness (ed. 1895) observed: Let an allegory be once scented and the divagations are endless. From Warburton (ed. 1747) to the present, theories of the identity and relationships of the mermaid, dolphin, stars, little white flower, and other parts of the description as well as the fair vestal have exercised the ingenuity of those seeking topical allegory, whether for its own sake, or in support of other interpretations of the occasion or meaning of the play as a whole. See further .
Attention has also been given to the speech’s poetic impact and dramatic effectiveness, and to its kinship with entertainments and pageants honoring the queen.
Warburton (ed. 1747) ends his pursuit of topical parallels with admiration for the poetic achievement: It is the noblest and justest allegory that was ever written. The laying it in fairy-land, and out of nature, is in the character of the speaker. And on these occasions Shakespear always excels himself. He is born away by the magic of his enthusiasm, and hurries his reader along with him into these ancient regions of poetry, by that power of Verse, which we may well fancy to be like what,—Olim Fauni Vatesque canebant [the Fauns and bards once sang, Ennius, quoted in Cicero, Orator 51.171]. Grinfield (MS 1834–63, p. 269) approves this elegant fancy. Maginn (1837, p. 376), praising the brilliantly bejewelled language of the fairy scenes: The compliment to Queen Elizabeth…was of necessity sugared with all the sweets that the bon-bon box of the poet could supply. Hunter (1845, 1:292–3): The allegory is written expressly to give a dignity to the flower: it is the splendour of preparation intended to fix attention on the flower, whose peculiar virtues were to be the means of effecting some of the most important purposes of the drama. The passage resembles, in this respect, one a little before, in which there is an interest given to the little henchman, by the recital of the gambols of Titania with his mother on the sea-shore of India, and the interest thrown around Othello’s handkerchief. Minto (1874, pp. 378–9): Oberon’s account of the effects of the mermaid’s song…dwindles all other hyperboles into meanness. Armstrong (1913, p. 33) likes the graceful and courtier-like, and not altogether unbusiness-like—Shakespeare sometimes had an eye to the main chance—references to the Virgin Queen…One hopes [she] appreciated the exquisite compliment of her poet player, laden with the music of real poetry and idyllic chivalry. Fripp (1938, p. 528), believing the passage to be among later revisions, considers it a purple patch,…superb poetry, beyond the capacity of even Shakespeare in 1594. Van Doren (1939, pp. 77–8) admires the specific geography.…The world is as big and as real as any world we know. The promontory long ago; the rude sea that grew—not smooth, not gentle, not anything pretty or poetical, but (the prosaic word is one of Shakespeare’s best) civil; the mermaid that is also a sea-maid; the direction west; and the cold watery moon that rides so high above the earth—these are the signs of its bigness, and they are so clear that we shall respect the prowess implied in Robin’s speed. Young (1966, pp. 76–80) writes that Oberon provides (p. 77) a vantage point for his description of a panoramic landscape (p. 76) with a remarkable sense of spaciousness and distance, which comes (p. 78) finally to rest on something small and familiar, the pansy; the perspective created gives a (p. 80) sense of surveying all of nature in order to discover man’s unique position in it. Citing Young, Yoch (1985, p. 204) adds: Sh.’s personal focus makes scenery expand rather than contain character, which ultimately brings spacious, mythological, and heroic beginnings to end in the readily recognizable, homespun, and vernacular scope of a single, cared-for detail.
Comment on analogous entertainments often focusses on the events at Kenilworth in 1575 and Elvetham in 1591, and is sometimes linked to special pleading for theories of topical allegory, or biographical connections. Other commentators argue for different events, or simply for general similarities.
Whiter (1794, pp. 186–8): When I have considered the singular imagery in this celebrated passage, and the abrupt mode, in which it is apparently introduced, I was ever accustomed to regard it as one of those wild excursions of the fancy, which seem totally independent of any kindred notions to supply or suggest the materials for its existence. I have since, however, discovered that all this is very naturally derived from the Masque and the Pageant, which abounded in the age of Shakespeare; and which…would often quicken and enrich the fancy of the Poet with wild and original combinations. The frolic beings, which were now exercising the invention of our bard—these creatures of a distemper’d Dream in a Midsummer Night; and the preposterous devices in the Interlude of Pyramus (where the absurdities of scenic artifice are burlesqued) would naturally impress upon his mind the various and extraordinary objects of the masque or the pageant; quoting the Goldingham anecdote (see n. 854–6), he speculates that Sh. had a particular entertainment in mind. Boaden (1837, p. 14), arguing that the eleven-year-old Sh. attended the Kenilworth festivities, fourteen miles from Stratford: He does not write as if the tracts of Gascoigne and Laneham lay upon his table. His description is exactly such as, after seventeen years had elapsed, a reminiscence would suggest to a mind highly poetical. Cunliffe (1909, p. 231) finds this claim fanciful. Halpin (1843, p. 14) argues that the topical allegory is essential to the meaning of the incident, which thus retrieves an importance suitable to the dignity of its imperial subject, worthy of the portents which mark the time and place of its occurrence, and is rescued from the derogatory position it has hitherto held in an undiscerning criticism,…of an episode, beautiful and ornamental indeed, but altogether superfluous and impertinent. Chambers (Occasion, 1916, pp. 156–7), claiming that earlier attempts at topical allegory may be summarily disregarded: Whatever else complimentary poetry is, it must be in the first place gratifying to the person complimented, and in the second place reasonably topical.…What Shakespeare was describing was, as it professed to be, a water-pageant with fireworks. But again, it is only a want of historical perspective or a sentimental desire to find a reminiscence of Shakespeare’s childhood in his plays, which can explain the common identification of this water-pageant with that given at Kenilworth as far back as 1575.…But there were many such entertainments both before and after, and if Shakespeare had any particular one in mind, it is far more likely to have been that which occurred comparatively recently, when Elizabeth visited the Earl of Hertford at Elvetham in September, 1591.…So far as subject-matter goes, Elvetham might, just as well as Kenilworth, have furnished the motive for the extremely sketchy reminiscences of Oberon. It may be added that at Elvetham the queen of the fairies, not for the first time in the history of Elizabethan pageantry, had made her appearance. She is called Aureola, not Titania, but names the king as Auberon. It goes without saying that Cupid all armed is not mentioned in either account. He could only be seen by Oberon. But it is to Cupid and the wound inflicted by his bolt on the little western flower that the whole description leads up. The flower has a part in the action of the play, and possibly we ought not to seek for any further motive for its introduction. Cowling (ed. 1922): The purpose of the passage is to emphasize the importance of Love-in-idleness, by associating it with mythology. Welsford (1927, pp. 341–2): The vision is divided into two parts. First of all, there is the description of what both Oberon and Puck saw, and that is simply an idealised account of a typical Court entertainment, possibly reminiscent of the shows at Kenilworth or Eltham. Then there is the account of what Oberon alone could see, and that is the allegorical meaning of the performance, a meaning which is expressed in clear-cut pictorial imagery, which has a complimentary reference to Queen Elizabeth, and is possibly connected with a definite piece of Court intrigue. Oberon’s vision is no mere isolated compliment, but an ingenious device for turning the main theme of the play into a piece of subtle flattery, and connecting the potency of Cupid’s flower as well as of Dian’s bud with the charm and chastity of the Virgin Queen. Barber (1959, p. 122), admitting that no connection with a particular entertainment or wedding has been demonstrated: What matters…is the kind of occasion Oberon’s speech refers to, the kind of occasion Shakespeare’s scene is shaped by. The speech describes, in retrospect, just such a joyous overflow of pleasure into music and make-believe as is happening in Shakespeare’s own play. The fact that what Shakespeare handled with supreme skill was just what was most commonplace no doubt contributes to our inability to connect what he produced with particular historical circumstances. Cf. among many according with Barber’s general point are Warren (1971, pp. 137–9); Brooks (ed. 1979, p. lxviii); Williams (1995, pp. 59–60). Arguing that Sh. attended Kenilworth, Hunt (1997–8, pp. 163–4): The reconstruction of the mermaid-passage out of details of the Kenilworth pageantry suggests a Coleridge-like imaginative recollection on Shakespeare’s part, rather than a reaction to texts read such as Laneham’s or Gascoigne’s.…[I]t is the near-divine harmony of the mermaid’s song that gives Oberon a supernatural vision of Love—armed Cupid—and of the means to control erotic desire.…Shakespeare’s Warwickshire of the mind in A Midsummer Night’s Dream is about the knowledge of how to control falling in and out of romantic love,…The lyrical power and dramatic importance of the recollection of a mermaid singing upon a dolphin’s back…derive from memory’s distortion of far-off events.
