Conlan (2004, pp. 125–6): Oberon’s vision encourages us to rethink how court comedies by Lyly and Shakespeare praised the English Queen.…[They] did not require their audiences to seek out secret correspondences or engage in abstract exegesis: but rather, in contrast to masques, Elizabethan court comedies staged fables about places geographically and temporally distant from England such that love-wounded characters from these other courts who resembled Elizabeth in position, power, and other qualities might serve as foils to her more perfect self seated upon the stage. Hardy (2006, pp. 40–2), embracing a bleak view of MND: Oberon’s long speech to Puck is also placed apart from action, but not detached like Mercutio’s narration [Rom. 1.4.53–95 (510–44)]. A fairy speaks to a fairy, all fantasy, in a set speech, formally asserted and separate, but part of the play’s motivating drive, planning a comic, unromantic, and cruel punishment.…Its subject, love-in-idleness, quietly begs the question of what love is.…Oberon’s story has a grand sweep, and large scale, in allusion and global skyscape, as well as a local habitat and flower-name. It plays an important part in enlarging and darkening the play’s brutal erotic fantasy—sometimes softened and sweetened to favour and to prettiness—and so well brought out, differently but always with grotesque sexual humour and hard elegance, by Benjamin Britten’s opera and Peter Brook’s famous production. For further comment on associations with Elizabeth and with mythology, see nn. 532–49, 532–41, 535.

524 gentle]
Apparently an odd epithet for the shrewde and knauish sprite (403), but see OED (3b): Used in polite or ingratiating address, or as a complimentary epithet. Cf. e.g. 171, 708, 1272, 1822.

525–30 Since…musicke]
Miriam Joseph (1947, p. 130) cites this passage and 630–7 as Sh.’s nearest approach to topothesia…the description of imaginary places. Gui (1952, pp. 258–9), quoting these lines, imposes a psychiatric interpretation unlikely to persuade the general reader: The situation here of a baby (Oberon) seated upon the promontory of the mother’s lap and listening to a seductive lullaby that intoxicates the infant with sexual stimulation is parallel with that of Bottom. See 1535–6. He goes on to describe Cupid as the stimulated infant flying with an erection, and notes that here as in Bottom’s case the frustrating agent is the moon. Brooks (ed. 1979, pp. lxiii, 142–3) finds a striking parallel with Seneca’s seascape with Cupid all armed (Hippolytus), supporting the claim with selective quotations from that play’s first act, but Holland (ed. 1994) is unconvinced. See also n. 538, fiery shaft.

525 Since]
Abbott §132, quoting this instance: Used conjunctively for when, long ago (OED, conj. 3b, quoting this instance first; Franz §559; Blake 5.3.2.4 [d]). Cf. n. 1307.

525 promontory]
Halpin (1843, p. 91), arguing for a reference to the entertainment at Kenilworth, notes: Probably the brayz mentioned by Laneham as linking a fair park with the castle on the south, and adjacent to the goodly pool of rare beauty, breadth, length, and depth. See Laneham, A Letter (1575), A3r-v. OED (Braye): A military outwork; a mound or bank defended by palisades and watchtowers.

526–30 a Mearemaide…musicke]
Apart from speculation about an historical parallel to the mermaid (see ), comment is directed partly to the visual image and partly to the power of the song. Thirlby (MS 1747–53) is the first of many to cite the fable of Arion and the dolphin, quoting TN 1.2.15 (65). Whiter (1794, p. 188): So favourite a representation does the riding on a dolphin appear to have been in the time of our Poet, that it was sometimes introduced among the quaint devices of the art of cookery [quotes Jonson’s Masque of Neptune’s Triumph, ll. 185–91; ed. Herford & Simpson 7:687]. Root (1903, p. 109): Thetis rides on a dolphin’s back in Met. 11[.236–7], Golding [11.270–1]. Baldwin (1944, 1:635) believes the fable was early impressed on Sh. by schoolbooks such as Camerarius’s edition of Aesop (1573). Bate (1993, p. 138): The mermaid on the dolphin’s back is sitting in for Arion, who in the Fasti (ii.79–118) played on his lyre, charming the waves and paying his fare in song to a dolphin who carried him to safety. See also Propertius, Elegies 2.26.17–18; Ovid, Art of Love 3.325–6.
Warburton (ed. 1747) notes that mermaids are equated with Sirens, and quotes Err. 3.2.45–6 (832–3) for the seductiveness of the mermaid’s song. Thirlby (MS 1747–53) adds a parallel in Luc. 1411. Wright (ed. 1877) quotes 3H6 3.2.186 (1710). Cook (1889, 356–7) compares Ariosto, Orlando Furioso 6.40.1–2: una Sirena Che col suo dolce canto accheta il mare [tr. Harington (1591): a Mermaid…That can with song the raging stormes appease.]. Brooks (ed. 1979) instances Seneca’s Medea, 769, where the enchantress has made the constellation Hyades fall by her incantations, and Marlowe’s Hero and Leander, like Sea-nimphs inveigling harmony (l. 105; 2:434). Cf. also Vergil, Eclogues, 8.69, carmina vel caelo possunt deducere lunam, [Songs can even draw the moon down from heaven].
Warburton’s claim that mermaids were noted for intemperate lust is given support by Batman vppon Bartholome (1582, bk.18, 97): The Mermaiden is called Sirena,…With sweetnesse of song this beast maketh shipmen to sleepe, and when she seeth that they be a sleepe, she goeth into the ship, and rauisheth which she may take with her, and bringeth him into a drye place, and maketh him fryst lye by her, and doe the deede of lechery, & if he will not or may not, then she slaieth him and eateth his body. Cf. Seager (1896; 1971, p. 289). Webb (1996, p. 56): Both mermaid…and dolphin have strong erotic associations. But Edgecombe (1999–2000, p. 182): The mermaid figures as a being as virtuous as she is glamorous, and…readings to the contrary misconstrue details of the presentation; he argues (p. 181) that the sea creature intended is a Nereid, to be identified with Elizabeth.

527 Vttering]
Schmidt (1875, 1), quoting also 1787: To emit, to send forth (OED, Utter, v.1 5a, quoting the instance at 1787). Thirlby (MS 1725–33) also quotes 1786–7 at this point, drawing attention to the amusing connection between mermaid and clowns.

527 dulcet]
Johnson (1755, 2), quoting this instance: Sweet to the ear; harmonious; melodious (OED 2).

527 breath]
Schmidt (1874, 6), quoting this instance and TN 2.3.21 (720–1): Used of singing. OED (9a): Whisper, utterance…speech. Cf. 1067, 1193, and, especially, 1788.

528 rude]
Johnson (1755, 2; 1): 2. Violent; tumultuous; boisterous; turbulent; playing on sense 1: Coarse of manners; uncivil, as in 1292 (OED, a. 6, quoting 2H4 3.1.20 [1441], rude imperious surge; and 4b, quoting 1292 first).

528 ciuill]
Johnson (1755, 10), quoting this instance: Complaisant; civilised; gentle; well bred; elegant of manners; not rude; not brutal; not coarse.

529 And…Spheares]
Thirlby (MS 1733–47 at 526) compares Jn. 5.7.74–5 (2684–5) and in MS 1747–53 at 526 Luc. 1523–6. Steevens (ed. 1793) quotes Tro. 2.2.46 (1029), a starre disorbd, as one of our author’s favourite images and adds Ant. 3.13.145–7 (2325–7). Halliwell (ed. 1856) compares Ham. 1.5.17 (702). Spurgeon (1935, p. 22): The conception of the stars moving in their spheres, and straying from them only as a sign or result of great disturbance or disaster…, seems to be the most constant of astronomical ideas in Shakespeare’s mind. Boaden (1837, p. 14) sees an allusion to the fireworks upon the water at Kenilworth, quoting both Gascoigne’s Princely Pleasures (in Workes, 1587, A3v) and part of Laneham’s description of the blaz of burning darts, flying to & fro, leams of starz coruscant, streamz & hayl of fiery sparks, lyghtenings of wyldefier a water & lond (A Letter, 1575, C1v). Brooks (ed. 1979) notes that at the 1591 Elvetham entertainment (which also featured fireworks) the Fairy Queen tells Elizabeth that amorous starres fall nightly in my lap (Lyly, 1:450); also (p. lxvii): Nashe, in Pierce Penilesse (1592), drawing, as I take it Shakespeare is, a contrast with the chaste virtue of the Virgin Queen, declares The Court I dare not touch, but surely there (as in the Heavens) be many falling starres, and but one true Diana. Perhaps the falling stars in Shakespeare and in Nashe are the same: the siren mermaid may personify the temptation of sexual desire. If individuals are concerned, as certain stars suggests, maybe they should be looked for among those who pursued amorous affairs at court.

529 certaine starres]
Commentators assign two meanings to the adjective. Delius (ed. 1859): Certain is an indefinite number = some stars, instancing Tmp. 5.1.55 (2006), certaine fadomes (OED, a. 7a: = some particular, some definite) (Ger.). Schmidt (1874, 3), comparing Luc. 1525: Fixed (OED, a. 1a). Furness (ed. 1895): finds an added beauty in taking certain in the meaning of sure, fixed; does it not heighten the power of the mermaid’s song, that it could bring down the very stars, fixed in the sky. [Quotes Luc. 1525.]…That this interpretation is hostile to the theory that the certain stars were the Duke of Norfolk and the Earls of Northumberland and Westmoreland [see ], is possibly an additional reason why it should be preferred. Some find the adjective unsatisfactory: Poole (1657, p. 518, s.v. Syrens) substitutes ravisht and Settle (ed. 1692) Twinkling. See also n. 101.

529 Spheares]
OED (Sphere, n. 4a), quoting this instance: With possessive pron. or genitive: The particular sphere (in sense 2 [as in 378]) appropriate to, or occupied by, each of the planets (or the fixed stars). See n. 378.

530 To heare]
I.e., at hearing or on hearing. See n. 265.

530 Sea-maids]
Johnson (1755), quoting this instance: Mermaid (OED, quoting this instance first).

531 Puck]
Kennedy (1998, pp. 187–190) argues that Sh. wrote Robin as his SP throughout and…the use of [Puck] was caused by type shortage. See .

532–49 That…sees]
Dodd (1752, 1:82): The metamorphosis is extremely fine, and most beautifully imagined. Tollet (MS 1740–79): See Shakespeare’s sonnet calld Cupid’s treachery [Son. 153]. Green (1870, pp. 400–5) notes that the illustration for Whitney’s De morte, & amore [Emblemes (1586), 1866, p. 132] shows Death and Cupid flying in mid-air, and discharging their arrows from the clouds as Cupid does in MND. Chambers (ed. 1897): Shakespeare’s conceit is founded upon stories in Ovid’s Metamorphoses, in which flowers are created or are changed in color by the blood of some hero or heroine. Such is the staining of the mulberry by the blood of Pyramus. Kermode (1961, pp. 216–17): Puck is, indeed, a blinding Cupid; and the passage in which he is, as it were, cupidinized is so famous for other reasons that its central significance is overlooked: [quotes 532–49]. The juice used by Puck to bring confusion to the darkling lovers is possessed of all the force of Cupid’s arrow, and is applied with equal randomness. The eye so touched will dote; in it will be engendered a fancy for the next live thing it sees. Puck takes over the role of blind Cupid. The love he causes is a madness; the flower from which he gets his juice is called Love-in-idleness, and that word has the force of wanton behaviour amounting almost to madness. Staton (1963, pp. 172–3), quoting Chambers: The theme here is not so much in the Ovidian as in the related Anacreontic vein. Precious little poems recounting new mythlets about Cupid in the manner of Anacreon’s Alexandrian imitators were very popular in the Renaissance; he quotes Passion LIII from Thomas Watson’s Hecatompathia (1582) [Complete Works, 1996, 1:195], and the headnote where Watson states that the two first parts of this Sonnet, are an imitation of…Theocritus (Idyl XIX). Purdon (1974, p. 189) argues that the speech is part of a pattern of mythological reference, presenting two icons, one of the singing mermaid and one of the clash between Cupid and the virgin queen as part of the Diana-Cupid psychomachia: What…both icons represent is the restoration of equilibrium, one through the power of harmony, and one through the counterbalance of strong opposite forces. Kott (1981, p. 132) praises Sh.’s wonderful syncretism in combining mythological icons with the folk love potion, and his use of the Neoplatonic transformation of Venus into virginal Diana. Barkan (1986, p. 257): Sh.’s myth corresponds to no particular classical example; rather it produces an original etiology in the Ovidian mode. Oberon creates a picture of sublime cosmic harmony with the familiar Ovidian layers of land, sea, and sky. Within that order Cupid is a disquieting element, and yet his irresistible force is balanced by the immovable object of the cold moon. The drama of the imperial votress does more than compliment Queen Elizabeth. It sets her in the great chain of powers that rule over human beings.…It is a myth of transformations via love.…Like many Renaissance Ovidians, Shakespeare is more interested in transformation as a cause than in transformation as an effect. So he transfers the metamorphosis from the end of the story to the beginning: instead of a memorial via the oozing blood of the dead lovers, he offers a cause for the passionate blood of the living lovers. The now-purple flower is itself an emblem of metamorphosis by love and, more important, it becomes the inspiration for the metamorphoses of passion. Garrison (1994, p. 146): Shakespeare’s passage…resonates…with suggestions of passion, and love’s wound—like Thisbe’s veil and the changed mulberry fruit—suggests the loss of virginity. Shakespeare has taken the original simile out of its Pyramus and Thisbe context and developed it for a different purpose within a different story. The burst pipe has become Cupid’s love shaft; the mulberry tree has become the love-in-idleness flower. Bate (1993, pp. 139–40): The image [of the mermaid] is…an overgoing of Ovid in its conjunction of the sea and the stars. Ovid often metamorphoses his characters into creatures of the sea or astral formations, but never both simultaneously; the mermaid’s music is an agent bringing transformation to diverse elements; quoting 532–49, he comments on the apparent paradox that the vestal’s chastity causes others to madly dote, attributing this effect to the normal upside-down operation of the etiology of the invented myth: Ordinary people do seem to spend much of their time madly doting; his task as an etiologist is to invent a story to explain the cause of this phenomenon.

532–41 That…free]
Ingersoll (1890, p. 197): Some have insisted that the paragraph ending with [540–1] referred to Queen Elizabeth; but it is impossible for me to believe that the daubed and wrinkled face, the small black eyes, the cruel nose, the thin lips, the bad teeth, and the red wig of Queen Elizabeth could by any possibility have inspired these marvelous lines. Yates (1947, p. 73) quotes this passage and points to another example of the image of the Vestall in the sieve portraits of the queen, which portray her as the Vestal Virgin Tuccia bearing her attribute, the sieve. Later (1975, p. 117–18) she expands on this theme and connects it with an entertainment in 1590 which included a temple of the Vestal Virgin. She also connects it with Petrarch’s Trionfi, seeing Sh.’s passage here as the Triumph of Cupid effaced by the Triumph of Chastity. Still later (1979, p. 149) she sums up: Shakespeare’s picture of Elizabeth as a Vestal Virgin, a chaste Moon who defeats the assaults of Cupid, an imperial votaress, is a brilliant summing up of the cult of Elizabeth as the representative of imperial reform. Bosch (1982, p. 111) compares Oberon’s praise of Elizabeth with Vergil’s praise of Augustus in Aeneid 6.789–97 and 847–53 (Catalan). Davies (1986, pp. 122–3): In 1595 Elizabeth I…was an elderly person of increasingly peremptory and capricious temperament.…Shakespeare emphasizes…young Cupid…and this…automatically places into relief the old age of the imperial votress.…[T]he moon…is as ancient as creation itself.…Yet the moon is as young as Cupid, or younger, when we remember that she is reborn from month to month.…Shakespeare’s image is a lyrical presentation of the possibility of eternal self-renewal, associated with an inviolable quality which is not—despite the fact that Oberon wishes to think of her as a cold moon—frigid or sterile.…Her maiden meditation, fancy-free speaks of a liberty to conceive thoughts: her course of contemplation is an image of human inwardness, the thinking mind, resembling the creative imagination. Bate (1993, p. 141): By identifying the Queen with the imperial votaress, Shakespeare denies the transgressive identification of her with Titania. Montrose (1996, p. 175): Ironically, the vestal’s very freedom from fancy guarantees the subjection of others. She is necessarily excluded from the erotic world of which her own chastity is the efficient cause. Hackett (2003, p. 342): In Shakespeare’s treatment of the queen as lunar deity, not far below ostensible celebration is considerable ambivalence about the inviolability and unbodiliness of the watry moon. As she glides ethereally across and out of the scene, her chastity seems ghostly, deathly, and sterile; after all, Theseus has already threatened Hermia with living [quotes 81–2, 85–7]. He has presented this as a fate equivalent to death [74]. As a whole, this is a play which emphatically celebrates youth, desire, and marriage. For a description of another devotee of chastity free from the assault of Cupids arrow see Rom. 1.1.208–11 (216–19). See n. 1017–19.

532 That]
= At that. For omission of a preposition in adverbial expressions of time see Abbott §202; Franz §542; Blake 6.3.3.

532 very]
Johnson (1755, 4): Same (OED 10a).

532 I saw]
Thiselton (1903, p. 30) Q2’s I say [see textual n.] is defensible as an archaism, and was possibly the original reading. In OED (Say) say is given as the obs. pa. tense…of see v., and say as an obsolete variant form of saw (See, v. A. 3a). Nonetheless, this is highly unlikely; Q2’s word is followed by the folios only.

532 thou…not]
Boas (1896, p. 187): With his zest for knavish pranks, for mocking practical jokes upon gossips and wisest aunts, this merry wanderer of the night is indeed a spirit different in sort from the ethereal dream fairies, and it is natural that Oberon’s vision of Cupid all armed should be hid from his gross sight. Brooks (ed. 1979): The mythology to be created about Elizabeth is thus placed on a level [appropriately] more sublime and occult than that about the mermaid (Barber [1959], p. 148). Bate (1993, p. 136): One could almost say that in Oberon’s etiology of love-in-idleness Puck could not see Cupid because he was Cupid.
Cartwright (1862, p. 168) identifies Robert Greene as Oberon and Thomas Nashe as Puck; since Greene was five or six years old in 1558 he saw the first year of Elizabeth’s reign, but Nashe-Puck could not because he was born in 1567.

533 colde Moone]
Chambers (ed. 1897): The moon is cold, physically, because her rays do not burn like the sun’s, and spiritually, as the patroness of chastity. See also 82 and nn.

534 Cupid, all arm’d]
Warburton (in Theobald ed. 1733): Surely, this presents us with a very unclassical Image. Where do we read or see, in ancient Books, or Monuments, Cupid arm’d more than with his Bows and Arrows? and with these we for ever see him arm’d. And these are all the Arms he had Occasion for in this present Action; a more illustrious One, than any, his Friends, the Classicks, ever brought him upon.—The Change I make [alarm’d: see textual n.] is so small, but the Beauty of the Thought so great, which this Alteration carries with it, that, I think, we are not to hesitate upon it. For what an Addition is this to the Compliment made upon this Virgin Queen’s Celibacy, that it alarm’d the Power of Love? as if his Empire was in Danger, when this Imperial Votress had declar’d herself for a single Life: so powerful would her great Example be in the World.—Queen Elizabeth could not but be pleased with our Author’s Address upon this Head. Warburton’s emendation was not welcomed. Thirlby (MS 1733–47) asks, How could Oberon see that Cupid was alarmd suggesting that perhaps he meant [arm’d] only with his usual weapons, and in MS 1747–53 compares Luc. 1544. Grey (1754, 1:52–3) argues that all arm’d means nothing more than being arm’d with bow and quiver, the proper, and classical arms of Cupid,…. The image then is not unclassical, and our author seems to have copied it from Tasso [Gerusalemme liberata] Canto 1, 47 [ll. 7–8, where Love is described as born already winged and armato; also quoted by Fraunce, The Arcadian Rhetorike (1588), E5v]. Cf. Apuleius’s winged Cupide…armed with fire & arrowes (see ). Heath (1765, p. 49): [The phrase] doth not mean…armed cap a pied, in a suit of compleat armour,…but ready armed, with his bow and arrow in his hand, prepared for immediate execution. Johnson (ed. 1765): All-armed, does not signify dressed in panoply, but only enforces the word armed, as we might say all-booted [OED, All adv. E. 8]. I am afraid that the general sense of alarmed, by which it is used for put into fear or care by whatever cause, is later than our Authour. OED supports this: the first uses of alarm (v. 3; 5) and alarmed (ppl. a. 2) in this sense are in the early 1650’s. Steevens (ed. 1778) compares Greene’s Never Too Late, 1616 [8:92]: Or where proud Cupid sate all armde with fire. Hailes (1786, p. 361): Dr Warburton is mistaken in saying, that the reading of the text presents an unclassical image. In the Medicean collection is a gem representing Cupid winged, putting on complete armour. See also n. 126.

534 all arm’d]
All used as an intensifier meaning wholly, completely is common in Sh. See Johnson (1755), All, adv. 1; OED, All adv. 2. For more Shn. uses see Abbott §28; Franz §371; Blake 5.1.3.1 (ii).

534 certaine]
OED (a. 2a): Sure, unerring. The Latin adjective certa is used of Cupid’s hand and dart in Seneca, Hercules Oetaeus 539–40, and 544. Cf. nn. 101, 529.

535 faire]
Instead of accepting F1’s unauthorial insertion of the before west (see textual n.), Kennedy (2008, p. 176) suggests emending faire to fairie, to help the metre, and to enhance the compliment to the Fairy Queen Elizabeth.

535 Vestall]
Schmidt (1875): A chaste woman in general, quoting this instance, identifying the vestal as Queen Elizabeth (OED, n. 2, quoting this instance first).

535 throned by west]
Thirlby (MS 1733–47) compares Jn. 2.1.29 (322), that vtmost corner of the West speaking of England. He also quotes the last line of the final verse of Jonson’s A Masque of her Maiesties. Love Freed from Ignorance and Folly, Bowing to the sun, throned in the west [378 (7:371)] i.e. king James. He adds, Note that the scene is near Athens, meaning that England would be west. Venezky (1951, p. 141): The phrase reflects the Queen’s customary station at these outdoor shows, where it was necessary to keep the sun out of the royal eyes; anticipated by Calthorpe (1942, p. 171), she notes the prominence of the word West in relation to the Queen’s position in the illustration to the twice-issued quarto which reported the Elvetham entertainment. (See Plate VIII. [facing p. 160]). (See also www.mediastorehouse.com/pictures_624593/elizabeth-at-elvetham.html). Bate (1993, pp. 139–40): Elizabethan England was claimed as a descendant from Troy and Rome by virtue of the westward shift of empire. See also n. 543 (westerne).

535 by]
For the original meaning near see Abbott §145, quoting this instance (OED, prep. 9a). On the omission of the definite article see Blake 3.3.4.2.2 (h), and Kennedy (2008, p. 176).

536–44 And…wound]
For possible sexual double meanings which have a bearing on comments such as those of Hardy (n. 524–45), Gui (n. 525–30), Garrison (n. 532–49), Liston (n. 538–41), see Williams (1994), arrow, bolt, bow, shaft. On bow, see also n. 12.

536 loos’d]
Dyce (1853, pp. 59–60): Properly the act of discharging an arrow; the archers terme, who is not said to finish the feate of his shot before he giue the loose, and deliuer his arrow from his bow. Puttenham’s Arte of English Poesie, 1589, p. 145 [ed. 1936, p. 174].…Descocher une fleiche. To shoote, loosse, or send an arrow from a bow. Cotgrave’s Dict. [1611] (OED, Loose, v. 4a). Wright (ed. 1877) compares H5 1.2.207 (354).

536 loue-shaft]
Schmidt (1874): Cupid’s arrow (OED, Love, n.1 15a, quoting this instance only).

536 smartly]
Johnson (1755): Sharply; briskly; vigorously (OED, adv. 1).

537 As it should]
Clemen (ed. 1963): As if it would. Johnson (1755, 8): As if (OED, adv. 9a). Abbott §107: The if is implied in the subjunctive. For other examples see Franz §644 (b) Anm. 3; Blake 5.3.2.2 (e); and 1288. F4 and Rowe (ed. 1709) alter should to would, the more modern usage. See Franz §616 (b) Anm. 1, quoting this instance.

538–49 But…sees]
Bloom (1998, p. 159): The arrow of Cupid, unable to wound the Virgin Queen, instead converts the pansy into a universal love charm. It is as though Elizabeth’s choice of chastity opens up a cosmos of erotic possibilities for others, but at the high cost of accident and arbitrariness replacing her reasoned choice. Love at first sight, exalted in Romeo and Juliet, is pictured here as calamity.

538–41 But…free]
Steevens (ed. 1793): In Queen Elizabeth’s Entertainment in Suffolke and Norfolke [1578] written by Churchyard, Chastity deprives Cupid of his Bow, and presents it to her Majesty: —and bycause that the Queene had chosen the best life, she gave the Queene Cupid’s Bow, to learne to shoote at whome she pleased: since none coulde wounde her highnesse hart, it was meete (said Chastitie) that she should do with Cupid’s Bowe and arrowes what she pleased. See Nichols (1823, 2:189). Venezky (1951, p. 194) notes that the sentiment was prominent in a number of later masques, and also refers to Chastity’s claim that her breast is armd with thoughtes of vertues rare Who to the fight doth bring no glittering shield But cleane conceytes, which pure and blessed are (1578, D2r; Nichols, 1823, 2:191). Chambers (Court, 1916, 1:82): After Alençon left England in 1582, Elizabeth permanently adopted the pose of a settled and obdurate virginity [quotes 540–41]. Brooks (ed. 1979): For thoughts free of concern with love, in a devotee of the moon-goddess, cf. Golding, 1.568 ff. [quotes 1.575, 579–80; see ]. Cf. also Emelye’s prayer to Diana [Chaucer, KnT 2304–11; see ]. Liston (1991, p. 157): Why should the chaste beams quench the shaft? (The image here, like many others in the play, is paradoxically sexual—the quenching of the love-shaft in something moist, and therefore endowed with fecundity.) The only answer, I think, is that the fair vestal had chosen her state freely, and the shaft aimed at her would have violated her own elected chastity—more specifically, virginity.
In Son. 154 The fayrest votary disarm’d Cupid and quenched his heart inflaming brand (5, 8, 9, 2).

538 might]
Abbott §312, quoting this instance: Used in the sense of was able or could. OED (May, v.1 3b): Nearly equivalent to did. See n. 171.

538 fiery shaft]
Cf. Chaucer, KnT 1564–5 Love hath his firy dart so brennyngly Ystiked thurgh my…herte; Seneca, Hippolytus (Tenne Tragedies, p. 146): Flamy Cupid armd with scorching gleed, And shaftes.

539 watry Moone]
OED (Watery, a. 6c): An epithet of heavenly bodies…thought to bring rain. Verity (ed. 1893): Either because she controls the tides…or (as I think) in allusion to the Elizabethan view that she was what Milton [PL 5.422] calls a moist continent, drawing up moisture from this earth. For other lunar connections see nn. 223 (watry), 1017–19, 1017–18.

540 imperiall]
Johnson (1755, 1), quoting this instance: Royal; possessing royalty (OED, a. 4, quoting this instance). Wells (ed. 1967): Imperious (OED, Imperial 5b) but this carries the unwelcome sense of domineering. Page (ed. 1897) compares the allusion to Elizabeth in H5 5.Chor.30 (2880), our gracious Empresse.

540 Votresse passed]
Finding the scansion…uncertain, Wells (ed. 1967) suggests two alternatives: vót’ress pássèd and vótaréss páss’d.

540 Votresse]
See n. 499.

541 In…free]
White (1854, p. 215): The alliteration, it may be said, adds nothing to the thought. True; but it does add to its charm. Without it, the line would not be that which always flashes on the memory when we think of a maiden who has lived till now with the depths of her heart untroubled. Verity (ed. 1893): Now a familiar quotation, like [144].

541 fancy free]
Rann (ed. 1787): Unaffected by love(OED, Fancy, n. B. 2, quoting this instance first). Abbott §430 paraphrases free from fancy (love). See nn. 40, 127, 165. Fripp (1928, p. 593) compares Marlowe’s Dido who has many suitors but is free from all (Dido 3.1.153; 1:30). Sh. may have caught the phrase from Golding: if I may be bold to speak my fancy free (1.201, Lat. 1.175), though here fancy means opinion (OED, Fantasy, 3b).

542–5 Yet…idlenesse]
The contest between Titania and Oberon for the child is paralleled with the struggle between Juno and Jupiter for the infant Hercules (see n. 495). That legend continues with Jupiter trying to steal the infant from Juno’s breast and the spilling of her milk which created the Milky Way. Bell (1861, 2:207–9) observes that Natalis Comes adds to the story that some of the milk fell to the earth and changed lilies from yellow to white. Bell also compares Herrick’s poem How Lillies came white where Cupid effects a colour change with his mother’s milk (Poetical Works, ed. L. C. Martin 1963, p. 74).

542 markt]
See n. 503.

542 bolt]
Though usually used of the missive from a cross-bow, it could designate any arrow (OED, n.1 a). See also n. 536–44.

543–50 It…herbe]
Magic flowers abound in classical literature, especially in Ovid’s Metamorphoses. Minerva sprinkles Ariadne with juice of Hecate’s flower (Golding 6.174: all references in this n. are to this tr.) to change her to a spider (6.174–81). Medea prays to Hecate for magic herbs with which to restore Aeson’s youth (7.258–89). Circe uses the juice of certain herbs (14.318) to metamorphose Ulysses’ men, and later a better juice of uncouth herbs (14.346) to return them to their former shapes. Mercury gave Ulysses a white Fair flower called moly (14.337–8) to protect him.

543 westerne flower]
For the identification of the flower as representing various ladies in Elizabeth’s court, see Phillips (ed. 1969), Furness (ed. 1895), and . Watt (ed. 1906): The flower…exists on its own merits as an important stage property.

543 westerne]
OED (a. 3c), quoting this instance first: Of or belonging to the west; found in the west. Deighton (ed. 1891): English. See 1628. Cf. n. 535 (west).

544 Before…purple]
The earliest commentators do not mention what seems obvious, that Ovid’s tale of the changing of the mulberry tree’s flowers from white to purple, caused by Pyramus’s blood (Met. 4.125–7), is the main source of the transformation of the flower here. Capell (1783, 2.3:106) says it’s metamorphosis is fiction, and plainly from some in Ovid, but assumes his readers’ familiarity with the author. Hunter (1845, 1:293) first instances the change of the mulberry, in Ovid’s story of Pyramus. Lloyd (in Singer ed. 1856, 2:426) adds: The transformation of the pansy…has…a natural base in the double variety of the flower; on the varied colouring, see n. 545 (Loue in idlenesse). Halliwell (ed. 1856): Shakespeare was so minute an observer of nature, it is possible there is here an allusion to the changes which take place in the colours of plants arising from solar light and the character of the soil. Sometimes these changes form a natural portion of the progress of the flower. As a possible source for the colour change Furness (ed. 1895) quotes Lyte’s Niewe Herball (1578, p. 147) where there is described a third kind of violet bearing floures as white as snow, and then the fourth kind, whose floures be of a darke Crymsen, or old reddish purple colour. Harrison (1946, pp. 177–8) finds another possibility in the same author. At 1588 Oberon calls love-in-idleness Cupids flower, which is Lyte’s name for the rose, which changed from white to red according to two myths. The first is from the Venus-Adonis story when Venus’s feet were wounded by thorns and the Roses were bedewed, and…became al red (Lyte, 1578, p. 656). This tale has parallels in Bion, Lament for Adonis, ll. 64–6, and in Ovid, Met. 10.731–9. The second myth, a less likely source, is that Cupid at a heavenly banquet knocked over the pot of nectar, which fell on roses and they became red (Lyte, p. 656). Arnold (1955, p. 106) finds a parallel with the white flower turning purple in Michael Maier’s emblem book Atalanta Fugiens (1618), where, in Emblem 41 (which originates in Ovid, Met. 10.705–39) Adonis is killed by a boar and Venus runs up, and wounded in the foot, Colors the roses red that once were white (translated by Joscelyn Godwin, 1989, p. 187). Arnold gives Maier’s interpretation: It is…the blood of Christ, which, pouring from the feet of Venus in her gardens planted with rose bushes, turns to red the roses which formerly were white,’ myth of the pollution of the universe by Venus-Cupid, by carnal passion (Fr.). Reynolds & Sawyer (1959, p. 517), referring to Frazer’s Golden Bough (1913, 8:45–71), see a similarity between Oberon’s flower and St. John’s Wort which has a red juice and was gathered on midsummer eve for its magical properties. Montrose (1983, pp. 82, 92–3 n. 44) discusses defloration, menstruation and flowers, and vaginal blood caused by deflowering, expanding on the subject in 1986 and 1996. Cox (1991, p. 74): Sh. transplants Ovid’s mulberry, which had changed color as a result of the lovers’ being confounded, transforms it to the love-in-idleness [quoting 544], and makes it the cause for the lovers’ confusion in [MND]. See also n. 532–49.

544 milke white]
Johnson (1755, Milkwhite), quoting this instance: White as milk (OED, Milk-white, a. a.). Proverbial (Tilley M931, quoting the instance at 2125). Wilson (1962, p. 198), supporting Halpin’s identification of Lettice Knollys as the flower, argues that Lettice…might be taken as a quibble on lettuce, the English form of the Latin lactuca, the milky plant, so called from the colour of its juice. Cf. n. 542–5.

545 And…idlenesse]
Warburton (ed. 1747): This is as fine a metamorphosis as any in Ovid: With a much better moral, intimating that irregular love has only power when people are idle, or not well employed. Steevens (ed. 1773) disagrees, quoting Shr. 1.1.150–1 (453–4), 155–6 (458–9) where Sh. uses the same phrase in describing Lucentio being smitten with love for Bianca, which Steevens argues was surely a regular and honest passion; he adds: It is scarce necessary to mention that love in idleness is a flower, instancing John Taylor’s quibble on thyme/time, When passions are let loose without a bridle, Then precious time is turn’d to love and [misquoted in] idle (The praise and vertue of a Jayle, 1623, C3r). Hense (1872, p. 269) quotes Ovid, Remedia Amoris, 139, Otia si tollas, periere Cupidinis arcus (Take away idleness, you break Cupid’s bow). This famous maxim is found in many florilegia such as those of George Maior (1551, p. 73), Octavian Mirandula (1553, p. 55), and Joseph Langius (1613, pp. 95 and 933), and recurs frequently in medieval and Renaissance writing. Among other examples, Olson (1957, p. 113) quotes Euphues declaring idleness is the onely nourse and nourisher of sensual appetite, the sole maintenance of youthfull affection, the first shaft that Cupide shooteth into the hot liuer of a heedlesse louer (Lyly, 1:250). Golding uses the tag in his Verse Epistle 113–14, where he points the moral of the stories (including Pyramus and Thisbe) told by the daughters of Minyas in Met. 4; see . Cf. Tilley I9 and Dent L513.1.

545 Loue in idlenesse]
OED (n.1 16b), quoting this instance: Heartsease, Viola tricolor. Lyte (1578, p. 149) gives the English names for the Pansy as Pances, Loue in idlenes, and Hartes ease. Gerard (1597, pp. 704–5): Harts ease is named in Latin Viola tricolor or the three coloured violet,…in French Pensees;…in English Harts ease, Pansies, Liue-in-Idlenes, Cull [i.e. Cuddle] me to you, and three faces in a hood; among the vertues of the plant he mentions that distilled water of the herbe and flowers…doth woonderfully ease the paines of the French disease. Thirlby (MS 1725–33) alludes to Stephen Skinner’s Etymologicon Linguae Anglicanae…Etymologicon Botanicum seu Explication nominum omnium Vegetabilium (1671), sig. Iiii 2v: Liveing-idly, vel Live-in-idleness. Idem (MS 1733–47) quotes Cotgrave (1611, Clavelé): Herbe clavelée. Paunsie, hearbe Trinitie, Hearts-ease. Furness (ed. 1895), quoting Ellacombe (1878, p. 151), adds other familiarly descriptive names. Cowling (ed. 1922): Usually identified with the pansy or heartsease, but more probably the purple spotted orchid (Orchis maculata). Tollet (in Steevens ed. 1778): The flower or violet, commonly calld pansies, or heart’s-ease, is named love in idleness in Warwickshire. Sh. says it is, now purple with love’s wound, because one or two of its petals are of a purple colour. Dyer (1883; 1966, p. 215): The phrase literally signifies love in vain, or to no purpose, quoting Taylor’s couplet, as above. Wilson (ed. 1924, Glossary) defines it partly as love without serious intention. Rydén (1984, p. 160), glossing the name as Dyer and Wilson do: However, none of the herbalists of the day mention lust-inducing properties for Viola tricolor, which may be an invention of Shakespeare’s or a reflex of some unrecorded country folklore. Strong (1985, p. 58) points out that the pansy, or the love-in-idleness is among the spring flowers embroidered on the Queen’s bodice in the Rainbow Portrait. For the synonym Cupids flower see n. 1588.

547–9 The iewce…sees]
Neil (ed. 1878) mentions magic draughts used to solve love- difficulties in Montemayor’s Diana and other romances. See also Dunlop (1814, pp. 137–8) and others (see ). Brooks (ed. 1979, pp. lxxxi–lxxxii) compares Diana and Gil Polo’s Diana Enamorada for Felicia’s use of magic philtres (ed. 1968, pp. 186–9, 375); see , . He cites Lyly’s Euphues and his England for the herb called Anacamsoritis,…whosoeuer toucheth it, falleth in loue, with the person shee next seeth (2:115); and Spenser, FQ 2.6.18, where Phaedria’s spell to prevent Cymochles awaking is administered like the juice of Love-in-idleness: while he sleeps, she with liquors strong his eyes did steep.

548 or…or]
Wright (ed. 1877): Either…or (Abbott §136; Franz §586, quoting this instance; Blake 5.3.1 [c, iv]).

549 it]
Deighton (ed. 1891): The being, whether man or woman, as in 548.

550 Fetch…herbe]
Clarke (1995, p. 4): When Oberon sends him to fetch the flower love-in-idleness to concoct a love-potion, Puck aligns with Hermes/Thoth as the deliverer of a pharmakon [elixir]. His argument, based on Plato’s Phaedrus, connects Puck with Hermes as trickster with a gift for metamorphosis.

551 Ere…league]
Gentleman (ed. 1774): Oberon’s present message must be of considerable extent, as he allows the time of a whale’s swimming a league to do it in; but Fairies, and their excursions, defy all calculation. Asimov (1970, p. 34): It is foolish, of course, to try to attach literal meaning to what is obviously poetic hyperbole, but—just for fun—leviathan is the whale, which can swim as speedily as twenty miles an hour. To swim a league (three miles) would require nine minutes. But see n. 175 (league).

551 Leuiathan]
Johnson (1755): A water animal mentioned in the book of Job. By some imagined the crocodile, but in poetry generally taken for the whale (OED 1). Wright (ed. 1877): The margins of the Bibles in Shakespeare’s day explained leviathan as a whale, and so no doubt he thought it. Brooks (ed. 1979) compares Job 41:31: He maketh the deep to boil like a pot;… as suggestive of speed. Schmidt (1874) cites also TGV 3.2.79 (1524) and H5 3.3.26 (1285).

551 league]
See n. 175.

552–4 Ile…iuice]
Pope (ed. 1725) is the first to make two verse lines of these wrongly printed lines (see textual n.). Some early editions view 552–3 as prose and, apparently considering roũd as redundant, omit it (see textual n. 552).

552–3 Ile…minutes]
Miriam Joseph (1947, p. 125) quotes this speech as an example of periphrasis, which Sonnino (1968, p. 37) lists under the Latin name, Circumlocutio…also known as circuitio, quoting Quintilian (VIII, vi, 59 ff): When we use a number of words to describe something for which one, or at any rate only a few words of description would suffice…a circuitous mode of speech, frequently used among poets for a decorative effect.

552 Ile…earth]
OED (Girdle, n.1 3b), quoting this instance first: To put (make, cast) a girdle (round) about: To go round, make the circuit of (the earth). Quoting this expression in six plays from 1612 to 1640 Steevens (ed. 1778): Perhaps, it is proverbial. It is not listed in proverb collections, but it was widely imitated, beginning in 1612 with Dekker’s If This Be Not a Good Play, the Devil Is In It 1.2.177–8 (3:134). Halliwell (ed. 1856) mentions more uses of it, and suggests: The idea and expression was probably derived from the old plans of the world, in which the Zodiac is represented as a girdle round about the Earth; he quotes the Compost of Ptholomeus (1530, ch. 16, g3v), which describes the Zodiak encircling the world in maner of a gyrdle or as a garlande of floures. Green (1870, pp. 413–15) suggests the image was prompted by Whitney’s emblem celebrating Drake’s circumnavigation of the globe (1577–80), Auxilio divino, since the illustration shows a globe with a ship at the top which is attached to a rope which circles the globe [Emblemes (1586), 1866, p. 203].
Campbell (1846?, 3:234) would like to see in this an intuition of one of the marvels of modern industry, the electric telegraph (Fr.). Others repeated the conceit, e.g. Barnett (1887, p. 16), and in a burst of imperial enthusiasm, Lucy (1906, pp. 25–26).
Asimov (1970, p. 34): It is interesting to note that Puck outdoes even the modern astronaut, who requires ninety minutes to go around the earth. To circumnavigate the planet in forty minutes means moving at the rate of 37,500 miles an hour or a little over 10 miles a second. Puck would be hard put to manage to stay close to the earth’s surface at this speed, for he would well exceed the escape velocity. For more on Robin’s speed see Empson (1994, pp. 11, 213–14).

552 Ile]
Collier (1853, p. 102) argues that Ile should be emended to I’d (see textual n.) because Oberon had not required any such task [circumnavigating the globe] of him [Puck], but merely to fetch a plant.…What Puck means is to show his readiness to obey, even if he had been commanded to do much more.

552 forty]
Schmidt (1874): Used for an indefinite number, where no exact reckoning was needed (OED, a. b). Verity (ed. 1893): Perhaps 40 gained some mysterious import through the Scripture. Thus the wanderings of the Israelites lasted forty years [Josh. 5:6], the fast of our Lord forty days [Luke 4:1–2]—likewise the fast of Elijah (1 Kings [19:8]), and the stay of Moses on the Mount (Exod. [24:18; 34:28]). Furness (ed. 1895) refers to Karl Elze, Notes on Elizabethan Dramatists (1889, pp. 230[–1]) for more instances of the use of forty as an indefinite number. Empson (1979, p. 6) derides the editorial acceptance of this interpretation, arguing that Sh. was initially addressing a private audience who would be aware of Thomas Harriot’s unpublished views on the radius and rotation of the earth. Empson later (1994, pp. 216–21) elaborates his argument that forty minutes was a slogan among Harriot’s friends.

554–65 Hauing…conference]
Wells (ed. 1967), questionably: Oberon addresses the audience. Also questionably, Frame (1999, pp. 52–3) argues that Oberon’s act of beholding is a type of voyeuristic power; he notes the frequent observation of sleeping characters by others, stressing the viewing of Titania and Bottom asleep (1560–90), and comments: The watcher remains unseen, thereby taking on the role of the voyeur with all of its accompanying potency and pleasure. The terms scopophile and voyeur are therefore appropriate for Oberon and Puck, although the arousing, vicarious substitutions for sex inherently suggested by these terms in human quarters do not necessarily apply so neatly to Shakespeare’s complex and unearthly fairy world.

554 once]
Following Schmidt (1875, 2), Page (ed. 1897): The possession of this juice being an accomplished fact.

554 iuice]
Mowat & Werstine (ed. 1993): Nectar from the flower.

555 Ile…sleepe]
Evans (ed. 1974): I.e. watch for a time when I can catch her sleeping.

555 watch]
Johnson (1755, v. a. 2): To observe in ambush (OED, v. 11a, quoting this instance first).

555 a sleepe]
= on or in sleep; see OED, asleep, adv. and pred.a. 1, and etymology; a, prep.1 11. This compositor’s regular use of the two word form (see also 657, 756, 1649, 1731, 2116) is changed to asleep by Q2, F, etc.

556 liquor of it]
Johnson (ed. 1894): (i.e., of the juice) is, of course, tautological. Doran (ed. 1959), seeming puzzled: Juice of the flower (?), or essence of the juice (?). No one else is bothered by the relationship of the phrase to iuice in 554.

557 then]
For then that. On the omission of the relative see Abbott §244; Franz §348; Blake 3.3.2.6 (f). For another example see 1793. A few early editions follow Q2’s substitution when (see textual n.) but Thiselton (1903, p. 31) is the only one to defend it.

558–9 Lyon…Ape]
Kott (1964; 1966, p. 227): All these animals represent abundant sexual potency. Foakes (ed. 1984): These animals suggest a jungle or wild place rather than the pastoral countryside of [468–75]; he compares 597 and 607–8. Chiang (1996, pp. 158–9) expands on Kott: The lion is so much in heat that at all times of the yeare both sexes desire copulation (Topsell, Beasts [1607, p.] 465). The bear has long been a symbol of lust and fornication; descriptions of the seven deadly sins riding in a procession sometimes show Lechery or Luxuria riding on a bear (Rowland [Animals with Human Faces, 1973] 33; Bloomfield [The Seven Deadly Sins, 1952] 249); the sexual implication is also found in Callisto’s transformation to a bear after she has been raped by Jove [Ovid, Met. 2.466–95; see Golding 2.591–600, ].…Tamora’s two lustful sons are called bear-whelps [Tit. 4.1.96 (1644)].…The wolf is conventionally used as an image of sexual greed. The bull as a sexual exemplar is, for instance, revealed in Jove’s metamorphosis [cf. Wiv. 5.5.3 (2484)]. The meddling monkey and the busy ape are animals associated with lecherous desire; he cites OED, Meddle, v. 5 (see n. 559). See also n. 681–2 for Oberon’s second list of lustful animals.

558 Lyon, Beare]
Cf. Chaucer, KnT 1640, leon or the bere. See .

558 Bull]
Colman (1974, p. 186): A regular and powerful copulator, sometimes deceptively passive.

559 medling…Ape]
Cf. Rosalind threatening to be more new-fangled then an ape, more giddy in my desires, then a monkey (AYL 4.1.152–3 [2060–2]).

559 medling]
Schmidt (1875, Meddle 3): To intrude on the concerns of others (OED, Meddle, v. 8). Deighton (ed. 1891): Mischievous. OED (Meddle, v. 5): To have sexual intercourse (with), quoting instances from 1340–70 to 1655.

559 Monky]
Partridge (1968, monkey): Sh. makes goats and especially monkeys the types of lechery-addicts; monkeys, at least, deserve the imputation; he quotes (p. 172): A was…lecherous as a monkie (2H4 Q1600, 3.2.313–4 [1844–5]).

559 busie]
Johnson (1755, Busy 2), quoting this instance: Bustling; active; meddling (OED, a. 5). Possibly there is a sexual connotation; cf. OED (a. 1e): To be busy with: To have to do with (sexually) (first recorded 1612). Cf. also Partridge (1968, business): Business. Sexual intercourse.

560 soule of Loue]
Rann (ed. 1787): Most ardent affection. Delius (ed. 1859): Quintessence of love (Ger.). Wright (ed. 1877): Most intense and passionate love. Moberly (ed. 1881): Deep-seated love. Brooks (ed. 1979): Animating principle [of love] (OED, Soul, n. 8a).

561 take…of]
Johnson (1755, Take, 90): To Take off. To remove (OED, Take, v. 58 and 85h).

561 of]
Variant form of off (OED, Of). Off was commonly spelled of in early times: OED, adv. 3a, where off means Expressing separation from attachment, contact, or position on; as in…to take off, quotes ten instances from c1000 to 1703 where of is used for off. For discussion of from off see Franz §485 Anm., quoting this instance. See 1290, 1580.

562 another herbe]
Oberon later calls the plant Dians budde; see n. 1588.

563 render vp]
Schmidt (1875, 4): To surrender, to give up, to yield (OED, v. 8, quoting this instance). Cf. n. 105.

564–623 But…well]
Theobald (ed. 1733): I thought proper here to observe, that, as Oberon, and Puck his Attendant, may be frequently observ’d to speak, when there is no mention of their Entering; they are design’d by the Poet to be suppos’d on the Stage during the greatest Part of the Remainder of the Play; and to mix, as they please, as Spirits, with the other Actors; and embroil the Plot, by their Interposition, without being seen, or heard, but when to their own Purpose. Cf. 1386.
Evans (1960, p. 35): [This scene] represents the play’s first use of a discrepancy between the participants’ vision and ours: the couple are ignorant by what immortal eye they are being watched.…Neither now nor ever after are Helena and Demetrius—or, indeed, any of the other human participants—to know that a force from outside their mortal circle has looked on their affairs, apprehended their dilemma, and interceded in the cause of true love. From this first point on, a gap divides the human participants’ view from ours. This gap is unique in Shakespeare’s comedies in that it remains open even at the end of the play.

564 I am inuisible]
Steevens (ed. 1793) refers to his note in Tmp. at 1.2.374+1 (519) where in the SD Ariel is described as inuisible: In the wardrobe of the Lord Admiral’s men 1598, was a robe for to goo invisibell [Henslowe’s Diary, 1961, p. 325, l. 208]. Collier (ed. 1842) alludes to Henslowe and adds, Possibly Oberon wore, or put on, such a robe, by which it was understood that he was not to be seen. Wilson (ed. 1924), arguing that the device is evidence of Sh.’s revision of an old play: This hint to the audience, not of course to Puck, is a little crude. Harrison (ed. 1937): Some cloak or robe was put on to show Oberon’s invisibility.…Wordsworth perhaps gives the clue to the peculiarity of the robe, and he quotes The Prelude 7.279–87 where Jack the Giant-killer wears a coat black as death, and the word Invisible flames forth upon his chest. Andrews (ed. 1989): Oberon…may simply retire to an unobtrusive position on the stage. Idem (ed. 1993): Oberon’s words may simply be a way of informing the audience that Fairies cannot normally be perceived by mere mortals. Sh. may have had in mind the belief that on Midsummer eve fern-seed became visible and could be gathered and used to confer invisibility on those who succeeded in procuring it. See 1H4 2.1.86–90 (721–5); Drake (1817, 1:329–30).

565 conference]
Schmidt (1874, 1): Discourse, conversation (OED, n. 4a).

566–623 Enter…well]
Douce (1807, 1:189): However forward and indecorous the conduct of Helena in pursuing Demetrius may appear to modern readers, such examples are very frequent in old romances of chivalry, wherein Shakspeare was undoubtedly well read. The beautiful ballad of the Nut-brown maid might have been more immediately in his recollection, many parts of this scene having a very strong resemblance to it. For the thirty twelve-line stanzas of this early 16th-c. poem see The Oxford Book of Ballads, ed. A. Quiller-Couch (Oxford, 1910, pp. 295–307). Furness (ed. 1895) quotes an MS note from the famous actress Mrs. F. (Fanny) A. Kemble (1809–1893): Was it not well devised to make the timid, feminine Helena the pursuer of her indifferent, inconstant lover?…The pale, pathetic figure imploring vainly a man’s love, and enduring patiently his contemptuous refusal, still represents a…tender and feminine idea. St. John (1908, pp. 159–60) condemns her immodesty; see .

567–623 I…well]
Blits (2003, p. 89): Throughout the exchange Helena uses the respectful personal pronoun you, but Demetrius alternates from speech to speech between you and the scornful thee. His analysis is incomplete, and does not address questions of usage and register. See Abbott §231: Thou in Shakespeare’s time was, very much like du now among the Germans, the pronoun of (1) affection towards friends (2) good-humoured superiority to servants, and (3) contempt or anger to strangers. It had, however, already fallen somewhat into disuse, and, being regarded as archaic, was naturally adopted (4) in the higher poetic style and in the language of solemn prayer; he claims §235: In almost all cases where thou and you appear at first sight indiscriminately used, further considerations show some change of thought, or some influence of euphony sufficient to account for the change of pronoun. See also Blake 8.1–8.1.5.

567–8 I…Hermia]
Blits (2003, p. 68): Demetrius contradicts himself right away. He tells Helena not to pursue him because he does not love her.…But he pursues Hermia even though she does not love him.

567 I…me not]
Brooks (ed. 1979, p. xlvi) quotes this and lines 570–1 as examples of epistrophe, the rhetorical scheme which has lines and phrases ending with the same word. Cf. n. 180–3. Brown (ed. 1996): Again harsh monosyllables, and negatives, begin another male-female confrontation. Cf. the meeting of Oberon and Titania at 434 ff.

568 Where…Hermia]
Abbott §336: Inflection in s with two singular nouns as subject occurs frequently. The only example Abbott gives from MND is from 1148 where F1 changes Q1’s come to comes and F2–F4 follow. Knowles (privately): Simple ellipsis: Where is Lysander? and where is fair Hermia? Many editors follow Dyce (ed. 1857) in deleting the comma after Lysander; however, the slight pause allows the actor the option of switching from Demetrius’s mood of aggressive pursuit to remembering his role as lover, with possible comic effect.

569 stay…stayeth]
Thirlby, originator of the emendation slay…slayeth (see textual n.), later (MS 1733–47) says: If the old reading be right, which I still disbelieve, the meaning I think must be: the one (Hermia) I’ll stop (from run[n]ing away with L[ysander;]) the other (Lysander) is my hindrance.…I w[oul]d not have put this nor indeed any conjecture (at least not any w[hi]ch alter’d the sense) into the text. Heath (1765, p. 50): There is not the least foundation for imputing this bloody disposition to Demetrius. His real intention is sufficiently expressed in the common reading [of Q1 etc.]. I will arrest Lysander, and disappoint his scheme of carrying off Hermia; for ’tis upon the account of this latter that I am wasting away the night in this wood. Jackson (1819, pp. 12–13): He will stay, that is, he will stop Hermia;…and prevent her from eloping with Lysander, who stayeth him, i.e. stops him from obtaining the object of his passion. Caldecott (MS 1813–33): Pursue [567] is the leading idea; &…our Author pursues that idea by saying I’ll stop or stay Lysander, who stays, stops, obstructs or stands in my (Demetrius’s) way. Or possibly: I’ll stay (or wait for) Hermia’s desired presence, though Lysander prevents my wishes (cf. 178, 614; OED, Stay, v.1 19). Knight (ed. 1839), Halliwell (ed. 1856), Mowat & Werstine (ed. 1993) and a few others support the old reading, but most agree with Capell (1783, 2.3:106): Slay and slayeth are…proper for Demetrius’ using, his love-passion consider’d, and his disposition towards his rival, both appearing in the words of the next period. Collier (ed. 1842): In a subsequent scene [1070–96], Hermia suspects that Demetrius has slain Lysander. The Clarkes (ed. 1864): To slay his rival, while his mistress’s cruelty slayeth him, is according to lover-phraseology.

570 were stolne]
For the use of be with the passive in intransitive verbs of motion see Abbott §295; Franz §631; Blake 4.2.7 (b), quoting this instance.

570 vnto]
F1’s into is not accepted by most modern editors (see textual n.). See 1345 where the same preposition initiates the same phrase.

571 wodde]
Pope (ed. 1725): Mad, wild, raving. Rann (ed. 1787): Frantick. OED, Wood, a. 3b, quoting this instance, ameliorates the meaning somewhat: Violently angry or irritated; enraged, furious. Cunliffe (ed. 1912), alone: One of Shakespeare’s worst puns. Clemen (ed. 1963): With perhaps an additional pun on wooed. Wright (1968, p. 32): It has been argued that the pun establishes an association between the woods and irrational behaviour as opposed to the order of Theseus’s city.

573 Hence]
Apart from its use to dismiss Titania’s fairies when she wishes to sleep (652, 675), this emphatic command to depart is associated with avoiding, or ridding the speaker of, something detested (436, 671, 739, 1295, 1347, 1731). For the ellipsis of a verb of motion, see OED, adv. 2.

573 get the gone]
Schmidt (1874, Get 9): Most frequent is the reflective use…in the sense of to betake one’s self, to go [noting especially the frequency of the phrase get thee gone] (OED, Get, v. 28c, quoting Err. 3.1.84 [745] as first instance; cf. 27b). See also Abbott §212; Franz §307; and, for a full discussion, Blake 4.3.3 (a). Similar uses are at 1353, 1365.

573 the]
Variant form of thee (OED).

574–7 You…you]
Brooks (ed. 1979): Throughout the following episode, Helena’s self-abandonment in obsessive love owes a good deal to Phaedra’s in Hippolytus. Here she shares with her the sense of not being her own mistress, quoting Seneca, 699. See also n. 582–9, and .

574–6 Adamant…steele]
Wright (ed. 1877) compares Tro. 3.2.177–9 (1810–12): As true as steele…As Iron to Adamant.

574 draw]
Johnson (1755, v. a. 26): To allure; to entice. OED (v. 25): To attract by physical force, as a magnet. Cf. 576.

574 Adamant]
OED (n. 3a; 4): (3a) Loadstone or magnet; (4), quoting this instance only: Confusing 3 with 1 [anything very hard: see headnote] or 2 [Diamond]. There is a conflation of proverbs here: Dent (A31.1): As the Adamant draws iron, and (A31.2): As hard as Adamant (often of hearts). Johnson (ed. 1894): Adamant means both diamond (a corruption of adamant) and a magnet. The hardness and attractive power are both referred to here. The Clarkes (1879) quote Florio: Looks of adamant to draw hearts of yron, like ships to the shipwrack (Second frutes, 1591, 2C3); this refers to the dangers of feminine beauty, and thus accords with Helena’s sense of the perversity of her attachment to Demetrius (cf. 610–13). Many examples of literary use are provided by e.g. Halliwell (ed. 1856); Craig (in Cuningham ed. 1905), from Huon (ed. Lee [1883] 1975, 1:369); West (1865, pp. 16–17) from Sidney, Arcadia (ed. Evans, 1977, p. 260); Theobald (1909, p. 99) from Pliny, Chaucer, Bacon, and others; Hankins (1953, pp. 238, 241–2) from Palingenius, and Ficino: As symbolic of the drawing power of love, it appears twice in Ficino’s Commentarium in Convivium, VI. ii, VII. iv, and may have become a commonplace from its use therein; Brooks (ed. 1979) from Lyly, Euphues and his England (2:167–8). Shaheen (1993, p. 96) notes parallels in Ezek. 3:9 and Zech. 7:12, but thinks it likely that Sh. was using a common expression rather than making a biblical reference.

575–6 But…steele]
Deighton (ed. 1891): If the reading is right, the meaning probably is, Though you draw my heart it is not a substance like iron, famed for its hardness, but a substance like steel famed for its truth. Verity (ed. 1893): I.e. in drawing (attracting) my heart you draw that which for its trueness is very steel. A poetic conceit or fancy. Without comment on Helena’s heart of steel, Wilson (ed. 1924): But yet you do not draw your sword to slay me.…Shakespeare constantly uses iron for sword, e.g. [Tro. 2.3.16–17 (1220)], drawing their massy irons. Wells (ed. 1967): The conceit is somewhat strained, perhaps with a deliberate effect of slightly comic inanity. Accepting Wilson’s reading Farrow (ed. 1972): You don’t draw your sword…because I’m perfectly faithful. McDonald (ed. 2000): The passage is full of puns on hardness, attraction, magnetism, and the violence associated with swords.

576 true as steele]
Proverbial (Tilley S840). Craig (ed. 1903) compares Chaucer, LGW Prol. F334, where the expression is used (somewhat ironically) to assert women’s constancy.

576 Leaue]
Wright (ed. 1877): Give up (OED, v.1 9).

576 draw]
See n. 574. Possibly a quibble on the meaning as in Johnson (1755, v. a. 10): To pull a sword from the sheath (OED, v. 33a). Cf. Wilson (ed. 1924) at n. 575–6.

578 Doe…I]
Scott (ed. 1898): These direct interrogations are a strong way of asserting the negative.

578 speake you faire]
Schmidt (1874, Fair adv. 6): Kindly, gently (OED, Fair, adv. 2a). See Franz §241, Anm.

580 not I cannot]
The not is regarded as a compositorial slip and emended to nor by almost all editors. See textual n. On the double negative as a means of emphasis see Abbott §406; Franz §588; Blake 6.2.3.1, quoting the instance at 966. For another example see 781.

581 euen]
Monosyllabic or elided (Abbott §466; Franz §50). Other examples of the same pronunciation are at 1091, 1252, 1432.

582–9 I…dogge]
Gentleman (ed. 1774): There is somewhat very mean, and we hope unnatural, in the open servility of Helena’s affection: though some women will bear great slights, yet not one, we think, would wish to rank with a dog, except one of that kind which commands peculiar estimation. Richmond (1971, p. 106): Shakespeare shows us how undisciplined passion can turn humans into beasts whose sexual reflexes are totally irrational and arbitrary. With this kind of perversity in mind, he makes Helena, a willing victim, exclaim to her alienated lover: [quotes 582–6]. Inevitably this servility fails to trigger the acquisitive sexual instinct of the crudely predatory male; but in passing Helena by, Demetrius only confirms her fixation. Indeed, he pushes it beyond neurosis into suicidal extravagance: [quotes 622–3]. The grotesque passion of Titania, the fairy queen, for the assified Bottom is scarcely more perverse. Brooks (ed. 1979) aptly parallels the behavior of Seneca’s Phaedra declaring her abject passion to Hippolytus: she will unrelentingly follow him, she throws herself at his feet, and she welcomes the threat of dying on his sword (Hippolytus ll. 698–712; tr. Studley, 1581, pp. 161–2). In Studley’s translation, Hippolytus’s horror of Phaedra’s shamelesse fawning Pawes touching his spotlesse body (cf. Latin ll. 704–5) may have prompted Sh.’s dog imagery. Webb (1988, p. 46): Helena’s passion for Demetrius is exposed in terms that would outrage every self-respecting feminist, being thrillingly masochistic.

582–3 I…on you]
Grey (1754, 1:54) first notes that this is a proverbial observation, quoting Ray’s Proverbs (1670, p. 59): A spaniel, a woman, and a wallnut-tree, The more they’re beaten, the better still they be; Tilley (W644). Brooks (ed. 1979) provides a closer proverb from Tilley (S705): The Spaniel, that fawns when he is beaten, will never forsake his master. Cf. TGV 4.2.14–15 (1636–7).
Landmann (ed. Lyly, Euphues, 1887, p. 146) quotes these lines in a note to Wilt thou resemble the kinde Spaniell, which the more he is beaten the fonder he is (p. 81), and describes the spaniel as a kind of dog known for its submissiveness. Cf. Sh.’s contemptuous dismissal of fond flattery as base Spaniell fawning in JC 3.1.43 (1250), and in H8 5.2.161–2 (3195–6).

583 I…fawne]
Deighton (ed. 1891): i.e. the more I will fawn.

584 spurne]
Johnson (1755, 1; 3): 1. To kick, with some sense of 3. To treat with contempt (OED, v.1 5; 6). See 1252, 1348.

585 loose me]
Halliwell (ed. 1856): Blot me out of your memory. Schmidt (1874, Lose 3): In a moral sense = to alienate, to act so as to forfeit the favour of (OED, Lose, v.1 3c). Deighton (ed. 1891): Cast me off and have nothing to do with me. Thiselton (1903, p. 32): Let me run loose. Cf. OED (Loose, v. 1b): To set free, release. Wilson (ed. 1924): Shakespeare made no difference in the spelling of [loose and lose], and loves to quibble upon them, instancing LLL 4.3.71 (1406) and 4.3.358 (1712). Kittredge (ed. 1939): Lose me, i.e., banish me from your thoughts. Andrews (ed. 1993): Unleash [me]. Cf. n. 123.

587 worser]
Johnson (1755): A barbarous word, formed by corrupting worse with the usual comparative termination. OED (a. etymological note): A double comparative. The word was common in the 16th–17th c. as a variant of worse, in all its applications. Abbott §11 observes that such doubling is used for greater emphasis. For more examples see Franz §217a; Blake 3.2.3.4 (b). Cf. n. 831 (more better).

588 And…mee]
Deighton (ed. 1891): And yet even that I look upon as a place of honour.

588 respect]
Regard: see n. 147.

589 Then…dogge]
Proverbial; ODEP, quoting this instance: To Use one like a dog; Tilley’s first instance is 1618.

590 Tempt…spirit]
For this construction see Franz §601, quoting this instance; Blake 6.2.2 (iv). See also 1341, 1720–1.

590 Tempt]
Johnson (1755, 2): Provoke.

590 spirit]
OED (n. 12): The emotional part of man as the seat of angry or hostile feelings; or possibly (13a): Mettle. Schmidt (1875, 6): Mind, soul. For pronunciation see n. 17.

591, 592 sick]
Schmidt (1875, 2): Affected with nausea. OED (a. 4a [d]), quoting 591 first: Deeply affected…by repugnance or loathing. But in 592 perhaps Helena means deeply affected…by sorrow or longing (OED (a. 4a [a & b]).

593–8 You…virginitie]
Rudd (1979, p. 187) finds several parallels with Ovid’s account of Pyramus and Thisbe: You do impeach your modesty too much, To leave the city…(urbis quoque tecta relinquant in v.86); To trust the opportunity of night (nocte uenires, in v.111); And the ill counsel of a desert place (perhaps a reminiscence of neue sit errandum lato spatiantibus aruo in v.87 combined with in loca plena metus in v.111); With the rich worth of your virginity (dignissima in v.109). Cf. Golding, 4.106; 137; 106, 136; 134; see . Brooks (ed. 1979): Cf. [TGV 2.7.59–61 (1034–6)], and its source, [Montemayor’s] Diana. Julia, planning to follow Proteus in men’s clothes, fears so unstaid a journey will make her scandaliz’d.…Felismena similarly follows Don Felix: love blinding my eies and minde with an inconsiderate regard of mine owne estate and condition.…I went…out of my dear reputation… ([ed. 1968], p. 87).

593 impeach]
Steevens (ed. 1793): Bring…into question (OED, v. 3, quoting this instance first). Caldecott (MS 1813–33): Disparage. Schmidt (1874, 2): Expose to reproach. Ridley (ed. 1934, Glossary): Endanger. Doran (ed. 1959): Discredit.

593 modestie]
See nn. 69, 709.

594 To leaue]
I.e., by leaving. See n. 265.

594 your selfe]
See n. 126.

596–8 To…virginitie]
Caldecott (MS 1813–33) compares Milton, Comus 400–3. Roberts (1991, p. 45):Demetrius, like a pale shadow of the rapist of the same name in Titus Andronicus, imagines the possibility of raping even his rejected lover.…His threat becomes more explicit [in 615–16]. Holland (Culture, 1994, p. 228) sees this as an oppressive phallic demand which is necessary to provoke Hermia’s dream [800–9].

596 To trust]
I.e., by trusting. See n. 265.

596 opportunitie of night]
Deighton (ed. 1891): The opportunity which the night-time affords; the subjective genitive (OED, Of, prep. 17a). See Blake 3.3.1.1 (b). See 619. Cf. 1256.

597 ill counsell]
Rolfe (ed. 1877): The evil suggestions. Phillips (ed. 1969): Temptation. Andrews (ed. 1993): [I]ll-advised (unwise).…But Ill also suggests that this place harbours evil dangers. See also n. 229.

597 desert place]
Page (ed. 1897): A lonely, solitary, uninhabited place (OED, a. 1; 2); he compares Mark 6:32. Foakes (ed. 1984): Reinforcing the images of wildness at [557–8] and [607–8]; the wood is at once a desert and a pastoral place.

598 rich]
Schmidt (1875, 3): Precious. Cf. n. 507.

599–600 Your…face.]
Explication of these lines has been complicated by emendations of the punctuation. See textual n. Tyrwhitt (in Steevens MS 1773–8) removes the colon and shifts the stop to the end of 599, explaining for that means for leaving the city. Steevens (ed. 1778) adopts the conjecture, followed for over a century by most editors, including Halliwell (ed. 1856) who expands Tyrwhitt’s explanation: I do really not impeach my modesty by my conduct, your virtue being a valid excuse for the course I have taken. Cuningham (ed. 1905) accepts Tyrwhitt’s emendations but demurs: I think…that that refers simply to virginity, and not so much to her leaving the city. Hunter (1845, 1:290), objects to the emendations and quotes QqFf punctuation of 599, finding it infinitely superior. Clark & Wright (ed. 1863) restore the QqFf punctuation of 599 to the text, and Wright (ed. 1877) paraphrases: Your virtue is my protection, because it is not &c. Furness (ed. 1895) finds the scales are so nicely balanced that he cannot choose between the readings of 599. Thiselton (1903, p. 32) is more decisive: Our three authorities [QqF] concur in the colon after priviledge, so that if any regard is to be had to the punctuation, Tyrwhitt’s reading, which expunges the colon and places a fullstop after that, must be ruled out. [Q1], further, capitalises For, which strongly confirms the correctness of the colon. It remains doubtful whether that is demonstrative, referring to vertue or priviledge, with for as a preposition; or whether it is a mere conjunctional affix (Abbott 287). The position of that would seem to favour the former alternative. Most 20th- and 21st-c. editors agree. Brooks (ed. 1979) explicates: From the premise Your virtue is my privilege, Helena’s argument shows that by the virtue which privileges or exempts her from what Demetrius has threatened, she means the especial quality, like that of a herb or precious stone (cf. Rom., 2.3.13–16 [1018–21]), which is his.…For that (= because) by the light of his countenance he has the virtue of banishing the night, the night presents no risk to her virginity. Cf. OED, Virtue, n. 11.
From Rowe (ed. 1709) to the present, the full stop following face is lightened to a semicolon or comma by all editors, without comment. Abbott §287 observes that omitting the full stop after face, [makes] for that (because) answer to therefore. However, the same holds true if Therefore begins a new sentence expanding the preceding argument, as Thiselton (1903, p. 32) recognizes: Nothing seems to be gained by converting the full stop after face into a comma.

599 Your…priuiledge]
Kittredge (ed. 1939): Virtue does not mean your moral goodness, but your transcendent attractions, which turn night into day and make this uninhabited place seem full of company. Doran (ed. 1959): Your power to attract is my warrant, gives me my special right to come (?), or your goodness is my guarantee of immunity from danger (?). Wells (ed. 1967): The effect of your qualities upon me puts me in a privileged position—that is, because when Demetrius is there the night seems like day. Other glosses of priuiledge include Thirlby (MS 1725–33): Protection, comparing TGV 3.1.160 (1229). Neil (ed. 1878): Surety. Watt (ed. 1906): Special arrangement. For a similar use see 1102; for a different, 49.

600 It…face]
Thirlby (MS 1747–53) refers to Tmp. 3.1.33–4 (1275–6), ’tis fresh morning with me When you are by at night. Johnson (ed. 1765, 8:2H7) quotes Tibullus, 4.13.11–12: Tu nocte vel atra Lumen, et in solis tu mihi turba locis [For me thou art…light even in night’s darkness, a throng amid the solitudes.—Loeb.] Steevens (ed. 1793) compares Ps. 139:12: Yea, the darkness is no darkness with thee, but the night is as clear as the day. Pye (1807, p. 53): With all due deference to the learned opinions of Johnson and Steevens, I do think it possible that Shakespear might have written this without any assistance either from Tibullus or King David. Walter (ed. 1964) compares Son. 43.13–14. Barkan (1986, p. 257) cites this as one of the many transformations wrought by love in the play.

602–5 Nor…mee]
Dodd (1752, 1:108) compares 2H6 3.2.360–2 (2075–7), A Wildernesse is populous enough, So Suffolke had thy heauenly company: For where thou art, there is the World it selfe. Cf. Lyly, Endimion 2.1.53–6 (3:32): The sweet remembrance of your loue, is the onely companion of my life, and thy presence, my paradise: so that I am not alone when no bodie is with mee, and in heauen it selfe when thou art with me. Walter (ed. 1964): All the world, (a) everything that matters, (b) everybody in the world, (c) perhaps the audience. Warren (1983, p. 33): When Shakespeare in his own Sonnet 112 says to his lover You are my all the world, the hyperbole has a compressed directness which sounds a convincing expression of feeling; but when he gives a similar hyperbole to Helena, he pushes it into parody by expanding it in a quibbling and mechanical way. Sorelius (1993, p. 173) notes the theatrical joke in 604–5.

602 worlds]
Johnson (1755, 8), quoting this instance: Great multitude. OED (n. 7f pl.), quoting this instance: Used hyperbolically for: a great quantity.

603 respect]
Regard: see n. 147.

606 Ile…thee]
Brooks (ed. 1979): Cf. the Nurse’s warning [to Phaedra] that Hippolytus will runne away (Studley’s tr. [Seneca, p. 145]). This is a response to Phaedra’s claim that she can tame wilde thinges.

607 leaue…of]
OED (Mercy, n. 5c) quotes 2H6 1.3.134 (524), left thee to the mercy of. Cf. Ovid, Heroides 4.116, Phaedra to Hippolytus, soror est praeda relicta feris [my sister he (Theseus) left at the mercy of wild beasts]. Cf. Chaucer, LGW 2192; see .

608 The…you]
S[tephen] W[eston] (in Steevens, ed. 1785) compares Ovid [Heroides 10.1, Ariadne to Theseus], Mitius inveni quam te genus omne ferarum [All beasts I find gentler than you], and Tim. 4.1.35–6 (1538–9), Timon will to the Woods, where he shall finde Th’vnkindest Beast, more kinder then Mankinde. Chaucer translates Ovid, Meker than ye fynde I the bestes wilde! (LGW 2198; see ).

609–13 Runne…flies]
Wright (ed. 1877) refers to the tale in Ovid, Met. 1.452–565 (Golding 1.545–696, see ). Rolfe (ed. 1877): The same myth is alluded to in [Shr. Ind. 2.57–60 (209–12)] and [Tro. 1.1.98 (132)]; in Shr. it follows an allusion to Venus and Adonis (cf. n. 566–623). Of course as Boas (1950, p. 77) observes, in MND the parts are reversed. Cox (1991, pp. 74–5), quoting Golding (1.611–14; see ): The principle of inversion or reversal seems clear enough: Apollo says Stay, whereas Helena says Run; Ovid’s stags flee from his lions, whereas Shakespeare’s mild hind pursues his tiger. The words The story shall be changed are plainly literal…. And the roles of the sexes are reversed to match the reversal of predator and prey.…But the most surprising change that Shakespeare makes in adapting the lines from Golding is to substitute the mythological griffon for the naturalistic goshawk.

609 story]
Scott (ed. 1898): Probably refers to the three illustrations given and means all history and experience will be changed.

610 holds the chase]
Schmidt (1874, Chase 3), quoting this instance only: To pursue (cf. OED, Chase, n.1 1e).

611–12 The…Tigre]
Vergil lists impossibilities when love is crossed, in Eclogue 8: iungentur iam grypes equis, aevoque sequenti / cum canibus timidi venient ad pocula dammae. (Griffins now shall mate with mares, and, in the age to come, the timid deer shall come with hounds to drink.) (27–28); nunc et ovis ultro fugiat lupus, (Now let the wolf even flee before the sheep,) (52). The phrase the milde Hinde is a close translation of timidi dammae.

611 The Doue]
For the dove’s harmlessness and gentleness, see nn. 182, 344–5.

611 the Griffon]
OED (Griffin1, 1a; 2): 1a. A fabulous animal…having the head and wings of an eagle and the body and hind quarters of a lion.… 2. A vulture. Most commentators agree with the first definition, some observing that people formerly believed in the existence of the beast. Halliwell (ed. 1856) gives two quotations from Sir John Mandeville, Travels, then this description by Sir Thomas Browne, Pseudodoxia Epidemica (1646, sigR1r): That there are griffins in nature, that is, a mixt and dubious animal,…many affirm, and most, I perceive, deny not. See also Batman vppon Bartholome (1582, 12.19) Of the Gripe (sig. 2I4v). Craig (in Cuningham, ed. 1905) refers to Huon of Burdeux’s encounter with the fearsome monster (ed. Lee [1883] 1975, 1:425–8). Watt (ed. 1906), however, thinks that Sh. can hardly mean more than an eagle here. Baldwin (1944, 1:627) refers to Aesop’s fable of the hawk and the dove (Accipiter & columba): This story is regularly used by Shakspere to illustrate reversal of nature, instancing MND, Ant. 3.13.194–6 (2382–4) and Cor. 5.6.113–16 (3784–7). Cf. also 3H6 1.4.41 (501). All show acts of extreme desperation. There are variations in the name of the larger bird. In Ant. The Doue will peck the Estridge, which most gloss as Ostrich, but Douce argues strongly for Goshawk (see Spevack, ed. Ant., 1990, p. 230 n. 2384). Cox (1991, p. 75) finds a reason for Sh.’s change in MND from the more usual goshawk: Like the griffon, which is a composite of two natural creatures, Bottom becomes a composite of man and ass, both of which exist in nature…and…[the] dove Titania pursues the griffon.

611 the milde]
Furness (ed. 1895) paraphrases Abbott §457 in calling the an unemphatic monosyllable, standing in an emphatic place. The grammarian gives 1555 as another example.

611 Hinde]
Johnson (1755, 1): The she to a stag; the female of red deer (OED, n.1 1). Webb (1996, pp. 86–7) adds, signifying weakness and vulnerablility, comparing R3 2.4.50 (1542).

612 Bootelesse]
See n. 407.

614 stay]
Schmidt (1875, 2 trans. h): To remain for the purpose of. OED (v.1 19), quoting this instance: To wait for;…to abide, sustain (a question). For omission of the preposition for see Abbott §200; Franz §630 (a); Blake 4.4.3 (b). See also 2055.

614 questions]
The Clarkes (ed. 1864): Inquisitions, cross-examinings, reproachful debate. Schmidt (1875, 7): Conversation, speech, talk. Wright (ed. 1877): The plural may denote Helena’s repeated efforts at inducing Demetrius to talk with her. Andrews (ed. 1989): Questions…were points at issue in formal debates. Demetrius says he has no time for Helena’s exercises in forensic ingenuity.
Thirlby (MS 1733–47) compares MV 4.1.346 (2263), Ile stay no longer question. Steevens (ed. 1793) conjectures question, i.e. discourse, conversation, quoting AYL 3.4.35–6 (1742–3). Walker (1860, 1:248) recommends the same emendation, and explains (1:234): The interpolation of an s at the end of a word—generally, but not always, a noun substantive—is remarkably frequent in the folio. Few editors adopted the alteration: see textual n.

616–18 But…mischiefe]
Brooks (ed. 1979, p. xlvi): Helena matches word against word (parison)…when she retorts to Demetrius.

616 But]
Johnson (1755, 7): Otherwise than that. Abbott §122: Anything except that, quoting R3 1.3.286 (759), Ile not beleeue but… See Franz §569, quoting this instance at (b); Blake 5.4.2 But.

616 mischiefe]
OED (n. 2a), quoting this instance: Harm or evil considered as the work of an agent. Cf. 618.

617 I]
The invariable spelling in Q1 of this variant form of aye meaning yes. Johnson (1755, 3): I is more than once in Shakespeare written for ay, or yes (OED, Aye, int. 1). For more examples see 628, 870, 903, 1310, 1323, 2095, 2133. Cf. n. 142.

617 Temple…fielde]
