Deighton (ed. 1891): The temple, the most sacred of places: the town, the most frequented of places: the field, the most open of places.

618 mischiefe]
OED (n. 2b): An injury wrought by a person. Cf. 616.

618 Fy]
Johnson (1755, interj.): A word of blame and disapprobation (OED, Fie, int. 1). For the other two instances in this play see 1322.

619 Your…sex]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Your cruelty to me; your, used subjectively. See n. 596 (opportunitie of night). Scott (ed. 1898): My feminine character (not the sex at large). Kittredge (ed. 1939): The wrong you do me in refusing to love me forces me to woo you—and such conduct is scandalous in a woman. Wells (ed. 1967): Cause me to act in a manner that disgraces my sex. Andrews (ed. 1989): Demetrius, having been betrothed to Helena, [see 1697] has humiliated her and, by extension, women generally.

621 We…wooe]
Grinfield (MS 1834–63) compares Milton, PL 8.503, That would be woo’d, and not unsought be won.

621 should be]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Ought to be, referring to Abbott §323.

621 wooe]
Q1 omits exits for both Demetrius and Helena. Q2 and the folios put Exit at 623, which may indicate both leave: compare 1020 and 1955 where Qq and F1 use Exit with a plural subject. A third of editions, roughly, have him exit at 616 or 621 and her at 623; over half have them leave together at 623. See textual nn. Granville-Barker (ed. 1924, p. li), alone: Exit Demetrius…is not marked in either Q[1] or F1. It is open to a producer to place it after [619], Helena speaking the next two lines to herself, Demetrius stealing off unseen while she does so; to leave it where it is [after 621],—which allows her a loud apostrophe to the just vanished gentleman; or to place it after [623], which implies that, having endured as much as he could, he breaks suddenly away. I prefer either 1 or 3 to 2. Capell (ed. 1768), after 621: Demetrius breaks from her, and Exit. Placing his exit after 621, Farrow (ed. 1972) comments: Helena’s voice rises to an indignant howl as Demetrius dashes off. Do you blame him for wanting to escape?

622–3 Ile…well]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Find the happiness of heaven in enduring the tortures to which you condemn me. Lees (ed. 1902): Find happiness in dying by the hand of one I love so well, and yet who tortures me by rejecting me. Thiselton (1903, p. 33): Helena means that instead of being wooed by Demetrius, she follows him contrary to the usual course, and even death at his hand—if that is the mischief he has threatened [616]—is welcome, for it releases her from her hell of suffering, and, so devoted is she, she prefers death at her beloved’s hand to desisting from following him. Brooks (ed. 1979) finds these words echo Phaedra’s sentiment when Hippolytus draws sword upon her in Seneca, Hippolytus, 710–12, especially the words in the last line, manibus immoriar tuis (by thy hands I die).

622 Ile]
White (ed. 1858) termed F1’s alteration to I a mere typographical error, and most agree (see textual n.), except Furness (ed. 1895) who found no reason for deserting the Ff here, and Thiselton (1903, p. 33): The Folio reading gives a better antithesis to the preceding line.

622 make…hell]
Chambers (ed. 1897): The opposite idea to that contained in [220]. Douce (1807, 1:190) and others compare Milton, PL 1.254–5.

622 heauen]
Schmidt (1874, 3), citing also 220: A place or state of supreme felicity (OED, n. 7. fig. a, quoting this instance).

622 hell]
See n. 150.

623 To dy]
I.e., by or in dying. See n. 265.

623 vpon the hand]
Andrews (ed. 1993): As a victim of the sword. See also n. 762.

623 vpon]
OED (prep. 2c), quoting this instance: By means of. Steevens (ed. 1793): By, comparing TGV 2.4.114 (764), Ile die on him that saies so but your selfe. But Seymour (1805, 1:45) argues that this means Proteus will kill him, or contend with him to death, and implausibly suggests that the latter sense applies in MND. Caldecott (MS 1813–33): Under; sink into death upon, comparing Ado 4.1.223 (1887), she died vpon his words. Schmidt (1875, prep., final par.) considers this a Singular use and compares on in 762 and JC 5.1.58 (2392). Wright (ed. 1877): With this [meaning, by,] is combined the idea of local nearness to the beloved object. Neil (ed. 1878): Resting on or upholden by. Chambers (ed. 1897) agrees with OED and Steevens, citing Abbott §191, who (like Caldecott) quotes Ado 4.1.223 (1887), defining vpon as in consequence of.

624–5 Fare…loue]
Keyishian (1995, p. 31) compares 522–3, where Oberon is offended by Titania’s refusal to surrender the changeling boy. Here he is offended by Demetrius’s abuse of Helena, and determines to punish him using the same mechanism by which he punishes Titania: the misdirection or redirection of affections. The effect of joining these two plot elements at this precise moment is to legitimate Oberon’s revenge and reinforce the idea that obedience to appropriate male authority is the best formula for stability in both the social and natural orders.
Brooks (ed. 1979) compares Oberon’s benevolent intervention here and at 641–7 with the aid given Huon by the fairy king; see .

624 Fare…Nymph]
Wells (ed. 1967): Oberon, who has been an invisible spectator, now comes forward.

624 Fare thee well]
For thee substituted for thou in this phrase see Abbott §212; Franz §283; Blake 4.3.3 (a). See OED, Fare, v.1 9b. Cf. 786, 1270.

624 Nymph]
Johnson (1755, 1; 2): 1. A goddess of the woods, meadows, or waters.…2. A lady. In poetry. OED (n. 2a poet.), quoting the instance at 1648: A young and beautiful woman; hence a maiden, damsel. Cf. Ham. 3.1.88 (1743). Farrow (ed. 1972), alone: Oberon uses the word in a sarcastic way. Foakes (ed. 1984): The use of the term here, as at [1648], playfully absorbs the lovers into a quasi-mythological world. Cf. 1162, 1253.

625 fly…seeke]
I.e. fly from, and seeke for. See n. 216.

626 Hast…wanderer]
A few desire to alter the text: for example, Jackson (1819, p. 13) thinking that according to the present reading—Welcome Wanderer, is made the name of the flower, places the salutation first. Most find no problem. See textual n. Thiselton (1903, p. 33): Oberon catches sight of the flower, until which he withholds his welcome, for he knows that Puck is given to negligence, mistake, or knavery, as the case may be.

627 Enter Pucke.]
All editors from Malone (ed. 1790) on adopt Capell’s (ed. 1768) transference of the SD to after 625.

630–7 I…in]
Pope (ed. 1725, 6:4L1r), in the few Descriptions of Places he indexes, lists this passage as Bank, flowry. Arnold (1866, pp. 122, 120) quotes the first four lines as an example of Greek clearness and brightness in the handling of nature, where the eye is on the object, but lightness and brightness are added. Fairchild (1937, p. 180) argues that such lines which, though oft-quoted, are still for us of undying beauty…stirred in the hearts of dwellers in the dirty and noisy city a patriotic feeling which was grounded in a love of the English countryside. Farzààd (1946, pp. 69–77) argues that this passage is echoed in [1511–14, 1553–7], and examines the use of images of scent in both places. Dobbs ([1948], p. 24) compares the locale where Socrates and Phaedrus debate love and lovers (Phaed. 229–31; see ). Stamm (1954, pp. 29–30) finds it A striking instance of the connexion between word-scenery and mood…, expanding the imaginary scene beyond the limits of the stage; he suggests that in open-air performances the effect of such passages may be weakened rather than strengthened by the sight of natural plants and trees. Doran (1974, pp. 61, 64) takes it as an example of the poetic fusion of the observed and the conventional, the real and the fanciful, the emotional and the ideational into an imaginary whole.…[64] The flowers…all have some kind of history, varying in richness of literary associations or emblematic meanings. To make this point is not to deny the experience of our senses. For the flowers of Titania’s canopy and bed have been chosen for their perfume as well as their meaning. Moreover, their names fall on our ears with a charming cadence.…Oberon’s lines…suggest the freshness of spring, a delicate sensuousness, the time of love and its vows of constancy, and beauty fit for a queen. Halio (1976, p. 43): The mellifluousness of the verse, the lulling rhymes of the end-stopped lines, and especially the loveliness of the imagery combine to take off nearly all of the edge of Oberon’s malice. Beaurline (1978, p. 95), fancifully: Oberon wraps himself in a snake skin and plans…to play a devilish role. Foakes (ed. 1984): These lines return us from the image of a desert place [597], or dangerous wood full of wild animals [558–9], to a bower suggestive of Warwickshire rather than Athens. See n. 525–30 (Since…).
Not all commentary is enthusiastic. Luce (1922, p. 406) dismisses the natural description as conventional, and contrasts the comparative stiffness, hesitancy, and perhaps even the untruth of this example with Cym. 4.2.218–29 (2528–39). Fluchère (1948, p. 230; tr. 1956, p. 173): It is a framework, stage-scenery painted…to receive his fairy characters.…The verse flows regularly, having to express only the gentleness of fairy landscape, springing from a calm vision of conventional English scenery. Here the poet’s sensibility is not concerned to produce anything but a pleasing decorative picture.
However, the many musical settings, both for and independent of production, show composers responding to the passage’s sound and imagery. See Gooch & Thatcher, 1991, #10312–49, and . The use in productions of MND of the very popular setting by Charles Edward Horn (1786–1849), originally composed for a revival of Wiv. in 1824 (see Gooch & Thatcher, 1991, #10757, 8876–87), is condemned by Fitzgerald (1908, p. 60) as a truly awful example of such interruptions of the action: Two presentable fairies come forward smiling, and go through the whole in thirds, warbling away melodiously with trills and flourishings, so as to enrapture all the hearers.…No one knows what it is all about, or why such warbling should be introduced. Noble (1923, pp. 54–5): The propriety of such treatment is very doubtful. If treated as a song, it is very difficult to avoid a sound of Italian opera, the unreality of which is so absolutely foreign to Shakespeare’s dramatic scheme. See also Griffiths (ed. 1996, pp. 132–3). On operatic settings of the play, see , .

630–3 I…Eglantine]
Wright (ed. 1877, at 633): Except in fairy land these flowers would not be found all at the same season. Rydén (1978, p. 23): In the enchanted fairy-land of MND…the humble flowers of the dry hillside grow side by side with those of the luxuriant arbour. Halliwell (ed. 1856) compares Spenser, FQ 2.5.29. Caldecott (MS 1813–1833) comments on the rich & overpowering sweets of the luxuriant Honeysuckle, & the sweet briar, and compares Tilney’s The flower of friendship (1568): A passing faire arbour…powdred with sundrie flowers, and wrethed about wyth the sweete Brier, or Eglantyne (A5v), where Lady Julia is crowned with roses as the sovereign who directs the discussion of love and marriage. Wilson (1948, p. 40) parallels the flowery bed to which Psyche is wafted in Apuleius’s Golden Ass (see ). Harrison (1956, p. 57) compares Marlowe’s Dido 2.1.317–19 (1:25). Holland (ed. 1994): The Entertainment at Theobalds provided for Elizabeth in 1591 included an arbour all of eglantine (Lyly, [1:418]); this arbour is set in a maze, and further beautified with roses and pansies partly coloured(1:417). Milton remembers these lines. Dodd (1752, 1:83) compares PL 9.1037–41. Warton (1785, p. 198) compares Comus 543–5. Wright (ed. 1877, at 633) quotes Lycidas 145–6.

630 I…blowes]
The metre and the suspected lack of a syllable attract attention (see textual n.). Cuningham (ed. 1905) quotes Ven. 151, Witness this primrose bank whereon I lie in support of Pope’s (ed. 1725) emendation. Malone (ed. 1790) states where is here used as [a] dissyllable; Keightley (1861, p. 264; ed. 1864) so marks it. In accord, Abbott §480 quotes this instance. Delius (ed. 1859) thinks time is disyllabic. Furness (ed. 1895): Let a pause before where take the place of a syllable, as in [378]. Similarly Luce (1935, p. 139 n. 8): Bank…has the value of a dissyllable. Sprague (ed. 1896) suggests making the line a tetrameter, scanning where the wilde as an anapest.

630 banke]
For speculation on how this is to be represented, see n. 676.

630 wilde time]
Beisly (1864, p. 46): Wild thyme (Thymus Serpyllum) grows on dry heaths and commons; it has small leaves, and heads of light purple flowers. The plant has a fragrant aromatic perfume. Ellacombe (1878, pp. 234–5): It is as a bee-plant especially that the Thyme has always been celebrated, quoting Ovid, Fasti 5.271–2. Thyme became proverbial as the symbol of sweetness.…And it was because of its wild Thyme that Mount Hymettus became so celebrated for its honey, quoting Ovid, Met. 15.80. Briggs (1959, pp. 248–9) gives a recipe from Bodleian MS. Ashmole 1406 for an ointment to give sight of the fairies which includes the flowers or toppes of wild time. See OED, Thyme, n. 1a, quoting this instance.

630 blowes]
OED (v.2 1, quoting this instance): To burst into flower; to blossom, bloom. Warren (1983, p. 18), alone: The wild thyme blows fragrance into the air.

631 Oxlips]
OED, quoting this instance: The name of a flowering herb: applied…to a plant intermediate in appearance between the Cowslip (Primula veris) and Primrose (P. vulgaris). Thirlby (MS 1725–33): The cowslip. Steevens (MS 1773–8): The greater cowslip. Beisly (1864, p. 46): Oxlip (Primula elatior)…is a handsome plant like the cowslip, but larger. Hunter (ed. 1870): A kind of primrose. Wright (ed. 1877) quotes Lyte’s Herball (1578; 1595), p[p]. 134[–5]: The Oxelip, or the small kind of white Mulleyn, is very like to the Cowslip aforesaid, sauing that his leaues be greater and larger, and his floures be of a pale or faint yelow colour, almost white and without sauour.

631 nodding]
Johnson (1755, Nod 1; 4): 1. To decline the head.… 4. To be drowsy (OED, ppl. a. 1, 2b). Echoing this Steevens (ed. 1793): I.e. that declines its head, like a drowsy person. Beisly (1864, p. 46): The distinction nodding, signifying bending or hanging down the head, is characteristic of the growth of this flower. Wright (ed. 1877) compares Drayton’s The Quest of Cynthia, 53–4: I ask’d a nodding Violet why, It sadly hung the head, (3:148). Keightley (1861, p. 264), claiming that violets do not nod, transposed ox-lip and violet (see textual n.).

631 Violet]
Halliwell (ed. 1856) quotes Gerard’s description of the violet as a beautifull flower sweetely smelling, of a blew darkish purple (Herball, 1597, p. 699). Gerard also praises violets in terms Oberon might have heeded: Gardens themselues receiue by these the greatest ornament of all, chiefest beautie, and most gallant grace; and the recreation of the minde which is taken heereby, cannot be but verie good and honest: for they admonish & stir vp a man to that which is comely and honest: for flowers through their beautie, varietie of colour, and exquisite forme, do bring to a liberall and gentlemanly minde, the remembraunce of honestie, comelinesse, and all kindes of vertues (p. 698). Ellacombe (1878, pp. 246–8): In all the passages in which Shakespeare names the Violet, he alludes to the purple sweet-scented Violet.…It was…the character of lowliness and sweetness combined that gave charm to the Violet in the eyes of the emblem writers: it was for them the readiest symbol of the meekness of humility.…Violets…always have had their joyful associations as coming to tell that the winter is passing away and brighter days are near…. Yet it is curious to note how…they have been ever associated with death, especially with the death of the young.

631 growes]
Wright (ed. 1877): Attracted into the singular by the nearer subject violet. Deighton (ed. 1891): The verb for the rhyme’s sake being made to agree with the singular noun only. Furness (ed. 1895): Singular…from the image in the mind of one bed of oxlips and violets growing together.

632 Quite…woodbine]
Some find difficulty with a hypermetrical syllable (see textual nn.). Pope’s (ed. 1725) alteration of ouer to o’er indicates the pronunciation needed to preserve the meter. For the softening of the v in over see Abbott §466; Franz §50. A few adopt the anonymous conjecture lush, found by Theobald (MS 1723–33), first reported by Johnson (ed. 1765) who calls it right, and who subsequently (in Steevens, ed. 1773) identifies it as Theobald’s. Ritson (in Steevens, ed. 1793) endorses the emendation, as does Douce (1807, 1:190) who accuses the printers of ignorantly, as well as unharmoniously substituting luscious for the antiquated word lush. The scansions proposed by e.g. Rolfe (ed. 1877), Gould (1884, p. 15), Furness (ed. 1895), Sprague (ed. 1896), and Chambers (ed. 1897) elicit some sympathy for Clark & Wright’s (ed. 1864) action, marking this line with an obelus as corrupt, with no admissible emendation having been proposed (Preface; the same mark is used at 1287, 1744–5, 1855–6, 1889). But Phelps (ed. 1851), whose experience is in the theater rather than the study, is surely right: This passage, which one would imagine sufficiently musical as it stands, has been sadly mangled by the metre-mongers. We have restored the old text.

632 ouercanopi’d]
Schmidt (1875): Covered as with a canopy. The unfamiliarity of the word is attested by the reading ouercanoped in Q2F. OED gives canope as a variant form of Canopy, n., and quotes Q2F as the first instance of Overcanopy.

632 lushious]
Schmidt (1874, Luscious): Sweet, delicious, delightful. OED (a. 1), quoting this instance: Highly pleasant to the taste or smell. For the emendation lush, OED (a.1 1; 2a): 1. Lax, flaccid; soft, tender;…2a. Of plants, esp. of grass: Succulent, and luxuriant in growth. The literary currency of this sense…is due to the recollection of the instance in Shakes. [Tmp. 2.1.53 (727): How lush and lusty the grasse lookes?]. A conjecture of Theobald’s,…lush,…in [MND 632]…is now discredited, but the passage as emended has had many echoes in 19th c. literature, quoting Keats, Shelley, etc.

632 woodbine]
OED (2), quoting this instance: The common honeysuckle, Lonicera Periclymenum, a climbing shrub with pale yellow fragrant flowers. Wright (ed. 1877) says that the herbals of Lyte (1578 [p. 390]) and Gerard (1597 [p. 743]) identify the woodbine and the honeysuckle as the same, but Shakespeare elsewhere [see 1555 and n.] makes woodbine and honeysuckle distinct, and apparently regards the former as the same as the convolvulus or bindweed. Brooks (ed. 1979, p. xxxvi) notes that Spenser links woodbine and eglantine in Amoretti LXXI; the embroidered flowers make a frame which is a metaphor for the sweet prison that his beloved will find marriage to be.

633 muske roses]
OED (Musk-rose), citing this instance: A rambling rose (Rosa moschata), having large fragrant white flowers, in panicled clusters; Etym. So called from its musky odour; cf. F. rose muscade and the botanical name. Ellacombe (1878, p. 197): This Rose (R. moschata) is a native of the north of Africa and of Spain; he quotes Hakluyt who dates its introduction into England after the time of Henry VIII, from Italy. It is highly prized…for the delicate scent of its flowers. Steevens (ed. 1793 at 653) mistakenly believed it unknown to English botanists in Sh.’s day. Webb (1991, p. 150) warns against inappropriate interpretation: The sexual implications of musk (the word itself probably deriving from foreign words meaning testicle, scrotum—OED) are unlikely to contribute to the eroticism of the scene, as the musk rose is of too common ref[erence] for such connotation without some added innuendo. Other instances of the flower are at 653, 1513.

633 Eglantine]
Johnson (1755), quoting this instance: A species of rose; (Rose 7): The sweet briar or eglantine (OED, Eglantine1 1, quoting this instance). Beisly (1864, p. 48): Sweet briar (Rosa rubiginosa), a favourite shrub, cultivated for the sweet fragrance of its leaves; he quotes John Parkinson’s Paradisi in Sole, Paradisus terrestris. Or A Garden of Pleasant Flowers (1629, pp. 418, 420), who says it is armed with the cruellest sharpe and strong thornes, and thicker set than is in any rose. Ellacombe (1878, p. 63): Eglantine…[was] one of the many thorny trees from which the sacred crown of thorns was supposed to be made, quoting from Sir John Mandeville that Jesus was crowned with Eglantine. See Mandeville’s Travels, ed. M. C. Seymour (Oxford, 1967), p. 10, line 14, crouned with eglentier. Sh.’s only other mention of this plant is in Cym. 4.2.223 (2533).

634–7 There…in]
Keightley (1861, p. 264) defends the transposition which most find unnecessary (see textual n.): Surely [636–7] should connect immediately with the description of the bank. See also Cartwright (1866, p. 10).

634 There…night]
Hornby (1992, p. 169) instances the sensuous, hissing alliteration in this line as an example of the rhythms and imagery that are an essential part of the experience of the play, to which the actor must be sensitive.

634 sometime]
Schmidt (1875, 1): From time to time, now and then (OED, adv. 5a, quoting this instance first). But see n. 408.

634 of]
Abbott §176, quoting also Ham. 1.5.60 (745), of the afternoone [Q2; Q1 and F1 have in]: When used with time, signifies during (OED, prep. 52a, quoting this instance). See Franz §516 and Blake 5.4.2 Of (d), both quoting this instance. See also OED, Night, n. 10, quoting this instance.

635 in]
Thirlby (MS 1725–33) conjectures on, and later (MS 1747–53) compares 976, on pressed flowers.

635 with…delight]
Moberly (ed. 1881), questionably: By the delight of her own dances. Verity (ed. 1893): Delightful dances (i.e. of her attendant fairies); an example of hendiadys, the figure of speech whereby one idea is expressed by two nouns or verbs connected by a conjunction.

636 there…skinne]
Grey (1754, 1:56–7): This may allude to Virgil, Æneid 2.471. &c.; in Phaer’s translation, Duke Pyrrhus stands glistring like a serpent shines…That lurking long hath vnder ground in winter cold ben hild, And now his cote of cast all fresh with youth renewd and pride (1584, D2r). For literary references to snakes casting their skins Grey also cites Georgics 3.438–9; Lucretius, De Rerum Natura 3.613–14; Spenser, FQ 4.3.29 [i.e. 23.7–9]. Harting (1871; 1965, p. 15) gives other Shn. instances of sloughing in H5 4.1.23 (1868); TN 3.4.68 (1591) [and 2.5.148–9 (1153–4)]; 2H6 3.1.229 (1531).
Maguin (1988, p. 105): The selective human mind separates the propitious from the baneful and chooses to forget the snake appended to the list of wildflowers or remembers it only on account of its colorful enamel.

636 enammeld]
Schmidt (1874): Glossy and variegated. OED (ppl. a. 2), quoting this instance and TGV 2.7.28 (1003) first: Having naturally a hard polished surface, resembling enamel; but sense 3, Beautified with various colours, seems more appropriate here. Gill (ed. 1981): Smooth and coloured.

637 Weed]
Johnson (1755, 2), quoting this instance: A garment; cloaths; habit; dress. OED (n.2 3) quoting this instance: Trans. and fig. (e.g. our garment of flesh). Halliwell (ed. 1856) quotes from Drayton, The Muses Elizium, Nymphal 8, ll. 77–82, where Tita’s Traine is made Of the cast slough of a Snake (3:311).

638–9 And, with…phantasies]
Anderson (1927, p. 92) points to Oberon’s action here as an example of the idea in Elizabethan psychology that the senses, as porters to the soul, may dominate thought: If the eye errs, the mind is likely to err also; reason and the imagination can judge only of what they receive. Minds swayed by eyes…may indeed be full of turpitude. See also nn. 40, 248. Farrow (ed. 1972): These venomous lines are suddenly shocking after the delicate picture of Titania’s flowery bed. McLuskie (2003, p. 37): He makes no mention of an ass or its sexual potential. His wish is simply that Titania should fall in love with some vile thing [685]. The eventual comic union with Bottom is a fortuitous turn of the plot, the product of Shakespeare’s imagination and not Oberon’s.

638 And…eyes]
Keightley (1861, p. 264) maintains that this line does not connect with what precedes…and that consequently…a line has been lost. For the line which he suggests, see conj., .

638 streake]
Schmidt (1875), quoting this instance only: To overspread, to anoint. OED (v.1 5), quoting this instance last: To rub or smear (a surface) with. Wright (ed. 1877): Stroke, touch gently (OED, v1 1, 7). Deighton (ed. 1891) adds as with a painter’s brush. Farrow (ed. 1972), questionably: Also implies that Titania’s sight will be striped and distorted with evil.

639 hatefull phantasies]
Hunter (ed. 1870): Disgusting or odious fancies. Deighton (ed. 1891): Love-fancies. Brooks (ed. 1979): Figments of imagination. See also nn. 40, 1797.

642 disdainefull]
Johnson (1755), quoting this instance: Contemptuous; haughtily scornful (OED 1). Deighton (ed. 1891): Sc. of her love. Cf. 785.

642–5 annoint…on]
Johnson (ed. 1894): Oberon assumes that only one Athenian can be spending the night in these groves, but the audience knows better. Evans (1960, p. 36) adds: Puck errs because Oberon erred in directing him, and thus, though the ultimate cause is the magical juice of the little western flower, the immediate cause of the comic action involving the lovers is a blunder made because immortal intelligence has fallen, for once, a little short of omniscience. Blits (2003, p. 74): Oberon should know that his description is insufficient, for Demetrius’s first words to Helena spoke of Lysander’s being in this wood ([568–72]). Cf. 1388.

642 annoint]
Johnson (1755, Anoint 1): To rub over with unctuous matter, as oil, or unguents (OED 1, quoting inter alia: 1611 Bible Rev. iii. 18 Anoint thine eyes with eye salue, that thou mayest see.). Cf. 1392 (nointed).

643 espies]
Johnson (1755, 3): To see unexpectedly (OED 2b). Gill (ed. 1981): Looks on.

644–5 man…on]
Cuningham (ed. 1905): The o [in bob] had the sound of the a, and not vice versâ. Cercignani (1981, p. 113) agrees: see n. 1389–90 where the same rhyme occurs. Steevens (ed. 1793), however, attributes the rhyme to the earlier prevalence in England of broad Scotch pronunciation. Singer (ed. 1856) believes: Our ancient poets were not particular in making their rhymes correspond in sound. Verplanck (ed. 1845): A common poetical license. Wright (ed. 1877) believes no inference can be drawn about Sh.’s pronunciation from these lines or 419–20, 1455–6, 1504, 1506, and 2082, all of which he regards as imperfect rhymes.

646 that]
For the omission of so, see n. 333.

647 fond on]
Schmidt (1874, Fond adj. 3): With on = loving (OED, Fond, a. 6). Brooks (ed. 1979): Enamoured of, with compulsive but not (as often) foolish passion. For other meanings of fond see n. 743.

647 on]
Of. See n. 276–7.

648 looke…meete]
For the subjunctive, see n. 71.

648 looke]
See n. 126.

648 ere…crowe]
Thirlby (MS 1747–53) conjectures Cocke-crowe, quoting 1H4 2.1.17–18 (652–3), since the first cocke. Deighton (ed. 1891): In [Ham. 1.1.147 (146)] the cock is spoken of as crowing not long after midnight. Verity (ed. 1893): In [Lr. 3.4.115–16 (1895–6)] the foul fiend comes forth at curfew and walks till the first cock: then fairies and spirits went home. Tannenbaum (1933, p. 113) insists that the time meant by Oberon was midnight, and that we must read ere the first Cock-crow (that is, the first crowing of the cock, not the crowing of the first cock), quoting a poem from Thomas Tusser’s Five Hundred Points of Good Husbandry, [1573, 2nd part, fol. 4], which says in part: Cock croweth at midnight…at three, and an hower ere day. Kittredge (ed. 1939): Before midnight. Phillips (ed. 1969): The supernatural world loses its power at the time when the first cock crows. However, the fairies are not subject to this rule, according to Shakespeare (see 1429–34), so Oberon uses cockcrow as a measure of time only. See OED, Cock, n.1 3.

648 crowe]
A temporal subjunctive: see Franz §643.

649 your…so]
Chambers (ed. 1897) compares Luke 7:8: I say…to my servant, Do this, and he doeth it.

649 shall]
Abbott §315: A mixture of I am bound to and I am sure to…often used in the replies of inferiors to superiors. Kittredge (ed. 1939): Will certainly.

649 Exeunt]
F1’s alteration to Exit may be due to a lack of space in the line, or it may be the not uncommon use of the singular SD for the plural (see the examples in n. 621, wooe). Or, Oberon may remain on stage, invisible, as he does at 565.

650 Enter…traine]
Verity (ed. 1893): The scene is now changed to the locality of the bank just mentioned by Oberon.

651 Roundell…song]
Forey (1998, p. 322) suggests a reminiscence of the Midas episode, where Golding translates the teneris…nymphis (Met. 11.153) to whom Pan played into fayrye elues that dawnced round toogither (11.171; see ).

651 Roundell]
Grey (1754, 1:57): From round comes roundel, and from roundel roundelet. The first the form of the figure, the second the dance in the figure, the last the song or tune to the dance. Anon.; as an example of roundel he (mis)quotes Chaucer, KnT 1529, And song in all the roundell lustily, where the singer is Arcite alone in the Maytime grove (see ), and which OED (9) quotes to illustrate the meaning A rondeau or rondel. Tyrwhitt (in Steevens, ed. 1773, 10:L16r): A circular dance (OED 10, quoting this instance and no other until 1825); he adds: B. Jonson seems to call the rings which such dances are supposed to make in the grass, rondels. Vol. v. Tale of a Tub, p. 23: I’ll have no rondels, I, in the queen’s paths (2.1.53; 3:27). Halliwell (ed. 1856) suggests that Jonson’s preceding line, Dance o’er the fields like faies to church, implies that rondels refers to the dances themselves. Thiselton (1903, p. 34) believes that since dancing was sometimes associated with rondels, this led to a confusion of meaning between roundell (originally, a particular kind of song) and a round (a particular kind of dance). Baskervill (1929, p. 10): It is important to keep constantly in mind the conventionality of dance with song.… This conventional union explains the large number of terms that were used interchangeably for song and for dance. Carol, round or roundel, and ballet or ballad came out of the Middle Ages, and they remained in use for both song and dance. See also n. 659–76.

652 for…hence]
Unable to accept that so many tasks could be achieved so quickly, some critics offer emendations and logical interpretations (see textual n. and conj., ). Theobald (ed. 1733): But the Queen sets them Work, that is to keep them employ’d for the Remainder of the Night: The Poet, undoubtedly, intended her to say, Dance your Round, and sing your Song, and then instantly (before the third part of a Minute) begone to your respective Duties. Warburton (ed. 1747): We should read third part of the midnight. The common reading is nonsense. Possibly Shakespear might have used the French word Minuit. Heath (1765, p. 51), reading in for for: That is, after your song and dance ended, vanish in the third part of a minute, and leave me to my rest. Capell (1783, 2.3:106–7): The queen’s command is expressive of the short time in which she should be asleep after their song and dance; that absence is enjoin’d, but ’till she were asleep; after which, they might return if they pleas’d, and follow the tasks she set them even about her cradle as Puck calls it, her sleep’s soundness would not be disturb’d by them: and this hint of it’s soundness is not unnecessary; for we see presently it is not broke by the persons that enter next, nor by the Clowns ’till Bottom brays-out his song. Kinnear (1883, p. 88), reading fly for for: i.e. take a short flight hence, measuring distance by time; so wingless bipeds say, a five minute’s walk.
Most find the expression appropriate to the fairies; e.g. Steevens (ed. 1773): Shakespeare, throughout the play, has preserved the proportions of other things in respect of these tiny beings; compared with whose size a cowslip might be tall, and to whose powers of execution, a minute might be equivalent to an age. Halliwell (ed. 1856): This quaint subdivision of time exactly suits the character of the fairy speaker, and her diminutive world. But the usage is not confined to fairies: Halliwell quotes In the forth part of a poore short minute from The Meeting of Gallants at an Ordinarie, 1604, where Famine is claiming that a rapid demise through Warre or Pestilence is Not worthy to be named a torturing Death (Plague Pamphlets, ed. F. P. Wilson, 1925, p. 111), and the command of present death is the context for the First Officer’s response in The Revenger’s Tragedy: We’ll not delay The third part of a minute (3.3.16–17, ed. Foakes, 1966, pp. 62–3).

653 kill…buds]
Warton (1785, pp. 9–10) comments on Milton, Lycidas 45: Shakespeare is fond of this image, who, from frequent repetition, seems to have suggested it to Milton; he gives (as Caldecott says in an MS note in the BL copy C.45.e.14) a round dozen of Cankers, ranging from Son. 70 through 1H6 2.4.68–71 (998–1001) and TGV 1.1.42–8 (47–52) to the present instance.

653 cankers]
Halliwell (ed. 1856): Caterpillars. OED (4), quoting this instance, adds or any insect larva, which destroys the buds and leaves of plants.

653 musk rose]
See n. 633.

654 Some…wings]
Guthrie (ed. 1954, p. 31), adducing this command, and the proposed treatment of the bees and butterflies at 986–91: The play is not well served if the ferocity, the greed, the sensuality and mischievous silliness of the Fairies is played down in favour of daintiness and respectability. See also .

654 Some warre]
Delius (ed. 1859): Scil. some, to war; but Wright (ed. 1877) disagrees: It seems rather that war is imperative, let some war &c.

654 Reremise]
Johnson (1755, Rearmouse), quoting this instance: The leather-winged bat. OED, quoting this instance: Now only arch. or dial. Sh. may have been influenced in choosing this name here only for the animal which elsewhere he calls a bat by Golding’s use of it in describing the fate of the daughters of Minyas who tell the tale of Pyramus and Thisbe (4.513; see ); Golding’s line is one of several 16th- and 17th-c. usages instanced by Steevens (ed. 1793). Littledale (1916, p. 517): The nocturnal Bat, from its ambiguity of structure, having wings like a bird and teeth like a mouse, was associated with works of darkness and things out of nature’s course.

654 for]
Schmidt (1874, 8), quoting 987 and 1032: In order to obtain (OED, prep. 9a).

654 lethren]
Variant spelling of leathern, OED (2): Leather-like. Said esp. of the bat’s wings.

655 Elues]
See n. 387.

655 keepe backe]
OED (Keep, v. 47): To restrain; to hold back forcibly.

656–7 The…spirits]
Kenrick (1765, pp. 14–15) compares Ariel’s song Tmp. 5.1.90 (2047). Lewes (MS 1842–78): What a picture! I fancy I see the Owl with his imperturbable anonymous gravity & staring eyes, gazing on these fairy pranks!

656 clamorous]
OED (2): Uttering loud and persistent cries. Walker (1860, 1:156–7): In many places it evidently signifies wailing; he cites this line and Lr. 4.3.31 (2347+32), 5.3.209 (3168+4), and Mac. 2.3.59–60 (809); and quotes Virgil, Aeneid 4.462–3.

656 nightly]
Johnson (1755): 2. Every night (OED 1, quoting this instance); or possibly 1. By night (OED 2).

656 hootes]
OED (3a): Applied to the cry of some birds, spec. of the owl. Tiessen (1877, p. 4) (Ger. ausschelten): Berate, scold (cf. OED 2a). Harting (1871; 1965, p. 89) distinguishes barn or screech-owls, which normally do not hoot, from the hooting brown owls. It seems unlikely that Sh. intended a distinction. See n. 2159 (scriech-owle).

657 queint]
Johnson (1755, Quaint [coint Fr.], 3): Neat, pretty (OED 4 Obs.). Schmidt (1875) quotes also Tmp. 1.2.317 (454), my queint Ariel. Possibly with some sense of OED (7): Strange, unusual, unfamiliar, odd, curious. Obs.; cf. Holland (ed. 1994): Also beautiful, pretty, dainty (OED Ia. A.1.8 and A.1.4) and spruce, brisk (Cotgrave). The Clarkes (ed. 1864): Delicately graceful, prettily fantastic. Wright (1968, p. 33) suggests an obscene pun (see OED, n.1), but Sh. never uses nor refers to the noun. See also n. 474.

657 spirits: Sing]
Emendations (see textual n.) are rejected by Edwards (1758, pp. 33–4; 1765, pp. 71–2): The persons of the fairies seem a properer object of wonder to the owl, than their sports; for which reason, as well as for the sake of the measure, the old reading, spirits, is preferable. If Mr. Warburton stole this emendation from Sir Thomas Hanmer,…he should have done him the justice to take the whole of it; and so have preserved the measure of the verse. Steevens (ed. 1773, 10:Oo8v), acting on the suggestion of Dr. Farmer (see Steevens, MS 1773–8 and ed. 1778), supports the interpretation but not the emendation: A spirit was sometimes used for a sport. In Dekkar’s play, If it be not good, the Devil is in it, the King of Naples says to the devil Ruffman, disguised in the character of Shalcan, Now Shalcan, some new spirit? Ruff. A thousand wenches stark-naked to play at leap-frog. Omnes. O rare sight! [3.3.166–7; 3:175].

657 a sleepe]
See n. 555.

658 to…offices]
Brooks (ed. 1979): Omitting, as often, the vb. of motion.

658 offices]
Rolfe (ed. 1877): Duties, employments; as often (OED 2b).

659–76 Fairies…Centinell]
After the heading, Q1 indents these lines; F1 indents and italicizes. Debate turns on who sings or speaks which lines (see textual n.), how song and dance are combined, and whether 675–6 are sung or spoken. Capell (1783, 2.3:106): The slovenly form of printing this song, has been—with Fairies sing at the head of it, no appearance of Chorus, and the couplet that dismisses the train made a part of the song; he assigns 660–3 to the First Fairy, 664–9 and the repetition indicated by 674 to the Chorus, 670–3 to the Second Fairy, and 675–6 to the First Fairy, spoken not sung. He adds: If the Chorus be further look’d into, and the performers consider’d, it will at least be probable that we see the roundel in that, being danc’d-to as well as sung; a newly recover’d reading in the second line of it [see n. 665] strengthens the probability. Noble (1923, pp. 57–8), following Q1 more closely, assigns the first stanza and refrain (660–69) to all the fairies, the second stanza (670–3) solo to the First Fairy, the fairies taking up the refrain as before, and the final couplet to the Second Fairy, as do most modern editors. He seems influenced by F1 in asserting that the last although not strictly part of the song ought to be sung. Furness (ed. 1895) disagrees with F1: Capell was the first to indicate that these two lines are not a part of the song; he has been followed, of course, by all the editors since his day, but Long (1955, p. 85), drawing an analogy with the coda of modern musical compositions, and Foakes (ed. 1984), on metrical grounds, entertain the possibility of the couplet being sung. Farzààd (1946, pp. 57–9), contrary to Capell, believes that the two quatrains make up the roundel, and that the repeated lullaby is the song. See also n. 1555–7. Long (1955, p. 85): The fairy attendants, probably about six in number, apparently join hands and dance around Titania as they sing the song. The song itself is an ayre in the form of a lullaby, a type of song quite popular during the period. The language, the subject-matter, and the eleven-line structure of the roundel all suggest an art song, as opposed to a folk form. Seng (1967, p. 31): The dance could have preceded the song rather than accompanying it. On musical settings, see .
Various analogues have been suggested. Upton (1748, pp. 412–13) parallels the dismissal of the animal kingdom with Milton’s description of Eve’s bower (PL 4.703–4) and Callimachus’s of Rhea’s childbed (Hymn I, 11–13). Wilson (1948, p. 40) compares the attendance of spirits on Psyche in Apuleius’s Golden Ass (see ). Brooks (ed. 1979): Cf., in reverse, the Pages’ song to awake Sir Thopas in Lyly, Endymion III. iii. 120, 122 f. Let Adders hiss[e] in’s eare, / Else Eare-wigs wriggle there [3:46].
General comments include: Drake (1817, 2:345): The song is…in character, as it forbids, in admirable adherence to poetical truth and consistency, the approach of every insect or reptile, that might be deemed likely to annoy the repose of such a delicate and diminutive being. Hülsmann (1856, p. 130) exclaims How lyrical, how simple, how childlike! (Ger.). Noble (1923, p. 53): The reader…will observe how easily and naturally the song comes into being, how it relieves from awkwardness and makes interesting Titania’s retirement, how it imparts to the whole a fairy-like atmosphere, how perfectly it is ended and the continuation provided for, and how it leads up to and facilitates Oberon’s little plot. Seng (1967, p. 32): This song is more than a lullaby, or even a magic lullaby; it is a charm to ward off evils.…Yet there is a delightful irony in the fact that…it does not preserve her from the preternatural magic of Oberon and his puck. Phillips (ed. 1969): Many of the creatures named were thought to be poisonous, and some were associated with witchcraft. The stage is being set for something sinister (Oberon’s magic). Riemer (1980, pp. 69–70): The songs of the attendant fairies avoid the pretty and the sentimental; they establish a familiarity with the natural world which recognizes its inherent threats.

660 spotted]
Hunt (1992, p. 222): The adjective spotted poetically links the snake with Demetrius, this spotted and inconstant man [119]. Cutts (1968, p. 52) finds these snakes metaphorically realized in Hermia’s dream (800–5) and in her accusation of Demetrius as a serpent (1095–6). See nn. 119, 800–5, 1093–5.

660 double tongue]
Johnson (1755) glosses double-tongued as: Deceitful; giving contrary accounts of the same thing. Steevens (ed. 1793) compares doubler tongue (1095) and cloven tongues (Tmp. 2.2.13 [1052]): By both these terms, I suppose, our author means—forked.

661 Thorny]
Deighton (ed. 1891): With spines which they erect at will.

661 Hedgehogges]
Johnson (1755, 1): An animal set with prickles, like thorns in an hedge. Dent (1972, p. 77) classes Hedgehogs with things of evil, witches’ familiars, and compares Caliban’s complaint that Prospero’s spirits torment him in the form of hedgehogs (Tmp. 2.2.10–12 [1049–51]).

662 Newts and blindewormes]
Steevens (ed. 1793): The newt is the eft, the blind-worm is the…slow-worm. They are both ingredients in the cauldron of Macbeth [4.1.14 (1541), 4.116 (1543)]. Halliwell (ed. 1856) quotes Topsell’s Historie of Serpents (1608, pp. 213, 240) for the belief that both were poisonous if provoked. Irving & Marshall (ed. 1888): It is impossible to imagine two animals more harmless than the two here named.…This ridiculous belief that the blind-worm is venomous still survives among the country people in most parts of England. But Johnson (1755), quoting this instance: A small viper, the least of our English serpents, but venemous. Also on the blindworm, Anon. (ed. 1887): Harmless, and…not a snake;…it has a pair of bright eyes. Kittredge & Ribner (ed. 1966): Actually a kind of legless lizard. OED: A reptile (anguis fragilis) also called Slow-worm. (Formerly applied also to the Adder.)

664 Philomele]
OED: A poetic name for the nightingale. Harting (1871; 1965, pp. 123–8) expatiates on the sweetness of the bird’s song. He notes that it is always referred to by poets as female even though the male bird only is the songster, probably because of the story of Philomela told by Ovid, Met. 6:424–674 (Golding 6.540–853; see ). He cites Sh.’s frequent references to the bird’s sweet singing, and use of the name Philomel, particularly in allusion to the tale of her rape and transformation in Tit. He gives several quotations that emphasize connections with night and melancholy; e.g. Luc. 1079–80, 1128–41; PP 20.9–20; Milton’s Il Penseroso 56–62. Grossman (1989/90, pp. 240–1): She is invoked to guard the body, the domain of her peculiar care—the night body of the lover; he compares the reference to the nightingale in Rom., quoting 3.5.1–5 (2033–7). Cf. n. 345.

664–5 melody…Lullaby]
On the pronunciation and rhyme, see Cercignani (1981, p. 304), who gives this pairing to illustrate the use of late Middle English ɚi, in the ending -y representing AN, OF -i[e]; cf. n. 200–1.

664 melody]
OED (1a), quoting this instance and Tit. 2.3.12 (747): Sweet music, whether vocal or instrumental; beautiful arrangement of musical sounds.

665 Sing in]
Although apparently an uncomplicated invitation to the nightingale to sing with the fairies, some editors have found the phrase unsatisfactory; see textual n. Andrews (ed. 1993), comparing Mac. 4.1.41–3 (1569–71): Literally, in-chant.

665 our]
Capell (ed. 1768) was the first to restore the Q reading, finding in it support for his disposition of the lines of the song (see n. 659–76). Rann (ed. 1787) by reading her (see textual n.) attributes the lullaby to the nightingale rather than the fairies.

667–8 Neuer…Come]
Deighton (ed. 1891): May it never come; let it never come.

667 Neuer…charme]
Gould (1887, p. 68): The line that rhymes with charme is omitted in all editions. For rearrangement of the line to achieve a rhyming couplet, see textual n.

668 nigh]
The original positive form, still common in Sh.’s time, of which near was the comparative; see Franz §218, Blake 3.2.3.4(c).

670 Weauing Spiders]
Halliwell (ed. 1856): The common spider which constructs its nets in the open air, on hedges, &c. Spiders are furnished with a poisonous fluid, conveyed in their fangs, but its effects are nearly innocuous in the species found in this country. Even in Shakespeare’s time, the old notion of the English spider being venomous was doubted by the naturalists of the day; to that effect he quotes Topsell, The Historie of Serpents (1608, p. 272), but illustrates the more usual view of the spider by quoting Robert Cawdray’s A Treasurie or Storehouse of Similies (1600, p. 839): The Spider gathereth poyson to the same flowers, that the Bee gathereth Honie. Phipson (1883, p. 429): The belief in the spider’s venomous properties was at this time universal. At the trial of the Countess of Essex for the murder of Sir Thomas Overbury, it came out, in course of the examination, that the lady had procured from one of the witnesses seven large spiders, as the strongest poison that could be obtained; she also quotes (p. 428) Stephen Batman’s uncomplimentary suggestion: It hath beene reported, that in Ireland be many spiders, and some verye great, and that being eaten of the Irishmen, have not performed any shewe of venime: it may be that the greater poyson subdueth the lesse [Batman vppon Bartholome, 1582, 18.11, fol. 347r]. Furness (ed. 1895): It is not necessary to suppose that any deadly or even venomous qualities are here attributed to spiders,…It is enough that they are repulsive. Cf. R2 3.2.14–15 (1374–5); WT 2.1.39–41 (636–8).

671 long legd]
OED (a), quoting this instance first: Having long legs: used spec. in the names of some animals.

671 Spinners]
Johnson (1755, 2), quoting this instance: A garden spider with long jointed legs (OED 1a). Brooks (ed. 1979) rejects the argument of Cairncross (1975, p. 166) that the creature referred to is the crane-fly, or daddy-long-legs: In MND the association of spinning and weaving seems to point to the spinning-and-weaving species.

673 Worme]
OED (2a): Any animal that creeps or crawls; a reptile; an insect. Obs. Johnson (1755): 1. A small harmless serpent that lives in the earth; 2. A poisonous serpent. Possibly the more innocuous creature is intended here, but cf. 1094.

673 offence]
OED (4a): Hurt, harm. Obs.

676 aloofe]
Johnson (1755, 1): At a distance.…It generally implies a small distance, such as is within view or observation (OED 3).

676 Centinell]
Obs. form of Sentinel. OED (1b), quoting this instance first: trans. and fig. One who or something which keeps guard like a military sentinel. Parsons (1953, p. 67), finding it strange that the guard apparently fails, suggests a stage direction Sentinel screams and goes to inform the other fairies of what Oberon has done would relieve her of the imputation of neglect of duty. See conj., , and textual n..
Wells (ed. 1967): There is no break in the action between Titania’s falling asleep and her awakening by Bottom at [946]. She must apparently remain on stage throughout this time. However, it is not necessary for her to be visible to the audience. It is possible that on the Elizabethan stage she occupied a recess that could be curtained off at the end of Oberon’s spell [685], and that the curtain was drawn to reveal her during the first verse of Bottom’s song [942]. Foakes (ed. 1984): It is equally possible that she remained in full view of the audience, but unnoticed by the lovers or the mechanicals, especially if there was no break between Acts 2 and 3.…This indeed would fit in with the play’s concern with illusion and theatricality. Titania’s bower, canopied with flowers, may have been suggested by hangings, and the bank [630] by a property such as was listed for the Admiral’s Men in 1598 as ij mose [i.e. moss] banckes (Henslowe’s Diary, p. 320). See also n. 813.

677 Enter Oberon]
For suggested stage business, see textual n..

678–85 What…neere]
Verity (ed. 1893): Short trochaic lines have a dainty, tripping rhythm most appropriate to dainty fairy beings. Note how often, for this reason, Shakespeare makes his fairies speak in trochaic verse. See especially [2154–222]. Arnold (1911, pp. 74–6) cites this speech, and 723–36 and 1497–1506, as examples of soliloquies used to indicate sleep by apostrophizing the sleeper; he notes that the soliloquy over the sleeper is a common device, citing Oberon’s grotesque spell over Titania as well as Cym. 2.2.14–23 (921–30), JC 2.1.229–33 (868–72) and 4.3.267–72 (2279–84); he notes Lyly’s Endimion has an apostrophizing incantation [3.3.109–27; 3:45–6], a trick frequently repeated in [MND]. See also n. 659–76.

679 Doe…take]
Duffy (1972, p. 59): The behaviour of the Green Knight, [of] the King of Anawn and of Oberon in [MND] shows a consistent tradition: the willingness of the king of fayrie, for that is who they all are, to offer his wife to another. Blits (2003, p. 76), understanding take to mean mistake (OED 48) and reading true loue as adj. and noun rather than a compound noun (OED, True-love, 2: A sweetheart, beloved.): Oberon distinguishes between love-juice love and true love.

680 languish]
Schmidt (1874, 2): To long without gratification; to pine away for love (OED 3, quoting this instance).

681–3 Be…appeare]
Even if it should be any one of these animals which appears before your eyes. On Be it that introducing a conditional clause, see Blake 5.3.2.5. To would more normally be found than in for the expression appear to the eye but cf. OED, Eye, v.1 8: Point of view, estimation, judgement and 4d: In one’s (mind’s) eye: in one’s mental view, in contemplation.

681–2 Be…haire]
Irving & Marshall (ed. 1888): Sh. did not think it necessary to be particular about the geographical distribution of the animals he introduced in connection with his fairies. Wentersdorf (1983, pp. 350–1) The animals named are known for their wild and dangerous natures; but Oberon’s reference to them as vile points to another quality they have in common—their lustfulness.…The ounce, better known as the lynx, is named by Virgil in a list of sexually vigorous animals including the bear and the boar (Georgics III.242–68); lynxes drew the chariot of the amorous and savage Dionysus-Bacchus (cf. Propertius, Elegy III.xvii.1–8). The cat, which in Oberon’s speech signifies a wildcat or catamount, was a term for a vigorous lover (cf. John Ford’s play The Fancies Chaste and Noble II.ii [ed. Hart, 1985, p. 119]). And the pard, a panther or leopard, is another symbol of unrestrained sexuality: like the lynx, it is associated with and hence a sign of Dionysus-Bacchus (cf. Ovid, [Met. 3.669]); Ripa describes the picture of a beautiful woman dressed in a pard’s skin, with a panther at her side, as one of the emblems illustrating the motif Libidine [Iconologia (Padua, 1611), pp. 313–14]. du Bartas’s Nimrod tests his strength against Boare, Libbard, Lyon, Tiger, Ounce, or Beare (The Divine Weeks & Works, tr. Sylvester, ed. S. Snyder, 1979, 1:423). Calderwood (1991, p. 420): The creatures [Oberon] cites are all noted for their ferocity and hence would be the most likely to repel, not invite, sexual overtures. See also n. 558–9.
Cheney (1955, p. 2188): Since Elizabeth had called her former suitors by animal names, there is a possibility that they are alluded to in the animals of Oberon’s charm.

681 Ounce]
OED (n.2, 1), quoting this instance: A name originally given to the common lynx,…From 16th c. applied to various other small or moderate-sized feline beasts, vaguely identified. Johnson (1755): A lynx; a panther. Idem (in Steevens, ed. 1773): The ounce is a small tiger, or tiger-cat.

681 Catte]
Wright (ed. 1877): Cat must here be the wild cat. Verity (ed. 1893) compares Tmp. 4.1.261 (1938).

681–5 Beare…neere]
Ness (1941, p. 129): Bear, hair, appear, dear, and near were probably pronounced alike. Cf. [1812–13]. See Cercignani (1981, pp. 147–8).

682 Boare…haire]
D[avenport] (1949, p. 525) finds it fitting, in a play full of ironic reversals, that as Circe, called by Ovid (but not Golding) Titania (Met. 14.382, 438), turns Macareus into a boar rough with bristled heare by using the iewce of certaine herbes (Golding 14.323, 318; see ), so Titania, her eyes charmed by the juice of love-in-idleness, should be in danger of becoming enamored of a similar beast. Cf. also the Calydonian bristled Bore (Golding 8.502; see ).

684 it…deare]
Deighton (ed. 1891): It is the object with which you shall fall in love.

684 deare]
Schmidt (1874, 4): As a subst., = lover, sweetheart (OED, n.2).

685 Wake…neere]
Generosa (1945, p. 200) compares Venus’s vengeful instruction to Cupid to make Psyche fall in love with the most miserablest creature liuinge in Apuleius’s Golden Ass (see ). Phillips (ed. 1969): There is real venom in this line. Oberon has the element of fairy nature which produces acts of kindness towards human beings; he has the malicious element, too. See also nn. 638–9, 954.
The need to repair QqFf’s failure to mark Oberon’s exit was recognized early and followed unanimously (see textual n.); Oberon is not present when Robin mistakes Lysander for Demetrius.

685 vile]
See n. 246.

687–717 Faire…prest]
Chambers (ed. 1897): There is a contrast between the maidenliness of Hermia in this scene and the somewhat on-coming disposition of Helena in [567–623]. Cf. n. 709–12, Garber.

687–90 Faire…day]
Verity (ed. 1912) sees the alternate rhyme forming a quatrain as a sure sign of early work. Cf. n. 1147–1323. On the use of rhyme in MND see also .

687–8 you…way]
Brooks (ed. 1979): In Huon…all who attempt the route through Oberon’s wood are bound to lose themselves (see ). Holland (ed. 1994): Angela Carter notes that the wood is, of course, nowhere near Athens;…[it] is really located somewhere in the English midlands; she distinguishes between the English wood and the dark necromantic forest. Hermia and Lysander may be lost but it is only likely to be a temporary problem for, as Carter suggests, an English wood, however marvellous, however metamorphic, cannot, by definition, be trackless, although it may well be formidably labyrinthine. Yet there is always a way out of a maze, and even if you cannot find it for a while, you know that it is there (Overture and Incidental Music for A Midsummer Night’s Dream [1982; in Saints and Strangers, 1986, pp. 87–8]).

687 you fainte]
Deighton (ed. 1891): You have become faint.

687 with]
Abbott §193: Often used to express the juxtaposition of cause and effect. See Franz §535d.

687–8 wandring…way]
Blits (2003, p. 77): Neither of them ever mentions their original destination again.

688 troth]
Wright (ed. 1877): Truth.

688 forgot]
On the curtailed form of the past participle, see Abbott §343, Franz §168. OED calls this form of the pa. ppl. arch. and poet. See also 1228, 1975.

689 rest vs]
OED (Rest, v.1 II. refl. 7a): To give (oneself) rest or repose. In early use with simple reflexive pronoun; in later use usually with self. See Franz §307; Abbott §223. See 1462, 1495.

690 And…day]
West (1865, pp. 9–10, 21) compares Sidney’s Arcadia, And rest their wearied bodies; till, the next day bestowing the cheerful use of the light upon them, Daiphantus…spake unto them (ed. Evans, 1977, p. 101), and Me seems I feel the comfort of the morning (ed. cit., p. 414). Thiselton (1903, p. 35), citing Aeschylus’ Agamemnon 264–5 and Ps. 30:5: The idea which recurs in this play is a very ancient one. Cf. 1480.

691 Bet it]
All editors correct Q1’s error (see textual n.). Thiselton (1903, pp. 35–6) postulates a misreading of t for e; ie bee. Wilson (ed. 1924): The compositor has set up the contracted form and then expanded it! Brooks (ed. 1979): Q1’s intrusive t came from comfor(t) in [690]; (see textual n.).

693–712 One turfe…distant]
The Sokols (2000, p. 295) suggest that the two lovers have different views of their legal marital status, depending on whether the private exchange of vows was considered sufficient, or whether a public ceremony was required: Hermia denies Lysander’s wish to sleep by her side, despite his claim [in 701–2]. So, as far as Hermia was concerned, but not Lysander, betrothal is not marriage, which was the position of the church, if not of church law; the same authors add (2002, p. 258): To translate this into terms that emphasize the paradox, Lysander correctly asserts that they are married, while Hermia correctly implies that they have not yet had their marriage.

693–4 One turfe…troth]
Rudd (1979, p. 188): This conceit, which is elaborated in the speech that follows, recalls the one/two combination found in Ovid: una duos inquit nox perdet amantes (108); nunc tegis unius, mox es tectura duorum (159); quodque rogis superest, una requiescit in urna. (166). Cf. Golding, 4.133, 192–3, 201; see . Cf. also Ariadne’s address to her deserted bed in Chaucer’s LGW 2211–12; see .

694 One…troth]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Since there is but one heart between us (i.e. as we are one in heart), one bed will serve for us to lie upon; though there are two bosoms, there is but one faith between us (i.e. that which we have pledged to each other). Furness (ed. 1895): After this line, in Garrick’s Version, the lovers sing a duet. It is scarcely worth while to cumber these pages with the words either of this song or of the fifteen others scattered through the rest of the play; cf. . Traub (1992, pp. 157–8) compares this seductive come-on with Helena’s evocation of the love she shared with Hermia (1230–9), in that both show the tension between unity and duality, merger and separation, oneness and twoness.

694, 702 troth]
Hassel (2005, p. 362): Pledge or promise.…Engagement to be married (OED n. 2).

695–714 Nay…say I]
Gentleman (ed. 1774): The passages here marked [this and 729–31] should be omitted, for though founded in delicacy, they may raise warm ideas. Grinfield (MS 1834–63): Wantons \or prudes/ may laugh at this, but it shows the true beauty of Shakspeare’s mind, and his fine perception of woman’s modesty. Gervinus (1849; tr. 1863, 1:293): By the side of the intoxication of fickle sensual love, he has approvingly laid stress on the contrast of maidenly discretion.

695 Nay]
See n. 309.

695 god]
OED: Obs. form of GOOD.

696 Ly…yet]
Thirlby (MS 1747–53), jestingly: Till Marriage think you?

696 yet]
OED (I. 1b): Used to strengthen a comparative: now more commonly expressed by EVEN (adv. 9e) or STILL (adv. 5a). But Schmidt (1875, 7): At least; though nothing else.

697–8 O…conference]
Warburton (in Theobald, ed. 1733), arguing for the transposal of the last word in each line (see textual n.), interpreted: Judge of my Meaning by the Drift of the other part of my Discourse; and let That interpret This; and The Innocence of your Love may teach you to discover mine. Heath (1765, pp. 51–2) and Johnson (ed. 1765) both object to the alteration, the latter interpreting: Understand the meaning of my innocence, or my innocent meaning: Let no suspicion of ill enter thy mind;…In the conversation of those who are assured of each other’s kindness not suspicion but Love takes the meaning. No malevolent interpretation is to be made, but all is to be received in the sense which love can find and which love can dictate. Henley (in Steevens, ed. 1793): There is no occasion for alteration. The idea is exactly similar to that of St. Paul: Love thinketh no evil [1 Cor. 13:5]. Deighton (ed. 1891): In talk between those who love, love catches the meaning intended by love; where two mutually love, each readily understands the thoughts of the other without the need of gloss or commentary on the words used. Page (ed. 1897) compares 1 John 4:18: There is no fear in love; but perfect love casteth out fear.

698 takes]
Tyrwhitt (1766, p. 34) suggests take (see textual n.): That is, Let love take the meaning.

699 it]
Q1’s unique reading could refer to Loue or to heart, but the sense is strained, and the sound of it knit is ugly. The t for s may result from foul case. All editors follow Q2, without comment.

699 knit]
See n. 183 (knitteth). For the alternate form of the participle with -ed omitted, see Franz §159; Abbott §342.

700 one heart]
Cf. the merchant’s ironic description of the marital ideal in Chaucer, MerT 1336; see .

700 wee can]
White (ed. 1858) defends the F reading can you (see textual n.): Lysander, in his attempt to meet the objections which Hermia makes to his proposition, may, with much more propriety and effect, attribute to his mistress alone the desire of separating him from her, than to make himself a party to such an endeavor.

700 it]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Used indefinitely, the circumstance, the fact.

701 interchained]
Johnson (1755), quoting this instance: To chain; to link together. OED, giving only two other instances, from the early 17th c., notes: Obs. rare. trans. Most editors prefer Q to F’s interchanged (see textual n.), Halliwell (ed. 1856) calling Q far more forcible and pertinent, but White (ed. 1858) argues for F: [Q] conveying the comparatively common place thought, that the lovers’ hearts were bound together; [F] representing them as having been given each to the other as the most solemn instruments are made, interchangeably. Irving & Marshall (ed. 1888) defend Q on the grounds that it is more consonant in sense with 699 knit, and, dubiously, that bosom…seems never to be used for the affections themselves. Sprague (ed. 1896): Bosoms interchained is at best a repetition and enfeeblement of an idea already sufficiently expressed; but bosoms interchanged adds a new and stronger one. Note also the result of the knitting: the two hearts became one [700]; but observe the result of the interchanging, the two bosoms still remain two [702].…Finally, eye hath not seen nor imagination conceived the modus operandi of chaining two bosoms together. Thiselton (1903, p. 36) provides an elaboration of Sprague’s argument.

702 So…troth]
Moberly (ed. 1881): Our hearts are interchained with such an oath, that it makes them as much one as their pledge is, seems to be the thought.

703 bed-roome]
OED (1), quoting only this instance: Room in bed, sleeping room or space. rare. Page (ed. 1897) compares 429. Fairchild (1937, p. 17): Bedroom, in the modern sense, does not occur in Shakespeare.…Bedchamber occurs several times.

704 lying…lye]
Delius (ed. 1859) points out the pun on speaking false and lying down. Homan (ed. 1970): a) prevaricate b) sexual pun. Farrell (1975, p. 104): Lysander confutes himself. Word play exposes lying to mean not simply innocent sleep at his beloved’s side, but also love-making and, as in Sonnet 138 [l. 13], prevarication.

705 Lysander…prettily]
Singer (ed. 1856) notes that the first and last syllables continue Lysander’s pun on lie, which is picked up again by Hermia in 707.

705 riddles]
OED (1), quoting this instance: To speak in riddles, or enigmatically. Obs.

705 prettily]
OED (1), quoting this instance: In a cunning or clever manner; ingeniously, neatly. Obs.

706 much…pride]
Delius (ed. 1859), construing the combination of manner and pride as equalling want of manners, glosses Shame on [pfui über] my impoliteness and haughtiness. (Ger.) Deighton (ed. 1891): I am not so ill-mannered and arrogant as to mean by what I said that Lysander was false.

706 beshrewe]
Tollet (MS 1740–79): It seems to be an imprecation or wish of such evil to one as the venomous biting of the shrew mouse. Ayscough (ed. 1784): Beshrew means the same as if she had said, Now ill befal my manners, &c. Johnson (1755, 2), quoting this instance: To happen ill to; he also inconclusively discusses the etymology. Schmidt (1874): Originally a mild, indeed very mild, form of imprecation.…Sometimes it is so far from implying a curse as to be uttered coaxingly, nay even with some tenderness (OED 3b). Kittredge (ed. 1939): A mild and altogether ladylike word. See also 2088.

706 manners]
Johnson (1755, 9): Manners in the plural. General way of life; morals; habits (OED, Manner, n.1 4. collect. pl. a). Kittredge (ed. 1939): My regard for mere conventional propriety.

708–9 But…modesty]
Doran (ed. 1959): An appeal to Lysander as a civilized human being, who is not a beast or a savage; the implication is in gentle, courtesy, and human. Kittredge & Ribner (ed. 1966): Out of regard for decorous modesty. Blits (2003, p. 78), interpreting Hermia’s use of modesty at 69 as a lack of presumption or impudence and Demetrius’s at 593 as mere reputation, argues that here modesty combines the morality of a fixed principle and the sweetness of love. It unites reason and passion. It is also not limited to women.

708 friend]
The Clarkes (ed. 1864): Often used for lover (OED 4). See also 712.

709–12 Ly…friend:]
The chief effect of the most common editorial changes in punctuation (see textual n.) is to set off two parallel commands, Ly further off and So farre be distant, softened by following gentle statements. Commentators have been troubled by syntax as well as punctuation. Douce (1807, 1:190): That is, let there be such separation, &c. A comma should be placed after modesty. Wright (ed. 1877): The sense is clear though the syntax is imperfect. Delius connects as may well be said with in human modesty, but the construction is rather in human modesty (let there be) such separation &c., and So far be distant is merely a repetition of the same thing. Deighton (ed. 1891): There seems to be a confusion of constructions between let there be such a distance between us as may be justly said is becoming between a virtuous, etc., and be so far distant from me as it may be justly said is becoming between, etc. Knowles (privately): Be is a simple optative subjunctive. Such separation, to be properly virtuous, should be this far in extent; but see n. 712. Watt (ed. 1906): As Hermia speaks she moves away from Lysander, and as there is action there is no need of syntax; in the headnote to the scene (p. 86) he observes: Hermia’s modesty in lying down so far from her lover had two disastrous results for her: in the first place, it confirmed Puck in the belief that Hermia and Lysander were the couple referred to by Oberon; and, secondly, it made it possible for Helena to rouse Lysander without seeing Hermia. Garber (1981, p. 134): Hermia’s prudery is in striking contrast to the active and energetic quest for love of Helena. Comtois (1985, p. 18), comparing these lines with 729–30: The comedy of this scene comes from juxtaposing human pseudo-morality with fairy amorality. Blits (2003, p. 78): Hermia seems to live up to her name. Hermes, after whom she is named, is the god both of boundaries and of the transgression of boundaries. He both respects and crosses them.

709 humane]
OED (1a): Gentle or kindly in demeanour or action; civil, courteous, friendly, obliging. Obs. Cf. 476 and 774, and see OED on the chronology of shifts in distinctions of meaning between humane and human. Wilson (1873, p. 249): The elder form of humane would be preferable, though it can scarcely be claimed as a purposed change, for this is the usual mode of spelling human in Shakespeare’s day. If any change is to be made, common modesty would better suit the context. Watt (ed. 1906): Human presumably refers to the standard in vogue among men, and is explained by what follows. To Titania’s wooing of Bottom human standards do not apply.

710–11 Such…Becomes]
Verity (ed. 1893): i.e. such as may well be said to become (=befit).

712 So…distant]
Hunter (ed. 1870): Keep at that distance from me. Knowles (privately) disagrees: Be is governed by the nominative separation, and is not an imperative. See 709–12 above.

712 distant]
OED (3a), quoting this instance first: Not near at hand, remote.

712 friend]
See n. 708.

713–17 Thy…prest]
Rolfe (ed. 1877) notes the four instances in these lines of the subjunctive used optatively or imperatively, citing Abbott §364, 365; see also n. 239.

713–15 Thy…loyalty]
Possibly echoing the marriage service; see n. 1192.

714 Amen]
Eaton (1860, pp. 134–5): Amen is introduced in such a way as to indicate the exclusive use to which it should be applied. Neil (ed. 1878), comparing R2 4.1.173 (2095): Lysander jocosely expresses perfect agreement by quoting the phraseology of the clerk in the church service, when making responses to the prayers of the priest.

716 all his rest]
Deighton (ed. 1891): All the peace he has in his gift.

717 With…prest]
Hunter (ed. 1870): Hermia means that she would not have for herself all the rest that sleep can give, as in that case Lysander could have none. Walter (ed. 1964): A playful sharing of benefits.

717 prest]
Kittredge (ed. 1939): Pressed down in slumber.

719–36 Through…Oberon]
On fairy meter, see , and cf. n. 678–85.

720 found]
Support for the present tense of Q2Ff (see textual n.) is lukewarm. Halliwell (ed. 1856) thinks the present tense seems preferable; but either reading of course makes perfect sense. Sprague (ed. 1896), comparing Mac. 5.1.1–2 (2095–6), claims: The change from perfect tense to the present is natural. Thiselton (1903, p. 37): The present tense seems more appropriate than the past, since Puck’s words night and silence [723] indicate that he is still all eyes and ears for sight or sound of the Athenian Lady and disdainefull youth, and that his search is therefore scarcely concluded. Understanding have with found to give the present perfect tense answers Thiselton’s objection to Q1.

721 approue]
Rann (ed. 1787): Make trial of (OED, v.1 8 Obs.). Phillips (ed. 1969): Demonstrate (OED, v.1 1a. Obs.).

722 force]
Johnson (1755, 3): Virtue; efficacy (OED, n.1 7b, quoting this instance first). See also 958, 1589.

723–36 Who…Oberon]
On the use of soliloquy, see n. 678–85.

724–6 Weedes…maide]
Verity (ed. 1893): Of course Oberon meant Demetrius and Helena; but as Lysander and Hermia too wear weeds (dress) of Athens, by which Puck was to recognize the lovers, his mistake is natural (so he pleads, [1388–92]). Foakes (ed. 1984): [This] suggests that the two lovers were similarly costumed, and the confusions would be funnier if they looked more or less alike.
Most editors soften the parenthesis, and recent preference is for dropping it altogether; this facilitates the gloss of Wells (ed. 1967): He that my master said. Grinfield (MS 1834–63) suggests hee…Who despis’d (see conj., ), implying possibly that the parenthesis should be taken to mean as my master said or according to my master. The parenthesis of the unamended QqFf text could signal in delivery some doubt in Puck’s mind about Oberon’s information, which is removed by discovery of the young man’s churlish distancing from the maiden.

727 sleeping sound]
OED (Sound, adv., 2): To enjoy undisturbed sleep; to be in a profound sleep.

727 danke]
Gildon (1710, Glossary): Moist, raw, &c. Johnson (1755), quoting this instance: Damp; humid; moist; wet. OED (2b), also quoting this instance: Damp: with the connotation that this is an injurious or disagreeable quality; applied to surfaces, app. Obs. after 1650, exc. in northern dialect, but revived by romantic writers in end of 18th c.

729–31 Pretty…Churle]
See n. 695–714.

729–30 Pretty…kil-curtesie]
Thiselton (1903, p. 37): It should be noted that the reason of the distance between the sleepers is that Lysander yielded to the request that Hermia made for love and courtesie [708]. The second line was not intended to run smoothly: its very roughness adds force to the terms of opprobrium. Krieger (1979, p. 41): That is, the forest spirit can interpret the separation only as one of antipathy; he has no sense of the transported city-manners that derive from an abstract moral and social convention that requires a physical separation between two lovers.

729 Pretty sowle]
OED (Soul, 14a): Used with a defining adj. to denote a person of particular character or in respect of some quality, freq. with a touch of contempt, compassion, or familiarity. Cf. 1186, 1932.

729 durst]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Preterite of dare, which, in the sense of challenge, forms another preterite, dared. See OED, Dare, v.1, note on Forms. See Franz §181.

730 Neere…kil-curtesie]
Dissatisfaction with the apparently irregular metre of this line prompts diverse procrustean solutions (see textual n.). Stretchers to 10 syllables: Pope (ed. 1725) adds to. Malone (in Boswell, ed. 1821), anticipated by Thirlby (MS 1733–47), reads near as a dissyllable and justifies the long line by citing 384–5. Walker (1860, 2:52), also anticipated by Thirlby: Read nearer for neare. The force of nearer, and Lysander’s discourtesy (as it appeared to our friend Puck), are explained by the scene immediately preceding between Lysander and Hermia.
Theobald (ed. 1733), accepting Pope’s to but cutting this lack-loue (which he believes to have been officiously clap’d in by the Players in order to explain the quaint and uncommon kil-curtesie) comes closest to trimming the line to the dominant 7-syllable, 4-stress trochaic rhyming measure of Puck’s speech. Johnson (ed. 1765) and Steevens (ed. 1793), cutting only the second this, stay closer to the QqFf text but as Seymour (1805, 1:45) points out, curtesie must then be read as a dissyllable and the accent wrenched. Verity (ed. 1893) also pronounces court’sÿ, but accepting QqFf takes the line as 8-syllable iambic, pointing out that this is the measure of 727 [and also of 736]. Sprague (ed. 1896) sees no difficulty in a 4-stress trochaic reading, with the last abbreviated syllable -esie feminine. Editors have not commented on 736, which like 727 is clearly iambic, nor on 722 and 726 which are probably so.
Wilson (ed. 1924): Assuredly the line, which though harsh and drawling can be spoken rhythmically without any difficulty, admirably expresses the contempt of the shrewd and knavish sprite as he points at Lysander. Brooks (ed. 1979): The rhythm keeps its tripping step: and the longer line concludes the first part of Puck’s speech, before the spell; carries his misdirected indignation to a climax; and highlights his mistake.

730 lack-loue]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Churlish fellow so wanting in love towards her who loves him; accent on the first syllable. See OED, Lack, v.1, 7, quoting this instance.

730 kil-curtesie]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Boor who murders courtesy, is utterly without good manners. See OED, Kill-, vb. stem, A, quoting this instance. On compounds formed from a verb + noun,…with a human referent, see Blake 3.3.1.4 (a); Franz §137.

731 Churle]
Johnson (1755, 1), quoting this instance: A rustick; a countryman; a labourer; Puck more probably intends Johnson’s second definition: A rude, surly, ill-bred man (OED 5).

732 owe]
Johnson (1755, 4): To possess; to be the right owner of. For owe, which is, in this sense, obsolete, we now use own (OED, B. 1a). Rann (ed. 1787): Contains.

733–4 When…lidde]
Steevens (ed. 1793) compares Mac. 1.3.19–20 (117–8): Sleepe shall neyther Night nor Day Hang vpon his Pent-house Lid. Delius (ed. 1859): May love prevent you from falling asleep again (Ger.). Daniel (1870, pp. 31–2) reducing the meaning to the absurd When Lysander awakes, he is no longer asleep!, wishes to emend (see conj., ). Furness (ed. 1895) opposes him, explaining: Puck’s charm is to awaken in Lysander such a feverish love that sleep will be banned from his eyes, a symptom of the passion common enough.

735 So…gon]
Deighton (ed. 1891): I leave you to wake after I have gone with this result (i.e. of your not being able to find sleep again).

736 For…Oberon]
Thiselton (1903, p. 37): Now is ere the first Cocke crow [648].

736 must]
For omission of go see n. 522 (Thou…).

737–9 Enter…thus]
Brown (ed. 1996): Another running entrance, and an explosion of monosyllables alters the mood.

738–41 Stay…goe]
For the stychomythia see n. 145–50. Hollindale (1992, p. 84): Their brief dialogue is a miniature duet of imploring and rejecting, meeting and parting, opening and closing of physical space.…The closed form of the couplet technically counteracts the ostensible meaning of the lines. Demetrius, in accepting the pattern of audible rhythmic completions, is so to speak participating linguistically with Helena in constructing their quarrel.…The exchange of antithesis (stay/hence/leave/go) confirms the countering sharedness of the dialogue and offsets its rancorous hostility.

738 Stay…Demetrius]
Fripp (1928, p. 593) compares Anna’s pursuit of Iarbas in Marlowe’s Dido (see ).

739 charge]
Johnson (1755, 8): To command (OED 14a).

739 haunt]
OED (v. 4): To run after. Johnson (1755, 2): It is used frequently in an ill sense of one that comes unwelcome. Evans (ed. 1974): Follow persistently. Holland (ed. 1994) adds: Since the wood is haunted at [918] and [1027], the more ghostly sense may also be present.

740 darkling]
OED (adv. A.), quoting this instance: In the dark; in darkness. lit. and fig. Johnson (1755), quoting this instance: A word merely poetical. Wells (ed. 1967) notes that the reminder of the darkness was especially necessary in the open-air Elizabethan theatre. Thirlby (MS 1725–33) compares Lr. 1.4.217 (728–9). The Clarkes (1879, 545 n.) suggest the word includes the meaning of baffled, deserted, bereft of light and help. Barnett (1887, p. 17): Helena may use it for looking black; that is, applying it to Demetrius’s expression rather than her own situation. See The Random House Webster’s College Dictionary (1991), adj. 4: Vaguely threatening or menacing, and cf. OED, Darkly, adv. 3. See also n. 222–3.
For the old adverbial suffix -ling see Franz §243. Furness (ed. 1895) calls the compositorial error of F4 darling an almost plausible sophistication. See textual n.

741 Stay…goe]
Deighton (ed. 1891): i.e. at the risk, if you follow me, of being ill-used by me:…I am determined to go unaccompanied by you.

741 on thy perill]
OED (3b), quoting 1201 to thy perill: Taking the risk or responsibility of the consequences: esp. in commands, or warnings.

743 fond]
Schmidt (1874): 1) foolish,…3) doting; here and at 1138 Schmidt finds these significations blent. See 1352. See also n. 647.

744 my grace]
Johnson (in Steevens, ed. 1773): My acceptableness, the favour that I can gain (cf. OED 6a). Schmidt (1874, 8): Good fortune, happiness, blessedness. Chambers (ed. 1897): Answer to prayer. Walter (ed. 1964): There is a quibble on the religious significance of prayer and grace.
