Griffiths (1994, p. 294), discussing moments in Sh.’s comedies which seem to reveal unsuspected vistas of implications: For the space of little more than one second, she sounds like a theologian of a Protestant cast; the more she petitions her god, the less he favours her, because her supplications express what she wants rather than seek to realise what he wills. The fleeting, implied simile between the soul’s pursuit of the divine and her unreciprocated sexual desire lifts the play momentarily, like a shuttle-cock, into a realm of conceptual punning. Possibly also punning on OED (1a): Attractiveness, charm; cf. n. 219.

745 wheresoere she lies]
Moberly (ed. 1881): Wherever she dwells, comparing Milton’s L’Allegro, 79, Where perchance some beauty lies. See OED, Lie, v.1 B. 5a.

746 she…eyes]
West (1865, p. 9) compares Sidney: ’Till my eyes had their curse from blessed eyne (ed. Evans, 1977, p. 211).

746 blessed]
OED (ppl. a. 3a): Enjoying supreme felicity; happy; fortunate; see also (2): The object of adoring reverence; adorable.

746 attractiue]
OED (a. 5), quoting this instance first: Having the quality of drawing (living beings) by influencing their will and action. Johnson (1755, 2), quoting this instance: Inviting; alluring; enticing. Kittredge (ed. 1939) compares 574, and Ham. 3.2.109–10 (1964). Andrews (ed. 1989): Literally, magnetic. Idem (ed. 1993) compares 574–7 and 193–214.

747 How…eyes]
Deighton (ed. 1891): What is it that has made her eyes. See OED, Come, 25a.

747–8 teares…hers]
For the rhyme, see Cercignani (1981, p. 80); however, he expresses some doubt about the pronunciation: As her may have ME &emacrondot; by lengthening of ǐ (OE hire) and tear by raising of e&asteriskmacron;, the rhyme might even depend on lME i*.

748 If…hers]
Deighton (ed. 1891): For, in that case, mine would be brighter than hers, seeing that they are oftener washed with such tears. Cf. 140–1.

748 washt]
Cuningham (ed. 1905) compares Ado 1.1.27 (30) and Lr. 1.1.268 (293).

749–50 No…feare]
For another comic comparison of a fearsome bear to an amorous woman, cf. Err. 3.2.154–5 (944–5). Cf. also 1812–13. For the rhyme, see Cercignani (1981, p. 144); cf. n. 681–5.

749 vgly…Beare]
Proverbial (Dent, questioningly, B125.1). For Helena to stigmatize herself in this way by contrast with the attractiveness of Hermia’s eyes, which she had called loadstarres (195), may have been suggested by wofull Callistope, who Was tourned fro a woman to a bere And afterward was she made the lode sterre (Chaucer, KnT 2056–9; see ).

750 beastes…feare]
Winter (2003, pp. 93–4): This flight of the monstrous confronted with the human forms a grotesque parallel to the rehearsal scene where Bottom is transformed to a strange beast (Fr.). See also n. 918–19.

751 no…though]
Schmidt (1875, Marvel, 2): Always followed by the subjunctive, = it is not strange that. Deighton (ed. 1891): No wonder (OED, Marvel, n.1 5).

752 Doe]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Subjunctive. Brooks (ed. 1979): Should.

752 as a monster]
Wright (ed. 1877): In apposition to my presence. Furness (ed. 1895): This refers not to Demetrius, but to Helena herself. Carroll (1985, p. 145): The ambiguous double grammar of as a monster implies an alteration in Demetrius as well.

752 as]
Brooks (ed. 1979): As if I were.

752 fly]
For omission of the preposition, see n. 216.

753–4 What…eyen]
Deighton (ed. 1891): How could any mirror be so wickedly treacherous as to make me think my eyes rivalled the star-like orbs of Hermia? Thiselton (1903, p. 37) quotes Puttenham (1589; 1936, p. 19): This phantasie may be resembled to a glasse…, whereof there be many tempers and manner of makinges,…for some be false glasses,and shew thinges otherwise than they be in deede, and others right as they be in deede.… There be againe of these glasses that shew thinges exceeding faire and comely, others that shew figures very monstruous & illfauored. See also n. 1797.

753 dissembling]
OED (ppl. a.), quoting this instance: Deceiving, hypocritical.

754 compare…eyen]
Cf. 1163.

754 compare with]
OED (v.1 4b): To vie with, rival. Schmidt (1874, vb. 2b): To think one’s self equal.

754 sphery]
Rann (ed. 1787): Bright as the stars. Schmidt (1875): Starlike, celestial (OED 1, quoting this instance first). Wright (ed. 1877): Sphere is used by Shakespeare to denote first the orbit in which a star moves, and then the star itself. Neil (ed. 1878): Round, full, large. See also n. 378 (swifter…sphere).

754 eyen]
For the plural form, see n. 256.

755–69 But…doue]
Hunter (Shakespeare, 1962, p. 11), defending the verse in this exchange: It does perfectly what the play requires it to do. It reduces the passions to a comic level where we do not feel called upon to share them; but it remains poetic and charming, and there is no difficulty in distinguishing the comedy of this scene from the farce of the Pyramus and Thisbe play.

755–6 But…wound]
Rudd (1979, p. 188): How close this is to the Pyramus and Thisbe story can be seen by turning on to [2116–17]. Blits (2003, p. 81): Thisbe finds Pyramus on the ground and, initially thinking him asleep, discovers that he is dead.…Here, the story is reversed.

755–6 ground…wound]
Verity (ed. 1912): In [R2 3.2.139–40 (1498–9)] <F1> wound rhymes with ground. See Cercignani (1981, pp. 195–6).

756 a sleepe]
See n. 555.

757–8 awake…sake]
Farrow (ed. 1972): The rhyme split between the two speakers makes his response seem much quicker and funnier. Sorelius (1993, p. 174): Lysander’s protestation of love is so sudden that it does not pause to start a new sentence but continues that of Helena.

758 And…sake]
Brooks (ed. 1979): In abruptness, the exclamation resembles Palamon’s when he falls in love at sight (Chaucer, KnT 1078; see ). Moisan (1998, p. 278) finds a gender-inverted echo of Studley’s translation of Seneca’s Hippolytus, when Phaedra declares through burning fire runne after thee I shall (Seneca his tenne tragedies, ed. 1964, p. 161).

758 runne…fire]
OED (Fire, n. 5d), quoting this instance: To go through fire: To submit to the severest ordeal or proof. Thirlby (MS 1733–47) quotes Ray’s Proverbs and compares Wiv. 3.4.103 (1670–1). See Tilley (F285).

759–62 Transparent…sworde]
Moberly (ed. 1881): What Lysander means is that, by the natural power of love, he sees the trouble which fills her mind, though he had been asleep when she spoke of it, and is therefore longing to revenge it upon Demetrius.

759–60 Transparent…heart]
Understanding Transparent as Frank, candid, ingenuous (OED, a. 2 fig. a, quoting this instance first), Deighton (ed. 1891): Though indicating also the brilliancy of her beauty, refers especially to the transparency of her nature which enables him to see her heart through her bosom. Understanding it as Diaphanous (OED, a.1a), Johnson (ed. 1894): The transparency of her nature serves, as it were, for a glass (a work of art), through which the heart within her bosom may be seen, and thus Nature and art, generally contrasted, are identified; he compares WT 4.4.95–7 (1906–8). Apparently drawing on Schmidt (1875, Transparent 2, Bright), Kittredge (ed. 1939), unhelpfully: Brilliant, gloriously bright, with reference to her blonde beauty. Walter (ed. 1964): A complex platonic thought perhaps prompted by Helena’s name which means fire (torch or meteor). Angelic or divine nature was identified with fire which was transparent. The divine soul or idea in the perfect beauty inhabited a shrine of crystal flesh, through which to shine [Crashaw, To his (supposed) Mistresse]. In Helena nature is thus reconciled and integrated with art. Farrow (ed. 1972) shows an anachronistic sensibility in claiming The picture this calls up of Helena with a glass-fronted chest is meant to be faintly ridiculous.
Because of the F1, F2 variants (see textual nn.), some commentary concentrates on the art/nature dichotomy. Steevens (ed. 1793) calls here a judicious correction.…Here, means—in the present instance. On this occasion, says Lysander, the work of nature resembles that of art, viz. (as our author expresses it in his Lover’s Complaint,) an object glaz’d with crystal [286]. Fleay (1886, p. 186) claims the line is from Peele’s Edward I…how nature strove in them to show her art (2:170, l. 2675). Irving & Marshall (ed. 1888): Nature has no art strictly speaking; the two are generally spoken of as opposites, at any rate as very different things; and therefore…for Nature to show art is an exceptional circumstance. Wilson (ed. 1924) glosses art as magic art. Phillips (ed. 1969) terms the nature-art contrast a mild oxymoron. Brooks (ed. 1979) compares Montemayor’s Diana (ed. 1968, p. 121), where Arsileus sang to Belisa, Nature in framing thee…shew’d so…suttle art.

760 bosome…heart]
Thiselton (1903, p. 38): In the Barriers in Ben Jonson’s Hymenaei the Angel says of Truth And you may see her heart shine through her brest [895 (7:239)]. Verity (ed. 1912): Probably he quibbles on bosom = front folds of the dress, stomacher; cf. [TN 3.1.121 (1334)]. See Johnson, 1755, 4; OED, n. 3a. Phillips (ed. 1969): He means that she is a candid, straightforward person. See also n. 35 (bosome).

760 thy heart]
Walker (1860, 1:300) defends the conjectural emendation my (see textual n.) by referring to the old poetical commonplace of the lover’s heart being transferred to the beloved’s bosom; he instances AYL 5.4.115 (2690) and Son. 133.9. Thiselton (1903, p. 38) objects that the change would rob the epithet Transparent [759] of its propriety.

761–2 Demetrius…name]
Furness (ed. 1895) quotes Tiessen (1877, p. 4) who asks why the name of Demetrius should be thus referred to. Is there a covert reference to demit, i.e. to humble, to subject, or to meat which is stuck on a spit? [i.e. De-meat-rius, I suppose. This insight of the way in which a learned German reads his Shakespeare would be interesting if it were not so depressing.] Ridley (ed. 1934): There is a dishonest conspiracy of silence amongst commentators as to why Demetrius’ vile name is so fit a word; and even Furness has nothing better to offer than a gibe at a ponderous German, who at least had the honesty to admit that he was puzzled. Spencer (1954, pp. 46–8) points to North’s Plutarch where the vileness of Demetrius Poliorcetes is described in the parallel life to Marcus Antonius; see . But Wells (1976, pp. 67–8) proposes that the Demetrius of Titus Andronicus is glanced at, and suggests unconvincingly that the associations aroused by the name would have added to the burlesque tone of the passage here. Nosworthy (1982, pp. 104–5) notes that Demetrius the Phalerean is mentioned in [Plutarch’s] Life of Theseus, but follows and expands upon Spencer (1954) in connecting to Demetrius Poliorcetes, noting that in The Life of Demetrius there are accounts of dreams and of bad weather. Holland (ed. 1994, p. 134): Most likely the silversmith of Ephesus who led a crowd of his fellow-workmen in an attack on St Paul and his companions because their teaching was damaging trade (Acts of the Apostles, 19: 24–41). This Demetrius made silver shrines for Diana (19: 24), a goddess of great significance for Dream and whose temple at Ephesus was supposedly built by Amazons (see, e.g., Nashe, Works, iii. 29). Carroll (2003, p. 54) argues that a search for a precedent in history or literature is mistaken: It is not that any particular Demetrius was vile or even that an unusually large number were. Rather, the name itself was vile.…Shakespeare introduces Orsino as A noble duke, in nature as in name (Twelfth Night, 1.2.25 (75)). A name can be ignoble whatever the character of its bearer. Such appears to have been the case with Demetrius, judging from a comment by William Camden in Remains concerning Britain (1605). I have observed that the change of names hath most commonly proceeded from a desire to avoyd the opinion of basenes.…So Demetrius Mega when he was there made free of the Citty, was called Publius Cornelius [ed. R.D. Dunn, 1984, p. 117]. He goes on to suggest that the base connotation is connected with an etymology from Gr. demos, thus a commoner, or from Lat. demetere, to reap, cut off; he notes that Pompey, Horace, and Cicero all have connections to slaves or former slaves called Demetrius. He suggests possible comic business, eg. Damn-etrius recalling Tit. 5.3.97 (Q1 2601) the damn’d Demetrius, or (following Tiessen—see above): The actor could pronounce De-meat-trius and then manoeuvre his sword as if sticking meat on a spit.
Knowles (privately) points out that the conceit that the name stands for and so should be treated as the person is elaborated in Julia’s speech on her own and Proteus’s names in TGV 1.2.106–26 (269–89).
See also DP under Demetrius.

761–2 Oh…sworde]
Deighton (ed. 1891): i.e. how well does the bearer of that vile name deserve to perish at my hands!; he compares 569, reading slay for stay.

762 vile]
See n. 246.

762 to…sworde]
Thirlby (MS 1733–47) compares 623, to dy vpon the hand.

763–4 Do…though]
Rolfe (ed. 1877): Lines like [763–4], and [780–1], certainly give support to the theory that parts at least of this play were written even earlier than 1594. Chambers (ed. 1897): These jerky lines, with their staccato emphasis, and reiteration of the sound so…so…though…though, may be looked upon as comparatively youthful work. Or they may reflect Helena’s emotional state. See n. 780–1.

764 What…though]
The repeated phrase carries two meanings: the first is nearly equivalent to what if…?, and the second absolute construction carries the sense, What does it matter? what then? See OED, What, pron. 5. Phrases. a. Castelain (ed. 1943) compares Lat. Quid si? Cf. 1259.

765 still]
See n. 206.

765–6 be…Content]
Schmidt (1874, adj. 4), citing 765: Be c. = be quiet, be calm (OED, a. [n.4] 1b); ibid. (1), citing 766: Satisfied, not demanding more (OED, a. (n.4) 1a, but quoting 765, not 766). Kittredge (ed. 1939): Lysander repeats the word with a shift in the meaning—a common trick of speech. Brooks (ed. 1979, p. xlvii) identifies the rhetorical figure, anadiplosis, which doth serve…to add a certain increase in the second member (Peacham, quoted in Sonnino, 1968, p. 157).

766–77 Content…booke]
Verity (ed. 1893): The speech is a specimen of the strained style, dealing in somewhat affected diction (cf. [774, 775]), and fanciful thoughts (cf. [776, 777]), which is not uncommon in Shakespeare’s early works.

766–7 Content…spent]
Harrison (1926, pp. 95–6) compares Montemayor’s Diana, when Don Felix repudiates the charge of inconstancy, declaring he has done his second love Celia the greater injurie, having placed my love first in an other, and not in her (ed. 1968, p. 95). Brooks (ed. 1979) compares a different episode in Diana: Sylvanus, transferring, thanks to the philtre, his love from Diana, exclaims: O faire Selvagia, what a great…folly have I committed, by imploying my thoughtes upon another, after that mine eies did once behold thy rare beautie? (ed. 1968, p. 187). Lamb (2000, p. 303) contends: The fairy ointment Puck rubs on Lysander’s eyelids [cf. 731–2] alludes to what need not be directly stated: that his love for Hermia cooled as she denied him the illicit sexual pleasure he desired.

768 Not…loue]
Bate (1993, p. 134 n.): Love’s changes are rendered in a crisp rhetoric that is in part learnt from Ovid: compare [Lysander’s] Not Hermia but Helena I love with a line in the Ars Amatoria, Scilicet Hermionem Helenae praeponere posses? ([2.699], So you would be able to prefer Hermione to Helena?).
Some editors accept Q2’s addition of now, which Collier (ed. 1842) asserts is to the injury of the metre. See textual n. and conj., .

769 Rauen…doue]
Harting (1871, p. 108): As the type of blackness, both as regards colour and character, we find the raven frequently contrasted with the white dove, the emblem of all that is pure and gentle, quoting TN 5.1.131 (2287). Verity (ed. 1893): Raven also alludes to Hermia’s dark complexion (cf. [1286]). Erickson (1993, pp. 499, 518–19), examining Sh.’s comedies in the context of a survey of images of blacks in visual art of the Renaissance period, points out that here Lysander defines his exchange of loves in terms of color; he adds: This language is sharpened by the ethnic edge of his names for Hermia at 1286 and 1294, and claims extravagantly that Theseus’ contemptuous evocation of a brow of Egypt [1803] contains a reference to darkness that reminds us how the motif of color difference has been one of the play’s shaping forces. On doue see also n. 182.

770–7 The…booke]
Watt (ed. 1906): The humour of the situation is that Lysander eloquently declares in favour of reason, and is sure that he has reached the highest point of human wisdom just when he has been bewitched, much as a drunken man protests that he is sober. Hunter (Shakespeare, 1962, p. 12), quoting these lines in contrast with Helena’s 246–55: Helena remarks on the capacity of love to work without knowing the evidence of the senses (the eyes); in Lysander’s case reason is only a means of returning to the eyes of his mistress, and reading irrational love stories. Foakes (ed. 1984): The play on reason is ironic; even Bottom knows that reason and love keep little company together nowadays [960–1], and Egeus’s will (inclination or, in a stronger sense, intent) in insisting that Hermia marry Demetrius [96, 127] had nothing to do with reason.…Tilley L517 lists Love is without reason as a common proverb. Hassel (2005, p. 270): The ironies behind Lysander’s certainty…are informed by the tradition of the distrust of reason; although the fool Bottom divides reason and love (960–1) and the narrowly reasonable Theseus confounds lunatic, lover, and poet (1796–1800), the lovers come to accept the blessed madness of love, its transcendence of reason.; despite Bottom’s division of reason and love (960–1) and narrowly reasonable Theseus’s dismissal of lunatic, lover, and poet (1796–1800), the lovers come to accept the blessed madness of love, its transcendence of reason. Reyher (1947, pp. 64–6) compares the images used by Lucilla and Euphues in Lyly’s Euphues to justify their inconstancy (Fr.). Cf. also the discussion of love and reason in Montemayor’s Diana; see .

770 will…swai’d]
Smith (1963, p. 102) cites Tilley R43: Let Reason rule all your actions. Andrews (ed. 1993): Swai’d recalls [205].…Ironically, when Lysander thinks his Will is most sway’d (ruled) by Reason, it is actually being sway’d (thrown off balance) by an irrational charm. See n. 205 (sway). Hassel (2005, pp. 390, 389) places Lysander’s perverse misunderstanding of himself, the experience, the reason, and the will in the context of the Reformation debate about grace and works.

770 will]
Clemen (ed. 1963): Desire. McDonald (ed. 2000): Possibly with specific sexual sense: will could also mean penis. See Williams, 1994, pp. 1536–7. Knowles (privately) demurs: Lysander is talking earnestly in terms of conventional Elizabethan psychology and morality, which held that the will is more wisely guided by reason (judgment) than by passion (blood). The joke is that he is being led by passions that arise in the senses (eyes). The speech offers no occasion or reason for suggestive double entendres.

771–2 And…season]
Brooks (ed. 1979): Shakespeare had not the psychologist’s term rationalization, but he knows the propensity and laughs at it. The comedy of lovers’ belief in their own wisdom amuses Theseus in Chaucer [KnT 1804–5]. See .

772 Things growing]
For transposition of participles see Abbott §419 (3); Blake 4.3.8 (b).

773 being]
Contracted; see Abbott §470.

773 ripe not]
Steevens (ed. 1793): I.e. do not ripen to it. Ripe in the present instance, is a verb; he compares AYL 2.7.26 (999). Schmidt (1881, p. 8), anticipated by Thirlby (MS 1733–47), proposed riped, an emendation praised as very judicious by Castelain (ed. 1943) (Fr.); see conj., . Watt (ed. 1906): Have not grown ripe.…The present tense is used of an action begun in the past and continued in the present. Noyes (ed. 1908): Till now I have not been old enough to form a reasonable judgment.
Rowe (ed. 1714), taking ripe as adj., transposed these two words (see textual n.); a few 19th c. editors followed his reading while not adopting the emendation. Caldecott (MS 1813–33): I.e. am not ripe, or at the maturity of. Hunter (ed. 1870): Not ripe enough to be able to reason. Rolfe (ed. 1877): For transpositions of not in S., see [Abbott §§]305, 420.

774 And…skill]
Steevens (ed. 1778): I.e. my senses being now at their utmost height of perfection, comparing H8 3.2.223 (2102).

774 touching]
Johnson (1755, To Touch, v.a. 2): To come to; to attain (OED, v. 13a).

774 point]
OED (n. 26): The highest part, the height, summit, zenith, acme. See also n. 278.

774 humane]
For the spelling, see n. 476.

774 humane skill]
Wright (ed. 1877): Discernment possible to man. Moberly (ed. 1881): A grown man’s experience.

774 skill]
Harness (ed. 1825; 1830): Judgment. Deighton (ed. 1891): For skill = sagacity, mental power, cp. [MM 4.2.155 (2021)]. OED (n.1 7): Knowledge or understanding.

775 Reason…will]
Johnson (ed. 1765): That is, My Will now follows Reason. Steevens (ed. 1778) compares Mac. 2.1.42 (622). Heron (1785, p. 110): My will now obeys the command of my reason. Delius (ed. 1859): Reason guides my formerly blind desire (Ger.). Aronson (1972, p. 208): This line recalls Hamlet’s outburst against his mother, whose conduct proves, he thinks, that her perverted judgment has defended the unreasonable cravings of her blood [3.4.69 (2453)], and that therefore her reason panders will [3.4.88 (2463)]—in both cases the Elizabethan sense of the word will being synonymous with sexual appetite.

775 Marshall]
Johnson (1755, 3): Any one who regulates rank or order at a feast, or any other assembly (OED, n. 5a fig., quoting this instance only). Heron (1785, p. 110): Director of an army, of a turney, of a feast. West (1865, p. 13) compares Sidney’s Arcadia: Her face…had been but as a marshal to lodge the love of her in his mind (ed. Evans, 1977, p. 91).

776–7 And…booke]
Thirlby (MS 1747–53) compares Jn. 2.1.485 (801). Steevens (ed. 1793) compares Rom. 1.3.85–7 (431–3). West (1865, p. 20) compares Sidney’s Arcadia (ed. Evans, 1977, p. 375): Are your eyes a fit book, think you, to read a tale upon? Is my love quiet enough to be an historian? Cuningham (ed. 1905) quotes LLL 4.3.348–50 (1701–4). See n. 766–7.

776 orelooke]
Rann (ed. 1787): Peruse. OED ( v. Overlook 3): To look over; to examine, scrutinize.

777 Loues stories]
Kittredge (ed. 1939): Love’s true stories, i.e., what love really is—as opposed to my boyish fancies.

777 loues…booke]
Bate (1993, p. 127): Not a written text to be studied at school like the Metamorphoses, but the eyes of Helena.

778 Wherefore…borne]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Why should I have been born to endure such bitter irony? i.e. I have done nothing myself to deserve it.

780–1 Ist…can]
Furness (ed. 1895): It is not easy to decide whether these repetitions here, in the next line, and in [784] are characteristic of Helena…or are the effects of sobbing. I think that when Helena finds that to the scorn of Demetrius is added the scorn of Lysander…she bursts into uncontrollable tears. And yet there are somewhat similar repetitions in [763, 764], where is no question of tears, which sound weak, unless they be a trait of character. See n. 763–4. Brooks (ed. 1979, p. xlvi) quotes 780 in noting in Helena’s speech, as in Egeus’ characteristic outburst (1679–81), a phrase or word repeated in immediate succession (epizeuxis) is used with great dramatic aptness.

780–1 Ist]
OED (Is’t): Archaic, poetic, colloq., or dial. abbreviation of is it; first instance quoted is 1610, Tmp. 1.2.245 (367).

781 nor neuer]
For the double negative used for emphasis, see n. 580.

782 Deserue]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Win by any attractions of mine; be thought really worthy of.

782 Demetrius]
See n. 182 (Venus).

783 flout]
Johnson (1755, v. a.), quoting this instance: To mock; to insult; to treat with mockery and contempt. Deighton (ed. 1891): Jeer at (OED, v. 1a). See 1363.

783 insufficiency]
OED (n. 1a): Unfitness, incapacity. Hunter (ed. 1870): Want of attractiveness. Deighton (ed. 1891): Want of power to win such a favour.

784 Good troth]
Schmidt (1875, Troth, 2): Used to express a slight oath. Kittredge (ed. 1939): In faith; upon my word.

784 good sooth]
OED (n. 4b), quoting this instance: An expletive used to emphasize an assertion. See Blake 5.1.3.7 (ii). Kittredge (ed. 1939): In very truth. See also 1297.

785 In…wooe]
Deighton (ed. 1891): To make a mock of seeking my love in these ironical terms of praise.

786 perforce…confesse]
Thirlby (MS 1747–53): I must needs own. Holzknecht & McClure (ed. 1937): Perforce: an intensive.

787 Lord of]
Rann (ed. 1787): Possessed of. Deighton (ed. 1891): Master of (OED 2b).

787 gentlenesse]
Percy (in Steevens, ed. 1773): The spirit of a gentleman. White (ed. 1858): Graciousness, courtesy. Helena…means to say, that she thought Lysander more of a gentleman than to behave as he had behaved to her. Baker (ed. 1896): Good breeding, the conduct of a gentleman. See OED, n. 3.

788–9 of…of]
For the usage, see n. 113.

789 abus’d]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Insulted (OED, v. 7). Ridley (ed. 1934, Glossary): Deceived (OED, v. 4a).

790 She…Hermia]
Blits (2003, p. 85): Had she seen her, Lysander seems to think, Helena would have realized that he no longer loves Hermia. Possibly Lysander is relieved that Helena did not see that he was still in Hermia’s company; his pausing to make a formal speech of rejection over Hermia’s sleeping body instead of immediately pursuing Helena is a comically exaggerated dramatic gesture. Knowles (privately): The speech is the counterpart of 766 ff., another demonstration of his delusion that he is acting rationally, here amassing proverbs to justify his actions.

792–7 For…mee]
Gildon (1710, 7:317): The Similes which Lysander uses to express or rather justify his Falsehood very fine. The Sokols (2000, pp. 132–3), quoting 792–7 and referring to Rom. 1.2.88–91 (337–40) and Ado 1.1.229–35 (223–8): There is pointedly no doctrinal meaning to a lover’s heresy; his defection reflects only the willfulness of passion. Such usages may suggest in reverse an arbitrary absurdity in bloody doctrinal disputes.…The cruelty of hating a woman just because she was formerly loved is paralleled with a passionate hatred of a faith formerly endorsed, now called heresy. This image may convey a sardonic view of the murderous sectarian conflicts of the sixteenth century. Hassel (2005, p. 160) quotes this passage in claiming that in Sh. heresy pertains, through metaphor, more often to romantic than religious experience.

792–3 For…bringes]
Farrow (ed. 1972): Just as too much (a surfeit) of the nicest food makes you sickest. Brown (1862, p. 95) compares Prov. 25:16. Shaheen (1993, p. 96) cites also Prov. 27:7, 25:27; he compares Rom. 2.6.11–14 (1403–6) and 1H4 3.2.71–3 (1890–2). See Tilley (H560), quoting this instance, and S1011. For other parallels see Smith (1963), #281, p. 114, and Strauss (1994), #559, 1:503–4.

794–5 Or…deceiue]
Brooks (ed. 1979): Heresies renounced are detested especially by the men formerly duped by them. The aphorism sounds proverbial: but is not in Tilley.

795, 797 they]
Delius (ed. 1859): Scil. The heresies. Thiselton (1903, p. 38): The Quartos are clearly right in reading they for that after those, since, having regard to but the most of me [797], of must here imply agency.

796 my…heresie]
Wood (ed. 1902): Of whom I have had more than enough, and in whom I erringly believed.

797 bee]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Subjunctive used optatively. See Abbott §364; Blake 4.3.2.1 (a), 7.2.8.

798 all my powers]
Hunter (ed. 1870): All ye my powers. Deighton (ed. 1891) paraphrases Lysander’s command to himself: And let all the faculties I possess bend their most loving and mightiest efforts.

799 be her knight]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Swear yourself to her service and honour; as knights swore themselves to the service and honour of their lady-loves. Doran (ed. 1959): True-love, true servant. Phillips (ed. 1969): Champion (OED 3).

800–11 Helpe…immediately]
Gentleman (ed. 1774): This soliloquy is in favour of an actress, and will employ both flexible powers of features and expression. Arnold (1911, p. 75): Sh. uses talking in one’s sleep as a transition from slumber to soliloquy, instancing here, and Cym. 4.2.291–5 (2613–17). Davies (1939, pp. 52–3): Hermia’s speech when she awakes to find her lover gone…is a piece of bravura which could only suffer by too realistic a performance, but which would have a considerable charm if performed as a set piece, as would Helena’s unhappy soliloquy earlier in the play [240–65].

800–5 Helpe…pray]
Dent (1964, p. 120 n.): Only Hermia has…an actual dream…, a prophetically accurate one.…The love-threatening serpent of her dream, symbol of male inconstancy, proves more destructive than the literal spotted snakes with double tongue [660] against which we have just heard the fairies sing. For spotted, double-tongued Demetrius see [119, 1093–6]. Weiss (1971, p. 88) notes that the snake charmed from Titania’s sleep soon crops up again; but—for us a fairly Freudian one—only in dream or nightmare; quoting 800–5 he observes: Threat is implicit here along with the possibility of greatest danger.…And the kind of cruelty that resides potentially in lovers themselves is surely indicated. Aronson (1972, p. 209): Whether we call this dream a premonition of what is to follow, Lysander’s smiling indifference to Hermia’s suffering, or the expression of a collective unconscious in which the snake-motif figures as repressed sexuality,…its ambivalent sexual character is evident. Faber (1972, p. 185): Hermia’s serpent gets into her nocturnal fantasy not only because it obviously, perhaps, too obviously, resembles the assaultive male member, but also because she associates it with effective deception. Garber (1974, pp. 6–7): Hermia’s dream of the serpent…functions locally in the plot as an intuition of bodily harm, but communicates at the same time a sense of the dark and irrational nature of dream vital to an understanding of the forest experience. Bevington (1978, p. 87): The serpent of which she dreams…is not an image of her own licentiousness but of an infidelity in which she is the innocent victim. Maguin (1980, pp. 568, 1048): The heart-eating serpent represents the cannibalistic perversion of the courtly ritual of the exchange of hearts.…Another image of the heart stolen by night is found at [1316–17] (Fr.). Holland (1978, p. 370): In effect, as Hermia tells the dream, she splits it into two parts. In the first, we hear the dream actually taking place. In the second, Hermia reports the dream to us after it is over. In the first part she makes a plea for help, but in the second we learn that Lysander wasn’t interested in helping at all—he was just smiling and watching the serpent eat Hermia. Holland (1989, pp. 10–15) notes that Hermia’s dream of the serpent is…the only real dream in the play. Everything else that is recounted by mortals or fairies as having been a dream experience is not a dream at all; he examines traditions of dream interpretation, using Artemidorus’s terms oneiros for a predictive dream, and enhypnion for a non-predictive one, such as anxiety-dreams and petitionary dreams. Granting that serpents were symbols of temptation and betrayal within a Christian context, he suggests the dream combines the serpent as phallus with the serpent as tempter, in that Hermia is troubled by her own desire for [Lysander] as much as his for her. He offers a reassuringly Freudian analysis of Hermia’s dream as enhypnion, enacting her anxiety about the problem of Lysander’s sexual desire, but finds it uncomfortably easy and unsatisfying; he notes that she provides an interpretation of the dream herself, in turning Demetrius into the threatening serpent [1093–6] and displacing the fear of Lysander into fear for Lysander and fear of Demetrius. He claims that her dream is fully oneiric in Artemidorus’s terms, and analyses its predictive nature and its associations with other parts of the drama. Roberts (1991, p. 81): Hermia’s dream of a snake crawling on her breast and eating her heart while Lysander sits smiling by…presumably reflects Helena’s supposed treachery in stealing Lysander from her [1315–17]; and later Lysander threatens to shake Hermia from him like a serpent [1291]. See also .
Green (1870, pp. 197–9) mentions these lines in connection with various emblems illustrating the well known fable of the Countryman and the Viper, and quotes Whitney’s explication of the motto In sinu alere serpentem,—To nourish a serpent in the bosom, which introduces Sinon who seems to have been the accepted representative of treachery in every form. Cf. 2H6 3.1.343–4 (1649–50). Walter (ed. 1964): Emblematic of grief and despair. In Ripa’s Iconologia, both emotions are represented by woodcuts of men with serpents gnawing at their breasts. Waddington (1988, p. 13) discusses both Whitney’s emblem and Ripa’s icons of Cordoglio and Dolore, concluding: In both of these personifications the idea represented by the serpent gnawing the heart is the potential self-destructiveness of emotional affliction—grief, anguish, sorrow—that is allowed to feed on one’s spirit.
A few other parallels are drawn. Fripp (1928, p. 593) compares Marlowe’s Dido (see ). Taylor(Hermia’s Dream, 2003, pp. 31–2) compares the snakes flung by Tisiphone into the breasts of Ino and Athamas in Golding’s Ovid 4.611–14 (see ); he further suggests that in the prospect of the loss of the innocence of childhood, Hermia’s dream also has about it shades of Eve and the Fall.

800–1 Helpe…brest]
Andrews (ed. 1993): As Hermia speaks [800–1] she is crying out during a nightmare. With [802] she stirs into consciousness.

802 Ay mee]
See n. 142 (Eigh me).

804 Me thought]
Schmidt (1875): = It seemed to me in sleep, I dreamt (OED, Methinks, v. 2a). The past tense is frequently used in literature from medieval times on in recounting a dream. See 1592, where Titania uses the expression in trying to evade confronting the actuality of her magically-induced infatuation with Bottom, and 1734–7, where Bottom comically repeats the expression as he tries to fathom what has happened to him. See also n. 6.

804 eate]
Thirlby (MS 1747–53) : Ate. White (ed. 1858): The present and preterite tenses were not distinguished even in pronunciation, but both had the pure sound of e [as in heat]. Doran (ed. 1959): Ate (pronounced et). Andrews (ed. 1989): Ate (and so pronounced). OED (Eat v.) allows all three pronunciations. See Dobson (1968, §30), and Cercignani (1981, p. 169). For the form of the preterite see Blake 4.2.4.1 (c)

805 And…pray]
Calderwood (1992, p. 40): Considered from an outside perspective, Hermia’s dream confirms what Sartre and Lacan maintain, that the potency of the gaze has nothing to do with eyes: the watching, smiling Lysander exists only in her fearful imagination. Within the dream, however, the male gaze has a gruesome bodily presence. Considered from this standpoint, the eating serpent is a physical extension of the smiling Lysander’s sadistic line of sight.

805 sate]
Variant form of sat (OED, Sit, v. A. 5 (δ)). See Franz §165; Blake 4.2.4.1. (c).

805 pray]
Variant form of prey (OED, n.). Schmidt (1875, 3): The act of preying. Mowat & Werstine (ed. 1993): I.e., attack (OED, n. 4a).

806–9 Lysander…feare]
Cf. Ariadne awaking, abandoned and crying out for Theseus; see Chaucer (LGW 2185–92; see ) and Ovid, Her. 10.9–23.

806, 807 what, What,]
For the construction and tone, see n. 436.

806 remou’d]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Have you moved away? Kittredge & Ribner (ed. 1966): Departed (OED, v. 9a). See also n. 169.

807 out of hearing]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Have you gone so far from me that you cannot hear my cries? See OED, Hearing, vbl. n. 1b, quoting this instance.

808 Alacke]
Johnson (1755): Alas; an expression of sorrow. OED (int.): An exclamation originally of dissatisfaction, reprobation, or deprecation = pity or shame that it should be so, and hence of regret or surprise. See 1974.

808 and if]
OED (And, conj.1 C.): Conj. conditional = If.…(1b): If: strengthened with following if. Abbott §105: If indeed. Blake 5.1.1 (iii): When…and occurs immediately before if, the two are taken together by most editors as a more elaborate form of if; but and is an adverb intensifying the clause as a whole. See Franz §564. See 1101. See also n. 313.

809 of all loues]
Rann (ed. 1787): I adjure you, as you love me; by all means. Singer (ed. 1826): By all that is dear. Delius (ed. 1859): For the sake of all love, or: of all the favours you can give (Ger.). Hunter (ed. 1870): For the love of all that should be loved; for love’s sake. Wright (ed. 1877): By everything that is loving. Kittredge (ed. 1939): By all the love that ever lover felt. OED (Love, n.1 7b), quoting this instance: A phrase of strong adjuration or entreaty. Obs. Used by Sh. elsewhere only in Wiv. 2.2.114 (878), and Oth. 3.1.12–13 (1530–1) but in Q only; the change in Ff, noted by Schmidt (1874, 1), to for loues sake, indicates the phrase’s obsolescence. But see Halliwell (ed. 1856) for other instances in 16th- and 17th-c. literature.
For of used in adjurations meaning for the sake of, by, see Abbott §169; Franz §518.

809 I…feare]
Bethell (1944, p. 71), arguing that much less can be expressed in action on the Elizabethan stage than on the modern, naturalistic stage, quotes this as an example of the audience being directly informed, in a brief phrase, of conditions which a modern actor would convey entirely without words.…This method of partial narrative is appropriate to poetic drama, since it renders the psychological situation clear without transferring attention from the verse to the processes of naturalistic induction.

809 swoune almost]
Rolfe (ed. 1877): I almost swoon. The transposition is a common one. See [Abbott §]29. F1’s unique reading sound seems to be a compositorial error caused by sound two lines directly above at 807, but it may be intentional: cf. OED (Sound, v.4): To swoon. Very common c1530–1650. For the excrescent d in swound in Q2 etc. see Franz §60; Cercignani (1981, p. 318). See 1781 (ribands), 2003 (vilde).

811 Either…immediately]
Bevington (1978, p. 89): The choice seems dire, but the comic sense of discrepancy assures us that the need for such a choice is only a chimera.

811 Either]
Monosyllabic or elided; see n. 402.

813–1506 Enter…well]
Verity (ed. 1893): We should observe how Shakespeare follows his usual plan of bringing the complications developed in the first two Acts to a crisis in the third Act (i.e. about the middle of the piece). In this play we have had two sets of complications: (1) the troubles of the lovers, and the anointment of Lysander’s eyes; (2) the dispute between Titania and Oberon, and the anointment of Titania’s eyes. Therefore we may expect two crises—and we get them: (1) in this scene, the meeting of Titania and Bottom, whence strange results; (2) in the next scene, the meeting of the pairs of lovers, whence also strange results. And in each case the main cause of confusion is the love-juice.

813–1020 Enter…silently]
Johnson (ed. 1765): In the time of Shakespear there were many companies of players, sometimes five at the same time, contending for the favour of the publick. Of these some were undoubtedly very unskilful and very poor, and it is probable that the design of this Scene was to ridicule their ignorance, and the odd expedients to which they might be driven by the want of proper decorations. Bottom was perhaps the head of a rival house, and is therefore honoured with an Ass’s head. Fleay (1886, p. 184): I believe the company originally satirised…was the Earl of Sussex’, Bottom, the chief clown, being intended for Robert Greene. Chambers (1923, 2:117): If any special company is satirized in Bottom and his fellows, I feel sure it must have been the Earl of Hertford’s. Sharpe (1935, p. 63): Sh.’s satire here was…immediate, specific, and personal, motivated by a really vital rivalry and applying directly to a current series of productions by the rival company, the Admiral’s men, to the author of these plays, Thomas Heywood, and to the principal actor in them, Ned Alleyn—and not without general and satirical implications against the typical audience and whole popular policy of the Henslowe-owned and Alleyn-dominated company.
Phillips (ed. 1969): This is the heart of the play: the ultimate transformation, the ultimate hallucination, the bringing together of incompatibles in a comic mock-marriage. Furness thinks the scene takes place by daylight, as is most probable for a rehearsal in early summer. For the argument of Furness (ed. 1895, p. xxxi), maintained despite Quince’s directive at 363, see .
For further critical commentary see , and on sources .

813 Enter the Clownes]
Doran (ed. 1959): The folio marks an act division here, but the first quarto indicates that the action was originally continuous, with the craftsmen rehearsing apart from where Titania lies sleeping. See also n. 676.

813 Clownes]
Granville-Barker (ed. 1914, p. iv): It is necessary, I am ashamed to say, to remark, that Clown does not, first of all, mean a person who tries to be funny. A clown is a countryman. See also . Wiles (1987, pp. 69–70): No generic term exists to cover the group of comic characters who play with the clown. Occasionally, therefore, the term is used generically for the clown and his companions. In Munday’s…John-a-Kent and John-a-Cumber, the dominance of the principal comedian is signalled by the direction for his and his companions’ first entry: Enter Turnop with his crew of clowns, and a minstrel.…In Shakespeare’s closely related Midsummer Night’s Dream, the author once writes Enter the Clownes when Bottom comes on with his companions [813]. However, when the clown (Bottom) is absent, we find instead a reference to the rabble [1746]. Two additional references to the clowns can be attributed to the book-keeper or editor [920, 2009; no SDs in Qq]. Cf. 266–8, n. 266–75. Snyder (1999, p. 35): Clown in Elizabethan usage can mean not only a kind of comic role, the ignorant bumpkin, but also the principal comedian of the acting company; she contrasts Bottom and Pompey with Touchstone. Schmidt (1874): 1) a rustic, churl…2) buffoon, jester (OED, n. 1, 3).

814 all met]
Bottom’s first words are perhaps ominous, for they echo Oberon’s first words to Titania, Ill met (434).

815 Pat, pat]
Johnson (1755), quoting this instance: Fit; convenient; exactly suitable either as to time or place. This is a low word, and should not be used but in burlesque writings. Rann (ed. 1787): Most opportunely. Deighton (ed. 1891): In the very nick of time. Baker (ed. 1896): Exactly as we agreed to meet. See OED, adv. 1. Thirlby (MS 1725–33) compares 1990. Wright (ed. 1877) compares Lr. 1.2.134 (463).

815–18 heres…Duke]
Stern (2000, p. 33): Quince…presents what seems to be a fusion between what I take to be a general rehearse (we will do it in action, as we will do it before the Duke, MND 817), and a partial rehearsal (sit downe…and rehearse your parts, MND 884). See also n. 359–63.

815 maruailes]
OED does not give this spelling as a variant form of marvellous. Clark & Wright (ed. 1863): Capell appears to have considered the reading of…Q1 as representing the vulgar pronunciation of marvellous, and he therefore printed it marvels. See textual n. Wright (ed. 1877): In the same manner wonders is found for wondrous in More’s Utopia (ed. Arber), p. 136 [1551, P2v]. Wilson (ed. 1924): A Shakespearian spelling. Or perhaps it is a compositorial spelling: see maruaile at 751. Kittredge (ed. 1939): A clipped form of marvellous. Kökeritz (1953, p. 270) speculates that here, at 1534, and in 2H4 (Q1) 5.1.35 (2823), -es may stand for -(o)us. For intensive adverbs like marvelous, having the same form as adjectives when occurring before other adverbs or adjectives, see Abbott §1, Franz §§241, 384, and Blake 5.1.3.1 (iii). See also n. 1534.

815 conuenient]
OED (a. 4a), quoting this instance: Suitable, appropriate. Obs.

816 rehearsall]
OED (n. 2a) quotes Cunningham’s suspect 1842 transcripts of the Revels Accounts of 1579–80 as the first use of this word; in this case Cunningham is supported by Feuillerat (1908, p. 326). OED quotes MND second. For a discussion of early uses see Stern (2000, pp. 22–5), Rehearsal lexically.

816–17 This…house]
Though both editors suggest that the locations could be indicated by gestures, Brooks (ed. 1979) argues that a stage door might be masked by a property-thicket and Foakes (ed. 1984) that A green cloth may have been used to suggest grass, and a hanging or property to suggest the brake or thicket. Dessen (1984, p. 61): To include an actual green plot and hawthorn brake is to lose part of the fun; rather, the original audience, looking at a real stage and tiring house, was being asked to imagine an imaginary brake and green plot metamorphosed into what they saw before them. See also Lieblein (1986), . Shapiro (1994, p. 152): Instead of inviting the spectators to pretend (along with his troupe) that the playing area is Pyramus’s father’s garden or Ninus’s tomb, as he [Quince] will later ask Theseus and his guests to do, he asks his troupe to imagine the forest as the playing area of a theatre, which of course to the audience it already is. What is for Peter Quince a triumph of the imagination—superimposing one scenic transformation upon another—is for the spectators a disenchanting yet comic return from the theatrical illusion they had agreed to enter to the everyday world where they sit in a playhouse. Williams (Moonlight, 1997, p. 20): This comic undercutting of the audience’s supposedly stable perception is integral to the play’s interest in the audience’s unfirm grasp of reality and identity.

817 hauthorne brake]
Wright (ed. 1877): Thicket of hawthorns. OED (Hawthorn 3. attrib.) quotes this instance. One of the plant’s alternative names, May (see n. 197), leads Wiles (1993, pp. 99–100) to argue that since Bottom’s transformation takes place here, it is linked to festive customs, and further to suggest that the bower to which Bottom is led is built from branches collected while maying. Webb (1991, p. 109): Presumably with no double sense allusion to green-room (the first recorded use of which is 1701). On the brake, see also nn. 886, 922.

817 tyring house]
Johnson (1755, Tiringhouse), quoting this instance: The room in which players dress for the stage. Collier (ed. 1842): I.e. Attiring-house. See OED, Tiring-house, Obs. or arch., quoting this instance first. Rolfe (ed. 1877): S. uses the word only here. Moberly (ed. 1881), questionably: Apparently retiring-house (not attiring; it was not a dress rehearsal).

818 in action]
Thirlby (1725–33), probably mistakenly: Blunder for exactly. Deighton (ed. 1891): Act it. Gill (ed. 1981): With gestures and movement (not merely reading the script). OED (Action, n. 12), giving only two instances, dated 1626 and 1710: Histrionic personation; acting of plays, performance. ? Obs. See n. 815–18.

819 Quince?]
Chambers (ed. 1897): Bottom has an important criticism to make. He clears his throat to call attention, and addresses himself in a loud voice to the stage-manager. Wilson (ed. 1924), explaining the alteration in his text from Q1’s ? to !: The ? as so often in the old texts stands for !. Exclamation marks were scarce with printers of this period; and the printer of the present quarto only possessed a few in an italic fount which he used occasionally. The highest number of ! in Q1 occurs in quire E where there are 7 italic and 1 roman !, for a total of 8. Since the compositor used only 2 in quire C, he had at least 6 in hand for quire D, where he used only 3. He could have employed an exclamation mark if he thought it was warranted, but obviously he felt that Bottom was about to put a question to Quince, which he does, at 824.

820 Quin.]
Thiselton (1903, p. 39): It should be observed that in assigning the speeches in this scene [Q1] uniformly uses Quince’s surname while [Q2] sometimes designates him by his Christian name: in which respect the Folio, except in its attribution of [901] to Puck, invariably follows [Q2], which surely strongly supports the view that the text of [Q2] was the basis adopted for that of the Folio.

820 bully…Bottom]
Sinclair (1966, p. 456) notes the happy choice of words in a Hungarian translation by János Arany, Buksi Zuboly: Zuboly is lumpish like its English counterpart: and it also has a lexicographic meaning of the yarn beam or roller of a weaving loom.…Buksi means bigheaded and also—a donkey!

820 bully]
Wright (ed. 1877): A term of familiarity addressed by his companions to a jolly blustering fellow [OED, n.1 1a, quoting this instance].…Florio (Ital. Dict.) gives, Bullo, a swaggerer, a swash-buckler [OED, n.1 3a]. It is used only by Sh.’s comic characters: here by Quince, by Flute at 1766, by the Host in Wiv. (frequently), by Pistol in H5, and by Stephano in Tmp. OED’s comment that in the popular etymological consciousness the word is perhaps now associated with bull n.1 may explain the fanciful speculation of Laroque (1984, p. 24): Quince’s address to Bottom as bully Bottom only a few lines before the latter’s translation, are clues which shift the stress from the myth of Actaeon to that of Pasiphae and of her bestial love for the white bull of Crete. Like the Minotaur,…ass-headed Bottom is another hybrid concealed in the heart of the forest’s maze. Cf. Golding’s Ovid 8.206–7, 225–6; see . See 1766.

820 bully,]
The intrusive comma is removed in all subsequent editions.

821 Comedy]
See n. 279–80.

823 abide]
Johnson (1755, v. a. 3): To bear without aversion; in which sense it is commonly used with a negative. Deighton (ed. 1891): Endure (OED, v. 17).

825 Berlakin]
Grey (1754, 1:57): By our ladykin, or little lady (OED, Byrlakin, quoting this instance). Halliwell (ed. 1856) adds: In allusion to the Virgin Mary. See also n. 279 (Mary). Hassel (2005, p. 191) notes that the expression is also used by the gentle Gonzalo, the reverend counsellor (Tmp. 3.3.1 [1516]). Blake 5.1.3.7: Such phrases which modify the whole clause rather than a word, are linked to interjections and various asseverations.…[They add] an emotional tone to the clause. Knowles (privately) notes: Franz §§104, 255 discusses this and other such interjections using the diminutive -kin.

825 parlous]
Grey (1754, 1:58): Perillous. Steevens (ed. 1773): A word corrupted from perilous, i.e. dangerous. See Abbott §461, on the contraction; Franz §25 on the e to a. Singer (ed. 1826): Alarming, amazing. Halliwell (ed. 1856): Excessive,…wonderful. Kittredge (ed. 1939): A vague emphasizer, like awful or frightful in modern jargon. See OED, Parlous, a. 2, quoting this instance. Becket (1815, 1:272), questionably: Peerless, i.e., very great.

826–7 when…done]
Schmidt (1874, Do 8): = after all. Verity (ed. 1912): Cf. when all is said and done (OED, Say, v.1 2l).

828 Not a whit]
Schmidt (1875, Whit): Not at all (OED, n.1 2b). See Blake 6.2.3.3 for the use of an intensifier after the negator.

828–33 I…feare]
Kittredge & Ribner (ed. 1966): Bottom has no understanding of the difference between drama and real life, and thus he is always concerned that the audience not take as real what is merely part of the play. Upon this inability to understand the nature of dramatic illusion much of the comedy of his role is based. Cope (1973, p. 223) finds here a classic statement about the radically double nature of the dramatic experience—at once illusionistic and anti-illusionistic. Bottom is at the center of wonder. Garber (1974, pp. 82–3): This is comic in part because the play as we perceive it is hardly realistic enough to contain the dangers he fears for it. But what is most telling about Bottom’s plan here is his emphasis upon the literal truth.…He sees the principal fiction of the Pyramus and Thisby play as somehow dangerous and misleading. By seeking to destroy or control the illusion, he demonstrates his faith in its power, together with his own limited understanding of that power.

828 I…well]
Chambers (ed. 1897): Bottom has not raised the difficulty without being prepared to solve it.

828 deuise]
See n. 364–5.

829 Write me]
For me as the old dative, where we should use for me, see Abbott §220. Blake 3.3.2.1 (a): The personal pronoun acts as an ethic dative, especially after imperatives. See Franz §294. See also n. 344, 345.

829 the Prologue]
Either the written speech, or the actor who plays the Prologue, as Quince does at 1905. Ignoring prologues such as those in Rom., 2H4, H5, or Per., the Clarkes (1879, pp. 689–90) condemn the use of an explanatory apology or prologue, to supply the deficiencies of former stage representation, and praise Sh. for introducing such clumsy expedients…merely in burlesque performances like the Show of the Nine Worthies and the Interlude of Pyramus and Thisbe. Chambers (ed. 1897): The Elizabethan prologue, unlike the Greek one, generally gave an outline of the coming plot.…Sometimes, however, it was rather of the nature of an address or apology from the actors or the poet to the audience. Ben Jonson so uses it; and that is what Bottom here proposes.

829 seeme to say]
Schmidt (1875, Seem 3): The word is almost periphrastical, quoting this use, and MV 2.4.10–11 (803–4), it shal seeme to signifie. Deighton (ed. 1891): Merely Bottomese for say. Thiselton (1903, p. 39) quotes Mac. 1.2.47 (70) where seemes to speake is probably equivalent to intimates speech of: so in [2045],…where seeme to be is the same as intimate that I am.…With Bottom here it is of course something of a redundancy, for seeme in the sense of intimate, is much the same thing as say. Kittredge (ed. 1939): Say as he acts his part. Cf. [2045]. Kinnear (1883, p. 91) argues that Seem means to indicate by dumb show, and that therefore: The humour lies in Bottom using a word which contradicts his object. In support he quotes Oth. 3.1.29 (1547), seeme to notifie, glossing: I.e. notify by dumb show, point out the way, beckon her.

831 more better]
See Abbott §11 for the use of more for the purpose of greater emphasis, quoting this instance. See Franz §217a; Blake 3.2.3.4 (b). Wright (ed. 1877): This double comparative was common in Shakespeare’s time, and is suitable to Bottom as being rather exaggerated language, and not because it was thought ungrammatical. See Schmidt (1875, More 5) for many examples. See also n. 587 (worser).

832–3 I…weauer]
Evett (1990, p. 229): Two features of the play represent the salient emphases of the last phase of Tudor art. The first is a self-conscious interest in the artist as character [quotes 832–3]. The second is an overt concern with audiences and onlookers, not only the publicly constituted audience of playhouse patrons watching a play about aristocrats gathered to see a play but also a hidden audience of voyeuristic stage managers peering through a screen of darkness and vegetation to see the results of their charms [quotes 892–3]. Kiernan (1996, p. 110): The actors are, in effect, relinquishing their roles before they’ve even started to rehearse the play.

833 put…feare]
Brooks (ed. 1979): Free them from fear: cf. I am out of fear / Of death [1H4 4.1.135–6 (2371–2)].

835 eight and six]
Malone (ed. 1790): I.e. in alternate verses of eight and six syllables. Collier (ed. 1842): I.e. In verse of fourteen syllables; the popular measure, which was sometimes divided into two lines, the first of eight and the last of six syllables. Delius (ed. 1859): The common English ballad meter (Ger.). Holzknecht & McClure (ed. 1937): The fourteen-syllabic line, with a marked caesura, which was usual in early sixteenth-century drama. Often printed in broken lines, e.g. Jasper Heywood’s translations of Seneca’s Thyestes (1560) and Hercules Furens (1561); in Thomas Newton’s Seneca The Tenne Tragedies these translations are printed in long lines. Capell’s (1783, 2.3:107) assertion that Bottom refers to lines rather than syllables, and so to the Shn. sonnet form, won little consent.
Smidt (1986, p. 121) is bothered by discrepancies between rehearsals of the interlude and its performance; it may be safely taken as an indication that Shakespeare to a certain extent extemporised as he wrote. Brooks (ed. 1979): It should not trouble us that the performance in v. i. does not tally with the plans and the rehearsal, either in the metre of the prologue, or in the dramatis personae…, or in the dialogue tried over at [895–917], which finds no place in it.

836–7 No…eight]
The Clarkes (ed. 1864): Bully Bottom has now turned critic as well as actor; objecting to everything, spying out defects, and contradicting for contradiction’s sake. Chambers (ed. 1897): Bottom’s only reason for objecting to eight and six is that he wants to have things his own way. Watt (ed. 1906): Bottom corrects merely for the pleasure of correcting. Holland (ed. 1994): Bottom always veers to excess.
Thiselton (1903, p. 39) sees broader implications: Behind all the changes that take place, whether in the dramatis personae or their speeches, or in the measure or manner of the prologue, or of the prologues, there is doubtless lying the thought that in the actual performances of the kind the original schemes of the poet or manager have to submit to much modification under the pressure of such circumstances as the limitations of the powers of individual actors, exigencies of stage representation, and the susceptibilities of members of the audience.

836–7 eight & eight]
Verity (ed. 1893): I.e. lines of four feet, also a favourite measure of simple, popular verse.

838–9 Will…you]
Gentleman (ed. 1774): The wonderful delicacy of these clowns fearing to fright the ladies, appears to be a stroke at some squeamish critics, who objected to deaths on the stage. Possibly Gentleman is referring to the critical debate over Horace’s Ars Poetica 182–5, which dictates that actions such as Medea’s murder of her children should occur offstage and be narrated. Castelvetro’s point that enacted deaths may produce the effect not of tragedy but of comedy (in Allan H. Gilbert, Literary Criticism: Plato to Dryden, Detroit, 1962, p. 350) is tested in the performance of the clowns’ play.

838 afeard]
Johnson (1755, 1): Frighted; terrified; afraid.…Now obsolete. Wright (ed. 1877): Though here used as a provincialism appropriate to rustics, the word was otherwise in good use, quoting MV 2.7.29 (1002). OED: Used more than 30 times by Shakespeare, but rare in literature after 1700. See Franz §169. Cf. 929.

838 Lyon]
Webb (1988, p. 31) suggests that since the lion was of old emblematic of youthful desire, it might signify that here by implication. He points ahead to 2090, and gives examples in Sh. of the lion used in analogy of human relations and copulation from AWW 1.1.91–2 (95–6); LLL 4.1.88–9 (1068–9); Tit. 4.1.98–9 (1646–7).

839 I…it]
Deighton (ed. 1891): I fear they will be afraid. Holland (ed. 1994): Does Starveling mean that he is afraid of the lion or that he is afraid the ladies will be afraid? Actors have played the line both ways.

839 promise]
Johnson (1755, v. n. 2), quoting this instance: It is used of assurance, even of ill. See OED, v. 5. See 1012–13.

840–56 Masters…Ioyner]
Watts (1983, p. 21), attacking structuralist, post-structuralist, and deconstructionist theorists, claims this passage reveals the real source of these doctrines. Here we see Bottom challenging illusionist realism, commending the self-subverting text and the dance of the signifiant, advocating the scriptible and even inaugurating the jouissance which occurs when the garment gapes. Parker (2004, pp. 660–1) compares Marx’s comment that Prussia wants to play the European lion, but as Snug, the joiner.…Marx’s reference to Snug commits the very misprision that Snug and his colleagues feared.…It says that for all Prussia’s disavowals, the lion costume signifies something truly lion-like about its wearer—namely, a predatory aggression. In the same way, Bottom’s ass-head is of course supposed to signify something genuinely asslike about his character.

840 Masters]
See n. 283.

840 your selfe]
Wells (ed. 1967): The Folio has yourselves [i.e. your selues]; but the Quarto’s singular form may well be a deliberate touch. See textual n. Foakes (ed. 1984): F corrects what was probably intended as one of Bottom’s solecisms. See n. 126 (your selfe). Thirlby (MS 1725–33) compares MM 2.2.126 (884).

840 selfe,]
The heavier stop introduced by Rowe finds favor as late as Brooks (ed. 1979); see textual n. Wright (ed. 1877) notes that with a comma the construction…is you ought to consider with yourselves (that) to bring in &c.

841–2 God…thing]
Malone (ed. 1790, 10:578–9): There is an odd coincidence between what our author has here written for Bottom, and a real occurrence at the Scottish court in the year 1594. Prince Henry the eldest son of James the First was christened in August in that year. While the king and queen, &c. were at dinner, a triumphal chariot (the frame of which, we are told, was ten feet long and seven broad) with several allegorical personages on it, was drawn in by a black-moore. This chariot should have been drawne in by a lyon, but because his presence might have brought some feare to the nearest, or that the sight of the lighted torches might have commoved his tameness, it was thought meete that the Moore should supply that room. A true account [i.e. reportarie] of the most triumphal and royal accomplishment of the baptism of the most excellent, right high, and mighty prince, Henry Frederick, &c. as it was solemnized the 30th day of August, 1594. 8vo. 1603 (rpt. Nichols, 1823, 3:353–69; see also ed. [Edinburgh, 1594], by [William Fowler], C3r–4r, STC 11214.6). Knight (ed. 1839): It is not improbable that Shakspere meant to ridicule this incident. Rickert (1923, p. 67), quoting also 333–9 and 2022–5, pursues her argument that King James is satirized in Bottom: This thrice-repeated joke is commonly taken as referring to the Stirling episode, but two factors not before noted in this connection are the notorious timidity of James and his part in devising the pageant, which would have led to the belief that he was responsible (as he may have been) for the change of plan. Because this reference posed a difficulty for his contention that MND was originally written for the May 2, 1594 wedding of the Countess of Southampton and Sir Thomas Heneage, Hunter (1998, p. 11) claims: It must be an ad lib by the clown in the 1596 production that found its way into the text.
Others find different analogues. Lanier (1902, 2:285) argues that Sh is exquisitely and gaily turning upside down Greene’s accusation of plagiarism: Listen to Bottom and his captivating asses discussing, not a tiger’s heart in a player’s hide, but a player’s heart in a lion’s hide. Walter (ed. 1964): The phrase…following the proverb, A lion among sheep and a sheep among lions [Tilley L309], could imply one who was only courageous in the presence of ladies. Velz (1988, pp. 48–9), referring to Brooke’s Romeus and Juliet, ll. 257–8 (see Bullough, 1957, 1:292–3): There is irony in Brooke’s description of lionlike Mercutio boldly making a stir among the lamblike maids—if we recall the proverb.…When he had the mechanicals regard their artificial lion as likely to be so terrible among ladies that they would shriek, Shakespeare retained and ludicrously exaggerated the ironies Brooke had slyly suggested about Mercutio. He also added a deft touch—it is by their anxiety lest Snug, their entirely inadequate lion, prove too fearsome that the sheeplike mechanicals reveal how timorously unleonine they are.

841 God…vs]
A precatory formula seeking protection; see OED, Shield, v. 1. Cf. 1496 and 1980. Cuningham (ed. 1905) erroneously compares Rom. 4.1.41 (2336), a deprecatory phrase = God forbid (OED, Shield, v. 5).

842–3 fearefull…foule]
Cuningham (ed. 1905): I think the source of this well-known expression of Bottom’s is to be found in Lord Berners’s Huon of Burdeaux, (ed. Lee [1883] 1975, 1:427): Huon behelde [the Gryffon] and sawe howe he was crewell fowle.…ferfull it was to beholde hym.

842–3 wilde foule]
Delius (ed. 1859): Bottom intends wild beast (Ger.). OED (Wild-fowl 1b), quoting this instance first: Humorously misapplied to a wild beast; hence allusively, also fig. of persons. Farrow (ed. 1972): Bottom doesn’t mean lions have wings, but that’s the effect.

842 fearefull]
Johnson (1755): 1. Timorous; timid; easily made afraid.…4. Terrible; dreadful; frightful; impressing fear. Evans (ed. 1974): (1) Dreadful (as referring to a lion); (2) full of fear (as referring to a bird). See OED 1 and 3.

843 your]
See n. 354–6.

843–4 looke toote]
Johnson (1755, 21), quoting this instance: To watch; to take care of. OED (Look, v. 21e), quoting this instance first: To look to it: to be careful, beware. See also n. 294. OED (Toot): Coalesced form of to it.

847–56 Nay…Ioyner]
Gentleman (ed. 1774): This device of Bottom’s to render the lion a familiar creature is admirably ludicrous, and very natural for such a wise stage projector. Priestley (1925, p. 4): His sketch of the prologue has in it the true note of artful entreaty.…Such a speech points to both knowledge of the sex and long practice,…We should like to see Bottom making love among his own kind; the result would have startled some of his critics. Farrow (ed. 1972): Like Bottom with the ass’s head. Snug ends up a kind of comic monster, half a beast and half a man—which is suitable, since the foolish players have no more brains than beasts. Barkan (1986, pp. 265, 187) suggests Bottom’s notion of the stage lion is borrowed from the images of characters in the midst of Ovidian transformation such as Lycaon and Actaeon depicted by Salomon (Metamorphose d’Ovide figurée, Lyon, 1557); however, the comparison is inexact since those images do not have a human face. Hawkes (1992, p. 24), tendentiously, believes the serious point animating this speech concerns the complex nature of the relationship between performer and audience, player and part.…The essence of that relationship lies less in its capacity to afford entertainment or distraction from material social reality, than in the reverse: its capacity to confirm and reinforce the complexities of the social fabric. For to be a man as other men are involves, in this setting, commitment to a social role over and above one’s temporary, liberating carnival or festival role in a play. And it is this continuing social role that, in an oral community, finally imprints itself upon its bearer in the form of a name.

847 Nay]
Schmidt (1875): Used, not simply to deny or refuse, but to reprove, to correct, or to amplify that which has been said before (OED 5a), quoting 1348. See 1348, 1377. Cf. n. 309.

847 name his name]
For the use of the verb with the cognate object see OED, Name, v.1 7a, quoting LLL 3.1.166 (931). See 855. Cf. nn. 861–2, 885. There is possibly an echo of Gen. 48.16, let my name be named in them, consonant with Bottom’s predilection for biblical sounding phrases.

849 speake through]
Thiselton (1903, pp. 39–40): If Bottom pronounced through the same as true…, there will be a further point than the mere error in his use of the word defect [849]. He refers to speake true at 1919, but see Cercignani (1981, p. 329): Of a dialectal change of ƥ to t Shakespeare’s works provide (despite Kökeritz, 320) no reliable evidence.

849 defect]
Delius (ed. 1859): For effect. Wright (ed. 1877): Bottom’s blunders are usually very intelligible. Gordon (ed. 1910): A commentator must not spoil these blunders by explaining them. Andrews (ed. 1989): But in fact the audience would see the device Bottom proposes in this passage as a defect in dramatic art. Idem (ed. 1993) adds: In a larger dramatic scheme, however, the one that makes this a Sweet Comedy [1789], this Effect Defective comes by Cause [Ham. 2.2.103 (1131)].

850–1 I would wish…intreat you]
See n. 360–1.

852 my…yours]
Deighton (ed. 1891): I stake my life for yours; I pledge you by my life that there is no reason for you to fear. Proverbial; see Dent (L260.1). Not in Tilley.

852–6 If…Ioyner]
Wiles (1993, p. 61), arguing that the mechanicals’ interlude is a parodic version of a wedding masque: Bottom advises the lion, qua symbol of the bridegroom, to identify himself as an ordinary human being, not as a paragon of sexual potency. The evocative name Snug the joiner suggests a cosy, non-violent act of sexual union. Cf. OED, Snug, a.1 2a.

853 it…life]
Wright (ed. 1877): It were a sad thing for my life, that is, for me. Moberly (ed. 1881): It would be all over with my life. Johnson (ed. 1894): He means that he is fearful of being punished for having frightened the ladies. Doran (ed. 1959): My life would be in danger. Gill (ed. 1981) adds: (He would have to ask for pity). Foakes (ed. 1984): I.e. I should be sorry, by my life, or my life would be to be pitied; of my life may relate to pity, but it was used commonly as a mild oath. See [2029]. Proverbial; Dent (P368.1): It is Pity of his (etc.) life. Cf. TN 2.5.12 (1027).

853 of]
Abbott §174: Of [ = ] concerning, about, quoting this instance. See Franz §494 Anm. 1; Blake 5.4.2 Of (g). Cf. nn. 999, 1652.

854 I am no such thing…are]
Proverbial; Dent (M395.1). Shaheen (1993, p. 96), citing Acts 14:15 and 10:26: Perhaps an analogy rather than a biblical reference. In Huon of Burdeux Oberon tells Charlemayn I am a mortall man as ye be; see .

854–6 let…Ioyner]
Malone (1780, 1:116–17) believes Sh. may be referring to an incident recorded in a manuscript titled Merry Passages and Jeasts (MS Harleian 6395, f36v): There was a spectacle presented to Q: Elizabeth vpon the water, and amongst others, Har: Golding: was to represent Arion vpon the Dolphins backe, but finding his voice to be very hoarse and vnpleasant when he came to performe it, he teares of his Disguise, and sweares he was none of Arion not he, but eene honest Har: Goldingham; which blunt disco= verie pleasd the Queene better, then if it had gone thorough in the right way; yet he could order his voice to an instrument exceeding well. Thoms (1839, p. 29) believes the anecdote derives from the entertainment at Kenilworth in 1575, where Arion appears on his oold freend the Dolphin; Goldingham is not named as the performer, but is referred to earlier as having lent Captain Cox a velluet cap at the same festivities (Laneham, A Letter, 1575, F2v, E3v). Knight (ed. 1839): It is by no means improbable that Shakspere was familiar with this local anecdote, and has applied it in the case of Snug the joiner. Brooks (ed. 1979, at 847): The story…rests on the dubious authority of a jest-book, much after Elizabeth’s time…, and sounds reminiscent of MND itself.

855 name…name]
See n. 847.

857 there is]
Abbott §335: Passages in which the quasi-singular verb precedes the plural subject…are very common, particularly in the case of There is. Deighton (ed. 1891) adds, Though here probably we have an intentional vulgarism. See Franz §672; Blake 6.1.1.2. See 1763.

858–72 to…Moone-shine]
Graves (1999, p. 207): No one suggests that a few token properties associated with night be brought onstage. Bottom proposes an illusionistic technique that is patently absurd. But although we may find Quince’s emblematic solution just as ludicrous, there is reason to believe that the Elizabethans found it less so, or at any rate considered a man holding a lantern only a little less sophisticated than other emblems for night—representations of moons and stars, for example. Halliwell (ed. 1856) quotes from the anonymous play Lingua (1607) where such personifications are parodied when the sky is presented by a Page bearing a Shield before Cœlum, clad in Azure Taffata, dimpled with Starres, a Crowne of starres on his head, and a scarfe resembling the Zodiack ouerthwart the Shoulders (Act.3.Sc.6., F4r). The image needs explicating: Phantastes asks, What blew thing’s that, that’s dappled so with Starres, and on being told He represents the heau’n, observes In my conceit it were pritty, if hee thundred when he speakes (F4v). Graves (1999, p. 262) refers to Johannes von Rist’s 1666 account of a German farce probably derived from MND which he saw in his youth, where the actors consider cutting a moon out of red and yellow paper and sticking it on the wall, or hanging up a piece of rotten wood that would glow in the dark, before settling on a lantern hung on a butcher’s skewer, which one of us will carry and move along with it a little every quarter hour; see Brennecke, 1964, p. 65.
McDonald (1994, p. 97): Shakespeare’s evocation of this problem of representing moonshine, in the context of the other problems of staging that are politically resonant, serves as a way to situate his play in relation to the court and court ideology.…[Quince] opts for disfiguring moonshine in the person of folklore, an iconography that predates the courtly Cynthia/moon symbolism. In evoking the folkloric man in the moon, Quince opens the way for the parody of the aristocratic strategy of iconologizing. See also n. 870–2.

859–60 Pyramus…Moone-light]
Brooks (ed. 1979) compares Golding’s Ovid 4.123. See .

859–64 Moone-light…Moone-shine]
This is the last reference to moonlight; except for 1936 (where the interlude’s lovers are said to meet by moonshine), all subsequent references to moonshine are to the personification, or disfigurement. Rabkin (1967, p. 202) may overstate the case in claiming for Shakespeare’s audience as for us, moonshine is nonsense. However, Sh.’s uses of the word in other plays are either ironically deprecatory as in Lr. 1.2.5 (339), 2.2.32 (1105), and LLL 5.2.208 (2107) (see OED, n. 1d and 2a), or associated with fairies as in Wiv. 5.5.38 (2520), 5.5.102 (2584), Rom. 1.4.65 (517), and Tmp. 5.1.37 (1988). He uses moonlight in only two other plays, in romantic contexts: in Lorenzo’s evocation of night (MV 5.1.54 [2466]), and in the moment of Kate reaching accord with Petruchio (Shr. 4.5.3 [2298]). See also n. 222–3.

861 Sn.]
Most editors give this line to Snout, but Sisson (1956, 1:127) argues: We should not deprive Snug of his one line in this scene.

861–2 play…Play]
The construction may be influenced by Bottom’s twice employing name his name (847, 855), but it is not uncommon: OED (Play, v. 31a) quotes Udall in 1548, Players on the stage do playe theyr playe, and also Ham. 3.2.88 (1940), whilst this play is playing. Cf. also 1H4 2.4.484 (1443), play out the play.

863 Calender…Almanack]
OED (Calendar, n. 2): A table showing the divisions of a given year into its months and days,…often also including important astronomical data, and indicating ecclesiastical or other festivals, and other events belonging to individual days.…Also a series of tables, giving these facts more fully; an almanac. Halliwell (ed. 1856) to show the manner in which moonshine was noted prints a facsimile of a page from the Almanacke of Walter Gray for 1591 (STC 451.4), where each quarter is represented by a crescent or round, together with a statement of the hour and minute of the phase. Each month’s calendar is headed by a verse; Sh. might have enjoyed the one for May: When May and Wyll accorde in one, Then all wagges well, as Louers wysh: If Wyl chaunce quayle when May is gon, Then starues poore Wyl, amyds his blisse (A8v). Neil (ed. 1878): A calendar is a perpetual almanac, and an almanac an annual calendar. The former refers to time in general, the latter only to that portion of time occupied in one circuit of the earth round the sun;…As the mingling of incongruities is one of the chief characteristics of a dream, it is one of the minor sly humours of Shakespeare in this play to make Bottom thus call for a calendar, to find out moonshine, as the Greek months always commenced with the new moon, real lunar months being that by which they reckoned. As they had no calends, the Roman phrase ad calendas Græcas meant proverbially never. Sir Harry Nicholas, in his Chronology of History, has pointed out the interesting fact, which is very little known, that an effort was made to reform the calendar in this country, in the reign of Queen Elizabeth, when Shakespeare had just come of age, by the introduction of a bill, entitled: An Act, giving Her Majesty authority to alter and make a new calendar, according to the calendar used in other countries. This bill was read a first time in the House of Lords, on the 16th of March (27 Eliz.) 1584–5. But the bill was not passed into an act. Perhaps the controversy arising from this attempt, suggested the use of the phrase here employed.

note.p: F1 inserts the SD Enter Pucke. At 888 Q1 has the SD Enter Robin which is also in F1. Most editors since Rowe (ed. 1709) regard the F1 SD at 865 as an error and omit it (see textual n.). Greg (1955, p. 244) asserts F1’s SD incorrectly duplicates Qq’s, and is among the signs of the clumsiness and incompetence of the F1 editor. Some commentators defend it. Thiselton (1903, p. 40): Puck will enter here probably crossing the back of the stage, and taking up a position of vantage for eavesdropping (i.e. in the Brake [886]), it being necessary that he should have some premises from which to draw the conclusion that a Play was toward [892]. Wells (ed. 1967): It is possible that the Folio’s apparently superfluous direction represents an early stage-practice of bringing Puck in to watch the mechanicals before he speaks. Wells & Taylor (1987, pp. 281–2) contrary to earlier practice, influentially retain the F1 SD and omit the Qq SD, attributing the duplication in F1 to passive error. They argue that Robin’s appearance immediately after Bottom calls for a calendar would, to the audience, guarantee comic mischief, even before he spoke; that the call for the book suggests the possibility that Robin might somehow comically supply it, or contribute to associated comic business; and that his silent presence throughout the following dialogue also creates dramatic opportunities. Holland (ed. 1994) follows the Wells & Taylor SDs, conceding that in a play so full of silent spectators Robin’s presence is not unlikely, and that there is evidence of theatrical viability in production. Implying an inclination to restore the later SD, he suggests: F’s second entry for Robin [888] could also mean little more than comes forward (compare F’s entry for Oberon and Robin at [1385] when they have clearly never left the stage). (Cf. Dessen & Thomson, 1999, pp. 54, 84 on come and enter.) Holland concludes: The evidence [for the earlier entry] is cumulatively persuasive, but I have a nagging suspicion that what, a play toward? [892] suggests that Robin has only just noticed what they are doing, unlikely if he has been watching them for twenty lines. For discussion of other discrepancies in SDs in this scene, see n. 900–36.

div.note:
866 Yes…night]
Commentators provide various solutions of the apparent contradiction of 6–14. Sprague (ed. 1896): Let Quince, not Shakespeare, blunder! All the better for that? Wilson (ed. 1924) avers that the problem is caused by lines 4–14 having been added at a time of revision. Phillips (ed. 1969): If we are to take the timing of the wedding for a new moon seriously, we shall have to remember that no night in the action could have had much moonlight. As there is such insistence on the moon throughout the play we must believe in the moonlight—although it is perhaps part of Shakespeare’s vast confidence trick.
