Morris (1985, p. 242), defending his time scheme against the contrary evidence of this line: (1) Quince’s assertion is one of the blockhead mistakes made by the rustics.…He makes the very same mistake the lovers make, not seeing that young love, symbolized by the very moon he thinks will always be there, will not always be there, will not always end happily. (2) Quince cannot know, although the almanac promises moonlight, that the sky will not be overcast, thus casting doubt over any such assertions. (3) I confess there is some moonlight, and Quince does no more. (4) In actual fact, the rustics are compelled to provide their own moon, indicating that there is not enough for their purposes.
It is not clear whether there is an actual prop. Thiselton (1903, p. 40) implies not: We must take this to be Quince’s deduction from a statement he has seen in a Calendar that there is a new moon on the night in question, which would be sufficiently ridiculous to raise a laugh. But Andrews (ed. 1993): The always prepared Peter Quince (see [365–7]) has brought a calendar to the rehearsal. Griffiths (ed. 1996, p. 144): Brook’s Moth threw down the almanac from the gallery (promptbook).

867–9 Why…casement]
Gentleman (ed. 1774): The obvious simplicity of this device is truly pleasant, and the various difficulties started in this scene, are admirable burlesque upon those laborious play-wrights who puzzle themselves with trifles, and after much pains reconcile them in a foolish, and less laughable manner. Clayton (1979, p. 19): Once in Ovid [Ex Ponto 3.3.5] a moon enters at a casement window—bifores intrabat luna fenestras. Graves (1999, pp. 155–6, 154) finds the suggestion startling that the windows be opened so that real moonshine might light the scene, since he contends that opening or shutting windows in playing spaces had nothing to do with indicating light or darkness in any illusionistic sense.

867–8 great chamber]
Halliwell (ed. 1856): The state-room. Cuningham (ed. 1905): The large reception-room in Elizabethan houses. Fairchild (1937, p. 19): Only the more pretentious houses belonging to the upper classes had a great or presence chamber, generally known as the Great Chamber of Estate.

870–2 one…Moone-shine]
See OED, Man in the Moon 1 and 2 for examples referring both to the imagined figure or face seen in the disk of the moon, and to the expression used as the type of an imaginary person. Thirlby (MS 1733–47) cites Tmp. 2.2.138–41 (1182–5) for another description of the man in the moon. Hunter (ed. 1870): An old superstitious notion about the man in the moon identified him with the man that gathered sticks upon the Sabbath-day, [Num. 15:32–6]. He was imagined to have a bush of thorns, or a bundle of faggots, on his back, and a dog at his side; he also quotes Henryson’s Testament of Cresseid, 260–3 (Chaucer, Woorkes, 1561, 203v). Knowles (privately) suggests that Moonshine would hold the bush in front of the lantern to represent the moon shining through the trees. Dyer (1883; 1966, pp. 65–6) notes that Dante calls him Cain (Inferno 20.124–6; Paradiso 2.49–51), and mentions various other beliefs of the figures in the moon—German (a woman with the man, instead of a dog), Jewish (Jacob), Chinese (rabbit), and Ceylonese (hare). Shaheen (1993, pp. 96–7) believes Sh.’s references to be based on English legend, as in Henryson, since the legend deriving from Num. was not current in England. For extensive examples of legends and superstitions connected with the figure, see Douce (1807, 1:15–17), commenting on Tmp. 2.2.138–41 (1182–5); Halliwell (1841, pp. 52–8 and ed. 1856 at 2045); Neil (ed. 1878 at 2045); and Emerson (1906, pp. 840–5). Brooks (ed. 1979 at 2045) cites Tilley (M240): The man in the moon, and Chaucer, Troilus and Criseyde, 1.1024.
Cope (1973, p. 222–3), quoting the stage directions and dialogue presenting a well by means of a bucket, boughs, and grass in Narcissus (1602): Enthusiasm for such outrageous symbolic props among amateur country players…seems to have been a common target of satire. Weimann (2000, pp. 81–2): What this richly loaded parody conveys in one sentence is so much more than a correspondence, let alone an equation, between disfigurement and presentation.…What is at issue is the depth of the gap that, unquestioningly, is taken for granted between what is to be represented and what and who is doing the (re)presenting. See also n. 1934–5.

871 lātern]
Q2F here and at 1934 change the standard spelling to lanthorn. See n. 2039.

871 disfigure]
Thirlby (MS 1747–53): Pro figure. Deighton (ed. 1891): Personate. Chambers (ed. 1897): Bottom means figure forth. Black (1965, p. 43): Perhaps the idea of disguise enters into his mistake. Cf. OED (Disfigure, v. 2): To alter the figure or appearance of; to disguise. Obs.; but still in use in the 16th century. Twycross & Carpenter (1981, p. 32), discussing this obsolete usage in the Chester Banns: The terms disguise and disfigure themselves suggest a recognition of the presence of the usual guise or figure beneath the mask. Miriam Joseph (1947, p. 75) gives this as an example of the ignorant misapplication of words, a form of cacozelia,…which sometimes achieves, as it were unconsciously, a happy hit through the misused word. Freedman (1991, p. 160): By mistakenly associating presentation and disfigurement, Quince reminds us that Elizabethan playwrights whose comparisons were perceived as a threat to the status quo could be physically disfigured by the state. See n. 59.

871–2 present]
Thirlby (MS 1747–53): Pro represent. See OED, v. 7b. See 878, 1036.

872 person]
OED (n. 1), quoting this instance: A character…in a drama.

873–5 Pyramus…wall]
For some sources and analogues of the story, all of which agree on this essential element, see .

875 chinke]
Thomas (1587, Rima): A chinck, chap, cleft, rift or crannie in wood or stone where it is not close ioyned. Johnson (1755), quoting this instance: A small aperture longwise; an opening or gap between the parts of any thing. See OED, Chink, n.2 2. Rudd (1979, p. 238, n. 22) observes tenui rima is used in an obscene sense by Juvenal (Sat. 3.97). Webb (1989, p. 24): Possibly in ref[erence] to the sexual orifices of the body; he cites Florio (1611, Fesso): Cleft…. Also a womans priuy chinke, quaint or water-box. Taylor (1990, p. 58; Everything, 2003, pp. 57–8) cites various Renaissance dictionaries and schoolbooks to show that chink and cranny were standard translations of Latin rima. Kennedy (2005, p. 18): Ovid mentions the wall’s significant defect (fissus erat tenui rima 4:65), even before the important information that it was common to both houses. The aperture has various names in English: Golding, crany; Chaucer, clifte; Gower, An hole which the lovers themselves make; Christine, clefte, hole; Conusaunce, chenke; Cooper, hole; GGGI, a litle rifte. Shakespeare has chinke (875, 1932, 1979), crany (881, 1963), a cranied hole or chinke (1959), hole (2003, 2004), using these variously for amusing rhymes or innuendo. See esp. nn. 1959, 1964, 1979, 2003–4. On the association in Shn. times of chinks and holes in walls with sexual activity, see Orlin (2007, pp. 152–4).

876 Sno.]
F2–F4 interpret Q2F1’s Sn. as Snug (see textual n.).

878–82 Some…whisper]
Chambers (1923, 3:98) suggests a wall may occasionally have been drawn across the stage, instancing Act 2 of Rom. where Romeo must have a wall to leap.…What is Pyramus and Thisbe but a burlesque of the Romeo and Juliet motive, which would have been all the more amusing, if a somewhat conspicuous and unusual wall had been introduced into its model? Craik (1958, p. 18), presuming the wall important to the staging of Thomas Garter’s Susanna (1578) to have been of lath-and-canvas: Such a wall was ideally required for the artisans’ play.… The comedy of [872–8] depends, not upon the supposedly primitive expedients of our early drama, but upon the artisans’ fantastic circumvention of what they ought to have been able to do; and the irony is the greater if it is Snug the joiner, the very man who should have built the stage wall, who sees the difficulty as insurmountable. In Peter Squenz, the 17th-c. German farce derived from MND, the first suggestion is to smear one of us all over with lime and plaster.…When Pyramus must talk, he must talk into the mouth, that is the hole in the wall. And when Thisbe wants to say something, the wall must turn his mouth in her direction; the devise adopted, however, is a picture frame with a paper depiction of a wall (see Brennecke, 1964, p. 77). See also n. 1930–1.
Some commentators find wider significances in the wall. Plasse (1992, p. 32) argues that using actors for Moonshine and Wall rather than inanimate property devices suggests a Shakespearean acknowledgment of the human body as a primary mediator between the world of the theater and the world outside the theater, between the substantial world of the stage and the insubstantial world of conceptions. Hawkes (1992, p. 29): All societies make use of walls, literally or metaphorically deployed, and they obviously supply a major means of generating and reinforcing meaning in any culture. To breach a wall, or to transgress the boundary it marks, risks challenging the structure of differences on which meaning in a society is based.…Pyramus and Thisbe’s Wall, which aids and abets its own breaching, clearly foregrounds the issue of boundaries and their transgression. See also n. 1931.

878 present]
See n. 871–2.

879–80 plaster…cast]
Fairchild (1937, p. 25), while recognizing Bottom’s words as humorous, nevertheless observes that Sh.’s allusions to plastering are couched in language chiefly derogatory; he instances Ham. 3.1.50 (1702), Cor. 1.4.31–2 (526–7), and 2H6 4.2.132 (2452).

879 lome]
OED (Loam, n. 2): A composition of clay and sand with an admixture of horse-dung, chopped straw, or the like, used in making bricks,…plastering walls, etc. See 1962.

879–80 rough cast]
Johnson (1755, Roughcast 2), quoting this instance: A kind of plaister mixed with pebbles, or by some other cause very uneven on the surface (OED, n. 2a, quoting this instance first). See 1962.

880 about]
OED (prep. 1): On the outside, on the outer surface of; on every side of, all around, around, surrounding. Bottom gives only vague indication of the extent to which the character should display some characteristic material of a wall.

880 or]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Altered by Collier’s M.S. Corrector into and, a reading which…does not seem to be necessary [see textual n.]. Bottom mentions two alternative ways in which the wall may be symbolized; first, by the actor appearing daubed with marks of his occupation; secondly, as the story was so well known, by his holding his hand out with the first and second fingers separated from the third and fourth to signify a chink in the wall. It is true that in the representation both means are adopted, but it does not follow that this was the original intention.

880–1 let…thus]
Phillips (ed. 1969): It is tempting to suppose that the gesture was a rude one. Hasler (1974, p. 88): Thus is…the most categorical of text-directions; it is precise with regard to timing, but vague as to the exact nature of the action required.…[W]hen the time comes to show his chink, every new Wall is still free to invent his own cranny. Clayton (1974, p. 103) sees no cogent reasons to infer that V-shaped fingers, rather than circle-making finger(s)-and-thumb, are necessarily entailed in thus. Dessen (1984, p. 56) envisages a circle-making gesture of fingers or even both hands. See also nn. 875, 878–82, 1959, 1979.

884 euery…sonne]
For the same phrase see n. 340.

884 reherse]
OED (Rehearse, v. 5a), quoting 884 and 1033 first: To go through or practise (a play, scene, part, etc.) in private, in preparation for a more formal or public performance. Holland (ed. 1994): Go over, repeat; the workers will be checking they have learned their roles while the rehearsal is going on. See OED, v. 1b. See n. 815–18.

885 spoken…speech]
A common expression: see OED, Speak, v. 22b, quoting this instance. Cf. MM 5.1.263–4 (2642–3), spoke…speeches. Cf. nn. 847, 861–2.

886 enter…Brake]
Hunter (1845, 1:295–6) argues that rather than meaning a clump of bushes or thicket (OED, n.2), brake here refers to a little space with rails on each side, which in this instance were formed or at least intertwined with hawthorn [OED, n.6 1].…We must form a correct idea of the brake before we can truly apprehend the meaning of the words of Puck [quotes 1036–39]. Bottom had entered one of these little inclosures, and Puck came behind him and fixed the ass’s nowl on his head. Neil (ed. 1878) embellishes the picture: Bottom entered the brake, leant upon the sparred sides of it meditatively, Puck came cunningly behind him, and at this advantage fixed an ass’s nowl upon his head. Both apparently envisage a realistic staging, with the transformation taking place in sight of the audience; cf. the removal of the ass-head at 1601–2. See also nn. 816–17, 817, 1037.

887 cue]
Johnson (1755, 2), quoting this instance: The last words of a speech which the player who is to answer catches, and regards as intimation to begin. Singer (ed. 1826) adds: Generally written out with that which was to be learnt by rote. For discussion of the etymology of cue, see OED, n.2. See further n. 913.

888 Enter Robin]
See n. [865].

889–91 What…Queene]
Wood (ed. 1902), fancifully: Puck naturally contrasts the rude Athenian swains with the fairy grace of Titania. They are to her as coarse hemp is to the flowers and gossamer threads that form the cradle of the fairy queen. See also n. 891. Freedman (1991, p. 155), unconvincingly: [This is] a question of the precise and limited conditions under which the different classes of poets and patrons can meet, which the play answers…through its fawning collaboration with state ideology.

889 hempen homespunnes]
OED (Hempen, a.1), quoting this instance only: Hempen homespun, homespun cloth made of hemp; hence, one clad in such cloth, one of rustic and coarse manners. Johnson (Homespun, 1755), quoting this instance: A coarse, inelegant, rude, untaught, rustick man (OED, n. 2, quoting this instance first). Deighton (ed. 1891): Rude rustics. Verity (ed. 1912): Cf. Volumnia’s contemptuous description of the people (plebs) as woollen vassals, [Cor. 3.2.9 (2094)]. Phillips (ed. 1969): I.e. country bumpkins. Hemp was used in making coarse cloth (also for the hangman’s rope, and there may be an echo of a stock joke here). Homespun (= folksy) is still in use; she suggests a verbal echo from folklore and at p. 14 quotes What have we here? hempen hampen? from Robin Goodfellow: His Mad Pranks and Merry Jests, noting that in William Warner’s Albion’s England (1602, continued 1606) this tag is referred to as the brownie’s Hempen Hampen Sentence. Brooks (ed. 1979), anticipated by Chambers (ed. 1897): What have we here? is Robin’s phrase in Scot, Witchcraft…Hemton, his next word, may have suggested hempen (see ).

889–90 swaggring]
OED (Swagger, v. 1a), quoting this instance first: To behave with an air of superiority, in a blustering, insolent, or defiant manner. Schmidt (1875): To rant, to be noisy and boisterous. Deighton (ed. 1891): Ranting and strutting about.

891 Cradle]
Page (ed. 1897): Observe the poetical use of this word, properly a receptacle for an infant’s bed; see OED (n. 4), quoting this instance first. Foakes (ed. 1984): Suggests the diminutive scale of fairies.

892–3 What…cause]
Brooks (ed. 1979): Cf. Munday, John a Kent, ll. 1014, 1030: Shrimp. Sir, yonder’s great preparations for a play…Iohn.…for in the play I purpose to make one.

892–3 What,]
For the construction and tone, see n. 436.

892–3 a…toward]
Johnson (1755, Toward, towards adv.): [It is doubtful whether in this use the word be adverb or adjective.] Near; at hand; in a state of preparation (OED, Toward, adj. 2b). Hudson (ed. 1880): Very often used so by the Poet; see Schmidt (1875, adv.) for Shn. parallels. Halliwell (ed. 1856) gives numerous non-Shn. examples of the construction. White (ed. 1858): A play in progress (OED, Toward, adj. 2c., but with first quotation 1838).

892–3 Ile…cause]
See Evett (1990), n. 832–3.

892 Auditor]
Johnson (1755, 1), quoting this instance: A hearer (OED, n. 1, quoting this instance). Schmidgall (1990, p. 18), citing various Shn. references to hearing rather than seeing a play, including Theseus at 1879, sees reflected in Robin’s word here the attitude of the typical Elizabethan theatergoer for whom vocalism took priority over gesture and production values. See n. 294, Audience.

893 Actor]
With a quibble on two senses, as given by Johnson (1755): 1. He that acts, or performs any thing [OED 3] and 2. He that personates a character; a stage-player [OED 4]. Of sense 3, OED comments: In later usage nearly always with fig. allusion to 4; the first example given for sense 3 is MM 2.2.37 (783).

894 stand forth]
See n. 30, 33.

895 Pyra.]
Wiles (1987, pp. 74–5), believing that the speech-prefixes are Shn. and reveal that Sh. constructs characters as a set of functions, a set of relationships: The name Bottom…relates to his trade. From the point of view of the stage directions, the personal Nick is an irrelevance. Bottom’s character is not conceptually separable from his socio-economic status. During the rehearsal, he becomes Piramus in the stage directions because this is now his function in respect of the group on stage. In his second scene with Titania, he is reclassified again: Enter Queene of Fairies, and Clowne, and Fairies, and the King behinde them—and he continues as Clowne in the speech-prefixes. The social dimension operates: clown/queen is a distinction of rank. But Bottom also functions as clown through initiating a visual and musical game with the fairies. He wakes up as Bottome (a Folio addition), but it is as Clo. that he addresses the audience and tells them he is no patched fool. Again, he is Piramus during the play, but Bot. when he drops out of role. However, the variations are more probably compositorial and due to type-shortage; see Kennedy (1998). See also n. 1509–50. Andrews (ed. 1993): The Quarto speech heading…suggests that Bottom has now become Pyramus. Soon he will undergo a second dramatic transformation. Meanwhile Flute has changed himself into Thisby.

895–917 Thisby…thine]
Capell (1783, 2.3:107): Not a word of the rehearsings in this place occurs afterwards in the Interlude: nor did reason allow it should; one attendance to compositions of such beauty more than satisfies. The Clarkes (1879, p. 777), comparing the variations between this passage and 1995–2007, believe Sh. purposely did this to avoid the flat effect upon his audience which an exact repetition would have produced. He preferred giving an inexactitude to giving a platitude. Kittredge (ed. 1939): We are…to imagine that the piece has been rewritten in the interval.…Who was the reviser? Was he Peter Quince or a subsidized poet? Bottom thinks Quince able to compose a good prologue and also a fine ballet of Bottom’s Dream [1740–3]. But if Quince wrote the Prologue, why did he make such a mess of it in the delivery? Kittredge & Ribner (ed. 1966), conceding the possibility of rewriting, but [Sh.] rarely observes consistency in such matters. See also nn. 322–6, 1905.

895–900 Thisby…appeare]
For attempts to correct the grammar, sense, and rhyme of these lines, see textual n., and conj., . Halliwell (ed. 1856): It is scarcely requisite to correct the sense of a speech which is probably intended to be ignorantly formed.

895 flowers…sweete]
Capell (1783, 2.3:107): This…, when corrected…as Quince bids, means to say—the odour of flowers savours sweet. Collier (ed. 1842), reading sauours as a noun: Possibly, we ought to read the flowers have odours, savours sweet, or odorous savours sweet. The Clarkes (ed. 1864): Bottom blunders of for have. Verity (ed. 1893): Flowers of odours for odours of flowers is…meant as a joke. Jourdain (MS c. 1860), implicitly suggesting parody, compares Peele, David and Bethsabe, Bright Bethsabe gives…Verdure to earth, and to that verdure flowers, To flowers, sweet Odors (Works 3:195), and Spenser, Am. 64 Comming to kisse her lyps,…Me seemd I smelt a gardin of sweet flowers: that dainty odours from them threw around. Sh.’s audience might be amused by a grimmer parallel with the exchange between Bellamira and Ithamore over Barabas’s poisoned flowers (Marlowe, The Jew of Malta, 4.4.39–40; 1:321).

895–6 odious…odorous]
Delius (ed. 1859) notes the jest on the reverse mistake in Dogberry’s Comparisons are odorous (Ado 3.5.16 [1611]) (Ger.). Chapman appears to have both plays in mind in Act 4 of Sir Gyles Goosecappe: Goos. Bee Caparisons odious Sir Cutt: what like flowers? Rud. O asse they be odorous. Goos. A botts athat stincking worde odorous, I can neuer hitt on’t (1606, G2r). Taylor(Everything, 2003, p. 63) suggests that Dogberry’s inversion of Bottom’s error indicates that Kemp played both roles.

895 sauours]
See Abbott §333 for the common occurrence of the 3rd person plural in -s; however, he suggests that in this case: It is perhaps intended to be a sign of low breeding and harsh writing.

895 sauours sweete]
Brooks (ed. 1979) and Holland (ed. 1994) find an echo of scriptural sweet (or sweetsmelling) savour (Gen. 8:21, 2 Cor. 2:15, Eph. 5:2).

896 Odours, odorous]
See textual n.. Sisson (1956, 1:128), understanding sauours as a verb, supports the Folio repetition of odours, since this is in accordance with Bottom’s reply. Of those understanding sauours as a noun, some favor emending Q Odours to odorous or odious. Thiselton (1903, pp. 40-1): Quince in correcting Bottom’s odious first substituted a vulgar form of odorous…which he forthwith corrected. Cf. n. 815, maruailes. Foakes (ed. 1984): Quince is demonstrating how the correct adjective is formed.

897–8 Odours…breath]
Rudd (1979, p. 182) notes that Ovid, Met. 4.72, inque uices fuerat captatus anhelitus oris is interpreted by the Loeb translator and Chaucer (LGW 750–2) as referring to the lovers hearing one another breathing: Golding, however, wrote: Now as at one side Pyramus and Thisbe on the tother Stood often drawing one of them the pleasant breath from other (89–90). Here the combination of drawing with pleasant suggests the idea of scent rather than hearing; and that is almost certainly the right interpretation.…[I]n the Ars amatoria (1.521) when Ovid is warning his readers against the hazards of bad breath he says: nec male odorati sit tristis anhelitus oris. So Golding was right. Apart from being more poetically arresting, this gave Shakespeare an opening for a joke [in 895–8]. See .

898 deare]
For the transposed adjective, see n. 315–16.

899–900 But…appeare]
Watkins & Lemmon (1974, p. 74): The departure off-stage is managed by the crudest of gambits. Blits (2003, pp. 95–6) notes that this interruption to the lovers’ conversation is not part of the traditional Pyramus and Thisbe story, but Quince’s needless change…has a crucial effect on Shakespeare’s plot.

900 by and by]
Johnson (1755), quoting the instance at 1705: In a short time. See OED (By and by, advb. phr. 4) and the alternate meaning (3a): Straightway, immediately. The few who comment are divided on these two meanings. See Franz §412; Blake 5.1.3.3 (soon).

900 appeare]
Schmidt (1874, 3): = come to thee. (Cf. OED, v. 1: To come forth into view, as from a place or state of concealment.)

900–36 Exit…Exit]
Qq provide little guidance for the comings and goings of this sequence. There is no exit for Robin to follow Piramus offstage, no re-entry for Piramus assheaded nor for Robin, no exit for the frightened mechanicals, no exit for Snowte (unless the exit for Quince at 936 covers Snowte too). F adds two SDs: at 920, The Clownes all Exit and at 927 Enter Piramus with the Asse head. Both raise questions: Does Bottom exit with the other clowns? What provokes the panic at 918–19 if Bottom/Piramus has not yet appeared? Most editions solve the latter question by moving the entry for assheaded Piramus to follow 914 or 916, sometimes adding an entry for Robin together with or following him. Nicholson (1870, p. 56) argues that no SD is necessary at 914–16 since Quince summons Piramus, that the SD at 920 includes Bottom, and the Enter at SD 927 is frequent for re-enter. He adds: Panic-struck with the rest, unconscious that he is himself the cause, he hurries out with and after them…. His odd gestures when frightened…alarm his comrades the more; and his following them ensures their dispersion this, that, and every way. Moreover, his then appearance of affright makes his after-assumption of confidence the more marked and ludicrous. Without reference to this article, Wells & Taylor (ed. 1986) provide an entry for Piramus at 916, and a re-entry at 927. Other recent editions have adopted this order. See textual nn. Holland (ed. 1994) justifies the decision: F’s direction does not exclude Bottom and he could quite reasonably exit in pursuit of them. Most editors take F’s entrance for him at [927] as misplaced for [916] but Bottom blundering around the stage might detract from the extraordinary power of Robin’s speech [921–6]. A bemused Bottom re-entering at [927 ] seems theatrically effective, perhaps comically confused and even a little sad. Cf. n. [865].

901 Quin.]
So Qq. Collier (ed. 1842): In the folio, 1623, [this line] is properly assigned to Puck, who has been standing behind. See textual n. Wilson (ed. 1924) argues that the error supports revision theories (see ): Possibly pu has been taken for qu by the compositor; and if so, it follows that the line was added in the margin at the time of the 1598 revision, seeing that Puck’s other speeches in this scene are assigned to Robin.

901 A…heere]
Caldecott (MS 1813–33): Such a sort of a representative of him as was never seen or presented here, or any where else. Johnson (ed. 1894): Notice that Puck takes up the rhyme and rhythm of Pyramus’ speech. Ness (1941, p. 128): The so-called capping verse; see also n. 520–1. Foakes (ed. 1984): The line shows that it is at this point that Puck conceives the plan to transform Bottom.

901 heere]
Steevens (ed. 1778): I suppose he means in that theatre where the piece was acting. The Clarkes (ed. 1864): Puck uses here…as Titania uses the word in [476];…as a kind of expletive, to fill up or round off the sentence: though it has, moreover, a good effect in the fairy-folk’s mouths, as conveying an impression of their looking on at humanity and its doings, in the character of creatures apart from its nature. Young (1966, p. 42): Does here mean in the woods or does it mean, as an aside to the audience, upon this stage? Whether the story had been staged before or not, it was already an excellent vehicle for parody.

903 marry]
See n. 279.

904 see…heard]
Grey (1754, 1:59), comparing TN 2.3.56 (756) To heare by the nose,: Butler probably had one or both these passages in view, [in] As Rosicrucian vertuosos Can see with ears, and hear with noses. Hudibras. Book 3. Canto 3, 15–16. Deighton (ed. 1891): Quince means that Bottom has gone to find out how the noise he heard had been caused, but of course the absurdity of seeing a noise is intentional; cp. [1737–9].

906–9 Most…tyre]
Abbott §501 calls these lines trimeter couplets and says that they are frequently [used] in comic…poetry and that the form is appropriate for Thisbe, quoting 906. Wright (1988, p. 143): Shakespeare seems to have been willing to use hexameters whenever they seemed apt to his purposes. For most such lines, that probably means whenever some possible pattern of antiphonal balance between echoing three-foot line-segments can achieve a momentary special effect.…[T]his readiness to divide into two halves [impossible for the iambic pentameter line] makes a reader or listener uncertain as to whether some verse is best described as hexameter or trimeter. Mowat & Werstine (ed. 1993): These lines include several words that simply fill out the six-beat doggerel lines (brisky [rather than brisk], juvenal [rather than youth], eke [i.e., also]) and words that seem desperate attempts to rhyme (Jew to rhyme with hue, tire to rhyme with brier). Part of the comedy in the Pyramus and Thisbe scenes turns on the very bad poetry of the script.

906–7 Most…bryer]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Whose complexion combines the delicate white of the lily and the brilliant red of the rose; cp. Constance’s poetical description of Arthur’s beauty, [Jn. 3.1.53–4 (974–5)]. Muir (1954, p. 145) parallels A gorgious Gallery (O1r), where the red and white so pure complexion has faded of late lusty Piramus, more fresh then flower in May. Donaldson (1985, p. 11), discussing how an amateur or bad poet may misuse poetic conventions by misapplying physical attributes of the sexes: Thisbe’s description of Pyramus in the rehearsal of their play…almost outdoes Chaucer’s description of Sir Thopas [725–7] in the process of demasculinization. See .

906 lillie white]
A stock epithet; see OED, Lily-white, a. 1a. Dent (L296.1) lists it as proverbial. See also n. 2120.

907 red…bryer]
Beisly (1864, pp. 48–9): The Dog Rose (Rosa canina)…is here referred to, as the term triumphant truly indicates; the blossoms are very conspicuous, hanging over the hedgerows in the months of June and July.

907 triumphant]
Schmidt (1875, 4): Glorious, of supreme magnificence and beauty. See OED, a. 3. Obs. Deighton (ed. 1891): Rearing itself aloft. Rohde (1935, p. 111): What more triumphant than a brave show of dog-roses in a hedge-row? Kittredge (ed. 1939): Transcendent. Lever (ed. 1961): The word is meaningless here; so is most lovely Jew.…Flute is putting in words to take the place of the ones he has forgotten. Foakes (ed. 1984): Noble, magnificent.…The word is, of course, absurd in relation to the wild rose.

907 bryer]
See n. 376.

908 brisky]
OED (a.), quoting this instance first: Of a brisk nature. Florio (1598, Grillante): Reioicing, glad, blithe, bucksome, briske, skipping for ioy. Rann (ed. 1787): Sprightly. Schmidt (1874): Brisk, 1) nimble, sprightly: 2) smart, trim: 3) full of fire, spirituous. Brisky, the same, in the scurrile poetry of Flute. Rolfe (ed. 1877): Of course burlesque for brisk. Gordon (1928, p. 274): A joke; cf. n. 251, vnheedy. Clayton (1979, p. 14) suggests this is Flute’s error for frisky. Wells & Taylor (1987, p. 282), emending to brisly i.e. bristly: The hall mark of a juvenile was his lack of a beard, so bristly…would be suitably incongruous.

908 Iuuenall]
Steevens (ed. 1778): I.e. young man. OED (n.1 Obs. B), quoting first LLL 1.2.7–8 (318–19); see also OED (n.2) for the figurative use of the Roman satirist’s anglicized name. Besides this instance, Sh. uses the noun five times in LLL 1.2.7–13 (318–24), 3.1.65 (836), and once in 2H4 1.2.19 (294), but nowhere else. Hibbard (1990, ed. LLL, p. 110) notes that these are all in comic contexts. In the quartos the word is sometimes capitalized but never italicized; in F1 it is more often both. The noun juvenile is not attested in OED before 1733; the adjective not before 1625. Bluestone (1953, pp. 326–7) emends to produce an anti-Jewish pun, Jew, venal, suggesting that this blunder of Flute’s was caused by Quince’s careless translation or transcription of Ovid’s description of Pyramus as iuvenum pulcherrimus (Met. 4.55; cf. Golding 4.72, see ).

908 eeke]
OED (Eke, adv. arch.): Also. Halliwell (ed. 1856): Eke…was becoming obsolete in Shakespeare’s time, and is used by him only in burlesque passages. Rolfe (ed. 1877): Eke…S. puts into the mouth of no other character except Pistol and the Host [Wiv. 1.3.96 (387); 2.3.74 (1134)]. Muir (1954, p. 149; 1977, p. 74) attributes the use of eke as a metrical filler to the influence of Mouffet, but Donaldson (1985, p. 14) suspects that Sh. had no particular poet in mind but all poetic ekers out, including, perhaps, Chaucer—who may also have had Chaucer in mind when he used seven very idle ekes in the short space of Sir Thopas’ two-hundred-odd lines.

908 Iewe]
Thirlby (MS 1725–33) compares LLL 3.1.135 (902), my in-conie Iew. Johnson (ed. 1765, 2:144), quoting MND against Warburton’s emendation of LLL to jewel: Jew, in our author’s time, was, for whatever reason, apparently a word of endearment. Rann (ed. 1787): An abbreviation of jewel, a term of endearment. Support for this may be found in Falstaff’s play on words in 2H4 1.2.18–19 (293–4): Send againe to your master for a iewell, the iuuenall the prince your master. The Clarkes (ed. 1864, 1:283): May have been a corruption of the French fondling words choux, chou-chou, or jou-jou;…or possibly an abbreviation of bijou, jewel. Baker (ed. 1896): Seems to have no meaning, and to have been suggested by the sound of the first syllable of juvenal. Scott (ed. 1898): As Pyramus was a Babylonian it was no great stretch to call him a Jew, the two races being readily confounded by the uneducated. Wilson (ed. 1924): A playful diminutive of Juvenal. Agreeing, Taylor (1990, p. 61) sees it as a strained attempt to translate Met. 4.55; cf. Bluestone re: Iuuenall. Doran (ed. 1959): Probably added merely to echo juvenal and rime with hue. See also Lever in n. 907 (triumphant).

909 As…tyre]
Johnson (ed. 1894): It is not easy to see the precise meaning of this line, and it was not probably intended to have any. Brooks (ed. 1979): The most unweariable and willing horse. Possibly an echo of the proverbial A flea-bitten horse [ie. a light-colored one with bay or sorrel spots] never tires (Tilley, H640), which would accord with the other absurdities of Thisbe’s praise of Pyramus. Knowles (privately): A comic pun; a true lover would be faithful in love; a true horse would be steadfast, reliable, never tiring.

910 Ile…toumbe]
van Emden (1975, p. 202) commenting on this line and 2005–6, notes that A Gorgious Gallery is the only one of Shakespeare’s putative sources to give dialogue for this decision at all, since the others remain narrative…at this point, most producing some translation…of statuunt [Met. 4.84]. Cf. Golding 4.104, Chaucer LGW 778; see , .

910 Ninnies toumbe]
Delius (ed. 1859) identifies as the grave of the old Babylonian king, and notes the pun (Ger.); OED (ninny1): A simpleton, a fool; first recorded instance 1593. Rudd (1979, p. 183): The point was the same in Shakespeare’s day as in ours: ninny meant a nit-wit. And the joke implies, I think, that Shakespeare had the Latin (as well as a translation) in front of him. For Ninny’s tomb has rather more point for someone construing ad busta Nini [Met. 4.88] than for one who is relying solely on Ninus tumb, which is Golding’s rendering. Because it is so close to the Latin, the phrase may in fact preserve an Elizabethan schoolboy joke. The error of Minus for Ninus in both A gorgious Gallery and A Handefull may also have contributed to Sh.’s mockery (cf. Ebsworth, ed. Q1 1880, p. xii). Kranendonk (1932, p. 211 n.): Ninus is mentioned in [FQ 2.9.21; 2.9.56] as King Nine and as old Ninus [1.5.48]. (Cf. M.N.D. old Ninny [2063]). See also n. 1937. Verity (ed. 1893, at 1937) notes that Ninus was the supposed founder of Nineveh, and husband of Semiramis, queen of Babylon, the city of Pyramus and Thisbe.

911–14 Ninus…tire]
Wilson (ed. 1924): Q. prints this as verse; perhaps Shakespeare wrote it in short lines in his manuscript. See textual n.

911 Ninus…man]
An unavailing correction. Cf. 1937, and the repeated error 2005, 2063.

913 part…cues]
Staunton (ed. 1857): To appreciate the importance of cues, it must be borne in mind that when the parts, or written language of a new play, are distributed, each performer receives only what he has himself to recite; consequently, if this were unaccompanied by cues, or catchwords, from the other parts, he would be utterly at a loss to know either when to make his entrance on the scene, or to join in the dialogue. Page (ed. 1897): Quince is either making a mistake, or exaggerates for the sake of effect. Flute has not spoken any cues of his own. Mowat & Werstine (ed. 1993): Flute seems…rather to have read two of Thisbe’s speeches as if they were one. Stern (2004, p. 124): As the cue never tyre repeatedly occurs…, it could be that the real actor actually starts to speak three times, for the very words with which Quince (the prompter) chides Bottom for neglecting his cue (your cue is past; it is never tyre) give him a false cue. After Quince’s never tyre, both Thisbe and Pyramus, who both now have never tyre as their next cue, may start speaking simultaneously. The whole section is, of course, dedicated to making jokes about country mechanicals who do not understand the theatre and cannot work out the cueing system. On cues and parts, see also nn. 329, 887; for Bottom’s concern over cues, see 1728, 1987.

914–16 tire…tyre]
See n. 909. Johnson (1755, Tire 2): A head-dress. Cf. also Feuillerat (1908, p. 369) where among accounts for Necessaryes are listed for foure yeallowe heares for head attires for woemen…xxvjs viijd and for a pound of heare…xijd. (But no remark is ever made on Thisbe’s wig.) There may be a personal in-joke; Mountjoy, with whom Shakespeare lodged in London, was a wig and tire maker: see e.g. Duncan-Jones (2001, p. 207). The repetition might lead to an expectation of a pun on tire. If, as seems most likely (see n. 900–36), Bottom with his ass’s head is now on stage and visible to the audience as well as to the clowns, there is an amusing point in drawing attention to his spectacularly extraordinary headdress.
The use of the definite article in F’s SD at 927, Enter Piramus with the Asse head, a direction which Greg (1955, p. 244) asserts undoubtedly had its origin in the playhouse, indicates, as Doran (ed. 1959) notes: There was evidently only one ass-head in the property room. It is probable that this property was an entire head rather than a half-mask or vizer, as suggested by Holland (ed. 1994), or merely ass’s ears, as in Lyly’s Midas (4.1.140 ff.; 3:143), although Bond (3:112) believes Midas’ asses’ ears as the punishment of arrogance and folly are, no doubt, the original of Bottom’s ass-head. Franke (1979, p. 292) envisages the fool’s traditional ass-eared cowl, indicative not only of stupidity but also of concupiscence. Twycross & Carpenter’s (1981, pp. 19–21, 30) consideration of the question of heads and visers in the mystery plays and for mummers may clarify what Sh.’s audience might have expected to see: The viser only covered the actor’s face, whereas the head sat over the whole head, and might even rest on the shoulders, as a helmet would. The famous Bodley Alexander mummers wear this sort of complete headpiece [including an ass-head], with the join at the neck concealed by a shoulder cape. Animal heads are more realistic if the entire shape of the head, with horns, ears, and possibly mane, is taken into account. It is noticeable that in the mystery play accounts, the word head is mostly used of devil costumes. In the tradition of masquing…the masks are exotic, indicating the strange, mysterious, and foreign. Griffiths (ed. 1996, pp. 145–6) reviews theatrical practice, from the full heads with working parts favored in the 19th. c. to the lighter or more symbolic expedients of the latter 20th. c.
Various literary sources are adduced for the magical trick of the transformed head. Douce (in Steevens, ed. 1793, at 1039) compares Albertus Magnus, De Secretis, translated anonymously in 1560: If thou wilt that a mans head seme an Asse head. Take vp of the coueringe of an Asse & anoynte [th]e ma[n] on his head (The Boke of secretes…sig. I7v). Steevens (MS 1793–1800): The metamorphosis of Bottom’s head might have been suggested by a similar trick played by Doctor Faustus. See his [Historie of the damnable life, and deserved death of Doctor Iohn Faustus, translated by P. F.…1592], Chap. XLIII.: Doctor Faustus made that euery one [of the group of Maskers] had an Asses head on, with great and long eares: but when they left his house they were restored to their naturall shape againe (sig. I1r). See also Reed (1959, pp. 252–4). Douce (1807, 1:193) quotes from Scot’s Discoverie of Witchcraft Bk. 13 Ch. 19 instructions for how to make people seeme to haue horsses or asses heads (see ). Thoms (1847, p. 1006) relates an amusing story of head transformation performed by a German folk figure analogous to Puck, Rubezahl, but without explaining how the traditional tales might have reached 16th.-c. England. Halliwell (ed. 1856) believes Sh. would have been familiar with the transformation of the ass in the Chester mystery play of Balaam, but here the device was of the whole animal, sturdy enough to be ridden and to conceal (probably) a small boy. Nicholson (1881, p. 2) calls attention to Scot’s tale of a man transformed to an ass (Bk. 5 Chs. 3–5; see ). The two last, and Apuleius’s Golden Ass (see ) involve the whole body. Chambers (ed. 1897, p. 163) also mentions Scot’s story of an appearance of Pope Benedict IX., a century after his death, with an ass’s head on (1584, 2Q3r; see ). Moore (1926, p. 47) notes: In all these analogues…the hero does not remain the unwitting victim of a trick. Barkan (1986, pp. 262–3) draws parallels between ass-headed Bottom and Ovid’s Actaeon, sometimes depicted in mid-transformation still retaining the body of a man.

917 If…faire]
Caldecott (MS 1813–33): Were I a very Adonis, instead of having an Asses head. Moberly (ed. 1881): Had I all the qualities which you so kindly state. Kittredge (ed. 1939): Apparently a modest disclaimer: If I were a handsome young man (as I am not), I would assert my claim to be thy devoted and accepted lover. Malone (1780, 1:117) seeks to alter the sense by moving the comma: If I were, [i.e. as true, &c.] fair Thisby. Staunton (ed. 1857) ridicules this attempt as making bully Bottom speak sensibly, which Shakespeare has taken some pains to show he was never designed to do. Nevertheless various editors added words to this phrase to clarify sense and amend metre (see textual n. and conj., ). Thiselton (1903, pp. 41–2), noting, as Dyce (ed. 1857) had done, that faire is associated with Pyramus elsewhere (at 1729 and 1922), argues in order to maintain the rhythm, it is necessary to make faire a dissyllable, and it seems not unlikely that Bottom here regards the word as being in the comparative degree, the meaning being Even if I were fairer than thou sayest I am. Jackson (2000, p. 70) argues that his proposed horse, fair emends the metre, refers to truest horse, agrees with Bottom being hoarse, and is comical in conjunction with the ass-head.

918–19 O…helpe]
Winter (2003, pp. 96–7): If flight is born of a desire for liberty in the case of Hermia and Lysander, it is also sometimes tied to fear of the monstrous, notably of the grotesque and frightening hybrid form of Bottom.…The incomprehension of Bottom [928–9] which amuses the audience is parallelled with the tragic error of Pyramus.…Beyond the comic aspects of the artisans’ play and the courtiers’ scoffing commentary, Thisbe’s flight allows for a mise en abyme of the motif of flight, which then leans towards a tragic mode [quotes 1938–48] (Fr.).

918 monstrous]
Florio (1598, Mostruoso): Monstrous, deformed, strange, against the course of nature. Minsheu (1599, Espaventoso): Fearefull, dreadfull, monstrous. OED (7), quoting this instance first: As an exclamation, in the sense greatly to be marvelled at, astounding. Obs. Webb (1996, p. 118), commenting on Sh.’s use of the monstrous for comedy, instances the amazed responses of unsophisticated folk, such as [Bottom’s] fellow-rustics. See also n. 314.

918 haunted]
OED (Haunt, v. 5b), quoting this instance and R2 3.2.158 (1518) first: To be haunted: to be subject to the visits and molestation of disembodied spirits. See also nn. 518, 1027.

918 Pray]
Knowles (privately): To ward off witches or demons who are responsible for the monstrosity?

919 masters]
See n. 283.

921–2 Ile follow…bryer]
For emendations intended to regularize the meter of these lines, see textual n. and conj., . Sipe (1968, p. 170) suggests that about was an intentionally irregular use for purposes of characterization, or that the vowel of you was meant to be elided before about.

921 Ile leade…Round]
Deighton (ed. 1891): I will lead you a pretty dance. Like jig, Round was associated with country song and dance; see Baskervill (1929, p. 13), citing Florio (1598, Strambotti), countrie gigs, rounds, catches or songs, and Cotgrave (1611, Strambot), A Jyg, Round, Catch, countrey Song. See also n. 516. Thiselton (1903, p. 42): The initial capital…is sufficient to exclude Furness’ suggestion that we should read around. OED (Round, n.1 12c), quoting this instance first, and no other until 1719, avoids the connection with dance: A roundabout way or course; one which turns round in a circle.

921 about]
OED (adv. 8): In a circuitous or winding course; with frequent turnings; hither and thither; to and fro; up and down.

922 Through bogge…bryer]
Johnson (ed. 1765): Here are two syllables wanting. Perhaps it was written, through bog, through mire. For other emendations based on alliteration or rhyme, see textual n., and conj., . Thirlby (MS 1725–33) compares 376. Malone (ed. 1790, 10:579) cites Spenser, FQ 6.8.32.1, misquoted as Through hills, through dales, through bushes and through briars. Halliwell (ed. 1856): Compare the ballad of Robin Goodfellow,—Through woods, through lakes, Through bogs, through brakes.

922 brake]
Holland (ed. 1994): Presumably the one that was their ’tiring-house at 817.

922 bryer]
See n. 376.

923–4 Sometime a horse…fier]
Collier (ed. 1842): So in Robin-goodfellow, his Mad Pranks and Merry Jests, reprinted by the Percy Society [ed. Collier, 1841]:—Thou hast the power to change thy shape To horse, to hog, to dog, to ape; and in the unique ballad in the Introduction to the same tract—Sometimes a walking fire he’d be, And lead them from their way. Thoms (1847, p. 982) offers examples from various authorities for the assumption by spirits of these different forms, among them: Grimm furnishes some instances of the Evil One assuming the shape of a hog; and, as in the transformations which Puck assumes the more malevolent features of the elfin character are discoverable, such instances as the present serve to show how, in the ever-varying phases of the popular mythology, the once harmless fairies have gradually got confounded with the spirit of evil. Hudson (ed. 1851) comments on Puck’s Protean versatility. Sprague (ed. 1896) quotes Vergil’s description of Proteus’s animal transformations, Georgics 4.407–10. See also n. 415–26.

923–4 Sometime…sometime]
See n. 408.

924 headelesse Beare]
Possibly prompted by A true and most Dreadfull discourse of a woman possessed with the Deuill: who in the likenesse of a headlesse Beare fetched her out of her Bedd,… (1584; STC 5681). See Rahter (1964), partly anticipated by Simpson (1932). The title page boasts a fearsomely clawed headless black bear. It is remotely possible that Sh. was amused by the conjunction of words in Golding’s Ovid, 8.475, the Dart could headlesse beare no sway; see . It might be questioned how a headless bear could roar (925). Little (1990, pp. 227–8) notes that the thief Thrasyleon in Apuleius (Bk. 4 Ch. 15) is disguised as a bear, which perhaps influenced Rimbaud in his poem Bottom (Fr.).

924 fier]
On Puck as Ignis fatuus or Will-o’-the-Wisp, Thoms (1847, pp. 1005–6) cites many parallels. See also n. 409.

925–6 And neigh…turne]
For the rhetorical figure, Rolfe (ed. 1877) compares Ham. 3.1.151 [1807] and Mac. 1.3.61–2 [160–1]. Brooks (ed. 1979) adds examples from Spenser (FQ 1.11.28), Sidney, (Arcadia, ed. Evans, 1977, p. 681), and Gil Polo, (Enamoured Diana, ed. Kennedy, 1968, p. 401). For the popularity of correlative or reporting verse (carmen correlativum, vers rapportés) in the latter 16th c., see William Ringler, The Poems of Sir Philip Sidney (Oxford, 1962, pp. 406, 409). Furness (ed. 1895): Note the pelting, rattling staccato, which sounds like the explosion of a pack of Chinese firecrackers, at the heels of the flying clowns? Knowles (privately): Besides correlation there is summary (synathroesmus, congeries, recapitulation); see Miriam Joseph, 1947, pp. 117, 317.

926 at…turne]
OED (Turn 10b), quoting this example first: On every occasion, continually.

928–9 This…afeard]
Deighton (ed. 1891): This is one of their knavish tricks played in order to make me afraid.

928 knauery]
See n. 1387.

929 afeard]
See n. 838.

930–7 see…knauery]
Blits (2003, p. 98): See is a key word in the exchange. Snout first uses it to mean what he perceives.…Bottom, repeating his words but shifting the sense, uses the word to mean both what Snout perceives and what he invents or imagines.…Finally, Bottom uses it to mean what he understands or thinks he knows of the others’ intentions.

930–3 Sn.…you?]
Johnson (ed. 1765) claims that Bottom’s words necessitated mention of the ass head by Snout, but Heron (1785, p. 110) rebuts: The phrase…is a trite vulgarism, when a person expresses a foolish amazement at some trifling oddity in another’s dress, or the like. See Tilley (A388). Commentators from Wright (ed. 1877) on frequently point out that Bottom is unaware of his transformation. For a similar irony, cf. Sidney’s Dametas: Many times he wished himself the back of an ass to help to carry away the new sought riches (an unfortunate wisher, for if he had as well wished the head, it had been granted him) (Arcadia, ed. Evans, 1977, p. 640). Scot scoffingly gives a recipe for setting an asses head upon a mans shoulders (see ). Vives refers to the tricks of natural magic, making men look headless, and headed like asses (commentary on Augustine, The City of God, quoted in Everyman ed. R. V. G. Tasker, 1945, 2:427).

935–6 Blesse…trāslated]
Clayton (1999, p. 76): This must have been accompanied by Quince’s making the sign of the cross, and it quite possibly carried an allusion to a bishop’s mitre and episcopal translation from see to shining see.

935 Blesse]
OED (3): To protect or guard, save, keep from (evil). Hassel (2005, p. 38): Used in a serious invocation of divine assistance.

936 trāslated]
Transformed; see n. 203. Sprague (ed. 1896): Is there not here an ironical suggestion, by way of joke, that Bottom is glorified as Enoch was, transformed to something celestial? See Hebrews, xi, 5. (Cf. OED 1b.) Baldwin (1944, 2:210–211) discusses Quintilian on metaphor and simile, and lists his four types of translation involved in metaphor: Bottom illustrates one of these types of metaphorical translation.…Because Bottom…was by simile like an ass, he got translated by a metaphor from an animate human being to an animate ass. Shakspere clearly knew that a metaphor was a translation. Watts (1991, pp. 42–3): Translated can mean transferred (often from a lower state to a higher), as in the biblical phrasing, God…hath delivered us from the power of darkness, and hath translated us into the kingdom of his dear Son [Col. 1:10, 13]; and Bottom is translated in this sense when he is borne away by his entranced lover, Titania. Wells (1999, pp. 18–22), noting that Quince’s recognition of Bottom shows that though translated he is not transmuted, explores implications of the word in terms of literary translation, and of stage performance. Parker (2004, p. 661), drawing on OED 4, hears a subtle registration of the theater’s changed economic structure. In addition to its more common uses, this had once denoted a particular form of labor: a weaver might be said to translate a garment by making adjustments to it.…Quince probably glances at that meaning in his outcry at Bottom’s asshead—as though the weaver had on accident made an adjustment to himself, sewing, as it were, an inhuman costume out of human flesh.

937 knauery]
See n. 1387.

937 make an asse of mee]
OED (Ass, n.1 1d), quoting this instance first: To treat as an ass, stultify. Johnson (1755, Ass 2), quoting Falstaff’s realization that he has been made an Asse in Wiv. 5.5.119 (2602): A stupid, heavy, dull fellow; a dolt.See Dent (A379.1). See also . For of used metaphorically with verbs of construction, see Abbott §172.

938 could]
On could in the role of modal auxiliary, expressing a hypothetical ability, especially in connection with conditional clauses, see Blake 4.3.7.9 (a, d). See also Abbott §307, Franz §638.

939 walke…downe]
Forey (1998, p. 322), arguing for Sh.’s use of Golding’s Ovid, notes that Midas vp & down with Pan…roues (11.165; see ).

940–64 I will sing…occasion]
Vickers (1968, p. 69): Bottom’s speech and behaviour throughout his transformation to an ass is a small masterpiece of decorum for Shakespeare, using the word in its Elizabethan sense of fitting style to character. We see it best by juxtaposition of the song that wakes Titania and the ensuing dialogue.

940 sing]
Schmidt (1875, Weaver), quoting 1H4 2.4.133 (1093–4): W[eavers] supposed to be good singers and particularly given to singing psalms (being most of them Calvinists and refugees from the Netherlands).

940 that]
Deighton (ed. 1891): So that.

940 shall]
Abbott §348 takes this as a future tense used instead of a subjunctive. Blake (4.3.7.7) notes that shall often overlaps with will, in part because English was trying to express volition, obligation and futurity through only two auxiliary verbs, and adds in 4.3.7.7 (c): In a subordinate clause shall may indicate the subject in the main clause is of higher authority.

942–50 The Woosell…nay]
Holzknecht & McClure (ed. 1937): This song…is almost a parody of the delicate little lullaby [660–74] which put [Titania] to sleep. Long (1955, p. 89): The song resembles a folk type such as would be appropriate for Bottom. See also . Halliwell (1841, p. 78): Why Bottom should here enumerate these birds is not very evident, except we believe, as Aristotle has assured us, that all small birds hate the ass [Historia animalium 609a 31]. Idem (ed. 1856), anticipated by Thirlby (MS 1725–33), notes similar enumerations of birds in the old English metrical romances. Chaucer has imitated one of these in his Rhyme of Sir Thopas [766–71; see ]. Hudson (ed. 1851) compares the passage of singing birds in Spenser’s Epithalamion 80–82. Cartwright (1862, p. 162) calls the song evidently a paraphrase of the bird-song in [Lyly’s] Campaspe [5.1.32–44; 2:351], ending with the note of the cuckoo. And who can say, sweet bully Bottom with his ass’s head is not a burlesque of the poet himself, as Midas. Seng (1967, p. 35), anticipated by McCloskey (1931, pp. 389–91), suggests that a parallel with A poeme of a Mayde forsaken, partly quoted by Steevens (see n. 942–3 below), is appropriate to Bottom’s poor literary taste and to his predilection for tragic lovers. Donaldson (1985, p. 17): Not birdsong but singing about birds gets Bottom a fairy mistress.…Bottom’s birds are all genuine songbirds, whereas the throstle (the song thrush) is the only real songbird on Sir Thopas’ list. Wiles (1993, p. 106) claims (unconvincingly): The birds in the first stanza are explicitly masculine, and implicitly phallic; the suggestion is developed by Callaghan (2000, p. 148).

942 Woosell cock]
Steevens (ed. 1773): I.e. The cock blackbird (OED, Ouzel, ousel 1a, quoting this instance). Prosser (1981, p. 132) suggests that here and in 2H4 3.2.8 (1541) Sh. is indicating a provincial pronunciation.

942–3 blacke…bill]
For the coloring, Malone (1780, 1:117) parallels The thrustle-cock that was so black of hewe (Richard Jones, The Arbor of amorous Deuises, 1597, B1r). Knight (ed. 1839) quotes Drayton, The woosel near at hand, that hath a golden bill (Poly-Olbion, Song XIII, 58). Wright (ed. 1877) notes Orange tawny is descriptive of the colour of the bill of the male bird only, which is of a deep orange yellow. Donaldson (1985, p. 17) observes that it is Bottom’s favorite color. See also n. 355.

944 Throstle…true]
OED: A thrush; esp. the song-thrush or mavis, Turdus musicus. Now only literary and dial. Holland (ed. 1994): Often specifically the song-thrush, Turdus philomelos. Webb (1996, p. 171) points out that the Latin name reminds the listener that the song-thrush rivals the nightingale in sheer beauty of sound; he adds: Like that of the nightingale, the sound is appropriate to lovers, incongruity (Bottom/Titania) notwithstanding; he suggests that true may mean not simply concordant but (platonically) both beautiful and good. (Cf. 664 and n.)

944 note]
Johnson (1755, 6): Tune; voice. OED (n.2 3a): A strain of music, a song. In later use only poet.

945 quill]
Rolfe (ed. 1877): Probably = pipe; not wing-feather, as Schmidt explains it. Cf. Milton, Lycidas, 188: He touch’d the tender stops of various quills (OED, n.1 1c). Cf. Kittredge (ed. 1939) glossing with little quill as with slender piping note. But Blythe (1996–7, p. 9) argues that the feather is intended (OED, n.1 3a), and hence the wren’s characteristic flitting movements; he cites Mac. 4.2.9–10 (1722–3) for its diminutive size and MV 5.1.104–6 (2518–20) for its lack of musicianship. Noting that elsewhere Sh. always uses quill to refer to pen, he concludes that size rather than song is referred to here.

946–1020 What…silently]
Barish (1994, p. 56) comments: The love-struck Titania…woos Bottom in rhymed verse, mostly pentameter couplets, while Bottom replies to her, as to her attendants, only in his own brand of clumsily courtly prose.…The amusement for us lies in the contrast, the incommensurability, between her urgent endearments and his awkwardly prosaic responses. For the staging, see , .

946–55 What…note]
Stevens (1964, p. 22): Titania’s amorous aberration in loving Bottom in his ass’s head is beautifully pointed by her being wakened by Bottom’s clownish singing…, a splendid contrast to the fairies’ lullaby. Forey (1998, p. 322) compares Titania’s ludicrous estimate of Bottom’s singing with Midas’s asinine preference for Pan over Apollo in Golding’s Ovid 11.180–202; see .

946 What…bed?]
Malone (ed. 1790): Perhaps a parody on a line in the Spanish Tragedy, often ridiculed by the poets of our author’s time: What outcry calls me from my naked bed? (i.e. What out-cries pluck me from my naked bed, 2.5.1, Works of Kyd, ed. Boas, 1962). Rolfe (ed. 1877): It could hardly have been the naked bed that was ridiculed, for that was a common phrase (OED, Naked, a. 2b).

946 Angell]
As Hassel (2005, p. 17) notes, a figurative and romantic use; cf. Rom. 2.2.26 (820) and MV 2.7.58 (1031).

948 plainsong Cuckow]
Warton (in Steevens, ed. 1773, 10:Appendix 2L6r): That is, the cuckoo, who, having no variety of strains, sings in plain song, or in plano cantu, by which expression the uniform modulation or simplicity of the chant was anciently distinguished, in opposition to prick-song, or variegated music sung by note. Skelton introduces the birds singing the different parts of the service at the funeral of his favourite sparrow:…To kepe just playne songe Our chanter shall be your cuckoue [Philip Sparrow, 427–8]. Rann (ed. 1787): Telling plain truth—with its uniform note or chaunt. Halliwell (ed. 1856) cites the Chaucerian poem The Cuckowe and the Nightingale where the cuckoo says my song is bothe trewe and playn (118; Chaucerian and Other Pieces, ed. Skeat [1897], p. 352). For the wordplay on cuckoo and cuckold, see OED (Cuckold n. 1 Etym.): The origin of the sense is supposed to be found in the cuckoo’s habit of laying its eggs in another bird’s nest. Cf. LLL 5.2.898–902 (2864–8).

949–50 note…nay]
Halliwell (1841, pp. 78–9): [This] refers to an opinion then common, that the unfaithfulness of a woman to her husband was always guided by a destiny which no human power could avert: he gives various parallels here and in ed. 1856. Grinfield (MS 1834–63, p. 282): The vulgar meaning is—I am not a cuckold. Walter (ed. 1964): Refuse to believe the implication of a cry cuckoo, that his wife was unfaithful. Bottom pronounces it neigh in donkey-fashion. See n. 309, Nay.

949 note]
See n. 944.

949 full]
OED (C. adv. 1b): Very. See also n. 501.

949 marke]
OED (14): To consider; give heed or attention to. See 2078. See also n. 503.

951–3 For…so]
Scott (ed. 1898): According to what he deems profound wisdom the bird is not worth answering. Bottom is quite devoid of real humour as well as of imagination. Wilson (ed. 1924): Bottom’s comment shows that he entirely misses the point. Hill (1964, p. 19): A clown unable to interpret a double-entendre—this is certainly a new slant on the old humour!

951 set…wit]
Clemen (ed. 1963): Use his intelligence to answer. Brooks (ed. 1979), citing Tilley (W547): Not to set your wit against a fool’s is proverbial.

951 wit]
Johnson (1755, 1), quoting this instance: The powers of the mind; the mental faculties; the intellects. This is the original signification (OED n. 2a). Andrews (ed. 1993), referring to AYL 4.1.160–70 (2069–78): Like Will, Wit could refer to the genitalia of either gender. Brissenden (ed. AYL, 1994, App. A, pp. 229–31) details instances of Wit used for genital reference. See also n. 1755–6.

952 giue…ly]
OED (Lie, n.1 2a): Contradict. Barber (1959, p. 118 n. 13): The stress on who would give a bird the lie separates men and nature with a comic literalness; and Bottom has a part of the right: one can worry too much.

953 neuer so]
Walter (ed. 1964): Without ceasing (OED, Never, adv. 4).

954–8 I pray…thee]
Starnes (1945, pp. 1031–2) details similarities in situation and language with the encounter of Lucius and the matron of Corith in Apuleius. See . Finney (1962, p. 92) comments on Sh.’s amused skepticism about Neoplatonic love, revealed when Titania,…gazing at the ludicrous Bottom, recalls Ficino’s three sources of love-inspiring beauty—music, shape, and virtue—in a highly humorous context and with no suggestion of spiritual refinement.…Furthermore,…most serious Neoplatonists excluded music from the realm of beauty. Wentersdorf (1983, p. 359) gives various instances of the ass as a symbol for lust, noting: In Greece and elsewhere, persons who had been found guilty of adultery were seated on asses and paraded in public. But Barkan (1980, p. 357) believes the passage clearly plays on the Platonic tradition in the Actaeon myth, for Titania’s language is particularly apt for the meeting of an animal and a principle of heavenly beauty. W. Slights (1988, p. 269): A force perforce forces Titania triply to swear to a love that will evaporate from her eye and her tongue in the space of a single act. The real forces celebrated in the play would seem to be not marital restraint, chastity, or even, perhaps, constancy, but natural sexual impulses and radically unstable sense-perceptions. Healy (1992, p. 132): Her reaction to his shape and his song parodies the relation between eloquence and object promoted by high cultural humanism. See also nn. 638–9, 685.

954 gentle]
Black (1965, p. 44): Highborn. Titania’s infatuation is so complete that she sees Bottom, ass-head and all, as a gentleman. See OED, a. 1; also 3a: Generous, courteous. Frequent in the phrase a gentle knight. Now only arch. Cf. 349, 982.

954 mortall]
OED (n. 2): A human being in contrast with an immortal. Johnson (1755, 2) comments that the noun is often used in ludicrous language, but OED 2b records as the earliest such usage the same example as Johnson (Prior, 1718). Titania and her fairy use the term without mockery (at 511, 993), but here and at 993 the use is tinged with absurdity because of Bottom’s asshead. Titania’s use at 1621 may be disparaging, and Robin’s at 1139 is scornful. See also n. 476.

note.p: According to NVS practice, the numbering of these lines follows F, which reproduced Q2’s disordering of the lines; see textual n.

div.note:
955 enamoured]
Charmed, fascinated; OED 2, quoting this instance first. See also n. 1592.

955 note]
See n. 944.

957 So…shape]
West (1865, p. 8) compares Sidney’s Arcadia: Thus are my eyes still captive to one sight, Thus all my thoughts are slaves to one thought still (ed. Evans, 1977, p. 131).

957 enthralled]
OED (2 fig.), quoting this instance: To enslave mentally or morally. Now chiefly, to captivate, hold spellbound, by pleasing qualities. Cf. 146.

958 thy…force]
Schmidt (1875, Virtue 5): = the power of thy beauty. Kittredge (ed. 1939): The attractive force of thy gifts of mind and body. Virtue does not mean moral goodness here, but is a general term embracing every kind of excellence.

958 force]
The Clarkes (1879, p. 554): Essential excellence, virtuous property or quality.

958 perforce]
See n. 396.

956 On…view]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Hers is love at first sight, as we say. Cf. OED (View, 5a), quoting this instance: A sight, or glance.

956 On]
For the temporal sense of on see Franz §488, Blake 5.4.2 (c).

959 Mee…mistresse]
Barber (1959, p. 155) characterizes this as a homely form of address, suggesting Bottom’s social world.

959 Mee thinks]
See n. 6.

959–60 you…that]
Porter & Clarke (ed. 1903): Bottom is probably more conservative than modest. He thinks the expression of love at first sight by the woman rash, yet not unlikely in his case and on general principles.

959–60 haue…for]
OED (Reason, n.1 8a), quoting this instance first: To have reason for, or to do something.

960–1 reason…together]
Verity (ed. 1893): Cf. the old proverb that a man cannot love and be wise, from the maxim, amare et sapere vix deo conceditur (Publilius Syrus, Sententiae). Cf. Spenser, Shep. Cal. March 119–20. See Tilley and Dent (L517). Reyher (1947, p. 54) compares Lyly’s Sapho and Phao 5.2 [2:411–14] and Gallathea 3.4 [2:453–6] on truth, love and reason (Fr.). Knowles (privately): The motto of the play, from its fool. See n. 1796–8.

962–3 The more…friends]
Characterized by Brooks (ed. 1979, p. cxxxvi) as: Bottom’s wise wish, which was not that love be subjected to reason, but that the two be made friends. C. Slights (1993, p. 3): When Bottom’s attention is on dramatic performance, he assumes a play-world populated by lovers and tyrants, but in considering a situation in his own life, he thinks in terms of people performing roles in a closely knit human community.

963–4 Nay…occasion]
Smith (ed. 1925): Bottom prides himself on having made a witty and satirical remark.

963 Nay]
See n. 309.

963 gleeke]
Gildon (1710, Glossary): Jeer, &c. Pope (ed. 1725): Joke, or scoff. Johnson (1755), quoting this instance: To sneer; to gibe; to droll upon (OED, v. 2, quoting this instance first). Halliwell (ed. 1854) points out that in Settle’s text for Purcell’s The Fairy Queen (1692, p. 27), the word is altered to break a jest.

963–4 vpon occasion]
OED (n.1 10b), quoting this instance: As occasion or opportunity arises.

965 Thou…beautifull]
Chambers (ed. 1897): The compliment contained in the present line is ambiguous, and the audience may take it in what way they will. Cf. King Edward III (ed. Melchiori, 1998) 1.2.162: As wise as fair, and Chaucer, Tr. 2.584: And be ye wis as ye be fair to see, Wel in the ryng than is the ruby set. Possibly suggesting the proverbial Fair and foolish, on which Sh. enlarges in Oth. 2.1.129–60 (903–35). See Tilley (F28) and Stevenson (1949, p. 135).

966 Not…neither]
Kittredge (ed. 1939): Spoken with an air of modest self-depreciation. Quoted by Blake (6.2.3.1) as an example of the strengthening of negation by the addition of neither as a type of intensifier (OED 3a).

966 wit]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Wisdom. See OED 6a.

967, 969 out…wood]
Brooks (ed. 1979), citing Tilley (W732, He has lost himself in a wood), sees an overtone from the proverbial in a wood = befogged. Holland (ed. 1994): Forest, of course, but also madness, state of confusion; compare Demetrius at [571].

967–8 serue…turne]
Deighton (ed. 1891): To suit my purpose. Baker (ed. 1896): To serve my needs. See OED, Turn 30b (f). Andrews (ed. 1993): The phrase…is frequently erotic in implication (see [Tit. 2.1.95–6 (656–7)]). This is fitting, because a sexual infatuation with a beast is precisely what Oberon wants to inflict upon his Queen. See [554–60]. Bottom, of course, does not intend such an implication.

967 owe]
Wilson (ed. 1924): Own (an archaic or provincial form). OED (†Owe): Shortened ME. form of OWN a. Wells & Taylor (1987, p. 282): This would not only be an exceptionally late occurrence of the form (13th–16th c.), but the only one in the Shakespeare canon, which has own over 800 times. Simple compositorial error seems almost certain. See textual n.

969–1020 Out…silently]
Staton (1963, pp. 175–6, 178): The theme of a nymph wooing a reluctant boy occurs most notably in Ovid in the stories of Salmacis and Hermaphroditus [Met. 4.285–388; Golding 4.347–481, see ] and Echo and Narcissus [Met. 3.344–401].…Its most notable treatment, however, was in the most popular of all Ovidian poems, Shakespeare’s Venus and Adonis. The points of similarity and of contrast between Titania’s wooing and Venus’ are so striking that it is hard to see how Shakespeare could not have intended them, nor how his audience, who knew the poem well, could have missed them. (1) Both love-scenes take place on flowery beds. (2) Both women forcibly detain somewhat reluctant lovers. (3) Both women boast of supernatural power:…(4) Both women promise their lovers rewards:…(5) Both love actions are similar: Titania coys Bottom’s cheeks and kisses his ears, while Venus strokes Adonis’ cheeks and kisses his brow, his cheek, his chin; Titania enrings Bottom as the ivy does the elm, while Venus entangles Adonis in her arms like a bird in a net. (6) Both reluctant lovers are compared to animals:…(7) Bottom’s hairiness is emphasized, while Adonis’ hairless face is several times alluded to. He adds: In the case of Titania’s wooing of Bottom he reinforces the parody of Pyramus and Thisbe by parody of his own version of Venus and Adonis. Forey (1998, pp. 323–4): Her attempt to prevent Bottom’s departure…recalls Venus wooing Adonis,…and an audience familiar with the iconographic tradition of that myth, in which Venus and Adonis amorously recline in flowery groves, might well have found an echo of it in the opening tableau of Act IV, Come sit thee down upon this flowery bed (1511). See also n. 1511–58.
Brooks (ed. 1979): Fairy power, the hero believes in Huon…, will never let them escape from the wood (see ).

970 whether…no]
For the construction see OED, conj. 5a. Proverbial; Dent (W400.1). Scott (ed. 1898): Nolens volens. Titania speaks with authority as a queen.

970 whether]
For the elision, see n. 78.

971 spirit]
See n. 375.

971 common]
Schmidt (1874, 3): Ordinary, mean (OED, a. 11a).

971 rate]
Johnson (1755, 3), quoting this instance: Degree. OED (n.1 2a): Value or worth; (2b): Estimation, consideration.

972 Sommer…state]
Usually interpreted as in Kittredge (ed. 1939): Summer is always in attendance as a member of my train of followers; but Moberly (ed. 1881): I always keep my state in the summer. Hardly It is always summer where I am; for fairies are not exempt from the seasons’ change; when it is cold they are in India. Walter (ed. 1964): Perhaps an allusion to the Summer Lady, or Queen, of the May games. Beaurline (1978, p. 87): Although her name may suggest the moon, Titania is a more fruitful, even wanton, generative spirit,…—that is, she maintains the burgeoning green life of the landscape.

972 still]
See n. 206.

972 state]
OED (16b): High rank, greatness, power; 17a: Splendour, magnificence.

974–8 Ile…goe]
Tillotson (Drayton, Works, 5:21) compares Phoebe’s promises to Endimion in Endimion and Phoebe 207–24. Le Comte (1944, p. 93) adds: The unhappy analogy can be carried still further. Titania tells her long-eared attractor, [quotes 977–8], no bad summary of what happens to Endymion. So maneuverable is the all-important boundary between the sublime and the ridiculous. See also Carter, 2006, esp. para. 19.

975 Iewels…deepe]
Steevens (ed. 1793) compares reflecting Gemmes, That woo’d the slimy bottome of the deepe, [R3 1.4.31–2 (867–8)]. Halliwell (ed. 1856) quotes from Sports of the Fairies, a poem in MS.Ashmol. 36.: Others dive downe to th’bottome of the deepe. Foakes (ed. 1984) refers to R3, and also to pearls and coral, made in the sea (Tmp. 1.2.398–9 [540–1]).

975 deepe]
OED (3a), quoting this instance: The deep sea, the ocean. poetical and rhetorical.

976 pressed flowers]
It is not clear whether the image is of flowers growing or plucked, fresh or dried. Deighton (ed. 1891): Flowers strewed as a bed for you. Page (ed. 1897): Pressed by your body as you lie on them. Black (1965, p. 45): Dry, having had the juice pressed out.

976 flowers…sleepe]
Fender (1968, p. 60), half seriously, finds in this scene and in 1511–58 ghostly echoes of the dream-vision genre, including a perverted version of the dream-vision garden in The Parliament of Fowls, replete with a flower-decked Priapus; he notes that the Hypnerotomachia Poliphili (Venice, 1499) includes a scene in which an ass covered with flowers is sacrificed by nymphs before the altar of Priapus. The description is accompanied by a woodcut..

977–8 And…goe]
Warton (1785, p. 104) draws attention to the parallel with the heavenly tune in Milton, Arcades 72–3, which none can hear Of human mould with grosse unpurged ear. Verity (ed. 1893): She will make Bottom’s mortal (and rather solid) frame as light and ethereal as that of a spirit of the air. Page (ed. 1897) compares MV 5.1.63–5 (2475–7) where Lorenzo refers to this muddy vesture of decay…grossly closing in immortal soules. Theobald (1909, p. 200) gives examples from classical literature of the gods purging the eyes of mortals to give them divine sight. Hankins (1953, pp. 221–3) parallels Palingenius castigating blinded Asses: That with your grosse capacitie, none other thing you see, But bodies grosse, nor true things knowe, but such as shadowes bee (The Zodiake of Life, 1576, p. 179). Brooks (ed. 1979) quotes Scot: To bring the bodie of a man…into such a thin airie nature…is verie impossible (see ). Paster (1993, p. 132): Titania’s plans to purge Bottom of mortal grossness seem to me equally poised between, or inclusive of, ethical and physical reference, just as her interest in his physical state seems poised between the erotic and the maternal. Montrose (1995, p. 68): The sublimation of matter into spirit is identified with the social elevation of the base artisan into the gentry. See also n. 375.

977 purge]
OED (v.1 1a): To make physically pure or clean; 2a: To make figuratively or ideally pure or clean; to free from moral or spiritual defilement. Mowat & Werstine (ed. 1993): It has been suggested that the medical meaning of purge [i.e., to cleanse the body through bleedings or laxatives] should be considered here (cf. Paster, 1993, pp. 130–43).

977 grossenesse]
Johnson (1755, 1), quoting this instance: Coarseness; not subtilty; thickness; greatness of parts. OED (3), quoting this instance: Thickness, density, materiality, solidity.

978 ayery]
OED (Airy, 5): Composed of air, of the nature of air; (6b): Like air in its lightness and bouyancy.

978 goe]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Move about: here, fly as spirits do.

979–80 Pease-blossome…Fairyes]
Theobald (ed. 1733): Here the common Editions have been so extravagant as to split four Fairies into eight: but the old Quarto Impressions both came into my Assistance to reduce ’em to their right Number. The Queen calls them by their respective Names, and immediately the four Fairies enter upon Call. (See textual n.) For the names of the fairies, see DP. For stage representation and doubling, see . For the probable cause of the duplication, see and Brooks (ed 1979 p. xxxi).

981 Fairies.…goe]
Early editors vary in distributing this line among the fairies (see textual n.). White (ed. 1858) no longer doubts that the replies in the singular are to be distributed among the four, and that the question is put by all, but objects to the practice of designating the fairies by number on the model of 993–5 rather than by name, finding this inconsistent with the other speech prefixes in the scene. Wilson (ed. 1924): [Q1’s] arrangement represents the swift appearance of the fairies, one after another, with beautiful precision. Honigmann (1976, p. 121) compares this use of a group prefix that cannot be taken to imply simultaneous speech with Cor. 5.6.120–2 (3793–5). Foakes (ed. 1984): These phrases do not in fact form a pentameter, and are printed here as prose. Wright (1988, p. 146) quotes this line as an example of an alexandrine (hexameter) made up of three or more speeches.

981 Readie]
OED (1c), quoting only this instance and MV (4.1.2 [1905]): Used in replying to a call or summons. Obs.

981 Where…goe]
Deighton (ed. 1891): On what errand do you wish to send us? Probably unaware that Settle had made the change in 1692 (see textual n.), Thirlby (MS 1725–33; MS 1747–53) wondered why this question rather than What shall we do?

982–1015 Be…Mustardseede]
Echoes of situation and phrasing of this episode in the introduction of Jonson’s satiric characterization of Anthony Munday provide an early indication of the impact on audiences: see The Case is Altered 1.1.20–36 (Works ed. Herford & Simpson, 3:106–7), esp. 20–1, 27, 33, 34–6.

982–92 Be…curtesies]
Gildon (1710, 7:317): Titania’s Order to the Fairies to Honour her Love [is] what Mr. Dryden has often instanc’d as one of the prettiest Flights of Fancy in Shakespear. Dodd (1752, 1:83–4) quotes Dryden without reference to Gildon, substituting humour for Honour; he also compares Beaumont and Fletcher’s The Faithful Shepherdess (1.3) where Cloe seeks to detain Thenot by listing the delights she offers him in the woods, and unspecified passages of Theocritus and Vergil. For the classical convention of a catalogue of delights offered to the beloved see e.g. Theocritus Idyl XI; Vergil Ecl. 2.45–55 (quoted Bradbrook, 1951, p. 159), Ovid Met. 13.810–20 (Golding 13.953–63, see ); cf. also Marlowe’s Dido, n. 984–6 and . Hazlitt (1817, pp. 130–1): Titania’s exhortation…is remarkable for a certain cloying sweetness in the repetition of the rhymes…. The sounds of the lute and of the trumpet are not more distinct than the poetry of [this] passage, and of the conversation between Theseus and Hippolyta [1624–48]. Drake (1817, 2:345) praises the passage’s originality and playfulness of fancy. Skottowe (1824, 1:260): Titania’s commands are admirably adapted to the capabilities of the delicate and fragile forms on which they are laid; the tasks she assigns them yielding delight in their performance.

982 this gentleman]
See n. 349.

983 Hop]
Johnson (1755, 1), quoting this instance: To jump; to skip lightly. OED (v.1 2): Dance. See n. 2178. See also n. 2095.

983 gambole…eyes]
The Clarkes (1879, p. 358): Frolic in his sight. OED (Gambol 2), quoting this instance: To leap or spring, in dancing or sporting; now chiefly of animals and children.

984–6 Feede…Bees]
Fripp (1928) compares Marlowe’s Dido (see ). Forey (1998, p. 324) compares the tale of Philemon and Baucis in Golding’s Ovid 8.852–5 (see ): Titania’s reference to honey stolen from the bees…picks up Golding’s new taken from the Bee, which does not appear in Ovid. And as so often in A Midsummer Night’s Dream, there is an inversion of Ovidian roles at this point, as Shakespeare’s fairy queen offers to the lowly mortal foods Ovid’s peasants offered to Jove.

984–5 Apricocks…Mulberries]
Taking literally in a physical sense Titania’s offer to purge Bottom’s mortall grossenesse, Paster (1993, p. 132) points out the laxative effects and aphrodisiac associations of a diet consisting entirely of fruits.

984 Apricocks]
Apricots. OED, Etym. suggests a link with L. præcoquum, early-ripe, ripe in summer.

984 Dewberries]
