Hanmer (ed. 1744, Glossary 6:3Z4v): Strictly and properly are the fruit of one of the species of wild Bramble called the creeping or the lesser Bramble: but as they stand here among the more delicate fruits they must be understood to mean Rasberries which are also of the Bramble-kind. Henley (Steevens, ed. 1785): Dewberries are gooseberries, which are still so called in several parts of the kingdom. Pye (1807, p. 56): The Dewberry is…the fruit of that bramble called by Millar Rubus minor fructu cæruleo, from which circumstance it is sometimes vulgarly called the blueberry. It is a very delicate fruit, and as well worthy of horticulture as the strawberry. OED, quoting this instance first under b: A species of blackberry or bramble-berry…: in Great Britain Rubus cæsius, a low-growing procumbent species, the black fruit of which has a bluish bloom; OED notes that the name is applied to various fruits, and that in Sh. it is mentioned among delicate cultivated fruits.

985 greene figges]
Fresh ripe figs, the fruit of Ficus carica. Ellacombe (1878, p. 70): The Fig tree, celebrated from the earliest times for the beauty of its foliage, and for its sweetness and good fruit (Judges ix, 11), is said to have been introduced into England by the Romans; but the more reliable accounts attribute its introduction to Cardinal Pole. Ellacombe is disapprovingly aware of vulgar connotations of the word: cf. Florio (1598, Fica): a figge. Also a womans conie or quaint. Also a flurt with ones fingers giuen in disgrace. Webb (1989, p. 43): An emblem of fecundity. Idem (1991, p. 102): The Fig makes its contribution to the various expletives which, by the first letter, recall the sexual four-letter word,…A suggestion confirmed by the image of the Fig, which resembles the sexual organs, both male and female.

985 Mulberries]
OED: The fruit of any tree of the genus Morus, esp. the Black Mulberry, M. nigra. For the connection with Ovid’s tale of Pyramus and Thisbe, see n. 544. Ellacombe (1878, pp. 128–9) notes its mention in Ovid’s description of the Golden Age (Met. 1.105), and quotes Spenser, An Elegie: With loue iuice staind the mulberie, The fruit that dewes the Poets braine (15–16). Rohde (1935, p. 147): Adonis was fed with mulberries (Ven. 1103).

986–8 The…eyes]
De Ornellas (2004, p. 131) finds that these harsh, exploitative instructions show a surprising lack of empathy with the non-human, natural world in a Queen who is, supposedly, integrated with it. Knowles (privately): Honey must always be stolen (bees don’t offer it as a gift), and shaving waxy fuzz is hardly harsh or exploitative. These minute imaginative details are essential to the delight that everyone finds in the passage.

986 The…Bees]
Sitwell (1948, p. 193): The change from honie-bags to humble-Bees…gives a faint velvetiness to the texture, like the coat of the bees. Armstrong (1946, p. 25) suggests an influence of the account of bee life given by Fidus to Euphues and Philautus in Lyly’s [Euphues and his England], quoting hauing gathered out of euery flower honny they return loden in their mouthes, thighs, wings, and all the bodye (2:45).

986 hony bagges]
Deighton (ed. 1891): The small cysts in which the honey is carried. See OED, Honey, a., 7a; Bag 11, quoting this instance. See 1523–6.

986 humble Bees]
Johnson (1755, Humblebee), quoting this instance: A buzzing wild bee. OED, quoting this instance: A large wild bee, of the genus Bombus, which makes a loud humming sound; a bumble-bee. See 1521.

987 And…thighes]
Deighton (ed. 1891): And crop their thighs of the wax with which they are laden, to serve as tapers: the pollen which is borne home by the bees on the outside of their legs being apparently taken by Shakespeare for wax: and waxen thighs not meaning literally made of wax, but laden with wax. See OED, Waxen, a, 3, quoting this instance first.

988 light…eyes]
Johnson (ed. 1765): I know not how Shakespeare, who commonly derived his knowledge of nature from his own observation, happened to place the glow-worm’s light in his eyes, which is only in his tail. Mason (1785, pp. 11–12): Sh. had studied with attention the book of nature…The blunder…is not in Shakespeare, but in [those], who have construed too literally a Poetical Expression. Knight (ed. 1839): Let us correct the poet, and make Titania describe the glow-worm with a hatred of all metaphor:—And light them at the fiery glow-worm’s tail. We fear this will not do. Wright (1877): Compare Herrick’s Night-piece, to Julia (Hesperides, [Poetical Works, ed. L. C. Martin, 1956, p. 217]): Her Eyes the Glow-worme lend thee. Harrison (Glowworms, 1971, p. 395): Sh. may well have read New World accounts of glowworms, or fireflies, belonging to another family, the Elaterids…These insects possess on each side of the thorax a round polished space from which light is given forth; these are the organs called eyes by the older writers.

989 haue]
Rann (ed. 1787): Attend on. Brooks (ed. 1979): As royal paramour, Bottom is to have the full honours of the couchée and the levée. Holland (ed. 1994): If Titania is thinking of having sex with Bottom, then arise will carry suggestions of have an erection. Knowles (privately) finds greater relevance in the description of sunrise, and Phoebus gins arise (Cym. 2.3.21 [983]).

990 painted]
Schmidt (1875, Paint 3): To diversify with colours. OED (3 fig.): Adorned with bright or varied colouring. See also n. 1330.

991 To…eyes]
Gentleman (ed. 1774): Titania proposes as agreeable Fairy entertainment for her gallant as might be wished. Page (ed. 1897): It was formerly believed that the moonbeams, falling on the face of a sleeper, had the power to deprive him of reason. Knowles (privately): The moonbeams might wake him.

991 Moone-beames]
OED, quoting this instance first: A ray of moonlight.

992 Nod]
Schmidt (1875, vb. 1): Intr. to incline the head…by way of making a slight salutation (OED, v.1, quoting this instance).

992 Elues]
See n. 387.

992 doe…curtesies]
OED (Courtesy, 8): The customary expression of respect by action or gesture, esp. to a superior. Obs.

993–5 1. Fai.…3. Fai. Haile]
Grey (1754, 1:60): Here are but three fairys that salute Bottom, nor does he address himself to more, though four had entred before whom the queen had call’d by name, and commanded to do their curtesies. In short, I cannot tell what is become of monsieur Moth, unless he be prudently walk’d off, for fear of Cavalero Cobweb: for we hear no more of him either here, or in the next act, where the queen, Bottom, and fairies are introduced again. Anon. Capell (ed. 1768) gave the first Fairy’s second haile to the second Fairy, and so on; thus, as Malone (ed. 1790), without acknowledging Capell, observed, each of them should address Bottom. See textual n.

996 cry…mercy]
Hunter (ed. 1870), comparing AYL 3.5.61 (1834): To cry any one mercy is to beg pardon (OED, Mercy †3).

996–1015 I beseech…Mustardseede]
Gentleman (ed. 1774): If Bottom is represented by a good low comedian, this scene must have a spirited effect; his application of the fairies names is very ludicrous. Cope (1973, p. 224): Bottom sees and knows [the fairies] as functionaries…assigned to assure his bodily comfort.…But at the same time he is so literal-minded that he reduces each of these creatures to their nominal symbolic value.…This double knowledge of the fairies on Bottom’s part echoes the double theory of the actor, but it also focuses the dream. Elam (1984, pp. 135–6): Each name-noun in Bottom’s fairyland roll-call has at once a personal and a substantive value, appertaining to the fairy bearer and at the same time standing for the part of nature that the creature embodies.…Bottom finds himself in the privileged position…of an unmediated communion with the natural essences underlying the lexicon.…In the fairy realm of Bottom’s bottomless dream, each nomen is also immediately and magically a numen.…Bottom’s effortless engagement with the occult semiosis of the forest amounts, of course, not only to a naturalizing of language but equally to a linguisticizing of the natural scene. Carroll (1985, p. 150): Each character is a reification. Bottom talks to them as people…yet comically reverses their transformations, reducing them back to objects at the same time. Griffiths (ed. 1996, p. 150): Hall’s fairies here were dressed in costumes appropriate to their names.
Malone (1780, 1:117) compares The Maydes Metamorphosis (1600) 2.2.73 ff., where there is a dialogue between some foresters and a troop of fairies, very similar to the present (Lyly, 3:360). Despite Malone’s belief that this anonymous play was written some years before 1600, it is usually dated post MND. Delattre (1912, p. 98) finds the punning upon their names…much more finely developed in Sh.

996–7 worships…worshippes]
Delius (ed. 1859) argues that Bottom first addresses all four, and then one in particular (Ger.). Editorial practice in indicating possessives, whether plural or singular, has varied. OED (n. 5a): With your or his: A title of honour, used in addressing or speaking of a person of note. But Johnson (1755, 3) admits it only as A term of ironical respect.

996 beseech]
OED (2), misquoting this instance: To beg earnestly for.

999 desire you of]
Steevens (ed. 1778) objects that the line has been very unecessarily altered (see textual n.), and gives many examples of the construction. On the same expression in AYL 5.4.53 (2631), Knowles (ed. 1977), explaining the construction as a verb of wishing with accusative of the person and an of-phrase specifying the thing wished for or required, cites OED, Desire, v. 6b; Franz §514. See n. 1006; cf. n. 853.

1000–1 if…you]
Halliwell (ed. 1856): In allusion to the web of the common house-spider being used in stopping the effusion of blood, a simple and good appliance now nearly obsolete. Irving & Marshall (ed. 1888): It is doubtful whether the remedy is a very efficacious one; as the amount of dirt introduced into the wound does more harm than the styptic power of the cobweb does good. Barber (1959, p. 156): When Bottom proposes to make bold with Cobweb, he treats him as a thing, undoing the personification on which the little fellow’s life depends.

1002 honest]
OED (1c), quoting this instance: As a vague epithet of appreciation or praise, esp. as used in a patronizing way to an inferior. (Cf. worthy.)

1002 gentleman]
OED (4b), quoting this instance: A polite term of address; obs. in sing.

1003 Pease-blossome]
OED (Pease B): The earlier form of PEA; (5) quoting this as the first instance of the comb. -blossom. Halliwell (ed. 1856) remarks on the character Peascod listed in the surviving plot of the lost romantic comedy The Dead Man’s Fortune, c. 1590–1 (Harbage (1964, p. 54).

1004 Squash]
Steevens (MS 1773–8), quoting TN 1.5.157–8 (451): An immature peascod. OED (n.1 1), quoting MND first: Also applied contemptuously to persons. Obs. exc. arch.

1005 Peascod]
Pea-pod (OED, Peasecod. Now arch. or dial.). Schmidt (1875): Quibbling with codpiece? Green pease a favourite present of rustic lovers. Knowles (ed. AYL, 1977, pp. 93–4): Peascod is a homonym in reverse, as it were, of codpiece; cod = the scrotum; improperly in pl. testicles (OED, Cod, sb.1 4). Hackett (2003, p. 344): Associated with love and sexuality in folk-magic, partly because of the verbal inversion of the term cod-piece—a cod was a bag of seeds. Of both Peaseblossom’s parents, Colman (1974, p. 27) observes: Bottom is being lumpishly facetious…: behind the primary meaning of squash, an unripe peascod, there lurks a suggestion of the human female squashed by the male in coitus, and the hint is strengthened by the nearness of peascod itself, since cod can have the secondary meaning testicle; he adds that apart from this instance and Quince’s reference to syphilitic baldness (358–9), the amateur actors reveal no special aptitude for risqué linguistic hair-splitting.

1006 you of]
Capell (1783, 2.3:108): The folio’s give us—of you, and the moderns make it Bottom’s phrase in [999] likewise, which it should not be in either; the present is better suited, and has examples (plenty) in other plays of that age. See n. 999.

1007 to]
I.e. too, also.

1010–14 I know…now]
Reed (in Steevens, ed. 1793): These words are spoken ironically. According to the opinion prevailing in our author’s time, mustard was supposed to excite to choler; he refers to the note on Shr. 4.3.23–5 (2001–3), where Thomas Walkington’s The Optick Glasse of Humors (1607, f. 30v) is quoted, advising a cholericke man to abstaine from all salte, scorched drye meates, from mustard, and such like things. Knight (ed. 1839): The patience of the family of Mustard in being devoured by the ox-beef is one of those brief touches of wit, so common in Shakspere, which take him far out of the range of ordinary writers. But his critics love common-place; and therefore Hanmer would read parentage,—Farmer, passions,—and Mason, passing. Reid then solemnly pronounces no change is necessary; and so half a page of the variorum Shakspere is filled. See textual n., and conj., . Verity (ed. 1893): I.e. submissiveness under the misfortune of being devoured by the cowardly giant, ox-beef: which misfortune (not, of course, the pungent strength of the mustard!) has often brought the tears into Bottom’s eyes—a quibble that explains itself.

1010–11 your patience]
Mowat & Werstine (ed. 1993): perhaps, your Patience, as in your Honor.

1010–11 patience]
Reynolds & Sawyer (1959, p. 519) through the homonym patience/patients link this speech to culinary and medical usages of mustard. Dobson (1968, §410[ii]) notes that Richard Browne in The English School Reformed (1700, p. 76) lists patients : patience as a homophone pairing. Andrews (ed. 1993) suggests a reference to Mark 4:30–2 where the meekness and patience required for the kingdom of God is compared to a grain of mustardseed. For the pronunciation cf. n. 162.

1011 gyantlike]
OED, quoting this instance: Gigantic.

1014 you more]
Dyce (ed. 1857): Here the old copies omit of, by an evident error. Compare the two preceding speeches of Bottom. Brooks (ed. 1979): Though the construction without of is in itself possible, that Bottom pointlessly varies his formula is less likely than that the compositor dropped a particle. See textual n.

1016–19 Come…chastitie]
Kott’s (1964; 1966, pp. 220–1) view is notorious: Titania drags the monster to bed, almost by force.…The monstrous ass is being raped by the poetic Titania, while she still keeps on chattering about flowers. Leggatt (1974, pp. 110–11, without any mention in the book of Kott): The suggestion of bestiality in Titania’s affair with Bottom is kept under control by the cool, decorative poetry as she leads him off the stage.…They are going off to bed, but there is nothing torrid about it. The forest might have been a place of unbridled eroticism, but it is not. Watts (2000, p. 137): Are we to believe that they actually have sexual intercourse?…Oberon will not have intended such an outcome, but it makes the play funnier and (arguably) morally better if he is cuckolded, since his own conduct is morally suspect.

1016 bower]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Properly means a chamber, thence used generally of a shady recess formed by trees and shrubs (OED, n.1 2a, 3). OED (n.1 2b): Especially applied to a lady’s private apartment (but see 1576). See n. 1029.

1017–19 The Moone…chastitie]
Chaudhuri (1990, p. 28): The watery moon is associated with virginity: once indeed in laudatory vein, in compliment to Queen Elizabeth [532–41], but chiefly to express the barren futility of such a condition. See also nn. 222–3; 532–41; 539; 1019.

1017–18 The Moone…flower]
Walker (1860, 3:48): Alluding to the supposed origin of dew in the moon; he quotes Mac. 3.5.23–4 (1453–4): Vpon the Corner of the Moone There hangs a vap’rous drop. Kittredge (ed. 1939) quotes Batman vpon Bartholome: Ambrose…calleth the Moone mother of dew [1582, 11.6]; The Moone gathereth deawe in the aire, for she printeth the vertue of hir moysture in the aire, and chaungeth the ayre in a manner that is vnseene, & breedeth and gendereth deaw in the vtter part thereof [8.29].

1017 me thinkes]
See n. 6.

1018 And…flower]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Their tears being the dew.

1018 little]
OED (3), quoting this instance: Used to convey an implication of endearment, or of tender feeling on the part of the speaker.

1019 enforced chastitie]
Opinion is divided over whether this refers to rape, to compulsory celibacy, or to both. The earliest editor to comment, Rolfe (ed. 1877), follows Schmidt (1874, Enforce 3) in understanding violation. Others understanding violation include Deighton (ed. 1891), Chambers (ed. 1897), Parrott (ed. 1938; 1953), Doran (ed. 1959), Clemen (ed. 1963), Wells (ed. 1967), Evans (ed. 1974), Brooks (ed. 1979), Foakes (ed. 1984), Hollindale (1992, p. 31). Possibly influenced by Kott (see n. 1016–19), Uman (2001, pp. 76–7) argues unconvincingly that Bottom as well as Titania is a rape victim, and that in tying his tongue Titania becomes Tereus and Bottom Philomel.
The earliest editor to interpret the phrase as compulsory celibacy is Johnson (ed. 1894): Chastity to which they are compelled by their own vows. He was possibly influenced by the characterization of Titania’s lines offered by Maginn (1837, p. 377): She mourns over the restraints which separate her from the object of her burning affection, and thinks that the moon and flowers participate in her sorrows. Sprague (ed. 1896) refers to Moberly (ed. 1881) who comments on 1018: The rathe primrose dies forsaken, says Milton; type of maidens who live and die unloved. Giordano-Orsini (Sogno ed. 1934) finds this interpretation appropriate to the tone of the comedy, which he considers epithalamic; he compares 74–87. Lever (ed. 1961): Grieving at the virtue that is forced upon her (spoken in pity of flowers that have not been able to bear seed; an idea well suited to a play in praise of marriage). Andrews (1984, p. 188): Titania’s meaning is clear: women in love are sometimes chaste against their wills (see, e.g., the plight of Hermia, as described by Theseus in I. i.). As for those whose love is not sought, casta est, quam nemo rogavit (Amores I. viii. 43). In either case, enforced chastity is chastity which has been denied the opportunity Titania does not mean to deny herself. See also Felltham’s Resolves, LXXXV, Of Marriage, and single life (1628, p. 246): And though the Romans had their Vestals, yet after their thirty yeeres continuance, the cruelty of inforced chastitie was not in force against them.
Among those entertaining the possibility of both options: Carroll (1985, pp. 152–3), Bevington (ed. 1988), Mowat & Werstine (ed. 1993), Holland (ed. 1994), Greenblatt (ed. 1997), McDonald (ed. 2000). Montrose (1983, p. 92 n. 44.; see also 1996, p. 174 f.) quoting OED Enforced, (1): Subjected to force; (2): That is forced upon a person; constrained: In one sense, then, the allusion is to sexual violation; in the other, it is to the injunction against sexual relations during menstruation (Leviticus 20:18; Ezekiel 18:6), which was commonly repeated by sixteenth and seventeenth century writers. Carter (2006, para. 19), argues that if violation is meant, then it seems to be of Bottom, since Titania is neither chaste nor unwilling, and that the possible parallel with the amorous moon of mythology, Selene, and her infatuation with Endimion…implies Titania has chastity forced upon her by the uncooperative Bottom. Both these readings…support the sexual aims of Titania; citing the behavior of each at 1511–58, she suggests: Bottom’s innocence and lack of perception render him oblivious to Titania’s advances, and therefore either interpretation of the enforced chastity will suffice. The non-sexual nature of the relationship chiefly serves to enhance the playful, though dark, exploration of the confusions of desire throughout the play.

1020 Ty…silently]
Thirlby (MS 1747–53): He was a prater, & that perhaps was to account for his silence. Furness (ed. 1895): Is it insinuated that however deeply Titania may be enamoured with Bottom’s fair large ears, and her eye enthralled to his shape, she can find no corresponding charm in his talk? There is a limit even to the powers of the magic love-juice. Thiselton (1903, p. 45): As Titania has found Bottom to be as wise as beautiful…, and since her ear is also much enamored of his note…, we can only presume that his tongue is to be tied, because she is jealous of his conversation with her fairy attendants. Walter (ed. 1964): Is Bottom braying too much? However, silence was traditionally necessary to enter fairyland. Wells (ed. 1967): This suggests that Bottom may (as he often does in performance) be making involuntary asinine noises. Phillips (ed. 1969): So that he does not attract Oberon’s attention by singing or braying. Or does she find his voice somewhat less than charming? Referring to Wells, Brooks (ed. 1979) compares the beasts braying in Oberon’s wood in Huon (see ).
Emendations of the line’s supposedly defective metre (see textual n.) ignored the common dropping or slurring of medial v (regularly printed u) in louers; cf. Abbott §466; Dobson (1968), §400–1. See also n. 156.

1020 Ty…tongue]
Halliwell (ed. 1856) gives several 16th–17th-c. examples of the phrase. Watt (ed. 1906): This was a common expression and survives in the form tongue-tied (OED, Tie, v. B.2). Cf. n. 1901. Scott (ed. 1898), implausibly: Titania fears that Bottom, being led to her special chamber, may utter some improper remark. She has not appreciated his comments on her followers.

1020 silently]
OED (1), quoting this instance: Without speaking; without noise or commotion. Usually understood as applying to Bottom, but Titania may be ordering the conduct of her fairies.

1020–1 Exit…Enter]
Wells (ed. 1967): Again there is no significant break between the scenes, in either time or place. Foakes (ed. 1984): A short break in time and a shift in imagined location…may mark an interval. Holland (ed. 1994): In modern productions with an interval the second half starts here.

1021 Enter…goodfellow]
Qq’s SD is probably a massed entry (see n. 25). All other editions follow F1 in putting Robin’s entrance later. See textual n.

1022–1506 I…well]
Wilson (ed. 1924) believes that much of this long scene…belongs…to the first draft…and may have seemed tedious to Shakespeare. He divides the scene into six sections, each containing revisions and cuts, but his theory depends partly on the change in the SP’s for Robin (to Puck) which he believes indicates a later revision, an idea which has been discredited by Kennedy (1998, pp. 187–190). See , . See n. 1509–50.

1022–56 I…Asse]
Gentleman (ed. 1774): Though Puck’s narration possesses spirit, ease, and painting, yet as it only recites what is already known, and not with uncommon force or humour, we think the scene would begin better with Oberon’s speech, supposing he had heard all from his attendant spirit before they appear. Wells (ed. 1967) and others note that this scene begins with a recapitulatory passage. Holland (ed. 1994) adds: Its expository style is common at the mid-point of a Shakespeare play.

1022–4 I…extreamitie]
Arnold (1911, p. 82): Sh.’s entrance soliloquies generally, in some way, prepare for the ensuing scene. For example, Oberon wonders what has happened to his queen ([1022–4]), whereupon Puck enters and informs him.

1023–4 Then…extreamitie]
Ness (1941, p. 128) lists this couplet as a cue rhyme: see n. 520–1.

1023 Then…was]
Hunter (ed. 1870): Then, I wonder what it was.

1023 next]
Verity (ed. 1893): I.e. immediately after she awoke = first.

1024 Which…extreamitie]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Which, from the potency of the drug, she will be compelled to love with ridiculous passion.

1024 must]
Furness (ed. 1895): Compelled by the love juice. See n. 447.

1024 in extreamitie]
Johnson (1755, n. 1): The utmost point; the highest degree (OED 3, quoting this instance). Abbott (p. 483 n. 5) compares Ham. 3.2.168 (2037), in extremity. Brooks (ed. 1979) quotes Diana (ed. Kennedy, p. 82), in what great extremities love hath put me.

1026 Here…messenger]
Arnold (1911, pp. 54–5) gives a dozen examples of the prepared entrance (including this one) where the soliloquizer concludes by signalling someone is approaching, a stereotyped device.

1026 How…spirit]
See n. 375.

1026 mad]
Schmidt (1875, 3): Frolicksome to wildness. Walter (ed. 1964): How should Puck’s entry justify this description? Why is he mad at this point? Farrow (ed. 1972): Wild. Perhaps Puck dances or cartwheels in; and he enjoys wild confusion better than reasonable behaviour. Weisstein (2000, p. 310): Oberon…refers to his usually willing but sometimes stubborn servant as a madcap fond of playing practical jokes on the common folk.

1027–1221 What…spight of mee]
The rhyme initiated by Titania at 969 (in counterpoint to Bottom’s prose) and consisting mostly of couplets, is continued by Oberon in his dialogue with Puck, and by the lovers until Helena turns her reproaches upon Hermia. It is interrupted by the incomplete verse at 1071–2 & kill mee to, and by the apparent lack of a syllable at 1103–4 (see nn.).

1027 nightrule]
Johnson (1755), quoting this instance: A tumult in the night. Steevens (ed. 1778): What frolick of the night, what revelry is going forward? Rann (ed. 1787): Prank. Douce (1807, 1:192): Rule in this word has the same meaning as in the Christmas lord of mis-rule. Nares (1822; 1905, Night-rule) holds that misrule means misgovernment, or misconduct; exemption from all common rule and order, and that nightrule means such conduct as generally rules in the night, (OED, Night 13a, quoting this instance only; and Rule, 13c: Misrule, disorder, stir, riot). Halliwell (ed. 1856) quotes from Statutes of the Streets of [this City against Annoiances]: No man shall, after the houre of nine at the night, keep any rule whereby any such sudden outcry be made in the still of the night, as making any affray,…or singing or reuelling…. (John Stow, Survey of London, [1633, item # 30], p. 666 [sig. 3L2v]). Chambers (ed. 1897): Order kept by night. Kittredge (ed. 1939): Actions in the night. McDonald (ed. 2000): Disorder, revels. Knowles (privately) compares Malvolio’s objection to the vnciuill rule of Sir Toby and companions (TN 2.3.123 [818]). Bates (2002, p. 115) interprets Oberon’s night-rule as art’s morally neutral alternative to the law-beholden rule of rational day.
Douce (1807, 1:192) believes rule is a corruption of revel formerly written reuel. Kökeritz (1942, p. 41) sees it as a phonetic development of revel merry-making. But Cercignani (1981, pp. 327–8) questions the reliability of the interpretation of reeles [Ant. 2.7.94 (1443); Ham. 1.4.9 (613)] and nightrule…(night-rule F) as contractions of revel (Kökeritz [1953], 189).

1027 haunted]
OED (ppl. a. 2a): Frequented by many people, much resorted to; (3a), first instance dated 1660: Frequented or much visited by spirits,…etc. See also OED, Haunt, v. 3, 5. Thirlby (MS 1747–53) conjectures hallow’d. Cf. 2171. Deighton (ed. 1891) is probably not alone among Sh.’s later audience in understanding Oberon to use the ghostly sense: Oberon applies to the presence of human beings the term which they would use of the presence of fairies, spirits, etc., though the sense in which the word is so used is a secondary one, the original meaning nothing more than to frequent.

1028–9 My…bower.]
The alteration in terminal punctuation in these two lines (see textual nn.) makes a crucial difference in meaning, first examined by Swander (1990, p. 86): In the authoritative text [Q1] Titania and Bottom are…at or near the bower: a shadowy offstage place of great importance. Earlier…she was at the bank of wild thyme. In [Q1] there are two distinct places, only one of which the audience will ever see. Knowles (privately) objects: The bank of wild thyme was quite over-canopied by woodbine, roses, and eglantine forming a shady recess.

1028 monster]
Webb (1988, p. 33): A prodigy of nature. See 1418.

1029 close…bower]
Cf. Venus’s secret bowre in FQ 4.5.5.4.

1029 close]
Johnson (1755, 2; 12; 17): 2. Secret; private.…12. Hidden; not revealed.…17. Retired; solitary (OED, a. 4a; 4b). Foakes (ed. 1984): This could also mean closed, curtained off; Titania’s bower may still be in view. Cf. FQ 3.9.5.8, close bowre.

1029 consecrated bower]
Wiles (1993, p. 121) presents literary examples to show that in wedding masques and epithalamia bower is equated with the marriage bed, and he notes that this bower is consecrated like the bride bed of 2187. See n. 2199.

1029 consecrated]
Cotgrave (1611, Consacré): Consecrated, hallowed, dedicated, deuoted vnto (OED, ppl. a. 1).

1029 bower]
See n. 1016.

1030 her…hower]
Deighton (ed. 1891): That period of time during which her senses are dulled by sleep.

1030 dull]
Johnson (1755, 10): Drowsy; sleepy. Schmidt (1874, 2): Lifeless, faint.…Hence = weary, sleepy. Mowat & Werstine (ed. 1993): Unconscious (because asleep).

1031 crew]
Johnson (1755, 1; 3): A company of people.…3. Now generally used in a bad sense (OED, n.1 3a; 4).

1031 patches]
OED (n.2), quoting this instance: A fool; a clown, dolt, booby. Grey (1754, 1:60–1) compares Tmp. 3.2.63 (1415), Thou scuruy patch. Johnson (ed. 1765): A term of opprobry like ragamuffin, or tatterdemalion. Steevens (ed. 1773, Appendix, 10:L16v ): Probably…from Patch, cardinal Wolsey’s fool. In the western counties, cross patch is still used, for perverse, ill-natured fool. Mr. Warton. Idem (MS 1778?–): So named from the patched or pye coats of the fools or jesters of those times. Tyrwhitt (in Idem, ed. 1778): A corruption of the Italian pazzo,…a fool, comparing MV 2.5.46 (883). Ayscough (ed. 1784): Low, paltry fellows. Pye (1807, p. 56) corrects his friend Tom Warton: Cross Patch is the nursery-name for a froward child. Caldecott (MS 1813–33): Asses. Singer (ed. 1826): Simpleton. J. D. (1880, p. 304): A boy, and hence a servant citing MV 2.5.46 (883) and Mac. 5.3.15 (2232). Moberly (ed. 1881): Perhaps the short for patch-breech, citing Per. 2.1.14 (563). Furnivall (1875–6, p. 313) quotes Florio (1598): Patarino, a base mechanical fellow, a porter or daie labourer. Doran (ed. 1959) compares 1736 (patcht a foole).

1031 rude]
Valpy (ed. 1833): Simple. OED (a. 1a): Uneducated, unlearned; ignorant; and (4b): Unmannerly. Parker (1996, pp. 83–4) explores various meanings, linking the term to the lexicon of class distinction.

1031 Mechanicals]
Florio (1598, Mecanico): Mecanical, base, vile, a workman in whom there is a good hand and a good wit, a handicraftsman, a man of an occupation (OED, n. B. 1, quoting this instance first). Sh.’s only other use of the noun is derogatory (2H6 1.3.193 [590]). The term handicraft man is used of Bottom at 1755–6.

1032 bread]
Johnson (1755, 2): Food in general, such as nature requires. Deighton (ed. 1891): Livelihood (OED, n.1 5a, but first recorded usage 1719).

1032 vpon]
Schmidt (1875): Denoting a…business in which a person is employed (OED, prep. 10a).

1032 stalles]
Schmidt (1875, 2): A small shed in which an occupation is carried on. OED (n.1 6a): A bench, table, board or the like, esp. one in front of a shop, upon which goods are exposed for sale; a booth or covered stand for the sale of wares at a market, fair, or in the open street.

1033 rehearse]
See n. 884.

1034 Intended]
Johnson (1755, 4): To mean; to design (OED, Intend, v. 19, quoting this instance first). For a different use see nn. 514, 1371.

1034 Theseus]
Disyllabic: see n. 26. For the genitive of proper nouns see n. 182 (Venus).

1035 The…sort]
Grinfield (MS 1834–63, p. 284): [1035 underlined, with marginal comment:] Description of all dull beings.

1035 shallowest thickskinne]
Thirlby (MS 1733–47) compares 1869 (Hard handed) and Wiv. 4.5.2 (2220–1), where the Host roughly addresses Simple as boor and thick skin. Halliwell (ed. 1856): A thick-skin was a common term of contempt for a stupid country bumpkin, derived from the notion that a thick skin and a dull understanding were generally found united in the same person. Deighton (ed. 1891): Most empty-brained blockhead. Verity (ed. 1893): Puck judges Bottom too hastily; his comrades regard him as their leader, with the best wit [1755]. Black (1965, p. 46): Most foolish…horny-handed dolt.

1035 barraine]
Johnson (1755, Barren 4): Unmeaning; uninventive; dull (OED, Barren, a. 8, quoting this instance first). Steevens (ed. 1793): Unpregnant. Caldecott (MS 1813–33): Unprolific. Valpy (ed. 1833): Stupid. The Clarkes (ed. 1864): Brainless, ignorant. Wright (ed. 1877): Witless. For a different use see n. 81.

1035 sort]
Most, beginning with Thirlby (MS 1747–53, at 1043), gloss company (OED, n.2 17). A few put crew which is found at 1031, or in Dyce (ed. 1864, 9:Glossary). Cf. 1043. Perhaps the more general term kind is intended, as in 1429, where the meaning is: Of a (certain) sort, of a certain kind, etc. [OED (n.2 1a).

1036 presented]
See n. 871–2.

1036 sport]
OED (n.1 5d), quoting this instance: A theatrical performance or show; a play. Brooks (ed. 1979): Entertainment; disport (aphetic form) (OED, Sport, n.1, etymological note, and 1). For similar uses see 1764, 1839.

1037 Forsooke]
Robin uses an excessively dramatic word—defined as To abandon, leave entirely,…desert (OED, Forsake, v. 4)—to describe Bottom’s exit, which led Phillips (ed. 1969) to comment: Deserted the action (in fact, of course, Bottom was meant to be off). More accurately, Deighton (ed. 1891): Left the stage. The inflated verb is in keeping with the exaggerated description of the whole passage.

1037 his Scene]
Delius (ed. 1859): The stage where he was acting (Ger.).

1037 entred…brake]
Thirlby (MS 1747–53) glosses into for in, and refers to the almost identical phrasing at 886. For in used for into with enter see Abbott §159, citing R2 2.3.160 (1271). See also OED, In, prep. 31.

1038 When…take]
Deighton (ed. 1891): When I caught him thus alone.

1039 An…head]
See n. 914–6.

1039 …nole]
Florio (1598, Testa): A head, a pate, a skonce, a nole. Gildon (1710, Glossary): Asses-Nole. Asses Head and Neck. Johnson (1755, Noll), quoting this instance: A noddle (OED, Noll 1).

1040 Anon…answered]
Deighton (ed. 1891): A moment later the time comes for him to re-appear on the stage and reply to the speech of him who acted Thisbe. Furness (ed. 1895) believes that this line indicates that the F SD at 927 is misplaced; see n. 900–36.

1040 Anon]
Soon: for explanation see n. 387.

1040 answered]
Abbott §474: At the end of a line ed is often sounded after er, citing this instance.

1041–56 And…Asse]
Roberts (1980, pp. 84–5): The nearly speechless terror of the mechanicals at Bottom’s appearance is…described at some length (in bestial terms of derogation) by Puck [quoting 1041–49, 1053–4]. Only as his concluding lines focus on the reaction of the fairy queen does terror dissolve safely into secure absurdity [quoting 1055–6].

1041 my]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Said contemptuously.

1041 Minnick]
This word, abandoned by all later texts (see textual n.), is defended by Becket (1815, 1:273): I believe minnick, i.e. minikin, will be the right word. It seems to be used for any thing proverbially small or trifling. He afterwards uses minimus, by way of contempt. So also Ebsworth (ed. Q2 1880, p. xxi). Citing Ebsworth, Chambers (ed. 1897): It would be used ironically of Bottom, my dainty fellow. Joseph Wright, The English Dialect Dictionary (1898–1905) gives minnik as a variant form of Minnock: 2. sb. An affected person. Minnock, the reading of Q2, is defended by Johnson (ed. 1765) as apparently a word of contempt. According to Wright, it is equivalent to Mimmock, also written (among other forms) mimick or mimmick: 1. sb. A person with a dainty or fastidious appetite or manner. Ritson (1783, p. 44) conjectured mammock which signifies a huge misshapen thing; and is very properly applyed by a Fairy to a clumsy over-grown clown. Cf. Wright, Mammock, 3. A scarecrow;…an untidily or absurdly dressed person.
Most editors follow F’s mimmick. Steevens (ed. 1778): Perhaps for mimick. Malone (1780, 1:118): I.e. my actor. See OED (Mimic, n. 1): A mime, burlesque actor (obs.); OED gives this instance, misleadingly dated 1590, as the earliest usage of n., a., or v. Brooks (ed. 1979): Minnik is a minim-misreading. Carroll (1985, p. 151): It is interesting that Puck refers to Bottom throughout this description only as Pyramus, and at one point as my mimic [1041], as if the mimetic transformation had indeed succeeded. Holland (ed. 1994): Actor (the common sense of impersonator is here only in so far as Bottom is such a bad actor as to be a kind of parody). Knowles (privately) points out that Sh. uses minikin only once (Lr. 3.6.43 [Q 1608]), and mimic or mime never.

1042–6 As…fly]
These similes are much praised. Gildon (1710, 7:317) calls them very apt. Pope (ed. 1725, 6:4N1r) includes the passage in his INDEX of some Similies, listing it as A Crowd dispers’d, to wild Geese. The lines are singled out by Moorman (1905, p. 226) and Raleigh (1907, p. 33) for special attention. Welsford (1927, p. 325) quotes them in her consideration of the scenic element in the play. Spurgeon (1935, p. 262) admires them. Halliday (1954, p. 96): The bird image in which the unromantic Puck so vigorously describes the flight of the rude mechanicals…is spontaneous, the half-forgotten memories of these sights and sounds observed in the fields about Stratford leaping into full consciousness as the perfect equivalent for the scattering and shrieking Athenians. See also Young (1966, p. 79), and many others.

1042–5 As…sky]
Leoni (ed. 1818) compares Dante, Purgatorio, 2.124–9 (tr. Dorothy Sayers, 1955): Like as, when pigeons gather on the tilth To make a goodly feast of wheat or tares,…if aught appears To scare them, off they go and quit their feeding, Driven by pressure of more urgent cares.
Harting (1871; 1965, pp. 245–6) mentions several descriptions of geese in Sh’s plays, adding to this one 2H4 5.1.67 (2856), 5.1.70–1 (2859–60), where foolish men flocke together in consent, like so many Wilde-Geese; Cor. 1.4.34–6 (529–31) where Martius addresses his fleeing troops as soules of Geese; and Lr. 2.4.46–7 (1322), where the fool refers to wil’d Geese in flight.

1042 geese]
Johnson (1755, Goose 1): A large waterfowl proverbially noted, I know not why, for foolishness (OED 1a, 1e). Webb (1996, p. 75) adds: A type of…cowardice.

1042 creeping]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Sc. in order to snare them.

1042 Fouler]
Johnson (1755, Fowler): A sportsman who pursues birds (OED, Fowler 1). Neil (ed. 1878) cites Prov. 6:5; Ps. 91:3. Elton (1904, p. 151) quotes J[ohn] S[mith]’s The experienc’d fowler (1697) which at pp. 42–51 describes fowling with a gun. See also G. Markham’s Hungers preuention: or, The whole arte of fowling (1621) especially Ch. VII, Of the Fowling-piece which advises that the best such gun is of the longest barrell, as fiue foote and a halfe, or sixe foote, and warns that to get close to nervous birds, you may have to creepe vpon your hands and knees vnder the bankes.

1043 russet pated choughes]
Brooks (ed. 1979): Drab-headed jackdaws. There has been debate over the color and bird intended. OED gives for the adj. russet Of a reddish-brown colour, but for the primary meaning of the n. A coarse homespun woollen cloth of a reddish-brown, grey or neutral colour; for chough 1. A bird of the crow family, formerly applied somewhat widely to all the smaller chattering species, but especially to the common Jackdaw. 2. Now restricted to the Red-legged Crow,…distinguished as the Cornish chough. Taking russet to mean red, 19th-c. commentators debated the bird intended, since some choughs have red legs and bills, but not red heads. Bennett’s (1832–4, p. 496) proposal to emend to the invented word russet-patted, for red-legged won brief support, but most follow Harting (1871; 1965, pp. 118–19) who quotes Roderic O’Flaherty, A…description of…Connaught [written in 1684, ed. from MS by John Hardiman, 1846], p. 13, who lists choughs, rooks, Cornish choughs, with red legs and bills, to show that there were distinct kinds of choughs, and that the first-mentioned choughs were in all probability jackdaws. Harting concludes that the jackdaw, though having a grey head, would more appropriately bear the designation of russet-pated than any of his congeners. For details of the debate see Furness (ed. 1895); on the color cf. Ham. 1.1.166 (165) and the commentary in Hamlet Works.
Strix (1953, p. 655) refers to OED, Russet [adj. 4. Rustic, homely, simple, quoting LLL 5.2.413 (2345)] and suggests, far-fetchedly (frivolously?), russet-pated…means simple-minded; he also notes that Chuff, sometimes spelled Chough, means rustic, boor, clown, churl, according to OED [Chuff, n.1 1], and choughs here means the variegated congregation of shorebirds whose reaction to the gun’s report is not (like that of geese or jackdaws) to clear off as quickly and unobtrusively as possible but does correspond to the distracted fear of the boorish mechanicals. Replying, Willink (1953, p. 697) suggests two distinct pictures. The wild geese scatter when they eye the fowler, stalking them with his net, not at the gun’s report.…In picture No. 2 it is sound, not sight, that gives the alarm. Rossiter (1953, pp. 726–7) magisterially (though not, as he points out, professorially) shoots down Strix’s claims.

1043 in sort]
Thirlby (MS 1747–53): [in a] Company (OED, n.2 17b, quoting this instance last). Rann (ed. 1787): Flight, flock. Brooks (ed. 1979): Not many different kinds but a numerous flock.

1044 gunnes]
Douce (1807, 2:287) notes the anachronism of guns at Athens, in the time of Theseus. Asimov (1970, p. 39) finds this a particularly amusing anachronism.

1045–6 Seuer…fly]
Cf. Tit. 5.3.68–9 (2572–3): Romans By vprores seuerd as a flight of fowle, Scatterd by winds and high tempestuous gusts.

1045 Seuer…sky]
For Capell’s (1783, 2.3:108) praise of this line see n. 1123.

1045 Seuer]
OED (v. 5b), quoting this instance: To break up, scatter, disperse.…Also refl.

1045 madly…sky]
Deighton (ed. 1891): In wildest terror dash hither and thither across the sky.

1046 at his sight]
The objective genitive: at the sight of him.

1047 our stampe]
OED (n.3 1a, quoting this instance first: A forcible downward blow with the foot. Johnson (ed. 1765) objects: Fairies are [not] of a size that should give force to a stamp. Steevens (ed. 1778): The stamp of a fairy might be efficacious though not loud; neither is it necessary to suppose, when supernatural beings are spoken of, that the size of the agent determines the force of the action. He quotes Olaus Magnus, and 1603–4 where Oberon proposes to Titania that they rocke the ground. Ritson (1783, p. 45) quotes Scot’s Discoverie of Witchcraft (1584) where Robin says, Hemton, hamten, here will I neuer more tread nor stampen (see ). Irving & Marshall (ed. 1888) call attention to the hard stamping of Rumpelstiltskin. Baker (ed. 1896): May be meant to show the utter panic of the rustics, since even the tiny stamping of a fairy can frighten them. Thiselton (1903, p. 46) compares the SD Succu.[bus] Stamps—and Exit from Middleton’s A Mad World, My Masters (4.1.72 [F2v line 14, 1608]), where the object of the stamp was probably to make the ground quake and cleave, so as to yield a passage for the thwarted demon. Puck’s stamp will cause the fugitive to tumble. He adds that just as at his sight [1046] corresponds with that the creeping Fowler eye [1042], so at our stampe corresponds with at the guns report [1044]. Cf. Studley’s translation of Seneca’s Medea (1566; ed. Newton 1581, rpt. 1927, vol. 2, p. 87; 738–9 Latin): Shee chauntes on those the magicke verse, that workes no lesser harme, With bustling frantickely shee stampes, and ceaseth not to charme.
Johnson (ed. 1765) suggests the clowns were falling over a stump; in his Appendix (8:2H7) he quotes Steevens’s demurral: I apprehend the stamp of a fairy’s foot might operate to the full as strongly on this occasion, as the stump of a tree. Moberly (ed. 1881): Stamp earlier meant to print the ground (stempeln) and thus merely to step [unattested by OED, etc.]. Johnson (ed. 1894): Our refers to Puck and Bottom, who entered together. [917]…Stamp, stepping, i.e., approach. Allen (MS in Furness ed. 1895): At one stamp,—as we might say: at one bound, at one rush. Sprague (ed. 1896): Our may be for my, meaning the stamp of which we spirits are capable.…Stamp is perhaps the sound of the feet (of the animals in [923–4])…in pursuit.—[Possibly stamp means] impression, print, or picture. The patches were frightened first at the semblance (stamp) of an ass-head on a human form [917–19], and next at the image (stamp) assumed by Puck of horse, hound, hog, headless bear, or fire [923–6]. Dey (1901, p. 481): Stamp refers to…the form…placed upon Bottom. Puck was the active agent in translating Bottom, but the our may refer to the joint conspiracy [of Puck and Oberon] to use the love-juice—our stamp, a fairy creation.

1048 He]
Thirlby (MS 1725–33) compares the Latin use of Hic—ille [ = this one—that one], implying that he means this one. Wright (ed. 1877): Used indefinitely for one, comparing Son. 29.6 and MV 4.1.54–5 (1959–60). Rolfe (ed. 1877): This one, referring to Abbott §217, who quotes this instance. Deighton (ed. 1891): Another.

1048 murther]
OED (Murder, n. 3): As a cry or exclamation uttered by one who thinks or pretends to think himself or some one else in danger of murder. The Sokols (2000, p. 234), citing this instance and Shr. 5.1.59 (2435–6): Many in Shakespeare’s plays cry murder when fearful of their safety.
On the spelling Schmidt (1875, Murder or Murther): The two forms [are] used indiscriminately. Cf. also 1079, 1080, 1081, 1083.

1048 cals]
Evans (ed. 1974): Calls for.

1049–50 Their…wrong]
Delius (ed. 1859): Both Their sense and lost with their feares are the subjects of made; i.e. their strong fear and their already weak minds made them believe that senseless things could cause them harm. Weak and strong stand in contrast, as do sense and senseless (Ger.). Deighton (ed. 1891): Their senses being thus weakened and bewildered by overpowering fear, even inanimate objects find courage to plague them. Baker (ed. 1896): That is, they are so panic-stricken that even senseless things seem to them to endeavor to hurt them.

1049 Their…strong]
Halliwell (ed. 1856): Fear is nothing else but a betraying of the succours which reason offereth, Wisdom of Solomon, xvii [:12].

1049 sense]
Perception or mental faculties (OED 6b, 10b).

1049 thus weake]
Johnson (ed. 1894): By these events made weak.

1049 lost with]
Johnson (ed. 1894): Driven away by. Gill (ed. 1981): Conquered by.

1051–2 For…catch]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Some of the briers and thorns strip them of their sleeves; some strip them of their hats; some strip of every article of dress those who are so ready to yield them. Thiselton (1903, p. 46): Some some and all [are] the subjects, while sleeves hats and things [are] the corresponding objects of catch. Gordon (ed. 1910): Let us take briers and thorns of [1051] to be the subject of catch, and the meaning is that while they catch some sleeves and some hats from the others, from those who give way (yielders) they catch everything. Those who give way to the briers, instead of bursting through them, only become more completely entangled. Kittredge (ed. 1939): This seems to be a general observation: Everybody and everything is ready to despoil the vanquished. Foakes (ed. 1984): Everything snatches at those who give way to fear. Knowles (privately) compares Tilley B673 To be in the Briers and C189 Catch as catch may.

1051 briers]
See n. 376.

1052 yeelders]
OED (n. 3), quoting this instance first: One who gives something up, or gives in; a surrenderer. Rolfe (ed. 1877) compares 1H4 5.3.11 (2902): I was not born a yielder. Greenblatt (ed. 1997): The timid.

1053 I…feare]
Deighton (ed. 1891): They being in this state of distraction, I led them hither and thither in all directions.

1053 distracted]
Schmidt (1874, Distract, vb. 2c): Mad, out of one’s senses.

1054 sweete Pyramus]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Said ironically, that Bottom of whose good looks they were so proud, citing 348.

1054 sweete]
Chambers (ed. 1897): Often used contemptuously by Shakespeare. Scott (ed. 1898): Used here with playful irony.

1054 translated]
See n. 935–6. See also n. 203.

1055 so]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Here used almost as a correlative to when.

1055 it…passe]
Johnson (1755, Come 49): To come to pass. To be effected. To fall out. Deighton (ed. 1891): Happened (OED, Come, v. 22a). For a similar construction see 1594.

1056 Asse]
Stratton (ed. 1920): In some productions Puck is acted as so delighted at all this confusion that he rolls upon the ground in glee.

1057 better…deuise]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Even better than I hoped when I planned my stratagem.

1058 latcht]
OED (Leach, v.2): Also…latch, letch. <Prob. repr. OE. lęccan to water…>. 1. trans., quoting this instance: To water, wet. (In the Shakes. quot. the vb. may possibly belong to latch v.2, in the transferred sense to fasten.) Hanmer (ed. 1744, 6:Glossary) explains that his emendation lech’d (see textual n.) means to lick over. Fr. Lêcher; many editors adopt the explanation but not the textual change. Thirlby (MS 1733–47): [John] Ray’s Coll[ection] of [English] words [1674] 40 [= p. 26]: Latching, catching, infecting (OED, Latch, v.1 4). Johnson (1755, 2), quoting this instance: To fasten [OED, Latch, v.2]; to close, perhaps in this place: unless it rather signifies to wash from lather. Harness (ed. 1825; 1830): Entrapped.—Nares’ Glossary [1822]. Halliwell (ed. 1856) echoes Thirlby (MS 1733–47), and is the first to allude to Mac. 4.3.195 (2037). Hunter (ed. 1870): To ensnare or beguile. Ital. allacciare. Wright (ed. 1877): Caught and held fast as by a charm or spell, but may be related to latch-pan…a dripping-pan, which catches the dripping from the meat (the second definition is in OED, Latch, v.1 3). Neil (1878): Closed up the power of proper vision in. White (ed. 1883) seizing on the hint of dripping in Wright: dropped (the juice).

1060 tooke]
Johnson (1755, Take 9): To surprize; to catch. Schmidt (Take, 1875) adds: To come upon unexpectedly (OED, v. 8b). Deighton (ed. 1891) refers to Ham. 3.3.80 (2356).

1062 That…ey’d]
Delius (ed. 1859): So that upon awakening she had to be the first thing that struck his eye (Ger.).

1062 of force]
Johnson (1755, 6): [Of] necessity (OED, Force, n.1 19a). Mowat & Werstine (ed. 1993): Inevitably. Abbott §168: Of, meaning from, passes naturally into the meaning resulting from, as a consequence of, quoting MV 4.1.421 (2342), of force. See Franz §518.

1064 Stand close]
Schmidt (1874, Close, adv. 2): So as not to stir, still. Wright (ed. 1877): So as to be unobserved, comparing the use of close at 1029, and Ado 3.3.103 (1431): Stand thee close. Kittredge (ed. 1939): In concealment. OED (Close, adv. 1): Very near. Esp. with stand, sit. For the uninflected adverb see Franz §241. Mowat & Werstine (ed. 1993): An order to step aside into hiding. Dessen and Thomson (1999, p. 213, p. 51): stand…surreptitious figures also stand close; close…close as near…is sometimes hard to distinguish from secret, as in such locutions as stand close. Cf. Lyly, Mother Bombie 1.3.63–4 (3:179), let vs stand close and heare all,…<They stand aside.> Cf. 1140 (Stand aside).

1066, 1068 you…thee]
Furness (ed. 1895): Demetrius uses the respectful you, while Hermia replies with the contemptuous thou. Brooks (ed. 1979) applies this remark to the entire scene (1066–1104), observing that Hermia’s tone changes momentarily in [1086]. Foakes (ed. 1984): You seems to reflect rather Demetrius’s tone of adoration, thou Hermia’s familiar treatment of him, noting that Hermia also used thou in speaking to Lysander at [179 ff.]. Stansbury (1982, pp. 60–1) examines ways in which usage of the second person singular pronoun exhibits relationships, especially among the young lovers. Freedman (2007, pp. 61–73) provides a further analysis, both expanding and adjusting Stansbury’s opinions. See nn. 36, 1147–1323.

1067 Lay…foe]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Keep such bitter words for one who deserves them.

1067 Lay]
Schmidt (1874, 6): Followed by prepositions;…to l. upon or on = To bestow,…to inflict (OED, v.1 31). Gill (ed. 1981): Use.

1067 breath]
Schmidt (1874, 6): Words, language (OED 9a). Kittredge (ed. 1939) compares Ham. 3.4.197 (2573).

1068 Now…chide]
Deighton (ed. 1891): So far I only use reproach.

1068 should]
On should used for ought to Rolfe (ed. 1877) refers to Abbott §323. See also Blake 4.3.7.8 (b), quoting 621.

1068 use thee worse]
Treat thee more harshly (OED, Use, v. 18 a.; and, quoting this use, Worse, adv. 1b).

1069 giuen]
Monosyllabic or elided: see n. 36.

1071–2 Being…to]
Delius (ed. 1859): Since you’re so deep in blood that it’s running into your shoes, dive into it completely, and kill me as you have Lysander (Ger.). Deighton (ed. 1891): Having gone so far in guilt, go further still; make your guilt complete. Thiselton (1903, p. 47): Having initiated a bloody career by killing a sleeping man, you will surely not shrink from crowning it by a fitting sequel, such as the murder of me a defenceless woman will be; ’twill be a braver deed for I am wide awake.
Rowe (ed. 1714) places the last four words in a separate verse line and is followed by most editors (see textual n.). Abbott §511 cites the short line thus obtained under the heading Single lines with two or three accents are frequently interspersed amid the ordinary verses of five accents. Schmidt (1881, p. 5) conjectures that something is omitted here. Furness (ed. 1895) disagrees, suggesting some dramatic action, such as offering her breast to him to strike, completed the line. Thiselton (1903, p. 47) believes that 1071–2 was delivered as one line, the words and kill me too being added with an impetuosity with which the attribution of them to another line is hardly consonant. The arrangement, then, is elocutionary. Cuningham (ed. 1905) conjectures nor leave me here to weep as an addition forming a triple rhyme with sleepe and deepe; he notes other triplets at 1184–6, 1191–3.

1071 ore shooes]
Thirlby (MS 1747–53): Over shoes over boots. Johnson (ed. 1765): An allusion to the Proverb. See Tilley (S379); and (S380), quoting this instance. Rann (ed. 1787): So far immersed (OED, Shoe, n. 2e, citing this instance). Steevens (ed. 1793) compares Mac. 3.4.135–6 (1419–20). Delius (ed. 1859) compares TGV 1.1.24 (27): ouer-shooes in loue. Walker (1860, 3:49) quotes Thomas Heywood, A Woman Killed with Kindness (1607), Once ore shooes, we are strait ore head in sinne [F2v; 4.2.112]. Wright (ed. 1877): Moderately deep. Verity (ed. 1893): = somewhat.

1071 the deepe]
The emendation knee-deep, a conjecture by Thirlby (MS 1747–53) and also Maginn (1837, pp. 378–9), but attributed to Coleridge by Walker (1860, 3:49), gained some favor (see textual n.). There are parallels, e.g. Peele, The Battle of Alcazar 4.2.1081 (2:335), Knee deepe in bloud; also the ballad Thomas Rymer and Queen of Elfland (Child’s Ballads, No. 37 A, ed. Sargent and Kittredge, n.d., p. 64): For forty days and forty nights He wade thro red blude to the knee. Cuningham (ed. 1905) argues that plunge is used in the general sense of a further advance in the tide of blood on the part of Demetrius; and besides the expression the deep, i.e. of blood, as a metaphor, seems highly overstrained. Objecting, Bullen (ed. 1905) compares Shr. 1.1.23 (322), plunge him in the deepe. Cowling (ed. 1922): Into the depth (of infamy). Kittredge (ed. 1939): The depths of bloodguiltiness.

1073 The…day]
Wright (ed. 1877) compares Tro. 3.2.177–8 (1810–11), As true…As Sunne to day.

1073 so…day]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Sc. in regulating the time. Johnson (ed. 1894): I.e. faithful in appearing every day.

1074 stollen]
Monosyllabic or elided: see n. 392.

1075 Frow]
OED (Fro, A. prep. 1) gives the variant form frow and (1): = from in all its senses.

1075–8 Ile…Antipodes]
Explication of these lines sometimes reflects ambiguity over whether Antipodes refers to people or to places on the opposite side of the world (OED, n. pl. 1, 3); see also textual nn. Furnivall (ed. 1908, p. viii): The funny notion of the earth being bored, and the moon creeping thru it to disturb folk in the Antipodes, may have been taken from a passage and woodcut in Caxton’s Myrrour of the World [1481], in which stones dropt from either pole of the world would meet in the middle of it (ed. Oliver H. Prior, EETS, 1913 [for 1912], p. 56). Caxton does not here use the term antipodes but he speaks of a man going half way round the earth, where he shold holde his feet ayenst oures (p. 52). Deighton (ed. 1891): By her sudden presence annoy her brother then holding noontide with the Antipodes, then at his zenith in the Antipodes. Walter (ed. 1964): This image extends the thought expressed in [1073]. It also reflects the theme of disorder through female dominance. Wells (ed. 1967): Hermia’s notion is that the moon, creeping through the earth, will displease (by bringing night with it) the noontide that the sun is experiencing among those who live on the other side of the world. The conceit is strained, no doubt for comic effect; but it has its place among the play’s other images of cosmic disorder, such as those in Titania’s speech [456–92]. McDonald (ed. 2000): I.e., it’s impossible: I could as easily believe that the solid (whole) earth could be bored through, and that the moon (a metonymy for night) could pass through the opening, disrupting the sunshine (Her brother’s noontide) and annoying those on the opposite side (th’antipodes). Cf. Lucan, Pharsalia 1.77–9 for the germ of the image.

1076 whole]
Johnson (1755, 2): Unimpaired (OED, a. 2a). Schmidt (1875, 3): Entire. Wright (ed. 1877): Solid, quoting Mac. 3.4.21 (1281). Brooks (ed. 1979): Impenetrable.

1076 bor’d]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Pierced right through from surface to surface.

1077 Center]
Verity (ed. 1893): The middle point of the earth, comparing Jonson, Catiline (1611) 5.627–8 (5:547). strike it through The center, to the Antipodes. Cuningham (ed. 1905) compares Ham. 2.2.159 (1190). Cf. Tro. 3.2.177, 179 (1810, 1812): As true as…Earth to th’Center.

1077 displease]
Capell (1783, 2.3:108): [B]y usurping on his [the sun’s] province. Caldecott (MS 1813–33): I.e. offend by interfering with. Walter (ed. 1964): Upset. Perhaps a quibble on displace. Is this another hint of female unruliness? For suggested alternative verbs, see textual n. and .

1078 Her brothers]
Warburton (ed. 1747): The sun’s. Root (1903, p. 52) cites Ovid, Met. 2.454, fraternis…flammis, translated by Golding (2.565) as her brother’s flames, where the antecedent for her is moon (2.564).

1078 noonetide]
Capell (1783, 2.3:108): [M]eridian height. Deighton (ed. 1891): Zenith.

1079, 1080, 1081, 1083 murdred, murtherer, murthered]
See n. 1048.

1080 So…looke]
Seymour (1805, p. 46) compares Mac. 1.2.46–7 (70), So should he looke, that seemes to speake things strange.

1080 dead]
Pope’s (ed. 1725) emendation dread attracts a few followers (see textual n.) but most agree with Steevens (ed. 1773): Dead seems to be the right word, comparing [2H4 1.1.71 (130)], so dead in looke. However, interpretation varies. Johnson (1755, 8): Dull. Capell (1783, 2.3:108): Pale (OED, a. 13a). Schmidt (1874, 3): Bringing death, deadly (OED, a. 9). Wright (ed. 1877): Death-like (OED, a. 2b). Castelain (ed. 1943): Livid.

1080 grimme]
Johnson (1755): Having a countenance of terror; horrible; hideous; frightful (OED 4). Schmidt (1874, 1): Of a threatening aspect.

1081 murthered]
On F1’s reading murderer (see textual n.) Collier (ed. 1842): The folio…is clearly wrong. Demetrius means that he looks more like a murdered man than a murderer. Thiselton (1903, p. 47), supporting F1: Why should not Demetrius mean: So should you the murderer look, and so also should I, the murdered. I look as I should look; you, on the other hand, look not as you should look, but far otherwise?

1082 Pearst…heart]
Scott (ed. 1898): A bold metaphor, comparing Luke 2:35: A sword shall pierce through thine own soul. Brooks (ed. 1979) compares Chaucer, KnT 1079 (see ).

1082 the]
Abbott §92: The is seldom used…for the possessive adjective. See Franz §263. See 1534.

1083 you…looke]
The folios take murtherer as the subject instead of you and change the verb to looks (see textual n.). See Franz §676.

1083 cleere]
OED (Clear, a. 2d. fig.), quoting this instance: Serene, cheerful; of unclouded countenance or spirit. Evans (ed. 1974): Shining.

1084 yonder…spheare]
The Clarkes (1879, pp. 80–1), quoting this instance together with 1215, 1421, and 1511, call attention to Sh.’s methods of denoting place and scene without modern scenic aids,…by poetically picturesque touches, marking the actual presence of the surroundings.

1084 Venus]
Rolfe (ed. 1877): The planet is again alluded to [at 1130].

1084 glimmering]
Shining faintly. On the ambiguity of this word see n. 452. Wright (ed. 1877): The epithet glimmering, or faintly shining, seems in contradiction to bright and clear of 1083. Thiselton (1903, pp. 47–8): The brightness of the Planet to the human eye faintly illuminates the neighbouring portion of the sphere that carries it along with it. It is the sphere that is glimmering.

1084 spheare]
See nn. 378, 529.

1085 Whats…Lysander]
Delius (ed. 1859): What has this to do with my Lysander? (Ger.). See Franz §530.

1087 I had]
Pronounced I’d. Some earlier texts emend to Ide (see textual n.) but unnecessarily as the metre obviously requires the elision.

1087 giue…hounds]
Walter (ed. 1964): The word [carcasse] deepens Hermia’s fears, and she jumps to a false conclusion. Hyland (ed. 1989): This…harsh comment…prompts Hermia to abuse him. On stage, she may well be directed to attack him. The disposal of the corpse may have its origin in Creon’s treatment of the bodies of the grieving widows’ husbands in Chaucer, KnT 941–7; or by this from MerT 1438, Yet were me levere houndes had me eten…. See , . Cf. JC 2.1.174 (807), hew him as a Carkasse fit for Hounds.

1088 Out…out]
Johnson (1755, interject.): An expression of abhorrence or expulsion (OED, Out, int. 2, and Out adv. 13a where, with ellipsis of intr. vb. (go, come, etc.) the word functions as a verb). For a similar use see 1294–5.

1088 curre]
OED (n. 1b fig.), quoting this instance first: As a term of contempt: a surly, ill-bred, low, or cowardly fellow.

1088–9 thou…then?]
Deighton (ed. 1891): You enrage me beyond what it is possible for a maiden to endure with calmness. Furness (ed. 1895) suggests altering the punctuation of the final two words to him. Then as more suitable to the wildness of Hermia’s grief.

1090 be…men]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Be accounted a devil rather than a man! Baker (ed. 1896): But, for your cruelty, be regarded as something not human. Watt (ed. 1906): A reference to the curse upon Cain [Gen. 4:12]. Walter (ed. 1964): I.e. you are now a beast—a cur.

1091 once]
OED (adv. 3): Emphatically. Once for all. Wright (ed. 1877): For once.

1091 tell true:]
Collier (ed. 1842): The emphatic repetition of tell true, is not in the folio, and the measure therefore defective. Only Thiselton (1903, p. 48) defends Q2F1’s reading.

1091 euen]
Monosyllabic or elided: see n. 581.

1092–3 Durst…sleeping]
Malone (ed. 1790): Hast thou kill’d him sleeping, whom, when awake, thou didst not dare to look upon?

1093–5 And…it]
Wells (ed. 1967): This recalls Hermia’s dream that she herself was attacked by a serpent [800–5]. Holland (ed. 1994, p. 14) goes further: Hermia turns him [Demetrius] into a serpent.…[S]he displaces the fear of Lysander [as in her dream at 803–4] into fear for Lysander and fear of Demetrius.

1093 brave tutch]
Johnson (ed. 1765): A noble stroke, un grand coup. Halliwell (ed. 1856) quotes Palsgrave [1530, Touche], A crafty dede. Wright (ed. 1877): Fine stroke, heroic exploit. Kittredge (ed. 1939): Brave…here mean[s]…Splendid—ironically.…The whole phrase means a fine specimen of character and conduct.

1093 tutch]
Grinfield (MS 1834–63, p. 286), dubiously: Is it not used for fellow? We used to say at college, a rum touch, for a poor dull fellow. See OED, n. †27, but the earliest instance is dated 1804. Hunter (ed. 1870): Feeling. Schmidt (1875, 5): = test or proof of bravery. Scott (ed. 1898): Achievement. Less probably, Tiessen (1877, p. 5): A sword thrust or stab (Ger.: ein Hieb oder Stich); Craig (ed. 1903): Wound. See 1182. See also 1320.

1094 worme]
Johnson (1755, 2): A poisonous serpent (OED, n. 1).

1095–6 An…stung]
Thirlby (MS 1733–47): Does an adder sting with his tongue? Writing of the Adder, Edward Topsell, The Historie of Serpents (1608, p. 52): They are a craftie & subtill venomous beast, biting suddenly them that passe by them.…When she hath bitten, with her forked or twisted tongue shee infuseth her poyson.

1095 doubler tongue]
Seymour (1805, p. 46): More forked, I suppose, and so more venomous. Delius (ed. 1859) explains that double tongue means both forked tongue, as the snake has, comparing 660, and two-facedness such as Demetrius shows (Ger.). Eaton (1860, p. 136) compares Ps. 140:3: They have sharpened their tongues like a serpent; adders’ poison is under their lips. Andrews (ed. 1989) alludes to the forked tongue of the duplicitous serpent in Eden (Gen. 3:1–15). Webb (1996, p. 6) cites R2 3.2.20–2 (1380–2): a lurking adder, Whose double tongue may with a mortal touch Throw death.

1096 serpent]
OED (n. 3b fig.), quoting this instance: A treacherous, deceitful, or malicious person.

1097 You…mood]
Caldecott (MS 1813–33): You throw away your rage on a misconceived ground of resentment, in mistaken anger. The Clarkes (ed. 1864): Demetrius means that Hermia vents her passion in a resentment that is mistaken in its ground of reproach.

1097 passion]
OED (6d): A passionate speech or outburst; (7b): Impassioned anger, angry feeling. Deighton (ed. 1891): Used by Shakespeare of any strong emotion. See also 1866, 2086, 2109 and nn.

1097 on…mood]
Rann (ed. 1787): —Erroneously, you mistake its object. Steevens (ed. 1793): In a mistaken manner. Delius (ed. 1859): On the basis of a wrong absurd mood (or humour) (Ger.). Kittredge (ed. 1939): Mistaken, since I am innocent of the charge. Foakes (ed. 1984): on me, misunderstanding my disposition.

1097 on]
Steevens (ed. 1793) conjectures in, remarking that on, is licentiously used by ancient authors, instancing Ant. 3.11.39 (2064). Collier alone adopts the reading, in his editions of 1853 and 1875. See textual n. See Abbott §180, quoting Ham. 3.1.43 (1694).

1097 mood]
Johnson (1755, 4): Temper of mind; state of mind as affected by any passion; disposition (OED, n.1 3a). Malone (ed. 1790): Anger, or perhaps…capricious fancy. Steevens (ed. 1793): Manner. Hunter (ed. 1870): Imagination. Hudson (ed. 1880): Mood and mode…were sometimes used interchangeably.

1098 bloode]
Johnson (1755, 7): Murder (OED, n. 3a). See also n. 77.

1099 for…tell]
Deighton (ed. 1891): So far as I know. For the construction, cf. 142.

1101 And if]
For the construction see n. 808.

1101 therefore]
Wright (ed. 1877): For that, thereby quoting Tro. 3.3.20 (1869). Deighton (ed. 1891): As a return for telling you.

1102 priuiledge]
Page (ed. 1897): Right or immunity.…Here used ironically. Cf. 49, 599.

1103–4 And…no]
Pope (ed. 1725) corrects the faulty lineation of Q1: see textual n.

1103 part I]
Capell (1783, 2.3:108): [S]o (a word of…[Pope’s] insertion at [1103] is necessary for the rime; and part I so means—I part as I would that you too should part from me, i.e. for ever. Wilson (ed. 1924): Crowding in the MS…and the similarity of so and see would account for the omission [of so]. Andrews (ed. 1993) defends his pre-Popean reading arguing that a dramatic departure from the expected norm [of rhyming couplets] would reinforce the breach Hermia depicts.

1104 Whether]
Delius (ed. 1859): Monosyllabic. See n. 78.

1105 no]
Johnson (ed. 1894): No use in.

1105 in…vaine]
Deighton (ed. 1891): While she is in this angry mood.

1105 vaine]
Johnson (1755, Vein 6): Humour; temper. OED (Vein, n. 14b): A temporary state of mind or feeling; a humour or mood. For a somewhat different use of the word see n. 302.

1107–10 So…stay]
Proverbial; Tilley (S662), quoting both 1107–10 and 1483–4: When Sorrow is asleep wake it not. The Clarkes (ed. 1864): There is a drowsy beauty about these lines, as the foiled lover lays him down on the ground and yields to slumber, that is in fine poetic keeping with the dreaminess shed over the scenes in this Midsummer Night play. The way in which the human wanderers drop asleep, and seek rest no less for their dazed thoughts than for their tired limbs, is even extended to the fairy folk, where Titania, with a kind of irrelevance, says—The moon, methinks, looks with a watery eye; [1017] as if the fairy queen herself were but half awake, and saw all things through a misty halo of dubious light. Thus does Shakespeare’s dramatic art make his productions harmonise in their every portion, the one with the other. Neil (ed. 1878) compares Tmp. 2.1.189–96 (866–74) where, at 867 and 873, Sh. plays on heauy as meaning drowsy, observing at 874 that sleep sildome visits sorrow, when it doth, it is a Comforter. Deighton (ed. 1891): As I am, in my present condition, the grief with which I am burdened becomes more burdensome in consequence of the debt that sleep owes to sorrow not being paid. Verity (ed. 1893): In plain prose—loss of sleep (in which sorrow may be forgotten—cf. [1483]) makes sorrow harder to bear. Kittredge (ed. 1939): Sleep owes each man a certain amount of repose, but, when a man is sorrowful, Sleep is like a bankrupt and cannot pay. On account of this unpaid debt (this wakefulness), the heaviness (of spirit) which sorrow feels grows heavier (with drowsiness). Farrow (ed. 1972): A complicated way of saying sorrow grows worse for lack of sleep.…Demetrius imagines sleep owes sorrow money, but is bankrupt (unable to pay) while he stays awake. But sleep will pay a little (in some slight measure) if he lies down to wait for its offer (If…stay). Andrews (ed. 1989): In other words, because of Demetrius’s pining for Hermia, he has gone too long without sleep and is now preparing to lie down and rest.
Irving & Marshall (ed. 1888, at 1110): It must be confessed there is an incongruous, prosaic and legal character about these lines which smells of an attorney’s office. Furness (ed. 1895, at 1110): The fondness of Shakespeare for similes drawn from bankruptcy…may be learned from Mrs Cowden-Clarke’s, and Mrs Furness’s Concordances, under the word bankrout. Cuningham (ed. 1905) adds: The explanation may lie in Shakespeare’s keen recollection of the paternal misfortunes and legal embarrassments before his departure from Stratford.

1107 So]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Possibly it is a mistake for since, the so—of sorrow being caught by the transcriber’s eye. Scott (ed. 1898): So may mean I find that. It cannot mean since (Deighton) since it introduces a principal sentence, dependence on the preceding line being prevented both by the full stop and the therefore. Kittredge (ed. 1939): Thus—as I now feel in my own case.

1107 heauinesse]
Caused by depression of spirit (Johnson, 1755, 2) and by extreme drowsiness (OED, Heaviness, e. and d.). Holzknecht & McClure (ed. 1937): Sadness. Farrow (ed. 1972): Sleepiness.

1107 heauier]
Weighed down by both sleepiness and dejection (OED, Heavy, a.1 23 and 28).

1108 slippe]
Thirlby (MS 1725–33) notes that for sleep Q1 and F1 have slip. Not listed in variant spellings by OED, but see Cercignani (1981, p. 150).

1109–10 Which…stay]
Deighton (ed. 1891), comparing Cym. 5.4.18–21 (3053–6): Which debt it will pay in part (as a bankrupt pays so much in the pound) if I wait here to receive its offer of such part. Bevington (ed. 1988): I.e., to a small extent I will be able to pay back and hence find some relief from sorrow, if I pause here awhile (make some stay) while sleep tenders or offers itself by way of paying the debt owed to sorrow.

1109 Which]
Verity (ed. 1893): I.e. the debt. Idem (ed. 1912) adds, i.e. respite from sorrow.

1109 in…measure]
See n. 295.

1109 it]
Verity (ed. 1893): Sleep.

1110 his]
Verity (ed. 1893): Its. See n. 470.

1110 tender]
Johnson (1755, 1): Offer; propose to acceptance (OED, n.2 2). Foakes (ed. 1984): This is the correct legal term for an offer in discharge of a debt (OED, n.2 1b).

1110 make…stay]
Hunter (ed. 1870): Wait a little (OED, Stay, n.3 3).

1110 Ly doune]
Furness (ed. 1895): Another [SD] in the imperative, betraying the stage-house copy. Doran (ed. 1959): Imperative form…common in Elizabethan plays. Brooks (ed. 1979, p. xxvii) cites Greg’s suggestion that the SD may be due to the book-keeper. But Holland (ed. 1994): The source of Q in foul papers would argue against that.

1112 loue iuice]
OED (love, n.1 16a), quoting only this instance in sense (a): A juice which dropped upon the eyes has the effect of a philtre.

1112 true loues]
OED (True-love, 2): A faithful lover; one whose love is pledged.

1113 misprision]
Johnson (1755, 2), quoting this instance: Mistake; misconception. See OED, Misprision1 3. Rann (ed. 1787): Blunder. Neil (ed. 1878) calls attention to the legal sense; cf. OED (1): A wrong action or omission; spec. a misdemeanour or failure of duty on the part of a public official.

1113 perforce]
See n. 396.

1114 Some…true]
Thiselton (1903, p. 49): The true love of Lysander for Hermia is only subject to temporary and purely accidental variation for which he is not responsible; while the false love of Demetrius for Helena…is in the course of the Play permanently converted into true love. Brooks (ed. 1979, p. xlvi) notes the antimetabole, repetition of words in inverted order. Cf. nn. 65–6, 180–3.

1114 loue turnd]
OED (Turn 36): To undergo change, to be fickle or inconstant; cf. also 29b.

1115–16 Then…on oath]
Moberly (ed. 1881): If my best efforts have only turned true love into hatred, there must be a fate in such things which naturally makes most lovers forsworn. Irving & Marshall (ed. 1888): What Puck intends to say is that fate o’er-rules chance here; for the chance is that, for one man true to his oath in love, one finds a million who are false to it. That may possibly be a corruption of the text for some other words; or, perhaps = in that, or seeing that. Deighton (ed. 1891): Puck’s excuse for his carelessness does not seem to be very logical. Possibly the meaning is, Then, if that happens, the fault is fate’s, who so often is too strong for men’s intentions that, for one man who keeps faith, a million, whatever their intentions, give way and break oath after oath, i.e. any number of oaths. Greenblatt (ed. 1997): Among the millions of faithless men, the one true man’s oath has been subverted by fate.
Gervinus (1863, 1:272): Whoever attentively reads [the fairies’] parts will find that nowhere is reflection imparted to them. Only in one exception does Puck make a sententious remark upon the infidelity of man, and whoever has penetrated into the nature of these beings, will immediately feel that it is out of harmony. Furness (ed. 1895) comments: Or, in other words, it does not happen to fadge with the scheme of fairydom which the learned German has evolved; and christened Shakespeare’s. Wilson (ed. 1924), arguing that these lines occur in a passage belonging to a first draft of MND: This sententious comment in Robin’s mouth is strangely out of keeping with Puck’s character.

1115 troth]
OED (n. 1): Faithfulness, good faith, loyalty; cf. also n. 694, 702.

1116 confounding…oath]
Wright (ed. 1877): Breaking one oath after another. For confound in the sense of ruin, destroy, see Lucrece, 1202 (OED 1, Obs. or arch.). Evans (ed. 1974): Invalidating one oath with another. See n. 2093. Brooks (ed. 1979, p. xlvi) gives this phrase and trueth killes truth (1154) as examples of ploce; Sonnino (1968, p. 64) quotes Peacham: A figure which repeateth a word putting but one word in between, or at least very few.…May be used to express any affection, but it is most fit for a sharp invective or exprobation.

1117 About…goe]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Search the wood in every direction.

1117 swifter…winde]
Theobald (1909, p. 200), giving many examples: The swiftness of the wind is a classical commonplace. See Tilley (W411). Cf. Golding’s Ovid, 13.950; see .

1118 Helena]
Blits (2003, p. 111): Oberon somehow knows Helena’s name, even though no one has mentioned it within his hearing.

1118 looke]
See n. 126.

1119 fancy sicke]
Johnson (1755, Fancysick): One whose imagination is unsound: one whose distemper is in his own mind. Ayscough (ed. 1784): Love-sick (OED, Fancy B.2, quoting this instance). Wright (1968, p. 39): In Elizabethan usage fancy has a wide range of meanings…: it would be wrong to take it as a regular synonym for love since it is also used to stress one particular aspect of love, love beglamoured by imagination. See nn. 40, 127, 165.

1119 cheere]
OED (n.1 1), quoting this instance as the only use later than 1483: The face. Obs. Richmond (2002, p. 98): State of mind or disposition (OED, n1 3a). See also nn. 131, 2092.

1120 With…deare]
Steevens (ed. 1793): So, in King Henry VI. we have blood-consuming,—blood-drinking, and blood-sucking sighs [2H6 3.2.61 (1761), 3.2.63 (1763), 3H6 4.4.22 (2326)]. All alluding to the ancient supposition that every sigh was indulged at the expence of a drop of blood. Anderson (1927, p. 48 n. 9): This…notion I have not found in psychological treatises.…It may be a poetical and popular development from the general belief that extreme passion consumes blood. The purpose of sighing is, normally, to ease the heart by cooling and refreshing it; she quotes La Primaudaye, The French Academie, 1618, p. 468: For howsoeuer griefe shutteth vp the heart…yet by groning, sighing and weeping the heart doth in some sort open it selfe, as if it would come foorth to breathe, least being wholly shut vp with sorrow it should be stifled. Cf. Rom. 3.5.59 (2092).

1120 costs]
Abbott §247: The Relative…frequently takes a singular verb, though the antecedent be plural. Blake 6.1.1.6 (c), quoting this instance: As most relative pronouns had no inflections, confusion as to what the subject was could arise. See Franz §675. Cf. 465–6.

1121 illusion]
OED (2a): An act of deceiving the bodily eye by false or unreal appearances; delusion, befooling. Obs. Disyllabic; see n. 19 (companion).

1122 charme]
Caldecott (MS 1813–33): Act upon by incantations or spells. See OED 1a. See also 1599.

1122 against]
See n. 134.

1122 doe]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Subjunctive.

1123 I…how I goe]
Capell (1783, 2.3:108): Passages of inimitable beauty in the descriptive way, the words painting the matter to you even in their sound, occur at [1045] & [1123]; but one is injur’d in some editions by pointing, the other by an insertion. See textual n. Brooks (ed. 1979): Suited to the action and emphasis, the line is lengthened in effect. Empson (1979, p. 6) claims that this is the strongest evidence that Puck really did fly; that is, was jerked off stage by a rope. See also Empson (1994, p. 219–22).

1124 Swifter…bowe]
Douce (in Steevens, ed. 1793) cites Golding’s Ovid 10.686–7; see . Evans (ed. 1974): Proverbial for swiftness. See Tilley (A322): As swift as an arrow. Halliwell (ed. 1856) points to Chaucer’s use in describing marital woes (MerT 1673; see ).

1124 arrowe]
On omission of the article, see n. 196.

1124 Tartars bowe]
Steevens (ed. 1793) quotes Rom. 1.4.5 (460): A Tartars painted Bow of lath.
