Douce (in Steevens, ed. 1793, 14:363): The Tartarian bows…resemble in their form the old Roman or Cupid’s bow, such as we see on medals and bas reliefs. Shakspeare used the epithet to distinguish it from the English bow, whose shape is the segment of a circle. Wright (ed. 1877): The Tartars were famous for their skill in archery, like the ancient Parthians. Lee (ed. 1907) asserts that Sh.’s Tartars is a more accurate rendering of Ovid’s Scythica (Met. 10:588) than Golding’s Turkye (10.687). Walrond (1916, 2:379), quoting this line, explains that bows are made either of wood or composite materials: The latter kind is used by Orientals, and is much the more powerful. Of this fact Shakespeare seems to have been aware. See also 1294.

1125–45 Flower…prepost’rously]
Wilson (ed. 1924), arguing that these lines may have been added in a 1598 revision: Considerations of space may have helped to determine the verse form.

1125–32 dy…remedy]
Crane (1951, p. 74): One rhyme serves for all the lines. See Cercignani (1981, pp. 304–305). Cf. n. 200–1.

1125–6 purple…archery]
See 542–4 and nn.

1127 in apple]
Blake (3.3.4.2.2 (h)): Many expressions which today take the definite article may be without them in ShE, especially when a noun follows a preposition. See also Abbott §89; Franz §267. See also n. 1567.

1127 apple…eye]
Deighton (ed. 1891): The ball of the eye, so called from being round. Brooks (ed. 1979): (1) pupil; (2) seat of what he most cherishes: the idiom still current (OED, Apple, 7a, b). Shaheen (1993, p. 97) cites Deut. 32:10, Prov. 7:2, and other biblical references; by Sh.’s time it had become a common expression. Nonetheless, most people probably became acquainted with [it] from Scripture.…See also Tilley A290; Dent A290.

1128–30 When…sky]
Farrow (ed. 1972): Helena must look like Love herself in Demetrius’ eyes. Holland (1989, p. 20): Demetrius’s emotion changes because his loved object will now appear like Venus. The charm makes his loved one appear glorious; it does not alter the object of his affections. The random effects of the drug on Lysander and Titania have become focussed to make Demetrius more intensely in love with the person with whom he is actually in love. He had turned his affections to Hermia but his true love is Helena; the charm leaves him in an intensified state of his previous affection. A similar argument is advanced by Clayton (1999, pp. 73–4).

1128 When…espy]
Cf. Golding’s Ovid, 4.85; see .

1128 his loue]
Mowat & Werstine (ed. 1993): i.e., Helena.

1128 espy]
OED (2b): Perceive unexpectedly.

1130 Venus…sky]
Deighton (ed. 1891): The bright planet Venus (OED, Venus1 5, quoting this instance).

1132 Begge…remedy]
For the construction, see OED, Beg 2e, quoting this instance only.

1132 remedy]
Kittredge (ed. 1939): I.e., for love’s wound.

1136 mistooke]
Obsolete form of the participle. See OED, Mistake 6b; Abbott §343; Franz §167. See 1388.

1137 louers fee]
Halliwell (ed. 1856): Three kisses were properly a lover’s fee. How manie, saies Batt; why, three, saies Matt, for that’s a mayden’s fee, MS. Ballad, circa 1650. Rolfe (ed. 1877): This may mean a lover’s reward or recompense, in a general sense. Furness (ed. 1895): I doubt if louer’s fee here means an honorarium, but its meaning is rather, estate, right by virtue of his title as lover. Cuningham (ed. 1905) compares Peele’s The Araygnement of Paris: And I will have a lovers fee: they saie, unkist, unkinde (1.5; 3:76). White (1911, p. 83): Demetrius is here placed in the attitude of a lawyer pleading for his fee.

1138 Shall…see]
Whiter (1794, pp. 55–6) compares with AYL 3.4.52 (1760): If you will see a pageant truely plaid. Hunter (Shakespeare, 1962, p. 10): Shakespeare limits our response by showing us the lovers as the mere puppets of the fairies. They act on their emotions, but what is action to them is only an act to those who (invisible themselves) watch, manipulate and comment. Dusinberre (1975, p. 154) quotes Erasmus: Yea, and it passeth, to see what sporte and passetyme the Goddes theim selues haue, at suche Folie of these selie mortall men.…[W]hiche syde of heauen bendeth most towardes the earth, there sitte they, and intentiuely beholde what mortall men dooe: and surely no spectacle can be more pleasaunt vnto theim. Good lorde, what a Theatre is this worlde? how many, and diuers are the pageantes that fooles plaie therin? (The Praise of Folie, ed. 1965, p. 68).

1138 fond pageant]
Rann (ed. 1787): Scene of courtship. Caldecott (MS 1813–33): Their fantastical & silly exhibition or shew. Venezky (1951, p. 196): Shakespeare associates the pageant or triumph with a love which is also artificial and ephemeral. Barber (1959, p. 131): The pageant is fond because the mortals do not realize they are in it, nor that it is sure to come out right, since nature will have its way.

1138 fond]
See n. 647.

1139 Lord…bee]
Bethurum (1945, p. 88) parallels Chaucer, KnT, 1799, 1806–14 (see ). Donaldson (1985, p. 49), citing Bethurum: The two poets agree that lovers are lunatics living in a dream world; but both would probably also agree that the dream world must underlie the real world as the real world underlies that of the dream.

1139 Lord]
The Clarkes (1879, p. 465): Scornfully. See n. 1286.

1139 bee]
For be used for are, see n. 380.

1140 Stand aside]
Thirlby (MS 1747–53) compares 1064, Stand close (see n.). Dessen & Thomson (1999, p. 15) divide the more than 550 examples of aside in SDs into two groups, of which Oberon’s directive corresponds to the first: (1) to stand or move aside so as to observe other figures or carry on some surreptitious activity.

1142–3 one…alone]
Daniel (1870, pp. 16–17) cites this among many other instances in Shakespeare in which the words on, one, alone, none, gone, moan, &c., rhyme with each other. See Cercignani (1981, p. 136).

1143 must needes]
See n. 350.

1143 sport alone]
Collier (ed. 1842) cites Wily Beguiled (1606 [p. 20; C3v]): There one of Robin Good-fellow’s exclamations is, Why, this will be sport alone, meaning most excellent sport. Halliwell (ed. 1856), giving several examples: A vernacular phrase. White (ed. 1858): I.e., sheer sport, only [alone] sport. Kittredge (ed. 1939): Unique sport; pure sport; sport in the highest degree. A common idiom. OED does not give the phrase a separate entry, but provides an amusing example from Holland’s Pliny (1601) under Duckling.

1143 alone]
The Clarkes (ed. 1864): Here used idiomatically, as the French use their word unique; meaning solely excellent, singularly good, matchless. See OED 2a and Etym.; Abbott §18. Gollancz (ed. 1895): I.e. by itself, without anything else; others render alone by above all things, without a parallel.

1144–5 And…prepost’rously]
Theobald (1909, pp. 254–5) compares 1H4 1.2.207 (308): And nothing pleaseth but rare accidents, parallelling Martial, De spectaculis, 14: What marvels do not arise from sudden mishaps.

1145 prepost’rously]
Johnson (1755), quoting this instance: In a wrong situation; absurdly; he defines the adj. as 1. Having that first which ought to be last; wrong; absurd; perverted, and quotes Bacon: Put a case of a land of Amazons, where the whole government, publick and private, is in the hands of women; is not such a preposterous government against the first order of nature, for women to rule over men, and in itself void? (Works, ed. Spedding et al., 1872, rpt. 1968, 7:33). Staunton (ed. 1857): Inversely (OED 1); he refers to his note on Preposterous ass (Shr. 3.1.9 [1304]): Shakespeare uses preposterous closer to its primitive and literal sense of inverted order, ηιστερον προτερον, than is customary now. With us it implies monstrous, absurd, ridiculous, and the like; with him it meant misplaced, out of the natural or reasonable course (cf. OED, Preposterous, a. 1, 2). On the rhetorical figure, cf. Puttenham (1589; 1936, p. 170; see also p. 255): We call it in English prouerbe, the cart before the horse, the Greeks call it Histeron proteron, we name it the Preposterous. The implications of the word and figure are explored by P. Parker (1987, pp. 122–3; 1996, pp. 100–1). Greenblatt (ed. 1997, Glossary): Ass-backward.

1146 Enter…Helena]
Foakes (ed. 1984): Lysander and Helena do not see Oberon and Puck (who are invisible…), or notice the sleeping Demetrius; it adds to the comedy, of course, if he is right under their noses.

1147–1323 Why…game]
Stylistic aspects of this sequence have received attention. On the rhyme, Ness (1941, p. 129) points out that 1147–52, 1153–8 (like 1479, 1491–6) are rhyme-royal stanzas fitting for materis of love.…Shakespeare uses this stanza frequently in his early plays and in Venus and Adonis. Foakes (ed. 1984) notes: The lovers speak in rhyme to [1221], when the mood changes as their quarrel becomes bitter. He ignores the departure from rhyme at 1161 (see n. below), where Lysander’s jibe is immediately and comically contradicted by Demetrius’s waking speech, and the bitterness of Helena’s response to both. See also Freedman at n. 1066, 1088. But Blake (3.3.2.1.1) notes that the plural forms had begun to adopt a singular meaning and usurp many functions of the singular; nevertheless he accepts that there is a balance of social and conversational dictates at work in the variation in usage, and concludes: Each scene has to be judged on its balance of social and personal relationships, and there is no ready yardstick whereby any example can be interpreted. See also Franz §289e. Elam (1984, pp. 31, 82–3) notes the way in which Sh. uses demonstrative words to provide direct commentary on the language of the comedy: Entire scenes or situations…are structured on the propagating force of the textual pointer, as in the pattern of avowal and uncomprehending rebuttal amongst the lovers in MND he quotes 1147; 1155; 1172–3; 1187–8; 1218–24; 1247; 1254–5; 1263; 1267–9; 1275; 1285; 1292–3; 1320–3, italicizing occurrences of these, thus, this, it, such, so, that.

1147 should wooe]
Abbott §328: Should, denoting a statement not made by the speaker. (Compare sollen in German.) Abbott quotes this line together with examples carrying the implication that the statement is false. Furness (ed. 1895): The idea of ought to, equally with sollen, may be imputed to [should] here.

1148 derision]
Disyllabic ending; see n. 159.

1149–53 Looke…true]
Walter (ed. 1964): Perhaps two thoughts underlie this argument (a) the mother’s grief at the pain of birth, (b) the repentance and vows at baptism, both matters of christian faith. See Genesis, iii.16 and St. John, iii. 5, St. Mark, i. 4.

1149–50 Looke…appeares]
Greenblatt (ed. 1997): The fact that I am weeping authenticates my vow’s sincerity.

1149 Looke when]
Eccles (1943, p. 391): Means whenever in the sense every time that, implying repeated action. Brooks (ed. 1979), citing Eccles: All the while.

1149–50 vowes…appeares]
Furness (ed. 1895): Walker [1860, 1:55] thinks that there is here an instinctive striving after a natural arrangement of words inconsistent with modern English grammar; and Abbott §§417, 376, classes vows so born either as a noun absolute or as a participle used with a Nominative Absolute. I cannot but think that both critics, misled by the singular appears, have mistaken the construction. Appears should be, according to modern grammar, in the plural; its subject is vows, it is singular merely by attraction; all truth is the predicate, not the subject. My paraphrase therefore, is: vows, thus born, appear, from their very nativity, to be all pure truth.…It can hardly be supposed that Lysander means to assert that all truth, universal truth, is to be found in such vows. Furness’s paraphrase seems right, but he is probably wrong in reading appears as singular. Blake (6.1.1.7): Early English had several different ways of forming the present plural of verbs, and one of them was the use of <-(e)s>, which still survives in some regional dialects.…[S]ome examples of the plural in <-(e)s> may arise from this source, especially in rhyme, where archaic forms are often exploited by poets. See also Franz §679.

1152 badge of faith]
Steevens (ed. 1793): This is said in allusion to the badges (i.e. family crests) anciently worn on the sleeves of servants and retainers. So, in The Tempest: Mark the badges of these men, and then say if they be true [Tmp. 5.1.267–8 (2262–3)]. Hunter (ed. 1870): Tears, the token of sincerity. Walter (ed. 1964): I.e. (a) his tears, (b) Christian faith.

1152 faith]
OED (10), quoting this instance: Fidelity, loyalty.

1153 You…and more]
Deighton (ed. 1891): You make your cunning more and more conspicuous by the language you use.

1153 aduance]
Schmidt (1874, 1d): To bring to view, to show; cf. also 1b: Used of standards, = to wave. Deighton (ed. 1891): The figure is that of bringing a standard more to the front.

1153 cunning]
See n. 44.

1154 When…fray]
Delius (ed. 1859): If one faithful vow counteracts or annihilates the other, the contest is devilish in its end, even if conducted by pious people (Ger.). Wright (ed. 1877): If Lysander’s present protestations are true they destroy the truth of his former vows to Hermia, and the contest between these two truths, which in themselves are holy, must in the issue be devilish and end in the destruction of both. Hassel (2005, p. 364–5) quotes this line among examples of Sh.’s religious usage, associating truth with God and opposing it to the devil.

1154 diuelish holy]
Verity (ed. 1893): At once good and bad (devilish-holy): good because one truth wins, and it is well that truth should prevail; bad because the other truth is beaten, and it is ill that truth should ever fail. Kittredge (ed. 1939): Devilish because your truth to Hermia is killed; holy because what kills it is the truth of your vows to me. Walter (ed. 1964): I.e. like the Devil you are using scriptural authority to gain your own ends. Helena scornfully sums up Lysander’s fanciful pleading with its christian allusion in a forceful oxymoron. Wells (ed. 1967): Holy because between truths, but devilish because the truths are incompatible. Gill (ed. 1981): Devilish (because truth is being destroyed) and holy (because truth is the victor).

1155 giue…ore]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Abandon your interest in her; throw her over, as we say colloquially.

1156–8 Weigh…tales]
Shaheen (1993, p. 98), comparing Dan. 5:27, Job 6:2 and 31:6, Ps. 62:9, Vergil’s Aeneid 12.725–7, R2 3.4.84–6 (1896–8), Tit. 1.1.55 (64): The idea of being weighed in scales to determine right from wrong was common.

1156 Weigh…waigh]
Deighton (ed. 1891): If you weigh the worth of your oath to her with the worth of your oath to me, you will find that you are weighing nothing at all; each of the oaths, as she goes on to say, being equally worthless. Verity (ed. 1893): His oaths to her and to Hermia will exactly counterbalance each other. Brooks (ed. 1979, p. xlvii) notes Sh.’s use of epanolepsis; Sonnino (1968, p. 163) quotes Peacham: A form of speech which doth begin and also end a sentence with one and the same word…to place a word of importance in the beginning of the sentence to be considered, and in the end to be remembered.

1156 you…waigh]
Greenblatt (ed. 1997): You will find that neither oath has any substance; you, Lysander, will be found to have no substance.

1158 tales]
The Clarkes (ed. 1864): Here used with a play on the word: in its sense of trivial stories, and in its sense of reckoned numbers, instead of actual articles (i.e. a reckoning by number would not have the solid weight of the articles themselves). Cf. Johnson (1755), 1 and 3; OED 5a, 6a. See also n. 143. Wright (ed. 1877): Idle words. There is the same contrast between truths and tales in [Ant. 2.2.133–4 (831–2)]. Moberly (ed. 1881): Fairy tales. Furness (ed. 1895): Stories of the imagination, pure fiction. Evans (ed. 1974): Lies. Andrews (ed. 1993): Helena may be punning on Tails, the lightest portions of animal anatomies. Cf. n. 425.

1159 I…swore]
Brooks (ed. 1979) compares 960–3.

1159 swore]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Sc. my oaths of loyalty.

1160 in my minde]
OED (Mind, n.1 9c): In my judgement or opinion, as I think. ? Obs.

1161–2 Demetrius…diuine]
Giordano-Orsini (Sogno ed. 1934) praises the charming coup de théâtre (grazioso colpo di scena) in the juxtaposition of these statements (It.).

1161 Demetrius…you]
F1 adds to the end of the line Awa.; later editors move the SD to follow the SP in 1162 (see textual n.). Walker (1860, 3:49): There is perhaps a line lost after the above. Furness (ed. 1895): The emphasis with which Lysander pronounces the name Demetrius may have awakened the bearer of it, and in the new turn given to the dramatic action the loss of a rhyming line was not felt. Wells & Taylor (1987, p. 282) speculate on the nature of a possible lost line, and note that the probability of neither speaker noticing Demetrius is reinforced by Oberon’s words at 1140–41. Holland (ed. 1994): The sudden waking of Demetrius seems to me more theatrical in that the poetic form does not end neatly. Instead, Lysander’s naming of Demetrius seems enough to wake him; it is as if he is conjured into the scene by his name. Brown (ed. 1996): There is no rhyme as Lysander states a bare, unpleasant fact, only to have Demetrius’ waking words contradict what he has just said.

1162–9 O Helen…blisse]
Gildon (1710, 7:317) considers this speech worthy looking on and finds its similes, like those of Puck at 1042–5, very apt. Chambers (ed. 1897): These lines are amusingly reminiscent of the traditional hyperboles in which Elizabethan sonnetteers celebrated the charms of their mistresses. Rylands (1928, p. 145): It is written with the tongue in the cheek, an amusing parody. Aldred (ed. 1935): It would seem that Shakespeare purposely uses this style to express the capricious suddenness of Demetrius’ artificial change. Lever (ed. 1961, p. xviii): We are not intended to take this seriously:…the style is laughed at when Thisbe describes Pyramus with the same images, but wrongly applied: [quotes 2120–1]. Holland (ed. 1994): Demetrius’ language here elevates Helena to the status of Helen of Troy, a semi-divine, perfectly beautiful woman.

1162 O…diuine]
Brooks (ed. 1979): At first sight of Emelye, Palamon starts, is uncertain whether she is a woman or goddess, but believes she must be Venus (Chaucer, KnT 1078, 1101–2; see ). Helena is so impressed by this accolade, despite believing it to be a bitter jest, that she repeats it almost verbatim at 1253.

1162 goddesse]
In A gorgious Gallery (1578), Pyramus twice calls Thisbie goddesse (O2v, O4r). Hassel (2005, p. 141): Usually a romantic compliment in Sh.

1162 nymph]
See n. 624.

1162 perfect diuine]
Andrews (ed. 1993), capitalizing Divine: Complete embodiment of divinity. Editors following Q2 in inserting a comma after perfect read diuine as an adj., which Johnson (1755, 3) defines as Excellent in a supreme degree. Thirlby (MS 1725–33) speculated perfect goddess, nymph divine.

1162 diuine]
Hassel (2005, p. 100): As a metaphor for the extraordinary, divine most often refers to things romantic; among several Shn. examples, he cites Berowne stigmatizing Dumaine profane for calling Kate divine (LLL 4.3.81–2 [1417–18]).

1163–4 To…muddy]
Glancing at the proverbial As clear as crystal, Tilley and Dent (C875).

1163 compare…eyne]
Cf. 754. See also n. 244.

1163 eyne]
See n. 256.

1164–5 Christall…cherries]
Cole (1973, p. 250), quoting Gascoigne’s Certayne Notes of Instruction: If I should undertake to wryte in prayse of a gentlewoman, I would neither praise hir christal eye, nor hir cherrie lippe, etc. For these things are trita & obuia (Complete Works, 1907, 1:465–6), adds: What was trite and obvious for the critic in 1575 could have hardly seemed very fresh to Shakespeare, twenty years later,…Thisbe’s beauties also include cherry lips [1993], perhaps to complement her lover’s cherry nose [2120]. In any case, Gascoigne’s comment substantiates our impression that the noble lovers who laugh at the conventions of the mechanicals’ presentation are themselves presented as conventionally as possible.

1164–5 O…growe]
Deighton (ed. 1891): O, how ripe your lips show, growing like two cherries resting against each other, and tempting one to pluck them; ripe and tempting used adverbially.

1164 ripe]
Schmidt (1875): Used of lips (= full and red). See OED 1d, quoting this instance first.

1165 those]
OED (II. 2b), quoting this instance first: Indicating things or persons as known to be such as described.

1166–8 That…hand]
Delius (ed. 1859) compares WT 4.4.362–5 (2185–8). Brooks (ed. 1979): Improving on the commonplace, a hand or arme whiter then the driven snow (Montemayor’s Diana, ed. 1968, p. 227). Foakes (ed. 1984): A white skin was highly prized; compare Oth. 5.2.4 [3243]: that whiter skin of hers than snow.

1166–9 white…white]
Thirlby (MS 1733–47): Ray’s Prov. 227: As white as the driven snow. Tilley (S591). Dent finds two other proverbs referred to: As black as a crow (C844) and He will say the crow is white (C853).

1166 coniealed]
Spelling of congealed current in the 15th–17th c.

1166 white]
Here and at 1169 perhaps with some connotation of OED (n.1 17b): Whiteness or fairness of complexion; and (17c): Fig. (or in fig. context) as a symbol of purity, goodness, truth, joy, etc.

1166 Taurus snow]
Johnson (ed. 1765): Taurus is the name of a range of mountains in Asia. Ruskin (Modern Painters 1846–60, Works 6:451): Taurus snow, and the frosty Caucasus, [R2 1.3.295 (559)] are used merely as types of purity or cold. See also n. 1713. Delius (ed. 1859) notes that this is the only mention of the mountain range in Sh. Brooks (ed. 1979) notes two mentions of Taurus in Seneca’s Hippolytus, ll. 168, 382–3 (Tenne Tragedies, ed. 1927, pp. 142, 151). Holland (ed. 1994) adds a reference in Ovid, Met. 2.217 (Golding, 2.275), where, however, Rhodope rather than Taurus is associated with snow (2.222; Golding, 2.282). Taurus is usually interpreted as a genitive, modernized Taurus’, but Blake (3.2.1.2 (b)) notes that in such constructions it is sometimes difficult to tell whether a genitive is meant or whether the first noun should be considered part of a compound or even a noun used as a modifier through functional shift.

1167 fand]
OED (Fan, v. 5), quoting this instance first: To blow gently upon; to cool.

1167 Easterne]
OED (3), quoting this instance: Blowing from the east. poet.

1167 crowe]
Webb (1996, p. 45): The bird of the genus Corvus, notable in various proverbs, esp. in relation to hue…Colour contrasts are common, especially with conventional objects, such as snow.

1168 thou…hand]
Foakes (ed. 1984), citing Talbert (1963, pp. 252–3): The line suggests an action; Helena presumably holds up one or both hands in a No, no don’t touch me gesture, quite misunderstood by Demetrius.

1168–9 O…blisse]
Dyce (1844, p. 48), objecting to Collier’s emendation impress (see textual n.): These two rapturous encomiums on the hand of Helena have no connexion with each other. Demetrius terms it princess of pure white, because its whiteness exceeded all other whiteness; and seal of bliss, because it was to confirm the happiness of her accepted lover. Thiselton (1903, p. 50), taking This Princesse of pure white as synecdoche: When Shakespeare uses such a figure of speech, we may be sure that it is no mere formality, but that lurking beneath the surface there is some special beauty and propriety. Here the reason for its use is that external contact between a princess and her subject is, as a matter of ceremony, only by his lips touching her hand: and when a princess extends her hand to a subject to be kissed by him, it is to some extent a sign of favour. Nor should the association of hand and seal be overlooked in interpreting this passage.

1169 Princesse]
The emendation of Hanmer (ed. 1744) provoked alternate conjectures, but most editors and commentators resisted alteration; see textual n. and conj., . Heath (1765, p. 53): I can see no objection to the common reading,…’Tis not an unusual expression to call the most excellent and perfect in any kind, the prince of the kind. Hudson (ed. 1851): Princess here plainly has the force of the superlative: the paragon, the purest, of white.

1169 seale]
OED (n.2 1b. fig.), quoting this instance: A token or symbol of a covenant; something that authenticates or confirms. Johnson (ed. 1765): He has elsewhere the same image [MM 4.1.4–5 (1774–5)], But my kisses bring again Seals of love, but seal’d in vain. Steevens (ed. 1793): More appositely, in Antony and Cleopatra [3.13.125–6 (2303–4)]: My play-fellow, your hand; this kingly seal, And plighter of high hearts. Walter (ed. 1964): Perhaps there is a glance at the sense of hand in marriage. See also n. 93–4.

1170 spight]
See n. 148.

1170 hell]
See n. 150.

1170 bent]
OED (ppl. a. †3): Determined, set.

1171 set against]
OED (v.1 128a): To make an attack upon, be hostile to. Obs. Deighton (ed. 1891) speculates: The figure here, as in [951], is probably from card-playing, in which a sum is set, staked, by one party against a sum staked by another party. Scott (ed. 1898): Set may have something of the idea of conspire. Cf. line [1175].

1171 merriment]
OED (2c), quoting this instance only: Entertainment, amusement. Obs. See also n. 16.

1172 ciuill]
See n. 528.

1172 curtesie]
Schmidt (1874, 3): Good manners. Cf. 708, 2054 and nn.

1173 iniury]
See n. 523.

1175 But]
Except; see n. 400.

1175 ioyne, in soules]
Steevens (edd. 1773, 1778), providing many parallels: Join heartily, unite in the same mind. See textual n. and conj. for the numerous emendations proposed or adopted from the early 18th to the late 20th c. Furness (ed. 1895) remains relevant: See Text. Notes for sundry emendations of a phrase which needs no help whatsoever. The notes attending these emendations are not here recorded; having no obscurity in the text to explain, they amount to but little else than an announcement by their authors of a preference of their own words to Shakespeare’s.

1175 soules]
OED (2a): The principle of thought and action in man.

1177 gentle]
Schmidt (1874, 4): Soft, tender, meek. See also 1321. Deighton (ed. 1891): Tenderly nurtured. Baker (ed. 1896): Well-born. Wells (ed. 1967): Perhaps noble rather than (or as well as) kind or mild. McDonald (ed. 2000): Mild-mannered.

1178 To vowe]
I.e., by vowing. For the gerundive use of the inifinitive, see n. 265.

1178 superpraise]
Schmidt (1875): To overpraise. Deighton (ed. 1891): Praise in exaggerated and insincere terms. See OED, Super-, prefix III.9b.

1178 parts]
Schmidt (1875, Part 4): That which is bestowed upon one, gift, endowment, quality; mostly in the plural (OED, n. 12).

1180–1 You…Helena]
Thomas (1587, Rivalis): He that loueth, wooeth, and sueth to the selfe same woman that another man doth, a riuall. OED (n.2 1a), quoting these lines: One who is in pursuit of the same object as another; one who strives to equal or outdo another in any respect. Brooks (ed. 1979, p. xlvi): with isocolon, Helena matches word against word (parison). See n. 180–3. Macdonald (1992, p. 42): The confusions in the wood and the interchangeability of characters there are the direct result of an impressive array of punning, figural, and antithetical uses of language—all contributing to a pervasive transgression of boundaries. The repeated use of the word rivals in antithetical senses…(the men are rivals in the sense of adversaries when it is a question of Hermia’s love, but rivals in the sense of partners when it is a question of mocking Helena) contributes to our sense of swiftly changing positions, and the convergence of opposite meanings in the same word suggests a sameness in what we otherwise think of as the greatest difference. See also 1399 and 1667.

1180–1 Hermia…Helena]
For the accented final -a see n. 29.

1182–4 A trim…derision]
Thirlby (MS 1725–33), followed without acknowledgement by Theobald and Steevens, is the first to draw attention to the parallel with Vergil, Aeneid, 4.93–95, where Juno sarcastically reproaches Venus for inducing Dido to fall in love with Aeneas: Splendid indeed is the praise and rich the spoils ye win, thou and thy boy; mighty and glorious is the power divine, if one woman is subdued by the guile of two gods! (tr. Fairclough, Loeb ed.). Baldwin (1944, 2:489): The parallel in form is rather striking but in itself inconclusive. Yet when we know that this passage of Virgil is the illustration of Ironia in Susenbrotus [ed. 1562, A8v]. I believe we must admit that Shakspere is echoing this stock illustration. These lines are also quoted by Abraham Fraunce, The Arcadian Rhetorike (1588), A7r.

1182 trim]
Johnson (1755), quoting this instance: [ʒetrymmeð, Saxon, completed.] Nice; smug; dressed up. OED (3): In ironical use: cf. fine, nice, pretty, in similar use. Obs.

1183 coniure…vp]
OED (9. fig. c), quoting this instance first: To bring into existence as by magic. Walter (ed. 1964): Raise. Perhaps a quibble on the original meaning of the word, swear together, which aptly recalls the oaths sworn by both men [1178].

1184 derision]
Disyllabic; see n. 19 (companion).

1184 sort]
Steevens (ed. 1778): Here used for degree or quality. Wright (ed. 1877) quotes Cotgrave (1611, Mise): Gens de mise. Persons of worth, sort, qualitie. Furness (ed. 1895): Not necessarily referring to rank.

1185 offend]
Schmidt (1875, 1d): Mortify, affront.

1185–6 extort…patience]
Johnson (ed. 1765): Harrass, torment. Rann (ed. 1787): Rack, weary out. Schmidt (1874, 1d): Wring, wrest.…(cf. to move, or wake a person’s patience = to make impatient). The Clarkes (1879, p. 348): Force a poor soul’s patience into wrath. Allen (MS apud Furness, ed. 1895): May this not possibly mean: to produce by torture the suffering of a poor soul. To take away from a poor soul her patience, seems to me commonplace. For patience compare I know your patience well [1010–11]. Kittredge (ed. 1939): Torture her power of endurance till it gives way utterly.

1186 poore soules]
See n. 1932.

1186 patience]
Disyllabic, because the final syllable is unstressed. Cf. n. 162.

1189–92 And heare…death]
Scott (ed. 1898): The words good will, part, yield up, bequeath seem all taken from business. Leggatt (1999, p. 53): Undrugged, [Lysander] showed he could respect [Hermia’s] wishes; now, like Egeus, he sees her as his to dispose of. Winter (2003, p. 91): The two young women become goods, merchandise, then Lysander draws out the legal metaphor with will, yield, part and bequeath (Fr.).

1189 heare]
Andrews (ed. 1993): Most editors emend the Quarto’s heare to here; but hear makes equally good sense. To an audience of course, it would be difficult if not impossible to tell the difference. The Folio prints here. See textual n. See also n. 1487.

1191 bequeath]
Schmidt (1874, 2): Leave, yield, bestow upon. Rushton (1869, pp. 18–22) discussing bequeath as a translation in wills of legare, quotes this among other examples to show that Sh. sometimes applies it to words and to things which do not suggest the idea of real or personal property (p. 20). Foakes (ed. 1984) cites OED 4a: To transfer, hand over, make over, assign, deliver. Obs.

1192 Whom…will do]
Shaheen (1993, p. 98) finds here and at 94, 713–15, 1414 an echo of the Marriage Service in which both man and woman agree to love so long as you both shall liue, and to remain married till death vs depart.

1193 waste…breath]
Proverbial: Dent (W81.1). See OED, Waste, v. 9d.

1193 idle]
Schmidt (1874, 2): Useless, unprofitable, futile. McDonald (ed. 2000): Pointless. Cf. nn. 1692, 2211.

1194 I…none]
Wright (ed. 1877): Will none of her, desire her not; for the complete phrase he quotes 2H4 3.2.252–3 (1785–6), Bullcalfe, grow till you come vnto it: I will none of you. For the emphatic use of none, usually followed by a partitive genitive, see Abbott §53; Franz §408; Blake 5.1.3.6 (ii). Cf. 1843.

1195 If…gone]
Brooks (ed. 1979) compares the effect of the philtre on Selvagia’s love of Alanius in Montemayor’s Diana: His memorie is now exiled out of my thoughts; see .

1195 ere]
Andrews (ed. 1993): Both (a) before, and (b) ever.

1196–7 My…returnd]
Malone (1783, p. 10) compares Son. 109: That is my home of loue, if I have rang’d, Like him that trauels I returne againe.

1196–7 My…soiournd]
Caldecott (MS 1813–33): Ie as towards Hermia, dwelt with her but as a guest. Delius (ed. 1859): To her is more generally in relation to her; in conjunction with guest-wise it can be read as a guest to her (Ger.). Wright (ed. 1877), comparing MM 1.2.180–1 (273–4): In regard to her my heart was but as a sojourner; so also Franz §530(e). Page (ed. 1897) suggests My heart only came to her and sojourn’d there in guest-fashion. Kittredge (ed. 1939): To her…belongs, not with sojourn’d, but with guest-wise: My heart paid (as it were) a visit, as a guest to her.
Johnson (ed. 1765) emends to to with (see textual n.). So to Capell (1783, 2.3:109) who opines: To sojourn to a thing…is not simply unusual, but a phrase unheard of amongst us; that verb requires absolutely the præposition it now receives from the editor. Irving & Marshall (ed. 1888) It is highly probable that, in this case, the to of the next line caught the transcriber’s eye.

1196–7 as guestwise]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Redundant; guest-wise by itself meaning in the way of a guest. See OED (B. adv.), quoting this instance first.

1196–7 soiournd]
Thomas (1587, Diversor): To resort to a place: to be at host, to soiourne, lodge, or Inne. Johnson (1755): To dwell any where for a time; to live as not at home; to inhabit as not in a settled habitation. Almost out of use. Because of the unusual preposition, Wilson (ed. 1924, Glossary): Travel to. Brooks (ed. 1979), citing Wilson: (1) travelled to, antedating OED’s sole instance, †4 Harington, 1608; (2) remained on a visit to. Abbott §490 tentatively accents on the second syllable.

1198–9 There…so]
The omission of Helen by Q2Ff gave rise to Pope’s added ever (see textual n.). Most editors prefer the Q1 reading. White (ed. 1858): The address seems to be as much demanded by the relations of the parties and the nature of the reply as by the measure. But Furness (ed. 1895): If one likes the pronunciation of Helen with the accent on the last syllable, there can be no objection to following the Q1 here. But where a line is divided between two speakers, the inevitable pause is, I think, to be preferred in scansion to the stop-gap of an ill-accented word.

1200–1 Disparage…deare]
Brooks (ed. 1979), on these lines and 1368–73: Cf. Palamon reproving Arcite’s trespass on his love (KnT 1128–43; see ).

1201 Least]
Andrews (ed. 1993): Lest. Least is the normal Shakespearean spelling for a word whose original meaning was by the less that; at times it plays on the more usual meaning of least. See 1426, and contrast 1902.

1201 to thy perill]
See n. 741; cf. n. 1678.

1201 aby it deare]
Halliwell (ed. 1856), giving many examples: To aby it dear was a favorite phrase with early writers. See n. 1472–3.

1201 aby]
OED (Aby, abye, v. 2): To pay the penalty for (an offence). arch. Rann (ed. 1787): Suffer for it. Schmidt (1874): To pay, to atone, to answer. Used by Sh. only in MND. Q2Ff’s reading abide (see textual n.) caused much comment, but see OED, Abide, v., note following #17: ¶ Through confusion of form with abye, q.v., when that verb was becoming archaic, and through association of sense between abye (pay for) a deed, and abide the consequences of a deed, abide has been erroneously used for abye = pay for, atone for, suffer for. See also 1373.

1201–2 deare…deare]
See conj. for efforts to avoid the rhyme on the homonym.

1202 Looke…deare]
Farrow (ed. 1972): With Hermia’s entry all four lovers are on stage together for the first time. True only if Helena does not enter until 191; see n. 25.

1202 Looke where]
For where meaning there after look see Franz §347(b). See OED, Where, adv. and conj. 1b (b).

1202 thy…thy]
Andrews (ed. 1989) suggests that the context requires stress on these words so that feet 2 and 5 become spondees.

1202 yonder]
See n. 1084.

1204–10 Darke…so]
Leggatt (1974, p. 91): We are reminded throughout of the workings of perception, and in particular of the way we depend on perception…for our awareness of the world. When Hermia finds Lysander…, her first words appear to be a digression: [quotes 1204–9]. The natural question—But why unkindly didst thou leave me so? [1210]—is asked only after she has discoursed in general terms on how the senses work. Foakes (ed. 1984) finds a connection with Hermia’s exit lines (806–11), strengthening the sense of a continuous action.

1204–7 Darke…recompence]
Bucknill (1860, p. 90): The increased intensity of one sense compensating for inactivity of another is expounded thus. [quotes 1204–7]. Theobald (1904, p. 245): A very accurate reflection of Bacon’s idea of sound heard in the night, with the contrast between hearing and seeing, and the intensification of one sense by the suspension of another.…(See [Sylva Sylvarum] 235). Farrow (ed. 1972): Darkness pays hearing twice for having weakened sight. Hollindale (1992, p. 90): These two pairs of lines are not quite identical in meaning. In the second couplet Hermia is refining her point by suggesting that the ear gains sensitivity to precisely the same degree as the eye loses it. She elaborates her point (and in the process makes it absurd) by claiming for it an exact and mathematical equivalence.

1204 Darke night]
For the confusion about when the moon shines and when it is dark see n. 222–3.

1204 his]
On the use of his as the neuter poss. pron. see n. 470.

1204 function]
Johnson (1755, 5): Office of any particular part of the body (OED, n. 3, quoting this instance first). Deighton (ed. 1891) adds of seeing.

1204 takes]
Johnson (1755, 75): To Take from. To deprive of (OED, v. 58).

1205 quicke]
OED (a. 20a): Keen or rapid in its function; capable of ready or swift comprehension. Deighton (ed. 1891): Lively (cf. OED, a. 17).

1205 apprehension]
OED (n. 5), quoting this instance first: The action of laying hold of with the senses; conscious perception. Holland (ed. 1994): Grasp, suggesting a physical action; compare [1797] and [1810 (apprehend)]. Richmond (2002, p. 35): For Elizabethans apprehension meant having full understanding or accurate perception.…Awareness, not fear, is the basic impression the word should communicate to an audience.

1206 Wherein]
Onions (1911; 1986, 2): In that in which, in whatever; (hence) though. OED (adv. 3 (b), quoting this instance first: In that respect in which. Brooks (ed. 1979): The respect is its effect on each physical sense. Cf. AYL 1.2.184 (347).

1206 double]
McAlindon (1991, p. 48): The word double ironically signals her impending confusion: when her lover declares that he now hates her, she tells him, You speak not as you think [1218].

1206 recompence]
Knowles (privately) suggests a double sense: 1) due payment and 2) restitution.

1209 Mine…sound]
West (1865, p. 19) compares Sidney, Arcadia (ed. Evans, 1977, p. 334), But making mine ear my guide, I left not many trees behind me before I saw at the bottom of one of them a gentleman, bound.

1209 thy sound]
Deighton (ed. 1891): The sound made by you, i.e. your voice.

1210 vnkindly]
Johnson (1755): Without kindness; without affection (OED, adv. 3, quoting this instance). Scott (ed. 1898): Either in the ordinary sense, or with an older meaning, unnaturally or contrary to thy own nature (OED, adv. 2b).

1211 presse]
Johnson (1755, 3): To constrain; to compel; to urge by necessity (OED, v.1 8a, quoting 1211 and 1212). Deighton (ed. 1891): Probably…used in the sense of pressing for service (OED, v.2, esp. with some sense of compulsion, as in 2).

1213 Lysanders loue]
Delius (ed. 1859): Helena.

1213 bide]
Johnson (1755, 2): To remain (OED, v. 3).

1214–15 Faire…light]
The Clarkes (ed. 1864): The reference to the stars in the present passage…affords an instance of Shakespeare’s dramatic art in marking time and place; just as previously he makes Demetrius say—As bright, as clear, as yonder Venus in her glimmering sphere [1083–4]. These allusions to the planet and the stars beaming overhead bring constantly to the imagination that the speakers are in the open air, and in the warm summer night, with the blue and star-gemmed sky above the full-leaved trees. Massey (1866, p. 34) compares Son. 28.12, When sparkling stars twire not thou gild’st the even. Walter (ed. 1964): Perhaps a glance at the meaning of the word Helena—meteor, torch (OED, Helena); see also n. 759. Brooks (ed. 1979): The eulogy outgoes that in Montemayor on [faire Diana more resplendant, then the Emerauld, or Diamond in the night (ed. 1968, p. 226)]; the nocturnal brilliants Diana outshines are only gems.

1214 engilds]
Schmidt (1874): To gild, to make splendent. Used by Sh. here only. OED, quoting this instance: Also fig. to brighten with golden light. Sipe (1968, pp. 68–71) notes the use of en- to furnish an extra syllable for metrical purposes.

1215 oes…eyes]
Grinfield (MS 1834–63): A poor pun (I suppose) on O’s & I’s. like the tailor, who said he knew the Os, because they were like the buttons; and the Is, because they were like the button-holes. Greenblatt (ed. 1997): Stars (punning on the vowels and on lovers’ exclamatory ohs and ays).

1215 oes]
Thirlby (MS 1733–47) compares Ant. 5.2.[79–92 (3296–3310)]. Steevens (ed. 1773): Shakespeare uses O for a circle, instancing H5 1.1.prol. 13 (14): wooden O. Delius (ed. 1859) compares LLL 5.2.45 (1933). See OED, O, n.1 2a, quoting this instance. Probably also intended is the image of decorative spangles (OED, n.1 2b). Steevens (ed. 1778) quotes Henry Hawkins, describing the heavens as the purple Canopie of the Earth, powdred ouer and beset with siluer-oes (Partheneia Sacra, 1633, [p. 81]. Tollet (in Ibid.): [Sir Simonds] D’Ewes’s Journal of [the…Speeches of the House of Lords and Commons of] Queen Elizabeth [1693], p. 650 [col. 2, ll. 23–6], mentions a patent to make spangles and oes of gold. Steevens (MS 1793–1800 [added in v1803]) compares silver oes and spangles from John Davies of Hereford, Microcosmos (1603, p. 233). Caldecott (MS 1813–33) quotes from Jonson’s Masque of Hymen (1606), which describes the mantles of the lords who danced made in various coloured silks, cut in leaues,…and imbrodered with Oo’s (7:230), and from Bacon’s Essays, 37, Of Masques and Triumphs: Oes, or Spangs which contribute a visual effect of Glory (1625, p. 225). Knowles (privately) notes the similar image in pattens of bright gold (MV 5.1.59 [2471]). Grey (1754, 1:61), followed by Johnson (ed. 1765) and Gentleman (ed. 1774), unnecessarily conjectures Orbs.

1215 eyes]
OED (Eye, n.1 1b. Poet.) the eyes…of night = the stars. Craig (in Cuningham, ed. 1905): Bright shining eye-like lights. Cf. Ham. 2.2.517 (1558), the burning eyes of heauen.

1216 this]
Rann (ed. 1787): Helena’s beauty. Deighton (ed. 1891): The fact of my leaving you.

1217 bare]
Ambiguous tense. Deighton (ed. 1891): Feel towards. Gill (ed. 1981): Felt towards. Andrews (ed. 1993): Bore.…[A] theatre audience could understand Lysander to be saying bear. OED lists bare among alternate spellings of Bear, v.1.

1218 speake…thinke]
Proverbial. Tilley (S725), quoting this instance. Smith (1963, p. 112) gives many examples from Sh. and earlier works, more usually in the positive form.

1218 It…bee]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Sc. that you hate me, as you say.

1219–1384+1 Lo…say]
Gentleman (ed. 1774): The puzzles of love are very entertainingly produced in this scene, but imagination seems too much sifted, and therefore frequently reproduces ideas by which they are unavoidably enervated. Lewes (MS 1842–78): This quarrel is droll, but it is not in keeping either with the character or the scene. It is descending from the Ideal to the vulgar passions of everyday life. Chambers (ed. 1897): There is more differentiation of character here than elsewhere in the story, between Hermia, the diminutive shrew, and Helena, the long-legged coward. Gordon (ed. 1910, pp. xix–xx): In their peculiar manner of conducting a controversy they are very like real women indeed, but women of a certain class.…It is, indeed, difficult to escape the conviction that…Shakespeare was irresponsible, and wished to make his audience laugh more than was good for it.…It is a ridiculous and unedifying scene; partly suggested, let us imagine, by the figure of some particular actors in the company. Massinger took the scene in the right way when he parodied its climax almost literally in his Duke of Milan [1.2.157–78]. (The parallel is first noted by William Gifford, ed., The Plays of Philip Massinger, 4 vols., 1805, 1:266; see The Shakspere Allusion Book, rev. John Munro, 1909, 1:296–7.) Stratton (ed. 1920): Quarrel scenes are usually effective on the stage. This scene between Helena and Hermia with its pathos, feminine jealousy, rush of words, misunderstanding, and humor is excellent. Do not forget that during it Demetrius and Lysander are not standing like sticks. Galway (1935, pp. 183, 185–6): Flyting is the oldest of all laughter-provoking devices in native English drama.…[This scene is] the one exception to Shakspere’s observation of social etiquette in the flytings between lords and ladies.…Shakspere’s literary manners were always elegant, and it is possible that he was hinting at the exceptional nature of this dialogue when he caused Puck to chuckle gleefully, This their jangling I esteem a sport [1394]. Hill (1964, p. 19): It is pure humour of character and situation. Nothing so refined or considered as wit had place here; and all dignity is cast to the winds in the bid for a husband. Scragg (1996, p. 72): The stress upon tall and height serves to accentuate Helena’s stature, while the terms low and dwarfish augment the physical distinction between the two women. At the same time, dwarfish and maypole imply deformity, or a movement away from human form, and this metamorphic process is sustained as the estrangement grows between the lovers. Vienne-Guerrin (2002, pp. 32–5): The insults which punctuate this scene sound the knell of courtesy (Fr.). She discusses at greater length than Galway (above) the elements of flyting in the scene.

1219–21 Lo…mee]
Bevington (1987, p. 514) objects to Wells & Taylor (ed. 1986) marking these lines as an aside (see textual n.): The lovers are openly confronting one another and Helena has no reason not to let everyone hear her outburst of suspicion.

1219 Lo]
Perhaps a pun on Hermia’s lowness? Cf. 1322, 1329–31, 1338–40, 1361–3.

1219 she…confederacy]
Deighton (ed. 1891): She has banded herself together with Lysander and Demetrius.

1219 confederacy]
Most commentators ignore this word. OED (n. 3) cites 1219 as meaning, A collective body of persons…united by league;…a confederation. But this is not what Helena means; at 1223 she accuses Hermia of having conspird with the two men; which fits the definition of OED (1.b.): Law (and thence gen.), in a bad sense; A league for an unlawful or evil purpose; a conspiracy. Florio (1598, Leghe), Minsheu (1599, Confederacion), and Cotgrave (1611, Confederacion) give a neutral meaning of league or confederacie, but also complots, plotting, or conspiracie. Cf. Jonson, The Alchemist (1610), 5.3.22–3 (5:392): This is one / O’ the confederacie; Bartholomew Fair (1614) 1.2.57 (6:22): Why, this is a confederacy, a meere piece of practice vpon her by these Impostors, (quoted by OED 1b).

1221 fashion]
OED (v. 2b), quoting this instance: To contrive, manage. Deighton (ed. 1891): Shape, concoct (Cf. OED, v. 1).

1221 false]
Johnson (1755, 7; 8): 7. Treacherous; perfidious; deceitful; hollow.…8. Counterfeit. Deighton (ed. 1891): Cowardly. Verity (ed. 1893): Wicked. Johnson (ed. 1894): This sport which is all false, as neither of these men really love me. See OED, a. A. III. 13.

1221 sport]
Jest (OED, n.1 4a). See 1266–7. Knowles (privately): Sense of game? Cf. the sport of bear-baiting, 1224.

1221 in…mee]
Johnson (ed. 1765) alone revises the text (see textual n.): In spite to me. Rolfe (ed. 1877): In derision of, comparing Rom. 1.1.78 (78). Barnett (1887, p. 20): From spite against me. Deighton (ed. 1891): Out of malice towards me. Verity (ed. 1893): So as to spite [me]. Sprague (ed. 1896): In defiance of? notwithstanding? Scott (ed. 1898): In my despite. Kittredge (ed. 1939): To vex me. See also n. 148.

1222–1388 Iniurious…mistooke]
Chambers (ed. 1897, p. 195) suggests these lines show signs of later work. Wilson (ed. 1924) notes the sudden change from couplets to blank verse, and suspects we are dealing with revision work here. See also Castelain (ed. 1943). Walter (ed. 1964): Does the change to blank verse mark—a passage of narrative, an alteration to the text, a change of subject or mood? Knowles (privately): There is a change at 1322 not just of mood but of kind of activity, from bemused questioning to aggressive quarreling.

1222–46 Iniurious…iniury]
Singled out by Pope (ed. 1725, 6:4M1v) as a notable description of Friendship betwixt two young Ladies, Helena’s speech has drawn much commentary both for its intrinsic force and as being pivotal in the development of an important theme. Grinfield (MS 1834–63): How suddenly S. pours out a strain of tender beauty in the midst of his frolics! Anon (1841, pp. 109–10), commenting on a reproduction of Joseph Severn’s picture of Helena and Hermia: What a simple picture is this of the every-day life of two maidens growing together in love and confidence, as thousands still grow; and yet how exquisitely poetical in its literal truth. The counsel shared together;—the little confidences gradually ripening into the revealing of the inmost heart, and thus becoming sister’s vows;—the longing to meet, the dread to part;—the common occupation, such as Mr. Severn has exhibited, but accompanied with that crowning circumstance—Both warbling of one song, both in one key. He that wrote this charming description is, of all poets, the one who has left us the truest delineations of the tenderness, the constancy, the intrepidity, and the purity of women. Strindberg (1909; 1966, pp. 226, 228–9): Their relationship is depicted extremely attractively by Helena,…[quotes 1222–1241]. Those are beautiful, glowing, innocent images—double cherry, one crest—and now one becomes interested in the young people.…Even the person who knows only a little English should read Helena’s verses…until he knows them so well that they flow off his tongue and do not get stuck…. Helena’s lines are beautifully musical, so I wonder if Mendelssohn was not simply directly inspired by the poet’s harmonies.…In Crowned with one crest, corona and crista are decidedly Latin; in [1222–4] appears much Latin, and that can be heard, too. See also Granville-Barker (ed. 1914, p. viii) at . Everett (1961, p. 321) sees the women here innocently throwing overboard a childhood loyalty which takes on an ornamental, emblematic, and Paradisaical peacefulness in contrast to their new world [that includes men], which both at different times find hellish: [quotes 1230–3, 1242–3]. Garber (1981, p. 32), discussing childhood twinning in Sh.: Frequently, nostalgic recollections of youthful experience utilize the twin image as a sign of interchangeability and lack of differentiation.…[quotes 1230–9]. The twinship of these two young women, innocent and explicitly parallel to twin forms in nature, is closely analogous to the relationship between Celia and Rosalind. Bellringer (1983, p. 212) admires one of the best speeches in a play full of well-controlled stylistic contrasts. He deems Helena’s idea of friendship…more appealing than Oberon’s tableau of the mysterious imperial votress [540], but it shares with it a certain artificial exaltation of sexual immunity. The embroidered flower which the two girls had created, though lovely and valuable, lacks the volatility of the rose distill’d [85], which Theseus praised as the essence of love in the first scene. Helena’s speech contains such an awareness, of course; it is peculiarly poised between formality and conviction. The jangling quarrel between the two young women is the almost inevitable next phase of their relationship. Shakespeare reveals none of the usual disdain for women in dispute. The ideal of harmonious amity remains valid. Snyder (1988, p. 75): The passage marks what must be left behind in the process of growing up and being fully inscribed in the patriarchal order. Hillman (1991, p. 74) says Helena’s idyllic vision is a self-serving fiction that she herself is about to retract unceremoniously (She was a vixen when she went to school [1360]) as part of the process by which the lovers’ multifarious naïveté is broken down by the darker feelings and tensions of mature experience. Under the fairies’ influence, idealization of sexual innocence joins other self-indulgent postures as discredited, in keeping with the initial equation of a maiden pilgrimage [84], however blessèd [87], with death. Gibinska (1998, pp. 82, 84) comments on the paradoxical opposition of one as double and double as one, finding the argument…self-defeating: the doubleness, the separation, the partition of the two I-s cannot be negated by the artificial construct of reality in which one cushion and one embroidery, one song, even one tune, can bridge the gap of difference between the two characters. No matter how much Helena would insist on seeming [1236, 1239], their hands and minds, also hearts, are not incorporate [1235]. The speech reflects Helena’s wishful thinking, her wish to escape the situation in which their difference means division, rather than her memory of the idyllic years together with Hermia. Their union depends on the partition.…Together they form an antithetical argument: their seeming differences in bodies…, and their real difference in tempers form together an idea of one womanhood where differences do not hinder a relationship of oneness. Helena’s pathetic monologue sums up in rhetorical patterns the essence of friendship between women: the paradox of union in partition depends on contrasts.
Several recent critics are interested in the bearing of this speech on matters of homo- and heterosexual relationships. Calderwood (1965, p. 512): Puck’s western flower [543] acts both as a dissolvent upon the natural homosexuality of school-days’ friendship, [1229] rending ancient love asunder, and as a catalyst hastening the girls’ transition to adult heterosexuality. In a much more domestic form, the girls’ experience thus mirrors the passage of Hippolyta from her Amazonian past to her Athenian present. Adelman (1985, p. 82): At the same time as the male bond is essentially eradicated from this play, concern with same sex bonding is made comically safe and distant by its transfer to the women. The strongest expression of same-sex fusion anywhere in Shakespeare’s plays is in Helena’s wonderfully poignant lines about herself and Hermia as a double cherry, seeming parted, But yet an union in partition.…Here Helena’s insistence on oneness—one occurs five times in three lines—makes the two women seem indeed incorporate.…But this statement of union comes only after its rupture. Holstun (1987, p. 846) calls the speech a hymeneal elegy reminiscent of Sapho to Philaenis (attributed to Donne). See also Traub (1992, pp. 157–8). J. Hall (1995, p. 109): As in so much Renaissance poetic rhetoric, the domains of art and nature merge, and the one flower produced by artificial gods becomes the sign of both a natural union of the double cherry and the social double-headed heraldic emblem. But there is a threat to patriarchy in this beautiful regressive fantasy to a state where union was possible without men. This is Amazon or Lesbian poetry. Ehnenn (1997, pp. 332–3): Helena’s description of the friendship is insistently physical; commenting on the creativity of their relationship, and on the adoption of heraldic terms, Ehnenn adds: Thus she appropriates terms from patriarchal discourse while simultaneously rejecting the ways of men. Lambs’ version of this speech does skim the surface of some of these issues…but Shakespeare describes a deeper, more physical, possibly homoerotic affection. Jankowski (2000, pp. 145–53) discusses the erotic implications of the imagery in 1230–46, and compares this friendship with other same-sex relationships such as Leontes/Polixenes, Celia/Rosalind, and Emilia/Flavina in TNK. Traub (2001, p. 144) observes that certain performance cuts made in this speech (as presented in Griffiths’s [ed. 1996, p. 161 n.]) silenced the homoerotic nature of their love. Boehrer (2004, p. 103): Here…heterosexual and homosexual unions acquire the character of a categorical opposition,…It is a move from one sort of incorporation—the idyllic union about which Helena reminisces bitterly—to another: the union of husband and wife as one flesh that Helena believes to be prefigured in Hermia’s emotional estrangement and antagonistic mockery. Likewise, it is a move from one sort of reproduction to another—from the artificial creativity of Helena and Hermia’s sampler, with its likeness of the living world, to the organic creativity of parenthood.
Some critics draw attention to analogous notable treatments of the theme of friendship. Dodd (1752, 1:84–5) first notes the similarity with Emilia’s speech in TNK 1.3.49–82. Reed (in Steevens, ed. 1793): Mr. Gibbon observes, that in a poem of Gregory Nazianzen on his own life, are some beautiful lines which burst from the heart, and speak the pangs of injured and lost friendship, resembling these. He adds Shakspeare had never read the poems of Gregory Nazianzen: he was ignorant of the Greek language; but his mother tongue, the language of nature, is the same in Cappadocia and in Britain. Gibbon’s Hist. [1781] Vol. III. [ch. xxvii] p. 15. Hudson (ed. 1851) provides a translation by Newman of the passage in St Gregory Nazianzen. Ward (1875, 1:384) dismisses the claim made by Rushton (1871, pp. 55–6): Lyly’s account of the friendship of Euphues and Philautus may have suggested Shakespeare’s beautiful description of the school-days’ friendship of Helena and Hermia; however, Bond (1902, 1:170) also sees a parallel. Marshall (1982, pp. 561–2) discusses the speech as a picture of the emblem and story of love which Plato has Aristophanes present in The Symposium…(which was extensively summarized in Ficino’s popular commentary…); quoting the translation by Walter Hamilton (New York, 1979, pp. 62–4), he continues: Love…restores us to our ancient state by attempting to weld two beings into one…This is what everybody wants, and everybody would regard it as the precise expression of this desire…that he should melt into his beloved, and that henceforth they should be one instead of two. Helena’s vision of her lost union with Hermia, [quotes 1234–5], evokes both this mythical, original condition and the restoration that love means in its sense of mending what has been sundered. Here Christian, classical, and mythic imagery seem to come together. (Webb (1989, p. 59), under Hearts (unity of): Love’s unity, represented by two hearts as one, or by the similar motif of hearts (or souls) exchanged. A popular exposition of the neo-Platonic argument is that of Ficino; he cites 694 as well as quoting 1239 together with Son. 22.6–7. Scragg (1996, pp. 65–6) discussing the theory of friendship and examples of variations on the faithful friends story, notes that here Sh. inverts the conventional story by making his tale one of female friendship; she compares 1225–41 with Elyot’s description in The Governour of the way that Titus and Gisippus formed such a mutual affection that their wills and appetites daily more and more…confederated themselves,…. The values of a male friendship are also implicit in the imagery that Helena uses to define their former relationship. The chivalric world of Arcite and Palemon is evoked in [1240–1], while the approximation to the divine is suggested through the capacity to create (cf. [1230–1]).

1222 Iniurious]
Johnson (1755, 2): Guilty of wrong or injury (OED (a. 1); perhaps also (1) Unjust; invasive of another’s rights. Wright (ed. 1877): Insulting (OED, a. 2), referring to 523 (iniury). Rolfe (ed. 1877) compares 3H6 3.3.78 (1812) Iniurious Margaret and Cor. 3.3.69 (2349) thou iniurious Tribune. Neil (ed. 1878): Insolent, like the Greek hubristes.

1223 conspir’d]
Johnson (1755, 1): To concert a crime; to plot; to hatch secret treason (OED, v. 1).

1223 contriu’d,]
Wright (ed. 1877): Plotted (OED, v. 1). Three editors introduce a comma here (see textual n.), apparently wishing to ensure that both verbs are seen to govern the infinitive. The added punctuation is unnecessary, since the parallel construction together with the natural pause at the end of the line makes the meaning plain.

1224 To baite]
Schmidt (1874, Bait, 2): To harass in a manner like that of dogs, (OED, Bait v.1 2; 2b). Baker (ed. 1896): Tease. Baiting bears with dogs was a favorite sport in Shakspere’s time. See Lee (1916, 2:428–36). Andrews (ed. 1993): Goad, torment.…Helena, seemingly besieged by her friends, feels like the victim of such false Sport [1221]. She is comparing herself to a Beare as she did at 749.

1224 foule]
Johnson (1755, 6): Disgraceful; shameful (OED, a. 13a, quoting this instance).

1224 derision]
For the disyllabic pronunciation of ion see n. 159.

1225–46 Is…iniury]
Dodd (1752, 1:110) cites these lines in illustration of AYL 1.3.73–6 (534–7); at 1:137, in illustration of WT 1.2.62–74 (124–38); and at 2:116, in illustration of MM 1.4.46–8 (396–8).

1225–8 Is…forgot?]
The first singular verb is governed by counsell, whose whole extent, substance, or compass (OED, All, a. 1a, b) is then specified; that in 1228 is governed by the summarizing all =Everything (OED 8a).

1225 counsell]
See n. 229.

1226 sisters vowes]
Upton (1748, pp. 332–3), arguing that Sh. uses substantives adjectively; or, by way of apposition, proposes to correct the text to sister. Capell (ed. 1768) first adopts the emendation, and a few others follow, adding a hyphen. Most, from Steevens (ed. 1778) on, add an apostrophe. See textual n. Deighton (ed. 1891): Vows of sisterly love; protestations such as two sisters would make to each other. Gill (ed. 1981): Promises to be sisters to each other.

1226 spent]
Page (ed. 1897): Spent together, in each other’s company.

1227 chid]
OED (Chide, v. 3b), quoting this instance: To scold, rebuke. Gill (ed. 1981): Reproached. On the curtailed form of past participles see n. 121 (spoke). Cf. n. 521.

1227 hastie footed]
Johnson (1755, Hasty, 1), quoting this instance: Quick; speedy (OED, Hasty, a., and D. Comb., quoting this instance first). Deighton (ed. 1891): So quickly slipping away. Rolfe (ed. 1877) compares Son. 19.6, swift-footed time. Cf. lighter heel’d (1459).

1228 For…forgot]
White (ed. 1858): The natural pause before O! fills up the time of the missing unaccented syllable. Delius (ed. 1859) believes the pause intended by Sh.; he notes the F2 and has been adopted by many, but that Malone’s now is without authority (Ger.). Wright (ed. 1877): The broken line is suitable to the hurried ejaculations of Hermia [i.e., Helena]. Irving & Marshall (ed. 1888): The O is here a prolonged exclamation, and the hiatus in the metre is filled by the emotion of the actress. Abbott §481: Monosyllables which are emphatic…often take the place of a whole foot; he gives eleven examples of O so used in §482. For the many conjectures of an added monosyllable to regularize the meter, see textual n. and conj., . For discussion of another seemingly syllable-lacking line see n. 378 (Moons).

1228 forgot]
On the curtailed form of past participles see n. 121 (spoke).

1229 schooldaies]
Some view this as a possessive and add an apostrophe, while others, like Kittredge (ed. 1939) take it as an adjective, not a genitive. OED, Schoolday 1 pl., quoting this instance first, seems to see it as a noun used attributively, parallel with childhood. See textual n.

1229 childhood innocence]
On noun-compounds, see n. 184.

1230 artificiall gods]
Capell (1783, 2.3:109): Gods working artificially, artificing gods. Caldecott (MS 1813–33): I.e. artist Gods. Powers creative. Halliwell (ed. 1856) quotes an MS. note by T. Hull: Our author meant, in the epithet artificial, to imply only, similar to—namely—We two, like two deities, were creating, &c. Page (ed. 1897): Helena likens herself and Hermia to gods on account of the flowers and other objects they created. Steevens (ed. 1778): Ingenious. Rann (ed. 1787): Skilful (OED, a. 6b). Hudson (ed. 1851): Artificial is here used for the worker in art, not the work; like its Latin original artifex, artist, or artificer. But Tyrrell (ed. 1850): Their happiness and the union of their tempers were so complete, that they were like two seeming or fictitious gods. Richmond (2002, p. 43): For Elizabethans [artificial] kept closer to its association with art, having a positive creative association, rather than a negative one of feigning or falsity: [quoting 1230–1]. Walker (1860, 1:154) finds an allusion to the story of Arachne and Minerva (with a variety) [Ovid, Metamorphoses 6.1–145]. See also Abbott §3.

1231 needles]
Steevens (ed. 1773): Probably written by Shakespeare neelds…otherwise the verse will be inharmonious and (ed. 1778) he emends his text and a few editors follow (see textual n.). But Malone (ed. 1790): Many words of two syllables are printed at length, though intended to be pronounced as one. Collier (ed. 1842) quotes Luc. 319: And griping it, the needle his finger pricks. See also Abbott §465.

1232 sampler]
Johnson (1755), quoting this instance: A pattern of work; a piece worked by young girls for improvement (OED, n.1 3b). Deighton (ed. 1891) adds, as a sample of their capacity. Baker (ed. 1896): Embroidery (OED, n.1 3b).

1233 warbling]
Singing with trills and quavers (OED, Warble, v.1 2a).

1233 of]
See n. 245 (admiring of).

1233 in one key]
Deighton (ed. 1891): In unison of note (OED, n.1 7, quoting this instance first). See n. 22. Wells (ed. 1967): That two singers of one song would be in the same musical key is so obviously desirable that this phrase sometimes causes laughter. But the phrase voices and minds in [1234] shows that for Shakespeare the song and the key were distinct and that in one key means in mental accord.

1234 sides]
Schmidt (1875, 2): Plur[al]…= frame of the body.…Especially = the breast, as containing the heart or the lungs.

1235–7 So…partition]
Lever (ed. 1961, p. xvii): This image…leads us to think of [the two girls] in connection with beautiful things in nature that grow and ripen; and this in turn shapes our attitude to Helena, Hermia and their problems of growing up, making us see these as part of a natural process for plants and human beings alike. Knowles (privately) demurs: The point of the comparison is not ordinary natural growth, but extraordinary closeness, as it were a fructal counterpart of Siamese twins, having a common heart (1239).

1235 incorporate]
OED (ppl. a. 1): United in one body. For the omission of d in the participle see Abbott §342; Franz §159. For similar uses see 2189 (create), 2199 (consecrate). Holland (ed. 1994): Trisyllabic.

1236 Like to]
See n. 8.

1236 double cherry]
OED (Double, a. 1a), quoting this instance: Forming a pair; coupled. Deighton (ed. 1891): A twin cherry originating out of a single blossom.

1236–7 cherry…partition]
Bicks (2003, p. 86), inappropriately: Has the cherry (a term commonly associated with the lips of a woman’s mouth and vagina) been parted or not?…The image recalls the maddening ambiguity of virginal markers. Knowles (privately) correctly objects: The cherry is the image for the whole beautiful person of each girl, not a virginal marker. See also n. 1995–6.

1236 seeming]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Seemingly, apparently (OED, ppl. a. 4).

1237 an…partition]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Really united in spite of the line of seeming partition. Verity (ed. 1893): I.e. at once united and parted. Vienne-Guerrin (2004, p. 87 n. 2) calls attention to the musical sense of the oxymoron; see OED, Partition, n. 8. Mus.

1237 partition]
Quadrisyllabic: see n. 159.

1238 louely]
While Collier’s (ed. 1853) emendation was generally rejected (see textual n.), Delius (ed. 1859) accepts the meaning loving for louely, comparing 2 Sam. 1:23 (Saul and Jonathan were lovely and pleasant in their lives,) and Shr. 3.2.123 (1506). Carruthers & Chambers (ed. 1861): Helena…appears for the moment absorbed in the image of the union in partition (1237)—the double cherry and the lovely berries—and it is to them that she means the epithet to apply. Dyce (ed. 1864), in his own edition of the Works of Robert Greene & George Peele (1861), compares Peele’s Arraignment of Paris, p. 358, 2.1.486: give thee many a lovely kiss and Greene’s James the Fourth, p. 189, 1.1.13: these lovely styles.

1238 moulded]
Johnson (1755, 1): To form; to shape; to model (OED, v.2 3, quoting this instance). Deighton (ed. 1891): Shaped by Nature.

1238 stemme]
Johnson (1755, 1), quoting this instance: The stalk; the twig (OED, n.1 2a, quoting this instance first). Association with the branches of a family and with a genealogical tree (OED, n.1 3a) possibly suggested the heraldic image of 1240–1.

1239 So…heart]
Proverbial; Dent (B503.1): One soul (heart, mind) in Bodies twain. Not in Tilley. See n. 700 (one heart). Halliwell (ed. 1856) quotes some contemporary parallels, and Daniel (1887–92, p. 246) gives one from Ariosto’s comedy La Scolastica.

1239 seeming]
OED (ppl. a. 3): Apparent to the senses or to the mind, as distinct from what is. But Baker (ed. 1896): With, seemingly, two bodies. Cf. 1236.

1240–1 Two…creast]
The emendation of first life to first, like adopted by Theobald (ed. 1733) and attributed by him to the Friendship and Communication of the ingenious Martin Folks, Esq; has been adopted by virtually all editors (see textual n.). The heraldic import of the emended passage elicited discussion. Hanmer (ed. 1744) explains the first: A term used in blazoning, when two Coats of Arms are quarter’d together, and the second is the same as the first. Capell (1783, 2.3:109): The comparison’s aptness lyes in having the two first or principal quarterings of a coat borne in two distinct shields; united at top, (as are now the arms of great heiresses to those of their husbands) and crowned with one crest. Mason (1785, p. 70): Every branch of a family is called a House, and none but the first of the first house can bear the arms of the family, without some distinction; two of the first, therefore, means two coats of the first house, which are properly due but to one. Malone (ed. 1790): Helena…means to say, that she and her friend were as closely united, as much one person, as if they were both of the first house; as if they both had the privilege due but to one person; but later (in Boswell, ed. 1821) he retracts this explanation, referring instead to the four quarters of a coat of arms: Two of the first…are two perfectly similar,…the first and fourth, which are in fact two of the first, due but to one and to be crowned only with one crest. Ritson (1792, p. 45) finds no difficulty with the general picture presented by the simile, but claims Sh. must have entirely misunderstood, since he has so strangely misapplyed, the expression Two of the first; which, in heraldical jargon, always means two objects of the first colour mentioned, that is the field. Douce (1807, 1:194): It may be doubted whether this passage has been rightly explained, and whether the commentators have not given Shakspeare credit for more skill in heraldry than he really possessed, or at least than he intended to exhibit on the present occasion. Helen says, we had two seeming bodies, but only one heart. She then exemplifies her position by a simile—we had two of the first, i.e. bodies, like the double coats in heraldry that belong to man and wife as one person, but which, like our single heart, have but one crest. Staunton (ed. 1857) claims that heraldical principles were familiarly understood in Sh.’s time; he quotes Gerard Leigh on the nine partitions of a shield: The first wherof is a partition from the highest part of escocheon to the lowest pointe;…And although it must be blazed so, yet is it a ioining togither.…It is also, as a mariage, that is to say, 2 cotes, the man on the right side, and the woman on the left (The Accedence of Armorie (1591, f. 25r), and adds: In different words, this is nothing else than an amplification of Helena’s own expression, seeming parted; But yet a union in partition [1236–7]. The debate over the nature and application of the heraldic references is rehearsed at some length by e.g. Furness (ed. 1895), Brooks (ed. 1979), and Foakes (ed. 1984).
A few commentators have defended the QqFf text. Prompted by F2 punctuation, Wilson (1873, p. 253) places a colon after life, and explains: Two bodies of the first life, would be moulded on one parental stem, and two coats in heraldry of the first life, would be due to one and the same descendant. Krause (1886, p. 172) suggests life-coats, believing that life-coat is an expression from heraldry that has not been transmitted elsewhere (Ger.). Andrews (ed. 1989) adopts this reading: It is consistent with the other powers Helena credits to herself and Hermia that these dear friends should also be able to bring to life, and combine in one Crest…two coats of arms. According to Helena, she and Hermia combined to become the first living, breathing coats of arms in the history of heraldry. Idem (ed. 1993): Compare [698–703]. Bellringer (1983, p. 212), without further explanation, suggests: There may be a specific, if obscure, paradisal touch in the unamended line [1240].
Some may sympathize with the action attributed to his old Corrector by Collier (1853, p. 105): The heraldic couplet…is struck out by the same hand, probably because, like most other readers, he did not understand it, and thought that audiences would be in the same condition.

1242 And…asunder]
Parker (1996, p. 94), fancifully, sees a reminiscence of the marriage ceremony: The echo is sounded not for a sanctioned joining but rather for a couple that cannot lawfully be joined i.e., two women. See The First and Second Prayer Books of Edward VI (1549): The Forme of Solemnizacion of Matrimonie, p. 254: Then shal the prieste ioyne theyr ryght handes together, and say. ¶ Those whome god hath ioyned together: let no man put a sundre. [1910, p. 254]. Knowles (privately), demurring: The verb phrase rend asunder can apply to a multitude of things, not just matrimony.

1242 rent]
Johnson (1755, Rent, rather to rend): To tear (OED, Rent, v.2 1. trans.).

1244 maidenly]
Johnson (1755), quoting this instance: Like a maid; gentle, modest, timorous, decent (OED, a. 3, quoting this instance; see also 2: Characterized by a maiden’s qualities, e.g. gentleness, modesty, timidity. Obs.).

1245–6 Our…iniury]
Gentleman (ed. 1774): This appeal to former friendship is natural, agreeable, and affecting; being spun out rather too far, we have marked some lines [1245–6] for omission. Deighton (ed. 1891): Though I alone suffer from your behaviour, our whole sex is dishonoured by it.

1245 chide]
See n. 1227.

1245 for it]
Elided: for’t. See Walker (1854, p. 79), quoting this line.

1247 I am…words]
F1’s insertion of passionate has been the most generally accepted emendation of Qq’s short line (see textual n. and conj., ), although as Knowles (privately) suggests, no added word is needed if the first two words are each given dramatic emphasis. OED (Passionate, a. 1c), quoting this instance first (as 1590): Of language, etc.: Marked by angry passion, angry, wrathful. White (ed. 1858) says: This is one of those corrections which are important as showing the authority of the folio. Furness (ed. 1895): The omission of this emphatic word in Q2, from which the Folio was printed, is another cumulative proof that this Qto had been a play-house copy, and had in it omissions supplied and corrections made, before it came to be used as the original from which the Folio was set up. But Ridley (ed. 1934): Any reader’s own conjecture has as much authority as this facile padding. Andrews (ed. 1993): Sh. could well have wanted a dramatic interruption of metrical regularity here. Marshall (1982, p. 560): [Hermia] is amazed…both at Helena’s paranoid assumption that there is a conspiracy to persecute her and at the passionate expression of her love.

1247 amazed]
Johnson (1755, Amaze, 3): To put into perplexity. Deighton (ed. 1891): Utterly bewildered. Verity (ed. 1912) adds: A stronger word then than now. But OED (ppl. a. 4), quoting this instance, gives a milder meaning: Lost in wonder or astonishment. Hermia utters the same phrase at 1384+1. Johnson (ed. 1894): Three syllables.

1249 set]
OED (v.1 112 a.): To put (a person) to a task.

1249 as in]
Thirlby (MS 1747–53): By way of. OED (As, adv. 33a) calls this use almost pleonastic.

1249 scorne,]
Thiselton (1903, p. 52) notes that Q2 and F1 omit the comma (see textual n.): I think, so that it may be clear that these words are in connection with what follows rather than with what precedes.

1252 euen but now]
Abbott §38: Scarcely longer ago than the present. Deighton (ed. 1891): Only a moment ago (OED, But, prep. 6b). Gill (ed. 1981): Very recently. According to Abbott §130 but is here used redundantly. Wright (ed. 1877) compares the same phrase in Ham. 1.1.81 (98)

1252 euen]
See n. 581.

1252 spurne]
See n. 584.

1253 goddesse…diuine]
See n. 1162.

1254 Pretious celestiall]
Nearly all editors follow Q2 in providing a comma, understanding both words as adjectives (see textual n.). However, Pretious could be an adverb (OED, C. adv. a.), or celestiall could be a noun (OED, n. 1a). Cf. 1162.

1255–6 wherfore…soule]
Delius (ed. 1859): Why does Lysander deny his love for you, that so abundantly fills his soul (Ger.). On the construction your loue, see Abbott §219; Franz §322.

1257 forsooth]
See n. 445.

1257 affection]
Quadrisyllabic: see n. 159.

1258 setting on]
Johnson (1755, Set on, 48): To instigate; to incite. McDonald (ed. 2000): Prompting. See OED, Set, v.1 148 set on. c. (b).

1259 VVhat, though]
The comma is an error, as all editors but Sisson have recognized. For the construction, see n. 764. For a different construction with what, see n. 1301, 1303.

1259 so in grace]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Looked upon with such favour (OED, Grace, n. 7).

1260 hung vpon]
Most commentators seem to follow Schmidt (1874, Hang 2 [intr.] b): With about and on = to cling to, citing this instance, and quoting Shr. 2.1.308 (1188), Ado 1.1.86–7 (81), and Ham. 1.2.143 (327), all of which are quoted in later commentary. Allen (in Furness ed. 1895) compares Son. 31.10 and JC 1.1.69 (77)—but he may have taken both quotations from Schmidt (1874, Hang, tr. 1e). Perhaps the phrase means decorated with the adornments of love, as in OED, Hang 5: To decorate with things.

1261 But…vnlou’d]
Deighton (ed. 1891): But suffering from that worst of miseries, the misery of loving without being loved in return.

1263 this.]
Wilson (ed. 1924) reads Q1’s punctuation as a comma, saying [this] suggests that part of Hermia’s original speech may have been cancelled. But the terminal punctuation is clearly a Gothic period in all eight surviving copies of Q1.

1264 I doe.]
Almost all editors follow Q2 in modifying the punctuation and reading I as an affirmative Ay (see textual n.). Ay is spelled I in all twelve places where it is used in Q1.
