Hunter (1845, 1:296) calls attention to Q2’s bad reading, and Scott (ed. 1898) calls Q1’s a possible reading, but only Andrews (ed. 1993) restores Q1, explaining: Helena’s brief sentence can be read two ways: (a) I do [understand], or (b) Ay, do [continue this sport at my expense]. OED (Do, v. 32), supports the majority, quoting this instance, from F1: Do, the imperative, was used absolutely, as a word of encouragement or incitement = Go on! go it! OED gives only two other instances,—one from c. 1440 and one from Tmp. 4.1.239 (1913).

1264 Perseuer]
Johnson (1755, Persevere): <…This word was anciently accented less properly on the second syllable.> To persist in an attempt; not to give over (OED, etym. note, and 1). Steevens (ed. 1773) compares AWW 4.2.36–7 (2061–2). Abbott §492 quotes this instance as an example of accentuation of the second syllable in former times when the accent was nearer the beginning than with us.

1264 counterfait]
Holzknecht & McClure (ed. 1937): Pretend (OED, v. 4). Fairchild (1937, p. 109): In London, Shakespeare came into an atmosphere permeated with talk about painting. He adapted himself to the vogue and, after the fashion of the day, played with conceits on such terms as counterfeit, substance, and shadow [cf. 1322, 1405].

1264 sad]
Thirlby (MS 1747–53): Grave. Johnson (1755, 1; 2; 3): 1. Sorrowful; 2. Gloomy; 3. Serious. See 1487. Cf. nn. 422 (saddest), 1613.

1265 Make mouthes]
Johnson (1755, Mouth 6), quoting this instance: Distortion of the mouth; wry face, in this sense, is said to make mouths. OED (Mouth, n. 4b), quoting this instance: To make a…mouth: to express disapproval, derision, etc., by distorting or putting awry one’s mouth, to grimace. Wright (ed. 1877): Make faces at me in scorn, quoting Ham. 4.4.50 [Q2 K3v]. Some earlier texts, from 1793–1839, substitute mows (grimaces) for mouthes: see textual n.

1266 Winke…vp]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Encourage each other in keeping up the fine joke you have between you.

1266 Winke]
Johnson (1755, 2): To hint, or direct by the motion of the eyelids (OED, v.1 7a). Brooks (ed. 1979): Tip each other the wink.

1266 holde…vp]
Johnson (1755, Hold, 31): To Hold up. To sustain; to support (OED, Hold, v. 44b, quoting this instance). Thirlby (MS 1725–33) compares Wiv. 5.5.105 (2588): hold up the jest no higher. Wright (ed. 1877): keep it going, carry it on; he quotes Ado 2.3.121–2 (955): He hath ta’en the infection: hold it up; that is, keep up the sport.

1266 ieast]
OED (Jest, n. 7, quoting this instance): A sportive action, prank, frolic; a trick played in sport.

1267 sport]
See n. 1221.

1267 carried]
Johnson (1755, 12): To manage (OED, Carry, v. 22a, quoting this instance first). Wright (ed. 1877) compares Ado 4.1.210–11 (1874–5): Marry, this well carried shall…Change slander to remorse.

1267 chronicled]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Thought worthy of being recorded as a good story.

1268–9 If you haue…would]
On the irregularity of mood or tense in conditional sentences see Abbott §370–1. Thirlby (MS 1725–33) suggests substituting had, comparing the use of the past tense in 1176 (If you were men,…You would), but only two editors actually emend (see textual n.).

1268 grace]
Johnson (1755, 14): Virtue; goodness. OED (n. 13b) adds: Sense of duty. Deighton (ed. 1891): Good feeling.

1269 argument]
Johnson (ed. 1765): Subject of light merriment (OED 6, quoting 1H4 2.4.281–2 [1236–7], the argument shall be, thy running away. Caldecott (MS 1813–33) compares Ado 1.1.255–6 (246–7). White (ed. 1858): Theme of such cruel ridicule. The Clarkes (1879, p. 530): Subject for mockery. McDonald (ed. 2000): Butt of a joke.

1270 fare ye well]
See n. 624.

1270 fault]
Rann (ed. 1787): For loving Demetrius.

1271 Which…remedy]
Wells (ed. 1967): The exaggerated threat helps to preserve the comic tone. Andrews (ed. 1993, at 1270): Helena now sees herself as faced with choices that recall the ones offered Hermia in [92–9].

1272 excuse]
OED (n. 2a), quoting this instance: A plea in extenuation of an offence. Kittredge (ed. 1939): Defence, exculpation.

1273 My…Helena]
Cf. Pyramus addressing Thisbie: My Goddesse cheefe, my loue, my life and all (A gorgious Gallery, 1578, O2v).

1273 soule]
OED (n. 7 fig.), quoting this instance: Applied to persons: a. As a term of endearment or adoration.

1275 Sweete…so]
Steevens (MS 1773–8, p. 66): This speech of Hermia is addressed to Lysander, she still thinking him to be only joking with Helena.

1276 she]
Hunter (ed. 1870): Hermia.

1276 entreat]
Johnson (1755, 2): To prevail upon by solicitation (OED 10).

1276 I can compell]
Deighton (ed. 1891): I can force Lysander to desist from mocking Helena.

1278 praise]
The almost universally accepted emendation prayers (see textual n.), which Thirlby (MS 1747–53) claims originated with him, was first adopted by Theobald (ed. 1733), who explains: There is a design’d Antithesis betwixt compel and entreat: this Contrast of Terms is wanting, in Threats and Praise: wherefore We need make no Difficulty of substituting Prayers; his further suggestion that the intended word might have been a Substantive plural, (from the verb, to pray) Prays found favor with Capell (ed. 1768 and 1783 [1780], 2:3.109) and Malone (1783, p. 10) (see OED, Pray, n.1). Andrews (ed. 1989): The actor playing Lysander could convey this sense [of prays] with a prayer-like gesture. For other conjectural emendations, see . The only half-hearted support for praise comes from Caldecott (MS 1813–33): The feeble efforts of her flattering endearments.

1279 by…life]
A strong oath: see OED, Life, n. 3d, quoting AYL 4.1.158 (2067). See 1310.

1280 by that]
Delius (ed. 1859): By my life (Ger.).

1280 loose]
See n. 123.

1282 I say…do]
Cf. the competitiveness between Palamon and Arcite in declaring the extent of their love for Emelye (Chaucer, KnT 1112–3; see ). Brooks (ed. 1979) cites specifically KnT 1116.

1282–3 do…to]
Ness (1941, p. 50): This rhyme, after many lines of blank verse, might seem to indicate the absurdity of the fight. See also n. 514–15.

1283 withdrawe…it]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Walk aside with me and prove it in mortal combat. Johnson (ed. 1894): Hermia at once clasps Lysander to keep him from going, on hearing these words [the first SD indicating this action is at 1285].

1284 Quick]
For quick used adverbially and imperatively see OED, adv. 1a and 2; Abbott §1; Franz §241(b)

1285 whereto…this]
Lagae (2002, p. 65) suggests that the same question, in its double meaning of significance and direction [de sens et d’orientation], might be asked of MND (Fr.). Jennens (MS 1733–73) conjectures a SD here (see textual n.).

1286–8 Away…follow]
Many editors believe this passage corrupt because the verse line 1286–7 is short, because of the change in address from heele to you, and because of the break between subject and verb from 1287–8 (see textual n. and ). The belief that, as Clark & Wright (ed. 1863) say, some words, perhaps a whole line, have fallen out of the text, encouraged a plethora of exuberant emendation and conjecture. There is, however, no need to emend the Q1 text. 1286–7 is an adequate iambic pentameter if Ethiop is trisyllabic and heele is pronounced as two syllables (on expanded contractions see Abbott §510, and cf. Alexander, ed. 1951, he will). Wright (1968, p. 40) explains the action intended in the change of address: Lysander and Demetrius are about to fight but Hermia runs to stop Lysander. Demetrius first tells her that she needn’t struggle to hold Lysander back, he’ll only put up a show of breaking away from her; then he turns to Lysander himself and taunts him. For similar explanations see Hunter (ed. 1870), Capell (1783, 2.3:110) and Halliwell (ed. 1856).
While some emendations serve only to smooth the versification, others affect interpretation and action and, since many derive from F1, deserve consideration. F1’s substitution of Sir for heele removes the abrupt transition in address and changes Seeme to an imperative. Pope (ed. 1725) by emending you to hee in 1288 delays Demetrius’s contemptuous taunting of Lysander until the second half of 1289. Sisson (1956, 1:129), approving this emendation without reference to Pope: The change in address comes after a series of sarcastic comments, with the sudden direct insult: You are a tame man, go! And the speech takes on full life and action. Malone (ed. 1790), combining Q and F (he’ll…Sir): Demetrius, I suppose, would say, No, no; he’ll not have resolution to disengage himself from Hermia. But turning abruptly to Lysander, he addresses him ironically:—Sir, seem to break loose; &c. Steevens (ed. 1793), also combining Q and F but as sir;…he will, asserts Demetrius first addresses Lysander: Lysander calls Hermia an Æthiop. No, no, sir: replies Demetrius; i.e. she is none; and then ironically speaks to her of Lysander, as of one whose struggle to break loose is merely a pretended effort. He next addresses his provocation personally to Lysander. Furness (ed. 1895) objects: Demetrius shows no such zeal [in defending Hermia] when Lysander afterward showers opprobious epithets on the damsel. To my ears No, no, Sir is a taunting sneer. Joicey (1893, p. 102) proposes giving the hemistich to Hermia. It seems probable that she would make some reply to Lysander; and in her fear what is more likely than that she would exclaim, No, no, he’ll kill you, or some such thing? Then before she finishes Demetrius interrupts with his taunts. Taylor (Crux, 1985, p. 48–9) argues that the whole speech is addressed to Demetrius, and assumes double error in Q1 itself: one short word omitted [sir], and the next [heele] misread. The argument for his proposed emendation is approvingly summarized by Holland (ed. 1994): No, no, sir, yield, corrects the metre by incorporating F’s Sir as an incomplete correction derived from manuscript and construes heele as a perfectly easy misreading of yeeld. Yield is Demetrius’ mocking suggestion to Lysander to give up struggling to get free of Hermia, as he is presumably trying to do. Greenblatt (ed. 1997), whose text is based on Wells & Taylor (ed. 1986), follows the emendation, but in a marginal note indicates that the imperative yield is addressed to Hermia.

1286 Away]
The Clarkes (1879, p. 458): Said loathingly. See also 1139.

1286 Ethiop]
Trisyllabic. OED (A. n.): = Ethiopian; hence, usually, a person with a black skin, a blackamoor. Delius (ed. 1859): Black in Sh. is usually a sign of feminine ugliness. In the same sense, with regard to dark colour is tawny Tartar [1294] (Ger.). Wright (ed. 1877): Hermia…was a brunette, quoting LLL 4.3.262–4 (1615–17). Neil (ed. 1878): Sunburnt, swart, tawny, resembling an inhabitant of Ethiopia, and therefore displeasing. Verity (ed. 1893): A term of contempt.…In Elizabeth’s reign it was the fashion to depreciate dark complexions and hair, probably out of compliment to the queen herself, who was fair. Rolfe (ed. 1877) cites Rom. 1.5.46 (619) and quotes the adjectival use from AYL 4.3.35 (2184). Fripp (1930, p. 121) mentions the Ethiopian as the source of a frequent pleasantry citing Ovid, Met. 2.235–8 [cf. Golding, Ovid, 2.299–301, ], LLL 4.3.116 (1455), TGV 2.6.26 (955), Ado 5.4.38 (2593), Per. 2.2.20 (730), and the same two as in Rolfe. Foakes (ed. 1984) compares Tilley (E186): To wash an Ethiope white and Jer. 13.23: Can the Ethiopian change his skin. K. Hall (1995, p. 1) argues unconvincingly that this epithet should be seen in the context of the elements of race, sexual politics, imperialism, and slavery, which form a prominent set of subtexts to the play.

1288 take on]
Rann (ed. 1787): Affect, make slight effort; Moberly (ed. 1881): Pretend (OED, v. 18d). Schmidt (1875, Take III. intr. 3): With on, = to be furious, to chafe, to fret, quoting also Wiv. 3.5.39 (1713) (OED 86j). J. D. (1880, p. 305) objects: In Wiv. Mrs. Ford affected to be very angry with her men and to scold them vehemently, but she did not chafe or fret. Deighton (ed. 1891): Behave in a furious manner. Kittredge (ed. 1939): Make a fuss as if. Wright (1968, p. 41): Put on an act. Brooks (ed. 1979): Rant and rage; cf. our colloquial: she does take on so!

1288 as]
For the implied if see n. 537.

1289 tame]
Johnson (1755, 2): Spiritless; heartless. Wright (ed. 1877): Cowardly (OED, a. 4).

1289 go]
Wright (ed. 1877): Be off with you, comparing Rom. 1.5.86 (663), You are a princox, go; and H5 5.1.69–70 (2965). Both Shn. quotations are in OED, Go, v. 21d. Kittredge (ed. 1939): A contemptuous exclamation.

1290–7 Hang…you]
The frequent spondaic variation makes these lines emphatic.

1290 Hang of]
OED (v. 25 Hang off a), quoting this instance only: To cease to cling; to leave hold. Craig (ed. 1931): Let go.

1290 of]
On the variant spelling of off see n. 561.

1290 thou…bur]
Chambers (ed. 1897): The point is in the way Hermia is clinging to him.

1290 cat]
Halliwell (ed. 1856), comparing Tmp. 2.2.83 (1125): A term of contempt (OED, n.1 2 fig. a). Deighton (ed. 1891) compares AWW 4.3.264 (2366). Wentersdorf (1983, pp. 372–3), quoting this instance: It may signify a sexually aggressive woman (cf. OED, n.1 2 fig. b: Slang. A prostitute. Obs.).

1290 bur]
Johnson (1755), quoting this instance: A rough head of a plant, which sticks to the hair or cloaths. OED (n. 3 fig.), quoting this instance first: That which clings like a bur; a thing or person difficult to get rid of or shake off. Proverbial; (Tilley (B724), quoting this instance: To stick like Burs. Kittredge (ed. 1939) compares Webster, The White Devil, 5.1.92, 93 (ed. Lucas, I, 166, 167): Women are like to burres; Where their affection throwes them, there they’l sticke. Brooks (ed. 1979): In Peele, Old Wives Tale (ll. 223 f.), Lampriscus complains that his handsome daughter hangs on me like a burre.

1290 vile]
See n. 246.

1291 shake…serpent]
Green (1870, pp. 342–3) parallels Acts 28:5 where Paul shooke off the Viper into the fire. Knowles (privately): May it not mean simply something loathesome as a serpent, to be flung off? Cf. 801, 804; Cor. 1.8.3 (727), etc.?

1292 rude]
Johnson (1755, 1): Rough; savage; coarse of manners; uncivil; brutal (OED, a. 4b, quoting this instance first). See also n. 528.

1294–5 Out…Out]
Cf. 1088 and n.

1294–5 Out…hence]
Pope (ed. 1725) smoothed the meter by omitting ô (see textual n.). Knowles (privately): No Elizabethan actor would have been surprised or troubled by an extra midline exclamation.

1294 tawny]
Furness (ed. 1895): Another reference to Hermia’s brunette complexion. Andrews (ed. 1989): Brown-skinned. See also 355, 943.

1294 Tartar]
Andrews (ed. 1989): Turk. Idem (ed. 1993): Lysander is probably punning on tar. Mowat & Werstine (ed. 1993): Gypsy. OED (n.2 2c), quoting this instance first: As an opprobrious appellation. But the best definition, not applied to this instance, is from OED (n.2 3 fig. a): A savage; a person supposed to resemble a Tartar in disposition; a rough and violent or irritable and intractable person: when applied to a female, a vixen, a shrew, a termagant, with the first quotation dated 1663. See also 1124.

1295 medcine…potion]
Doran (ed. 1959): Both often used synonymously with poison (OED, Medicine, n.1 3; Potion 1). Walter (ed. 1964): Perhaps a suggestion that Hermia had acted on him like a drug or love potion. If so, it is an ironic comment in view of the potion on his eyes. Foakes (ed. 1984), questionably: Referring to her love with which she seeks to cure his harshness.

1295 medcine]
Johnson (1755, Medicine): It is generally pronounced as if only of two syllables, med’cine (OED, Medicine, n.1, etym. note). See Abbott §467; Franz §50. Sh. often uses the word with the sense of a drug with magic powers: cf. AWW 5.3.102 (2814), 1H4 2.2.18 (754) and 2.2.20 (755), Oth. 1.3.61 (398), Ant. 1.5.36 (565).

1295 hence]
See n. 573.

1296 Doe…ieast?]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Surely you must be jesting. Cf. WT 2.1.58 (658). Granville-Barker (ed. 1924, p. li), questionably: The not must be strongly emphasized.

1297 sooth]
OED (adv.): Truly; in truth…used interjectionally. See also 784.

1297 and]
For and meaning almost…exactly,…frequently found before so and after ejaculations, such as sooth see Abbott §§97, 98.

1297 so…you]
Kittredge (ed. 1939): Helena still regards Hermia as in league with Lysander and Demetrius to make sport of her [1219 ff.].

1298–9 I…bond]
Dent compares (M458): An honest Man’s word is as good as his bond.

1298 keepe my word]
See n. 235. Clemen (ed. 1963): My promise to fight with you.

1299–1300 I would…you]
Deighton (ed. 1891): I wish I had something more than your word,—your bond; for I see (alluding to Hermia’s throwing her arms round Lysander and so detaining him) you are easily held by a bond. Furness (ed. 1895): Demetrius scornfully intimates that Lysander, from cowardice, does not really wish to be free. This explains Lysander’s vehement reply.

1299–1300 bond…bond]
Rolfe (ed. 1877): For other instances of play upon the word, see [Err. 4.4.125 (1417)] and [Cym. 5.4.28 (3063)]. Wells (ed. 1967): Both pledge and tie (here, Hermia, who is holding Lysander). Wilson (ed. 1968, Glossary): (a) Signed contract, (b) fetter.

1301, 1303 What?]
For the construction and tone, see n. 436. Furness (ed. 1895): In the way of punctuation, I prefer the interrogative What? of Q1 to the What! of Collier and the What, of all the rest. See textual n. However, all other instances of the construction in MND are punctuated with a comma (436, 806, 807, 892, 1316, 1320, 1355, 1537, 2082, 2116).

1301 kill…dead]
OED (Kill, v. 2c): With complement expressing the result: to kill dead. Page (ed. 1897): For this pleonastic expression compare [Tit. 3.1.92 (1233)]: he kill’d me dead. Cf. humane mortals at 476.

1303 What…hate]
Deighton (ed. 1891): I.e. you need not be scrupulous about striking me, for no personal injury you can do me will be worse than your hatred. Verity (ed. 1893): It is now that Hermia realises the change in Lysander.

1304 wherefore]
Probably accented on the second syllable, as Seymour (1805, 1:46–7) notes, citing Err. 3.1.39 (664). Rolfe (ed. 1877) cites Abbott §490, who quotes this instance. On the variable accent, see also OED, adv. Pronunciation.

1304 what newes]
Collier (1853, pp. 105–6) explains his emendation (see textual n.): News was formerly spelt newes, and so it stands in the folios, and the printer or copyist misread meanes newes. Hermia’s question ought, indisputably, to be,—What means my love? which is a natural inquiry for an explanation why Lysander had abandoned her. White (1854, p. 215) objects to the emendation: This is Hermia’s first interview with her former lover since Puck’s application of the flower to his eyes; and she may well express surprise at the novelty of his declaration that he hates her. Halliwell (ed. 1856): In other words, what novelty is this?; he calls the emendation very plausible, but unnecessary. Dyce (ed. 1857, 1:cxciv) quotes Shr. 1.1.224–5 (531–2), what’s the news, saying it makes Collier’s alteration a very doubtful one. Schmidt (1875) paraphrases: What is the matter. Defending Collier, Kinnear (1883, pp. 90–1): The phrase what’s the news? in Shakespeare…invariably asks for information.…Hermia had only too much information; her only hope is that it does not mean what it says. Adopting the emendation, Irving & Marshall (ed. 1888): Hermia, in this speech, is under the influence of strong emotion. She is shocked at Lysander saying that he hates her. Is it likely, under such circumstances, that she would employ such a colloquial phrase as What news?…The exclamation O me! is…a cry of real mental anguish; and I cannot think anyone with a due sense of dramatic fitness would admit the reading what news? Deighton (ed. 1891): That is a strange story to tell me (sc. that you hate me). Furness (ed. 1895): It seems to have been generally supposed that What news? can be uttered only in an idle, indifferent way, but it is conceivable that very tragic pathos can be imparted to the word news. Moreover, the continuity of thought upholds the original text in contrasting the new present with the old past: I am as fair now as I was, [1306] &c. Above all, the sound rule that durior lectio preferenda est [the more difficult reading is to be preferred] should be ever present. Lee (ed. 1907): What new-fangled notion. Crook (1914, pp. 106–7), deleting the comma after newes, News is a transitive verb, and the question means, What revolutionizes my love?—which is exactly the sense required. But the latest quotation given by OED for new, v. is a1555, and the earliest quotation for news, v. is 1650. Castelain (ed. 1943): What news = Quid novi, what’s new? (whence comes this change) (Fr.). Andrews (ed. 1989): Suddenly Hermia realizes that Lysander has some bad news for her: he has not been merely pretending affection for Helena. See n. 27.

1305 Am…Lysander]
Calderwood (1965, p. 513), quoting this line: The breakdown of sexual relationships noted earlier [see n. 1242] becomes part of a general dissolving of past identities, analogous to the actor’s submerging of his personal identity in a fictional role. Girard (1979, p. 203) also views this as a crisis of identity.

1306 ere while]
Johnson (1755, Erewhile), quoting this instance: Some time ago; before a little while (OED, adv.). Wright (ed. 1877), citing AYL 2.4.89 (874–5): A short time since, just now.

1307 Since…left mee]
Moberly (ed. 1881): In the course of this night, not since the end of the night, as Puck presently says that the dawn has not yet come [1420]. Kittredge (ed. 1939): Not so long ago as the beginning of this night. The second since night means in the course of this same night. Gill (ed. 1981): Since night fell. Rolfe (ed. 1877): For the tense after since, see [Abbott §]132, 347. Cf. [n. 525].

1308 ô…forbid]
Andrews (ed. 1993) compares the very different tone this kind of expression has in 841 and 1759–60.

1310 I]
Andrews (ed. 1993) alone: Both (a) I [did], and (b) Ay. For I as Ay see n. 617.

1310 by my life]
Ironically he uses the same oath to confirm his love for Helena at 1279.

1312 Therefore…doubt]
Many eds. follow Pope (ed. 1725), treating question as a disyllable and omitting the third of in order to make a decasyllabic line; see Abbott §456 for the quasi-suppression of of. See textual n. Lettsom (in Dyce, ed. 1864) compares 617 for the omission of the preposition. But many agree with White (ed. 1858): This line is an alexandrine—question being here used as a trisyllable. Brooks (ed. 1979): The emphasis with which Lysander dwells on each of the three parallel phrases carries and is even helped by the disturbance this of makes in the metre. Walker (1860, 3:49) places Therefore on a separate line. Moberly (ed. 1881) suggests omitting of hope. See .

1315–18 O…him]
Castelain (ed. 1943): Instead of accusing the man she loves of inconstancy, Hermia rounds on Helena, charging her with deceit and theft. A very feminine twist which turns the quarrel in a new direction (Fr.). Holland (ed. 1994): Hermia’s accusation is strikingly like Egeus’ of Lysander in 1.1 [38–40, 44]; both Lysander and Helena are accused of stealing love by night. Cf. Andrews (ed. 1989) commenting on 1315. Winter (2003, p. 91) also connects these words with Hermia’s refusal to believe that Lysander could willingly have stollen away (1074) from her (Fr.). See also Maguin at n. 800–5.

1315 O…blossome]
A few find a syllable lacking and emend (see textual n.) while most (including Abbott §477) agree with Thirlby (MS 1725–33): Jugler [is] pronounc’d as a trisyllable. Cf. n. 1856, wōdrous. But Furness (ed. 1895): A needless deformity, when an exclamation-mark can take the place of a syllable.

1315 iuggler]
Johnson (1755, 2), quoting this instance: A cheat; a trickish fellow (OED (n. 3), quoting this instance). Cf. Err. 1.2.97–8 (263–4), 5.1.239–40 (1715–16) and 2H4 2.4.131–2 (1158). Scot (1584, Bk. 13 Ch. 22, 2C1r): The endeuor and drift of iugglers is onelie to abuse mens eies and iudgements.

1315 canker blossome]
Many agree with Steevens (ed. 1773): Not in this place the blossom of the canker or wild rose…but a worm that preys on the leaves or buds of flowers (OED, Canker 8, quoting this instance only). Cf. cankers in 653. Very few follow Delius (ed. 1859): A blossom eaten by a worm (Ger.), comparing TGV 1.1.42–3 (46–7): In the sweetest bud The eating canker dwells. Schmidt (1874, Canker-blossom): A blossom eaten by a canker; or the same as canker-bloom [i.e., the wild rose]. Wright (ed. 1877): Generally taken to mean a blossom eaten by a canker, having a show of fairness but hollow within. But it is probably…equivalent to blossom-cankerer; Hermia comparing Helena to a canker that has stealthily eaten into and destroyed Lysander’s love for her. Furness (ed. 1895), referring to Capell (1783, 2.3:110) who sees canker as a verb: You who cankers blossoms. A few follow Chambers (ed. 1897): This may mean either (1) a worm i’ the bud…; or it may be (2) the blossom of the dog-rose, Rosa canina [OED, Canker, n. 5].…If this is the meaning…Hermia’s point will be that Helena has juggled herself into Lysander’s affections, and is as poor a substitute for her rival, as the canker-blossom [the wild rose, the dog-rose, etc.] is for the garden rose. He compares Son. 54.1–8 and 1H4 1.3.175–6 (498–9).

1316, 1320 what, What,]
For the construction and tone, see n. 436.

1318 Fine, I faith]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Truly a fine apostrophe that! Andrews (ed. 1989): Fine, indeed! Helena assumes that the ruse has simply entered another phase, with Hermia now pretending to believe not only that Lysander loves Helena but that Helena has deliberately stolen his love away from Hermia.

1318 I faith]
See n. 309.

1320 touch]
OED (n. 19): A slight amount or trace. Verity (ed. 1893): Particle. Thirlby (MS 1733–47): I.e. feeling, sense (OED, n. 14). Rann (ed. 1787), comparing Tmp. 5.1.21 (1971): Sense; spice. Deighton (ed. 1891), comparing TN 2.1.13 (621–2) and H5 4.Chor.47 (1836): Spice, smack. Possibly also tincture or stain, suggesting a blush; cf. OED, n. 2c.

1320–1 What…tongue]
Deighton (ed. 1891): What, are you determined by your abuse to compel impatient answers from one so gentle of speech as you know me to be? Vienne-Guerrin (2002, p. 32) notes that Helena presents her injury as a transgression of the rules of courtesy (Fr.).

1322 counterfait]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Pretended friend of mine. Cf. TGV 5.4.53 (2172). Holzknecht & McClure (ed. 1937): Imitation in miniature. Righter (1962, pp. 91–2), citing also Oth. 5.1.43 (3133) and TGV 2.4.10–12 (664–6), notes that though often signifying simple imitation: Counterfeit is also…a virtual synonym in Elizabethan English for the actor and his art.…Once involved, however, with the idea of malicious deceit, counterfeit tends to darken, to acquire connotations of treachery. Walter (ed. 1964): Play-acting cheat. Helena may be calling her a pretender or an imposter (OED, n. 2), or even a deformed or misshapen person (OED, n. 4) where the quotation from 1578 combines Dwarfes (cf. 1329 and 1364) and other deformed counterfeits.

1322 you puppet]
Johnson (1755, 1; 2): 1. A small image moved by men in a mock drama.…2. A word of contempt, quoting TGV 2.1.94–5 (487). Deighton (ed. 1891): You doll, who in the hands of others are made to play any part they like. Chambers (ed. 1897): You doll that dost ape humanity. Doran (ed. 1959): (1) Doll, mere counterfeit of a woman (2) little person (OED 1). Walter (ed. 1964): (a) dwarf, (b) despicable actress, i.e. in playing a part according as the plot of the three would have the strings pulled. Evans (ed. 1974): Hermia takes it as a reference to her small stature. Gaubert (1945, p. 204), citing also Lr. 2.2.36 (1109) and Ant. 5.2.208 (3450): Sh. does not hold puppetry in esteem, because it is an intermediate form of art, infantile, frivolous, and mean (Fr.).

1323 Puppet…so?]
Deighton (ed. 1891): What makes you call me a puppet? What is the point of your calling me a puppet? Kittredge (ed. 1939), who in his edition of 1936 changed the punctuation after so to an exclamation point (see textual n.): Ironical—Very well! Good! Brooks (ed. 1979) approves the change: Hermia is unlikely to ask Why so? (Why do you term me puppet?): she knows the excuse for the insult only too well. But Foakes (ed. 1984), adhering to Q1’s text: Hermia is taken aback by Helena’s use of puppet, her first term of contempt. And Holland (ed. 1994), also following the punctuation of Q1: I prefer a Hermia who takes a moment to make the mental leap from understanding a puppet as a counterfeit human to its being a comment on her size.

1323 I…game]
Johnson (1755, Game 2; 6; 7; 8): 2. Jest.…6. Scheme pursued.…7. The chase.…8. Animals pursued (OED 2a, 5a, 9, 10a). Schmidt (1874, Game, 6), quoting this instance, paraphrases: (= this it is you aim at). Deighton (ed. 1891): Discovering, as she thinks, Helena’s meaning, she adds, ah, now I see the game you are playing. Lever (ed. 1961): That is the direction your thoughts are following. game: birds or beasts to be hunted; as if Helena’s thoughts are following the idea of smallness like a hunter pursuing game. Thirlby (MS 1747–53) quotes 3H6 4.5.14 (2358–9): Huntsman. This way, my Lord, for this way lies the game. Proverbial; Dent (W144.1).

1324–5 Now…height]
Seymour (1805, 1:47) wonders if this is a reference to the jealous coquettry of Queen Elizabeth, displayed in her recorded conversation with Sir John [i.e. James] Melville, about Mary of Scotland? It would doubtless have been a very dangerous allusion. When Melville was sent to reconcile Elizabeth to Mary’s marriage to Darnley in 1565, Elizabeth asked which of the two Queens was the fairest, and also inquired which of them was of highest stature? I said, my Queen. Then, saith she, she is too high, for I my self am neither too high nor too low (Memoirs, 1683, p. 50). Although Melville’s recollections were recorded many years later, and not printed until 1683, it is tempting to speculate with Seymour that this episode may have been earlier repeated and remembered even in England.

1324 compare]
Johnson (1755, 1): Comparison (OED, n.2). On verbs used for nouns see Abbott §451.

1325 vrg’d]
Schmidt (1875, 5b): Allege, assert (OED, Urge, v. 1). Verity (ed. 1912): Dwelt on, emphasised. Andrews (ed. 1989): Put forward as a point in a debate. Mowat & Werstine (ed. 1993): Put forward as a recommendation.

1326 And…tall personage]
There is some disagreement on the accentuation of this line. Abbott §476 places the emphasis on the second personage: And wíth | her pérson | age, hér | tall pér | sonáge. But Irving & Marshall (ed. 1888): This line has a peculiar rhythm; it must be read thus: And with her pérsonage, her táll personage, the accent, with a slight pause, being on táll. Furness (ed. 1895) disagrees with Abbott: The two strongly emphasized words are, the first personage and tall. Cuningham (ed. 1905): There is no particular difficulty in the scansion of this line; nor is there any reason for special emphasis on the word tall. Knowles (privately): The humor of the line depends on a strong accent on tall, making the word a vituperation. The same must be true of height in the next line, stressed so as to imply grotesque altitude.

1326 personage]
Johnson (1755, 2), quoting this instance: Exteriour appearance; air; stature. Holland (ed. 1994), referring to OED (n. 3): A person of rank and stature. But OED (2b) is closer: A person of (such and such a) figure or appearance.

1326 tall personage]
Walter (ed. 1964): A mocking over emphasis. Personage was associated with dignity and importance (OED 3).

1327 forsooth]
See n. 445.

1328 growne so high]
Delius (ed. 1859): Double entendre = grown so tall and = having grown in appreciation, in contrast to the following double entendre on low (Ger.).

1329 dwarfish]
Johnson (1755), quoting this instance: Low; small; little; petty; despicable (OED, a., citing this instance).

1330 painted May-pole]
OED (1): A high pole, painted with stripes of different colours and decked with flowers, set upon a green or other open space, for the merrymakers to dance round on May-day; (1b), quoting this instance first: Transf. Applied jocularly to a tall object, esp. a tall slender…woman. Proverbial: Tilley (M778), quoting this instance. Steevens (MS 1793–1800) quotes Stubbes’s animadversions on the maypole and the customs associated with it (The Anatomie of Abuses, 1583, M3v). Halliwell (ed. 1856), referring to Falstaff calling himself a maypole in Wiv. Q1 (1602, G3v): The term maypole appears to have been generally one of contempt, applied not only to a tall person, but to any one who was the object of derision.…The reference [here] is obviously to the height of Helena, the immense length of the maypole rendering it an excellent object of the derisive comparison. Deighton (ed. 1891): In painted Hermia hints that Helena owes her complexion to art. More kindly, Furness (ed. 1895): An allusion to the clear red and white of Helena’s blonde complexion. Asimov (1970, p. 45): Not only does Hermia in this way refer disparagingly to Helena as tall and skinny (and perhaps with as little figure as a maypole), but she also implies that the men, Lysander and Demetrius, are dancing about her with immoral intent.

1333 gentleman]
All edd. follow Q2 in emending to gentlemen.

1334 curst]
Johnson (1755), quoting this instance: Froward; peevish; malignant; mischievous; malicious; snarling. Dodd (1780, 1:228): Shrewish. Rann (ed. 1787): Quarrelsome. Malone (ed. 1790): Perverse; wicked. Singer (ed. 1826): Cross, ill-conditioned, or ill-spoken. Staunton (ed. 1857): Irascible. The Clarkes (ed. 1864): Spiteful. Craig (ed. 1903): Savage, vicious. See OED, Cursed, curst, ppl. a. 4. See 1382, 1487. See also 1983.

1335 shrewishnesse]
Johnson (1755), quoting this instance: The qualities of a shrew; frowardness; petulance; clamorousness (OED, n. 2b, quoting this instance first). Mowat & Werstine (ed. 1993), comparing shrewd at 1359, note that this and curst were used to describe women who were considered quarrelsome, talkative, or sharp-tongued. Thiselton (1903, p. 54) mentions the possible connection with Aristotle’s shrewish wife; see n. DP 6, Hermia.

1336 right maid]
OED (Right, a. 17a): Having the true character of, true, real. Abbott §19, quoting also Ant. 4.12.28 (2784): real, down-right. Page (ed. 1897): A true or genuine maid; one having the modest, retiring, and timid disposition proper to the female nature. Clemen (ed. 1963): True young woman. Kittredge & Ribner (ed. 1966): Regular, ordinary girl. Mowat & Werstine (ed. 1993): I.e., a girl indeed, a real sissy. Bertram, Atwan & Roberts (ed. 1997): I.e. truly feminine.

1338 something]
Johnson (1755): In some degree. OED (B. adv. 1d) adds, to some extent; somewhat, rather, a little. See Abbott §68; Franz §§398, 453. Blake 3.3.3.6 (b) identifies something as an intensifier, but points out the ambiguity found here where something lower could be a shorter something, or somewhat shorter.

1338 my selfe]
See n. 126 (your selfe).

1339 match]
Johnson (1755, 3): To equal. The Clarkes (1879, p. 357): Be even with her, give her as good as she brings, OED (v.1 3a), quoting this instance: To encounter with equal power, prove a match for. Doran (ed. 1959): I.e. in a fight.

1340 Lower?]
Holland (ed. 1994): The punctuation could as easily be an exclamation mark (a furious shriek) as a question. Although printers frequently used queries for exclamation points, a question mark is sometimes a question mark.

1341–2 Hermia…Hermia]
The first is disyllabic, the second trisyllabic (see n. 29). The meter of 1341 also requires elision of -er in bitter with. The meter suggests the rapid delivery of 1341 and the slower pleading emphasis of 1342.

1341 do…mee]
OED (Do, v. 30d), quoting this instance first: As auxiliary…in the Imperative negative, do not. See Franz §601 Anm., quoting this instance; Blake 6.2.2 (iii), quoting this instance, and 1720–1.

1342 euermore]
Thiselton (1903, p. 54) gives MM 4.2.147 (2013) and other examples of evermore with a distinctly past reference. Contrast 1701.

1343 counsels]
See n. 229.

1344 Saue that]
Baker (ed. 1896): Except that (OED, Save, conj. 2).

1344 in]
Blake 5.4.2. In (e), quoting this instance: Because of (OED, Love, n.1 4a).

1345 stealth]
Wright (ed. 1877): Stealing away (OED, n. 3, quoting this instance first). Same at 1685.

1346 He…him]
An interestingly balanced line. As Delius (ed. 1859) points out, the QF punctuation ensures that for loue refers to Helena’s love, not Demetrius’s.

1346 for]
Johnson (1755, 1): Because of. Deighton (ed. 1891): Out of. See Abbott §150; Blake 5.4.2 For (a).

1347 chid]
Johnson (1755, 2), quoting 3H6 2.5.16–17 (1150–1): To drive away with reproof (OED, Chide, v. 4, quoting this instance first). For the curtailed form of the past participle see n. 121 (spoke).

1348 spurne mee]
See n. 584.

1348 nay]
See n. 847. Blake 5.1.3.6 (ii), quoting this instance: Nay can act as an intensifier meaning indeed (OED, adv.1 5a).

1349 so]
If: see n. 47 (Be it so).

1349 quiet]
Gill (ed. 1981): Peacefully.

1350–1 To…goe]
Brooks (ed. 1979): With Helena’s repentance that she ever entered the wood cf. the hero’s in Huon [see ]…Cf. Bottom, [966–7].

1352 simple]
The few who comment use Schmidt’s (1875, 5) term, Silly (OED, a. 10a), but the meaning seems closer to Johnson (1755, 1): Plain; artless; unskilled; sincere; harmless (OED, a. 1).

1352 fond]
In the four places where it is used the word is associated with Helena. See n. 743.

1353 get you gon]
See n. 573.

1355 What,]
For the construction and tone, see n. 436.

1357 Helena]
Disyllabic: see n. 166 (Hermia). For Walker’s emendation to Helen see textual n.

1358 No…part]
Mowat & Werstine (ed. 1993): she: i.e., Hermia; her: i.e., Helena’s.

1358 though…part]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Even though you espouse her cause…and are thus guilty of an officious piece of interference. As below, [1368–71], Demetrius resents even an act of kindness towards one whom he considers to belong entirely to himself, and whose cause he claims to uphold alone. Kittredge (ed. 1939): Thus in effect challenging him. Andrews (ed. 1993): Lysander is now holding Hermia (to keep her from attacking Helena), an amusing reversal of the previous situation but one that makes Demetrius assume that he is still evading a duel. Another possibility is that Lysander is now being clutched by Helena, or perhaps shielding Helena from Hermia’s threats.

1359 when she is]
The meter requires the pronunciation she’s, but no change in the text is necessary (see textual n.).

1359 keene…shrewd]
Kittredge (ed. 1939): Sharp of tongue and savage. Clemen (ed. 1963): Shrewish. Brooks (ed. 1979): Malicious. Cf. 403, 778, 1335 and nn.

1360 vixen]
Johnson (1755), quoting this instance: Vixen or fixen is the name of a she-fox; otherwise applied to a woman whose nature and condition is thereby compared to a she fox. Verstegan (Richard Verstegan, A Restitution of Decayed Intelligence, 1605, pp. 334–5, with minor changes). OED (n. 2), quoting this instance: An ill-tempered quarrelsome woman; a shrew, a termagant. Webb (1996, pp. 179–80): Although the emphasis here is on the fierceness, Helena also has in mind the fox’s untrustworthiness, citing 1381.

1361 though she be]
See n. 329–30.

1363 suffer]
Johnson (1755, 3): To allow; to permit (OED, v. 14a).

1363 floute]
Mock. See n. 783.

1364 Let…her]
Brooks (ed. 1979, p. cxiii): What a vixen she shows…herself,…clamouring to be let get at her tormentor.

1365–7 Get…acorne]
Pironon (1997, p. 264) quotes these lines in illustration of how diminution is used in MND as part of a process of euphemization, sometimes overwhelming meaning almost to the point of burlesque obsessiveness (Fr.).

1365 Get…gon]
See n. 573.

1365 dwarfe]
Cf. n. 1329.

1366 minimus]
Johnson (1755), quoting this instance: (Latin). A being of the least size (OED, n. 1, quoting this instance first). Theobald (ed. 1733): This is no Term of Art, that I can find; and I can scarce be willing to think, that Shakespeare would use the Masculine of an Adjective to a Woman. And he conjectures, You, Minim, you,— i.e. You Diminutive of the Creation, you Reptile, comparing Milton, Paradise Lost 7.482, Minims of Nature. Thirlby (MS 1747–53) objects: Dubitanter [doubtfully]. Upton (1748, pp. 308–10): Sh. uses…the Latin word itself…Minimus. [Quotes Theobald.] Mr. Theobald…might have remembered that the masculine gender is often used, where the person is considered more than the sex; as here ’tis by Shakespeare. Milton’s expression seems to be from Prov. [30:24], Quatuor ista sunt minima terræ [There be four things which are little upon the earth].… Minim in music is half a semibreve. Neil (ed. 1878) alters the text to Minima properly…in the feminine, not little [1361, 1362] only, but least. Sprague (ed. 1896) agrees, but suggests perhaps the humor of it is in making her a male for the nonce! Foakes (ed. 1984): This seems to be Shakespeare’s coinage, from minim meaning extremely small (compare minimal, which OED first records in 1666). Wells & Taylor (1987, p. 283): Johnson’s [1765] was the first edition not to italicize this word: this is its first occurrence in English literature, it exactly reproduces the Latin form, and was almost certainly perceived as Latin.

1366 hindring…grasse]
OED (n. 1), quoting this instance: The plant Polygonum aviculare, a common weed in waste ground, with numerous intricately-branched creeping stems, and small pale pink flowers; an infusion of it was formerly supposed to stunt the growth. Grey (1754, 1:xii, 61) quotes Dr Tathwell, a learned, and ingenious physician, who finds the term applies well to small-statured Hermia hanging upon Lysander because it is a low creeping plant…apt to entangle the feet of those that walk through it, and hinder them from passing freely. Another quality of the plant, also pertinent to Hermia’s littleness, is first given by Steevens (ed. 1773): [It] was anciently supposed to prevent the growth of any animal or child, quoting uses in two plays by Beaumont and Fletcher. Beisly (1864, p. 52): The allusion here made is to the character of the plant as hindering the growth of useful plants, as it spreads in thick masses, and is very tough and deep-rooted. Hunter (ed. 1870): So called from the [knotty] joints of its stem. Suggesting another example of hindering, Rolfe (ed. 1877, Addenda, p. 191) quotes a botanical authority who says that in the north of England, the plant being difficult to cut in the harvest time, or to pull in the process of weeding, it has obtained the soubriquet of the Deil’s-lingels, explaining that a lingel is a thong [or a waxed thread (OED, Lingel, n.1)] used by shoemakers. Furness (ed. 1895), referring to Grey suggests that since Hindering applies not only to knot-grass, but also to Hermia…[it is] a botanical pun. Webb (1991, p. 117) mentions two early Italian authorities who give details on children turned into dwarfs by knotgrass.

1367 bead]
Schmidt (1874, 2): Any thing extremely small, quoting this instance, and referring to the name of the fairy in Wiv. 5.5.49 (2531). OED (4a), quoting this instance: A small perforated body, spherical or otherwise, of glass, amber…used as an ornament. Walter (ed. 1964) adds: That which hangs round the neck (OED, 4b). Wright (ed. 1877): As beads were generally black, there is a reference here to Hermia’s complexion as well as to her size. An unsubstantiated claim.

1368–78 You…iowle]
Chambers (ed. 1897): The duel between Lysander and Demetrius for Hermia may be suggested by that between Palemon and Arcite for Emilia in the Knightes Tale. Brooks (ed. 1979) compares the rivals’ counterclaims and recriminations in Chaucer, and (later) their agreement to fight. See KnT 1128–43, 1845–71, , . See also n. 1200–1.

1368–9 You…seruices]
Deighton (ed. 1891): You put yourself forward a great deal too much in offering to help one who scorns both you and your offers of help.

1368 officious]
Johnson (1755, 2), quoting this instance: Importunely forward (OED, a. 3). Schmidt (1875): In a bad sense = obtruding one’s service, meddling. Holzknecht & McClure (ed. 1937): Zealous in duty (OED, a. 2).

1369 her…that]
On her as antecedent of the relative, see n. 90 (his). On that rather than who see Abbott §258–60.

1371 Take…part]
See n. 1358.

1371 intend]
OED (v. 18b): Purpose, design; cf. 514. Rann (ed. 1787): Make tender of. Steevens (ed. 1793): Pretend, comparing Ado 2.2.35–6 (813–14). Caldecott (MS 1813–33): Hold out, suffer to appear, comparing R3 3.7.45 (2258). Knight (ed. 1839): Direct. Evans (ed. 1974): Offer.

1372 Neuer so]
Johnson (1755, 2): A form of speech…lately accused, I think, with justice, of solecism and now…propriety requires it to be expressed thus…ever so (OED, Never, adv. 4). Abbott §52: Never is used where we now more commonly use ever. See Blake 5.1.3.6 (ii).

1375–6 to…Helena]
Delius (ed. 1859): Most refers to right: whose right to Helena, mine or yours, is the greatest (Ger.). Deighton (ed. 1891): To put to the test of combat the question which of us has the better claim to Helena: a confusion of two constructions (1) to try whose right, yours or mine, is most, etc. (2) to try which, of you and me (i.e. us), has most, etc. See Abbott §409; Blake 7.6 (p. 268). See 1755–6, 2046.

1376 Of]
Many of the earlier editors from Theobald (ed. 1733) to Craig (ed. 1891) adopt Thirlby’s (MS 1725–33) conjecture Or (see textual n.) but he himself compares Tmp. 2.1.28 (703) [Which, of he, or Adrian, for a good wager,], and in MS 1733–47 cites the Q1 reading. While adopting the emendation, Malone (ed. 1790): I am not sure that the old reading is corrupt. If the line had run—Of mine or thine, I should have suspected that the phrase was borrowed from the Latin:—Now follow, to try whose right of property,—of meum or tuum,—is the greatest in Helena. Delius (ed. 1859): Sh. uses of generally after an interrogative pronoun (Ger.).

1377 Nay]
See n. 847.

1377–8 cheeke…iowle]
Johnson (1755, Jole, 1), quoting this instance: The face or cheek. It is seldom used but in the phrase cheek by jole. Rann (ed. 1787): Side by side (OED, Cheek, n. 5a). See also OED, Jowl, n. 4, quoting this instance. Proverbial; Tilley C263, quoting this instance.

1379 coyle]
Johnson (1755, Coil, 1), quoting this instance: Tumult; turmoil; bustle; stir; hurry; confusion (OED, Coil, n.2 1, quoting Johnson). Rann (ed. 1787): Disturbance.

1379 long of]
Johnson (1755), quoting this instance: By the fault [of]; by the failure [of]. A word now out of use, but truly English. Rann (ed. 1787): Occasioned by. Caldecott (MS 1813–33): Owing to. Hudson (ed. 1880): Because of (OED, Long, a.2). Cf. Cym. 5.5.271 (3567), long of her. See Abbott §170.

1380 Nay]
See n. 309.

1380 goe not backe]
Scott (ed. 1898): Helena having lost the protection of the men is drawing back in fear. Bevington (ed. 1988): I.e., don’t retreat. (Hermia is again proposing a fight.)

1381 I…I]
Caldecott (MS 1813–33), unsupported: By the iteration of the pronoun, it was the usage of that day to express the utmost contempt. Wright (ed. 1877): Repeated for emphasis, quoting TGV 5.4.132 (2257). Blake 3.3.2.1 (g), quoting this instance, agrees with him, saying that the personal pronoun is repeated at the end of the clause for emphasis or possibly as a colloquialism. Helena may be mocking Hermia’s first and last word in 1379—You…you.

1382 curst]
Verplanck (ed. 1845): Many a modern reader may take this phrase as answering to the participle cursed, and will of course be shocked by its vulgar profanity in a lady’s mouth and a poetic scene. But the word curst, as used here and elsewhere by Shakespeare, had then the very common sense, now antiquated, of ill-tempered, malicious, shrewish. Andrews (ed. 1989): Probably both shrewish and cursed. See n. 1334.

1383–4+1 Your…say]
Ness (1941, p. 128) remarks that there are three triplets in this scene (1184–6 and 1191–3 are the others), all used for emphasis, here to put an end to the quarrel between the lovers. The third line, as in the previous triplet, is an answer to the first two.

1383–4+1 My…say]
Gentleman (ed. 1774): This line [1384], and the idea it contains by being exceedingly low, may create laughter, and the following one…gains some value from its insignificance. In representation the former had better be altered, and strengthened.

1384 to…away]
Deighton (ed. 1891): for running away.

1384+1 Her.…Exeunt.]
F1’s omission of this line (see textual n.) was noticed but not commented on until White (ed. 1858): The line is so unsuited to Hermia’s quickness of temper and tongue, to the state of her mind and to the situation, and so uncalled for by Helena’s speech which elicits it, that we should gladly accept the testimony of the authentic copy that it is either the interpolation of some player who did not want to leave the stage without a speech, or a piece of the author’s work which he cancelled as unsatisfactory or superfluous. Scott (ed. 1898) agrees. He finds the omitted line not highly appropriate; and the existence of a triplet is very unusual. Shakespeare may have cancelled it later without caring to supply any stage direction. However, Arnold (1911, p. 88) asserts it to be a rare instance of a line…serving as an exit speech…truly a soliloquy, and Ness (see n. 1383–4+1 above) sees it as the completion of a triple rhyme. It is not clear how it is uncharacteristic—as Granville-Barker (ed. 1924, p. li) terms it—when almost half the speech is a repetition of her own words at 1247. Wells & Taylor (1987, p. 283): White defended F’s omission: the line is pretty feeble, and a silent exit chasing Helena would be as or more effective.

[1385] Enter Oberon and Pucke.]
F1’s SD Enter Oberon and Pucke seems unnecessary, but cf. n. 865. Wells & Taylor (1987, p. 283): A parallel for F’s odd direction occurs in Dekker’s If This Be Not a Good Play, the Devil is in it, where the Quarto has Enter King as a Frier at 5.2.19, though the King speaks an aside at 5.2.10 and hears at least some of the dialogue before his quarto entrance [If it be not good, the Diuel is in it, 1612, K2v]. In Dream, no editor has taken F’s direction literally.

1386–7 This…wilfully]
Thiselton (1903, pp. 55–6): Puck has only mistaken once [at 731–2]; and if his knavery has been at work, Oberon is hardly clear of being an accessory [1136–45]. We must therefore regard this speech of Oberon as a very human outburst of impatience.…Like any man, he would shift the whole blame on to another. Wells (ed. 1967): The change into blank verse marks the change in speakers and tone, but the rhymed verse resumes at [1391].

1386 still]
See n. 206.

1387 knaueries]
OED (2), quoting this instance first: In weakened sense: Roguishness, waggishness, playing of tricks. Hense (1851, pp. 12–13) has a lengthy footnote here where he examines Sh.’s use of the words knavery, knave, and knavish in a number of his plays, in their positive and negative senses. In MND knavery means maliciousness in a mild sense, and he refers to the similar use of knavish at 403 and at 1488 (where Cupid is called a knavish lad) (Ger.). See 928, 937.

1387 wilfully]
Johnson (1755, 2): By design; on purpose (OED, adv. 4, adds occas. implying maliciously).

1388 king of shadowes]
Baynes (1880, p. 102) quotes the Ovidian terms umbrarum rex and umbrarum dominus [Met. 7.249 and 10.16] used of Pluto, showing Sh. connects Oberon with the underworld. The phrase also indicates Sh.’s knowledge of the Latin, since Golding translates the first term as Pluto (7.325, see ) and the second king of Ghosts (10.15, see ). Montrose (1996, p. 203): The appellation recognizes Oberon as the principal player in the action, whose powers of awareness and manipulation also mark him as the play’s internal dramatist.

1388 shadowes]
Johnson (1755, 6): Any thing perceptible only to the sight; a ghost; a spirit, or shade (OED 7, quoting this instance). Delius (ed. 1859): Shadowy beings, creatures not of flesh and blood (Ger.), comparing 2207. Gould (1884, p. 15): Fairies. Craig (ed. 1903) compares Wiv. 5.5.37–8 (2519–20). Kittredge & Ribner (ed. 1966) add: Darkness, when the fairies are abroad. Andrews (ed. 1989): A reference to the Fairies’ invisibility. See also 2015, 2207 and nn.

1389–90 man…on]
For the rhyme see n. 644–5.

1390 had]
Only Thiselton (1903, p. 56) defends the hath reading (see textual n.) finding it much more pointed, the meaning being By the Athenian garments which you cannot deny he is now wearing.

1391 so farre]
Holland (ed. 1994): At least to this extent.

1392 nointed]
For the dropping of the prefix see Abbott §460; Franz §49. OED (Noint): Aphetic form of anoint, meaning to anoint, moisten. Wright (ed. 1877) compares WT 4.4.784 (2666) nointed over with honey.

1393–4 And…sport]
Deighton (ed. 1891): And my gladness that matters turned out as they did is proportionate to the amusement their quarrelling affords me; so and As are correlative. Furness (ed. 1895): I am not sure that in a modern text there should not be a semicolon after sort,…to indicate that this As does not follow the so in that line (unlike the so and As in [1399, 1400]), but means because, since. Canning (1903, p. 464): Oberon, knowing he can rule him, never notices the imp’s last words, which seem hardly consistent with obedience, and proceeds to give him directions of a mysterious, yet benevolent nature.

1393 so did sort]
Johnson (1755, Sort, 6), quoting this instance: To fall out (OED, Sort, v.1 6a). Johnson (ed. 1765): So happen in the issue. Caldecott (MS 1813–33): [So] chanced. Furness (ed. 1895), referring to the derivation from Lat. sors, sortem: An allusion to fate (OED, n.1).

1394 iangling]
Johnson (1755, To jangle), quoting this instance: To altercate; to quarrel; to bicker in words (OED, vbl. n.). Neil (ed. 1878): (1) Prating, babbling, idle-talking; (2) wrangling. Brooks (ed. 1979): Discord.

1395–1418 Thou…peace]
Wells (ed. 1967) sees this as a turning-point in the action of the play. Brooks (ed. 1979): Fatal consequences are averted by the fairy ruler’s preventing the duel, as in Chaucer by the human ruler’s interruption of it. See .

1396–8 Hy…Acheron]
Verity (ed. 1893): It was a very old and widespread belief that fairies, spirits and magicians had the power to affect the heavenly bodies, especially the moon, and hide their light, comparing Fairfax’s translation of Tasso’s Jerusalem Delivered 9.15, The moon and stars for fear of sprites were fled. Brooks (ed. 1979): Cf. Oberon’s power, in Huon [see ]…to conjure up a black river to frustrate the mortals. These lines echo Marlowe’s night deep-drencht in mistie Acheron: Hero and Leander, I. 189 [2:436]. Acheron is one of the four rivers of Hades. Sackville, in his Induction in The Mirror for Magistrates (ll. 480 f.), makes it a lake that boyles and bubs up swelth [foul water] as blacke as hell. By synecdoche, Ovid and Virgil call Hades itself Acheron.
Lawrence (1919, p. 452) asserts: The stage became temporarily obscured through the emission of perfumed fumes from a trap. In court masque and theatre play alike this was the approved method of conjuring up fog or mist. He concedes that no stage direction in any of the old copies of the Dream exists to substantiate this; but argues for proof from Histriomastix: Here we have burlesqued in due sequence the raising of the fog, the falling asleep, the sleeping in full sight right through the act-interval, and the awakening to the sound of music (The Plays of John Marston, ed. H. Harvey Wood, 1939, 3:268, 277). He notes that Richard Simpson (The School of Shakspere, 1878, 2:88–9) had surmised that Histriomastix parodied MND. Lawrence’s belief that this stage effect was common practice is not supported by Dessen & Thomson (1999, pp. 143–4).

1396 Hy]
Cotgrave (1611, s’Envoler): To flie away, depart, get packing, hie away speedily, hast away quickly (OED, Hie, v.1 2 adds Go).

1396 ouercast]
Johnson (1755, 1), quoting this instance: To cloud; to darken; to cover with gloom (OED, v. 4a).

1397 starry]
See n. 222–3.

1397 welkin]
Gildon (1710, Glossary): The Heavens, Skye, &c. Johnson (1755, 1): The visible regions of the air. The Clarkes (ed. 1864): Sky (OED, n. 2).

1397 anon]
OED (adv. 4): At once, instantly. For the more usual use of the word see n. 387.

1398–1405 With…sleepe]
McPeek (1972, p. 78) compares Apuleius: Psyche was invaded by an infernal and Stygian sleep, a dense fog of sleep (crassa soporis nebula) which poured over her entire body so that she fell to the ground like a sleeping corpse (see ).

1398 drooping]
Sinking, descending (OED, Droop, v. 2, quoting this instance).

1398 Acheron]
The Clarkes (ed. 1864): One of the rivers of the infernal regions. Deighton (ed. 1891): Also used in late classical writers for the whole of the lower world. See OED, quoting this instance first; but Tit. 4.3.45 (1910) is earlier—1594. Fripp (1930, p. 102) compares Ovid, Met. 5.541 and 11.504. See Golding, 5.671 (see ) and 11.581. Cf. Tilley H397: As black as Hell.

1399 teasty]
OED (Testy, a. 1): Of headstrong courage; impetuous, precipitate, rash; Aggressive, contentious. Johnson (1755), quoting this instance: Fretful; peevish; apt to be angry (OED, a. 2). Deighton (ed. 1891): Quarrelsome. Kittredge (ed. 1939): Choleric, irritable.

1399 Riuals]
See n. 1180–1.

1399–1400 so…come]
On relatival constructions and the use of the subjunctive therewith see Abbott §275.

1400 As]
Johnson (1755, 3): That; in a consequential sense (OED, As, adv. 19a). Wright (ed. 1877) compares Ham. 2.1.92 (992), observing the quartos read As, the folios That.

1401–3 Like…Demetrius]
Brooks (ed. 1979) compares the ventriloquie and plaine cousenage of a supposed witch as described by Scot (see ).

1401 Like to]
See n. 8.

1401 frame]
Thirlby (MS 1725–33): Speak. Deighton (ed. 1891): Attune your voice (OED, Frame, v. 5c).

1402 stirre…vp]
See n. 16.

1402 wrong]
Wright (ed. 1877): Reproach, insult. Neilson & Hill (ed. 1942): Taunts.

1403 raile]
Johnson (1755): To use insolent and reproachful language (OED, v.4 1a). Farrow (ed. 1972): Hurl insults.

1404 from]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Away from.

1404 looke]
See n. 126.

1405 death-counterfaiting]
Wright (ed. 1877) compares Cym. 2.2.31 (938): O Sleep, thou ape of death. Holzknecht & McClure (ed. 1937): Death-imitating (OED, Death, n. 18b, quoting this instance only). Tilley (S527), quoting this instance: Sleep is the image of death; see Ovid, Amores 2.9.41.

1406 leaden]
Johnson (1755, 3): Heavy; dull, quoting R3 5.3.105 (3547), leaden slumber (OED 2b).

1406 Batty]
Johnson (1755), quoting this instance: Belonging to a bat. Moberly (ed. 1881): Bat-like (OED, a. 1, gives both these definitions, quoting this instance first). Deighton (ed. 1891): [Bats] fly abroad in the night-time only; hence slumberous. See n. 251 (vnheedy).

1407 this hearbe]
Doran (ed. 1959): Not love-in-idleness, [543–5], but another herb; cf. [562, 1588].

1408 liquor]
Rolfe (ed. 1877): Juice. Cf. 556 and n.

1408 vertuous property]
Johnson (ed. 1765): Salutiferous. Rann (ed. 1787): Salutary effect. Mowat & Werstine (ed. 1993): Potent power (OED, Virtuous, a. 6).

1409 errour]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Delusions. Andrews (ed. 1993): Both a) mistakes, and b) wandering.

1409 his might]
Hunter (ed. 1870): Its efficacy. For the use of his for its see n. 470.

1410 And…sight]
Johnson (ed. 1894): And make him see things as he generally does. Bethurum (1945, p. 90) parallels this action with Chaucer MerT 2356 (see ).

1410 wonted sight]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Usual vision.

1411–12 all…vision]
Guest (1838, 1:146–7): doubly-accented rhime seems to owe its origin to the lavish use of the substantives in ion, quoting these lines (reading division for derision). For the pronunciation of the two rhyme words see n. 159. Wells (ed. 1967): In the epilogue [2209–12] Puck suggests that the play may have the same effect on the audience. Holland (1989, p. 18): [Oberon’s] next line suggests that this dream is not a fruitless vision at all, since the result will be that back to Athens shall the lovers wend / With league whose date till death shall never end [1413–14].…Viewed in this light, Oberon’s line suggests a different emphasis. It is not that the derision is a dream, but that it should seem so.

1411 derision]
Rann (ed. 1787): Scene of mockery. Deighton (ed. 1891): This deception of which they have been made the fools. Kittredge (ed. 1939): Ludicrous delusion. Phillips (ed. 1969): This is how the lovers’ confusion seems to Oberon. Cf. 1223–4.

1412 seeme a dreame]
Guest (1838, 1:125): sectional rhime, is that which exists between syllables contained in the same section, quoting (p. 130) this example.

1412 fruitelesse]
Johnson (1755, 2): Vain; productive of no advantage; idle; unprofitable (OED, a. 2, quoting this instance). Deighton (ed. 1891): Empty. Baker (ed. 1896): Having no result, without consequence. Kittredge (ed. 1939): Meaningless; of no significance. For a different meaning see 82.

1412 vision]
Johnson (1755, 3): A supernatural appearance; a spectre; a phantom. (4) A dream; something shewn in a dream. A dream happens to a sleeping, a vision may happen to a waking man. A dream is supposed natural, a vision miraculous; but they are confounded. OED (n. 1a): Something which is apparently seen otherwise than by ordinary sight; esp. an appearance of a prophetic or mystical character, or having the nature of a revelation, super-naturally presented to the mind either in sleep or in an abnormal state. Hassel (2005, p. 382): A supernaturally sent message, dream, insight or prophecy.

1413 wend]
Johnson (1755, 1), quoting this instance: To go.…This word is now obsolete, but its preterite, went, is still in use (OED, v.1 13b).

1414 With…end]
Chambers (ed. 1897): Here, as in Theseus, Shakespeare keeps in mind the difference between the vagaries of love in its early stages, and the assurance of confirmed love. Mowat & Werstine (ed. 1993): United in a compact that will last until death. Possibly echoing the marriage service; see n. 1192.

1414 league]
OED (n.2 2): Alliance. Deighton (ed. 1891): Sc. of friendship. Clemen (ed. 1963): Union.

1414 date]
Johnson (1755, 5): Duration; continuance (OED, n.2 4).

1415 Whiles]
Johnson (1755, 3): At the same time that (OED, II. 4. conj.); Abbott §137: Whiles (genitive of while) means of, or during, the time. See Franz §555; Blake 5.3.2.4 (i) (f).

1416 Ile…boy]
Scott (ed. 1898): Apparently the idea is that Titania will be conscious of her ludicrous position, and that in return for the restoration of her sight she will surrender the page. Or perhaps her love for Bottom will have stopped her love for the changeling, and she will willingly surrender the boy; after which (without her consciousness thereof) Oberon will undo the charm.

1416 Ile to]
For omission of go see n. 522 (Thou…).

1417 And…release]
Baker (ed. 1896): I will remove the charm that makes her look lovingly on the monster, Bottom.

1417 charmed]
Caldecott (MS 1813–33): Enchanted. Mowat & Werstine (ed. 1993): Bewitched (OED, ppl. a. 1).

1418 From…viewe]
Deighton (ed. 1891): From the sight of the monster with whom she is in love; for the omission of the article, see [Abbott §89]. Titania claims to loue Bottom On the first viewe at 956; see also 1028 and nn.

1420 For…fast]
Steevens (ed. 1793) compares Cym. 2.2.48 (955), Swift, swift, you dragons of the night. Heron (1785, p. 111): The image of dragons drawing the chariot of night, is derived from the supposed watchfulness of that fabled animal; is classic, and as such often used by Milton. In support of Heron’s first assertion Malone (ed. 1790) quotes Golding, Met. 7.280–1 (see ); the latter points are unsubstantiated. Caldecott (MS 1813–33), objecting that Night is normally associated with sluggishness, cites 2H6 4.1.3–7 (2172–6), Milton (Comus 559), and Marlowe (Hero and Leander 1.107–8 [2:434]) among others: Speed is however here unequivocally assigned the attribute of the steeds of Night’s Chariot. Brooks (ed. 1979) believes Sh.’s image originates from Marlowe’s. Halliwell (ed. 1856): The allusion is probably classical.…Shakespeare, however, may possibly have had in his recollection some of the ancient engravings of dragons of the night, one of which, in Pynson’s edition of the Shepherds Kalendar, is here copied,—Shepardys that lyes the nyghtys in the feldes do se many impressions in the ayer above the erthe that they that lythe in theyr beddys sees not; some tyme they see in the ayer comynge in the maner and fassyon of a dragone spetynge fyer by the throte, and some tyme as a lepynge fyer (1506, N2V). Watt (ed. 1906): Sh. bestows a dragon car on Night, probably because of the fantastic shapes of the night clouds. In [Ant. 4.14.2 (2826), 4.14.8 (2833) a clowd that’s Dragonish] is called one of blacke Vespers Pageants.
Several object that the image is not classical. Wright (ed. 1877) quotes Keightley’s note on Milton’s Il Penseroso, 59, where the moon has dragons: Ceres alone had a dragon yoke. Ovid, however, describes both Medea and Ceres as having dragons: see , . Neil (ed. 1878) asserts that Night one of the oldest of the classical deities, was represented as riding…in a chariot, drawn by everwatchful dragons, but adduces no evidence. Verity (ed. 1893): The chariot of Night (personified) is yoked with horses, citing Statius, Thebaid 2.60 and Milton, Nativity Ode, 236; see also Simpson (1955, pp. 7–8). Root (1903, p. 92): For such a conception [Night drawn through the sky by a yoke of dragons] there is no classical authority. See also n. 2167.

1420 nights swift]
OED (Night, n. 11c) records the erroneous reading from F1, night-swift (dating it 1590). See textual n. Every editor after F4 follows Q1.

1420 cut…clouds]
Schmidt (1874, Cut vb. 3) quotes Tmp. 4.1.93 (1753) where Venus was Cutting the clouds towards Paphos.

1420 full]
See n. 501.

1421–8 And…night]
Thirlby (MS 1725–33, at 1423) compares Ham. 1.1.150–64 (149–63). Verity (ed. 1893): The spirits [1423] meant here are not fairy beings [cf. 1429], but the restless Ghosts [1422] of the dead. Hankins (1941, p. 170): Reginald Scot states that many Protestants believed that the souls of men could return from hell or from heaven, though the Church did not teach this.—A Discourse of Diuels and Spirits, xxvii [Discouerie, 1584, 2Q2v; at 2Q4r he adds that some affirme that damned soules walke oftenest]. Catholics believed that such souls could return by divine permission.—Aquinas, Summa Theologica, III, lxix, 3; lxx, 3 (Supp.). Brooks (ed. 1979): Two kinds of ghost are distinguished: the wandering ghosts—walking spirits [Scot, Discouerie; see ] is the technical term—departing only now; and Damned spirits, gone already. He compares 2162–5 for the first type. Greenblatt (ed. 1997): Robin is differentiating here between two types of spirits: those who wandered from their churchyard graves and those who have no proper resting place. These two types, both ghosts of former humans, are differentiated in turn from the fairy spirits by Oberon in the ensuing lines. But Hassel (2005, p. 339) connects the ghosts with the Damned spirits citing this passage in discussing the controversy over purgatory. See n. 375.

1421 And…harbinger]
Douce (1807, 1.195): Lucifer, the morning star, quoting Milton’s Song on May Morning: Now the bright morning star, Day’s harbinger, / Comes dancing from the East [l. 1–2]. Neil (ed. 1878) adds Phosphorus, the bringer of light; the planet Venus. Fripp (1930, p. 123) quotes Ovid, Met. 2.112–15. Cf. Golding (2.151–5), where Aurora and Lucifer are translated as earely morning and morning star (see ).

1421 yonder]
See n. 1084.

1421 Auroras]
Johnson (1755, 2): The goddess that opens the gates of day; poetically, the morning. OED (1): The dawn.

1421 harbinger]
Johnson (1755): A forerunner; a precursor (OED, n. 3). Deighton (ed. 1891), adds: properly an officer in the royal household, whose duty it was to allot and mark the lodgings of the king’s attendants in a royal progress.

1423–4 damned…buriall]
Steevens (ed. 1773): The ghosts of self-murderers, who are buried in cross-roads; and of those who being drowned, were condemned (according to the opinion of the ancients) to wander for a hundred years, as the rites of sepulture had never been regularly bestowed on their bodies. Idem (ed. 1778): That the waters were sometimes the place of residence for damned spirits, we learn from the ancient bl[ack] l[etter] Romance of Syr Eglamoure of Artoys, no date [c. 1350]: Let some preest a gospel saye For doute of fendes in the flode. Henley in Bell (1787, pp. 71–2): The laying of ghosts in the red-sea is a circumstance sufficiently notorious, referring to a note on the bishop of London’s Lectures on the Hebrew Poetry, translated by Mr. Gregory. Hankins (1941, p. 227): Sh.’s reference to floods is explained by a passage in Lavater, who says that the bodies of suicides were sometimes thrust into sacks and carried far out to sea for burial, [Of Ghostes and Spirites walking by Nyght [1572], I, xvii, p. 80]. Brooks (ed. 1979): The bodies of some who drowned themselves would not be recovered. Since these are Damned spirits all, the reference, pace Steevens [and others] cannot be to the superstition that want of burial would a long time deny rest to the souls of even the accidentally drowned.

1423–4 all…buriall]
Cercignani (1981, pp. 308–9) gives this rhyme as evidence of the retained prevocalic î in words occurring in metrically stressed endings.

1423–4 floods]
See n. 478.

1425 wormy beds]
Gentleman (ed. 1774): Though all departed bodies have wormed beds, yet giving them to the wicked peculiarly is finely conceived. Warton (1785, p. 295) observes this phrase was borrowed by Milton in On the Death of a Fair Infant, l. 31. Halliwell (ed. 1854): Imitated, perhaps unconsciously, by Shelley (in A Summer Evening Churchyard, Lechlade, Gloucestershire, l. 22). Chambers (ed. 1897) cites Lamb’s larceny in his poem Hester, l. 7.

1425 wormy]
Johnson (1755), quoting this instance: Full of worms (OED, a. 3, quoting this instance first). See n. 251 (vnheedy).

1426 least]
See n. 1201.

1426 looke…vpon]
On the transposing of prepositions see Abbott §203.

1427–8 They…night]
Thirlby (MS 1725–33): Forte hic incipit Oberon * [perhaps Oberon begins here, i.e., with the word They]. Brooks (ed. 1979): [Thirlby’s] suggestion that [1427–8] belong to Oberon…may well be right: Harold Jenkins observes (privately) that the stern explanation is much more in character for Oberon; makes a forceful rejoinder to Puck’s merely descriptive lines, and by reinforcing the antithesis between they and we, prepares for and gives more point to But we are spirits of another sort. Holland (ed. 1994): Some editors argue that these lines belong to Oberon…but the emphatic change from Robin’s horror to Oberon’s reassurance is surely right. See conj., .

1427 They…light]
Thirlby (MS 1725–33): exil’d. Ridley (ed. 1934) comments on changing the punctuation of 1425 and 1426, and reading exil’d here (see textual nn.): The passage makes sense as it stands, and I have not ventured to emend; but I suggest that if we assume a transposed punctuation and an e:d confusion (both very common), we get much more pointed sense. Brooks (ed. 1979): Q1, self-contradictorily, makes souls already Damned who must consort with night, wilfully exile themselves from light by the present act of returning to their wormy beds. But that is not an act of their will in the present; they must; decision for them is past. Thirlby’s conjecture resolves the contradiction: they exil’d themselves from light wilfully by their last act of will in deciding to kill themselves; and therefore must for ever consort with night. The emendation assumes only the misreading, extremely common, of secretary-hand d as e. Perhaps F’s dxile resulted from a marginal correction, d, with the d then substituted for the wrong e. With exil’d the punctuation at the end of [1425–6] must be emended; but once the Q1 compositor had read the sentence in the sense which exile gave it, he was bound to punctuate it as he did, complicating the error. Foakes (ed. 1984): The emendation to exil’d adopted by Brooks may be correct,…but the sense is clear anyway, and all other verbs in these lines are in the present tense.

1427 wilfully]
See n. 1387.

1427 exile]
Johnson (1755): This had formerly the accent on the last syllable, now generally on the first.…To banish (OED, v. 2, quoting this instance). See Abbott §490.

1428 for…night]
Thirlby (MS 1733–47) compares Ven. 1041: still consort with ugly night.

1428 aye]
See n. 80.

1428 consort]
Johnson (1755): To associate with;…to keep company with (OED, v. 5b, quoting this instance first). Neil (ed. 1878): From Latin consors, one who casts in his lot with others, citing Acts 17:4. Brooks (ed. 1979), alone: Keep (bad) company; pejorative, as in [Rom. 3.1.45 (1476)], but not in [LLL 2.1.177 (675)].

1428 black browed]
Johnson (1755): Having black eyebrows; gloomy; dismal; threatening. OED (ppl. a. a), quoting this instance first: Having a dark brow or front; frowning, scowling. Thirlby (MS 1733–47) compares Rom. 3.2.20 (1664), black brow’d night. Steevens (ed. 1793) compares Jn. 5.6.17 (2573), the black brow of night.

1428 night]
OED (n. 1c), quoting this instance first: Poet. Personified as a female being or deity.

1429–36 But…day]
Steevens (ed. 1793): What the fairy Monarch means to inform Puck of, is this. That he was not compelled, like meaner spirits, to vanish at the first appearance of the dawn. Moberly (ed. 1881): This may be well illustrated from a catechism of about A.D. 1400, quoted by Mr. Skeat (Piers the Plowman [ed. 1869], p. 105): Where be the anjelles that God put out of heven and bycam devilles? Some into hell, and some reyned in the skye, and some to the erth, and some in waters and in wodys. This, says Mr. Skeat, was an easy way of accounting for all classes of fairies; for the fallen spirits were supposed to be not equally wicked. Even Oberon, however, must vanish before the sun is high, avoiding the sunshine, though he need not fear it. The Clarkes (ed. 1864): Oberon has to state that he and his fairy people are privileged beings, uncompelled to retire with the night, and able to range in the broad, blessed light of day: but with what poetic gusto is it told; with what grace of allusion to the sylvan loveliness of Grecian mythological beliefs; with what gorgeous colouring of natural description, flooding the lines with blended rosy, golden, and sea-green hues! Young (1966, p. 29): Robin must know already what Oberon so eloquently tells him. Shakespeare apparently thought the exchange a necessary piece of exposition, a firm reminder to his audience that his fairies were not demons. Leggatt (1974, p. 105): [The fairies] are spirits of the night, but they are not involved with its darkest and most shameful secrets.…They are spirits…of the border country between night and day. Bevington (1978, pp. 80–1): Oberon may frolic until late in the dawn, though by implication even he may not stay abroad all day. The association of Oberon with sunlight and dawn is thus more symbolic than practical; it disassociates him from spirits of the dark, even though he must finish up this night’s work before night is entirely past.…This debate between Puck and Oberon reflects a fundamental tension in the play between comic reassurance and the suggestion of something dark and threatening. Cf. Barber (1959, p. 146), who misquotes 1436, substituting must for may. Brooks (ed. 1979) compares Oberon’s repudiation of a connection with evil spirits in Huon (see ), and the contrast between eluish ghosts and frendly Faeries in Spenser’s Shep. Cal. June 23–6.
Brown (1966, pp. 13–14), commenting on the dynamic nature of dramatic energy, analyzes wider effects of this passage: The plot is scarcely forwarded by the information of the first six lines, but much else is accomplished. Time and the general situation are established with talk of the eastern gate, all fiery red. And the gold and fiery light of the sun in opposition to cold water, repeats an important theme of the play, echoing earlier references to a cold fruitless moon [82], salt tears [747], the moon, the governess of floods, Pale in her anger [478–9], or the sea’s contagious fogs [465]. But to understand its theatrical effect enactment must be considered as well; he continues to discuss the effect of these lines on the character of Oberon (see ).

1429–36 sort]
Company or kind. See n. 1035.

1430 I…sport]
Steevens (ed. 1773): Tithonus was the husband of Aurora, and Tithonus was no young Deity. How such a waggish spirit as the King of the Fairies might make sport with an antiquated lover, [added in 1778: or his mistress in his absence,] may be easily understood. Capell (1783, 2.3:110): Morning’s love may mean—the star Phosphorus; possibly, the sun; and the sense be, that the speaker had sported with one or other of these, i. e. wanton’d in them; but the simpler sense is, that he had courted the morning, made her his love-addresses; the lady’s name is—Aurora. Rann (ed. 1787): Have oft paid a sportsman’s devoirs to the morning;—or the words may allude to the amours of Tithonus and Aurora—morning light. Holt White (in Steevens, ed. 1793): By the morning’s love I apprehend Cephalus, the mighty hunter and paramour of Aurora is intended. The context, And, like a forester, &c. seems to show that the chace was the sport which Oberon boasts he partook with the morning’s love. For the Cephalus-Aurora (Procris) story see Golding 7.853–1117, and 2001–2. Root (1903, pp. 43–4) thinks that 3H6 2.1.21–2 (673–4)—the Morning opes her golden Gates, And takes farwell of the glorious Sunne—means that the sun is a lover sent forth by Morning to run his course and return to her again, and, applying this to MND, interprets the mornings love as the sun, paraphrasing 1430 as I have often sported in sunlight. He also cites Chaucer, Tr. 3.1464–7. Halliwell (ed. 1856), objecting to the emendation light for loue (see textual n.): Oberon merely means to say metaphorically that he has sported with Aurora, the morning’s love, the first blush of morning; and that he is not, like a ghost, compelled to vanish at the dawn of day. Wright (1968, p. 42): The romance Oberon was a figure of grace and would naturally associate with the powers of light. Cf. DP n. 18.

1431 forrester]
Johnson (1755, 1): An officer of the forest (OED, Forester 1). Chambers (ed. 1897, at 1624): The Elizabethan forester was rather a huntsman than a woodcutter. See 1624, 1629.

1431 groues]
See n. 398. Brooks (ed. 1979) compares Chaucer, KnT 1505, 1688. See .

1432–4 Euen…streames]
Knight (ed. 1839): This splendid passage was perhaps suggested by Chaucer, KnT 1491–6. Verity (ed. 1893): Cf. the image in Psalm xix.5 of the Sun coming forth out of his chamber. Page (ed. 1897): In Ovid [Met. 2.113; see Golding 2.151–2, ], Aurora is represented as opening the purple gates in the east. Brooks (ed. 1979): The eastern gate and fiery-red sunrise are inspired by Chaucer’s orisont and firie Phebus [KnT 1493–4; see ],…echoed also at [1463] and [1612].

1432–4 Euen]
For the monosyllabic pronunciation see n. 581.

1432–4 faire blessed]
Warburton (ed. 1747) explains his emendation (see textual n.): The Epithets fair blessed are an insipid unmeaning expletive. Shakespear, without doubt, wrote, FAR-BLESSING beams: i.e. whose genial rays have the most extensive influence. Heath (1765, p. 54): That, fair, is a significant and proper epithet of the sun beams, I should have imagined no one would have disputed; nor can there be any just exception to the other, blessed, that is, whose genial influence is universally acknowledged and celebrated.
