Halliwell (ed. 1856): Fair-blessed is one of the numerous compound adjectives used by Shakespeare, meaning fair and blessed; but Warburton strangely altered it.

1434 salt greene]
This collocation of epithets bothered some commentators. For their attempts at correction see . Knowles (privately) compares Son. 33, l. 4.

1435–6 But…day]
Greenblatt (2001, p. 163): This prudent haste leaves a lingering doubt about how sharp the difference actually is between ghosts and fairies. Knowles (privately): Or Oberon simply wants to finish his business before the lovers awake.

1436 effect]
See n. 646.

1436 businesse]
See n. 133.

1436 day]
Foakes (ed. 1984): No exit is marked for Oberon in Qq or F here, but Rowe’s addition has been generally accepted, since Oberon returns at the beginning of the next scene. See textual n.

1437–9 Vp…one]
Most editors print these lines as a rhyming quatrain, followed by a fragmentary line (see textual n). They could conceivably be three pentameter lines ending them, town, one, but the usual editorial choice is probably correct, in being characteristic of fairy verse: see e.g. 1125–45.

1437–9 Vp & down]
OED (adv. 2): Hither and thither; to and fro.

1438 feard]
See textual n. and n. 417.

1439 Goblin…downe]
Of 1437–8 Collier (ed. 1842) says: These four lines are possibly a quotation from some lost ballad respecting Puck and his pranks. He would otherwise hardly address himself as Goblin. The exit of Oberon is not marked in the old copies, and the last line might belong to him. In answer White (ed. 1858): But see what the Fairy says [quoting 410–11]. Staunton (1874, p. 863) suggests the line is no other than a nickname given to the mischievous sprite, to indicate his will-o’-the-wisp propensities, and to be read,—Goblin-lead-them-up-and-down. Furness (ed. 1895) quotes Thirlby’s conjecture (MS 1725–33), Goblin’ll, calling it an emendatio certissima, a clear case of absorption. But Thiselton (1903, p. 57): The comma after Goblin in [Qq and F1] is subversive of Thirlby’s emendatio certissima. I see no difficulty in Puck, on assuming the character of Hobgoblin, addressing himself as such. All editors agree with this last sentence, except Cuningham (ed. 1905): Possibly I is understood before Goblin unless will is understood after it.

1441–77 Where…visited]
Chambers (ed. 1897, at 1399): The scene irresistibly reminds one of the battle between Tweedledum and Tweedledee in Through the Looking-glass, and the characters of those heroes are about as much differentiated as those of Lysander and Demetrius. Wells (ed. 1967): In modern performances, artificial smoke is sometimes used to suggest the fog that Oberon has instructed Puck to cause [at 1398]. But the scene can be equally effective if this is left to the audience’s imagination. Dessen (1984, p. 79): The final confusion involving Demetrius and Lysander, in which each takes Puck for his intended opponent and is therefore led astray…, becomes less a consequence of dim light (this scene is often played in a stage mist or gloom) or even of Puck’s magical control but rather acts out in one climactic (and very funny) moment the brand of myopia or blindness everpresent in this part of the comedy. The implicit suggestion in many modern productions that physical darkness causes these and other errors may blur some shrewd comic insights into the nature of love and lovers. Cf. n. 1491–6.

1441–7 Speake…againe]
For the lineation and rhyme see textual nn. 1441–2 and 1444–5, 1447.

1443 drawne & ready]
Delius (ed. 1859): With sword pulled and prepared for combat (Ger.) (OED, Drawn, ppl. a. 2, quoting this instance). For passive participles sometimes used as epithets to describe the state which would be the result of the active verb, see Abbott §374. See Franz §661.

1444 be with]
OED (With, prep. 22d), quoting this instance first: The phrase to be with, used in menace, etc. = to be avenged on, chastise, be even with; quoting also Shr. 4.1.167 (1799) where Petruchio threatens his servants, Ile be with you straight. Brooks (ed. 1979) considers the primary meaning is be beside (OED, With, prep. 22a).

1444 straight]
Johnson (1755): Immediately; directly (OED, C. adv. 2a). Cf. 1056, 2005 (straight way). See 1575.

1445 plainer]
Those who comment agree with Deighton (ed. 1891): More level, and so more suitable for their combat (OED, Plain, a.1 1a, quoting this instance).

1445 ground.]
Although she follows most editors and puts an exit for Lysander here, and an entrance at 1456 (see textual n.), Doran (ed. 1959): But Lysander may have stayed on stage and groped about to suggest the fog; cf. [1398]. The folio has a direction shifting places, though out of place at [1460], suggesting a way of managing action with both Lysander and Demetrius on the stage part of the time. Bevington (ed. 1980), without comment, gives an SD [Lysander wanders about, following the voice.] Wells & Taylor (1987, p. 283) object: Since Robin apparently proceeds to mimic Lysander’s voice, it would be particularly confusing if he remained on stage, following Robin’s changed voice and not responding to Demetrius’ real one. Bevington (ed. 1988), giving the same SD as his ed. 1980: It is not clearly necessary that Lysander exit at this point; neither exit nor reentrance is indicated in the early texts; the SD and a similar note are also given in his ed. 1992. See also n. 1456.

1449 Speake…bush.]
For the now mainly accepted punctuation originating with Capell (ed. 1768) see textual n. Andrews (ed. 1993) is one of the few editors who retains the Q1 punctuation here, explaining: The implication…is that Demetrius expects Lysander to be hiding in a bush, a sense compatible with the tone of his remarks in lines [1284–9, 1299–1300]. [He] may be alluding satirically to Moses’ encounter with the burning bush in Exodus 3:4, where God called unto him out of the midst of the bush, and said, Moses, Moses. And he said, Here am I.

1449 in…head]
Cf. Palomon fearfully hiding in a bushe (Chaucer, KnT 1517; see ).

1451 thou…warres]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Are in eager expectation of a combat.

1452 recreant]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Coward (OED, n. 1).

1452 childe]
OED (n. 3b), quoting this instance first: In contemptuous address.

1453 whippe…rodde]
Deighton (ed. 1891): I.e. treat you as an insolent child deserves to be treated. Shaheen (1993, pp. 98–9), comparing 1 Kings 12:11, Ps. 89:32, 14, Prov. 26.3: The language seems to be based on Scripture. He compares also 1H4 1.3.239 (567) and 3.2.10–11 (1828–9). Moberly’s (ed. 1881) parallel with Herodotus (Histories 4.3.1–4), where the Scythians lay down their arms and take horsewhips against the army of the children of slaves, is not entirely apposite.

1453 whippe]
OED (v. 6a), quoting this instance: To punish or chastise with a whip or rod;…Also loosely, to beat (esp. a child)…, to spank.

1453–4 He…thee]
Farrow (ed. 1972): It would be dishonourable to fight a baby like you with grown-up weapons.

1455 Yea]
OED (adv. 4a), quoting this instance: = Indeed; Well, well then.

1456 weele…here]
Delius (ed. 1859): We will test our valour elsewhere (Ger.).

1456 Exeūt]
Believing that Lysander exits at 1445, Wells & Taylor (1987, p. 280): 3.2…should be broken into two scenes: the scene is apparently cleared at [1456], when Robin and Demetrius leave before Lysander enters. Although the action of Robin’s abuse of the men clearly continues, the cleared stage can suggest a gap in time and place, which usefully contributes to the ease with which an audience accepts the subsequent wearied surrender of Lysander and Demetrius. Holland (ed. 1994, at 1457) demurs: But the audience would not have had any way of noticing a new scene and interpreting the break as representing the passage of time unless there were a sizeable time-gap at this point, a gap which would seem undesirable in its disruption of the action. Since the action either side of [1457] is remarkably similar, something Shakespeare never does across scenes, and since in Elizabethan performance the action would here surely have been continuous as usual, I have reverted to the traditional lack of division.
However, Exeunt does not necessarily indicate a cleared stage (cf. 1384+1), and F1 has here Exit. It is possible that only Demetrius leaves, so that Robin remains, with Lysander falling asleep without ever leaving the stage (see n. 1445). The Q1 SD at 1465 uniquely lacks Enter; this, together with the comma after Robin, suggests that Robin is already present, with Demetrius added.
The question of who is on stage when, and what they are doing, is most fully discussed by earlier editors in relation to F’s SD shifting places at 1460. White (ed. 1858) believes the line misplaced: It plainly refers to Puck, Lysander, and Demetrius, and belongs several lines above. Furness (ed. 1895): White’s suggestion is not altogether satisfactory. Just below Demetrius accuses Lysander of shifting every place, which certainly seems to refer to this stage-direction, and may indicate some unusual alacrity on the part of Lysander in his attempts in the dense darkness to find Demetrius.…[I]f Demetrius plunges into the darkness and we lose sight of him mad in the pursuit of Puck’s voice, and then see Lysander…rush hither and thither, half frenzied, shifting his place every minute, then the conviction is forced on us that this is a fight to the death, and the somnolent power of Puck’s charm in allaying the fury is heightened. There is another point which adds somewhat to the belief that this stage-direction is correctly placed: it is not mandatory, as are many other stage-directions in this play…; it does not tell the actor what to do, but describes what he does. Wilson (ed. 1924, p. 156) arguing that this SD comes from a marginal notation in the promptbook which provides the copy for F, that it refers to the whole page rather than a particular line, and that it is a special note to the stage-manager: The stage-business, involving as it did the entry and re-entry of both men, one after the other, to say nothing of still more complicated movements on the part of Puck, would need careful and probably frequent rehearsal. Brooks (ed. 1979): This SD is not in the imperative, and doubtless comes not from the promptbook, but (editorially) from [1468]. Wells & Taylor (1987, p. 283) dispute Brooks, and draw on Wilson in suggesting that a vertical line in the margin would indicate that the direction applies to a range of the preceding and following dialogue; in their accompanying old spelling edition (1986) they repeat shifting places opposite all of the speeches to which it most probably applies. Their modernized edition of the same date gives shifting place at the same points. Holland (ed. 1994) concurs, but is more restrained in adopting this expedient, using it to indicate the more necessary shifts, while accepting that in production the movement might well have occurred more often.

1457 still]
See n. 206.

1457 dares me on]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Challenges me to come on.

1458 When…gon]
Thiselton (1903, p. 57): The apparently irregular metre of [F1] and [Q2 ] is more effective than that of [Q1] [see textual n.], the only emphasis required being on come, cals, and gon. Such regularity of metre as [Q1] furnishes does not harmonise with Lysander’s excitement. Surely an actor would make Q1’s an exciting, if not humorous, line.

1459 lighter heel’d]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Nimbler in running; cp. [1H4 2.4.48 (1010–11)] (OED, Light-heeled, a. 1, quoting this instance first). Cf. hastie footed (1227).

1460 fast…faster]
Guest (1838, 1:136, 139): inverse rhime is that which exists between the last accented syllable of the first section, and the first accented syllable of the second. Brooks (ed. 1979, p. xlvii) notes Sh.’s use of polyptoton, which Peacham describes as A figure which of the word going before deriveth the word following.…To delight the ear by the derived sound and to move the mind with consideration of the high affinity and concord of the matter.

1461 That…way]
Farrow (ed. 1972): So I’ve come to rough ground where I can’t see my way. But the darkness and difficulty are also in Lysander’s mind. Cf. n. 222–3.

1461 That]
So that: for the omission of so see Abbott §283.

1461 fallen…I]
Lever (ed. 1961): I find myself (not I have fallen).

1461 fallen]
Monosyllabic or elided. See n. 36.

1461 in]
For in used with verbs of motion like fall see Abbott §159.

1462 rest me]
See n. 689.

1463 gray]
Johnson (1755, 3): Dark like the opening or close of day (OED, a. 5a). Deighton (ed. 1891): As the light is before the sun is above the horizon. Thirlby (MS 1733–47) quotes Tit. 2.2.1 (701), The morn is bright and gray. Irving & Marshall (ed. 1888) compare Ham. 1.1.166 (165), where they maintain that russet…means grey. Craig (ed. 1903) compares Chaucer, KnT 1492: The morowe graie. See .

1464 spight]
Johnson (1755, Spite 1): Malice; rancour; hate; malignity; malevolence (OED, Spite, n. 1). Deighton (ed. 1891): Insult. Holzknecht & McClure (ed. 1937): Outrage. Brooks (ed. 1979): Bad turn (done me by malicious fortune). See also n. 148.

1466 Ho…ho]
Ritson (1783, p. 46): It may be remarked that this exclamation is peculiar to Puck. In the old song, printed by Peck, in which he relates all his gambols, he concludes every verse with Ho, ho, ho! He here forgets his assumed character. Malone (ed. 1790): The song above alluded to may be found in Percy’s Reliques of Ancient Poetry [3:203]. Ritson (in Steevens ed. 1793) gives several quotations to show that the Devil himself [had], if not a better, at least an older title, to [the cry ho, ho, ho]. Wright (ed. 1877) responding to the final remark of Ritson in 1783: There is nothing so exceptional in the cry as to make it inappropriate to Puck in an assumed character. Halliwell (ed. 1856): There is an old Norfolk proverb, still current, to laugh like Robin Goodfellow, that is, very heartily. Neil (ed. 1878) locates the source for this as the Rev. Robert Forby, in his Vocabulary of [East Anglia (1830)]. Doran (ed. 1959): Meant as laughter; devil’s traditional entrance cry in medieval religious drama. OED (Ho, int.1 4), quoting this instance: Repeated, ho! ho! or ho! ho! ho! it expresses derision or derisive laughter.

1467 Abide]
Johnson (1755, 4): To wait for, expect, attend,…await (OED, v. 12a). Wright (ed. 1877): Wait for me, that we may encounter. Furness (ed. 1895): Stand still (unattested by OED). Foakes (ed. 1984): Face, encounter [OED, v. 14].

1467 wot]
Johnson (1755): To know; to be aware. Obsolete (OED, Wit, v.1 1b). See Franz §182. See 1689 (wote).

1468 shifting…place]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Changing your place every moment. See also n. 1456.

1472 Nay]
See n. 309.

1472–3 Thou…dear]
Johnson (ed. 1765): Thou shalt pay dearly for this. Though this is sense, and may well enough stand, yet the poet perhaps wrote thou shalt ’by it dear. So in another place [1201], thou shalt aby [corrected in the appendix from abuy] it. OED (Buy, v. 3a) supports this explanation fully, even the conjecture, giving as an equivalent for buy ABY v. 2 (of which it was probably an aphetic form). Circe warns Picus in similar terms of the consequences of rebuffing her in Golding’s Ovid 14.438 (). See n. 1201 (aby).

1475 goe thy way]
See n. 522.

1475 Faintnesse]
Schmidt (1874, 1): Exhaustion, weariness.

1476 measure…length]
Schmidt (1874, Length, 1): To measure out my l. = to lie down. OED (Measure, v. 2f), quoting this instance first: To measure (out) one’s length to fall prostrate. Andrews (ed. 1993) compares Rom. 3.3.69–70 (1873–4); Levenson (ed. Rom., 2000, ad loc.), citing MND: Romeo enacts a morbid version of To measure one’s length (Dent MM12).

1477 looke…visited]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Expect to be met and punished by me.

1477 visited]
Kittredge (ed. 1939): Sought out, encountered, attacked. Cf. [1H4 4.4.36–7 (2625–6)].

1479–84 O…companie]
On the stanzaic form see n. 1147–1323.

1479–81 O…light]
Dent compares (N164): After Night comes the day.

1479 O weary…night]
Andrews (ed. 1993, at 1480): [This line] anticipates [1972–4] where Shakespeare burlesques the trials of Helena and her companions. Both places seem to be parodying the convention of the nocturnal apostrophe.

1480 Abate…houres]
Schmidt (1874, Abate, 1b), quoting this phrase: = shorten (OED, Abate, v.1 13). The Clarkes (1879, p. 526): Curtail, diminish.

1480 shine comforts]
Theobald’s (ed. 1733) punctuation (see textual n.) makes it probable that he views comforts as a vocative. Delius (ed. 1859): Maybe comforts is the object of shine, while the daylight, which this verse refers to, has to be added mentally (Ger.). Wright (ed. 1877): Cause comforts to shine.…Or it may be simply let comforts shine, &c; just as below we have And sleep…steal me awhile, &c. Deighton (ed. 1891): The imperative used optatively. Furness (ed. 1895): comforts may be an accusative, the object of shine; it may be a vocative, like night; or it may be a nominative, with shine as its verb; whichever the reader may think the most pathetic. The opposite to this expression is comfort-killing Night, Luc. 764.

1481 may]
OED (v.1 9b), quoting this instance: With ellipsis of a vb. of motion. See n. 522 (Thou…).

1482 my…company]
Gill (ed. 1981): The company of poor me.

1482 detest]
Johnson (1755): To hate; to abhor; to abominate (OED, v. 2). Walker (1860, 2:311): In the writers of that age detest is used in the sense which as then it still retained from its original, detestari, being indicative of something spoken, not of an affection of the mind. Cf. OED, v. 1: To curse, calling God to witness, to denounce, execrate. However Schmidt (1874) agrees with Johnson (1755): Abhor. Brooks (ed. 1979), quoting Walker: Repudiate. McDonald (ed. 2000): Hate being with me.

1483 sleepe…eye]
Phillips (ed. 1969): Gives rest even to the sorrowing. Proverbial; Tilley (S662), quoting this instance. Douce (1807, 1:195) compares Mac. 2.2.34 (693). West (1865, pp. 9, 18) compares Sidney’s Arcadia, where Pyrocles lies down with intention to make forgetting sleep comfort a sorrowful memory, and Cecropia’s sleep, which a little before day, overwatched with sorrow, her tears had as it were sealed up in her eyes (ed. Evans, 1977, pp. 130, 322). See n. 1107–10.

1483 eye]
(OED, n.1 3c), quoting this instance: Fig.; esp. as attributed to the heart, mind, [etc.].

1484 Steale…companie]
Walter (ed. 1964): I.e. as the others detest her company so she too also wishes to be free from her own sorrowful company.

1484 Steale]
May steal. For the optative use of the subjunctive dispensing with let, may, &c. see Abbott §365. Deighton (ed. 1891): Gently remove. Farrow (ed. 1972): Hide.

1485–90 Yet…madde]
On the four-beat verse of the fairy dialect (Guest, 1838, 1:192) see nn. 375–85, 378 (Moons).

1485, 1487 Yet…Heare]
Some attempt to emend these two lines to create seven syllables as in the other three lines of Puck’s jingle. Hanmer (ed. 1744) adds a word (see textual n.). Rolfe (ed. 1877) pronounces three as a disyllable, referring to Abbott §484, who quotes this instance; and points to Abbott §486 who instances comes in line 1487 as quasidissyllabic. Accepting these disyllabic pronunciations Verity (ed. 1893) defines the metre of the two lines as a trochaic measure of three feet with extra syllable at the end. But Furness (ed. 1895) suggests: Why not say that these two lines are made up of amphimacers, and so avoid any barbarous prolongation of syllables? Thus: Yèt büt thrèe | Còme öne mòre, and Hère shë còmes.| Cùrst änd sàd. Scott (ed. 1898): With regard to the rhythm and metre of these five lines, note that in the first and third lines…a middle syllable is wanting, and in all the lines the opening unaccented syllable is wanting. These variations were frequent in the older four-beat measure, according to the original idea of which each line consisted of two balanced halves. The omission of a middle syllable was made on exactly the same principle as the omission of the opening syllable, though it was done less frequently. The loss of a syllable was atoned for by the lengthening of the voice on an emphatic one. Cf. n. 1691.

1485–6 Yet…fower]
Smyth (1995, pp. 637–9) quotes 1485–6, 1504–6 then plays with various mathematical propositions, in the manner of Wittgenstein, concentrating, on p. 639, on Puck’s turning the innocent orderings of arithmetic into laws of exclusion: 2 X 2 kinds = 4.…What Puck most obviously means here is male and female, the most banal of such laws. But the two kinds also refer to a more general bifurcation whereby the 3 is also distinguished from the 1, as the loved or lovable is distinguished from the unloved or unlovable—since it is for the now un-loved Hermia that Puck is waiting. Thus the 1 (which numbers Helena and Hermia successively) twice plays a part identical to 0 (the excluded Bottom of love). He continues to give three main patterns of the love relationships of the four, calling the combination a Wittgensteinian overdetermined four-footed calculation if ever there was one. See n. 378 (moons) on metre.

1485 Come…more]
Andrews (ed. 1993): Either (a) Let one more come, or (b) Come, one more.

1486 Two…makes]
On the singular form of the verb with a plural subject see Abbott §333.

1486 both kindes]
Delius (ed. 1859): Two men and two maidens (Ger.). Macdonald (1992, p. 40) thinks this phrase and calling the women females [1490] tends to have a distancing and diminishing effect, to deny what we like to think of as the dignifying distinction between human nature and the rest of the animal kingdom.

1486 fower]
For possible reference to the Pythagorean tetrad, see n. 5.

1487 Heare]
While this is a variant form of here, and is so interpreted by all editors after Q1, the other meaning is possible, for Hermia may well be angrily muttering, or sobbing. See n. 1189.

1487 curst]
The main sense is shrewish (OED, Cursed, curst, ppl. a. 4a), ill-tempered (Hunter, ed. 1870), or, as Craig (ed. 1903), in a temper, but Rann (ed. 1787) suggests vexed, (unattested by OED), and Walter (ed. 1964), Perhaps under the curse or spell of love. See n. 1334.

1487 sadde]
See n. 1264.

1488 knauish ladde]
Fripp (1930, p. 112) says this is Sh.’s translation of Ovid’s lascivus puer, a phrase applied to Cupid (Met. 1.456; cf. Golding, wanton baby, 1.550, see ); he compares 403 where the Fairy calls Robin a knauish sprite. Hibbard (1981, p. 147) goes a step further: Puck equates himself with Cupid when he says quoting 1488, 1490. See n. 403 (knauish).

1490 females]
OED (n. 2b), quoting this instance: As a mere synonym for woman. Rolfe (ed. 1877): We do not find in S. the modern vulgarism of female for woman. He uses the word only in its strict sense, as opposed to male, except when he puts it into the mouth of clowns. He quotes LLL 1.1.263–5 (263–4) and AYL 5.1.47–50 (2388–91). Barber (1959, p. 131): Females, ordinarily a graceless word, works nicely here because it includes every girl. The same effect is got by using the names Jack and Jill, any boy and any girl quoting 1501–6.

1490 madde]
Johnson (1755, 2): Over-run with any violent or unreasonable desire. Partridge (1968, mad): Infatuated; madly in love (OED, a. 4a).

1491–6 Neuer…fray]
Young (1966, pp. 68–9): The four lovers, after all, are puppets while they are in the woods, the helpless victims of supernatural enchantments. Their state is pointed up and made amusing by the artificiality of their movements and their speech. Were they more fully characterized we would develop an interest in and sympathy for them which the pace of the play does not allow, and the detachment that we need to laugh at their misfortunes would be threatened. Here, for example, is Hermia at a moment very close to despair: [quotes 1491–6]. Having said this, she immediately goes to sleep among the other three lovers. Our attention rests not on her despair but on the completion of a formal movement directed by Robin Goodfellow and the comic discrepancy between what Hermia thinks is going on and what we know to be the truth. Briers and desires both have serious potential, but paired in a rhyme they maintain the formal and comic tone.…Artificiality is the means of achieving aesthetic distance throughout [MND]. Dessen (1984, p. 73): Errors and confusion in imaginary darkness are especially appropriate for comedy.…Often the comic effect is linked to the inability of the characters to see what would be clear in a day scene and what is clear to the audience (as when Puck gets the four lovers to lie down on stage without seeing each other). Cf. n. 1441–77. For the stanzaic form see n. 1147–1323.

1491–4 Neuer…desires]
Brooks (ed. 1979) compares Golding’s Ovid 1.614–16; see .

1491 Neuer so]
Abbott §52: I have never been so weary.

1492 Bedabbled]
Johnson (1755), quoting this instance: To wet; to besprinkle. It is generally applied to persons, in a sense including inconvenience (OED, Bedabble, v., quoting this instance first). Schmidt (1874) compares Ven. 703, the dew-bedabbled wretch.

1492 briers]
See n. 376.

1494 can…desires]
Massey (1888, p. 44) compares Son. 51.9: Then can no horse with my desire keepe pace.

1494 desires]
Longings, wishes (OED, n. 1). See also nn. 7, 76.

1495 rest mee]
See n. 689.

1496 Heauens…fray]
Celenza (Sogno, ed. 1934): A happy touch: poor Hermia, notwithstanding the abandonment and the insults of Lysander, still prays for him (It.).

1496 Heauens shielde]
See n. 841.

1496 Heauens]
For pronunciation see 156.

1496 meane a fray]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Intend to fight.

1497–1500 On…eye]
Tathwell (in Grey, 1754, 1:63) explains that he rearranged the verse lines (see textual n.) [b]ecause verses with the middle rhime which were call’d leonine, or monkish verses, seem to have been the ancient language of charms and incantations. Adopting Warburton’s (ed. 1747) division Guest (1838, 1:185–6): [Verse] of two accents is rarely met with as an independent verse. The cause was evidently its shortness. Shakespeare, however, has adopted it into that peculiar rhythm, in which are expressed the wants and wishes of his fairy-land. This rhythm consists of abrupt verses of two, three, or four accents; it belongs to the common measure, and abounds in the sectional pause. Under Shakespeare’s sanction, it has become classical, and must now be considered as the fairy dialect of English literature.

1498 apply]
Rowe’s (ed. 1709) addition of to (see textual n.) is followed silently by all editors until Collier (ed. 1842) remarks: [It] seems necessary to the sense. But Halliwell (ed. 1856) omits to, arguing that Apply did not necessarily require the addition of the preposition. The verb occurs without it in [A preaty Interlude called,] the Nice Wanton, 1560 [sig. A2r]. The versification is irregular. White (ed. 1858) counters that the preposition is required both by sense and rhythm, and asserts that the single case which he cites in his support appears to be itself the result of typographical error. Scott (ed. 1898): The preposition to here is inserted almost unanimously, but none the less certainly wrongly, by the editors. It is our duty to accept the metre as Shakespeare deliberately put it. Note that not in this line only, but also in the other alternate lines a syllable is wanting. Sleep sound, thou tak’st—in these cases also each of the two syllables has to receive a lengthened accent. It may appear that In the sight is different; but it is not materially so. In the and of the were expressions recognised in Elizabethan literature and in Milton as practically one syllable. When false theories and emendations are abandoned it is easy to see how Shakespeare meant these two-syllabled lines to be uttered. On Sleep, Your and Thou the voice is lengthened; in a soothing manner in the first instance though gradually the tones become exhilarating. Thiselton (1903, p. 58) supports the early reading, and makes your a dissyllable. In quoting this instance OED, v. 3, supplies to in square brackets.

1498 gentle louer]
Farrow (ed. 1972): Lysander hasn’t always behaved very courteously, but Puck is restoring him to his true nature.

1498 remedy]
Brooks (ed. 1979): The spell is never taken off Demetrius’ eyes: without its lasting effect the love-tangle could not be resolved. As in Huon [see ], Oberon’s help is indispensable to the happy outcome. Holland (ed. 1994): The fact that Demetrius stays under the influence of the first drug troubles many commentators but since its effect on him has been different he does not need curing. Ibid. (p. 68): His true love is Helena. The charm leaves him in an intensified state of his previous affection, not in a new state of unwanted and unwonted desire.

1499 thou tak’st]
For attempts to mend the metre see the textual n. See also conj., . Tyrwhitt (in Steevens, ed. 1778): [The] line is deficient both in metre and construction, and he suggests altering the phrase to see thou tak’st. However, Malone (1780, 1:118): I do not perceive any defect in the metre of the second line. It is the same as in the former stanza [sleepe sound]. Seymour (1805, 1:47) admits the line is lame; but Mr. Tyrwhit’s emendation cannot be admitted: the speech of Puck, in this place, is only declarative; the imperative, therefore, see thou tak’st, will not agree with the context: the second line in the preceding stanza seems to have the same defect. Guest (1838, 1:292): The propriety of Shakespeare’s rhythm will be better understood, if we suppose (what was certainly intended) that the fairy is pouring the love-juice on the sleeper’s eye, while he pronounces the words, Thou tak’st. The words form, indeed, the fairy’s charm, and the rhythm is grave and emphatic as their import.…I know not how the construction is bettered [by Tyrwhitt’s emendation], and the correspondence, no less than the fitness of the numbers, is entirely lost. Halliwell (ed. 1856): There is no necessity for any alteration, the uneven metre being evidently intentional. The commencement of the speech is spoken with pauses, and with great precision, but when the juice is dropped, and the incantation finished, Puck, as if pleased at being relieved from any serious occupation, bursts forth joyously with his country proverb in his usual wild strain. Dyce (ed. 1864) also objects to Tyrwhitt’s emendation: See would require take.

1500 True]
Thirlby (MS 1747–53): Peculiar to this place & opposed to the former delusions.

1501–2 And…owne]
Deighton (ed. 1891): And the proverb so well known to rustics. Tilley (M209), quoting this instance: Let every man have his own. Delius (ed. 1859), perhaps unfamiliar with English proverb lore, points to 1504–6, rather than to 1502.

1503 In…showen]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Shall be exemplified in your case when you awake.

1504–6 Iacke…well]
Steevens (ed. 1773): These three last lines are to be found among [John] Heywood’s [A dialogue…of…prouerbes, 1546, G1v]. Gentleman (ed. 1774): This is a very peculiar jingle to end an Act with suggesting it would be better if it were set to musick. For a Wittgensteinian analysis of these lines see Smyth (1995, pp. 637–9). See n. 1485–6.

1504 Iacke…ill]
Thirlby (MS 1725–33) refers to LLL 5.2.875 (2836), Iacke hath not Gill. Idem (MS 1733–47) quotes from Ray’s Proverbs [1670 p.] 124: Every Jack must have his Gill. Idem (MS 1747–53) compares Wiv. 1.4.120–1 (508): all shall be well. The full proverb is in Tilley (A164): All shall be well and Jack shall have Jill. Halliwell (ed. 1856): The nicknames of Jack and Jill, as generic titles for a man and a woman, are of great antiquity.…Shakespeare has varied the proverb for the sake of the rhyme. Delius (ed. 1859) quotes Shr. 4.1.49–50 (1684–5).

1505–6 The…well]
Thirlby (MS 1733–47) quotes Ray, Proverbs, 1670, p. 202: All is well, and the man hath his mare again. See Tilley (A153) and (A150). Halliwell (ed. 1856) finds part of the proverb in Jyll of Brentford’s Testament, [c. 1567, A2v]: The poor man shall have his mare again. The meaning of the first part of the proverb—The man…again—is first given by Schmidt (1875, Mare 1) who paraphrases: All shall be right again.
Steevens (ed. 1793) proposes changing well to still to rhyme with ill. See conj., . However, Mason (1807, p. 530): This observation is injudicious, for Puck’s poetry concludes with the preceding distich, and this line contains merely an assertion in plain prose, aspiring neither to rhyme or metre. Halliwell (ed. 1856): It had rather be considered as forming a loose jingling rhyme with the preceding couplet. Some interpret mare as woman: see OED, Mare1, 1c, Applied, orig. contemptuously, to a woman, quoting this instance. Talbert (1963, p. 256): The bawdy overtones of [the woman-mare] analogy are compatible with the next reference to the lovers [1663–4].

1505–6 all…well]
Moffatt (2004, p. 185), observing that the remedy here applied by Puck removes all errour (1409) and applies to euery man (1502), argues that his words refer to a re-ordering more widespread than one for just two lovers.

1506 well]
See textual n. at 1507. Collier (ed. 1842): The old stage-direction in the folio is, They sleep all the Act, meaning that they are supposed to continue asleep during the interval between the third and fourth acts; and they are still sleeping at the opening of the fourth act, until they are suddenly roused by the horns of Theseus’ huntsmen [1662]. Discussion turns on the meaning of Act, on questions of continuity of performance, on act and scene division, and on the provenance of the SD. Dessen & Thomson (1999, p. 2) represent most modern opinions in understanding F’s SD as an instance of act for music between the acts. Greg (1955, pp. 243–4), discussing the division into acts in F as a major difference from Q: This was clearly not original, for one of the divisions has been made at a point where the lovers are asleep on the stage, necessitating the direction They sleepe all the Act, that is, throughout the interval. This presumably means both that the division was made in the theatre and not merely for printing, and that the intervals were of some duration, probably with music. We may reasonably surmise that in this case, and possibly in others, the division was introduced on the occasion of a performance at the Blackfriars house; he refers to his earlier discussion (pp. 143–5) of act division and intervals. Harbage (1955, pp. 47–9) distinguishes between private playhouse texts and amphitheatre texts, noting: The mere division of a text into acts, even when the points of division are marked by choral spokesmen, dumbshows, or divertissement, does not in itself prove that there were intervals in performance; he offers as a logical explanation of F’s SD that sometime after 1608 when the King’s company acquired the Blackfriars private playhouse for winter use, musical intervals were interpolated into performances of a play that had been constructed for continuous presentation. See also Riehle (1990, p. 93) who argues from the evidence of Plautus’s Amphitruo that continuous action and Act division need not be mutually exclusive, as long as the interval between the Acts remains short. Baldwin (1959, p. 475 n.): One is reminded of an analogous device. In James IV, the chorus is asleep without comment at the end of the third act, since no further comment is needed here to point out these two acts of the epitasis. Similarly, in The Spanish Tragedy, Revenge as chorus sleeps symbolically for the third and fourth acts, causing someone to miss his cue and label both of them the fourth act. In A Shrew, Sly as chorus also goes to sleep. So Shakespere’s device of sleeping through the act was not a startling deviation. Fraser (1962, p. 10): What could more emphatically make the point of the play than the living tableau with which Act IV opens: the sleeping lovers, whose love is engendered in the eye and, over against them, the delicate queen, cozened also by appearance, kissing the large ears of a clown. The enforced presence of the two couples is…made to serve, by juxtaposition, as an emblem, dumb poesy, of all the play. Holland (ed. 1994): The importance of the lack of a clear stage at this point is to alert us to the onward flow of the action: the sequence from the beginning of 2.1 to the end of 4.1 moves with extraordinary connectedness until the play leaves the wood for the return to Athens.
Rhodes (1922, pp. 72–3) opposes any idea of an interval, arguing that the actors must feign sleep for a long time, from [1511 to 1662], and asserts: I cannot conceive any producer adding to their misery by directing them to sleep also through an interval. Herford (1923, p. 19) suggests there is a short interval, only as long as Puck’s song [1497–1506]. Wilson (ed. 1924, pp. 158–9): An absurd arrangement which kept the four lovers lying in full view of the audience during the interval for music could hardly have been tolerated by Shakespeare.…If the King’s Men were determined upon their act, why were they forced to leave the sleeping lovers thus exposed while it lasted? Why did they not arrange for them to sleep on the inner-stage, where they could be concealed by the drawing of the traverse when the interval came? An effective answer to these questions would be that no inner-stage was available for the performance of which the [F1] text is a record.…It would seem, therefore, that the [F1] performance, if we may so call it, took place not in a public theatre but at some private house where no inner-stage could be provided. Are we to look for yet another noble wedding as the occasion of this private performance? Granville-Barker (ed. 1924, p. xvi): [The SD] is a clumsy business.…The lovers must lie on the outer, the open stage. They cannot be crowded together; besides, the inner stage will be needed a dozen lines later. Foakes (ed. 1984): The absence of this direction or of act-divisions in Qq suggests that Shakespeare did not conceive an interval here. However, act seems generally to have its modern sense from the early seventeenth century, and to refer to the action, not the interval.…It is possible, then, that this SD means They sleep through the action, i.e. through [1960], and is a prompter’s reminder that the lovers must remain on stage when Titania enters in [1509]; if so, it should be linked with the SD at [1620] in F: Sleepers Lye still; he presents his arguments at greater length on pp. 141–3.

1509–1745 Enter…death]
Johnson (ed. 1765): I see no good reason why the fourth act should begin here when there seems no interruption of the action. In the old quartos of 1600 there is no division of acts, which seems to have been afterwards arbitrarily made in the first folio, and may therefore be altered at pleasure. Clark & Wright (ed. 1863): [Johnson] does not alter the arrangement of the Folios, which, in the absence of any good reason to the contrary, we also follow. Verity (ed. 1893): As a rule, the IVth Act is the least important in the scheme of one of Shakespeare’s plays. It often marks a pause after a great crisis in the complications, and merely indicates how their solution is likely to be attained: the solution itself, i.e. the catastrophe, being reserved for the Vth Act. But in [MND] the IVth Act is very important, because in it…the two sets of complications that formed the main subject-matter of Acts II. and III. are practically brought to an issue, and the marriage of Theseus is celebrated. Thus the way is cleared for the performance in Act V. of the interlude in honour of the marriage. Furness (ed. 1895), quoting Johnson: It is precisely because there is so little interruption of the action that it is necessary to have an interruption of time, which this division supplies; he adds, evidently with 19th- rather than 16th-c. staging in mind: At the close of the last scene the stage is pitch-dark, doubly black through Puck’s charms, and a change to daylight is rendered less violent by a new Act. Phillips (ed. 1969): Here the unravelling of the knots begins. The climax of the unravelling, when the spells begin to work towards good and harmony, is crowned with dance and song. This is not only typical of the masque-form, which has had considerable influence on the organization of [MND]: it expresses the harmonising and unravelling power of music and of poetry and, no doubt, some of Shakespeare’s own belief about what it is that makes the world go round. It is significant, too, that the sane and calm Theseus reappears when the unravelling has taken place. What place could he have had in the world of misrule? On act division, see also .

1509–50 Clowne…Clo.]
In the SD and in the SPs in this scene Bottom is designated throughout as Clowne, or some abbreviation of that name. Several explanations are given. Fleay (1886, pp. 182–3) believes that there are at least two versions of MND, the stage play of 1592, and a revised one of 1595 or 1596. One of the traces of alteration he perceives is variation in SPs: such variations are almost always marks of alteration, the unnamed characters being anterior in date; he instances Clown for Bottom in this scene. See also Wiles at n. 895. Meagher (1997, pp. 146–7) thinks the change in SPs is because Sh. is thinking of the actor who plays the part, Will Kemp; and here he is evidently thinking of this part, as it reaches its full flower in 4.1, especially in terms of the generic type to which it belonged: the Clown. Kennedy (1998, p. 187) suggests that the changes are due to type shortage. See n. 1022–1506.

1509–10 Enter…them]
Ff’s duplicated entry for Oberon at 1559 (see textual nn.) has elicited some discussion. Granville-Barker (ed. 1924, p. xvii) believes Oberon must exit at some unspecified moment, and that Bottom must be retired to the inner stage, or he will be sadly in the way when Theseus and Hippolyta appear and wake the lovers. Most commentators and directors, however, find little difficulty in accommodating all actors. Styan (1967, p. 127): The lovers occupy the neutral corners of the stage, and the next entrance, that of Titania and Bottom, follows immediately. These two ignore, as if unaware of, the little heaps of lovers, nor do they see Oberon who has also entered behind them as a jubilant observer. The Fairy Queen and the Weaver play their grotesque love scene until they too fall asleep, and Oberon and Puck advance [Puck having been welcomed to the stage by Oberon upon his entrance at 1559] to survey the amorous carnage of this night accidents [1583]. Bodies are now strewn about several parts of the arena, and all the lovers are caught within the same magic. Foakes (ed. 1984): At the end of 3.2 the lovers all lie down and sleep without noticing one another, but their sight is fogged, and they can be grouped close together; this adds to the comedy, and makes good stage sense when they all start up (SD in Qq and F at [1662]) and kneel together before Theseus. Part of the stage is needed for Titania and her train, with Oberon watching behind [1510], and on their exit Theseus and his train enter, presumably by another door. Bottom remains asleep and unnoticed by Theseus or the lovers until [1726]. Thus four separate areas of the stage are used in this scene for the various groupings, and until Bottom is left alone at the end there are always between ten and fifteen actors on stage, depending on the number of fairies or attendants in the trains of Titania and Theseus. Wells & Taylor (1987, p. 284) omit Oberon from the SD at 1509–10, on the grounds that Sh. made no dramatic use of Oberon’s presence during the subsequent fifty lines, and that F’s reading presumably derives from the prompt-book. Holland (ed. 1994) tentatively agrees, but notes that the later SD is parallelled by F’s direction at [1385], Enter Oberon and Pucke, when they come forward, never having left the stage; if this parallel is significant it would tend to justify the presence of Oberon on-stage from the start of the scene. Oxford points out that Oberon’s previous encounter with Titania is structurally parallel to F’s form here if Oberon enters at [1559]: in both Titania dismisses her train and falls asleep; Oberon then enters and applies the magic juice.…In isolating the Titania/Bottom scene from Oberon’s gaze it allows their relationship its full innocence,…less practicable in Q’s version which comes closer to making the audience share something prying and voyeuristic in the husband’s watching, the cuckold peeping at his wife and her lover. F’s Oberon has completed his revenge off-stage in the brutal, taunting manifestation of male power and triumph he describes at [1563–78]. Q’s version displaces the significance of this description through its emphasis on the continued fascination the sight of Bottom and Titania has for Oberon. The play is of course full of silent spectators but Oberon’s silence in Q seems unusually gnomic; there is nothing in his subsequent behaviour to define his response to seeing Titania entwined around Bottom. Oberon’s first words, Welcome, good Robin [1560], however, tend to support Q’s staging; welcome is most commonly used by Shakespeare as a word of greeting from a character already on stage to one arriving. The editorial decision is finely balanced; readers or directors can make their choices.

1511–58 Come…thee]
Phelps (ed. [1851]): The admirable coolness with which Bottom takes the love of the Fairy Queen as a matter of course, orders about her tiny attendants as if they were so many apprentices at his loom, and conducts himself in fairy-land as complacently as in his workshop, is strikingly happy. Halliwell (ed. 1856): It requires a close examination to enable us to reconcile the discourse of Bottom, in the present scene, with the conclusions that have generally been drawn from his language in the earlier part of the drama. Here he is a clever humourist, and although, as throughout the play, exhibiting a consciousness of superiority, yet he is without his former absurdities. Is it quite certain that his wrongly-applied phrases in the second scene of the first act are not intended to proceed from his whimsical humour? Chambers (ed. 1897): The contrast between Bottom’s coarse tastes, and the dainty delights which Titania proffers to him, is humorously touched. The point is emphasized by making Titania speak in blank verse, and Bottom in prose. Priestley (1925, pp. 12–13): Even his remarks on the subject of music…and provender…have to our ears a certain consciously humorous smack, as if the speaker were not quite such an ass as he seems but were enjoying the situation in his own way. Riemer (1980, p. 74): Titania’s rapt love-words to the transformed Bottom are simultaneously deeply moving and farcical, transcendental and silly. Vickers (1989, pp. 33–4): After Titania’s quatrains—the most artificial verse-form in drama, presupposing as it does that the speaker has four lines already prepared, with rhymes, confident of not being interrupted—Bottom’s prose truly belongs to the world of unromantic everyday appetites:…Bottom may have been translated in shape but nothing can elevate him to verse and romance—apart, ironically enough, from his role as Pyramus, out of whose Pistol-like doggerel he is ever ready to step in order to explain the play:…Bottom is the antithesis not only of romance but of any appeal to the imagination.
Staton (1963, p. 178), commenting on parody in Titania’s wooing of Bottom: Instead of the lovely boy Adonis, we have a hairy beast-man, whose sitting down upon this flow’ry bed must have sadly depressed the flowers. Titania, on the other hand, must be small and delicate. Price (1983, p. 20), arguing against the vulgar distortion that Bottom is usually played as a corpulent, middle-aged, or even elderly, low comedian, notes that Titania addresses him as lovely Youth in The Fairy-Queen (1692, p. 29; also Charming Youth p. 27), and that in the frontispiece to Bell’s edition (1774) he is a tall, slim, handsome young man, with a handsome ass’s face, with whom it is credible that Titania might fall in love.
On parallels with Apuleius, and with the tales of Midas, Acteon, and Pasiphae, and for further commentary on the encounter of Titania and Bottom, see , , . See also nn. 969–1020, 969–70.

1511–12 Come…coy]
Halliwell (ed. 1856) compares Dekker’s The Shoemaker’s Holiday (1599), 2.1.1–2: Here sit thou downe upon this flowry banke, And make a garland for thy Lacies head.

1511 flowry bed]
Wells (ed. 1967): It is possible that this would have been represented by a piece of stage furniture on Shakespeare’s stage. See also n. 946.

1512–14 While…eares]
Gerstner-Hirzel (1957, p. 113): Titania’s gesture is comical because of its contrast between action and word or rather between the action and its object.

1512 amiable]
Johnson (1755, 1): Lovely; pleasing. Halliwell (ed. 1856) adds to these two epithets: Worthy to be loved (OED, a. 2).

1512 cheekes]
Webb (1989, p. 23): Symbolic of beauty and an object of delight to the lover [citing Tro. 4.4.118 (2512), AYL 3.2.144–5 (1342–3)]. Lovers’ caresses, venereal signs, etc. [citing Cym. 1.6.99–100 (711–12), Tro. 4.5.55 (2612), this line from MND, and several more].

1512 coy]
Johnson (ed. 1765) quotes Skinner: To sooth. Steevens (ed. 1778), adds to stroke and quotes three contemporary examples, one from Warner’s Albion’s England (1602), which is also quoted by Malone (ed. 1790, 10:579). Phelps (ed. [1851]): To caress (OED, v.1 2, quoting this instance). The Clarkes (ed. 1864): Fondle, smooth, pat. Wright (ed. 1877): Coax (OED, v.1 3a). Steevens (ed. 1793) compares Golding’s Ovid 7.161, Their dangling Dewlaps…he coyd, and 290–1, her hand had coyd / The Dragons’ reined neckes. See .

1513 And…head]
Moberly (ed. 1881): As dandies did sometimes, comparing Jn. 1.1.142 (150). Cf. also Nicholas Hilliard’s miniature Young man with roses (Victoria and Albert Museum), where, though not actually in his hair, the roses are all around it.

1513 musk roses]
See n. 633, and especially the comment by Webb (1991, p. 150). Brunel (ed. 1974, p. 82): One is reminded of Lucius in Apuleius who must eat sacred roses in order to regain his human form [see ] (Fr.). Wiles (1993, p. 122), unconvincingly: Bottom’s garland of roses plucked from the bower symbolizes his sexual conquest.

1513 sleeke]
OED (a. 1), quoting this instance first: Of animals, their limbs, etc.: Having, or covered with, hair or fur which lies close and smooth, usually a sign of good condition or careful attention.

1514 thy…eares]
Halliwell (1841, pp. 18–19) quotes T. Hedley’s Ballad of Midas hys fayre long[e] eares (a one-sheet translation of Ovid’s tale, where Midas is described as having two eares as hath an Asse [line 32]; dated 1552? by STC, no. 18969.5). Cf. Chaucer, WBT 976; see . See also n. 1539.

1514 eares]
Webb (1989, p. 36): One of the organs of the body which receives erotic attention, quoting this line.

1514 my…ioy]
Scott (ed. 1898): Joy is used by metonymy for the object of delight or the person who caused the joy.…Still so used in Yorkshire. Gentle is an epithet of compliment combining the ideas of meekness and superiority. Levi (1988, p. 140): Why do the last three words make [the quatrain] so funny? The answer has to do with rhyming and timing, but also with convention and inappropriateness. The word gentle, meaning well-born, seems misapplied to the boorish Bottom. Knowles (privately): Is gentle also funny because it implies a mildness, whereas Bottom is irrepressibly boisterous?

1514 ioy]
See n. 397.

1515 Pease-blossome]
On this and the other fairies’ names, see DP and nn. 996–1015, 1003, 1004, 1005, 1010–14.

1517–18 Mounsieur]
White (ed. 1858): Mounsieur Cobweb:—The folio and the quartos always carefully mark this characteristic corruption of the French monsieur—often as it occurs in this Scene; but all modern editors, except Capell, have disregarded this trait of the old text, if, indeed, they ever noted it. Clark & Wright (ed. 1863) agree, stating that this spelling represents a pronunciation more appropriate to Bottom, like Cavalery a few lines lower down. We are aware, however, that the word was generally so spelt. The only dissent is from Wilson (ed. 1924): [Mounsieur] is just the old form, and was not intended as a mistake on Bottom’s part. OED (Monsieur, 1b) quotes this instance as a jocular use. Deighton (ed. 1891, at 1520) compares Pistol’s French in H5. Bristol (1985, p. 176), quoting 1520–7: Bottom, waited upon by the fairy attendants, is the parodic double of a nobleman waited upon by his servants. Andrews (ed. 1989): We should of course note how polite and magnanimous Bottom is in his new role as consort of the Fairy Queen. Bottom uses this Frenchified honorific eleven times from 1517 to 1533, in contrast to the familiar English forms of address at 996-1015. See also n. 1532.

1521 red hipt]
Irving & Marshall (ed. 1888): What Shakespeare meant by the epithet red-hipped is doubtful; many of the humble-bees (of the genus Bombus) have the lower half of the abdomen bright-coloured; one of the commonest species (Bombus lapidarius) has the three last abdominal segments bright red. One of the Apathi, a genus of bees much resembling the Bombi, but parasitical on various members of that race, has the upper part of the leg red. OED (Red, a. 14b) quotes this instance alone. Webb (1996, p. 98) compares AWW 4.5.6–7 (2487), red-tail’d humble Bee.

1521 Humble Bee]
See n. 986.

1522 thistle]
Neil (ed. 1878): A common wild plant.…Its leaves are spinous, its seed-cups globose, its feathery plume purple, and its stem tall and branched. Ellacombe (1878, pp. 229–30): It is the recognized symbol of untidiness and carelessness, being found not so much in barren ground as in good ground not properly cared for.…If the Thistles were not so common, and if we could get rid of the associations they suggest, there are probably few of our wild plants that we should more admire: they are stately in their foliage and habit, and some of their flowers are rich in colour. Cf. H5 5.2.44–6 (3031–3).

1523 hony bagge]
See n. 986.

1523–4 Doe…action]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Don’t fatigue yourself too much in doing it.

1523 fret]
Holzknecht & McCclure (ed. 1937): Exert, trouble (OED, v.1 8).

1523 your selfe]
See n. 126.

1524 action]
Andrews (ed. 1993): Performing of the deed. Bottom’s phrasing suggests that Cobweb’s exploit will involve high drama. Perhaps not just deed but with the connotation of an engagement with the enemy (OED, n. 11a), in keeping with the martial language of weapons and kill [1521]. Cf. 818.

1525–6 ouer-flowen]
Malone (1780, 1:118–19): It should be overflow’d.—Yet the mistake is as likely to have been the author’s as the transcriber’s. Idem (ed. 1790): It appears from a rhyme in another play [AWW 2.1.138–9 (747–8)] that the mistake was our author’s. But Steevens (ed. 1793): I perceive no mistake. Overflown is the participle passive. See Johnson’s Dict[ionary, 1755, To over-flow. 2, quotations from Bentley and Swift, both of which have over-flown].Agreeing, Chedworth (1805, p. 70) refers to Newton’s note on Milton’s Paradise Lost (1749), 1.502: overflown is sometimes used for overflow’d. Disagreeing, Seymour (1805, 1:48): Flown is the participle passive of to fly; flow’d, of to flow; and so of the compounds, overfly, overflow. However, OED: Overflown, ppl. a. [The original pa. pple. of overflow v.] = overflowed ppl. a. The meaning is generally agreed on: Knight (ed. 1839): Flooded—drowned (OED, Overflow, v. 1a). Deighton (ed. 1891), however, suggests smothered, and Brooks (ed. 1979) flowed over, and (cf. overlaid) perhaps suffocated. Knowles (privately): Bottom’s warning underlines the smallness of Cobweb.

1526 signior]
OED (Signor, n.), gives signior as a variant form. It is so used frequently in Sh. Wells & Taylor (1987, p. 283), add an exit for Cobweb here, saying QF elsewhere omit necessary exits. The action here has been obscured by the crux at [1532–3]. Andrews (ed. 1993, at 1524): It is not clear whether Cobweb exits to fulfill Bottom’s wishes.

1529 neafe]
Pope (ed. 1725): (Yorkshire) for fist. Theobald (1726, p. 146) connects this with Pistol’s use in 2H4 2.4.186 (1207). Johnson (1755, Neaf), quoting this instance: A fist. It is retained in Scotland; and in the plural neaves. Heron (1785, p. 111): Skinner derives it from the French nœud, a knot: more likely from the Cimbric kneffe, the fist. White (ed. 1883): Pronounced nafe. OED (Nieve, nief) gives the pronunciation of nief as neef, and derives it from Old Norse, noting that it is not found in the other Teutonic languages.

1530 leaue…curtsie]
Many follow the Clarkes’ (ed. 1864) interpretation, Cease bowing, referring to their note in LLL at 5.1.98–9 (1831–2) where Armado says remember thy courtesy—I beseech thee, apparel thy head, a polite command bidding a person who is courteously standing with his hat off to put it on; equivalent to Be covered. Both Schmidt (1874, Courtesy 2) and Wright (ed. 1877) appear to echo parts of the Clarkes’ note. Verity (ed. 1893) asks did fairies wear hats? Wilson (ed. 1924): Put on your hat. Mustardseed is making a profound bow; and Bottom, like other Elizabethan grandees, shows his affability by excusing such obeisances in his inferiors. Watt (ed. 1906): Possibly leave off bowing and scraping. Titania had told the fairies to nod to Bottom and do him courtesies [992], and a good deal of amusement could have been had from the business. Foakes (ed. 1984): Stop bowing. Bottom seeks to shake his hand (neaf), but the fairy continues to show respect, as instructed by Titania at [992].…Did fairies in any case wear hats? [cf. Verity] OED (Curtsy, n. 2; 3): 2. The customary expression of respect by action or gesture, quoting Ado 2.1.52–3 (463–4), but no other Sh.; 3. An obeisance…, quoting no Sh. Under Courtesy OED at 8 and 9 gives the same definitions as 2 and 3 in Curtsy. Knowles (privately): So Touchstone, playing the grandee to William [AYL 5.1.16–17 (2357–8)].

1531 what’s…will]
Deighton (ed. 1891): What do you desire of me? said as though he were addressing some great personage.

1532 Caualery]
Delius (ed. 1859): Derived from Cavalero, a fashionable mode of address, like earlier monsieur and signior. So Qq and F1: later editions [from F2 on—see textual n.] silently correct to cavalero [or Cavaliery] (Ger.). The Clarkes (1879, p. 761) list this among facetious titles. Deighton (ed. 1891): Bottom’s version of Caballero, Spanish for cavalier, chevalier, literally a horseman. Verity (ed. 1893, Glossary): Gallant, man of fashion (OED, Cavalier, n. 2); here used as a sort of title.…Shakespeare meant Bottom to blunder over what was still almost a foreign word. Wells & Taylor (1987, p. 283): The [Qq, F1] spelling is normal for the period, except in its odd -y suffix. Taylor(Everything, 2003, pp. 63–4), notes that both this honorific and monsieur were associated with Kemp who probably played Bottom (cf. Wiles, 1987, pp. 54–5, 74–5); he quotes Kemps nine daies wonder, where Kemp introduces himself as my selfe, thats I, otherwise called Cavaliero Kemp (1600, A3.r), and An Almond for a Parrat which is dedicated To that most Comicall and conceited Cavaleire Monsieur du Kempe (1589, A2r). Because Kemp was a burly, round figure, Taylor finds it ironic that in MND both soubriquets are attached not to Kemp-as-Bottom but to fairies which he envisions as beautiful, sylph-like figures.

1533 Cobwebbe]
Thirlby (MS 1725–33), referring to [1517]: It sh[oul]d have been Peaseblossom but perhaps it was Shakespeare’s forgetfulness, indicating the alliteration of C in the salutation. Many agree that Sh. erred. Some, like the anonymous contributor to Grey (1754, 1:64), note the mistake without assigning blame. Capell (1783, 2.3:111) doubts that this can be a compositorial error (though some, like Wells & Taylor (1987, p. 283) think it may be); he suggests: Cobweb laughs, and goes not, but joins the other in scratching. Sprague (ed. 1896): May not Cobweb have disobeyed the human ass? Some agree with the Additional notes to Knight’s Cabinet ed. (1856, 2:361), which is the first to suggest that Bottom is confused. Delius (ed. 1859) adds that it is the Poet’s touch, a deliberate mistake (Ger.).

1533–46 I must…fellow]
Halliwell (ed. 1856): The exquisite humour of this dialogue, Bottom’s discovery of his long hairs, and the singular dexterity with which his new condition is revealed without the discovery of his own transformation to himself, would hardly have required a note, had not a recent editor [i.e. Collier, ed. 1842] observed, in reference to Bottom’s praise of good hay,—this is consistent with the notion that Bottom really partakes of the nature of the ass: not so his declaration,—I must to the barber’s, &c.; he confuses his two conditions. Bottom’s desire for hay is of course involuntary, and has no connexion with any knowledge of his condition. Barkan (1986, p. 261): Only his head is transformed: that may suggest a changed consciousness (e.g., wanting to eat hay), but it also means that the victim cannot see his own metamorphosis. He is quite surprised, in fact, when he feels his head and discovers that he is marvelous hairy about the face.

1533–46 I…Barbers]
Holland (ed. 1994): It is tempting to find an echo in this passage of the story of Midas whose ass’s ears were revealed to all by his barber’s uncontrollable urge to gossip. See .

1533–46 must to]
See n. 522 (Thou…).

1534 me thinkes]
See n. 6.

1534 maruailes]
See n. 815.

1534–6 And…scratch]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Bottom compliments himself on his delicate sensitiveness, as he has before done on his various accomplishments, and does immediately afterwards on his good ear for music. Johnson (ed. 1894): Bottom calls himself an ass in the spirit of affectionate raillery without being aware how well he is looking the part. Gui (1952, pp. 257–8), implausibly: [Bottom’s] transformation…is into an itching, hairy animal, animal hairiness denoting sexuality and itching indicative of sexual tension.…This scratching is here displaced from below upwards…, and it can represent only some form of masturbative genital play that Bottom wishes to receive from the hands of Titania. Knowles (privately): Bottom is speaking to Cobweb, not to Titania.

1537–59 What…thee]
It seems evident that there is an intended contrast between Titania’s verse and Bottom’s prose, but problems of lineation and meter have elicited various emendations (see textual nn.). Q and F print 1537 as prose, although its rhythm and scansion are perfectly regular. Wilson (ed. 1924), mistakenly asserting that Q prints 1547–9 as prose: It is possible that the dialogue was originally all in verse and that Shakespeare, in translating Bottom’s part into prose, did not trouble to preserve the verse-arrangement in Titania’s shorter speeches. The decision of a few 20th-c. editors to print Bottom’s speech 1550–2 as verse has not been defended. See also nn. 1547–9, 1548–9. Hibbard (1981, pp. 148–9) sees these lines as the culmination of dialogues that are models of courtesy and good manners between Titania as Queen of the Fairies, and Bottom as the acknowledged though uncrowned King of the Mechanicals.…The antithesis is complete: verse rich in similes, a love of music, and an instinctive response to the wealth and beauty of nature, on the one side; prose, no figures of speech, only the most elementary conception of music, and a practical response to the demands of the stomach, on the other.

1537–8 What…loue]
Thiselton (1903, p. 60): Bottom is again (cf. [1020]) paying too much attention to Titania’s attendants. But there is, also, special point in her question, for Stubbes in the Second part of the Anatomie of Abuses, referring to Barbers’ establishments, writes…your musicke againe, and pleasant harmonie, shall sound in your eares, and all to tickle the same with vaine delight [1583, H1r].…Titania has, of course, no fault to find with Bottom’s amiable cheekes [1512], shaggy though they be. His mention of the Barber [1533] does not therefore suggest to her the process of shaving, and she assumes he is desirous of hearing the music to be met with in a Barber’s establishment.

1537 What,]
Cf. 436 and n. However, Titania’s intention seems more in line with OED (B. I. int. 1): Used to introduce or call attention to a statement.

1539 I…musique]
Halliwell (1841, pp. 19–20): How pointless is Bottom’s answer taken separately, and yet how full of rich satire and humour, if the speaker be considered a second Midas! Bottom had not, like Midas, received the asses head, as a punishment for his presumption, ignorance, and self-conceit; but, even in that point of view, the metamorphosis would have been justifiable; and, at the risk of being thought to overstep the bounds of probability, we are glad to convict our poet of one very good joke. The Clarkes (ed. 1864): The complacency with which the human Ass plumes himself upon his musical ear is delightfully characteristic. Hunter (ed. 1870): Weavers were noted for their love of music, quoting 1H4 2.4.133–4 (1093–4) and TN 2.3.58–9 (757–9). Lever (ed. 1961): [I have] a fair ability to enjoy music (eare draws attention to the ass’s ears on Bottom’s head).

1540 tongs…bones]
Thirlby (MS 1725–33): Clever & bones. OED (Cleaver1 2b): marrow-bones and cleavers: freq. referred to as instruments of rough music. But the first quotation is c. 1712. Grey (1754, 1:64): The key and tongs, and marrow-bones and clevers. The first rural musick, and both probably in use in Shakespeare’s days. Reed (in Steevens, MS 1793–1800 [added in Addenda, pp. 421–2 of Reed, ed. 1803]) adds a paragraph from Chauncy’s Hertfordshire describing a banquet for the King and Queen in 1633 where keys and tongs were played; and [added in v1803] quotes two lines from Marston: Yee wel-match’d twins (whose like-tun’d tongs affords Such musical delight) (The Scourge of Villanie, 1598, Ad Rithmum, E1r). Harness (ed. 1825; 1830): What is commonly called Rough music—played upon the fire-tongs by striking them with a bone or sometimes with a key (OED, Tongs, n. pl. 2b, quoting this instance first). Phelps (ed. [1851]): This reads almost like a fore-taste of modern Ethiopian minstrelsy (OED, Minstrel 4 and Christy). Halliwell (ed. 1856): [Bones] were played between the fingers, in the same way as we still hear them every day among boys in the streets (OED, Bone, n. 5b pl., quoting this instance first). Staunton (ed. 1857) has a lengthy note on the history of this barbarous kind of music. Ruskin (The Elements of Drawing 1857, Works 15:205), discussing why there are laws of composition he cannot explain, such as those relating to nobleness and ignobleness, or vulgarity: It being just as impossible, I think, to show, finally, why one succession of musical notes shall be lofty and pathetic, and such as might have been sung by Casella to Dante, and why another succession is base and ridiculous, and would be fit only for the reasonably good ear of Bottom, as to explain why we like sweetness, and dislike bitterness. Moberly (ed. 1881) is reminded of the rough music sometimes used, even in our own time, as an ironical welcome to a widower who has married again within the year. Phillips (ed. 1969), going further, claims: There is a reference here to the rough music (in some parts of the country called a Skimmington) used in villages up to the nineteenth century as a form of social comment. The villagers would make up a tin band, beating on kettles and pans, and parade the streets to the house of the person they wanted to condemn, sometimes shouting or chanting an appropriate slogan. Among the actions condemned by rough music were ill-treatment of a marriage partner, or a disporportionate marriage such as one between an old man and a young girl. The matching of Bottom and Titania certainly comes into this category. Knowles (privately) believes the more appropriate term for this behavior is charivari rather than skimmington, but questions the application of either here.

[1541] Musicke…Musicke]
See textual n. for this SD added by F1. Capell (1783, 2.3:111): [The SD] is certainly an interpolation of the players; as no such direction appears in either quarto, and Titania’s reply is a clear exclusion of it. Collier (ed. 1842): Such music [of tongs and bones] seems to have been played out of sight at this desire from Bottom; for [F1] has [this SD]. Halliwell (ed. 1856): Probably an editorial addition.…A mere example of the tongs behind the scenes may, however, be all that is intended. The Clarkes (1879, p. 711) say that Titania’s next line gives it to be inferred that it was not the poet-author, but the theatrical manager, who inserted this stage direction, that a treat might be afforded to those among the audience who had asses’ ears as well as Bully Bottom. Granville-Barker (ed. 1924, p. xxv): [This SD] may fairly be held suspect. Not to speak of its absence from the Quartos, the run of the text here almost forbids any such interruption. The only likely occasion for it is when Peas-blossom and company have been dismissed. There would be a pleasing, fantastic irony in little Titania and her monster being lulled to sleep by the distant sound of the tongs and the bones; it would make a properly dramatic contrast to the still music for which she calls a moment later, her hand in Oberon’s again. Brooks (ed. 1979), citing Greg (1955, p. 245): Probably it [the F1 SD] was concocted on the suggestion of the text. Rurall Musicke looks like a duplication: perhaps an elaboration, in preparing copy for F, of Musicke Tongs or the like in the prompt-book. Wells & Taylor (1987, p. 283): Although a prolonged musical interlude seems unlikely, background music is entirely possible. Moreover, the odd duplication in F[1]’s direction increases the probability that the action was always intended. It is difficult to explain why the book-keeper should add Rurall Musicke and then duplicate his own addition with the further addition Musicke Tongs. But if the vague Rurall Musicke stood in the fair copy, a book-keeper might well add the marginal specification, Musicke Tongs. The fair copy could, of course, have been autograph, and if so Rurall Musicke is Shakespeare’s own descriptive direction.
Long (1955, pp. 89–91) discusses whether or not music was intended to be performed at this point, concluding (p. 91): As far as direct evidence is concerned, the Quarto version is obscure. The Folio, however, provides a definite statement…that should be decisive.…[W]hat kind of music is described by the term Rurall? The reference to tongs and bones indicates that it was quite crude. Also, since the purpose of the scene is to draw humor from the contrast between the airy Titania and the earthy Bottom, we would expect the music he likes to partake of his nature. The instruments used…in Inigo Jones’s [Britannia Triumphans, 1637, B2v] would also have been appropriate for this scene. They include a rebec, a gridiron, knackers (bones), and tongs and key.

1543 pecke]
Johnson (1755, 1; 2): 1. The fourth part of a bushel;…2. Proverbially. [In low language.] A great deal (OED, n.1 1; 3).

1543 prouander]
Johnson (1755, Provender): Dry food for brutes; hay and corn (OED, n.1 2). Andrews (ed. 1989): An amusing reminder of Bottom’s Mortal Grossness [977] in contrast to Titania’s refined delicacy.

1543 mounch]
Andrews (ed. 1993): Bottom’s pronunciation of munch. However, Johnson (1755) presents the word as a separate entry quoting Mac. 1.3.5 (102), and, under Munch, quoting this instance, gives the definition To chew by great mouthfuls. OED gives mounch as a variant form of munch, and, quoting this instance, (v. 1): Said of persons audibly masticating food which offers resistance to the teeth, and of cattle chewing their fodder.

1544 your]
See n. 354–6.

1544–6 Me thinkes…fellow]
Nicholson (1881, p. 2) calls attention to Scot’s reference to the asseheaded man who must either eate haie, or nothing (see ), and later (1886, p. 543) cites it as evidence that Sh. knew The Discouerie of Witchcraft. Cf. Dromio of Syracuse, who is persuaded by his longing for grass that he is truly transformed to an ass (Err. 2.2.195–201 [590–7]).

1545 bottle of hay]
Johnson (1755, 3), quoting this instance: A quantity of hay or grass bundled up (OED, Bottle, n.3 1). Harness (ed. 1825; 1830): The old phrase for a truss of hay: hence the proverbial expression of seeking a needle in a bottle of hay (Tilley N97). Halliwell (1841, p. 80): Not merely a bundle, but some measure. Hunter (1845, 1:296): A bottle of a; hay, or ale, for the actor was no doubt to speak in such a manner that both these words should be suggested. The snatch of an old song that follows is in praise of ale not hay. Bottom sings, stirred to it by the rural music. Hudson (ed. 1851), erroneously: Bottle is…from the French boteau. More aptly, he quotes Charles Richardson, A New Dictionary of the English Language, 2 vols. (London, 1836–7), who says, It is still common in the northern parts of England to call a truss or bundle of hay a bottle. Halliwell (ed. 1856) adds to his earlier remark examples of measures of a bottle taken from a court-book of 1551: The halfpenny bottle of hay is stated to weigh two pounds and a half, and the penny bottle five pounds, and provides uses of the proverb. Of Hunter’s comment he states: The context does not appear to confirm this somewhat subtle conjecture. Furness (ed. 1895) also objects: I can find no trace of any song such as Hunter thinks that Bottom quotes; sweet seems scarcely a fit adjective for ale. But songs such as William Stevenson’s Back and side go bare might be considered sufficient ground for Hunter’s speculation on possible puns. Watt (ed. 1906): [Bottle is] a diminutive of bott, a bundle of hay. It has nothing to do etymologically with bottle. Corroborated by OED (Bottle, n.3 1): <a. OF botel, dim. of *bot, masc. form = botte bundle>.

1545 sweete]
On Sh.’s affection for this adjective, see n. 100.

1545–6 fellow]
Johnson (1755, 4): Equal (OED, n. 5b). Cuningham (ed. 1905) compares JC 3.1.62 (1270), no fellow. Proverbial; Tilley (F175); Dent (F181.1).

1547–9 I…nuts]
Thirlby’s (MS 1725–33) division of the lines is universally followed. See textual n.

1547 venturous Fairy]
Anon. (ed. 1887): One daring enough to fight with a squirrel. Baker (ed. 1896): For a fairy even a squirrel was a serious antagonist.

1548–9 that…nuts]
Commentators offer various suggestions to supply a supposed missing syllable. See textual nn. at 1549 for some emendations. Upton (1748, p. 372) scans hoord as a disyllable. Abbott §484 prolongs newe to add the effect of an extra syllable. Thiselton (1903, p. 60) agrees with this and would follow Q1 in spelling newe. Wright (ed. 1877): In the distinct enunciation of fetch thee the time of a syllable is gained, as in the case of moon’s [378], and night’s [1614] both of which he calls disyllables. In adopting Kinnear’s conjecture (see textual n.) Cuningham (ed. 1905): Titania, in her desire to gratify Bottom, would naturally seek to bribe him with the newest and freshest nuts, and, as Q1 has newe it is possible that the final letters of newest (-st) may have been omitted in the Qq from confusion with the final letters of nuts (-ts). However, it is highly unlikely that handwritten st and ts could be confused; see e.g. affrights, distild, wasts in Harington’s poem in Anthony G. Petti, English Literary Hands (1977, p. 80). Wells & Taylor (1987, p. 284) argue for adding off because it is a short word which is often omitted—though they reject in because it would not take the necessary stress. Most, however, agree with Sisson (1956, 1:130): The line as it stands in Quarto as in Folio may be spoken in more than one way to offer a reasonable rhythmical pattern within the orbit of the decasyllabic metre. Wells (ed. 1967): As it stands the line gives most effective stress to new nuts, as a special treat. See conj., .

1549 nuts]
So in Palingenius’s The Zodiake of Life, the shepherd tells his beloved Philetus: I would thy lap with Fylbertes fyll, and Nuts of diuers kinde (1576; ed. Rosemund Tuve, 1976, p. 43); see also n. 1566–7. Nosworthy (1982, p. 103), seeing this as confirmation that Sh. used Apuleius: Fresh nuts, as Bottom’s expressed preference for a handful or two of dried peas indicates, are not normally fed to donkeys. See also .

1551 pease]
Johnson (1755): [Pea, when it is mentioned as a single body, makes peas; but when spoken of collectively, as food or a species, it is called pease.] Guilfoyle (1883, n.p.): The form of Pea best known in the time of Shakespeare, was the field pea, most used as fodder for cattle, and probably not much eaten as a garden vegetable. The garden Pea was introduced into England about the sixteenth century, from the South of Europe, and has since then been much altered by cultivation. Wells (ed. 1967): In Shakespeare’s time this was both the singular and the plural form. It both means and sounds the same as peas (OED, Pease, n., etym. note).

1551 stirre]
Thirlby (MS 1733–47): Perhaps awake (OED, v. 16b). Holzknecht & McClure (ed. 1937): Disturb (OED, v. 4. fig.).

1552 an exposition of sleepe]
Delius (ed. 1859): Bottom means to say disposition (Ger.). The Clarkes (ed. 1864): Bottom’s grandeur for a disposition to sleep. Doran (ed. 1959): I.e. inclination. Hunter (ed. 1870): Meant for imposition. Hibbard (1981, p. 149): Editors and commentators are agreed that it is a malapropism, a mistaken version of disposition. They may be right, but if they are, why does Bottom call it an exposition of, not an exposition to? The more likely word that he would seem to have in mind is surely imposition, meaning the laying on of a burden. But even that seems a less evocative word than exposition; and so one is driven back to the conjecture that what Bottom really has in mind is some kind of portmanteau word, fusing the sense of extreme imposition into a single verbal unit.

1553 winde]
Johnson (1755, 8), quoting this instance: To entwist; to enfold; to encircle (OED, v.1 16a). Gill (ed. 1981): Wrap.

1554 be…away]
Theobald (ed. 1733): What! was She giving her Attendants an everlasting Dismission? No such Thing; they were to be still upon Duty. I am convinc’d, the Poet meant;—and be all ways away. i.e. disperse your selves, and scout out severally, in your Watch, that Danger approach us from no Quarter. Most editors adopt this emendation. Capell (1783, 2.3:111): [Theobald’s reading] departs least from old editions; and is, besides, expressive of the lady’s love-rapture, which is now in it’s acme: explanation is needless. Becket (1815, 1:275): Always away is right. It means not that the fairies were never to return, but that they should not presume to disturb Bottom—that during his repose they should keep aloof. The expression is according to the idiom of the French—Voila mes ordres; restez toujours a Paris.—This is by no means to signify that the person so enjoined should never return from Paris, but that he should make it his principal place of residence—that he should remain there until he was recalled. Jackson (1819, p. 15), unconvincingly, suggests altering the punctuation: Be always:—Away! In the Fairy language, meaning:—Be invisible, though present.—She orders the fairies to be invisible while her lover seeks repose.—Thus, Titania displays her power. Not mentioning Hanmer’s (ed. 1744) original change (see textual n.), Collier (1853, p. 106) says the manuscript-correction in the folio, 1632 reads be a while away. The error arose from the compositor confounding the words a while and away, which come next each other. Andrews (ed. 1993), along with Holland (ed. 1994) and McDonald (ed. 2000), returns to the Q1 reading: It seems much more in character for her at this moment to mean always—to get lost and stay lost until the Fairy Queen summons them back.
