She wants to be left completely alone with the paragon she dotes upon, and she is eager to comply with his every wish, including his request to let none of your people [attendants] stir me [1551]. Winter (2003, p. 93), discussing the significance in the play of flight and of the frequent repetition of away, notes that Titania tells her followers to remain far off, but also to avoid stasis, since it is of their very being to reject immobility (Fr.). See also n. 378.

1555–7 So…Elme]
Rydén (1978, pp. 81–3): In Shakespeare’s time (as today) honeysuckle and woodbine normally meant the same plant, i.e. lonicera periclymenum L.…As in dialects today, woodbine was however also used of other plants of a climbing habit, such as bindweed (Calystegia/Convolvulus) and ivy, honeysuckle of other flowers yielding honey. The problem in MND is that Sh. uses the two names concurrently. Referring to Furness (ed. 1895) for a summary of early discussion, Rydén analyses the two main interpretations: (1) If we accept the punctuation of Qq and F1 as that of the author, the sweet honeysuckle must be taken as appositive to (and synonymous with) the woodbine and entwist as used intransitively,…not otherwise recorded in absolute function.…(2) If we regard the punctuation as inorganic (with entwist in transitive function) there are two solutions: (a) woodbine and honeysuckle represent different plants, in which case woodbine may stand for bindweed…(b) [they] represent one plant, Lonicera, though different parts…the sweet honeysuckle referring to the flowers…, the woodbine to the rest. Rydén favors the last interpretation, which was originally proposed by Anon. (1748, p. 17), and followed by Johnson (ed. 1765) and Thiselton (1903, p. 61). Those favoring (1), that the two names refer to the same plant and are in apposition, include Warburton (ed. 1747), Thirlby (MS 1733–47) Capell (ed. 1768), Steevens (ed. 1773), Becket (1815, 1:275) Hunter (1845, p. 297), Sisson (1956, 1:130–1) and others. Those understanding two plants include Gifford (1816, 7:308) in a note on Jonson’s Vision of Delight (1617), Knight (ed. 1839), White (ed. 1858), and others.
Furness (ed. 1895): To me it makes little difference what specific flower Titania calls the woodbine; she means herself by it just as she designates the repulsive Bottom with two fairies busy scratching his head, under the name of that sweet, lovely flower, the honeysuckle; and as these two distinct vines entwist each other, so will she wind him in her arms. McDonald (ed. 2000): The bindweed, or woodbine, twists to the right, the honeysuckle to the left, creating an inseparable tangle, as the syntax implies. Shaheen (1993, p. 99) refers to Ps. 128:3 and Tilley (V61).
See further Farzààd (1946) and on the ivy/elm topos Demetz (1958); see also n. 1556.
General comments on the passage include: Beisly (1864, p. 37): The comparison made by the queen is strikingly beautiful. This flower, in Shakspere’s time, was regarded as an emblem of fidelity. Brown (1942, p. 134): What a master of unostentatious alliteration was Shakespeare! The magic of these lines owes so much to the subtle interplay of e’s and f’s, of g’s and r’s. Mehl (1969, p. 143), discussing the emblematic force of the elm/vine or ivy topos as used with tragic-ironic effect by Kyd in The Spanish Tragedy (ed. Philip Edwards, 1959, 2.4.44–5) and with comic effect by Sh. here in MND and in Err. 2.2.173–80 (567–74), contends that most commentators have overlooked the way in which the comedy of the enchanted Titania enclosing the donkey-headed Bottom in her arms is significantly increased by the stylized wedding emblem (Ger). Hasler (1974, p. 19): This nature imagery comes more convincingly from the lips of Titania in her bower than from the rather shrewish Adriana in the commercial ambiance of Ephesus. Titania’s lines…become a part of the all-pervading word scenery. Hackett (1997, p. 52) claims Sh. gratuitously renders [the emblem for married love] more genital by introducing the metaphors of the vaginal ring and the phallic finger. Several recent critics have found the lines erotic. Nosworthy (1982, p. 103) hears the language of inordinate desire. Neely (1985, p. 36): Their eroticism, the opposite of Oberon’s bestial fantasies or Theseus’s phallic wooing, is tenderly gynocentric: [quotes 1555–7]. Carroll (1985, pp. 154–5), claiming sexual consummation, finds these lines about as explicit as a Fairy Queen can get. Young (1988, p. 341): The Apuleian echoes and the inherent eroticism of the topos itself add a potent sexual dimension. Calderwood (1991, p. 422): The enringed finger is a familiar Shakespearean metaphor for coition, most explicitly in All’s Well (3.2.57–60 [1460–3]). Others are reminded of Ovid. Brown (1999, p. 67), noting that Ovid’s Salmacis, like Titania, is compared to entwining ivy (see ): The traditional emblem of feminine dependence is particularly well suited to these tenacious, clinging females. Nuttall (2000, p. 56): A sense of physical incompatibility is present in the faint Ovidian surrealism of Titania’s image of the female ivy encircling the barky fingers of the elm.…Think of Apollo feeling the breast of Daphne as the bark began to form over it, the heart fluttering under the roughened surface, at Metamorphoses, 1.554.

1555 the sweete]
See n. 611 (the milde).

1556–7 entwist…enrings]
Baker (ed. 1896): En…has the force of about, around. Miriam Joseph (1947, p. 51): The addition of a syllable at the beginning of a word was called prosthesis.

1556 female Iuy]
Thirlby (MS 1733–47) quotes Err. 2.2.174–80 (568–74), Thou art an Elme my husband, I a Vine. Steevens (ed. 1773): [Female] because it always requires some support, which is poetically called its husband, quoting Milton, Paradise Lost 5.215–17. Verity (ed. 1893) compares Horace, Odes 1.36.20, lascivae hederae, wanton ivy. Brooks (ed. 1979): Does Shakespeare deliberately substitute the ivy for the vine (the wife) because Titania’s embrace, like that of the ivy in [Err. 2.2.178 (572)], is not marital? Webb (1991, p. 22) compares Spenser, FQ 2.5.29.3, wanton Yuie.

1557 Enrings…fingers]
Henley (in Steevens, ed. 1793): There is…an evident reference in the words enrings and fingers, to the ring of the marriage rite.

1557 barky fingers]
Deighton (ed. 1891): The sprays [twigs] covered with rough bark. Thiselton (1903, p. 61): Probably Titania is holding one of Bottom’s hands the feel of the back of whose fingers reminds her of the ridgy bark of the elm trunk.

1559 Enter…goodfellow]
See textual n. Thiselton (1903, p. 61): The marking of Oberon’s entry here [by F1 etc.] amounts to no more than that he comes forward from the lurking place, whence he has been observing Titania and Bottom. See n. 1509–10.

1560–84 Welcome…dreame]
Gentleman (ed. 1774; at 1585): This speech has great merit, and with many others, makes considerable amends for some weaker passages. In the margin at the beginning of Oberon’s speech Grinfield (MS 1834–63, p. 300) comments: Elegant language and 1567–71 is marked by a line.

1562 dotage]
Sprague (ed. 1896): Senility? imbecility? silly affection? over-fondness? Brooks (ed. 1979): Infatuation. Gill (ed. 1981): Obsession. See also n. 117, 118 (dotes).

1564 fauours]
See textual n. Johnson (1755, 7): Something given by a lady to be worn (OED 7a). Rann (ed. 1787): Garlands. Steevens (ed. 1793): [Q1’s reading], taken in the sense of ornaments, such as are worn at weddings, may be right. Collier (ed. 1842): Either [reading] may be right, but savours seems preferable. Dyce (1853, pp. 62–3): Titania was seeking flowers for Bottom to wear as favours; compare Greene; These [fair women] with syren-like allurement so entised these quaint squires, that they bestowed all their flowers vpon them for fauours. Quip for an Vpstart Courtier, sig. B2, ed. 1620. White (1854, pp. 216–17): Mr. Dyce must have forgotten that savours, the word in the authentic folio, is supported by the fact that Bottom in this Scene expresses a wish for the sweet savour of a honey bag, and that Titania begs him to sit that she may stick musk roses in his head, and in a previous Scene thus commands her attendants [quoting 984–6]. The Queen of the Fairies was evidently seeking sweet savours. Alluding favorably to Dyce, Halliwell (ed. 1856): She [Titania] had, amongst other favours, gathered the musk-roses she stuck in Bottom’s sleek smooth head, [1513] having previously adorned his temples with a coronet of fresh and fragrant flowers [1567]. Dyce (ed. 1857, 1:cxciv): Bottom uses no such expression [sweet savour of a honey-bag] in this scene or any where else, except in a different sense at 895. White (ed. 1858) adopts fauours in his text, noting [F1] has ſavours, which is hardly distinguishable from favours. The last editors to put savours are the Clarkes (ed. 1864) who explain that it seems more in accordance with the passage that follows, where their own disgrace has the effect of pointing to the drops, as savours. See n. 382. Cf. 417 filly.

1564 this…foole]
Andrews (ed. 1989): This clown, whom she would loathe if she were not charmed into Dotage or infatuation. Holland (ed. 1994): Oberon’s terms are surprisingly strong, his hatred for Bottom, who has, at least to some extent, usurped his position as husband, surfacing in his speech.

1566–7 For…flowers]
Hankins (1953, pp. 238–9) compares Palingenius (1576, ed. Rosemund Tuve, 1976, pp. 42–3) where fairies have given thousand kisses and floured garlands to the lovely youth Philetus. Hankins further notes that the enamored shepherd who is speaking refers to his own hairiness and rough body.

1566 rounded]
Johnson (1755, Round 1): To surround; to encircle (OED, Round, v.1 11a). Wright (ed. 1877) compares R2 3.2.160–1 (1520–1).

1567 With…flowers]
Brooks (ed. 1979) compares Golding’s Ovid 2.33: with a crowne of fresh and fragrant floures (see ) and Spenser, Shep. Cal. December, 109, 112, fragrant flowers…dewed with teares. Cf. 397 and n.

1567 With coronet]
Furness (ed. 1895): Abbott, §89, refers the omission of the definite article here to that class of cases where it is omitted before a noun already defined by another noun. It seems to me, however, that it is, possibly, a case of absorption in the th of With. See n. 1127.

1568–71 And…bewaile]
Johnson (ed. 1894): The dew-drops, which formerly suggested the idea of bright pearls, now appeared to me like the tears with which the pretty flowerets did bewail their own disgrace (in being so used). Homan (1969, p. 75), unconvincingly: The dew falling from the flowers’ eyes is at one with the tears of shame shed by Titania, humiliated that she has been enamored of an ass.

1568–9 And…pearles]
See n. 224.

1568 that same]
See n. 188.

1569–71 orient…teares]
Cf. Montemayor, Diana (ed. Kennedy, p. 12): there fell out of those faire eies teares like orientall pearles. Rylands (1928, pp. 131–2): The familiar imagery and conceits of crystal and pearl and tears…are as abundant in the poems, as in Spenser and the earlier Elizabethans.

1569–71 orient]
Johnson (1755, 2; 3): 2. Eastern.…3. Bright; shining; glittering; gaudy; sparkling, quoting R3 4.4.321–4 (3106–9).

1570 flouriets]
Johnson (1755, Floweret), quoting this instance: A small flower (OED, quoting this instance). Wells & Taylor (1987, p. 284): [Qq and F1’s] anomalous i may be an error.

1570 eyes]
Steevens (ed. 1793): The eye of a flower is the technical term for its center, quoting Milton’s Lycidas 139, Throw hither all your quaint enameld eyes. OED’s earliest use of the word in this sense (n.1 10c) is 1772. Knowles (privately) compares Cym. 2.3.24–5 (986), and daisy = day’s eye.

1572–5 When…mee]
Holland (ed. 1994): Oberon’s view of the correct relation of husband and wife is reasserted brutally in the contrast between his taunting and her meek submission as much as in his success in being given the changeling. Unlike their on-stage quarrel in 2.1, this time it is Oberon who has taken the opportunity to speak at length, taunting her at my pleasure.…His sadism is offset by her drug-induced mildness and our realization that the spell has made her give up so easily the boy of whose significance she spoke so movingly in 2.1.

1572 at my pleasure]
Deighton (ed. 1891): As long as I pleased, with no one to stop me. Baker (ed. 1896): Freely.

1573 patience]
Trisyllabic: see n. 162.

1574 changeling]
See n. 393.

1575 straight]
See n. 1444.

1575 her Fairy]
A few editors think the plural is required here (see textual n.). The Clarkes (ed. 1864): The word bear [1576], that follows, gives the idea of more than one fairy carrying the human changeling child. Wright (ed. 1877): Her chief attendant fairy. He argues that here, as at 436, Titania gives the order to the fairy who was in immediate attendance. See n. 436.

1576 my bower]
Holland (ed. 1994): Contrasted with Titania’s at [1016, 1029].

1578 hatefull imperfection]
Walter (ed. 1964) refers to hatefull phantasies (639).

1579 gentle]
See n. 524.

1579–80 take…swaine]
Brooks (ed. 1979): The business is effected at [1596 ff.]. Cf. Scot, Witchcraft…bk V, ch. iii (see ).

1579–80 transformed scalpe]
Deighton (ed. 1891): This transforming head, this head with which he has been transformed: scalp is properly the skin of the head on which the hair grows.

1580 swaine]
Defined as a young man, a country servant, or a pastoral youth by Johnson (1755, 1; 2; 3), or, as OED 5, expanding on the third meaning, A country gallant or lover; hence gen. a lover, wooer, sweetheart. Oberon may be thinking the latter, ironically. McDonald (ed. 2000): The word had lower-class associations.

1581 other]
A few early editors emend to others but most agree that the singular form is used to designate the plural (Abbott §12). OED (B. absol. 4 α), quoting this instance: The remaining ones. Malone (ed. 1790, 10:579) compares 1H4 2.4.182–3 (1141–2) and 2H4 3.2.120 (1655). Wright (ed. 1877) and Verity (ed. 1893) give further examples.

1582 May all]
Thirlby (MS 1725–33) first conjectures all may; Grey (1754, 1:64 & errata p. xiv) is not alone in agreeing, but no one alters the text, and most follow Abbott §399: [Though the phrase] might be explained by transposition…more probably they is implied for [w]here there can be no doubt what is the nominative, it is sometimes omitted, quoting this instance and citing 1895.

1582 repaire]
Deighton (ed. 1891): In this sense from Lat. repatriare, to return to one’s country (OED, v.1 2, quoting this instance; cf. also n.1 2).

1583–4 thinke…dreame]
These lines are echoed in 2208–12.

1583–4 no…But]
See n. 343.

1583 accidents]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Incidents; cp. [Tmp. 5.1.306 (2303)] (OED, n. 1a).

1584 fearce vexation]
Thirlby (MS 1733–47) compares Tim. 4.2.30 (1580), fierce wretchednesse. Rann (ed. 1787): Lively impression, strong illusion. Schmidt (1874, 3; 1875, 2a, quoting this instance): Wild, disordered; agitation. Deighton (ed. 1891): The fancies by which one is tortured in a dream. Foakes (ed. 1984) compares Eccles. 2:17, All is vanitie, & vexation of the spirit (Geneva). The phrase relates to the play’s title, and reminds us that dreams can be nightmares, as well as delightful visions like that of Bottom. Sagar (1995, p. 41): Oberon’s word vexation marks the end of the process necessitated by Egeus’ entry Full of vexation [28].…The alchemist sought in his laboratory to correct the imbalance of matter. He used, like the poet, marriage (the chymical marriage, or coniunctio) as an image of the perfect union, fusion of opposites, which he sought. Shakespeare in the laboratory of the spirit often used terms from alchemy as images. Vexation is one such term, a dangerous but necessary stage in the great work where the mixture had to be violently agitated.

1586–7 Be…see]
Rushton (1867, p. 19): Shakespeare sometimes makes one word both begin and end his verse, as in [1586–7]; a sort of repetition which Puttenham [1589; 1936, p. 200] calls Epanalepsis, or the Echo sound.

1586, 1587 wast]
Less common in Sh. than wert. See Franz §173.

1586 Be]
A few notice the F1 reading. Halliwell (ed. 1856): The shorter line appears to be more suitable to the nature of a metrical incantation. White (ed. 1858): The insertion of thou in [the authentic folio]…is an accident.

1588 Dians…flower]
Witte (1996, p. 53) sees these two plants showing the essential tensions in the play between Diana and Cupid, between virginity (the bud) and sexuality (the flower), between celibacy and marriage, between the white mulberry and the purple (Fr.). See also nn. 543–50, 545, 547–9.

1588 Dians budde]
This flower together with its chief vertuous property is mentioned at 561–2 and 1407–18, as Thirlby (MS 1725–33) points out. Malone (ed. 1790): [It] is styled Diana’s bud, because it is applyed as an antidote to that charm which had constrained Titania to dote on Bottom with the soul of love [560]. Holland (ed. 1994): More important than a precise botanical identification is the transition here from the power of Cupid and Venus to the dominance of Diana. However, the various attempts to identify the plant yield interesting associations. Steevens (ed. 1793): The bud of the Agnus Castus, or Chaste Tree; he later (MS 1793–1800) quotes Macers Herball: The vertu of this herbe is to kepe man & woman chaste (1552, A1v). Halliwell (ed. 1856) quotes Gerard’s Herbal: Agnus castus is a singular medicine and remedie for such as woulde willingly liue chaste, for it withstandeth all vncleannes, or desire to the flesh, consuming and drying vp the seede of generation (1597, 4H7v). See also Lyte’s translation of Dodoens’s A Nievve Herball, 1578, 3N2r, and n. 630–7. Wright (ed. 1877): It is more probably a product of Shakespeare’s imagination, which had already endued Cupid’s flower, the heart’s ease, with qualities not recognized in botany; however, in support of Steevens he quotes the Chaucerian The Flower and the Leaf, 472–5 (Chaucerian and Other Pieces, ed. Walter W. Skeat, 1897, pp. 361–79). Neil (ed. 1878) refers to Pliny’s Natural History Bk. 24 ch. 18, which explains that the plant is called chaste for that the dames of Athens, during the feasts of the goddesse Ceres,…made their pallets and beds with the leaves thereof, to coole the heat of lust, and to keep themelves chast for the time. (1601, 2:187).
Other plants are suggested. Ellacombe (1884, pp. 80–1) proposes Artemisia, the herb of Artemis or Diana, also called Wormwood [Artemisia absinthium]. Root (1903, p. 51): It is as patroness and type of chastity that Shakespeare most often alludes to Diana.…In this capacity, Diana is antithetic to Cupid (or Venus), comparing Ado 4.1.57–8 (1714–15). Rohde (1935, p. 179): Possibly mugwort (Artemisia vulgaris). Harrison (1946, p. 178) quotes Lyte who refers to Pliny’s statement that mugwort was once called Parthenis, that is to say Virginal (Nat. Hist., 1601, 2:222). He then mentions the Agnus Castus and suggests [p]ossibly Shakespeare imaginatively combines two plants into one. Rydén (1978, pp. 76–7) reviews much of the discussion claiming that [i]n sixteenth century botanical nomenclature, Artemisia was applied to the mugwort, Artemisia vulgaris L., but not to the wormwood, Artemisia absinthium L.,…but presumably [Sh.] was merely equating the Greek and Latin names of the goddess [Diana] (irrespective of botanical reference).…The contrast bud (with a connoted sense of purity and virginity): flower should be noted. Vlasopolos (1978, pp. 25–6) argues for mugwort because the Agnus Castus dries up the seed of generation (referring to Gerard, as does Halliwell above), and is hardly an appropriate measure to ready a lover for his wedding, while the mugwort is…a powerful charm against…spells, especially to protect or cure the eyes.
Anon. (ed. 1887) proposes Moon-wort, i.e. Lunary, or Honesty. Gui (1952, p. 263) claims the bud is the nipple itself, part of Diana’s or Titania’s own anatomy. Cohen (1983, pp. 119–20) suggests the blending of two…plants, Euphrasia, or Eye-bright, of which the juice dropped into the eyes…taketh away the darkenesse and dimnesse of the eyes, and cleareth the sight (John Gerard, The Herball, 1633, p. 663), and Spurge which yields a milky juice and is commonly known as Virgin’s Nipple; traditionally it could only be gathered on St. John’s Eve (on Spurge see also OED, n.1 1a and v.1 1). Otten (1988, p. 466) argues that the plant should be Amaranthus which causes remorse (which in Sh.’s day meant having pity or compassion) and that it restores Titania and the lovers: [while] its force and blessed power extends to Oberon, referring to [1562]. Halpin (1843, p. 13), arguing for contemporary allegory in Oberon’s vision (532–49), refers to Bacon’s final prophecy in Greene’s The honorable historie of frier Bacon, and frier Bongay (1594, H7v-H8r) where he foresees the flourishing of so rich and faire a bud which will become Dianas rose, and claims: Shakespeare’s Dian’s Bud is the same as Greene’s Dian’s Rose bud; that is to say, the figurative Queen Elizabeth. Verity (ed. 1893, p. 151) tentatively concedes the possibility that the herb with which Oberon touched Titania’s eyes was a rose, and that Shakespeare chose the rose as a symbol.…It is possible…that these lines—indeed, the whole notion of this healing herb—are an allegorical way of saying that the maidenhood of the Queen is superior to the power of Cupid.

1588 or]
Thirlby’s (MS 1725–33) conjecture o’er introduced to the text by Theobald (ed. 1733) has been followed by all subsequent editors.

1589 force]
See n. 722. See also n. 958.

1590–1 my…My]
See n. 131 (my).

1592 Me…Asse]
Chaudhuri (1990, p. 30): A terrifying deep-seated exposure of love, drawing out a profound revulsion—or at least a profound apprehension—hidden at its core, is brought into sharp focus in Titania’s confession at the end of her affair: [quotes 1592]. This is a half-abashed, half-amused declaration from the frivolous Fairy Queen; but it is one of the most frightening lines in Shakespeare. A similar primordial fear is awakened…by Hermia’s dream of a serpent at her breast [804]. Both speeches begin with Me thought. Wright (1994, p. 66) quotes this as an example of lines that give pleasure by exhibiting an unexpected congruence between metrical pattern and authentically speech-like phrasing.

1592 Me thought]
See n. 804.

1594 came…passe]
See n. 1055.

1595 O…now]
Thirlby (MS 1725–33) notes that here and at 1598 the rhyme is interrrupted. Both lines raise textual questions; see textual nn. Wickham (1969, p. 188), questionably: As Titania awakes from her thraldom so she is consumed with shame and disgust at the folly of her conduct.…The destruction of sensuality is thus achieved in this instance by sensuality itself.

1595–6 doe…this]
All major editors since the mid-19th c. prefer Q1’s readings to those of Q2/F (see textual nn.). Only Thiselton (1903, p. 62) attempts to defend Q2/F.

1596 Silence]
The Clarkes (ed. 1864): Oberon enjoins silence while the spell is being performed which is to remove the ass’s head from Bottom’s shoulders, comparing Tmp. 4.1.59 (1717), 4.1.126–7 (1790–1). Walter (ed. 1964) suggests also that Oberon wishes to avoid arousing the lovers, [and] to postpone the explanations to Titania’s excited inquiry. Foakes (ed. 1984): Oberon calls for silence while soft music plays (in contrast to Bottom’s tongs and bones (1540) and the sprightly dance of (1603–4); this is to charm the sleep of Bottom, and of the lovers, so that they are not woken by the dance, and to symbolise the healing of their disordered minds.

1597–1603 Titania…Musick]
Foss (1932, p. 126), questionably: The music does not come to her bidding, he having put a stronger spell on the air, and she marvels why she is not obeyed and thus understands his greater power. Long (1955, pp. 92–3): It is the function of the music to restore the sleeping mortals to their senses. Here we see (p. 93) Sh.’s use of music to suggest the magical powers of the fairies, his adaptation of a…belief in the curative powers of music,…and his use of music, as in [TGV], to underscore the turning point of the play.

1597–8 musicke…sense]
Thirlby (MS 1733–47): That it might not be said that the dance [1603–4] should have wak’d em. Deighton (ed. 1891): Summon your fairies to play to you,…and overpower, more completely than ordinary sleep would do, the sense of these five.

1597–8 strike…sleepe]
Brooks (ed. 1979): In Diana, Felicia’s philtres cast Syrenus, Sylvanus, and Selvagia into a deep sleep, unbreakable by ordinary means (Kennedy, p. 186 [in 1598 ed., p. 124]). See .

1597 dead]
Cuningham (ed. 1905) compares JC 4.3.267 (2279) and Tmp. 5.1.230 (2219).

1598 sleepe…fine]
See textual n. Theobald (ed. 1733): [Quoting 1597–8 as in Q1] This, most certainly, is both corrupt in the Text, and Pointing. Would Musick, that was to strike them into a deeper Sleep than ordinary, contribute to fine (or, refine) their Senses? My Emendation, I am persuaded, needs no Justification. The five, that lay asleep on the Stage, were, Demetrius, Lysander, Hermia, Helena, and Bottom.—I ought to acknowledge, that Dr. Thirlby likewise started and communicated this very Correction. A few argue for retaining fine. An anonymous contributor to Halliwell (ed. 1856) claims, questionably: The word fine here signifies mulctare [cf. OED, Mulct, v. 2, To deprive], and consequently Titania does the very thing Oberon desires. She fines or deprives them of their sense. Porter & Clarke (ed. 1903): Such sleep as would fine the sense is not inappropriate to help on the efficiency of Dian’s bud o’er Cupid’s flower. Thiselton (1903, p. 63): The conjecture…gives a meaning weaker than the unaltered text, which explains…why the sleep is to be more dead than common sleep, viz. to purify and refine the sleepers’ sense of what has happened [OED, Fine, v.3 1]. This purification, then, is absolutely essential to the scheme of the drama, if it be only on account of Demetrius, in whose case the love-juice is never counteracted by Dian’s bud (see [1395–1418]), and who, without any modifying influence, would, in the future, nauseate Helena with his mad doting (see [625]); he adds a weak argument of the refining effect on the other young lovers and Bottom. Andrews (ed. 1989), implausibly: The word fine can be read as either end or finish off, [OED, v.1 3; 4, finish off (a building)] and of all these fine the Sense can thus be interpreted as an amplification of strike more dead / Than common Sleep. Cf. n. 7 (waues).

1599 howe]
OED (Ho, int.1 2): An exclamation to attract attention. This spelling is not recorded by OED.

1599 charmeth]
Schmidt (1874, 2a): To produce, to call forth. Verity (ed. 1893): Produces as by a charm. Not in OED in this sense, but cf. Charm, v.1 1c, 5a.

1599 sleepe]
For the SD that F1 adds at 1600, Musick still., see textual n. Collier (ed. 1842): [The SD] means, probably, that the music was to cease before Puck spoke, as Oberon afterwards exclaims, Sound music! [1603] when it was to be renewed. Dyce (1844, p. 48): [quoting Collier] Music still is nothing more than Still music: compare a stage-direction in Beaumont and Fletcher’s Triumph of Time,…where, according to the old eds., the epithet applied to Trumpets is put last; Jupiter and Mercury descend severally. Trumpets small above. The Music, instead of ceasing before Puck spoke, was not intended to commence at all till Oberon had said, Sound, music! The stage-direction here…was placed prematurely, to warn the musicians to be in readiness. Verplanck (ed. 1845): Still music [means] low and quiet strains. Staunton (ed. 1857): Soft, subdued music, such music as Titania could command,—as charmeth sleep; the object of it being to—Strike more dead Than common sleep—. This being effected, Oberon himself calls for more stirring strains while he and the Queen take hands—[quoting 1604]. Collier (ed. 1858): If, as Mr. Dyce…suggests, still music had been meant, the direction would not have been music still. He evidently does not understand the force of the adverb: he mistakes it for the adjective. Dyce (1859, pp. 62–3) answers Collier, repeating his arguments, adding only: To the said still or soft music…some sort of a pas de deux is danced by the fairy king and queen. Irving & Marshall (ed. 1888): The instruments used for still music would be, probably, recorders or flutes, and perhaps some stringed instruments. Baker (ed. 1896): This may mean music steadily, constantly, that is, lasting through the lines that follow, but it is more probably a direction to the musicians to begin playing softly here. They would increase their force when Oberon cries: Sound, music. Thiselton (1903, p. 63): Immediately in response to Titania’s call is heard that incidental subdued music with which all theatre-goers are familiar: the effect here being to show that the music is at hand ready to receive Oberon’s order Sound musick. Stevens (1964, p. 23): If, as is possible, Oberon’s own awaking of Titania was accompanied by music, then the direction to musicians to go on sounding is not so nonsensical. Walter (ed. 1964): Presumably it means that while Puck is busy with Bottom music sounds softly, and then ceases before Oberon calls for dance-music. Foakes (ed. 1984): Probably means music continues and relates back to F’s SD at [1541], as a prompter’s reminder that the musicians need to keep alert. Brooks [ed. 1979] interprets it, following Staunton, as meaning still, or soft, music, citing the SD for Still Musicke at AYL 5.4.107 [2682], but this is implausible in relation to the order of the words, and to the parallel SD at [1620] in F, Sleepers Lye still. For still meaning continues, Knowles (privately) compares the repeated SD storm still in Lr. (F1 1615, 1655, 1780, 1880, 1942).

1601–2 Now…peepe]
Deighton (ed. 1891): See things with your own foolish eyes, as you have been wont to do, and not with the eyes of an ass with which you have lately seen things. Leggatt (1999, p. 60): Productions sometimes devise ways of hiding the moment from the audience to make it seem more like magic when Bottom appears as his old self again. But the open stage of an Elizabethan playhouse, lit by daylight,…is no place to hide anything. I suspect Robin should remove the head in full view of the audience, so that we see Bottom emerge again as if we were in the tiring-house watching an actor shed his costume and his character, to emerge as himself. To watch the play’s magic at work is, simply and frankly, to watch theatre at work.

1603–10 Sound…iollitie]
Bates (ed. 1895): Note the fairy fashion of clinging to a rhyme. Compare [983–92]. Welsford (1927, p. 331): The rhythm of the poetry is a dance rhythm, the lines rock and sway with the movement of the fairies. Long (1955, p. 94) speculates that the dance here was a pavane. Wright (1968, pp. 44–5) comments on their reunion in harmonious wedlock. Dancing was a common analogue for the ordered movement of the cosmos to the music of the spheres. [Quotes Sir John Davies, Orchestra, 267–80]. Their new unity is reinforced by the single rhyme sound of the next eight lines. Brissenden (1972, p. 90): There is no direction given for when they are to dance; it is most likely after the phrase, whereon these sleepers be. A full stop occurs here in all the early editions, and the dance clearly has two purposes. One is to ensure that the lovers and Bottom sleep well and wake refreshed—the dancers will rock the ground as a mother rocks a cradle. The second, wider, meaning is to confirm the reconciliation of Titania and Oberon, and re-establish their domestic harmony. Brooks (ed. 1979, p. cxxiii): The dance…corresponds inversely to the dance of Titania’s fairies from which Oberon was absent, and to his earlier refusal to dance with her on her terms. And as they move in harmony, he announces the further ritual which will crown the fairy action. Howard (1993, p. 335): Oberon does not join a dance with just anyone; he initiates and leads the comely damsel of his fancy, establishing his dominance.… Oberon commands and Titania…accedes.…[T]his is more than a dance that betokeneth concord between man and woman; it is a dance that explicates the conditions upon which concord is achieved. See also Idem . Williams (Moonlight, 1997, p. 26): We do not know the type of dance done originally or to what music, but clearly it would have consisted of vigorous, vital motions, reestablishing their dynamic, complementary relationship, broadcasting this synergy to the elements that their quarrels had once so disturbed.

1603 take hands]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Join hands (OED, Hand, n. 47, quoting Tmp. 1.2.376 [521]).

1604 And…ground]
Delius (ed. 1859): The image is borrowed of a cradle, in which one rocks a child to sleep (Ger.). Anon. (ed. 1887): As the floor of a room is shaken by a party of dancers. The sleepers would be rocked to sleep. Johnson (ed. 1894): i.e., by their dancing. Fairy feet might produce on the ground an appearance of motion by the shaking of the flowers and blades of grass. Porter & Clarke (ed. 1903): This suggests a cradling of the sleepers’ senses in the dizzy maze of fairy dancing to faint and undulatory fairy music, which makes the ground seem to them to rock, rather than any actual earthquaking.
As at 1047, some commentators question how strong an effect fairy motions or stampe could have. In his edition of Jonson’s Works (1756, 5:275–6) Whalley proposed to emend rocke to knock (see conj.) because he did not believe that these dapper elves should shake or rock the ground; he compares Horace, Odes 3.18.15–16, Gaudet invisam pepulisse fossor Ter pede terram, [the delver delights to have thrice stamped on his foe the earth—Page’s tr.], and quotes Spenser, FQ 6.10.10.4, …many feet fast thumping th’hollow ground. Moberly (ed. 1881): Unless this is a too literal rendering of Horace’s Gratiæ…alterno terram quatiunt pede [Odes 1.4.6–7, The Graces…tread the earth with tripping feet—quatiunt means shake], the rocking must be a different process from the dancing. Wiles (1993, p. 125), alone: The long-delayed sexual act is suggested mimetically by the dance.

1605 are…amitie]
Wright (ed. 1877): Are again friends. It is difficult to say whether new is here an adjective or adverb. Probably the latter, as in [Ham. 2.2.488 (1528)]. Scott (ed. 1898) agrees: [The adverb] gives the better sense, the objection to it being that the line already contains the kindred adverb now.

1606 solemnely]
Schmidt (1875): Gravely, ceremoniously, formally (OED, adv.). Cf. n. 14.

1607 Theseus]
For the pronunciation see n. 26. For the genitive of proper nouns ending in -s see n. 182 (Venus).

1607 triumphantly]
Delius (ed. 1859): Festively (Ger.). Schmidt (1875, 2) adds: Rejoicingly. Cf. n. 23.

1608 faire prosperitie]
Warburton (ed. 1747) preferring Q2F’s reading posterity and emending faire to far (see textual nn.) glosses to the remotest posterity. Heath (1765, p. 56), arguing for Q2F unamended: The meaning is, And bestow on it the blessing of a fair fortune to all posterity; or, to come nearer the literal construction, And bless it so that the fortunes of all posterity who shall enjoy it may be fair. Endorsing Q1, Capell (1783, 2.3:112): The sense [is] simple, and obvious;—to the enjoyment of all fair prosperity, or, that all things may prosper in it: a properer fairy blessing (’tis thought) than either that which is made by the second quarto’s corruption, or the refinement of that corruption [i.e., Warburton’s emendation]. However, Mason (1785, pp. 70–1): Fair posterity is the right reading. In the concluding song, where O’Beron [sic] blesses the nuptial bed, part of his benediction is, that his posterity shall be fair: [quoting 2193–8]. Malone (ed. 1790) argues for the Q1 reading, quoting 446–8 and emphasizing prosperity. Halliwell (ed. 1856): Either reading makes perfect sense. Thiselton (1903, p. 63) chooses posterity, attacking Q1 because the idea of [prosperitie] is already contained in blesse. And how could prosperitie be other than fair? The tautological feebleness of the [Q1] reading should have been its death-blow.

1610 Theseus]
For the disyllabic pronunciation see n. 26.

1611 attend]
Schmidt (1874, 1b): = to be attentive, to listen (OED, v. 1b).

1612 Larke]
Harting (1871; 1965, p. 131) mentions other references to the bird in Rom. 3.5.6 (2038), Tro. 4.2.9 (2268), and Ven. 853–6. Johnson (ed. 1894): The fairies hearing a lark, the signal of Day, must fly away to the other side of the globe, where Night is just coming on. Bradbrook (1932; 1968, p. 42): This passage has a clear connection with the aubade in [Rom. 3.5.6–10 (2038–42)], but there the effect was merely episodic; here it is part of the structure, of the sense of the dissolving of the enchantments of the night. McKenzie (1964, p. 45): The sound of the lark, symbol of the ascent of the soul towards heaven, heralds the happiness of the end. Brooks (ed. 1979) compares Son. 29, ll. 11 f., Cym. 2.3.20 ff. (982 ff.), and Chaucer, KnT 1491 (see ).

1613 sad]
Upton (1746, pp. 172–3) quotes Milton, Sh. (Ado 2.3.221 [1044]), and Spenser (FQ 1.10.7) to show that sad means still, sober,…sedate,…grave (OED, a. 4b). Cf. nn. 422, 1264. Walter (ed. 1964): Perhaps without music and song. See note to [1020], where he suggests that silence is necessary to enter fairyland. Theobald (ed. 1733), apparently understanding it to mean sorrowful or mournful (OED 5a): Why, sad? Fairies, according to the receiv’d Notion, are pleas’d to follow Night. For that Reason, and for bettering the Rhyme, I think it very probable that our Author wrote;—in silence fade; i.e. vanish, retreat, comparing Ham. 1.1.157 (156). See textual n. Warburton (ed. 1747): Mr. Theobald…to mend the rhime, spoils both the sense and grammar; he gives the same meaning as Upton, comparing WT 4.[4.310 (2135)]. Becket (1815, 1:276): Warburton censures Theobald as though fade were used by him as an adjective, (fade silence) but fade is clearly a verb: go, vanish. Halliwell (ed. 1856) calls the alteration a curious…example of unnecessary emendations, and most agree. Furness (ed. 1895): Theobald’s emendation…was well meant, but it is not a success. The defective rhyme certainly exposes sad to suspicion. The rhyme is not defective; see Cercignani (1981, p. 175).

1614 Trippe…shade]
Rann (ed. 1787) compares 2169. Deighton (ed. 1891): Let us lightly follow the darkness of the night to that part of the globe which it will be shadowing. Phillips (ed. 1969): The fairies are pictured as darkness-lovers.…Their speed of movement makes them able to accompany or precede darkness around the globe when they wish. Brooks (ed. 1979) compares Spenser, Shep. Cal. June 25, Faeries…can chace the lingring night.

1614 Trippe]
Johnson (1755, Trip 4), quoting this instance: To run lightly (OED 1). In his note on Comus 961 Warton (1785, pp. 253–4) says Oberon orders his fairies to dance after his ditty Trippingly [2179]. But to Trip seems to have been the proper pace of a Fairy, instancing Comus 118 and this line. Brissenden (1972, p. 89): Flying was used for long distance and speed, and in one place seems to be synonymous with trip; [at 1614] when Oberon invites Titania to Trippe, she replies, [1617–21]. See also 2205, and nn. 436 (skippe), 2179 (trippingly).

1614 nights]
Here once again compositors, editors, etc. play with the metre by adding a foot, beginning with Q2. See textual n. On the see n. 611. Delius (ed. 1859): [Q1] omits the; in that case the final s of night’s and the following s of shade should have a euphemistic e added. Compare 378 (Ger.). Keightley (1867, p. 135) [Q1] has nights, of which I have made a dissyl[l]able, as being more Shakespearean than the night’s of [F1] and [Q2], which most feebly and inharmoniously throw the emphasis on the. This gen[itive] occurs more than once in our poet’s earlier plays. Kinnear (1883, pp. 84–5) alludes to 734 (Sleepe, his seat) as an example of the old form of the possessive case. Furness (ed. 1895): The necessity of making night’s a disyllable is removed by the slight pause which we are forced to make between night’s and shade, to avoid the conversion of the two words into one: nightshade. See also n. 378 (Moons).

1615–16 We…Moone]
Cf. Milton’s Comus 1016–17 as quoted in n. 378. Verity (ed. 1893) compares 552–3.

1615 Globe]
For other global and celestial references see 378, 529–30, 533–5, 1075–8, 1129–30, 1215.

1615 compasse]
Johnson (1755, 1): To encircle (OED, v.1 5). Greenblatt (ed. 1997): Orbit.

1616 Swifter…Moone]
Verity (ed. 1893) compares the Fairy’s similar boast at 378, and gives literary parallels for the wandering moon from Horace, Satires 1.8.21, vaga Luna; Milton, Il Penseroso l. 67; Shelley, To the Moon ll. 8–9. Scott (ed. 1898): The errans of the Latin poets, Virgil and Horace.

1619 found]
The SD added by F1 at 1620 (see textual n.) means, according to Collier (ed. 1842) that the Sleepers were not to be disturbed by the horns. White (ed. 1858): This [SD] is another of the many prompter’s guides which show that [F1] was printed from the stage copy. Wilson (ed. 1924, p. 158): [This SD] was a very necessary addition, seeing that the sleepers are to start up at the sound of horns after [1660] and might therefore easily mistake the horns which announce Theseus at [1622] for their cue. The SD is also helpful for readers of F, to avoid confusion over which characters are intended by the immediately following Exeunt.

1621 mortals]
The fairy term for human beings: for comment on humane mortals see n. 476.

1622 VVinde horne]
The placing of the SD Winde horne[s] on 1623, 1622, or 1621 signifies nothing: the horn is sounded as the ducal hunting party enters. The SD in Qq comes at the extreme right, under the SD Exeunt, and on the same line as the entrance SD for Theseus etc. But the page, F4, is crowded, and there is no room to put the horn SD on a separate line, as the compositor does in the folios and many subsequent editions. The sequence of the three SDs is clear: Exeunt, Wind, and Enter. Furness (ed. 1895): Again the mandatory direction of a stage-copy. Thiselton (1903, p. 64) quotes John Brand’s Observations on the Popular Antiquities of Great Britain, 1777 (rev. 1848–9, 3 vols.; rpt. 1970, 1:213) referring to the blowing with hornes on May Day at Oxford. These horns may, therefore, merely signify that May-day has arrived. Granville-Barker (ed. 1924, p. xxv), fancifully: The winding of the horns…should be quite elaborately symphonic. This is Shakespeare’s picturing of sunrise. Manifold (1956, pp. 42–3): The principal theatrical use of horns is to give notice of a hunting scene.…A deft use of hunting horns could imply, the scene is now a forest, where a hunt is in progress; among examples he gives besides MND are Tit. 2.2.10+ (711, 712) and Shr. Ind. 1. 15+ (18). Wells (ed. 1967): The horn was a signalling instrument, not used for music. Styan (1967, p. 43): Sh. so accustoms the spectator to the nocturnal mood in [MND] that the dawn of the May morning arrives…with a winding of horns as a surprise and a revelation. Dessen & Thomson (1999, p. 116): When linked to hunting the sounds probably consisted of one note blown in various rhythmical patterns depending on the stage of the hunt so as to create a considerable and specific aural effect. See 1662, 1661. OED (Horn, n. 13b) oddly overlooks Shn. examples of the expression, and gives the first quotation from Heywood, 1611; see also Wind, v. 2 3b, first example 1599.

1623 Enter…traine]
Booth (1979, p. 107): The entrance of Theseus and Hippolyta…, immediately after the same actors have exited as Oberon and Titania…—the entrance that caused Ringler to say that the kings and queens could not have been successfully doubled—particularly delighted the two audiences I observed as they watched the Brook production. Also speaking of the same production, Holland (ed. 1994): The actors simply by turning upstage and then walking back downstage can define the metamorphosis perfectly easily, indeed magically.

1624–48 Goe…heare]
Whalley (1748, p. 26) praises Sh. as excellent in his Imagery of hunting; for which I might appeal to so inimitable a Description of a Pack of Hounds, that there is scarce a Country ’Squire in the Nation, who hath heard of the Name of Shakespeare, but can repeat it entirely by Heart. Grey (1754, 1:66–8): This is a fine description of hunting, &c. and a pack of hounds: and none of our English writers upon that subject, have come up to it, excepting the ingenious Mr.[William] Somervil[l]e, who has borrow’d some of his images, quoting several passages from his poem The Chace (1735). Hazlitt (1817, p. 132): Even Titian never made a hunting-piece of a gusto so fresh and lusty, and so near the first ages of the world as this. Knight (1932, p. 164): The music thought here is most interesting, especially in its blending of diversity—which gives tempests—with unity, which is music. We have a musical confusion [1631], so musical a discord, and such sweet thunder [1639]: clearly this is another variation being played on our tempest-music opposition, and one which reflects the final impression of the whole of Shakespeare’s work: tempests dissolved in a sublime unity of music. Van Doren (1939, pp. 81–2): Had Shakespeare written nothing else than this he still might be the best of English poets. Most poetry which tries to be music also is less than poetry. This is absolute. The melody which commences with such spirit in Theseus’s fifth line has already reached the complexity of counterpoint in his eight and ninth; Hippolyta carries it to a like limit in the line with which she closes; and Theseus, taking it back from her, hugely increases its volume, first by reminding us that the hounds have form and muscle, and then by daring the grand dissonance, the mixed thunder, of bulls and bells. The passage sets a forest ringing, and supplies a play with the music it has deserved. See also Goddard (1951), quoted . Dawson (1978, p. 64): It is an index of Shakespeare’s imaginative grasp on his material that,…at a seemingly unimportant moment, we get a metaphorical enactment of the central action of the play. Dundas (1985, p. 110): There is a true carelessness of art that is beyond calculation. It is as though this carelessness is the point at which art and nature meet:…The musical confusion of hounds and horns in [MND] is of this kind, where nature and art become virtually the same. Holland (ed. 1994): The hunt is strongly associated with Diana as huntress and goddess of chastity and links to the new view of chastity the play is beginning to take.
Grey (1754, 1:65–6) is the first of many who note parallels with Chaucer’s KnT 1673–1702 (see ). See also Hippisley (1837, ). Poirier (1947, p. 485) quotes Sidney’s description of Kalander’s hounds (Arcadia ed. Evans, 1977, p. 115), noting in both the harmony produced by cries, horns and echo, and that the hounds are selected with particular attention to the pitch of their voices. Brooks (ed. 1979) cites Theseus’s participation in the Calydonian boar hunt, as recorded by Plutarch and Golding (see , ), and points to further analogues in Hippolytus’s hunt at the beginning of Seneca’s play and Actaeon’s in Golding’s Ovid (3.245 ff., see ). Hunter (John Lyly, 1962, p. 113) suggests the speech may have been prompted by the success of the hunting scene in Edwards’ Palamon and Arcite performed before the Queen at Christ Church in 1566, or that in the performance at court in 1571 of Hunnis’s Narcissus.

1624 Goe…forrester]
Warren (1983, p. 25) suggests that Theseus and Oberon (who likens himself to a forester at 1431) might meet in the forest: To connect the two figures for a fleeting moment as the glimmering night [452] gives way to the shimmering dawn would emphasise how Shakespeare contrasts yet relates the two worlds. Foakes (ed. 1984): Theseus calls for his gamekeeper twice in this speech, but like the huntsmen of [1659], he remains offstage; the sense of a hunt about to begin is created by means of bustle, the noise of horns and the dialogue.

1624 forrester]
See n. 1431.

1625 obseruation]
Hanmer (ed. 1744), questionably: Meaning the observance of the time prescribed for their nuptials. Johnson (ed. 1765): The honours due to the morning of May. I know not why Shakespear calls this play a Midsummer-Night’s Dream, when he so carefully informs us that it happened on the night preceding May day. See n. 177 and 0.1-0.3. Verity (ed. 1893): It is an anachronism to associate Theseus thus with May-day ceremonies. Shakespeare is thinking of Elizabethan England, not of the classical Athens. Andrews (ed. 1989), questionably: [Theseus] is also referring to his role as a noble lord surveying his estate to be sure that all is well [in ed. 1993: estate and showing it to his bride-to-be].

1626 vaward]
Johnson (1755), quoting this instance: [van and ward] Fore part. Rann (ed. 1787): The day is yet but young. Clark & Wright (ed. 1864, glossary), citing this instance: The advanced guard of an army.…Hence, metaphorically, the first of anything. Delius (ed. 1859) compares 2H4 1.2.176 (437), which OED (Vaward 1c) gives as its earliest example of the figurative use.

1627–46 musicke…horne]
Gervase Markham’s advice for achieving sweetenesse, loudnes, and deepnes of cry in a Kennel of hunting dogs (Countrey Contentments, 1615, pp. 7–8) is frequently referred to. Drake (1817, 1:284), having quoted Markham: Shakspeare, who frequently avails himself of the language, imagery, and circumstances attendant on this diversion, has particularly noticed, in a passage of much animation and beauty, the care taken to arrange the notes of the kennel, and the pleasure derivable from the varied intonations of the hounds. See also Baynes (1872, pp. 349–51), Cook (1904, pp. 494–5), and Fortescue (1916, 2:347). West (1865, pp. 10–11) quotes Sidney’s Arcadia: Their cry being composed of so well-sorted mouths that any man would perceive therein some kind of proportion, but the skilful woodmen did find a music (ed. Evans, 1977, p. 115). Verity (ed. 1893) compares Lyly, Midas 4.3.[5–8; 3:147]: Such as you are vnworthie to be…huntsmen, that know not when a hound is fleet, faire flewde, and well hangd, being ignorant of the deepenesse of a houndes mouth, and the sweetnes. Warren (1971, p. 138) refers to praise of the sounds of the Chase at Kenilworth, as described in Laneham’s Letter (1575, C2r).
Some recent commentators are less convinced of the attractions of this music. Stockholder (1987, pp. 29–30): As Theseus invites Hippolyta to the mountain top to hear [quotes 1631–2, 1643–5], the images of distanced baying suggest the temporary retreat of the grotesque violence, disordered passions, and misused authority that in King Lear will be represented by images of dogs and hounds. Birkinshaw (1992, p. 48), quoting Erasmus (The Praise of Folly, tr. Chaloner, ed. Essex House Press, 1901, p. 33): To the Goddess, Folly…such folkes also rave pleasantly, as preferre huntying before all other pastimes, protesting what an incredible pleasure thei conceive, so often as thei here that foule musike, which a horne maketh, beyng touted in, or the howlyng of a meny of dogs.…[T]here can be no question that Shakespeare is making conscious use of the Encomium here.…It is inconceivable that Theseus’s hounds…should produce sounds to enrapture any but the most deluded. Yet, by a mysterious alchemy the last act of the play will help to explain, Folly’s foule musike and howling [sic], though they lurk in the scene, are transformed to a tumultuous harmony that is almost sublime: [quotes 1635–8 neuer…cry]. What really triumphs in this joyous episode is unreason on a heroic scale.

1628 Vncouple…goe]
Some editors find this line hypermetrical and seek a remedy. See textual n., and conj. . Thirlby (MS 1747–53): Uncoupl’ i’ th’. Collier (ed. 1842): Although to omit let them might reduce the line to ten syllables, we are not authorized on that account to leave out words which probably came from Shakespeare’s pen. Hudson (ed. 1880): The Poet often intersperses Alexandrines among his regular pentameters; though, to be sure, he does it very little in this play. Irving & Marshall (ed. 1888) believe that the Q1 reading makes a very awkward and unrhythmical line; an Alexandrine being quite out of place here. We…omit western.…There seems to be no particular meaning in the western valley. The mountain, in [1630], is not particularized. Cuningham (ed. 1905): Let them is useless to the sense, as all that is required is already expressed in the single word uncouple.…Go in this line is…imperative.…The insertion of the words let them is a mere blunder…of a compositor.

1628 Vncouple]
Johnson (1755), quoting this instance: To loose dogs from their couples. (Couple 1): A chain or tye that holds dogs together. See OED, v. 1b, quoting this instance. Fortescue (1916, 2:347–9): Hounds seem always to have been wild, for they were invariably taken to the hunt in couples [quotes 1627–8]; on coupling, he quotes and discusses Shr. Ind. 1.16–29 (19–32). Brooks (ed. 1979) quotes Sidney, Arcadia, (ed. Evans, 1977, p. 115).

1629 forrester]
See n. 1431.

1630 Wee…vp]
Deighton (ed. 1891): The verb of motion omitted (OED, Up, adv.1 29b [Elliptic uses], quoting this instance). See n. 522 (Thou…).

1630 faire Queene]
Cf. Chaucer, KnT 1685; see .

1631–2 the…coniunction]
Deighton (ed. 1891): The harmonious blending of the baying of the dogs and the echo of that baying. Kittredge (ed. 1939) compares Tit. 2.3.17–20 (752–5). Cf. also Shr. Ind. 2.45–6 (197–8). Brooks (ed. 1979) compares Seneca’s Hippolytus: With the youlping noyse of houndes the hollow rockes shal ring (ed. 1927, p. 138). Creaser (2002, p. 81) finds an echoing con-fusion of three senses of that word: disorder (OED 5), tumult and commotion (OED 6), and conflation, or the con-fusion of intimate mingling and blending (OED 7; see esp. 7b: Fusion together, illustrated by a quotation from 1651: Perfect friendship…is a…confusion of two souls…A confusion, not only a Conjunction and joyning together.); later (p. 98) he sums up: The moment…is a turning point of reflection that gathers up the whole play, its drawing of order out of chaos, of harmony out of a Babel of voices.

1631–2 confusion…coniunction]
For the pronunciation see n. 159.

1633 Hercules…Cadmus]
Macaulay (1907, p. 33): Cadmus had been turned into a snake some generations before Hercules was born. This may be added to the list of Shakespeare’s anachronisms. Neil (ed. 1878): Shakespeare finely enters into the spirit of the antique mythology, hereby bringing together the two dragon-slayers in a hunting expedition, accompanied by the bouncing Amazon, [445] Hippolyta, in a land well known to Theseus. Porter & Clarke (ed. 1903): This unchronological hunting-party has caused question. But the allusion carries with it an atmosphere of godlike hunts in other times and other lands of myth, and is perfectly in keeping with [MND]. Besides, it is an inkling that preserves for us a glimpse of Shakespeare’s memory of Golding’s Ovid, iii., wherein Cadmus goes hunting, and of the passage in North’s Plutarch wherein it is written that some are of the opinion that Theseus went with Hercules against the Amazons, and gave him his Amazon wife (see ). Elton (1904, p. 175) sees the influence of Chaucer in the reference to Cadmus (KnT 1546–7, see ). Wilkinson (1955, pp. 420–1) suggests the mention of Cadmus derives from the casual association of the hero and Crete in the first lines of Goldings’s Met. 3 (see ). Bate (1993, p. 137): Not a direct memory of Ovid, but an elegant variation on him, for her image of herself, a beautiful huntress, in the company of the legendary heroes in pursuit of the bear seems to be adapted from Meleager’s hunting of the Calydonian boar in the company of…Theseus, and…Atalanta (Met. 8. 260–546). See also n. 1634, Beare.

1634 Creete]
Brooks (ed. 1979): Where Theseus slew the Minotaur and, thanks to Ariadne’s thread, escaped from the labyrinth; for references linking Theseus with Crete he cites Chaucer (KnT 978–80; see ), Golding (8.225–37; see ), and North’s Plutarch (see , ).

1634 bayed]
Johnson (1755, To bay), quoting this instance: To follow with barking. OED (v.1 5), quoting this instance first: To pursue with barking like a pack of hounds; to drive to bay with barking. Gill (ed. 1981): Brought to bay (i.e. drove the bear into a position where it was forced to turn and face the hounds).

1634 Beare]
Many argue for a change to Boar but only a few actually emend. See textual n. Theobald (Letter to Warburton, 1729, in Nichols, 1817, 2:235): Should it not be Boar? The Eryma[n]thian Boar…is famous among the Herculean Labours. Dodd (1752, 1:87) does emend, justifying his action by referring to the Calydonian boar hunt in Ovid, Met. 8.260–444, in which Theseus took part. Capell (1783, 2.3:112): The bear is no animal of such a warm country as Crete. But Steevens (ed. 1778) quotes Chaucer, KnT 2018; see . Tollet (in ibid.) quotes Holinshed (Chronicles, 1:206), the beare is a beast commonlie hunted in the East countries and (1:226), Alexander…hunted…the beare. He also alludes to Pliny, Plutarch, and Turberville’s Book of Hunting (1576, rpt. Oxford, 1908), and, (in Malone, 1780, 1:119) adds another reference to hunting the bear in Chaucer’s KnT 2150. See also KnT 1640; see . Malone (ed. 1790, 10:579–80): In [WT 3.3.58 (1500)] Antigonus is destroyed by a bear, and also compares Ven. 883–4 (where both boare and beare appear). Wright (ed. 1877): According to Pliny ([8.]83) there were neither bears nor boars in [Crete]. Supporting boar Kinnear (1883, pp. 92–3) quotes Ant. 4.13.2 (2811) and TNK 1.1.79, adding: Shakespeare never mentions bear hunting but once [in Ven. 884].…All the other allusions…are to baiting the bear. Warren (1971, p. 138) tentatively suggests the influence of Laneham’s description of Kenilworth, where around the garden walls at the Castle were several white bearz, all of stone upon theyr curioouz basez [Letter, 1575, I2v]. Referring, like Dodd above, to the Calydonian boar hunt in Ovid, Bate (1993, p. 137) concludes differently: The transformation of Ovidian boar into Shakespearian bear is imitatio at its quickest.

1635 hounds…Sparta]
See n. 1640–7.

1636 gallant chiding]
Rann (ed. 1787): Melodious notes. Andrews (ed. 1989): Like sweet Thunder [1639], this linking of seemingly contrary things is part of the musical…Discord [1639] Hippolyta is describing.

1636 gallant]
OED (a. 4a): Loosely, as a general epithet of…praise: Excellent, splendid. See also 291.

1636 chiding]
OED, vbl. n. 3, quoting this instance first: Brawling or angry noise: spec. of fox-hounds. Steevens (ed. 1773): Means only sound, comparing H8 3.2.197 (2072). Scott (ed. 1898): Chiding has here no reference to anger or reproach; it was used by poets of the sounds of inanimate nature. The chiding in this case was both brave and musical. Gordon (ed. 1910): Used of echoing noises, where the one noise seems to rebuke the other. Bevington (ed. 1988): Yelping. Mowat & Werstine (ed. 1993): Barking. See also n. 521.

1637 fountaines]
Questioning whether this should read mountains (see textual n.), Thirlby (MS 1725–33) quotes Shr. Ind. 2.45–6 (197–8), but in support of Q1 quotes Ausonius Mosella 167–8, adstrepit ollis | Et rupes et sylva tremens et concavus amnis [and all the hills, and shivering woods, and channelled river, ring with their cries, Loeb tr.]. Theobald (ed. 1733) reports that an anonymous person proposed mountains, but as we have the authority of the Antients for Lakes, Rivers, and Fountains returning a Sound, I have been diffident to disturb the Text; in support he quotes Ausonius, together with Ovid, Met. 3.499–501 (reading lacus for locus in 501), Vergil, Aeneid 12.756–7, and Propertius, Elegy 1.20.49–50. Thirlby (MS 1733–47): He [Theobald] had ’em all from Mr Jortin as Mr Jortin told me and as I supposed, gave him these places. Holland (ed. 1994) quotes from Laneham the excellent Echoz between whiles from the woods and waters in valleiz resounding the noises of the hunt (Letter, 1575, C2r). In support of mountains, Kinnear (1883, pp. 93–4) cites TN 1.5.272 (565), and, quoting JC 1.1.45–7 (52–4), observes that the river does not reply, the concave shores repeat the sounds. Irving & Marshall (ed. 1888): Crete is a very mountainous island.

1638 Seeme…cry]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Seemed all to share and re-echo the cry to each other.

1638 Seeme]
Almost all follow F2’s emendation, agreeing with Steevens (ed. 1793): Hippolyta is speaking of time past. Walker (1860, 2:61, 63): Final d and final e [are] confounded, quoting this instance. A few demur. Halliwell (ed. 1856): The first editions may…be correct, the present being sometimes used for the past tense. Sprague (ed. 1896): Is the present more vivid, as if she were again beholding the scene?

1638 cry]
Johnson (1755, 10): Yelping of dogs. See OED, n. 12a. See also 1645.

1639 So…thunder]
Strutt (in Seymour, 1805, p. 48): Such a pleasing unity of things discordant: the lady means to express, in musical terms, that the harsh voices of the dogs and hunters, joined with the confused echo, was music. The Clarkes (ed. 1864): Sh. evidently felt the intense delight that lies in certain discords for the educated musician’s ear. Sidney’s Arcadia, like a musick made of cunning discords (ed. Evans, 1977, p. 497). Cf. West (1865, pp. 21–2) at n. 1856–7. Scott (ed. 1898): This line consists of two oxymorons. Cuningham (ed. 1905): In Shakespeare’s early poetic period, at least, he was particularly fond of these verbal antitheses, comparing 1855–6, Ven. 431–2. Proverbial; Dent (D351.1.)

1639 sweete]
On Sh.’s affection for this adjective, see n. 100.

1640–7 My…Thessaly]
Warton (in Steevens, ed. 1773, Appendix, 10:2L6v) was the first to call attention to the description of Actaeon’s hounds in Golding (3.267–9, 3.247; see ). Steevens (ed. 1778) compares Heywood’s Brazen Age, 1613: the fierce Thessalian hounds With their flag ears, ready to sweep the dew From their moist breasts; Wright (ed. 1877) corrects the last line From the moist earth. Dodd (1780, 1:230): Sophocles in Ajax, at the beginning, speaks of Sparta’s hounds of scent sagacious. Caldecott (MS 1813–33) quotes the encomium of the hounde…of Spartan kinde in the hunting scene of John Reynolds’ Dolarnys primerose (1606, D2v; also described at D3r is The hunting musicke resounding from the ecchoing wood). Halliwell (ed. 1856) quotes Golding’s Ovid and Lucan [Pharsalia, 4.441], and, among contemporaries, Jonson, and Oth. [5.2.361 (3674)]. Wright (ed. 1877) adds Vergil’s Georgics [3.405] and Callimachus. Fortescue (1916, 2:347) believes the reference to Spartan kind suggests that Sh. read Xenophon’s Cynegeticus; (see Xenophon, op. cit. at n. 1647, III.1, X.1 and 4, for the Laconian or Spartan hounds; the Cretan hounds are also mentioned favorably at X.1); but Moberly (ed. 1881) believes Sh. describes not real Spartan hounds, but a genuinely English pack, such as Sir Thomas Lucy may have ridden to. Brooks (ed. 1979, at 1647) compares Seneca, Hippolytus. Bate (1989, p. 137): Ovid’s Actaeon is almost certainly the source for Theseus’ My hounds are bred out of the Spartan kind. See also nn. 1624–48, 1627–46.

1640 My hounds]
Baynes (1872, p. 349): In enumerating the points of the slow, sure, deep-mouthed hound, it can hardly be doubted he had in view the celebrated Talbot breed. Elton (1904, pp. 175): [W]here the country was nearly covered with woods, it was necessary to use large and heavy hounds descended from the old slow-hound stock.…This seems to be the breed which Theseus praised to Hippolyta. Fortescue (1916, 2:347): Shakespeare apparently preferred the deeper and more robust music of the bassets.

1640 kinde]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Breed.

1641 flew’d]
Hanmer (ed. 1744, 6:Glossary): Flews are the large chaps of a deep-mouth’d hound. See also Warton at n. 1640–7.

1641 sanded]
Hanmer (ed. 1744, 6:Glossary): Of a sandy colour, which is one of the colours belonging to a true blood-hound (cf. OED, ppl. a. 1, quoting this instance first). Johnson (1755, 2), quoting this instance: Marked with small spots; variegated with dusky specks. Collier (ed. 1842): Sanded may refer to the sandy marks on the dogs, or possibly it is a misprint for sounded, in allusion to their mouths. See conj., . Dyce (1844, p. 49): Did Mr. Collier really believe that sounded could be used in the sense of having, or giving forth, a sound? Besides, the earlier portion of this speech is entirely occupied by a description of the appearance and make of the hounds…; in a later part of it, Theseus describes their cry.

1642 eares…deawe]
See Steevens (ed. 1778) at n. 1640–7. Deighton (ed. 1891): So long that they almost touch the ground.

1643 Crooke kneed]
Schmidt (1874 ): Bandylegged. See OED, Crook, a. C. Comb. b.

1643 deawlapt]
Johnson (1755, Dewlapt): Furnished with dewlaps, quoting Tmp. 3.3.45 (1574). See n. 421 (dewlop). Neil (ed. 1878): Fleshy-throated. Brooks (ed. 1979) compares Golding’s Ovid 7.161: Their [the bulls] dangling Dewlaps…he [Jason] coyd (see )

1643 Thessalian Buls]
Brooks (ed. 1979): For Thessalian bulls, see Seneca, Hippolytus, 296–8. On Thessaly see Ant. 4.13.2 (2811), and n. 1647.

1644 Slowe in pursuit]
Markham lists among the divers kinds of hounds the slow-hound, which is a large great dogge, tall, and heavy, particularly suitable for hunting in woodland, and Mountainous Countries (Countrey Contentments, 1615, p. 4). See also n. 1640. Moberly (ed. 1881): Fast hounds would run down the chase too soon. Richmond (1971, p. 110), mistakenly, regards with some scepticism the judgment of a warrior who…trains his hunting dogs so that they are unfit for tracking, quoting this line. Homan (2004, p. 75): Careful or dogged in pursuit.

1644 matcht…bels]
Delius (ed. 1859): Mouth = The yelping, the barking of dogs. The barking of the different hunting dogs formed [bildete] a complete scale; like the individual [einzelnen] bells in the carillon (or chime), each one sounding more deeply than the other (Ger.). The Clarkes (ed. 1864): The present editors once had an opportunity of observing the singular felicity of Shakespeare’s simile in this sentence. Chancing to be near a kennel of hounds,…the dogs set up their tuneable cry, and as it chimed musically forth, the very sound of bells here poetically recorded was sensibly audible, and caused the hearers to look at each other in sudden recognition of the simile’s truth. Irving & Marshall (ed. 1888) compare John Day’s The Ile of Guls (1606, C2v), and Henry Shirley’s The Martyr’d Souldier (1638, D4r-v), which are possibly reminiscent of MND.

1644 mouth]
Johnson (1755, 5): Cry; voice, quoting H5 2.4.69–71 (961–3) (OED, n. 3 p, quoting this instance).

1645 cry]
Hunter (ed. 1870): A pack. Doran (ed. 1959): Noise of the pack, hence the pack itself. See OED, n. 13a, quoting this instance first. See also n. 1638.

1645 tunable]
See n. 196.

1646 hollowd]
Johnson (1755, To Holla): To cry out loudly. Ibid. (To Hollow): To shout; to hoot. Hunter (ed. 1870): Holla! was the cry with which a sportsman called to the hounds. See OED, Hollo, Hollow, Holla v. 1b, where this instance antedates the first one given there (TNK 2.2.46–8).

1646 cheerd]
Johnson (1755, 1): To incite; to encourage; to inspirit. See OED, v. 7, quoting this instance.

1647 In…Thessaly]
Moberly (ed. 1881): All three the homes of aristocracies, and therefore likely to have citizens leisurely and rich enough to delight in hunting. Xenophon’s view is that the gods gave the skill in this noble science to the Thessalian centaur Chiron, and that Theseus was one of his early pupils. See Xenophon, Cynegeticus, in Scripta Minora, trans. E. C. Marchant, Loeb, 1925, I.1–2.

1648 Iudge…these]
Sutherland (2000, p. 147): We never do hear; she never does judge.

1648 But…these]
Grey (1754, 1:68): Theseus expresses a surprize upon his first seeing the two duelists, Palamon and Arcite [KnT 1703; see ].

1648 soft]
Johnson (1755, interj.): Hold; stop; not so fast. See OED, adv. 8a. Kittredge (ed. 1939): Wait a moment!

1648 nymphes]
Scott (ed. 1898): A playful suggestion that ladies found in the woods at night must be wood-nymphs. Also a compliment to their beauty. Black (1965, p. 53): Theseus has an eye for the girls only. See n. 624.

1649 this]
See textual n. Brooks (ed. 1979): Q1’s this is either a mistaken use of the colloquial contraction, authentic in [Lr. 4.6.183 (2625)], This a good block, or an accidental failure to repeat identical letters (haplography). Foakes (ed. 1984): The addition of is is required by the metre.

1649 a sleepe]
See n. 555.

1651 Nedars]
See n. 116. White (ed. 1883): This seems like a trace of some story on which the play was founded. Knowles (privately): Metrically interesting: technically decasyllabic, but missing unaccented syllables between This and Helena and between old and Nedars, which any actor would be as untroubled by as by the extra unaccented syllables (twice) in Helena. Four instances of metrical irregularity in one line perfectly suited to Egeus’s halting wonder—a nice demonstration of the difference between academic rules of prosody and flexible dramatic speech.

1652 I wonder]
Bates (ed. 1895): Why should he wonder? He apparently had neither attempted any guard over his daughter nor noticed her absence. Theseus, too, judging from the explanation he offers with royal confidence, counts it nothing remarkable that a woman with so grave a doom impending over her should go a-Maying.

1652 of]
See OED, Wonder v. 1, quoting this instance. See Abbott §174; Franz §513, Anm. 1. Cf. nn. 853, 1657. Malone (ed. 1790), objecting to the emendation at (see textual n.), compares AWW 2.3.123–4 (1025–6).

1652 their]
Q2’s error (see textual n.) is explained by Collier (ed. 1858): This was caught from the line above. Or from the two this’s two lines above.

1653–4 they…May]
See n. 177. Grey (1754, 1:68) quotes Chaucer, KnT 1673–5 (see ).

1654 right]
OED (n.2 ), quoting this instance first: Erron. spelling for rite n. Furness (ed. 1895): From the apparent confusion in the spelling of the words right and rite, we are hardly justified…in imputing ignorance to the compositors. They spelled for the ear (and probably by the ear), and not, as we spell, for the eye. More probably right should be considered an alternative spelling for rite, as rite is so given by OED for Right, n.1; right=rite appears five other times in F1, and rights=rites eight times. The reverse is also true, sometimes both occurring in the same play: see Ado 5.1.51 (2133) and 5.3.23 (2543), and cf. Ham. 5.2.389 (3885) and 5.2.399 (3900).

1655 in…of]
Wright (ed. 1877): In honour of. See OED, Grace, n. 1d, quoting this instance first.

1655 solemnitie]
Holzknecht & McClure (ed. 1937): Festivity. Doran (ed. 1959): Observance of May Day. See OED 1b. Deighton (ed. 1891), possibly having in mind the usage in 14: Marriage ceremony.

1656 Egeus]
For the pronunciation, see n. 27.

1656–7 the day…choyce]
The Clarkes (ed. 1864): Here…Shakespeare marks his dramatic time.…[T]he interval stated in the first scene [10–11] was supposed to have elapsed, and…the nuptial-day and decision-day had arrived. See . Deighton (ed. 1891): On which Hermia is bound to tell us which of her two lovers she accepts. But this was not the choice open to her; see 92–9.

1657 That]
Here used for at which time; when. See Abbott §284. See 2163.

1657 of]
Johnson (1755, 4): Concerning; relating to. See OED, Of, prep. 26, where the word, meaning Concerning, about, is used after verbs such as tell, relate. See Abbott §174; Franz §517; Blake 5.4.2 Of (g).

1662–1 Shoute…hornes]
See textual n. Thiselton (1903, p. 64): The Quartos would make the sleepers awake at the shout; and not at the winding of the horns in consonance with Theseus’ order. Wilson (ed. 1924): It seems probable that Shoute within was the original form of the réveillé, rejected later for the more appropriate hornes. It may be noticed that Egeus’ It is, my lord stands now as a broken line, which Go, bid the huntsmen shout would complete. Editors sometimes provide rationales for their rearranged versions of the SDs. Wells & Taylor (1987, p. 284): F’s addition clearly duplicates part of what it retains from Q2, since the lovers can hardly start vp unless they wake. Moreover, this combination of retained and added directions gives the impression that the horns precede the shout; Q1’s direction, by contrast, suggests that the waking precedes the horns. F independently confirms what may be suggested by Q: that Winde hornes was added separately from (and after?) the rest of the direction. Theseus’ speech specifies what logic would infer, that the shoute (Goe, bid) precedes the hornes, which wake the lovers. As here transposed, Winde hornes can be both a playhouse direction and the context of the off-stage shout. Holland (ed. 1994): It is clear that the SDs in Q and F refer to two different off-stage events, a shout and the sound of horns. The shout which must have preceded the horns is, I have assumed, Theseus’ order relayed to the huntsmen who are the horn players. Equally clearly, F envisages two different on-stage events: the lovers wake and then start up. I have produced an SD which combines these four events into a sequence which may appear too logical: the off-stage order wakes the lovers who then leap up, startled, at the sound of the horns. The lovers, of course, have no idea that they have all four fallen asleep so close to each other; their amazement is as much at finding each other there as at the sight of the court out hunting.
Long (1955, p. 95): Why would Shakespeare have wanted the lovers awakened in such a complicated fashion? One dramatic consequence we may at least observe: the music of the horns is closely related to Titania’s musicke, such as charmeth sleepe. [1597–8.] Her music marks the curative spell under which the lovers sleep; it is fairy music. The hunting horns end the spell and return the lovers to a world of reality. Fairy music charms; mortal music awakes. Brown (ed. 1996, pp. xvi–xvii), with possibly anachronistic sensibility: The lovers are woken from sleep by the sounds of hounds newly roused and in full cry—an aural representation, provided by Theseus, of a terror that would strike fear before provoking laughter…[T]his was a cry to set a bear at bay…[The device may serve to make the audience] wonder, for the moment at least, what strange beasts are about to unsettle the pleasures of the play’s ending. See also n. 1622.

1663–1726 Good…dreames]
Kott (1964; 1966, p. 223), idiosyncratically: The entire scene of the lovers’ awakening in the morning abounds in that brutal and bitter poetry that every stylized theatre production is bound to annihilate and destroy.

1663–4 Good…now]
Warren (1969, p. 133) suggests this subtle but pointed mockery of lovers is a characteristic derived from Chaucer’s Theseus (KnT 1785–6, 1798, 1801; see ).

1663–4 Saint…now]
Bourne (1725, pp. 174–5): It is a Ceremony, never omitted among the Vulgar, to draw Lots, which they term Valentines, on the Eve before Valentine-day.…There is a rural Tradition, that on this Day every Bird chuses its Mate; in a footnote he quotes Chaucer, PF 379–89. Steevens (ed. 1773): Alluding to the old saying, That birds begin to couple on St. Valentine’s day. See Tilley (S66). Rann (ed. 1787): The season for pairing. Caldecott (MS 1813–33): See Ophelia’s song on Valentine’s day [Ham. 4.5.48–66 (2790–2803)]. Douce (1807, 2:252–8), commenting on Ophelia’s line, connects the date and customs of St. Valentine’s day with those of the Roman feast of Lupercalia, and also gives various examples of Valentine poems. Laroque (1991, pp. 105, 112) describes the fecundity rituals of the Lupercalia: Similarly, in England, Saint Valentine’s Day was an occasion for games amid an atmosphere heavily impregnated with eroticism, and it is certainly by no mere chance that Thomas Nashe used this festival with all its customs as the setting for his libertine poem, The Choice of Valentines; he notes that Lysander arranges to meet Hermia in the woods, at the very spot where they first met during the May Day Festival [175–8]. In fact, the misadventures of Lysander and Hermia in the woods provide a good illustration of the temporary and inconstant nature of many of these unions, for it would appear that many of the amorous idylls initiated in May were just as fleeting as those of Saint Valentine’s Day.…[L]ike Saint Valentine’s Day, May Day was permeated with an atmosphere of erotic licence and made exciting by its many amorous games and pitfalls. He later (pp. 208–17) extends the connection to Midsummer festivities, noting that Sh. seems to have wanted to synthesize all these popular traditions. Wiles (1993, pp. 98–113) devotes a chapter to the structural importance of these three festivals to MND, discussing Saint Valentine’s Day particularly (pp. 105–113). He quotes the Paston letters to show the important part which Saint Valentine rituals played in shaping the emotional aspect of relationships within a system of arranged marriages, and cites Lydgate’s The Flower of Courtesy as exemplifying the tradition of the lover going into the greenwood on this day to lament his lovelorn state and watch the happily coupling birds; so too the lovers seek the woods, and Bottom sings of birds [942–50]: (p. 107) The bird motif ties the love of Bottom and Titania to the important Saint Valentine tradition which likes to contrast the natural coupling found in the greenwood with the constraints which society imposes on human beings. He finds the Valentine customs of the lottery, and that (p. 108) whereby one’s Valentine is the first person whom one sees when one wakes in the morning relevant to the plot structure of MND, since (p. 110) [a] random principle seems to govern the pairings of the young couples, and (p. 112) Titania’s awakening to the sight of an ass represents an extreme version of a common [result of the latter custom], a temporary liaison between people of divergent status. When Titania plies Bottom with gifts,…she behaves precisely as a Valentine ought towards a social inferior. As permitted by the ritual, the liaison ends as abruptly as it begins. He notes the linking of this festival and May Day in Chaucer’s LGW (F107–145), and concludes: Saint Valentine’s Day, May Day and Midsummer Night were nodal points within a pagan calendar that had its roots in Roman and Celtic practice. This calendar was only loosely tied to the Christian.…Shakespeare in A Midsummer Night’s Dream drew upon a popular understanding that the calendar was not a source of numbers and dates, but a source of meaning in relation to the mystery of life. He manipulated the calendar in order to give meaning to the phenomena of courtship and marriage.

1663 Saint Valentine]
Scott (ed. 1898): Used by metonymy for St Valentine’s day. Halliwell (ed. 1856): An amusing anachronism.

1664 Begin…couple, now]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Are these woodbirds so late in pledging their faith? woodbirds, because they had been found in the wood; the figure being kept up in couple. The superfluous comma is found only in Q1.

1664 but…now]
To couple only now; cf. Abbott §129, 130.

1664 to couple]
Holland (ed. 1994): (a) Join in marriage (OED, v. 3), as the lovers will go on to do; (b) have sexual intercourse (OED, v. 4, quoting this passage), as the lovers may appear to have been doing overnight in the wood, following common practice on St Valentine’s Day; (c) to join in pairs like dogs (OED, v. 1; compare Theseus’ use of uncouple [1628]); or more generally, to link together in pairs (OED, v. 2a).

1665–6 Pardon…vp]
Thirlby (MS 1747–53, at 1666): Did they kneel?
