Furness (ed. 1895): [Capell] here added a very superfluous stage-direction [see textual n.].…Whoever is so dull as not to see the meaning in Theseus’s I pray you all stand up, had better close his Shakespeare. However, Wilson (ed. 1924): On the contrary, without [the SD] a reader might imagine that Theseus’ injunction [quoting 1666] had something to do with the awakening of the lovers. Brooks (ed. 1979): Kneeling and rising were a ready form of meaningful stage-business, founded upon the significance they had in the rituals of Elizabethan society. They are used with supreme effect in [Oth. 3.3.460–9 (2110–20); Lr. 4.7.58 (2812); Cor. 5.3.50–7 (3399–408).] Here they point the appeal for mercy, and the grant of a fair hearing. Chaucer’s Theseus honours the distinction to be made in favour of those who humble themselves [KnT 1779–81; see ]. Knowles (privately) demurs: Mercy for what? They are asking pardon only for their indecorously lying on the ground instead of showing courtesy to the prince, which they immediately do.

1667 Riuall enemies]
Gill (ed. 1981): Enemies in rivalry (for Hermia’s love). See n. 1180–1.

1668–70 How…enmitie]
Deighton (ed. 1891): How does such gentle concord prevail in the world that hatred is so completely a stranger to suspicion as to sleep side by side with hatred without fearing any injury? i.e. how is it that you and Demetrius, who are known to hate each other so bitterly, should be found lying close to one another, each without any fear of injury from the other? for jealousy,= suspicion, cp. [H5 2.2.126 (755)].

1668 concord]
Olson (1957, p. 117), comparing Chaucer’s Theseus describing the faire chaine of loue as he resolves the tale (KnT 2987–3105; see ): This concord is a reflection of the concord between Oberon and Titania, between their [the young lovers’] loves. It suggests a return of the world of nature from seasonal disorder to a similar harmony. And the state comes to its own order; Theseus now preserves hierarchy by overruling Egeus.

1668 in the worlde]
Johnson (ed. 1894): Into existence. OED (n. 20a): In the world. on earth, in existence.

1669–70 hatred…hate]
Sipe (1968, p. 104): Alternation determined by the meter can clearly be seen in the use of the two forms here. Knowles (privately) demurs: Sh. uses polypton (Miriam Joseph 1947, p. 306) limitlessly, without its being determined by meter.

1670 To sleepe]
For the omission of as after so see Abbott §281.

1670 hate]
Rann (ed. 1787): The person hated. Evans (ed. 1974): A foe. OED (n.1 1b): The object of hatred. poetic, quoting Rom. 1.5.138 (721).

1670 enmitie,]
On the comma, see n. 66 (looke,).

1671–8 My…lawe]
Verity (ed. 1893): The confused style of the speech reflects Lysander’s confused state. See also Newman, .

1671 amazedly]
Johnson (1755, 1): Confusedly, quoting WT 5.1.187 (2951). Neil (ed. 1878): In a state of mental bewilderment, comparing Mac. 4.1.126 (1673). Mowat & Werstine (ed. 1993) add: As if lost in a maze. See OED, adv., quoting this instance first. See n. 1247. See also n. 488 (mazed).

1672 Halfe…waking]
Proverbial; Dent (A347.1), ODEP (p. 21). Wright (ed. 1877): Some editors regard sleep and waking as adjectives, and print the former ’sleep=asleep. Dr. Schmidt [1875]…gives this as an instance of the same termination applying to two words, so that sleep and waking=sleeping and waking. He quotes, as a possibly parallel case, [Tro. 5.8.7 (3504)], Even with the vail and darking of the sun. In this case however vail may be a substantive formed from a verb, of which there are ma[n]y instances in Shakespeare. I am inclined to think that both sleep and waking are here substantives, and are loosely connected with the verb reply; just as we find in [Wiv. 3.2.68 (1328–9)], He speaks holiday; he cites also TN 1.5.106–7 (399–400) and AYL 3.2.214–15 (1408–9). However, Cuningham (ed. 1905): The loose connection of the words with the verb seems to render Wright’s construction altogether too strained. Most editors regard sleep and waking as adjectives, not substantives. Brooks (ed. 1979): Perhaps being half of me (possessed by) sleep, half of me waking. Knowles (privately) compares Lr. 1.2.15 (349): Got ’tweene a sleepe, and wake.

1675 so it is]
Deighton (ed. 1891): This is the state of matters. Knowles (privately): True it is, I remember now, this is in fact what happened.

1676–7 Our…gon]
On the construction, see n. 121.

1677–8 might…lawe,]
Thirlby (MS 1733–47) conjectures might marry (proposing to substitute hence for Athens). Watt (ed. 1906): He was about to say marry when Egeus interrupted him. Most comment is on the addition of be (see textual n.). Collier (ed. 1842), supporting Q1: This is…beyond dispute the correct reading, Lysander being interrupted by the impatience of Egeus, with Enough, enough! The printer of [Q2] (which [F1] followed) added be after might in the preceding line, in order to complete the sense at Athenian law, but to the destruction of the metre, and in opposition to the clear meaning of the poet. Knight (ed. 1839): One of the quartos omits be, and leaves the sense incomplete. Singer (ed. 1856): The sentence [is] incomplete, which was most probably intended. Irving & Marshall (ed. 1888) emend as Hanmer, by which the violent ellipsis of [Q1], and the unrhythmical line of the other old copies, are both avoided. Thiselton (1903, pp. 64–5) favors the Q2 reading, treating Athens as a monosyllable, and admitting Q1 probably gives the passage as originally acted. See n. 145, 147, 149.

1677 where]
Wright (ed. 1877): Wheresoever. Deighton (ed. 1891): To some place or other. Sprague (ed. 1896): Wherever? anywhere? to some place which? See OED, adv. and conj. 9.

1678 Without…of]
Rann (ed. 1787): Beyond the reach (OED, Peril, n. 3c, quoting this instance only). See also n. 1201.

1678 Without]
See n. 175.

1678 lawe,]
Dyce (ed. 1857): [Q1] has a comma after law, to show that the speech is interrrupted by Egeus. See n. 66 (looke,).

1679–84 Enough…wife]
Cady (1929, p. 24): Egeus’s language is prolix, repetitive, it lacks restraint.…Authority, pride, disappointed love, all are there provoking the emotions; and the emotions…vent themselves in querulous garrulity. See n. 780–1.

1679–80 Enough…head]
Delius (ed. 1859): You know enough to sentence him according to the law (Ger.). Brooks (ed. 1979) compares Chaucer, KnT 1744–5; see . Arnold (1998, p. 15): When Egeus’s demand for his legal rights reaches an intensity worthy of Shylock and manifests itself as a hectoring challenge, Theseus simply dismisses him (1704). Sh. thus insists on the necessity of Theseus’s overbearing of Egeus; he makes Theseus’s liberation from Egeus’s political oppression the positive condition of the lovers’ liberation from Egeus’s sexual oppression. See also n. 1704–6, and .

1680 law…head]
Deighton (ed. 1891): I.e. The application of the law; comparing MV 4.1.142 (2051).

1680 head]
Johnson (1755, 2): Person as exposed to any danger or penalty. Cf. OED, n.1 35.

1681–2 They…away]
Lynch (1999, p. 104) suggests an echo of Chaucer’s BD; see .

1682 Thereby…me]
Deighton (ed. 1891): So that they might in that way disappoint both of us; comparing H5 4.1.166–7 (2014) and Son. 20.11. On the use of the perfect infinitive, see n. 121.

1682 defeated]
Schmidt (1874, 2): To disappoint. Verity (ed. 1893): Defrauded. Kittredge (ed. 1939): Deprived. Doran (ed. 1959): (1) Cheated, (2) deprived. See OED, v. 7; 7b. Knowles (privately): Thwarted.

1685–1701 My…it]
Chambers (ed. 1897): Demetrius’ fancy for Hermia is no less a freak of love, a bit of love’s lawlessness, than that of Lysander for Helena, although we do not see it brought about by visible enchantment. Newman (1985, p. 85): Demetrius’ long account…does not portray conflict, as represented in Lysander’s speech [1671–8], but simply reports his actions and aims in going to the wood. See also .

1685 stealth]
See n. 1345.

1686 purpose hither]
Gill (ed. 1981): Intention to come here. See OED, Purpose, n. 1b, quoting this instance, where the noun is used with vb. of motion implied.

1687–8 And…mee]
McDonald (2001, p. 6): Perceive the intensity created by the alliteration. Perhaps the hilarity, or the ridiculousness?

1688 fancy]
Johnson (ed. 1765), comparing 165: Fancy is here taken for love or affection, and is opposed to fury. Possibly also whim (OED 7a) or delusive imagination (OED, Fantasy, 3a). See also n. 40.

1689 wote]
See n. 1467 (wot).

1691 melted…snowe]
For the editorial gropings (Furness [ed. 1895]) to find a lost syllable, see textual n. and conj., . The addition of doth by Capell (ed. 1768) is supported by Malone (ed. 1790, 10:580) who quotes H5 3.5.50 (1429). Abbott §486 suggests that there is a prolongation of the…sound in the first part of melted so as to give a quasi-dissyllabic sound. See also n. 1485, 1487 (Yet…Heare). Proverbial; Dent (S593.1).

1692 idle gaude]
Doran (ed. 1959), following Delius (ed. 1859): Worthless trinket. OED (Idle, a. 3a): Useless. Cf. nn. 1193, 2211; 41.

1694–6 And…Helena]
Deighton (ed. 1891): All the firm loyalty of my heart has Helena for its mark, she is the sole object of delight to my eye.

1694 faith…heart]
Brooks (ed. 1979): His heart’s orientation to fidelity and virtue; virtue meaning also its especial quality (see [599–600]). Hassel (2005, p. 118), stating that Erasmus once calls romantic and religious faith and love nere sybbe, almost siblings (Praise of Folly [Tr. Thomas Chaloner, EETS 257], 122), cites this line among examples of the delightful Erasmian romantic-religious metaphor.

1694 vertue]
Kittredge (ed. 1939): Power (OED, n. 11).

1695 obiect]
Kittredge (ed. 1939): Beloved object. Farrow (ed. 1972): The centre of my attention. Brooks (ed. 1979): (Of the eye): cynosure.

1697 see]
Wells & Taylor (1987, p. 284) refer to their note on p. 273 to LLL 4.1.69 (1049): See is an archaic past tense, comparing 2H4 (Q1) 3.2.29 (1562). See OED, v. A. Inflexional Forms. 3, where see is given for the past indicative first and third person singular from the 13th c. on, though now vulgar. See Franz §166; Blake 4.2.4.1 (c). Johnson (ed. 1888, p. xv): See for saw occurs very commonly in dialect usage in Maine, and presumably in Northern New England generally, Soons he see me cummin, he run. See Golding’s Ovid, 8.136–7, where past tense she see is used to rhyme with the preceding shee.
Most editors emend to saw (see textual n.). Halliwell (ed. 1856): It is by no means unlikely that [see] is the correct reading, but it is so discordant to modern ears, the usual reading is retained. Keightley (1867, p. 136) assumes that see is an error both here and in LLL. Porter & Clarke (ed. 1903): [The] question [is] whether Shakespeare or his printers fell into the common dialect here, where [Qq] and [Ff] alike read see. Wilson (ed. 1924), (as Keightley above) noting the same error, twice, in [LLL ], asks, Can Shakespeare himself have been responsible? Brooks (ed. 1979) responds: In LLL, the compositor may have been set wrong by see (twice, correctly) in l. 72; in MND, by the tense of is in [1696].
A few read see as the present tense. Thiselton (1903, p. 65): It is difficult to find any justification for changing see to saw, the sequence being I was betrothed to Helena: I see Hermia and at once all is changed. Andrews (ed. 1993), who has see in his text, glosses saw. It may be that Demetrius switches to the present tense to convey more vividly the change of heart he experienced upon seeing Hermia. Others emend to did see, although this causes an awkward accent on Hermia. See textual n.

1697 Hermia]
For the pronunciation see n. 29.

1698–1700 But…wish it]
Foakes (ed. 1984): Compare the proverb Health is not valued till sickness comes (Tilley H290). Demetrius seems to move from I loathed this food like a disease, via an unstated and as if I were ill, to But now I am well again. This interpretation seems consonant with Chambers (ed. 1897): The grammatical apposition is somewhat loose, between a sickness and the substantive idea contained in did I loathe this food.
There are several less satisfactory interpretations. Capell (1783, 2.3:112): A sick thing, or, one sick; a common metonymy of the abstract for the concrete; he appears to influence Rolfe (ed. 1877), Sprague (ed. 1896), and Brooks (ed. 1979). Some draw on the emendation to in sickness conjectured by Farmer to parallel as in health and adopted by Steevens (ed. 1793). See textual n. Halliwell (ed. 1856) quotes from an anonymous passage in The Student (Oxford, 1750) where Farmer’s emendation is anticipated, and the paraphrase given, that is, like as I were in sickness did I loath, &c. See also Thiselton (1903, p. 65). Wright (ed. 1877): I am not satisfied with this reading [i.e., in], and the repetition of But inclines me to suspect that there is a further corruption. Furness (ed. 1895) agrees: The corruption…lies in the repetition of But.…I suppose that the latter But has intruded into the place of Now. The strong contrast between his present and his former state, which Demetrius emphasises, warrants the repetition: Now, as in health, come to my natural taste, Now do I, &c. As for Farmer’s change, it is as harmless as it is needless.
Cf. the commentary on the change of state from sickness to health in Plato’s Theatetus (167).

1700–1 Now…to it]
Brooks (ed. 1979) compares Sylvanus’s words when his affections have been altered by Felicia’s potion; see .

1702–6 Faire…knit]
Brooks (ed. 1979) compares Chaucer’s portrayal of Theseus’s magnanimity, citing KnT 1772–4, 1818, 3004–5; see , , . Duke Solinus similarly ignores the previously immutable law when he forgoes the offer of the ducats which would have paid Egeon’s ransom (Err. 5.1.390–1 [1878–9]; 1.1.18–22 [22–6]).

1702 fortunately met]
The opposite to Oberon’s Ill met. See n. 434.

1703 we…here]
See textual n. There is some brief tentative comment by Lettsom in Walker (1860, 3:50) on the unusual variance among the three early authorities, but most editors do not provide particular reason for their choice.

1703 here]
Variant form of hear, as at 143.

1703 anon]
See n. 387.

1704–6 Egeus…knit]
Andrews (ed. 1989): Demetrius is now determined to abide by his earlier betrothal contract with Helena, and Theseus would be failing to uphold another Athenian law if he now insisted on enforcing Egeus’ Will. Whether Egeus accepts this outcome without rancor is left for each interpreter of the play to decide; the text is mute. Langley (1991, pp. 125, 127) alludes to how Solon [i]n repealing Draco’s laws…had ensured that his own were written somewhat obscurely, and might be diversely taken and interpreted…In so muche as the judges by this means came to be somewhat above the lawe: for they dyd even expounde it as they would them selves (North, 1579, p. 96 [1595, p. 95]); see . Coullie (1992, p. 64), questionably: Rituals such as weddings function in the gender system to position both men and women.…The wedding as ritual coercion of the father is exposed when Theseus disregards Egeus’s desires. Holland (ed. 1994): Theseus asserts his power over Egeus (and also over Athenian law) with generosity but also with a full awareness of his power, not least to add to the glory of his own wedding-day. See also n. 1679–80.

1704–6 ouerbeare]
Hunter (ed. 1870): Overrule (OED, v. 2b). Cf. 467.

1705 by…by]
See n. 900.

1706 knit]
See n. 183 (knitteth).

1707 for]
Johnson (1755, 1): Because. See Abbott §151. See Franz §560. Cf. AYL 3.2.126 (1324).

1707 somthing worne]
Johnson (ed. 1894): Somewhat advanced. The hours have passed rapidly since [1626]. On somthing, see n. 1338.

1707 worne]
Wright (ed. 1877): Exhausted, consumed, wasted; used of time, as in [1828] and [Cor. 2.1.69 (965)]. See OED, Wear, v.1 17, quoting this instance.

1709–10 Away…solemnitie]
Halliwell (1841, p. 12) finds Chaucer, KnT 2700–2 bears too remarkable a resemblance to these lines to have been accidental. See . Cf. also Chaucer, MerT 1709–10 (see ). See n. 14. Brooks (ed. 1979) compares these lines and 1607, 1709 with the end, under Felicia’s auspices, of the lovers’ troubles in Diana: riding on their way, they came to Dianas temple.…There they were all married with great joy, feasts, and triumphes ([ed. 1968], p. 242). On the rhyming couplet, see n. 520–1.

1709 Away, with]
Verb of motion suppressed. See OED, Away, adv. 15. Cf. 192.

1710–11 Weele…Hyppolita]
Grey (1754, 1:69) observes that the addition by Hanmer (ed. 1744) (see textual n.) makes the speech identical with the one at 131. Capell (1783, 2.3:113) favors the emendation because without it the hemistich is not metrical. Wilson (ed. 1924): There is no mention of Hippolyta in the [Q1 SD] at [1623]; and she certainly appears to be an afterthought here.

1712–26 These…dreames]
The Clarkes (ed. 1864): In [Demetrius’s] and the few following speeches of the four lovers, the same effect of dream-like vision is still preserved. Cunliffe (ed. 1912): The charm still rests upon the eyes of Demetrius. Cody (1969, p. 143) aptly compares the discussion of waking and dreaming in Plato’s Theatetus (158D). Holland (ed. 1994): The lovers’ wonder here, in its beautifully tentative language, produced from Britten the finest passage of his opera, A Midsummer Night’s Dream (1960).

1712–20 These…dreame]
Gilman (1978, p. 159): Half sleep, half waking [1672], the lovers occupy a mid-point between two modes of experience and are capable for that moment of an apprehension that touches both worlds. Against the disjunctive either/or logic of Athenian law, the law of comedy proposes its own logic in which both/and is the governing conjunction. The lovers’ double vision also corresponds to the audience’s experience of the play, for we watch with parted eye, simultaneously aware that the dramatic illusion before us is both an airy nothing [1808] and something of great constancy [1817]. Riemer (1980, p. 204): The play goes beyond its concern with levels of reality, forcing us to examine the clear-cut relationship we presume to exist between daylight reality and night-time fantasy; it also suggests that the manipulation of this fantasy, whether proper or misapplied, may have results and significances that are among the most vivid and most important of our experiences. Nevo (Transformations, 1980, p. 108): Perception and self perception have passed through the alembic of dream and have been catalyzed. Draper (1991, pp. 32–3): The figures here are those of simile and paradox, which seem to separate and distinguish, but do so only to merge and identify. Solid mountains turn into vaporous clouds; possessions are owned and not owned; single things are seen double; and sleeping and waking, seeing and dreaming, are perilously near to being equated. Spevack’s (ed. Ant., 1990, p. 273) note on sources and parallels of Antony’s speech about how cloud shapes may be perceived (4.14.2–8 [2826–31]) is relevant to this dialogue.

1712–15 These…double]
Cook (1976, pp. 23–4): The retrospective look they take is spoken not from lover to lover but from a now successful suitor to the girl who will wed his former rival. [Quotes 1712–15.] The female vision of seeing doubleness in distinct entities caps the male vision of blurring and diminishing all things.

1712–13 These…clouds]
Furness (ed. 1895): Capell [1783, 2.3:114] queries if this speech should not be given to Lysander, but gives no reason. Probably, however, for the sake of a more even distribution of speeches. See conj., . Welsford (1927, pp. 325–6): It is a fine image, giving just that suggestion of awe and uncertainty which was needed to soften the transition from dream to waking life. The magic of the phrase lies in the words small and undistinguishable. A lesser poet would probably have given the abstract idea in the first line and in the second its concrete illustration. But the word small (instead of strange, vague or some other word of that kind) at once sets the imagination to work and suggests the picture which the next line expands, and the sound of the word undistinguishable, with its accumulated syllables trailing off into silence, does for the ear what the word small does for the eye, suggests the shimmering atmosphere, the blurred outline and the gradual vanishing of the distant mountains on the horizon. Barber (1959, p. 135): The teeming metamorphoses which we encounter are placed…in a medium and in a moment where the perceived structure of the outer world breaks down, where the body and its environment interpenetrate in unaccustomed ways, so that the seeming separateness and stability of identity is lost.

1712 These…vndistinguishable]
Deighton (ed. 1891): These matters to which I attached so much importance, now that I am awake and in my right mind, seem so trifling as to be scarcely perceptible. Andrews (ed. 1989): Referring to the events of the previous evening, Demetrius says he now has trouble visualizing them, as one does things far-off [1713]. Holland (ed. 1994): At [475] Titania has spoken of the overgrown mazes that have become undistinguishable; now the word is taken over by Demetrius, after the mazes the lovers have trod in the wood, to describe these things. But are these things the experiences in the wood or the strangeness of the encounter with Theseus and the new relationships in the waking, dawn world? Hermia uses the same phrase at [1714].

1713 turned into]
Rann (ed. 1787): The form of; almost lost in. Transformed into: see OED, Turn, v. 38.

1714–15 Me…double]
Bucknill (1860, p. 90): Another singular reference to the physiology of vision. By an effort of will, the axes of the two eyes can be parted, or held from converging, so as to produce the effect described. From a different point of view, Kott (1964; 1966, p. 223), hilariously: She is ashamed. She does not quite realize yet that day has come. She is still partly overwhelmed by night. She has drunk too much. Moffatt (2004, p. 186): [The lovers] are able, for a moment, to stand in the two worlds of dreaming and waking,…to see partly into the sylvan reality behind the newfound Athenian concord.

1714–15 these things]
Phillips (ed. 1969): This could refer to the dream experiences in the wood; it could equally—if the lovers’ minds are still with their dream, as often happens to people just awakened—mean the events of the present and the conversation they have just held. See Holland (ed. 1994) at 1712 above.

1714–15 with…eye]
Johnson (1755, To part v. a. 1): Divided. See OED, Parted, ppl. a. 1, quoting this instance first. Deighton (ed. 1891): As one would if one’s eyes were not in focus with each other. Scott (ed. 1898): Though her eyes were not anointed, she had the mortification of seeing her associates in two characters—Lysander loving and rejecting her, Helena friendly and estranged; Demetrius loving first herself and then Helena. Calderwood (1992, p. 134), questionably: The pun on parted I sums up the frightening divisions and multiplications of identity experienced by the lovers.

1716 mee thinkes]
See n. 6.

1717–18 And…mine owne]
Warburton in Theobald (ed. 1733) sees these lines as a response to and affirmation of Hermia’s description of divided perception: Demetrius is here compar’d to something that has the Property of appearing the same, and yet not being the same: and this was a Thought natural enough, upon her declaring her Approbation of what Hermia had said, that every thing seems double. Others focus on the finding of something lost, and the nature of ownership. Thirlby (MS 1725–33): Things found are yours & yet not yours[.] Idem (MS 1733–47) adds: Treasure trove[.] (For the tradition (and law) governing the ownership of a found treasure, see OED, Treasure-trove.) Heath (1765, p. 57) paraphrases: I feel myself in the same situation as one who, after having long lost a most valuable jewel, recovers it at last, when he least hoped to do so. The joy of this recovery succeeding the despair of ever finding it, together with the strange circumstances which restored it to his hands, make him even doubt whether it be his own or not. He can scarcely be persuaded to believe his good fortune. Malone (1780, 1:120): Having found Demetrius unexpectedly, she considered her property in him as insecure as that which a person has in a jewel that he has found by accident, which he knows not whether he shall retain, and which therefore may properly enough be called his own and not his own.…A kindred thought occurs in [Ant. 4.12.7–9 (2761–3) and MV 3.2.181–3 (1528–30)]. The Clarkes (ed. 1864): She has found Demetrius like a found jewel; hers by right of finding, not by right of previous ownership. Or the sentence may be thus explained—she has found her lover like a stray jewel; hers when she first had it and then regained it, not hers while it was lost to her. Batten (1876, p. 12) noting that double in the jewellery trade means a counterfeit stone: Helena seems to regard Demetrius as her own when he was true to her, and not her own when he was false to her. Wood (ed. 1902): Perhaps a reminiscence of the parable of the lost treasure. (Cf. Matt. [13:4].)
More recently, critics have been struck by an enigmatic profundity in the lines. Merchant (1964, pp. 80, 87), discussing the use of property and nature in The Phoenix and the Turtle in terms of the Athanasian creed: Nothing could more precisely express the confounding paradox of love, conveyed in The Phoenix and the Turtle, than that closing statement, not by confusion of Substance: but by unity of Person, a realization that was to be achieved at the dramatic level by the visionary insight of the lovers after the confusions of [MND]. It is expressed concisely by Helena in terms which exactly echo the precious mine of The Phoenix and the Turtle [36, Either was the others mine.]: And I have found Demetrius, like a jewel, Mine own, and not mine own. This is the moment which Benjamin Britten in his opera makes the splendid fugal climax of the lovers’ vision of their reuniting. [T]he mutual exchange of fidelity at mine own yet not mine own matches the comic profundity of Bottom’s vision. Berry (1984, p. 175): The mysterious ease with which the boundaries between the two realms dissolve, the rapidity with which mine own can become not mine own, is central to the experience of this play and others. See also Summers, . Bishop (1996, p. 92), giving several Shn. examples of similar paradoxes: Puttenham called such figures The Wondrer and certainly in their dramatic context they often have the qualities at once of profound representational power and of equally profound self-consciousness.…Helena’s grasp of the right way to word the world in her feeling catches its own quality of stumbling on truth right there for us: her metaphor crystallizes as the wonderful jewel of itself.…The possibility of metaphor as the granting of the world we want, our own and not our own, catches up the vibration of erotic desire in its strong toil of grace. Leggatt (1999, p. 58): Helena’s Mine own and not mine own suggests an improvement in the quality of the love. In place of the obsessive, exclusive demands we have seen the lovers make is an acknowledgement that the possession of love is never total. Cf. nn. 1712–26, 1712–20, 1712–13.
Warburton, not content with his sensible gloss (see above), proposed reading gemell (twin) for Iewell (see textual n., and Furness (ed. 1895) for the ensuing controversy). Johnson (ed. 1765): This emendation is ingenious enough to deserve to be true. It did elicit from Theobald (ed. 1733) interesting parallels with the moment of recognition in Err. 5.1.332 (1816) and TN 5.1.216–17 (2380–1). Smith (1803, pp. 9–12) argues for Gimmal: One half of the Gimmal Ring…being worn by the lover, the other by his mistresss, it might with the strictest truth be predicated, as well of his part as of her’s, when either spake of it, it is Mine own, and not mine own. See OED, Gemel 4. A suggestion by Anon (1746, p. 4) is even more farfetched, but cf. Merchant, above: If Shakespear does not allude to the King of Zeylan’s Mine of Rubies (mentioned by Le Blanc [Les voyages fameux du sieur Vincent Le Blanc, 1648], and other Travellers) out of which the King had all that exceeded the Weight of four or five Carrats, and none under that Weight, on which Account the Jewels of that Mine might be said to be his own, and not his own; these Lines in Shakespear want still to be cleared up.

1718–1718+1 Mine…sure]
Some struggle to make this a decasyllabic verse line. See textual n. Malone (ed. 1790): Sure is here used as a dissyllable: so sire, fire, hour, &c. Disagreeing, Steevens (ed. 1793): I cannot accede to a belief that sure was ever employed as a disyllable, much less at the end of a verse. Fire (anciently spelt fier) and hour (anciently spelt hower) might be disyllabically used, because the duplicate vowels in each of them were readily separated in pronunciation. Abbott §484: The second own is perhaps emphasized by repetition (or Are is a dissyllable, as fare, ere, where, [§]480).

1718+1 Are…awake]
F1’s omission of this sentence (see textual n.) is rightly deplored by Capell (1783, 2.3:113–14): It will seem strange to readers not appriz’d of the negligent manner in which the task of publishing Shakespeare has been all along perform’d, that these words (which are an easy and natural introduction to what Demetrius now says, and are manifestly alluded-to in what he says next) should be taken no notice of by editors who profess to have consulted the quarto’s. However, Steevens (ed. 1793), White (ed. 1858), and Granville-Barker (ed. 1924, p. lii) emphatically reject Q. Knight (ed. 1839) omits, but Idem (ed. 1842) restores them bracketed: The words in brackets are omitted in the folio. They are scarcely wanted. Watt (ed. 1906) suggests: [The words] were probably purposely omitted from [F1]. Wells & Taylor (1987, p. 284): Shakespeare is the man most likely to have cut these words, and probably intended to do so even in the foul papers: the sentence bears all the hallmarks of a first shot abandoned currente calamo. Holland (ed. 1994) follows F, but with a different justification: Compositorial excision is unlikely since the line is far from nonsensical. F’s authority, probably promptbook, must have cut the line.

1720 we dreame]
Verity (ed. 1893): The events of the night afterwards seem like a dream—alike to the lovers and to Bottom (cf. [1732–4]). So Oberon promised [1411–12]. Brooks (ed. 1979): In Enamoured Diana, a company of lovers, thanks to Felicia about to be united, thought they were in a dreame, standing like enchaunted persons, and not beleeving their owne eies ([ed. 1968], p. 376). Cf. 1672 above, and Err. 2.2.182–3 (577–8). See also n. 11.

1720–5 Do…him]
Hunter (1995, p. 42): The desire to escape from the tyranny of law into the freedom of dream is exactly matched by the desire to awake from the vexation of dream into the stability of law.

1722 Yea…father.]
Goldberg (1985, p. 124): It is her last word. Figured and disfigured; doubly stamped by the authorizing powers: testaments to the inscription of the letter, the circuit of desire turning back upon itself, encompassing both father and daughter, male and female, within its course. Hinely (1987, p. 128): Hermia’s response, her last words in the play, testify to the restoration of parental bonds, if not of parental tyranny.

1725–6 Dem.…dreames]
Holland (ed. 1994): Recounting dreams…is for many theorists a crucial part of the transforming experience of dreaming: Intimates who tell each other their dreams seek, I believe, to enhance their self-conceptions by making each other witnesses of aspects of themselves which have not become assimilated into their waking selves but which are, they hope, emergent (Charles Rycroft, The Innocence of Dreams (1979), p. 56). If the lines should be verse, Q2F’s reading of let vs for the second lets regularizes the meter, as Wells (ed. 1967) observes.

1726 by the way]
Deighton (ed. 1891): As we go along. See OED, Way, n.1 31b, quoting this instance.

note.p: Holland (ed. 1994), commenting on the expansion of F1’s SD (see textual n.): Cox’s [ed. 1661] SD, After a while Bottome wakes, suggests the necessary pause before the audience is allowed to realise that there is one sleeper still left on stage.

div.note:
1728–45 When…death]
Gentleman (ed. 1774): Our Author has shewn great knowledge of nature, making Bottom wake with his part of Pyramus in his head; for whatever possesses strict attention while the faculties are at large, is apt to impress them when locked in slumber, and to break out with the waking moment: the whole of this soliloquy is very pleasant, and much in character. Chambers (ed. 1897): That he has been an ass he has no notion, only that he has been adored by a most fair lady. Hence the irony of his situation. Arnold (1911, pp. 107–8): Sh.’s use of the comic soliloquy exhibits the transition of the monolog from the funny story told the audience to the laughable revelation of the soliloquizer’s inmost thoughts and emotions.…In the class of clownish narration may be included Bottom’s disjointed account of his dream…, the fun springing from the incompleteness of the discourse. Goddard (1951, pp. 79–80): The moment when Bottom awakens from this dream is the supreme moment of the play.…[W]hat Shakespeare has caught here in perfection is the original miracle of the Imagination, the awakening of spiritual life in the animal man. Bottom is an ass. If Bottom can be redeemed, matter itself and man in all his materiality can be redeemed also. Willeford (1969, p. 138) demurs: The scene appears to play upon such a hope; yet that hope founders upon the fool’s unintelligibility in our terms, or rather, upon our inability to penetrate and secure with our forms of understanding the element within him that seems to be of value. Vickers (1968, pp. 70–1): He has a vague memory of what has happened, struggles to recapture it, and unwittingly stumbles on it yet again with the word ass: the symmetries catch exactly the movement of his brain towards the recognition and away from it, and finally—as if he had in fact divined the truth but decided that it was safe to withhold it—the structured syntax shows up his growing confidence as he takes refuge in a comprehensively confused hypallage: [quotes 1730–3, set out to show parallel structures]. That is Bottom’s great moment, and a daring piece of theatre as we teeter on the brink of his discovery. But the symmetries do not mock him—they anatomize the process of his thought, and reinforce our impression of his artless, harmless simplicity. Gooch (1986, pp. 320–3) analyzes the speech as an example of the inexpressibility topos, and argues that it should not be played merely for humor, since it shows Bottom changed from overweening self-confidence to recognition of his inabilities;…he surrenders the writing of his ballad to Peter Quince, and also because of its suggestions of profundity, and ambiguity. Cf. Mousley (2000, p. 89). Kay (1992, p. 194): The speech represents a very important counterbalance to the relentless pursuit of wit and eloquence by the fairies and nobles.…[I]t draws attention to two Shakespearean commonplaces: the inadequacy of all human discourse when faced with the supernatural, and the crucial role played by the motivation of the speaker or listener—the goodwill or gentle patience or willing ears that Shakespearean orators habitually crave.
Other commentators focus more on the idea of the dream or vision, often connected with the experiences of Apuleius and St Paul. Rashbrook (1952, pp. 49–50) argues that besides the allusion to 1 Cor. 2:9, Bottom’s speech echoes 2 Cor. 1:1–6; he suggests that in writing the speech Sh. also had in mind 1 Cor. 1:27: But God hath chosen the foolish things of the world to confound the wise, et seq. and that in expressing this idea in the play, by association he uses the other passages of St. Paul. See also Hassel (1980, p. 54). Kermode (1961, p. 219), linking the visions of Apuleius and St. Paul: What they have in common is transformation, and an experience of divine love. Bottom has known the love of the triple goddess in a vision.…Bottom’s dream is oneiros or somnium; ambiguous, enigmatic, of high import. And this is the contrary interpretation of blind love; the love of God or of Isis, a love beyond the power of the eyes.…Bottom is there to tell us that the blindness of love, the dominance of the mind over the eye, can be interpreted as a means to grace as well as to irrational animalism; that the two aspects are, perhaps, inseparable. See also McKenzie (1964, p. 48). Frye (Perspective, 1965, pp. 108–9): From Heraclitus, who says that in dreams every man is his own Logos, to Freud, who says that every dream has a point at which it is unfathomable, a link with the unknown, it has been recognized that a dream is the heart of the dreamer’s private universe, and is therefore incommunicable. Yet the dreaming power is closely connected with the creative faculties, which are powers of communication. This is the paradox that Bottom struggles with…It would be wrong to sentimentalize Bottom, but equally wrong not to feel that perhaps Bottom, with what Puck calls his own fool’s eyes [1601], has seen something in the heart of comedy that our wisdom does not see, just as the insane Lear sees something in the heart of tragedy that our sanity does not see. Young (1966, p. 124): Here, where dream and rare vision are equated and the suggestion of an encounter with divinity is strengthened by a comically scrambled passage from the Bible, we are closer, amid our laughter, to the mystery of dream experience than at any other point in the play. Bottom’s awe at this vision past the wit of man is enormously suggestive in terms of our normal contempt for the shadowy, irrational world of dreams, for, all things considered, he is right, and his healthy respect for his limitations gives him a more accurate sense of what has passed than is possessed either by the lovers or by Theseus. Barkan (1980, p. 358): This is the richest moment in the play because it spans the greatest possible gap. The ass, who began as a fool, descended to a jackass, and affirmed his humanity in the face of god and beast, has had an experience simultaneously beastly, human, divine. Now, like Apuleius’s hero or Bruno’s Actaeon, he is dead to the ordinary world and alive to a visionary experience. Thus he expresses himself in the ecstatic language of St Paul. Wyrick (1982, p. 446): Like Dromio of Ephesus’ resonant speech on patient suffering, Bottom’s account of his most rare vision [1732] briefly irradiates a comically human ass with an aureole of sanctity. He becomes an asinus portans mysteria. Evans (1985, p. 75): Bottom, who joins Holofernes in being translated into an ass, expounds the bottomless overdeterminability of what Derrida calls the metaphorization of metaphor [White Mythology: Metaphor in the Text of Philosophy, New Literary History 6 (1974), p. 44]. The ballad of Bottom’s Dream, a silent text within the text recording, within the Dream, a dream of a (real) translation, is so called because it hath no bottom…, containing as it does both infinity and a great deal of nothing.…[W]hile echoing the mystical illuminations of St Paul and Erasmus’s Dame Folly, the dream is also the world turned upside-down, a radical unhinging of empirical and metaphysical givens.
Belsey (1994, pp. 68–72) offers a psychoanalytic interpretation drawing on the case history of the visionary experiences of Anna O., published by Josef Breuer in 1893. Richmond (2000, pp. 118–19) presents a detailed theological interpretation, arguing that a broader Pauline and Catholic context indicates that Bottom is less garbled than many suppose; however inadequate his analysis or language, he recognizes the Spirit. Palmer (1987, pp. 112–13) points out that the Pauline allusion and its context have their intermediate source in Erasmus’ Praise of Folly; he quotes several passages from Chaloner’s translation (ed. Clarence H. Miller, EETS 257, 1965, pp. 126–8), and concludes: Bottom’s experience can hardly be described as religious but the simpleton has enjoyed a paradisal interlude denied to wiser men. See also Birkinshaw, 1992, p. 49. Callaghan (2000, pp. 157–8): Bottom’s speech…resonates quite remarkably with Montaigne’s An Apologie for Raymond Sebond, an essay essentially concerned with the problem of human perception,…which demonstrates both a deep reverence for animals and a startling egalitarianism. In particular, the essay questions a hierarchy even more fundamental than that of gender or that of menial and mental labor, yet one to which Bottom’s transformation implicitly refers, namely, the human dominion over nature.
See also nn. 1737–40, 1742, 1744, and 1745; .

1728 cue]
See n. 913.

1729 next]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Sc. cue.

1729 Hey ho]
Kittredge (ed. 1939): A tremendous yawn. Wells (ed. 1967) adds, perhaps with a hint of Hee-Haw! Mowat & Werstine (ed. 1993): This may signal either a call or a big yawn. See OED, Heigh-ho, int. A, quoting this instance.

1731 Gods my life]
OED (God 8b), quoting this instance first: Used (profanely) as mere exclamations of surprise.…Shortened forms Gods ( = God save) my life, etc. Wright (ed. 1877) compares Ado 4.2.70 (2060).

1731 a sleepe]
See n. 555.

1731–2 I…dreame]
McNeal (1938, p. 37): May be taken as a travesty of the title of [Greene’s] A Most Rare and Excellent Dreame; he further argues that the putative content of Bottom’s Dream is a burlesque on Greene’s poem. See also n. 1742.

1732–3 I…was]
Hale (1985, p. 219) finds Chaucer, BD 276–9: A precedent for this use of what may be called the uninterpretability topos. Cf. Golding 15.162–3; see .

1732 vision]
See n. 1412. Hassel (2005, p. 382): Here Shakespeare associates the word vision not only with dreams but also through allusion with religious insight and mystical experience.

1732–3 the…man]
OED (Wit, n. 2a), quoting this instance: The wit of man = human understanding.

1733 Man…Asse]
Verity (ed. 1893): The same unconscious humour in his choice of words as at [932, 937]. Garber (1974, pp. 7–8): The pun is a kind of dream condensation, containing at once a literal and a metaphorical or hidden meaning. In Shakespearean dream utterances the same enriching ambiguity obtains: when Bottom says Man is but an ass, if he go about to expound this dream…, the play on ass functions to remind the audience that the dream world brings together reality and illusion in language as well as in action.

1734 goe about]
Johnson (1755, Go 50. To Go about ): To attempt; to endeavour; to set one’s self to any business. Hunter (ed. 1870): Set his wits to work. Rolfe (ed. 1877): Undertake. Brooks (ed. 1979): Should start trying. Mowat & Werstine (ed. 1993): Try. See OED, Go, v. 49b.

1734–7 Me…had]
Davis (2003, p. 213): His bewilderment exemplifies an air of lost or unknowable selfhood that recurs throughout the comedies and which contests the ethical focus of traditional rhetoric.

1734–7 Me…thought]
See n. 804.

1735–7 Methought…had]
Stewart (1981, p. 47): Surely the blanks are there to be filled in. The verb had leans by turns toward the was of attribution and toward sexual possession: Methought I was an ass and had an ass’s head and/or Methought I was a great lover and had a queen. In the double grammar of his dreamlike experience, verbs of definition and possession simultaneously articulate both the excoriating self-analysis and the compensatory ingredient of sexual fantasy made flesh. Holland (ed. 1994): In Reinhardt’s outdoor production in Oxford in 1933 this passage began with a nervous groping, to see if the long snout and the long ears were still there; then a quickening of gesture, a nervous laughter; a sudden cut to silence; a fifty-yard run to the pond; a look at the reflection in the water; a scream of relief; and a jubilant dance off through the trees towardes Athens [J. L. Styan, Max Reinhardt, Cambridge, 1982, p. 59]; the same business was preserved in his film version in 1935. See also Griffiths (ed. 1996, pp. 184–6).

1736 patcht a foole]
Brooks (ed. 1979): Q1’s patcht a foole (proclaimed—by his patches—a fool) might be accepted (praestat difficilior lectio) as the reading less likely to have arisen from its specious appeal to a copyist. If he had a patched fool before him, he would have no motive for a normalizing change, whether conscious or unconscious. But even without such motive, the transposition could easily have been made inadvertently. The Q1 phrase does not, to my mind, satisfy the criterion of sitting naturally in its context. Thiselton (1903, p. 66): We may compare poore a thousand crownes in [AYL 1.1.2–3 (5–6)]. Holland (ed. 1994): Q1’s reading…is possible: a fool clumsily put together.
Most explanatory comment refers to F1, the accepted reading (see textual n.). Thirlby (MS 1725–33): Motley. Johnson (ed. 1765): That is, a fool in a particoloured coat. Becket (1815, 1:277): By a patched fool, I rather think he means a mended fool, i.e. little better than a fool. In other words, man will not be found without foolishness, however able he may appear to be. Staunton (ed. 1857): I have met with remarkable proof of the supposed connexion between the term patch, applied to a fool, and the garb such a character sometimes wore, in a Flemish picture of the sixteenth century. In this picture, which represents a grand al fresco entertainment of the description given to Queen Elizabeth during her Progresses, there is a procession of masquers and mummers, led by a fool or jester, whose dress is covered with many-coloured coarse patches from head to heel. Schmidt (1875): Patch, subst. 4) a paltry fellow; Patch, vb. 1)-ed = paltry…(according to some, = parti-coloured). Wright (ed. 1877): A motley fool [AYL 2.7.13 (986)], a pied ninny [Tmp. 3.2.63 (1415)]; he refers to patch at 1031. See n. 1031. See also Knowles, ed. AYL 1977, pp. 113–14. Walter (ed. 1964): Perhaps the sense intended is utter fool. Brooks (ed. 1979): The fool commonly wore a child’s long coat: parti-coloured, but, as Hotson showed in Shakespeare’s Motley, 1952, not in quarters but in the weave. It was left to E. W. Ives (in Sh.S. 13, 1960, pp. 90 ff.) to correct Hotson’s idea that the colours were mingled as in modern tweeds: that requires advanced manufacturing techniques. They were arranged in simple patterns, stripes or checks for example, which could be produced with yarns of two colours on the hand-loom: hence the patched or pied look. (This account is from Agnes Latham’s fuller one, in [Arden ed. AYL, 1975, p. lv]). From the dress, Wolsey’s fool was named Patch, which strengthened the association.

1737 offer]
Johnson (1755, v. a. 4): To attempt. Verity (ed. 1893) adds, almost = dare. Greenblatt (ed. 1997, Glossary): Venture. See OED, v. 5b.

1737–40 The…was]
Henley (MS note in Steevens ed. 1773) notes the very improper scriptural allusion. Douce (in Steevens ed. 1793) specifies the passage Bottom is here blundering upon…1.Cor.ii.9. Halliwell (1841, p. 81) demurs: We should not think that any such allusion is intended, and there is no necessity for the conjecture. However, subsequent critics agree on the allusion to these words of St. Paul and/or possibly other scriptural passages. Heraud (1865, p. 181): Bottom parodies a text of Holy Writ, for which the poet has been accused of profanity. What he meant by it himself was quite the opposite. The quotation is made in a religious spirit, and for the purpose of suggestion. Shakspere thereby intended to imply, that by the changing and translating of Bottom, he meant to shadow forth the manner in which we shall be transformed in the future life to which we are destined. Wilson (ed. 1924): It must be remembered that Bottom was a weaver, and therefore possibly of a puritanical turn of mind. Cf. [1H4 2.4.133 (1093–4)]. Miriam Joseph (1947, p. 55): By the use of hypallage in this instance Shakespeare achieves the tone of grotesque wonder suited to the situation without incurring the risk of mockery of the Scripture (1 Cor. 2:9) which might otherwise have resulted. Bottom seems to be addicted to this misplacing of words whether he speaks as Pyramus or for himself [quotes 1995–6 and 2136–7]. On the effects of the hypallage, see also Langley (1991, p. 123). Coleman (1955, p. 8): This obviously is a paraphrase of the words of Isaiah: For since the beginning of the world men have not heard, nor perceived by the ear, neither hath the eye seen, O God, beside thee, what he hath prepared for him that waiteth for him. Isa. 64:4. Repeated, in essence, by Paul, in I Cor. 2:9. Merchant (1972, pp. 56–7): Much nearer [than 1 Cor. 2:9] to Bottom’s vision and to the sacramental quality in the two phrases Man’s hand is not able to taste, his tongue to conceive, is the passage in St John’s first Epistle: That which we have seen with our eyes, which we have looked upon, and our hands have handled, of the Word of life. Bottom has been neither wholly confused nor wholly deceived in his dreaming world. Stroup (1978, p. 81): The Scriptural allusion, perverted as it is, nevertheless brings Bottom to his epiphany, his self-recognition.…Bottom has discovered the bottom of God’s secrets—has realized that they are indeed bottomless. Willson (1979, p. 408) objects that the effect of Stroup’s claim is to transform a witty romantic comedy into a morality play. Foakes (ed. 1984, p. 35): The garbled allusion does not mean that Bottom has some access or means to grace, but suggests rather that the heaven revealed to Bottom corresponds to the limits of his imagination, and he is as impervious to the deepe things of God as he is to Titania’s attractions. MacCary (1985, p. 140) believes the parody calls in question all mystic experience; there is no mystery, no divine plan, only individual experience. Bradbrook (1987, p. 17) points out that the statements in these lines are literally true. Bloom (1987, p. 3): Bottom’s scrambling of the senses refuses St. Paul’s easy supernaturalism, with its dualistic split between flesh and spirit. Our prophet Bottom is a monist, and so his dream urges upon us a synesthetic reality, fusing flesh and spirit. Peters (1988, pp. 46–7), referring to Stroup (1978) and Willson (1979), argues that Bottom’s revelatory experience is genuine, despite his garbled reaction to it, citing in support Augustine on dreams from De Immortalitate Animae (PL, ed. Migne 32, 1032–3) and Donne’s Sermons (ed. G.R. Potter and E.M.S. Simpson, 1953–62, 8:221): Bottom, it might be argued, is not confusing his senses nor misquoting Scripture. Having mentioned the eye first, it does not matter to him that the remainder of the vehicles of sense and their perceptions are mismatched, because all senses are subject to sight, and he has had the ultimate vision in the play. Patterson (1988, p. 38) objects to critics who focus only on his jumbling of the biblical text, rather than on its context of profound spiritual levelling; see further .
Commentators also provide analogues for similar uses of hypallage. Grey (1754, 1:69) quotes Pegge Pudding’s vows to William Cricket in Wily Beguil’d (1638, D1r). Halliwell (ed. 1856) gives other examples, including TN 2.3.56 (756), and comments: This kind of humour was so very common, it is by no means necessary to consider, with some, that Shakespeare intended Bottom to parody Scripture. Smith (1982, p. 87) parallels Rom. 1.2.39–41 (286–8). Rhoads (1985, pp. 80–1) compares Castiglione’s The Courtier [Everyman ed. 1928, p. 313], And as a man hearest not with his mouth, nor smelleth with his eares; the beauty to which true love aspires is seen with the eyes of the mind rather than those of the senses. Dundas (1993, pp. 54–5), quoting Son. 23.13–14: Sh. links true expression and true interpretation by the same ability to overcome the limits of one particular sense—in this case, the sense of seeing—so that it may hear. But, characteristically, he can turn the same conceit, which he uses for the exaltation of truth, to lampooning, as in Bottom’s speech…[quotes 1737–42]. It is only when we read this speech in the paragone of the senses that we begin to see how interchanging the senses may be essential to describing the life of the imagination. See also n. 1995–6.
A few note verbal parallels. Rushton (1872, p. 292) fastens on the word conceiue [739] and compares Mac. 2.3.64–5 (817), and Lyly’s Euphues and his England (2:186, ll. 20–2). Walter (ed. 1964) refers to Isaiah, lxiv.4 where perceived by the ear is suggestive.

1740–1 I…dreame]
Hale (1985, pp. 219–20): Chaucer’s dreamer also regards writing a poem as a suitable consequence of his experience: [quotes BD 1330–3].

1740–1 Ballet]
Halliwell (ed. 1856): The usual ancient orthography of ballad. Usually modernized editorially; see textual n. Brooks (ed. 1979): A topical narrative set to a popular tune, like those printed as black-letter broadsides. Wiles (1987, p. 55) sees here an anticipation of the transformation of Kemp/Bottom into Kemp the jig-maker. Weimann (2000, p. 99): Almost certainly it was Kempe who performed a jig or ballet as Bottom…Such dance with song and music was to be preferred [to an attempt to expound], because it hath no bottom—which, if read as a pun, is also to say, it was Bottom-less. In other words, there was no longer any imaginary character called Bottom in the ballet when this song and dance was Kempe’s job. Cf. OED, Ballad, n. 1, 3. Andrews’s (ed. 1989) suggestion, that Ballet is a characteristic mispronunciation by Bottom, is unlikely.

1742 Bottoms…bottome]
Chambers (ed. 1897): Mr. Fleay suggests that there is here a hit at Robert Greene, who called one of his poems A Maiden’s Dream, for the apparent reason that there was no maiden in it. See also n. 1731–2.

1742 no bottome]
Delius (ed. 1859): Because the dream cannot be fathomed. At the same time a wordplay with Bottom and bottom (Ger.). Bell (1861, 2:315): Poor as this pun is, it is not original: it is one common amongst the Germans…that der Boden See (the Lake of Constanz) so genannt wird weil bodenlos—as we should say, the Bottom Lake is so called because bottomless. Page (ed. 1897): Bottom’s application of the principle of lucus a non lucendo. Wells (ed. 1967) adds: Has no reality. Evans (ed. 1974): Is all tangled up because it lacks a core (bottom). Brooks (ed. 1979, p. cxvii) sums up the triple pun: [It] combines the old academic joke of non-sequitur nomenclature, lucus a non lucendo, with the two opposites implied: no bottom because no foundation, and no bottom because unfathomably profound.…No bottom, for a weaver, is also no skein to weave from. McDonald (ed. 2000) adds another possible meaning: [I]ts meaning is unclear (he can’t get to the bottom of it) (OED, n. 12). Nuttall (2001, p. 276), citing Quintilian Inst. I.6.34 and Priscian Inst. IV.32: It has long been acknowledged that the dramatist probably intends a glancing allusion to the old chestnut of inverse nomenclature: lucus a non lucendo (It is called a wood—lucus—because there is no light—lux—there).…[I]t has suddenly struck me that the allusion may be less casual, more central to the play than we thought. If we pause on the thought of a wood—perhaps a sacred wood—without light, we are thinking about the distinctive world of [MND]—a darkened pastoral excursion where the trees grow closer than they will later, in the Forest of Arden. It is deeply appropriate that this thought should be secretly planted in the foolish, unfathomably profound speech of Bottom, at this point in the play.

1743 latter end]
OED (Latter, a. 3b): Latter end. the concluding part (of a period, etc.); the end of life, (one’s) death. Also punningly, the posteriors. Webb (1988 and 1989, under Bottom) calls attention to the pun. Cf. LLL where the conjunction of latter end and ass for Jud-as (5.2.627–8 [2579–80]) provides to Parker (Name, 2003, pp. 16–17) a suggestive connection with Bottom’s name. David (ed. LLL, 1951, pp. 178–9) notes the same quibble in Lyly’s Midas, 4.1 (3:143).

1743 a Play]
See textual n. To support his second conjecture Thirlby (MS 1725–33) cites 1764, our sport and 1783, our play. Steevens (ed. 1785): I.e. the play in which he was to perform. Capell (1783, 2.3:114): Some other play, a play generally. Sprague (ed. 1896): A was sometimes colloquial for our? Brooks (ed. 1979): Emendations…are false to Bottom’s only half-focused state of mind, and curtail the poetic flight of his (and Shakespeare’s) imagination.

1743–4 Peraduenture]
Johnson (1755, 1): Perhaps (OED, adv. arch. 3).

1744 gratious]
Johnson (1755, 6): Graceful; becoming. Schmidt (1874, 5): Lovely, attractive, beautiful. Evans (ed. 1974): Elegant. Brooks (ed. 1979): Appealing (OED, Gracious, a. 2). Scott (ed. 1898), questionably: Gratifying. As gracious is the appropriate epithet of the Duke, and it is naturally applied to his actions and feelings, it is probably used here as another illustration of Bottom’s inaccurate use of language. Boyle (1982, p. 19): I find gracious in Bottom’s speech sharing the Pauline insight into grace; it indicates, as I read it, an ultimate link between literature and theology—both face the human mystery of death and point to dreams beyond biology. Bottom’s speech suggests that something overwhelming (and for non-poets ineffable) looms up for those who choose to see and hear. Those who attempt to read Shakespeare with their eyes alone should do something more useful, like knitting or chemistry.

1745 at her death]
Steevens (ed. 1773): He means the death of Thisbe, which is what his head is at present full of. Chambers (ed. 1897): That is, at Thisbe’s death, as an epilogue.…But has not Bottom confused the incidents of his dream with those of the play, and identified Titania with Thisbe? Cf. Munro below. Scott (ed. 1898): The death of Thisbe, the heroine of the piece. This is of course an absurdity as Pyramus dies before Thisbe, but it is worthy of Bottom. The comic effect is twofold; first in the impossibility, secondly in the high spirits which Pyramus is ready to feel at the instant of his lover’s death. Wilson (ed. 1924): Bottom’s mind is full of the play—When my cue comes, call me are his waking words—and of the part (if possible, parts) he is to perform therein. The Ballad of Bottom’s Dream fires his imagination and he sees himself rising at Thisby’s death (which ends the play) and singing it (with that voice of his!) before the whole Court, the prostrate form of his love still lying at his feet. Munro (ed. 1957): The facts seem to be that Bottom, awaking from his dream, is still Pyramus; but his dream is so marvellous that it is good enough for a ballad at the end of a play. It then occurs to him that a suitable occasion is at hand, and he will sing it at Thisbe’s death. Walter (ed. 1964): In plays produced by children like Edwards’ Damon & Pithias the death of the hero or heroine was inevitably accompanied by a death-song; cf. Edwards, Works, ed. Ros King (2001), pp. 174–5, lamenting the expected death of Pythias, and p. 184, a final song to the Queen.
Theobald (ed. 1733), objecting that there is no mention of any She-Creature, to whom this Relative can be coupled, proposes to emend to after. See textual n. Capell (1783, 2.3:114) refers to Theobald’s tedious note as needless, and Wilson (ed. 1924) scoffs at it. But Malone (ed. 1790), reading as Q1 in his text: Theobald…might have quoted [Tmp. 2.2.44 (1083–4)] in support of his emendation. Collier (ed. 1875): No doubt Bottom vulgarly pronounced it arter death, and that might be the origin of the corruption. Wright (ed. 1877): Theobald’s emendation is certainly ingenious and may be right. Thiselton (1903, p. 66: To sing at Thisbe’s death would make the ballet an integral part of the Play which would be absurd, as Piramus would then be dead; but to sing after death would merely mean by way of epilogue for which Bottom was doubtless prepared; cf. [2134–44]. MacSweeney (1918, p. 40): The Shearmen and Taylors’ Pageant and The Weavers’ Pageant ([Two Coventry Corpus Christi Plays,] E.E.T.S., [Extra Series 87,] ed. Dr. Hardin Craig, [1902]) both end with songs that need not of necessity form an integral part of the plays.…Quince’s ballet may therefore have been sung at the latter end of a play in accordance with a more or less accepted dramatic custom. Empson (1994, p. 246), supporting Theobald: To sing it after his own death, on or off the stage, is a plan adequate to his vanity, and gives the resonant exit line that must have been intended.

1746 Enter…rabble]
Theobald (1729, in Nichols, 1817, 2:237–8) argued against F1 that the Fifth Act should begin here, since the scene had evidently moved from the wood to Athens. Gentleman (ed. 1774, p. 190): The short low comedy scene between these mechanical actors has merit, and concludes this Act well; which has more spirit than the third. Leech (Function, 1969, p. 107) notes that Theobald’s suggestion would make this scene parallel the corresponding scene, 1.2., but believes that here Sh. wished to give the whole of Act V…to the marriage celebrations at Theseus’ court. Wilcher (1997, p. 52) discusses this scene as particularly interesting because it forms the final phase of Act IV in the First Folio, and in the undivided First Quarto simply stands as a buffer between the last of the three rituals that bring the narrative phase of the play to an end (Bottom’s soliloquy about his dream) and Theseus’s discourse on the tricks of strong imagination, which is effectively a prologue to the selection, performance and criticism of the Mechanicals’ play in the semi-autonomous dramatic entity of Act V.

1746 Flute, Thisby]
Ebsworth (ed. Q2 1880, p. xi): The first error of the Quartos was the omission to mark (not Thisbie, but) Thisbie’s Mother—a character that had been allotted to the timid Robin Starveling, although she does not speak when the interlude is afterwards acted. Her part is dumb-show. Thiselton (1903, pp. 66–7): Neither [Q1 nor Q2] give a word in this scene to either Snout or Starveling, and they both absurdly treat Flute and Thisbe as different persons. The Folio attempts to correct, but carelessly preserves the distinction between Flute and Thisbe in the stage direction, which perhaps arose from Flute being attired as Thisbe as for a dress rehearsal…and it consequently appearing more correct to designate him as Thisbe, Thisbie being accordingly inserted, and Flute carelessly allowed to remain. Wilson (ed. 1924) attributes the duplication to Sh.’s forgetfulness; Brooks (ed. 1979) also sees it as authorial.

1746 and the rabble]
Brooks (ed. 1979, p. xxiii) asserts: Imperfectly specific directions are an acknowledged sign of foul-paper copy and calls this phrase a delightful specimen of author’s language. McDonald (1994, p. 99) objects: Given the unstable nature of a play script, we might draw back from judgments about Shakespeare’s particular attitudes and instead contemplate what the stage direction tells us about the intersection of stage and world, of stage type and social type. By this term, the stage roles of the artisans translate directly to the rabble of Elizabethan culture, associating these stage artisans with the street rabble that stirred up trouble for the City and Crown. See also Wiles at n. 813 (Clownes).

1746 rabble]
Dessen and Thomson (1999, p. 174): A disorderly group or mob, usually of the lower classes. See OED, n.1 2a, c.

1749 Flut.]
See textual n. Collier (ed. 1842): In the 4to. editions this speech is assigned to Flute, but it is corrected in the folio; where, however, as in the quartos, there is still some confusion of persons, owing to the actor of the part of Thisbe being called This. in the prefixes. Brooks (ed. 1979): The speaker must be either Snout or Starveling, and F’s [Staru.] may have been recovered from the prompt-book.

1749 heard of]
Andrews (ed. 1989) glosses: Heard from; but more probably = found. Cf. OED, Hearken, v. 8: To get to hear of; to search out or find by inquiry. Obs.

1749 Out of doubt]
Baker (ed. 1896): Without doubt. Gill (ed. 1981): Certainly.

1750 transported]
Staunton (ed. 1857): Or, as Snout expressed it when he first saw Bottom adorned with an ass’s head, translated, that is, transformed. Compare Thomas (1587, Translatus): Transported, translated, conueyed from one to another; also farre fette, turned, changed. Schmidt (1875, 3), quoting also MM 4.3.68 (2149): To remove from this world to the next, to kill (euphemistically). Deighton (ed. 1891): Made away with, carried off by some agency; it seems hardly necessary to take the word as an euphemism for murdered, as Schmidt does. Verity (ed. 1893): Carried off by the fairies. Idem (ed. 1912, pp. 152–3): Almost a technical term in this connection. In…Bodin’s De la Demonomanie des Sorciers (1580) there is a long discussion (bk. ii. chap. 4) of the question whether men can be transporté from one place to another…by the agency of spirits. Reginald Scot has a chapter (bk. v. chap. 7) in his Discoverie (1584) concerning transportations. Holland (ed. 1994): Starveling may mean translated, transformed, but the effect is that Bottom has been conveyed from one realm to another or that he is enraptured. See also nn. 203, 247, 935–6, 1815 and see 1054, 1579.

1751 mard]
Gill (ed. 1981): Spoiled. Possibly also hampered or stopped; see OED, Mar, v. 1. Obs. Possibly with a quibble on the variant spelling Mard of Merd; OED (a): Dung, excrement;…a turd.

1751–2 It…Doth it]
Wells (ed. 1967): F reads It goes not forward, doth it? It is just possible that Q’s division into two sentences represents a deliberate pointing of the words. Flute is unsure of his first two assertive statements and so ends with a question.

1751–2 goes not forward]
Schmidt (1874, Forward 2): Proceed, take place. See OED, Forward, B. adv. 9.

1753–4 You…he]
Chambers (ed. 1897): Bottom has succeeded in persuading the rest of the company to take him at his own valuation.

1754 discharge]
See n. 354. Andrews (ed. 1989) suggests: Quince means perform, but another sense (kill) is also applicable (unattested in OED).

1755–6 hee…Athens]
Baldwin (1944, 2:147), quoting Susenbrotus on Antiphrasis: Shakspere knew also the Acutum ingenium pro stolido ac hebeti [Sharp wit for the doltish and dull]. Ingenium is wit. Bottom hath simply the best wit of any handicraft man in Athens; though this was said in admiring earnest, we and Shakspere know that Bottom is stolidus et hebes. Cf. n. 1031.

1755–6 best…man]
See n. 1376 (most).

1755–6 handy-craft man,]
Johnson (1755, Handicraftsman), quoting this instance and 2H6 4.2.11 (2330): A manufacturer; one employed in manual occupation. See also n. 266–8. Q1’s comma could be read as Flute being first assertive in his praise, but then adding Quince’s qualification.

1755 simply]
OED (adv. 6d), quoting this instance first: Without exception; absolutely.

1755 wit]
Baker (ed. 1896): Mind, intelligence. Doran (ed. 1959): Intellect, brain. Andrews (ed. 1989): Applied intelligence, know-how. See OED 5b. Andrews (ed. 1993) suggests: Like Will, Wit can also have a genital sense. See n. 951.

1757–8 Quin.…voice]
Maginn (1837, p. 372 n.): I propose that the second admirer’s speech be given to Snout, who else has not anything to say, and is introduced on the stage to no purpose. The few words he says elsewhere in the play are all ridiculous; and the mistake of paramour for paragon is more appropriate to him than to Quince, who corrects the cacology of Bottom himself. [see 895–6]. And besides, Quince, the playwright, manager, and ballad-monger…is of too much importance in the company to be rebuked by so inferior a personage as Flute. Ebsworth (ed. Q2 1880, p. xii) argues for Flute, since it is he who habitually mistakes his words [see 910 and 2005, but Ebsworth mistakes in attributing to Flute the repetition of the error for Ninus]. But Furnivall (ed. 1908): We know that Quince doth not stand upon points [1916], that His speach was like a tangled chaine [1922], and that he said Bottom went to see a noyse that he heard [904]. He might therefore mistake Paramour for Paragon; he was but one of the hempen home-spunnes [889], patches, rude Mechanicals [1031]; and we therefore hold that no sufficient reason has been shown for changing the text, in which Q1, 2, F are firm.

1757–8 is…voice]
Caldecott (MS 1813–33): Has in that respect every accomplishment of a lover.

1757 person]
Holzknecht & McClure (ed. 1937): Bodily figure; OED 4b. Andrews (ed. 1989): Physical attributes; Idem (ed. 1993): Here it is hard not to be reminded of that part of a man’s person that seeks to discharge itself. Cf. OED (6b), earliest example quoted 1834: Euphemistically, the genitals.

1757 very]
See n. 419 (very).

1759–60 This.…nought]
Phelps (ed. [1851]) assigns this speech to Quince, as the best informed of the party (see textual n.). Thiselton (1903, p. 67) would not…deprive Thisbe of the correction, as it is more pointed as coming from one who is ready to represent Piramus’ lady-love.

1759–60 God blesse vs]
An exclamatory invocation asking for protection from evil; see OED, v.1 9a, quoting this instance first.

1759 Paragon…Paramour]
Starkie (1916, pp. 218–19): This figure is due to a false conclusion that two or more words, from being analogous in form, structure, or conjugation, are analogous in meaning also. Errors of this kind are common in ordinary speech, and are called solecisms, or barbarisms; in Comedy, however, they are deliberately employed for the sake of laughter. Brooks (ed. 1979): The malapropism may have been suggested by Mouffet’s calling Pyramus a paragon (Muir [1977], p. 74). Shakespeare first uses the word in [TGV 2.4.146 (798)], perhaps from Diana ([ed. 1968], p. 152 and elsewhere). On the claim of influence from Mouffet, see .

1759 Paragon]
Gildon (1710, Glossary): Peer or Equal. Rann (ed. 1787): Pattern of excellence.

1759 Paramour]
Johnson (1755): 1. A lover or wooer. 2. A mistress. It is obsolete in both senses, though not inelegant or unmusical. Malone apud Boswell (ed. 1821): The double meaning (understanding paramour in the sense of concubine) was undoubtedly intended to be conveyed. See [R3 1.2.97–100 (101–5)]. Martz (1971, p. 73): Bottom is indeed not a paramour, for, one way or the other, there has been nothing illicit in his relationship to Titania. Paragon better describes his sweet voice of courtesy, his essential innocence, his stance of wonder. Andrews (ed. 1993): Sexual partner (used especially of the men and women who serve royalty or the nobility as lovers and mistresses). Quince speaks more accurately than he realizes when he describes Bottom as a very Paramour, for a sweet Voice (Compare [946–1020]).

1760 thing of nought]
Theobald (ed. 1733): This is a Reading, I am sure, of Nought. My Change of a single Letter gives a very important Change to the Humour of the Passage.—A Thing of naught, means, a naughty Thing, little better than downright Bawdry. So, in Hamlet [3.2.147 (2014)], Ophelia, when He talks a little grossly to her, replies; You’re naught, you’re naught, my Lord; &c. Johnson (ed. 1765): A thing of nought, which Mr. Theobald changes with great pomp to a thing of naught, is, a good for nothing Thing. Steevens (ed. 1778): A thing of nought is the true reading. So in Hamlet [4.2.28–30 (2657–9)], Ham. The king is a thing—Guil. A thing my lord? Ham. Of nothing. Verity (ed. 1893, Glossary): The words are the same.…But commonly the form nought was used = nothing, and naught = something bad. In Elizabethan E[nglish] the adj. naught or naughty had the strong sense worthless. See OED, Nought and Naught. Colman (1974, p. 17), discussing pudendal or vaginal meanings of nothing: The whole matter is further complicated by the fact that nothing is synonymous with nought, which Elizabethan spelling did not consistently differentiate from naught. Such a quibble as Flute’s…or Richard III’s Naught to do with Mistress Shore? is not necessarily anatomical. It may simply be invoking the general idea of sexual sin (as in naughty). Shaheen (1993, p. 99) compares Ps. 144.4 and Mic. 1.7; Idem (1987, p. 108) comparing the same references with Ham. 4.2.28–30 (2657–9), comments: The expression, however, was common.

1762 Masters]
See n. 283.

1763 there is]
See n. 857.

1764 sport]
See n. 1036. Andrews (ed. 1993) links with Puck’s sport (1143) of watching lovers, with Pyramus’s experiences as hinted by the discussion of Paramour, and with made men to suggest a bawdy subtext.

1764 gon forward]
See n. 1751–2.

1764–5 made men]
Most commentators agree with Ayscough (ed. 1784): Meaning, we had all made our fortunes. See OED (Made, ppl. a. 7a): Of a person: Having his success in life assured. Chiefly in phr. a made man. But Johnson (ed. 1765): In the same sense as in the Tempest [2.2.30–1 (1069–70)], any monster in England makes a man. Montrose (1996, p. 180), unconvincingly, interprets the phrase as meaning that the mechanicals collectively parody the changeling’s gender-specific transition from the matriarchal world of infancy to the patriarchal world of manhood.

1766 bully]
See n. 820.

1766–70 six pence…day]
Steevens (ed. 1778) suggests a reference to Thomas Preston (author of Cambyses), who was given by queen Elizabeth for an acting performance that pleased her a pension of twenty pounds a year, which is little more than a shilling a day; see Nichols, 1823, 1:181, 245. Arkwright (1906, pp. 401–2) suggests the allusion is to the sixpence a day that the Master of the Children of the Chapel Royal received for each child’s board. Barber (1959, p. 149): The repetition of sixpence a day seems loaded: if Bottom in Pyramus is worth sixpence, what is Kempe in Bottom worth? For Bottom is to Theseus as Kempe was to the nobleman for whom the play was first produced. The sum is considerable; Brooks (ed. 1979): A joiner (cf. Snug) in 1595 at the Lancs. rate received fourpence a day and also his food: without meat and drink his pay was eightpence: M. St. Clare Byrne, Elizabethan Life, p. 168; on p. 169 she reckons a master-carpenter’s annual earnings as averaging out to less than threepence a day. Foakes (ed. 1984): Corresponding roughly to the wages of a skilful craftsman at the time; see Shakespeare’s England, I, 331.

1767 scaped]
OED (Scape, v.1): Aphetic var. of ESCAPE v. Frequent in prose use till near the end of the 17th c.; subsequently only arch. and poet., and often written ’scape. See also Franz §49. See 2217.

1768–70 And…nothing]
Deighton (ed. 1891): If he were rewarded at all, as he was sure to have been, the reward for his playing Pyramus could not have been less than sixpence a day for life.

1768 And]
See n. 313.

1770 deserued]
See n. 782.

1770 in Pyramus]
Wright (ed. 1877): In the part of Pyramus.

1771 Enter Bottom]
Verity (ed. 1893): When he left the wood and returned to Athens,…Bottom must have gone, not to his own home (cf. [1747]) but to the palace, to learn what had been decided with regard to the play. From the palace he has hurried straight to Quince’s house, expecting to find the company assembled there. Hence he has later information than his friends.…Would any one of the others have been so quick to perceive, so prompt to do, just the right thing? or so resolute in deferring all questions about his adventures till afterwards?

1772 lads]
Young men of spirit and vigour; see OED, n.1 2a, b. OED (n.1 1): A man of low birth and position is also applicable, though not intended by Bottom.

1772 harts]
OED (Heart, 15a): As a term of appreciation or commendation: Man of courage or spirit. Andrews (ed. 1993): Bottom probably means hearts, but Harts (frequently symbolising royalty or nobility in Shakespeare) would fit the context too.…An audience would perceive no distinction.

1773–4 Bottom…houre]
Canning (1903, p. 479): The incident of the donkey’s head that had so frightened the others is never again mentioned, which is extraordinary, as Bottom was wearing it when his friends last saw him, and they would surely have wished to know how he obtained, and how he got rid of it. But Quince’s tentative curiosity in 1778 is immediately silenced by Bottom’s repeated refusal to respond to questions.

1773 couragious]
Delius (ed. 1859) suggests a blunder for auspicious. Hunter (ed. 1870): Encouraging. Wright (ed. 1877): It is not worth while to guess what Quince intended to say. He used the first long word that occurred to him without reference to its meaning, a practice which is not yet altogether extinct. Kinnear (1883, p. 96): Of a good heart, heart-inspiring. Verity (ed. 1893), as Thirlby (MS 1747–53) had noted: Cf. the colloquial use of brave = fine. See OED, Brave, adj. 3. Andrews (ed. 1993): Without realizing it, Quince plays on the Latin word for heart, cor, from which courageous derives.

1773 happy]
See n. 5.

1775 Masters]
See n. 283.

1775 am to]
OED (To, B. II. 11a [a]): Is to…= intends to…, is going to…, will… See also n. 360. Abbott §405: I am (ready) to; the meaning is partly of desire and partly of necessity.

1776 For…Athenian]
Moberly (ed. 1881): As I am a true Athenian, I will tell you nothing for the asking. So Falstaff would give no reasons on compulsion, if reasons were as plenty as blackberries [see 1H4 2.4.238–40 (1196–8)]. Chambers (ed. 1897): Cf. Acts, xvii. 21, For all the Athenians, and strangers which were there spent their time in nothing else, but either to tell, or to hear some new thing. Bottom’s anxiety at once to tell his tale and to keep up the mystery of it, is very humorous. Brooks (ed. 1979): Rather, the comedy (apart from the involuntary tantalizing of his comrades) lies in his refusals to tell being cover for not being able to.

1776 not true]
Sisson (1956, 1.131): There is no need to read, with Folio, Kittredge and others, no true Athenian. It is not even true that there is nothing to choose between not and no. There is a fine shade of idiom here in favour of not, even if the choice were free.

1777 right]
Delius (ed. 1859): Exactly, just; lacking in the Folio (Ger.).

1777 fell out]
OED (Fall 94g): To happen, chance, occur.

1779–89 Not…away]
Morley (1866, pp. 71–2), describing Samuel Phelps as Bottom in 1853: His dream clings about him.…[H]is parting No more words; away; go away [4.2.45], was in the tone of a man who had lived with spirits and was not yet perfectly returned into the flesh. Quoted also by Holland (ed. 1994). Ward (1875, 1:498): Mr. Phelps…appears to me in his otherwise admirable representation of Bottom the Weaver to err precisely in the direction of over-characterisation.

1779 of]
On of used for out of see Abbott §166.

1780 dined]
Andrews (ed. 1993): Completed his main, midday meal. It is now early afternoon. By the time the Mechanicals perform their play, the Duke and his court will have also finished supper [1827–8].

1780–1 good strings]
Capell (1783, 2.3:114): The strings of those false beards in which the parts of this gentleman, and of his other companions, were to be discharg’d…and good strings are desir’d for them, lest they should drop off. See n. 354. Dessen & Thomson (1999, p. 23): In the earlier troupe plays in which a small number of actors played many parts in rapid succession beards were attached by strings. But Grey (1754, 1.70) sees an allusion probably to the whimsical fashions in wearing their beards in Shakespeare’s time; Steevens (ed. 1793) offered some support in believing the strings ornamental, and citing the Duke’s instructions for disguising the head of Barnardine: Shaue the head, and tie the beard (MM 4.2.175 [2041]).

1781 new…pumpes]
Cf. Rom. 3.1.27–9 (1456–9): Didst thou not fall out…with another, for tying his new shooes with old Riband. Linthicum (1936, pp. 253): In the latter part of the sixteenth century, latchets appear on low shoes which resembled pumps.
