These latchets, which were tied together in front, must account for Bottom’s injunction.

1781 pumpes]
Florio (1598, Scarpini): Linnen socks. Also dancing pumps or little shooes. Johnson (1755, 2), quoting this instance: A shoe with a thin sole and low heel.OED (n.2 a): A kind of light shoe,…kept on the foot by its close fit, and having no fastening. Baker (ed. 1896) quotes W. W. Skeat’s Etymological Dictionary: So called because used for pomp or ornament; but OED (Etym.): There does not appear in the early use of pumps any clear connexion with pomp or show. It may have been an echoic word, suggested by the dull flapping sound made by slippers, as distinct from the stamp of heavy shoes. Linthicum (1936, pp. 253–4): Although worn also by gentlefolk, pumps were the footwear of servants.…[S]ince they did not add to the height of the wearer, they were associated with comedy.…[T]heir presence in Bottom’s Pyramus and Thisbe is unexplainable, unless they were used as part of the parody, or, as the usual footwear of Bottom’s fellows, were not changed.

1782 presently]
Schmidt (1875): Immediately.

1782–3 euery…part]
Possibly his idiosyncratic capitalization prompts Andrews (ed. 1993, p. 122, n. 21) to see this statement in conjunction with the next phrase as inadvertently bawdy.

1782–3 looke ore]
OED (19a), quoting this instance first: Peruse; pass in review.

1783 part]
For this theatrical term, see n. 329.

1783 short…long]
OED (Long, a.1 B. quasi-n. and n. 3): The sum total, substance, upshot. Brooks (ed. 1979), citing Tilley (L419): A colloquial idiom.

1783 preferd]
Theobald (ed. 1733): This Word is not to be taken in its most common Acceptation here, as if their Play was chosen in Preference to the others; (for that appears afterwards not to be the fact;) but means, that it was given in, among others, for the Duke’s Option: And, in this Sense, we say,—preferr a Petition; i.e. give it in, lodge it, for the Judge’s Answer; he cites JC 3.1.27–8 (1233–4): Where is Metellus Cimber, let him go, And presently preferre his suite to Cæsar. Wright (ed. 1877): Offered for acceptance; if Bottom’s words have a meaning, which is not always certain. See OED 4. Brooks (ed. 1979): Characteristically, Bottom considers it as good as chosen. Delius (ed. 1859) suggests the word is Bottom’s blunder for performed or presented. On the selection process, see n. 1830–81.

1784 In…linnen]
Stewart (1908, p. 115) questions whether this remark is prompted by some half conscious effect of the dainty vision of Titania.

1784 linnen]
OED (B. n. 3 collect. a), quoting this instance: Garments normally made of linen. Often spec. = undergarments, e.g. shirts.

1785–6 hang out]
OED (27a), quoting this instance: To protrude with downward direction.

1786 clawes]
Glancing at the proverb To know a lion by his claws. Erasmus, Adagia 1.9.34. See Tilley (L313).

1786–9 And…away]
Dent (1971, p. 85): Bottom, besides wanting to play at least three characters in the little tragedy of Pyramus and Thisbe, also envied Quince his job as stage director. Witness his instructions to the whole company just before the actual performance…which is the purest Granville Barker, or Kominarjevsky, or Peter Brook, at the end of a dress rehearsal when it is hoped it will be all right on the night.

1787 garlicke]
Grey (1754, 1.70): See Horace’s advice to Mæcenas, Epod.3. Delius (ed. 1859): The bad smell from eating garlic is mentioned as a characteristic of workingmen in Cor. 4.6.98 (3015) (Ger.). Beisly (1864, pp. 54–5): This and other plants having similar properties were much eaten by the common people in Shakspere’s time; among other references he quotes (p. 56) William Bullein’s Bulwarke of Defence, 1579: It is a grosse kynde of Medycine, very vnpleasaunt for fayre Ladyes, and tender Lilly Rose colloured Damselles, whych often tymes preferre sweete breathes before gentle Wordes (fol. 3r). See also Ellacombe (1884, pp. 102–3) and Seager (1896, pp. 124–5). Both Crosby ([1903], p. 12) and Stirling (1949, p. 69) comment on Sh.’s particular sensitivity to the smell of the artisan class. Duffy (1972, p. 156 & n.), citing Baskervill, 1929, pp. 292–5: Garlick was an obscene farce jig, much loved by unsophisticated audiences; so too were jigs about onions, both for their presumed aphrodisiac qualities. Whether onions and garlic were aphrodisiac or prophylactic (including of the pox) I’m not sure. The jig Garlick isn’t recorded until 1612 but in a reference that indicates it was older.

1787–9 sweete…Comedy]
Verity (ed. 1912) notes the quibble on sweete. Schmidt (1875): Pleasing to any sense;…agreeable to the smell, fragrant.…Hence, in general = pleasing, delightful.

1788–9 sweete breath]
Vienne-Guerrin (2002, p. 37) suggests that this means metaphorically sweet to the ears of the nobility, and she compares the gentle wordes in communication recommended by Della Casa (Galateo, 1576, M2v) (Fr.).

1788–9 breath]
Verity (ed. 1893): There is a quibble on the ordinary sense and on the meaning words. See nn. 527, 1067.

1788–9 I…hear]
For the construction, see Franz §569.

1789 Comedy]
See n. 279–80.

1789 No…wordes]
Chambers (ed. 1897): No one has had much chance of any words but Bottom himself. Cf. MM 3.2.206 (1693).

1792 Philostrate]
For the Folio’s substitution of Egeus for Philostrate here and elsewhere this scene, see textual n., , and .

1793 Tis…of]
Suhamy (2002, p. 236): The paranomastic echoes between strange and speak, continued in the next line by strange and true, the assonances formed by ’Tis-Theseus-these-speak, the near alliteration of Theseus-that-these, do not sound gratuitous or fortuitous. Hippolyta remembers what the young people have been relating and she herself delivers a sentence vibrating with the memory of another speech. Not the sounds themselves but their haunting coherence produces a reverberating effect, an indirect mimesis, like an acoustic shadow.

1793 my Theseus]
Clayton (1999, pp. 65–6): Theirs is…a civil(ized) relationship of mutual love and good liking; and that is expressed most succinctly by my Theseus: one doesn’t address a stranger to one’s affections thus, and this is the opening note of act 5. Morse (2003, p. 53): The mutual use of my as a vocative (my Oberon, my Theseus, my Hippolyta) is the best indicator of new amity; the pronoun recognizes relation, not simply possession. In this case the mutuality is delayed, since Theseus’s only such address is at 131; mutual accord is more readily apparent in the exchange between Oberon and Titania at 1590–1, or less closely between Hermia and Lysander at 179, 238. The only instances in the play of my as a vocative combined with a proper name occur at 131, 179, 238, 524, 898, 1590, 1591, and here. See n. 131 (my).

1793 Theseus]
For the pronunciation see n. 26.

1793 that]
That which. For omission of the relative, see Abbott §244.

1794–1813 More…Beare]
Johnson (ed. 1765): These beautiful lines are in all the old Editions thrown out of metre. They are very well restored by the later Editors. See textual nn. 1797 to 1806–9. For discussion of mislineation and theories of revision, see .
While the poetic force of this speech is generally admired, commentators often disagree or express uneasiness about whether it propounds Shakespeare’s own view of the ideas put forward, or whether its ironic context undercuts its apparent presentation of what has been frequently seen as an ideal expression of the art of poetry.
Gentleman (ed. 1774) believes that the poet is distinguished from lunatic and lover: This speech is most inimitably fine; it is exceeding rich in thought, and powerful in expression; the distinction between a poetical frenzy, and a real one, is pictured with philosophical precision in a fine flow of versification, two points not easily united; the contractedness of the former, mostly jarring with the latitude of the latter. But Griffith (1775, p. 19): The deceptions of an enthusiastic or over-heated fancy, with the vain terrors of a dejected mind, are well described in [this] speech; in which our author classes the lunatic, the lover, and the poet, together; and might have taken in the fanatic too, along with them. Macaulay (1825, p. 308): By poetry we mean, the art of employing words in such a manner as to produce an illusion on the imagination, the art of doing by means of words what the painter does by means of colours. Thus the greatest of poets has described it, in lines universally admired for the vigour and felicity of their diction, and still more valuable on account of the just notion which they convey of the art in which he excelled. [Quotes 1806–9 As…name.] These are the fruits of the fine frenzy which he ascribes to the poet,—a fine frenzy doubtless, but still a frenzy. Truth indeed, is essential to poetry; but it is the truth of madness. White (1886, p. 16) cites the speech as an exemplar of Sh.’s mental growth, manifested in that poetical sententiousness which is so peculiar a trait of Shakespeare; that faculty of welding together truth, wisdom, and fancy in such a closely wrought unity that they are essentially one,…Such…is that passage upon imagination, with its fine distinction between apprehension and comprehension, and its description of the poet’s method and function.
Roffe (1851, p. 29) objects that Macaulay, like ninety-nine out of every hundred readers is wrong to think that the lines contain Shakespeare’s own idea; since it is the Skeptical Theseus who views the poet as being the embodier of the unreal, and not as the Copyist of what is true,…we may be sure that the Poet who wrote the lines for him thought precisely the very reverse. Roffe (p. 27) also makes a point frequently reiterated by later critics, that though every line [of the speech] is skeptical, yet the conduct of the Play falsifies the Duke’s reasonings, or rather his assertions. (Furness (ed. 1895) quotes Roffe from the differing ed. London: T. Scott, 1851. He also quotes Wedgwood (1890, pp. 583–4) on the modern scepticism towards the supernatural expressed in the speech.) Among those agreeing with Roffe in rejecting the identification of Shakespeare with Theseus’s views, and/or calling attention to the limitations of Theseus’s arguments, are MacDonald (1868, 3:117), Dowden (1875, p. 69), Chambers (ed. 1897), de Selincourt (1911, p. 5), Van Doren (1916, pp. 409–10), Bundy (1924, p. 536), Nemerov (1956, pp. 633–6), Cecil (1957, p. 40), Phillips (ed. 1969), Farrell (1975, p. 99), Carroll (1985, pp. 176–7), Patterson (1989, p. 53), Absher (1990, p. 92), Taylor (1995–6, p. 5), Creaser (2002, pp. 97–8). See also nn. 1804–9, 1806–9.
Some seek to locate the root of the speech’s ambiguous effect. Bethurum (1928, pp. 62, 67) finds it an example of the conflict between the demands of poetry and those of dramatic interest in any poetic drama. Cf. Pohl (1972, pp. 56–8). Pettet (1950, p. 44): Having given to the Duke—and with perfect dramatic appropriateness—two common notions about poetry that he himself shared to some extent, Sh. gives two reasons for differentiating the poet’s state of mind, first, because the poet’s fantasy…has an altogether exceptional range; and secondly…because the poet’s play of imagination is to a large degree controlled. Olson (1957, p. 97) argues that since poets are not named with lovers and madmen in 1796–8, they [i]mplicitly…may deal in imaginings apprehensible in more rational terms. Sewell (1960, pp. 115–16), quoting 1794–9: One can almost hear the dry voice of Bacon appealing to the dry light of rationality.…But when we come to the poet, it is as if Shakespeare…speaks himself,…[quotes 1800–9 name]. Immediately after, Shakespeare-Theseus becomes Bacon-Theseus again,…Meantime, however, the statement stands. Dent (1964, p. 129): Theseus links lunatic, lover, and poet indiscriminately. Shakespeare, by contrasting the role of imagination in love with that in dramatic poetry, discriminates. Fender (1968, pp. 49–50) believes that the echoes of 156 and 246–7 invite the audience to balance Theseus’s dismissal of imagination as merely delusive against the opposite view of imagination as a vision of a better world. Kernan (1975, pp. 317–18): Theseus is right in one way in denying apprehension in favour of comprehension…but in so doing he and the civil world he represents place themselves in a most ironic position…[since he] is himself no more than the imagined idea of government and reason given, in legend and play, the name of Theseus and the habitation of Athens. Beaurline (1978, p. 101): The reality of love and concord…rests upon these seeming follies that the audience can understand are rooted in nature’s mysteries, behind the apparent jesting of Theseus’ speech. Brooks (ed. 1979, p. cxl) quotes T. S. Eliot saying that sometimes in drama the words may not have quite the same meaning for author and character (On Poetry and Poets, 1957, p. 100), and adds: Theseus intends them as censure; but his eloquence…blesses altogether. Riemer (1980, p. 196): This speech resembles a set passage, and its latter portion,…despite its overtly censorious tone, sounds rather remote from Theseus’s generally unsympathetic view of the lovers’ story. Finch (1981, p. 5): Theseus’s speech illustrates the impossibility of trying to describe the activity of the imagination without using words that are themselves resonant with imaginative life.…Despite Theseus’s sceptical irony, the lines tell us that the poet’s art is a second creation. Faas (1986, p. 137): Through use of a multiple irony, Shakespeare makes us affirm what Theseus negates; for an argument rejecting Faas’s claim, see Baxter (1996, p. 28). B. Parker (1987, p. 199) argues that Theseus is rejecting the disordered imagination of the lunatic and lover, not the creative imagination of the poet. Brown (1999, p. 64) compares the similarly misplaced scepticism of Ovid’s Pirithoüs, who dismisses the story Achelous tells at Theseus’s request as ficta (Met. 8.614; Golding 8.788 feyned fancies).
Some of the ambivalence of response to the speech flows from whether critics admire Theseus as a character of nobility and wisdom, or whether they deplore his insistence on rationality and common sense; both groups frequently point to this speech and the subsequent discussion in support of their position (see ).
An even greater source of ambiguity is found in the wider question of theories of imagination and attitudes to poetry. The conflicting views expressed in Elizabethan writings are explored by Rossky (1958, p. 49) whose study proposes, first, to examine the psychological account of the operation of imagination and thus to show not only the disrepute but the specific grounds of the disrepute of the faculty and, secondly, to indicate briefly how the particular grounds for disrepute influence the view of imagination expressed by poets and literary critics, are indeed converted into a justification of the poet’s imagination (p. 49). The ideas set out by Rossky influence discussions of the themes of imagination and poetry in MND (see ).
The variety of sources and backgrounds suggested for the speech reflects the Renaissance disputes. Most frequently drawn upon for illustration are Plato, Aristotle, Renaissance Italian critics of both schools, Montaigne, Puttenham, and Sidney. The fullest discussions are found in Rhoads (1985, esp. pp. 61–76, 87–9) and Faas (1986, esp. pp. 136–43); both recognize the multiplicity of 16th-c. views. Rhoads, giving prominence to Sidney, stresses the political and ethical dimensions of the issues raised by Theseus. Faas argues that Theseus differs from most Renaissance critics whether Aristotelian, Neoplatonic, or Christian; his comments (p. 141) while alluding to the furor poeticus, reverse some of the claims traditionally associated with that notion.
Others making specific reference to Plato and Platonists include the following. Simpson (1868, p. 28) draws on the Symposium for his analysis. Anders (1926, p. 159) parallels passages from Phaedrus 245 and Ion 534 (Tr. Jowett 1:451, 502). Pettet (1950, pp. 31–2) sees a general influence of the Platonic furor, described in Ion (533–4; tr. Jowett 1:501–2), as transmitted through Sidney’s Defense. Hammerle (Poet’s Eye, 1953, pp. 105–7) finds parallels with Theaetetus and Phaedrus, and assumes that Platonic ideas spread from Italy through various channels (Ger.); see . Nitze (1955, pp. 495–7) also points to Phaedrus on love as the noblest of the madnesses (244–5; tr. Jowett 1:449–51), possibly transmitted through Ficino and Spenser. Heninger (1987, p. 217) sees the speech as a paraphrase of Plato’s Phaedrus (244A–245C). Absher (1990, p. 92) likens Theseus to a stand-in for Plato and the patriarchal consciousness of The Republic. Mukerji (1965, p. 151) finds echoes of the Longinian concept of ecstatic imagination. Rudd (1979, p. 192) cites the Platonic tradition, particularly Plotinus. Ferrara (1964, p. 203) quotes Ficino Sopra lo Amore. Salingar (1974, p. 281) suggests an echo of Bruno’s De gli eroici furori (1585).
Sidney’s Defense of Poesie is also often discussed, for both Platonic and Aristotelian elements in his arguments, though few agree with Poirier (1947, p. 488) that the passage is nothing else but a sort of compendium of what Sidney says on poetic imagination. Thaler (1947; 1967, pp. 8–9) offers verbal parallels. Reyher (1947, p. 61) compares Sidney’s Arcadia on conceits of the poets (ed. Evans, 1977, p. 114). Pettet (1950, p. 44) and Young (1966, p. 133), among others, also quote Puttenham (1589; 1936, pp. 18–20) as representative of some ideas in the speech; see also n. 1797 shaping fantasies.
C. (1870, p. 594) finds 1804–8 an amplification and improvement of Plautus, Pseudolus 401–3 [A poet…hunts for what is nowhere on this earth, yet finds it, and makes a lie look like the truth (tr. Paul Nixon)] ; see also Collins (1904, p. 22) and n. 1875. Hammerle (Poet’s Eye, 1953, pp. 102–5) cites Plutarch (tr. North, 1979, p. 1072); Bryant (1961, pp. 5–16) traces a background of scriptural exegesis through Dante, Aquinas, and Augustine. Hankins (1978, pp. 90–4) draws on La Primaudaye and Aquinas to explicate the terms used in the speech’s systematic exposition of the functioning of the phantasy and the imagination. Rosenblum (1981, p. 359) suggests a debt to Cesare Ripa’s representation of poetic fury (Iconologia, 1593), which depicts a youth holding pen and paper, standing on earth but looking to the heavens. Mowat (1989, p. 343) connects Theseus’s concerns with the deluding (and empowering) effects of strong imagination with Scot’s Discoverie of Witchcraft; see also Brooks, ed. 1979. Pierce (1994, pp. 150–1) sees some influence of the teachings of Pyrrhonism. Moisan (1998, p. 286) compares a similar impatience with poetic sources in North’s Plutarch (see , , ). See also nn. 1806–7, 1875.
Henze (1974, p. 115) attempts to show that though Sh.’s dramatized theory of the imagination resembles somewhat that of Sidney and his colleagues, it reveals itself finally to be…unfettered by either Platonic frenzy or Aristotelian ideal imitation as it demonstrates the selective imitation that will proceed from observation of life, experience with common images, and awareness of the nature of the audience’s relationship to a play.

1794–5 More…fables]
Brooks (ed. 1979): Lyly opened the last Act of Endymion with the like comment on the foregoing magical adventures: Eumenides hath tolde such strange tales as I may well wonder at them, but neuer beleeue them [5.1.1–2; 3:63].

1794 More…true]
Cf. Tilley (S914): It is no more strange than true. Holland (ed. 1994): Theseus would have done better to follow the proverb’s normal form.

1794 neuer]
Blake (6.2.1.2): Never was used instead of not, though it may have additional emphasis. See also Franz §407. See also 1372.

1794 may beleeue]
See n. 72.

1795 antique]
See textual n. Schmidt (1874, Antic, O. Edd. promiscuously antick and antique, but always accented on the first syllable, adj., 3): Old and quaintly figured. OED (Antic, a. 2): Absurd from fantastic incongruity; bizarre; (Antique, a. 1): Belonging to former times, ancient, olden; and see headnotes to each. Moberly (ed. 1881): Out of the way, as modern in Shakspere means common. Riemer (1980, p. vii): These fables are both antique—reminiscent of hoary mouldy tales, and antic—foolish, grotesque and undignified. Without the aid of annotations it is not possible to convey to contemporary readers the rich possibilities inherent in Theseus’s remark. Barkan (1986, pp. 252, 234) compares with Spenser’s use of the same pun to identify (p. 234) the antic nature of metamorphic images with the antique source of such representations in FQ 3.11.51. Holland (ed. 1994): Antic suggests a world of performance, the theatrical context of a play which this Theseus will watch but also the one in which he is a character. Clayton (1999, p. 66): It is not clear why editors who often follow F prefer Q1 here. Since antique makes inferior sense otherwise, one supposes the preference due to its ready association with Theseus, who is routinely faulted, accordingly, for disbelieving in his own mythical identity.

1795 Fairy]
Thirlby (MS 1747–53): W[arburton] should read airy & perhaps if he had thought of it condemning fairy because the lovers knew nothing of the machinery & defending airy by [1808 ayery]. Meagher (1997, pp. 54, 196) argues that 1894–5 make it plain that the lovers had spoken of the fairies.

1795 toyes]
Johnson (1755, 6), quoting this instance: Old story; silly tale. OED (3a), quoting this instance: A fantastic or trifling speech; a foolish or idle tale; see also (2): A fantastic act; an antic, a trick.

1796–1809 Louers…name]
Gildon (1710, 7:320): All his Fairies, Goblins, and the like are of this Kind, which he describes here. Pope (ed. 1725, 6:4K1r) lists the passage among notable Thoughts, or Sentiments as Imagination, strong in Lovers, Poets, and Madmen.

1796–8 Louers…comprehends]
Dent (L505.2): Love is a madness (lunacy); he also compares Tilley (L517): Love is without reason. See n. 960–1.

1796 Louers…braines]
Chambers (ed. 1897): In the mind of Theseus, his own deep but sane affection for Hippolyta is a thing apart from such passions and absurdities of youthful lovers as this play treats of. Brooks (ed. 1979) compares Scot’s scorn for those who fear supposedly supernatural manifestations (see ).

1796 seething braines]
Rann (ed. 1787): Warm ideas, enthusiastic notions. Malone (ed. 1790) compares WT 3.3.63–5 (1505–7): Would any but these boylde-braines of nineteene, and two and twenty hunt this weather? Steevens (ed. 1793) compares Tmp. 5.1.59–60 (2015–16): Thy braines (Now vselesse) boile within thy skull. Delius (ed. 1859) cites Mac. 2.1.38–9 (618–19): A false Creation, Proceeding from the heat-oppressed Braine. The Clarkes (ed. 1864): Sh. has elsewhere employed the idea of boiled brains as indicating sodden, sapless, inapt brains; but here seething brains rather means bubbling, working, toiling, active brains. Cuningham (ed. 1905): Note the antithesis to cool in [1798].

1797–1809 Such…imagination]
See textual n. Wilson (ed. 1924) argues that the mislineation results from the later addition of the lines The lunatick…imagination. On mislineation, and theories of revision, see .

1797 shaping]
Johnson (1755, To Shape 3), quoting this instance: To image; to conceive.

1797 shaping phantasies]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Fancies capable of giving form and shape to things that have no existence. Gill (ed. 1981): Inventive imaginations. See Puttenham (1589; 1936, p. 19) on perspective glasses, at n. 753–4; he continues: Euen so is the phantasticall part of man (if it be not disordered) a representer of the best, most comely and bewtifull images or apparances of thinges to the soule and according to their very truth. If otherwise, then doth it breede Chimeres & monsters in mans imaginations. See also nn. 40, 1794–1813.

1797–8 apprehend…comprehends]
Hense (1851, p. 19) notes Sh.’s fondness for this type of wordplay, citing AWW 1.3.147–8 (471–2) opprest/expresse, 2H4 1.2.181–2 (441) decreasing/increasing and 3.2.261–2 (1794–5) charge/discharge (Ger.). Deighton (ed. 1891): apprehend, seize hold of; perceive the existence of. comprehends, takes hold of and assimilates to itself. The hasty clutching at an idea by fancy is contrasted with the deliberate manner in which reason examines an idea before accepting and making it a part of herself. Fender (1968, p. 50): Comprehension is the art of generalising, or relating things to categories; it is one of the tools of reason.…Apprehension circumvents the process of reason; it is the sudden, inexplicable knowledge that a thing is so. According to Aquinas, it is the way angels think, and Hamlet grants the faculty to men, at their best. Carroll (1985, p. 176): Theseus’s facile distinctions between apprehend and comprehend, and his condescending vision of simple deception, represent a myopic anti-metamorphic belief. The loss is his, not ours. Bradshaw (1987, p. 39): Our sense of local and contextual ironies modifies our understanding of Theseus’s Hector-like contrast between apprehending and comprehending. Holland (ed. 1994): Bacon, in the dedication of The Advancement of Learning (Works, ed. J. Spedding et al. (1857), iii. 262), praised the King for having a composition of understanding admirable, being able to compass and comprehend the greatest matters, and nevertheless to touch and apprehend the least, whereas it should seem an impossibility in nature for the same instrument to make itself fit for great and small works. Hassel (2005, pp. 22–3) : Theologians can use [apprehend] as an antonym of comprehend which suggests either transcendent knowing or transcendent foolishness.…[T]he apprehension of angels or of gods would be considered superior to mere human understanding, or comprehension. See also 1810–11.

1799–1800 The…compact]
Suhamy (2002, p. 237): The sentence made axiomatic by the usage of generalities expressed in the singular, and by the hyperbaton that enables the construction to culminate in the word compact, is itself compact in the modern sense. In Shakespeare’s English the adjective means compounded. It also suggests intricate density, perhaps some opacity. See also n. 1800.

1799 The lunatick…Poet]
Tilley (W579): Great wits (poets) to madness sure are near allied. Malone (1783, p. 11): An ingenious modern writer supposes that our author had here in contemplation Orestes, Mark Antony, and himself; but I do not recollect any passage in his works that shows him to have been acquainted with the story of Agamemnon’s son—scelerum furiis agitatus Orestes: and indeed, if even such were found, the supposed allusion would still remain very problematical. Pye (1807, p. 57): If Mr. Malone does not write this ironically, or is not much mistaken as to the genius of the writer: this is a sad proof of the excessive imbecility a man of genius may fall into. Alden (1932, p. 85) lists this line as an example of a line with fewer than five accents (lun-, -tic, lov-, po-), but the fifth stress may fall on and, to emphasize the addition to Louers and mad men in 1796.

1799 lunatick]
Johnson (1755), quoting this instance: A madman. Foakes (ed. 1984): The word still retained its sense of insanity brought on by changes of the moon (from Latin luna = moon).

1800 of…compact]
Steevens (ed. 1778): I.e. made up of mere imagination, quoting AYL 2.7.5 (977): If he compact of iarres, grow Musicall. See OED, Compact, ppl. a.1 2.

1800 compact]
Andrews (ed. 1993): This word may also be meant to imply that those who have entered into a subversive compact (plot or conspiracy) are likely to have the seething Brains of those who let their wild imaginations carry them away. Cf. OED, ppl. a.2; n.1 1c.

1801 One…holde]
Chambers (ed. 1897): Chalmers [1799, p. 361] found in this passage an allusion to Lodge’s Wit’s Miserie and the World’s Madnesse: discovering the incarnate devils of this age (1596). But this is the emptiest of empty critical theories.

1801 vast]
OED (2), quoting this instance first: Of immense extent.

1802 That…frantick]
Common elisions for That is and louer allow for a ten-syllable line, though with some unusual stresses; however, inserting a comma after is precludes the first elision. Suhamy (2002, p. 234) reads with feminine endings both at the caesura and the end of the line; he adds: The device does not seem fortuitous. In expounding his theory with intellectual complacency Theseus builds up an enumerative argument. The construction is so impellingly didactic that it cuts across the lineaments of versification. Pope’s emendation of the meter, omitting That is and inserting While, has not been adopted by editors after Johnson. See textual n.

1802 all as frantick]
Mowat & Werstine (ed. 1993): I.e., just as insane.

1802–3 louer…Ægypt]
Verity (ed. 1893): He is frantic because he sees in the face of a dark-complexioned woman (cf. [1286]) beauty worthy of Helen of Troy. Suhamy (2002, p. 238) compares Lucretius, De natura rerum, 4.1153–70 on lovers’ illusory perception of the beauty that their own desire has created.

1803 Helens…Ægypt]
Parker (1992, p. 386) sees in the conjunction of Helen and Egypt a glancing reference to Stesichorus’s account of the two Helens, where Egypt is the place of the chastely distanced double or lookalike.

1803 Helens beauty]
Cuningham (ed. 1905): Familiar to Elizabethans from the gorgeous lines of Marlowe [in Dr. Faustus]: O thou art fairer than the evening air, Clad in the beauty of a thousand stars (5.1;1780 [2:220]).

1803 brow of Ægypt]
Steevens (ed. 1793): Shakspeare means no more than the brow of a gipsy. Caldecott (MS 1813–33): I.e. In the unlovely & sallow complexion of a native of Egypt. Delius (ed. 1859) asserts that in Oth. 3.4.56 (2204) Egyptian means gipsy. For the belief that gipsy derived from Egyptian see OED, Gipsy, 1a, and Egyptian, B. n. 2. Levenson (2000, ed. Rom., p. 231): With their dark skin and hair, gypsies represented the antithesis of the English Renaissance ideal of beauty. (See Rom. 2.4.41 [1146].) See also n. 769; cf. 1286, 1294.

1804–9 The Poets…name]
Anon. (1747, p. 22): How exactly has he painted himself in his Midsummer Nights Dream! Cf. John Mortimer’s idealized 1775 portrait Poet (W. L. Pressly, A Catalogue of Paintings in the Folger Shakespeare Library, 1993, Frontispiece and p. 12). Fly (1976, p. 30) compares Heminge and Condell’s description in the Folio of Sh.: His mind and hand went together: And what he thought, he vttered with that easinesse, that wee haue scarse receiued from him a blot in his papers.

1804 in…frenzy, rolling]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Rolling in the ecstasy of inspiration. Johnson (ed. 1894): Glowing with noble passion. For Q1’s superfluous comma, cf. 1664.

1804 fine frenzy]
OED (Frenzy, n. 2. fig.), quoting this instance: Agitation or disorder of the mind likened to madness; a state of delirious fury, rage, enthusiasm, or the like; also, wild folly, distraction, craziness. F. (1874, p. 336) compares Horace, amabilis insania (Odes 3.4.5–6). Moberly (ed. 1881): One which he knows is admired. Page (ed. 1897): Refined, delicate, or subtle species of delirium. Brooks (ed. 1979), citing Tilley (W579) (see n. 1799): The famous furor poeticus; but see Faas in n. 1794–1813. Brown (1981, p. 20): In Shakespeare’s day, the word frenzy—however clearly and perhaps ironically linked with fine—described the last outrages of madness, an uncontrollable state of unreason, beyond the merely frantic. Andrews (ed. 1993): Here fine may carry sarcastic overtones, as in [1318 and 2142]. See also n. 1794–1813.

1805 heauen…heauen]
For the pronunciation see n. 156.

1806–9 as…name]
Bate (1986, pp. 128–9) discusses William Blake’s response to Theseus’ definition of the imagination. An earlier reader of Blake’s copy of Swedenborg’s Heaven and Hell had written Theseus’s lines on the half-title page. Blake annotated the annotation: Thus fools quote Shakespeare; the above is Theseus’ opinion Not Shakespear’s. You might as well quote Satan’s blasphemies from Milton and give them as Milton’s Opinions.…Later Blake…was reported as saying that the things imagination saw were as much realities as were gross and tangible facts. It was to Theseus’ aery nothing that he objected…. For Shakespeare himself, Blake implies, the imaginative world…is the one that endures.

1806–7 as…shapes]
Strutt (in Seymour (1805, 1:48–9): As imagination brings forth from her womb, strange and unnatural forms of things, the poet, in his inspiration, turns them to shapes well known,…: there is an evident distinction made between the unknown infinite forms of things, bodied forth by the imagination, and the forms of things known: turns has the force of alters; and I think, after the word shapes, familiar or known, is implied; Seymour adds: I once wished to read, instead of the forms, a mass of things, but I am much better pleased with the preceding explanation. The form of things unknown is the idea of the unlicked bear-cub that carries no impression like the dam. Lee (1967, p. 134) reports from an article by Tudor Vianu in a Romanian journal: After commenting on the vogue which Plotinus’s conception of internal form…enjoyed among writers on aesthetics in the later Renaissance—being variously called disegno interno, disegno fantastico and imitazione fantastica—he suggests that Shakespeare was under the influence of that idea when he wrote these lines. Rudd (1979, p. 192) sees a debt to Ovid: The opening words of the Metamorphoses are: in noua fert animus mutatis dicere formas / corpora. My mind bids me tell of forms turned into new shapes. Cf. Golding’s Ovid 1.1; see .

1806 Imagination]
Johnson (1755, 1): Fancy; the power of forming ideal pictures; the power of representing things absent to one’s self or others. OED (4b), quoting this instance: The creative faculty of the mind in its highest aspect; the power of framing new and striking intellectual conceptions; poetic genius. In early dictionaries imagination is often linked with 'fantasy' or 'idle conceit' as well as 'invention.' See also Cotgrave (1611, Mesure): Cuider n’est pas iuste mesure: Prov. Coniecture is no iust measure; the iust conclude not by imagination.

1806 bodies forth]
Johnson (1755), quoting this instance: To produce in some form. OED (Body, v. 4a), quoting this as the only instance before 1820: To represent to oneself as in bodily form. Cited by Theobald (1726, p. 8) as an example for his dictum: It is a Licence in our Poet, of his own Authority, to coin new Verbs both out of Substantives and Adjectives. Brooks (ed. 1979): Embodies in forms which the mind’s eye, ear, etc. can take in; find (in T. S. Eliot’s phrase) an objective correlative for. Gill (ed. 1981): Expresses. Andrews (ed. 1989): Gives birth to.

1806 formes of things]
The phrase is used by North in Cassius’s disquisition on imagination (see ), as partly noted by Hammerle (Poet’s Eye, 1953, p. 104) (Ger.).

1807 shapes]
Hudson (ed. 1880) defends Pope’s emendation (see textual n.): One of the commonest misprints is that of singulars and plurals for each other. Cuningham (ed. 1905) counters by citing LLL 4.2.66–7 (1230–32): A foolish extrauagant spirit, full of formes, figures, shapes, obiectes, Ideas; and TN 1.1.14–15 (18–19): So full of shapes is fancie, That it alone, is high fantasticall. Bradbrook (1951, p. 47): Shape is the technical term for an actor’s costume; see OED, n.1 8. Brown (2002, p. 32): Shapes was in those days close in meaning to shadows, the word Theseus uses of actors [2015]. For play on shapes and shadows cf. TGV 4.2.124–31 (1747–54).

1808–9 ayery…name]
Lee (1926, pp. 8–9), arguing the power of verse as verse to transform the obvious into the memorable, notes the miraculous pat felicity of that local habitation and a name which everyone quotes daily; the enchanting grace of that airy nothing.

1808 ayery]
OED (7a), quoting this instance first: Unsubstantial, vain, empty, unreal, imaginary. Cf. 978.

1808 ayery nothing]
Verity (ed. 1893): What is unsubstantial, non-existent. Hankins (1978, p. 92): To Sh. this does not mean non-existent but non-tangible.

1808 nothing]
Willbern (1980, pp. 248–9) compares Leontes’ desire to make something of nothing by naming in WT 1.2.284–95 (377–89), and claims: [Theseus’] characterizations (but not use) of imagination and poetic creativity make him a benevolent version of Leontes.

1808 locall habitation]
Gill (ed 1981): Particular dwelling-place. See also Psalm 25 (26).8, locum habitationis; verses 7–8 praise God’s creation.

1809–18 Such…admirable]
Bryant (1961, p. 3): For [Hippolyta] the transfigured story is the dream that is real, vital, and efficacious; and that dream…is poetry.…[H]er view, if not exclusively Christian, is…consistent with distinctively Christian teaching. Palombo (1983, pp. 301–2): Theseus offers…the Freudian viewpoint on the tales told by the young lovers.…In our post-Freudian language, the tales are fantasies of wish fulfillment, psychodynamically indistinguishable from dreams.…[Hippolyta’s] view follows naturally from the psychological movement of the play, which turns on the resolution of Demetrius’s neurotic transference to Hermia through the therapeutic effect of a dream experience. See also n. 1814–18.

1809–11 Such…that ioy]
Brooks (ed. 1979): The trick is perfectly exemplified in the self-deception of Chaucer’s Troilus (Troilus and Criseyde, 5.115 ff., 1157 ff.).

1809 name]
Gill (ed. 1981): Identity. See OED 4a; for implication of the divine see also II.3.

1809 trickes]
Delius (ed. 1859): Peculiarities; strange ideas (Ger.). Schmidt (1875, 2): Knack, art, dextrous contrivance. OED (1c): An illusory or deceptive appearance. arch. or Obs.

1809 strong imagination]
Brooks (ed. 1979): [This] is Apelles’ phrase in Lyly, Campaspe, [4.4.13; 2:348] though he is not taking himself in.

1809 imagination]
For the pronunciation, see n. 159.

1810–11 That…that ioy]
Chambers (ed. 1897): The mere idea of a joy is enough incentive to a strong imagination to conjure up and believe in the presence of something which causes that joy. Jones-Davies (1994, p. 103): In his Outlines of Pyrrhonism [Loeb, tr. R. G. Bury, 1933], Sextus [Empiricus] explains that apprehension of impressions—real or unreal—does not preclude inquiry through reason…(2:157–59).…Theseus’ words recall Sextus’s terminology when describing the stages in the process of thinking: the apprehension of the sense impression, then the comprehension by the intellect (cool reason) of the objects represented by the imagination. We read in Sextus that the reason proceeds from the apprehension of sensibles to the apprehension of intelligibles (3:357).

1811 comprehends]
Bradshaw (1987, p. 2): In Theseus’s speech the sense of comprehend shifts, to allow that the object of supposedly rational comprehension may itself be one of fancies images, a product of shaping fantasies; at pp. 39–40 he suggests Theseus is in this use being ironic.

1812–13 Or…Beare]
White (1854, p. 218) denies that the lines are genuine, believing them an interpolation by some player; Idem (ed. 1858): Would Shakespeare, after thus reaching the climax of his thought, fall a twaddling about bushes and bears? Note, too, the loss of dignity in the rhythm. The Clarkes (ed. 1864): Viewed dramatically the couplet serves to give character and naturalness to the dialogue as Theseus brings back the conversation to the point of the last night’s visions. Furnivall (ed. 1877, pp. xxvi–vii) citing also the undignified quarrelling of the ladies (1330–85) and the comical comparison of the moon tumbling through the earth (1076–8): The descent to bathos in Shakspere’s passage about his own art, from the poet’s eye in a fine frenzy rolling to how easy is a bush supposed a bear, would have been impossible to Shakspere in his later development. Chambers (ed. 1897): These lines are rather bald after what they follow. If the scene has been rewritten…, perhaps we have here a survival from the earlier version. Porter & Clarke (ed. 1903, pp. xxxii–iii) argue that the conclusion is appropriate to the contemptuous tone of the whole, and is an indication of Theseus’ character. Harrison (1924, p. 45) stigmatizes the lines as incredibly bad and a dreadful couplet. For Tillyard (1938, p. 64) it is the final essential couplet revealing Theseus’ healthy attitude. Young (1966, p. 71) argues that the couplet is germane to the rest of [Theseus’] speech and to the rest of the play, as well as to his characterization. Hawkes (1992, p. 18): Theseus is making the case that in those of strong imagination, the activity of apprehension, involving the imaginative generation of an emotion, leads almost immediately to its comprehension, by means of concrete actualization in the material world. This, he then claims, applies equally well to the two opposed spheres of joy on the one hand and fear on the other.…As Theseus uses it here, or links two polarities whilst maintaining the difference between them.

1812–13 feare…Beare]
For the rhyme, see n. 749–50.

1812 imagining]
For the participial construction, see n. 63 (wanting).

1812 feare]
Schmidt (1874, 5): A thing to be dreaded, an object of fear. See OED, n.1 5d. The Clarkes (1879, p. 809) list among examples of Sh.’s use of some words for the causes of that which they directly signify.

1813 bush…Beare]
Brooks (ed. 1979), citing Tilley (B738) He thinks every bush a bug-bear (bear) and (B737) He that is afraid of every bush will never prove good hunstman: The appeal to proverbial wisdom confirms and rounds off Theseus’ observations: by critics who fail to appreciate this the couplet has been thought weak. Cf. 3H6 5.6.12–14 (3086–8); Luc. 973. For MND’s recurrent bear images, see 558, 681, 749, 924, 926, 1634 and nn.

1814–18 But…admirable]
Deighton (ed. 1891): But the narration in all its particulars of what happened to them in the night, with the fact of their minds being all at one time affected by a similar transformation, gives proof of something more than fanciful imagination, and taken together has the appearance of real consistency; but this in any case, be that as it may (i.e. consistent or not), is worthy of wonder. Porter and Clarke (ed. 1903, p. xxxiii): Hippolyta ventures to question the practical man’s easy acceptance of external facts as the only possible truth, on the doubtful evidence that the coherence of so many personal experiences lifts the story upon an equal plane of credibility with fact. The poet, non-committal behind them both, insinuates the greater truth of art to life. Arnold (1955, p. 103): This is incontestably the conclusion of the poet himself (Fr.). Nemerov (1956, pp. 638–9): We have…no warrant for identifying Hippolyta’s reply with the views of Sh., but her lines have resonance far beyond the play, forming one of these miraculous instances in which poetry can be seen to have for ultimate subject its own self. Bryant (1961, p. 4): Theseus’ poet orders the data of experience in a dream that we expect to reject as soon as we have grasped the reality to which it points. Hippolyta’s poet recreates the data of experience in a dream that is truth itself, or all we shall likely get of truth this side of paradise. Frye (Recognition, 1962, p. 236): What critical ability there is in that family belongs entirely to Hippolyta, whose sharp comments are a most effective contrast to Theseus’s amiable bumble. Calderwood (1965, p. 520): Hippolyta’s lines…apply not only to the forest drama and the lovers’ minds but to [the play] and the collective transfiguration of mind wrought by it in the theater as well. Weiner (1971, p. 333) sees in her words an acknowledgement of the poet’s power to present not merely fairy toys but the eternal and unchanging truths governing the universe, whether we call them Platonic Ideas or embodiments of Christian Sapience. Dusinberre (1975, p. 155): Hippolyta lacks Theseus’ cool masculine conviction that he is right, and her doubt brings her nearer the truth. Girard (1980, p. 120): Hippolita…says that there is more to Midsummer Night than gratuitous imagination and…points to the systematic interplay of the four characters in that invention of mythology.…These five lines…confirm that A Midsummer Night’s Dream is Shakespeare’s own genetic theory of mythology. Berry (1984, p. 177): The mark of a mature lover, and true poet, it seems, is an ability to believe and disbelieve simultaneously, to hold the opposed worlds of truth and fiction in a precarious balance. Rhoads (1985, p. 88) relates Hippolyta’s words to Sidney’s theory that verisimilitude has more reality than historical fact. Horn (1986, p. 68): Her acceptance of [the story] is, of course, in accord with the prior action. Pearson (1987, p. 32): What she is telling us is that as a wife she refuses to subdue her own judgment to that of the man she has married, signalling her disagreement through more witnesseth and the But…which introduces her speech. Girard (1991, pp. 67–8) argues that Hippolyta contradicts Theseus on three basic points…The first is that myth is a collective rather than an individual phenomenon;…The second…, myth should not be confused with pure fiction,…The third…, in spite of its effervescent genesis and fantastic content, myth is something of great constancy. It has a stable structure…with all sorts of consequences that the purely subjective theory of Theseus cannot take into account.

1814 But,]
Q1’s comma, deleted by most editors, may be seen as a guide to delivery of the line.

1815 transfigur’d]
Sh.’s only use of this word. Schmidt (1875): To transform, to metamorphose. Holzknecht & McClure (ed. 1937): Changed, altered. Wells (ed. 1967, p. 33): The word transfigured helps to suggest that the woodland scenes represent for [the lovers] a genuine shaping experience. Warren (1969, pp. 133–4) cites Palamon’s reaction to Emelye, Venus, if it be thy wyll You in this garden, thus to transfigure Beforne me (Chaucer, KnT 1104–6; see ) and Christ’s transfiguration to argue that the word implies something far more powerful than simply changed: it suggests a revelation of the true nature of the lovers’ minds, and affections. Frye (1983, p. 78): The word…indicates that the experience is neither real nor illusory, but has got clear of that commonplace antithesis. The dramatist is not interested in the credible, but in…the reality that incorporates all the dreams of illusion. Andrews (ed. 1989): What Hippolyta refers to is the miracle by which the discords that separated the lovers have been replaced by a concord in which they are all of one mind. Hillman (1996, p. 82), referring to disfigure at 59 and 871: The first use is an ominous threat to any refusal to accept a harsh patriarchal dictate; the second is improper discourse, a disfigurement of standard meaning.…Hippolyta’s transfigur’d is a transcending of both disfigurings—orthodox and heterodox—manifested by Theseus and the mechanicals. See also nn. 203, 935–6 (translated), n. 247 (transpose), n. 1750 (transported).

1816 More…images]
Andrews (ed. 1989), simplifying: Testifies to something more substantial…than the illusory images produced by Fancy. Hibbard (Adumbrations, 1978, p. 81): Her deliberate substitution of fancy for Theseus’s imagination almost suggests that Shakespeare was on the point of making Coleridge’s distinction between the two. But he does not cite Harding (1964, p. 319) who, having analyzed Sh.’s uses of fancy/fantasy and imagination according to the categories of meanings given by Schmidt (1874) and in the light of theories pre- and post-Sh., believes: If Shakespeare may have subscribed to Coleridge’s description of imaginative activity as the balancing and reconciling of opposite and discordant qualities, or to Collingwood’s statement that the life of imagination is one in which all human beings participate, he would not, we venture to think, have agreed with the former that the poet’s mind subordinates art to nature; still less with the latter’s dialectical motion that the spiritual activity of Imagination is independent only in children and primitive peoples. On fancy, fantasy, and imagination, see nn. 40; 1797 (shaping phantasies); and cross-references.

1816 witnesseth]
Schmidt (1875): With an object, transitively, = to give testimony to, to attest. The context encourages suggestions of biblical overtones; see the examples given at OED, Witness, v. 1e. Deighton (ed. 1891): The singular as though we had with instead of and.

1817 something]
Holland (ed. 1994): Answering Theseus’s airy nothing.

1817 constancy]
Johnson (1755, 5), quoting 1814, 1816–18: Certainty; veracity; reality. Johnson (ed. 1765): Consistency; Stability; Certainty. OED (5), quoting this and one other instance, asserts: Certainty. Obs.; but wavers towards an association with Consist. Hallam (1839, 2:389) sees constancy in the sense of consistency as a Latinism. The Clarkes (ed. 1864): Used for consistency; in its combined senses of congruity and substantiality. Tiessen (1877, p. 5): logische Folgerichtigkeit (logical consistency in Ger.). Brooks (ed. 1979): (1) self-consistency (and therefore to be relied on); (2) able to stand all tests. Cf. OED, Consist, v. 1 and 2. Foakes (ed. 1984): The word constancy also means fidelity and refers to the newfound steadfastness of love confirmed in marriage.

1818 howsoeuer]
OED (3), quoting this instance: In any case, at any rate: = However 2.

1818 strange]
The Clarkes (ed. 1864): Sh. uses this word with forcible and extensive meaning. Here, and in the opening lines of the scene, he uses it for marvellous, out of nature, anomalous. See OED 10a.

1818 admirable]
Johnson (1755): To be admired; worthy of admiration; of power to excite wonder; always taken in a good sense, and applied either to persons or things. White (ed. 1858): Here used in its radical sense—to be wondered at—no approbation being implied. Rhoads (1985, p. 89): These words refer to the Aristotelian and Renaissance association of wonder with learning. Richmond (2002, p. 21): Conveys not simply an attitude of approval but a condition of overwhelming wonder.

1819–20 Enter…Helena]
Wilson (ed. 1924): We suspect that Shakespeare wrote Enter Louers and that the compositor or the prompter added the rest. Brooks (ed. 1979) adds: If so, the latter had annotated the foul papers used as copy for Q1, in readiness for transcribing the prompt-book from them. Tucker & Holden (ed. 1990): Hermia and Helena have nothing to say in Act V though both have been loquacious before. Perhaps now they have their man, there is nothing that they want to say, a much more interesting way of playing the last act.

1821–3 Here…hearts]
Bates (ed. 1895): Hippolyta is right, but Theseus does not take the trouble to discover it. When she falls to logic, he lightly changes the subject.

1822–33 Philostrate]
See n. 1792.

1822–3 fresh…loue]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Days in which love will have lost none of its first freshness. Brooks (ed. 1979): Cf. the bliss and melody at Palamon’s marriage [Chaucer, KnT 3007 ff.; see ].

1824 More]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Sc. [namely] joy.

1824–5 waite…bedde]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Be with you ever and everywhere.

1824 waite]
Schmidt (1875, 2c): To attend, to accompany.

1825 boorde]
OED (Board 6a): Table.

1826 maskes]
OED (Masque 2): A form of amateur entertainment, popular at Court and amongst the nobility in England during the latter part of the 16th c. and the first half of the 17th c., originally consisting of dancing and acting in dumb show, the performers being masked and habited in character; afterwards including dialogue (usually poetical) and song. For a history of the genre, see Welsford (1927). Cunliffe (1910, p. 78): The Athenian mechanicals describe their play as an interlude [273, 1956]…Shakspere does not describe any of the entertainments actually offered by Philostrate as a masque.

1828–33 To…Philostrate]
See textual n. For the substitution of Egeus for Philostrate, see .

1828–32 wear…hour]
Cf. the impatience of January for bedtime (MerT 1814–17; see ).

1829 Or]
Wilson (ed. 1924): The contracted or for our has been read as or. Wells & Taylor (1987, p. 285): Q misinterprets the abbreviation, and in mislining the passage capitalizes the word as the beginning of a verse line.

1829 after supper]
OED (After-supper), quoting this instance first: The time that intervened between supper (when this was at an earlier hour) and bed-time.…Also Obs. = rere-supper; Rere-supper: A supper (usually of a sumptuous nature) following upon the usual evening meal, and thus coming very late at night. Staunton (ed. 1857, p. 385) argues that rere-supper is intended, and was to the supper itself what the rere-banquet was to the dinner—a dessert.…Supper was usually served between five and six; and this…was frequently followed by a collation consisting of fruits and sweetmeats, called, in this country, the rere-supper; in Italy, Pocenio, from Latin Pocœnium. Thomas (1587, Pocoenium): A banquet after supper, a reare supper. Irving & Marshall (ed. 1888): Harrison mentions this supplementary meal in his Description of England [1577], Book ii. ch. vi.: Heretofore there hath beene much more time spent in eating and drinking than commonlie is in these daies, for whereas of old we had breakefasts in the forenoone, beverages, or nuntions after dinner, and thereto reare suppers generallie when it was time to go to rest (a toie brought into England by hardie Canutus). Foakes (ed. 1984) notes that this work is included in Holinshed 1587.

1830–81 Where…Ladies]
Brooks (ed. 1979): Philostrate…is Theseus’ Master of the Revels; Queen Elizabeth’s, at this period Sir Edmund Tilney, perused and saw in rehearsal…plays submitted by available companies, in order to select those to be acted before her. (E. K. Chambers, Elizabethan Stage, I. 223 f.). The process is described in the Revels Accounts, as transcribed by Feuillerat, 1908, pp. 326, 365 (see also Cunningham 1842, pp. 159, 188–9). In the festive period 1579–80 Tilney is paid for examynynge and Rehersinge of dyuers plaies and Choise makinge of x of them to be showen before her Maiestie at Christmas twelfetide Candelmas and Shrovetide and their sondry rehersalls afterwardes till to be presented before her Maiestie; also among the charges incurred for presenting entertainment to the Queen for the festive seasons from the end of October to February 1584–5 were those arising by meanes of attending making choyce, reforminge and altering of suche plaies Comodies maskes and inventions as were prepared sett furth and presented before her maiestie at the tymes aforesaid. There follows a list of the seven Playes shewes & devises presented, including A pastorall of phillyda and Choryn and The history of felix & philiomena, Bk.2 the latter presumably drawn from Montemayor’s Diana.

1830 Where…mirth]
Wells & Taylor (1987, p. 285): Perhaps [this line] should have been deleted, when the name was altered here and elsewhere.

1830 vsuall]
OED (3c), quoting this instance first: Of persons. Commonly employed in a particular capacity. The pronunciation is disyllabic. See Franz §66.

1831 Reuels]
See n. 388.

1832 a…hower]
Wilson (ed. 1924) takes the discrepancy with the three hours of 1828 as a sign of revision.

1832 torturing]
Andrews (ed. 1989): Two syllables.

1833 Call Philostrate]
See nn. 15, 1792, 1828–32.

1834 Theseus]
For the pronunciation see n. 26.

1835 abridgement]
OED (1b (?) fig.), quoting this instance only: A means of shortening or whiling away. Steevens (ed. 1773): Our author means dramatick performance, which crowds the events of years into as many hours. So in Hamlet, [2.2.420 (1465), 2.2.524–5 (1565)] he calls the players abridgments, abstracts, and brief chronicles of the time. Henley (apud Steevens, ed. 1793): Does not abridgement in the present instance, signify amusement to beguile the tediousness of the evening? or, in a word, pastime? Baker (ed. 1896): Diversion…; that which cuts time short, abridges it. Jonas (1918, p. 355): A dramatic performance of short duration…[or] a compendium of a larger work…I cannot find any contemporary use of this term, as it is employed by Shakespeare. Wright (1968, p. 48): If abridgement, maske, and music are three alternatives then abridgement is a short or shortened play; if, however, maske and music are instance of abridgements then it means something that makes the time seem shorter.

1837–8 How…delight]
Brooks (ed. 1979) compares Spenser, Shep. Cal. October 2–3, and adds: This eclogue may be part of the context of Shakespeare’s thought at [1850–2], since according to E.K. it is made in imitation of Theocritus his XVI. Idilion, wherein he reproved the Tyranne Hero of Syracuse for his nigardise towards Poetes…And the like also is in Mantuane. See 2149–50, for Theseus’ acknowledgement that the selected delight succeeded in beguiling the time.

1837 beguile…tyme]
Proverbial; Dent (T340.1).

1837 beguile]
Johnson (1755, 3): To deceive pleasantly; to amuse (OED, v. 5). See 2149.

1838 lazy tyme]
Wright (ed. 1877): Which moves so slowly, and in which we are idle.

1839–73 There is…heare it]
Wickham (1959, 1:110): The mechanicals’ show…, though likened by Theseus to a Royal Entry [1847–8], is a regular play performed by amateurs. As such it is scorned by the rude courtiers and may be better compared with an amateur performance today of some recent West End or Broadway success. Of the sports listed he says (1:234 n.): Shakespeare, very obligingly, gives us a thumbnail critical note on the interlude and its sources. Patterson (1989, p. 63): Theseus’s selection of the tragical mirth of the artisans’ play is carefully articulated as a rejection of learned, humanist entertainments. Wiles (1993, pp. 47–8) notes that Theseus is offered a musical recitation and a masque-like device, both these celebrating Theseus’ exploits, and a satire. He rejects two entertainments which flatter his own achievements, and rejects also an elite Spenserian view of art. He goes to the opposite extreme, and accepts a play written by uneducated craftsmen.…[His final choice] would serve to justify the action of an aristocrat who chose to employ common actors to perform a stage play at an elite wedding. Holland (ed. 1994): Of the four entertainments offered to Theseus, no fewer than three are based on Ovid’s Metamorphoses. Bruster (2000, p. 106) sees a correspondence between the three rejected entertainments and the categories of Theseus’s discourse on imagination: the Dionysian revellers are caught up in madness, the battle with the Centaurs is caused by illicit desire for the bride, and the poet is indicated by the thrice three Muses.

1839 briefe]
Johnson (1755, 1), quoting this instance: A writing of any kind. Steevens (ed. 1778): A short account or enumeration. See OED 6, quoting this instance first.

1839 sports]
See n. 1036.

1839 ripe]
See textual n. Theobald (ed. 1733): I have chosen to restore from one of the old Quarto’s printed in 1600, ripe, as the most proper Word here: ripe, signifying any thing ready for Use; rife, only the great Increase of any thing. See OED, Ripe, a. 7; Rife, a. 5. Thiselton (1903, p. 69): I cannot see how it can be established that rife is a manifest misprint for ripe.…Philostrate simply means there is plenty of choice, and ripe would be hardly a suitable term for the interlude subsequently produced.

1840 Make…first]
Johnson (ed. 1894): If Philostrate means to ask Theseus in what order the sports are to be represented, his question is misunderstood or disregarded. The Duke decides only to see one of them. Holland (ed. 1994), accepting F’s substitution of Egeus for Philostrate: F turns [Theseus’s speech] into an exchange between Lysander and Theseus…I have therefore included an SD for Egeus to hand the scroll with the list of titles to his new son-in-law Lysander, a gesture of reconciliation and a mark of the new family harmony.

1840 of which]
Blake (5.4.2 Of, p. 190) quotes this line to illustrate the use of of with relative pronouns and related words where it does not occur in [Present-day English]. See also Abbott §179.

1841–57 The.…discord]
See textual n. Theobald (ed. 1733): Here the sixteen Lines, that follow, from the Time of the first Folio Edition put out by the Players, have impertinently been divided, by two Verses alternately, betwixt Theseus and Lysander. But what has Lysander to do in the Affair? He is no Courtier of Theseus’s, but only an occasional Guest; and just come out of the Woods, so not likely to know what Sports were in Preparation. I have taken the old Quarto’s for my Guides, in regulating this Passage. Theseus asks after entertainment. Philostrate, who is his Master of the Revels, gives him in a List of what Sports are ready: upon which, Theseus reads the Titles of them out of the List, and then alternately makes his Remarks upon them. And this, I dare say, was the Poet’s own Design and Distribution. Thirlby (MS 1733–47), in sarcastic response to Theobald: You should have said that he was so amaz’d with what happen’d in the wood that he could not read for there is no need of knowing. Capell (1783, 2.3:115): This reading and commenting of two persons, alternately, has something aukward in it; and seems a change of the players, calculated for the ease of the actor who presented [Theseus]. Harness (ed. 1830): The division of the folio…is more princely and more dramatic. Collier (ed. 1842): The more natural course seems to be, for Theseus both to read and comment. Verplanck (ed. 1845): Perhaps the change into dialogue was an afterthought to add to the theatrical effect. Delius (ed. 1859) speculates that the alternation reflects the stage practice in Sh.’s theatre (Ger.). Wells (ed. 1967) cautiously echoes Delius, but later (1984, p. 61) imagines that the actor or Shakespeare himself may have made the change, hence An editor must choose whether to print the passage as it stood in Shakespeare’s manuscript before it was put into rehearsal, or to print it as it was acted by Shakespeare’s company; and eventually asserts (1991, p. 19): It seems undeniable that the change was made by Shakespeare’s own company, and probable that it had his approval.

1841–4 The battell…Hercules]
Knight (ed. 1839): Shakspere has given to Theseus the attributes of a real hero, amongst which modesty is included. He has attributed the glory to his kinsman Hercules. Wright (ed. 1877) notes that the battle of the centaurs and Lapiths is told by Nestor, who omits any mention of Hercules, in Met. 12:210–541. Cf. Golding’s Ovid. Verity (ed. 1893) suggests the battle here referred to is not their famous contest with the Lapithæ, but an incident in the life of Hercules, who, while in pursuit of the Erymanthian boar, was attacked by the Centaurs, but repulsed them (see Apollodorus 2.5.4 and Diodorus Siculus 4.12). Root (1903, pp. 45–6): The two battles were very early confused; he notes the similar confusion in Plutarch’s Life of Theseus (see ), which is further commented on by Porter & Clarke (ed. 1903). Tanner (ed. 1995, pp. cxxxvii–viii), assuming that the reference is to the Ovidian account: Theseus acquitted himself honourably, as befits a hero; but it would hardly be an auspicious event to rehearse on this wedding day, just while they are waiting to consummate their marriages. (The figure of a eunuch would be no more auspicious on such an occasion!)…[T]he figure of the half-animal, half-man (as opposed to the eunuch who is half-man, half nothing at all) has a general, if oblique, reference to [the] play as well. Specifically, it refers us back to Bottom who, but recently, was half-ass, half-man. He suggests a reminder of another Ovidian monster of double shape (see ), the Minotaur at the center of the labyrinth: Bottom, also referred to as a monster [1028, 1418], is somewhere at the centre of this play, which is also a maze, full of amazed people.

1842 Athenian Eunuche]
Cartwright (1862, p. 161) believes this is a reference to Henry Chettle.

1842 Eunuche]
Burney (in Steevens, ed. 1793): This seems to imply a more ancient practice of castration for the voice, than can be found in opera annals. Malone (in Boswell, ed. 1821): So, in Whetstone’s Heptameron, 1582: Which done, the eunuch, with a well-tuned voice to the lute, sung this following,—Care, away.

1842 Harpe?]
Halliwell (ed. 1856) avers that though sculptured representations of the harp are discovered on some of the Egyptian monuments…it is not found in any of the known relics of the ancient Greeks, so that the poet has probably unwittingly fallen into an anachronism. Porter & Clarke (ed. 1903): Shakespeare followed Golding’s Ovid, which speaks more than once of Orphey’s harpe in the story of Orpheus (eg. 11.19, 53; see ). Brooks (ed. 1979): Here and below [1846, 1850, 1854], Theseus reads the titles in a tone which enquires will this do? Hence Q1’s question marks.

1843 Weele none]
Mowat & Werstine (ed. 1993): I.e., we’ll have none. For the emphatic use of none, see n. 1194.

1844 kinsman Hercules]
Grey (1754, p. 71): Plutarch in the life of Theseus, observes, that he and Hercules were nearly related, being born of cousin germans (see ).

1845–6 The ryot…rage]
Grey (1754, 1.71): An allusion to the story of Orpheus, who was said to have been torn in pieces by women, at the time of sacrificing to Bacchus, because, out of sorrow for the loss of his wife; he abandoned the company of all other women. See Ovid Met. 11.1–60 (Golding, 11.1–57; see ).

1845 tipsie]
Johnson (1755), quoting this instance: Drunk; overpowered with excess of drink. See OED, Tipsy 1.

1845 Bachanals]
OED (B. n. 1), quoting this instance first: A devotee of Bacchus; a Bacchant.

1846 Thracian singer]
Cartwright (1862, p. 160) identifies the Thracian singer as Sh. himself. Root (1903, p. 93): The myth of Orpheus represents to Shakespeare, as to the ancients, the power of music and poetry as a civilizing and pacifying force. Wilson (ed. 1924): There is perhaps some topical allusion here. Cody (1969, p. 15) sees a reference to Poliziano’s Orfeo. Hunt (1992, p. 233): Renaissance writers believed that Orpheus’s name meant best voice. The Orpheus of Theseus’s court is the silenced singer.…And yet Orpheus is present in A Midsummer Night’s Dream in the best voice of Shakespeare, transporting auditors through the many sounds of his art.

1847 old]
The Clarkes (1879, p. 287): Before-used, stale, worn-out. Cf. OED, a. 3.

1847 deuise]
OED (Device 11), quoting this instance: Something devised or fancifully invented for dramatic representation; a mask played by private persons, or the like. arch. or Obs. See also n. 364–5.

1848 When…conquerer]
Chambers (ed. 1897): The conquest of Thebes by Theseus is told of in Chaucer’s Knightës Tale (KnT 985–1028; see ); he also cites Plutarch (see ), at p. 173 quoting the parallel passage.

1849–52 The thrise…ceremony]
Montrose (1996, p. 187): This conspicuous irrelevance has two operative points: the first, that its subject is the familiar complaint of Elizabethan cultural producers that they lack generous and enlightened patronage from the great; the second, that Duke Theseus—the play’s personification of courtly patronage—doesn’t want to hear about it.

1849–50 The thrise…beggery]
Among those believing that these lines allude to Spenser’s The Teares of the Muses (in Complaints, 1591) are Warburton (ed. 1747), Warton (in Johnson, ed. 1765, 8:2H7v), Rann (ed. 1787), Halliwell (1879 [1855], p. 22), Neil (ed. 1878), Grosart (1882–4, 1:91–3) and Bednarz (1983, p. 80). Griffith (1775, p. 20) points out that any such allusion is unnecessary, since this was not properly a complaint of that age, only; it has been so much the grievance of all times, that it has, long since, obtained into a proverb, As poor as a poet. Kranendonk (1932, p. 211) suggests it may be interpreted as a description, with a slight tinge of mockery in it, of what Spenser makes the Muses lament so much. Others expressing doubts about specific contemporary allusion include Knight (ed. 1839, 1:332–3), Halliwell (1841, pp. 59–67), Delius (ed. 1859), Verity (ed. 1893) and Brooks (ed. 1979), citing Tilley (M1316): From classical and Renaissance Latin authors, the sentiment is illustrated in Burton on The Miseries of Scholars (Anatomy of Melancholy, I.2.3.15).

1849–50 death…beggery]
Candidates for a possible topical allusion range from Spenser, first suggested by Steevens (ed. 1778) and discussed by Ritson (1783, p. 47), Malone (in Steevens ed. 1778, 1:287–8, and in ed. 1790), Knight (ed. 1839, 1:332–3), Halliwell (1879 [1855], pp. 22–3; ed. 1856, 5:6–7), Collier (ed. 1875) and Thiselton (1903, p. 69); Robert Greene, proposed by Knight (ed. 1839, 1:333), supported by Kenny (1864, p. 176), Neil (ed. 1878), Wilson (ed. 1924) and (tentatively) Halliwell (1879 [1855], pp. 20–1; ed. 1856, 5:7), Harrison (ed. 1937) and Brooks (ed. 1979); Marlowe, conjectured by Massey (1866, p. 151); Tasso, advanced by Chambers (1930, 1:360) and given some support by Brooks’s (ed. 1979, p. xxxviii) citation of a sidenote in Churchyard’s A Musicall Consort (1595, p. 42) which refers to Tasso’s death this last Aprill 1595; Peele, suggested by Martin (1935, p. 48); Kyd, mentioned by Greenblatt (ed. 1997).

1849 thrise three Muses]
Kranendonk (1932, p. 211) compares Spenser, FQ 1.10.54.8, the thrise three learned Ladies. Bradbrook (1955, pp. 49, 222) notes that the Muses nine appear in [Edwards’s Damon and Pithias] mourning for the imminent death of Pithias (1571, G3v-G4r; ed. D. Jerry White, 1980, pp. 88–9). Root (1903, p. 86): Usually the word Muse is merely equivalent to poetic talent.

1851 Satire]
Holzknecht & McClure (ed. 1937): I.e., directed against a nobility that neglected its duty of patronage of the arts.

1851 criticall]
Steevens (ed. 1773), comparing Oth. 2.1.119 (892): Here means criticizing, censuring. Rann (ed. 1787): Censorious. See OED 1, quoting this instance first. Spencer (1964, p. 159): It is not of much significance that the word critical is first recorded in Shakespeare.…Of more interest, it seems, is the fact that Shakespeare usually put his ideas on literature into a comic or ironic context or situation.

1852 sorting with]
OED (v.1 18a), quoting this instance first: To suit, fit, or agree; to be in harmony. Now arch.

1852 ceremony]
Furness (ed. 1895): This example may be added to the many collected by Walker [1860, 2.73–6] of the trisyllabic pronunciation of ceremony. See Cercignani (1981, p. 280).

1853–4 A…mirth]
See n. 279–80. Stratton (ed. 1920): Note that the title is somewhat changed.

1853 tedious]
Delius (ed. 1859) believes the Mechanicals have misunderstood tedious as amusing (Ger.).

1853 tedious brief]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Not necessarily a contradiction in terms, as Philostrate afterwards explains, though here so taken by Theseus. Irving & Marshall (ed. 1888): Though, as a rule, opposites, are not necessarily so. For it is quite possible to be brief and yet to be tedious, in the sense of being wearisome. Atherton (1951, p. 337) compares the Vulgate: Exiguum et cum taedio (Wisdom II, 1). Brief and tedious, would seem to be the best translation. Forey (1998, p. 327) compares Golding’s Ovid Epistle, 301, were labor infinite and tediousnesse not small (see ) and Preface, 78, will not tedious be. She adds: The word brief has also already appeared in Golding’s Epistle when Golding decides breefly to conclude (Epistle, 607), a claim to brevity which, after so extended an epistle, already carries unintended humour. Cf. Ham. 2.2.90–92 (1117–19) on brevity and tediousness.

1855–7 Merry…discord]
For lineation, see textual n. Garrison (1994, p. 152): [Theseus] is struck…by the quintessential device of the Pyramus and Thisbe tradition—paradox.…[He] has already caught on to a major artifice of the play, and by creating some paradoxes of his own, he foreshadows the kind of comments he and others will make when the play is presented.

1855–6 That…snow]
Clark & Wright (ed. 1864) mark this line with an obelus (†) as corrupt: see n. 632.

1856 And…snow]
Caldecott (MS 1813–33): I.e. snow of as strange a quality, as the ice, just described, continuing his enumeration of an union of opposites. Halliwell (ed. 1856): In other words, ice and snow, wonderous hot and wonderous strange; or hot ice, and strange snow as wonderful. This kind of metaphor was common, and was probably derived from Proverbs, xxvi.1, as snow in summer, &c.…A variety of unnecessary emendations of this line have been offered. The Clarkes (ed. 1864): Strange, as Shakespeare occasionally uses it (in the sense of anomalous, unnatural, prodigious), presents sufficient image of contrast in itself. Verity (ed. 1893): The existing text is supported by the fact that Shakespeare twice uses the phrase wondrous strange, viz. in [3H6 2.1.33 (685) and Ham. 1.5.164 (861)]. Heninger (1953, p. 483), calling on Renaissance meteorological theory: The formation of snow…depended upon the presence of both cold and heat, and in Theseus’ mind it is a self-contained paradox. Wondrous and strange are only adjectives describing this prodigy of nature, and are not syntactical correspondents to hot in the phrase hot ice. See also the poet that Elyot recommends for the education of children of noblemen (The Boke Named the Gouernour ed. H. H. S. Croft, 1883, rpt. 1967, 1:69): Yet—marvellous to tell—the mountain-top is white and harbours ice and flame side by side. The burning peaks are stiff with perpetual frost, eternal winter lies on the lofty summit, and hot snow is hidden beneath black ashes (Silius Italicus, Punica 14.66–9, tr. J. D. Duff). Cf. also Colonna’s Poliphilus: I knewe not vppon the suddaine what [to do], but to offer her my vnworthy and vnfit hande; Which when it was streined in hers, me thought that it was in hot snowe and curded milke (Hypnerotomachia, Tr. R. D[allington], 1592, X4v).
Many editors and commentators have objected to this phrase on the grounds that it does not provide a satisfactorily antithetical parallel to hot Ise, and (believing 1855–7 to be verse) because it appears to be metrically a syllable short. Some address the latter question by reading wõdrous as three syllables: e.g. Steevens (ed. 1773). Some alter strange to a two-syllable word: e.g. Hanmer (ed. 1744), scorching; Collier (ed. 1853), seething. Others add a word after strange: e.g. Upton (1748, p. 205), black. For the full range of emendations and conjectures, from modest to drastic, see textual n. and conj. . For representative commentary, see Steevens (ed. 1773), Malone (ed. 1790), Seymour (1805 1:50–1), Bailey (1862, 1:196–200), Elze (1867, p. 538), Neil (ed. 1878), Leo (1880, p. 708; 1885, pp. 12–14), Joicey (1893, p. 102), Cuningham (ed. 1905), Hart (1905, p. 425), Wells & Taylor (1987, p. 285).

1856 wõdrous]
Franz §384: In a strange degree. Blake (3.3.3.6 (a)): Best understood as an intensifier, since it contrasts with hot ice.

1856–7 How…discord]
West (1865, p. 22) compares Sidney’s Arcadia: so perfect agreement in so mortall disagreement, like a music made of cunning discords (ed. Evans, 1977, p. 497). Guilhamet (1975, pp. 258–60) finds this question central to his interpretation of the play as a whole, and examines classical and Renaissance sources of the theme of discordia concors; citing as the most obvious for the Renaissance eye…Ovid’s myth of creation (Met. 1.432–3); cf. Golding 1.513–18, see . Foakes (ed. 1984) and Andrews (ed. 1993) also draw attention to the question’s larger significance. Blaise (2002, p. 82) finds the line emblematic of all Theseus’s actions in the play, because he seeks to smooth over faults and find harmony in chaos, as with the cries of the hunting dogs (1630–47) (Fr.). Dent (D351.1) treats it as proverbial; see also n. 1639.

1858–61 A…Play]
Seymour (1805, 1:51): The first four lines of this speech end, alternately, with the words, long, and play. They could not, surely, be meant as rhymes.

1858 some…long]
Scott (ed. 1898): Possibly a familiar way of saying, very short. Some = about. Round numbers have ever been used to denote vague quantities or periods. Black (1965, p. 56): Philostrate’s understatement is intentional.

1860 by…long]
Scott (ed. 1898): By its whole length, as it was not worth the writing and is entirely valueless.

1862 fitted]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Given a part suitable to his capacity. See n. 328.

1863–7 And…shed]
For the mislineation, see textual note and .

1864–5 Which…water]
Wright (ed. 1877): We must supply it as the nominative; that is, the seeing of the play rehearsed. For this ellipsis see Abbott §399. Page (ed. 1897): Which is made to do double duty; as object to rehearsed and subject to made. The sentence may be made regular by supplying it before either of these verbs.

1864–5 when…Rehearst]
Verity (ed. 1893): When did Philostrate see the play? Not at the rehearsal (which was the first) in Act III., nor after the rehearsal, for who was there to take the part of Pyramus?…Perhaps the play was preferred [1783] before Philostrate saw it rehearsed (he may have been attracted by the title), and there was a final rehearsal, to which he here alludes, after the actors, in accordance with Bottom’s instructions [1782], had met at the palace. Kittredge (ed. 1939): No time-analysis affords an answer. The problem, however, would never occur to an audience. Holland (ed. 1994), citing Chambers (1923, 1:223–4): Plays offered for performance at court were performed (rehearsed or recited according to the accounts) before the Master of the Revels so that he could make a choice of an appropriate play for performance. The chosen play would be further rehearsed so that it could be censored for court performance. Stern (2000, p. 51) notes that the office Philostrate fills is critically portrayed.…He uses his rehearsal-knowledge to do down the play before performance, even though he had initially selected the actors and set them to the trouble of preparing. Cf. 272–5. See nn. 359–63, 1830–81.

1866 passion]
Doran (ed. 1959): Usually explained as passionate outburst [OED, 6d], but probably ironic, i.e. the grief of loud laughter, in keeping with tragical mirth. See OED 6a, c; see also nn. 1097, 2086.

1868–9 What…here]
Furness (ed. 1895): Schmidt [1881, p. 7] finds in these lines two difficulties which could not have been in the original MS. The first is the incomplete verse of [1868], and the second is the blunt answer which, so he says, no Englishman would ever think of giving to a prince. He, therefore, thus emends: What are they that do play’t? Hard-handed men, | My noble Lord (or My gracious Duke) that work in Athens here. Cuningham (ed. 1905) rejects what he calls Schmidt’s sneer and supplies Philostrate at the end of 1868. See textual n., and conj. .

1868 What]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Of what kind; less definite than who.

1869–78 Hard…seruice]
Blakeway (in Boswell, ed. 1821): In 1575, when Queen Elizabeth visited Kenilworth, and was entertained with every sport which either the refinement or rusticity of the times could furnish, among those of the latter kind,—certain good harted men of Coventree understanding how carefull his honour [the Earl of Leicester] waz that by all pleazaunt recreations her Highnes might best fynd her self well com—made petition that they mought renue their old storial shew (see Robert Laneham, A Letter, 1575, Ev).

1869 Hard handed]
Johnson (1755), quoting this instance: Coarse; mechanick; one that has hands hard with labour (OED 1, quoting this instance and one other from 1883). Thirlby (MS 1725–33) compares JC 4.3.74 (2051) the hard hands of Peazants, and cites (MS 1733–47) W. Thomas (1550, Calloso): Harde, as the hande woaren with labour.
