Halliwell (ed. 1856) quotes Thomas Dekker’s The Wonderfull Yeare. 1603: What Mechanicall hardhanded Vulcanist (seeing the dice of Fortune run so sweetly, and resoluing to strike whilst the iron was hote) but perswaded himselfe to bee Maister or head Warden of the company ere halfe a yeare went about? (1603, C2v).

1869 Hard…men]
Gill (ed. 1981): Labourers (not gentlemen).

1870 Which]
Seymour (1805, 1:51): The neuter relative, which, to men, was common anciently,—we find it frequently in the translation of the Scriptures.

1871 toyled]
Schmidt (1875, 2): To put to pains and labour, to strain. Rolfe (ed. 1877): Exerted, strained.

1871 vnbreathed]
Johnson (1755), quoting this instance: Not exercised. Steevens (ed. 1773): Unexercised, unpractised (OED, ppl. a.1). Rann (ed. 1787): Untried, inexperienced. Sieveking (1916, 2:418) sees it as a metaphor from horsemanship.

1872 against]
See n. 134. Richmond (2002, p. 22) suggests that there may be a concealed pun in the potential double meaning of opposed or contrary intention (see OED 10), since the interlude ridicules love.

1872 nuptiall]
See n. 134.

1873–5 And…world]
For lineation, see textual n. and .

1873 And]
See Blake 5.3.1 (a)(i) for and used in an adversative sense but, however.

1873 heare it]
Andrews (ed. 1989): Plays were normally heard rather than seen in the Elizabethan theatre, at least by cultivated patrons, a distinction preserved in the word audience (as opposed to spectators, the usual term for viewers at sporting events). Cf. nn. 294, 892.

1874 Phi.]
The only SP for Philostrate instead of Egeus that survives in F. See n. 1792.

1874 not for you]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Not fitted for one of your greatness.

1875 And…world]
Bruster (2000, p. 106): The phrasing here resonates with Plautus’s quod nusquam gentiumst, that which is nowhere on earth [Pseudolus 402]. To Philostrate, the nothingness of the Mechanicals’ play disqualifies it from serious consideration. But both dramas require that the impossible become real—indeed, that a poeta figure accomplish it through this very nothingness. See also n. 1794–1813. Lagae (2002, pp. 66–74) points to the emphasis here given to nothing by repetition, position, and contrast with world, and proceeds to examine the many occurrences of the word and its variants, especially in the final act, concluding that often in Sh. what seems insubstantial is a sign of the essential (Fr.). See also n. 1885–6.

1875 nothing, nothing]
See n. 1885–6.

1875 in the world]
The Clarkes (1879, pp. 403–4): Whatever. On the common use of this phrase as an intensifier after a negator, see Blake 6.2.3.3. See also OED, World, n. 20a (a).

1876–8 Vnlesse…seruice]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Unless the fact that they have desired to please you, and with that desire have laboured to the utmost in getting up their parts, will afford you amusement in spite of their shortcomings; properly speaking, it is not the intents that are Extremely stretch’d, but their labour due to those intents. The construction that so puzzled Johnson (ed. 1765) that he suspected a line lost, and led Douce (1807, 1:196) to propose printing 1876 as a parenthesis and Gould (1884, p. 16) to believe 1876 and 1877 transposed, is explained by Singer (ed. 1856) who points out the double sense of entents: Used both for endeavours, and the subject of the endeavours—the play,—which are respectively stretched to the utmost, and painfully conned to do the Duke service. Cf. OED (Intent 3): Intent or assiduous effort, endeavour; and (1b): Design, plan, scheme.

1876–8 entents…seruice]
Rann (ed. 1787): Entertainment, which in compliment to you, they have rack’d their brains to devise.

1877 stretcht]
Kittredge (ed. 1939): This suggests the torture of the rack. Parker (1992, pp. 383–4 n.) finds double meanings in dilation or stretching and seruice: There is a sense of the sexualized language of class difference as the metaphor of sexual service extending to all servants; see also Parker (1996, pp. 342–3).

1877 cond]
See n. 361.

1877 cruell]
Halliwell (ed. 1856) argues amusingly for this being used as an intensifier, in the same way as monstrous in vulgar parlance (see n. 314, and OED, Cruel, a. 5), but adds: The word cruel, in the text, may merely imply that the pain inflicted by the getting up their parts, was a self-imposed cruelty perpetrated on themselves.

1879–80 I will…it]
For the lineation, see textual n. and .

1879–80 For…it]
Gildon (1710, 7:318): The Reflection of Theseus on the Diversion offer’d by the Clowns is just. Dodd (1752, 1.88), anticipated in the reference by Thirlby (MS 1725–33): He has a thought of the same kind whimsically exprest, in [LLL 5.2.516–18 (2460–2)]. Gentleman (ed. 1774): The condescension here manifested by Theseus, is an excellent lesson to greatness, of kind humility; persons in high stations should ever graciously receive the most trifling offices of simple loyalty or attachment; the intention, to every generous mind, gives or takes away real value. Theseus in his speech sensibly vindicates his acceptance of an unpolished attempt to please. Steevens (ed. 1785): Ben Jonson in Cynthia’s Revels has employed this sentiment of humanity on the same occasion when Cynthia is preparing to see a masque: Nothing which duty and desire to please, Bears written on the forehead comes amiss [5.6.79–80; Works, ed. Herford & Simpson, 4:163]. Steevens (ed. 1793, 12:493) parallels with Ant. 2.5.8–9 (1034–6): And when good will is shewed, Though’t come to short The Actor may pleade pardon; see Spevack, ed. Ant. 1990, p. 111, n. 1034–6. Theobald (1909, pp. 312–13): The sentiment is a noble one, and corresponds with a sentence in the address of Pliny to Vespasian; in Philemon Holland’s translation: No marvell is it, if those that doe their dutie unto you…come with great respect and reverence.…And yet the gods reiect not the humble praiers of poore countrey peasants…who offer nothing but milke unto them: and such as have no Incense, find grace and favour manie times with the oblation of a plaine cake made onely of meale and salt; and never was anie man blamed yet for his devotion to the gods, so he offered according to his abilitie, were the thing never so simple (Naturall Historie, 1601, ¶iir).

1880 simplenesse and duety]
Yachnin (1992, p. 56): Theseus is presented as unaware that the mechanicals’ sport is undertaken for profit as well as out of simpleness and duty. Cf. 1764–71.

1880 simplenesse]
OED (1), quoting this instance: Absence of pride or pretentiousness; plain or unassuming disposition or manners. Mowat & Werstine (ed. 1993): Sincerity; lack of sophistication.

1881 take…Ladies]
Callaghan (2000, pp. 146–60) discusses the staging, and the dramatic and cultural implications of Theseus’s injunction.

1882–7 I loue…mistake]
Bates (ed. 1895): Might it have been courteous in mighty Theseus to let Hippolyta choose her own bridal masque?

1882–3 I loue…perishing]
Griffith (1775, p. 21): Hippolita…makes the same objection [as Philostrate], but from a motive of humanity, only. Deighton (ed. 1891): It is no pleasant sight to me to see poor wretches labouring under a task too heavy for them, and those who from a feeling of duty offer their services failing in their attempt to acquit themselves well; Hippolyta is unwilling that the poor rustics should be allowed to play before her and break down in the attempt.

1882 wretchednesse]
Scott (ed. 1898): This seems to be Hippolyta’s conception of what Theseus calls simpleness. It indicates poverty, or low birth. Johnson (ed. 1894): Poor, humble people. Wells (ed. 1967): The lowly in both social position and intellect.

1882 orecharged]
OED (Overcharge, v. hence ppl. a.): Overburdened, oppressed.

1883–4 perishing…thing]
Ness (1941, p. 130) considers this unintentional rhyme.

1885–6 nothing…nothing]
Goddard (1951, p. 77): That four-times reiterated nothing is Shakespeare’s way of sending our mind’s back to the airy nothing of Theseus’ earlier speech which, he then said and now proves, imagination has power to turn into something actual.

1885–7 kinde…mistake]
Delius (ed. 1859) notes the word play, kind = Art, and kind = friendly; and also to take = receive or accept, and to mistake = to do something wrong, to make a mistake (Ger.). But Schmidt (1875, p. 1421) lists kinde…kinder among involuntary obscurities caused by the confusion of different significations of the same word. Black (1965, p. 57), including the repetition of nothing in 1885–6: Theseus’ high spirits are shown in word play.

1885 kinde]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Way, i.e. of acting.

1886–1902 The…capacity]
Gildon (1710, 7:318): [Theseus’s] Reflections on Duty and Respect are fine. Pope (ed. 1725, 6:4I2v) lists the passage among notable Thoughts, or Sentiments as Duty express’d with simplicity acceptable. Recently, distaste for the class distinctions of early modern society has engendered unsympathetic readings. Palmer (1992, p. 67): We should not be surprised, [Theseus] suggests, that the lower orders attempt civil speech genres that fail in the presence of true civility. The aristocratic host keeps them in their place and treats them with kindness by taking what they mistake, by picking welcomes from what the lower orders cannot express. Consequently, Theseus’s kindness, his ruler’s philosophy of hosting, implies a strategy of cultural conquest. Wikander (1993, p. 23): The rationalist Theseus seems to be suggesting that a charitable act of the imagination, a faculty he deplores, can read the love that motivates a stumbling speech and pick a welcome from silence.…But throughout Theseus’s endorsement of this principle we sense the hostility to imagination that animated his repudiation of lunatics, lovers, and poets.…Theseus’s condescension, like the word condescension itself, is double-edged.…[It] is coupled with contemptuous dismissal. Montrose (1996, p. 188): The other form taken by Theseus’s opinions concerning the drama is that it should serve as a gratifying homage to princely power, one that simultaneously provides an occasion for the exercise of royal magnanimity:…Theseus’s self-gratifying sense of his subjects’ love and simplicity is counterpointed by those subjects’ own voicings of the material interests and personal fantasies that have motivated their desire to perform before their lord.…What the ruler chooses to take as a spontaneous act of homage is the subject’s calculated means to acquire a pension. Clayton (1999, p. 68) appears deliberately to oppose such readings: The speech seems in earnest for both Shakespeare and Theseus.…It is in earnest for good reason, its theme noblesse oblige, which might not be necessary in a genuinely classless society of the sort the world has yet to see. Where there are inequalities, their effects are substantially ameliorated when the more fortunate—or powerful—show care for the less. And where there is personal contact between power and vulnerability, kindness finds a way to level differences to some degree. The kinder we episode speaks well for Theseus, and it appears to speak also for the play.

1886 The…nothing]
Seymour (1805, 1:52): This sentiment occurs, on a similar occasion, in Hamlet, the less they deserve, the more merit is in your bounty [Ham. 2.2.531–3 (1572–3)]. Walter (ed. 1964): Theseus playfully quibbles on Hippolyta’s nothing and kind.

1887 Our…mistake]
Steevens (ed. 1773): Voltaire says something like this of Louis XIV. who took a pleasure in seeing his courtiers in confusion when they spoke to him. Hailes (1786, p. 361): Mr. Steevens has evidently misunderstood this passage. He assigns to Theseus a malignant in place of a humane sentiment. [Quotes 1887] We will accept with pleasure even their blundering attempt.

1888–9 And…merit]
Theobald (ed. 1733), inserting willing after poore, and beginning a second verse line with Noble: What Ears have these poetical Editors, to palm this first Line upon us as a Verse of Shakespeare? ’Tis certain, an Epithet had slipt out, and I have ventur’d to restore such a one as the Sense may dispense with; and which makes the two Verses flowing and perfect. The ensuing nearly 300-year-long spate of emendations and conjectures is documented in the textual n. and conj. . Johnson (ed. 1765) had difficulty with the sense as well as with the metre, but was answered by Kenrick (1765, pp. 20–1): Nothing, however, I think, can be more plain, or more consistent with truth, than the passage as it now stands.…That is, not as an act of ability, though not of merit, as Dr. Johnson says; but as an act of merit, though not of ability: thus in consequence of its inability, taking the will for the deed; viz. accepting the best in its might to do, for the best that might be done. Some commentators followed Steevens (MS 1773–8) in taking might not as a noun but as an elliptical expression for what might have been. Malone (ed. 1790) draws on both Johnson and Steevens: And what dutifulness tries to perform without ability, regardful generosity receives with complacency, estimating it not by the actual merit of the performance, but by what it might have been, were the abilities of the performers equal to their zeal. Becket (1815, 1.278) reads might as noun but seeks to answer Johnson’s difficulty by glossing as not ability but endeavour; desirous of doing; assiduous, laboring at.
Other paraphrases and interpretations of the passage include: Hunter (ed. 1870): Takes it according to the degree of effort that is made, not according to the degree of excellence achieved. Moberly (ed. 1881): A considerate spirit takes it not by the value of what they accomplish, but by its proportion to their ability. Deighton (ed. 1891): And in a case where poor creatures, anxious to show their duty, fail in their efforts, a generous mind accepts those efforts, taking into consideration their capacity as performers rather than the merit of their performance. Brooks (ed. 1979): The noble way of looking at it…accepts it, not at its actual value, but with respect to the power, the resources of those who offer it. So [Matt 12.42] is the widow’s mite assessed; and Eliza’s shilling, in Pygmalion, as the biggest offer Higgins ever had. The will for the deed is proverbial [Tilley (W393)]; he also cites Tilley (S730): That is well spoken (There is nothing ill said) that is well taken. Clayton (1999, p. 69): Might is used to mean both power (might is more powerful than merit) and potentiality (what they might do). Hassel (2005, pp. 212–15, MERIT, MIGHT) considers the passage in the context of Sh.’s playful awareness (p. 212) of the Reformation debate over salvation by works or grace: (p. 214) Theseus has such grace that he Takes it in might not merit.…Because his might literally means what they intended in contrast to what they actually executed, it can suggest in Pauline terms the would that they do not (Romans 7.19). It is this inevitability that requires Luther’s theology of grace.

1888–9 respect]
Johnson (1755): 1. Regard; attention (OED, n. 13a).…7. Manner of treating others. See also n. 147 (in respect of).

1889 Takes…merit]
Clark & Wright (ed. 1864) mark this line with an obelus (†) as corrupt, but the mark was deleted from the 1887 ed.: see n. 632. Brooks (ed. 1979): The short line closes the preceding movement of Theseus’ speech, so that he can modulate into the key of his reminiscence, which…is also a tribute to Elizabeth’s behaviour on like occasions.

1890–1902 Where…capacity]
Irving & Marshall (ed. 1888): These lines may have been suggested by some of the addresses received by Queen Elizabeth in her various progresses. They contain an excellent hint to princes who suffer under a plethora of such addresses nowadays, and to the various officials who have to make such addresses. Holland (1933, p. 9) first refers to the episode described by Wilson (1980, p. 39): That Elizabeth was fully aware of this aspect [of progresses as public relations exercises] is shown by the care she took to exercise her enormous personal charm on these occasions, as when she calmed the Recorder of Warwick in 1572: 'she called Mr. Aglionby to her, and offered her hand to him to kiss, withall smyling, said, "Come hither, little Recorder. It was told me that you would be afraid to look upon me, or to speak boldly; but you were not so afraid of me as I was of youe; and I now thank you for putting me in mynd of my duety, and that should be in me"' (Nichols, 1823, I.315–16). Theseus’s practice [here] seems based on that of Elizabeth. Harrison (ed. 1937): Queen Elizabeth had often to endure long orations whenever she went on progress. Her visit to Oxford in 1592 was fairly recent when the play was written. Various academic exercises were performed before her. At one of these stood up one Mr. Sydney (who was placed in the lowest form) being thereunto required by the Proctors. He forgat his congés, used no speech at all to Her Majesty, but dealt with the answerer as though Her Majesty had not been there. (Nichol’s Progresses of Queen Elizabeth). The general complaint however was that the Oxford scholars once on their feet could not stop. Venezky (1951, pp. 63 ff.) describes a number of such orations in both literature and life, remarking (pp. 69–70) that Theseus’s behavior reflects Elizabeth’s own customary graciousness to the orators on such public occasions; of particular interest is the story of the schoolmaster Stephen Limbert of Norwich, who in 1578 was too nervous to be able deliver his welcome address until encouraged by the Queen, and whose (undelivered) farewell oration praises the hero Theseus as an example of friendly benevolence. Brown (1966, pp. 55–6) comments that in the Nine Worthies episode in LLL 5.2.484–715 (2425–670), Sh. uses varying discrepancies between text and subtext, between attempted and achieved performance, for comic effect:…In the play of Pyramus and Thisbe, the device is repeated in a more elaborate way, and, in preparation, Theseus explains that he values subtext above text, tongue-tied modesty of fearful duty above saucy and audacious eloquence:…Clearly Shakespeare was aware of more than comic implications of a failure to sustain a part by an appropriate impression of emotional and physical truth. Elam (1984, p. 216): What [Theseus] upholds is not so much heartfelt verbal expression as artless non-expression, opposing it to facile oratorical fluency:…Theseus’s valorization of stammering ineloquence as an index of sincerity effectively reverses the ideological force of the expressivity principle as expounded by the rhetorical enthusiasts of the age, for whom eloquence represented an absolute moral and social, indeed quasi-religious, value.

1890–5 Clerkes…off]
Malone (1780, 1:120): So, in Pericles:…Deep clerks she dumbs [5.Chor.5; Q1609].

1890 Where…come]
Kittredge (ed. 1939): In different places that I have visited.

1890 Clerkes]
Wright (ed. 1877): Scholars, learned men; learning having been at one time almost confined to the clergy.

1891 premeditated]
OED (ppl. a. 1), quoting this instance first: Composed beforehand; previously planned.

1892 Where]
Verity (ed. 1893): On which occasions. See Franz §347 Anm. 2.

1892 shiuer]
OED (v.2 1): Tremble with fear.

1893 periods]
Malone (1780, 1:120): Used in the sense of full stops.

1894 Throttle…feares]
Deighton (ed. 1891): In their nervous excitement choke the utterance of those sentences which they had spent so much pains in committing to memory.

1894 Throttle]
Johnson (1755), quoting this instance: To choak; to suffocate; to kill by stopping the breath.

1894 practiz’d accent]
Page (ed. 1897): Language well rehearsed beforehand. See OED, Practised, ppl. a. 2, quoting this instance first.

1895 dumbly]
OED (adv.): Speechlessly, mutely.

1895 haue broke]
White (ed. 1858): It is…probable that they is understood; for such license was common in Shakespeare’s day, or rather, it was hardly license then. See Abbott §399.

1896 paying]
OED (v.1 8), quoting this instance first: To render, bestow (something considered as due).

1897 pickt]
Johnson (1755, 1), quoting this instance: To cull; to chuse; to select; to glean; to gather here and there. OED (v.1 5b. fig.), quoting this instance: To deduce, make out. Page (ed. 1897): Pluck’d, as a flower; cf. OED, v.1 5a.

1898–1902 And…capacity]
Andrews (ed. 1993): No doubt Theseus means what he says. But as soon as the play begins, he and his companions appear to forget about such noble graciousness.

1898–1900 And…eloquence]
Deighton (ed. 1891): And in the nervous bashfulness which these loyal creatures have betrayed, I have discovered as much real welcome as in the glib, fluent speech of those who were hindered by no scruples of diffidence.

1898 modesty]
Holland (ed. 1994): Deference; cf. OED 2c. Obs.

1898 fearefull duty]
Delius (ed. 1859) draws attention to the paradox of devotion being prevented by fear from expressing itself aloud, and to the similar contrast between a modest hanging back and the obtrusive behavior indicated by saucy (Ger.). Baker (ed. 1896): The duty that felt fear, awe, of the sovereign.

1899 rattling]
OED (ppl. a. 2a), quoting this instance: Characterized by a rapid flow of words or liveliness of manner.

1901–2 Loue…most]
Cf. Tilley (L165): Whom we love best to them we can say least. Dobbs ([1948], p. 24) sees in Theseus’ insistence on the value of sincerity the influence of the view of love propounded by Socrates in Phaedrus. Richmond (2000, p. 116) detects an echo of the Beatitudes (Matt. 5.3–11).

1901 Loue…simplicity]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Loving and simple-minded creatures, the abstract for the concrete.

1901 Loue]
Delius (ed. 1859): The affection of the subjects for their ruler (Ger.).

1901 tong-tide simplicity]
Quoted Tilley (T416) To be tongue-tied, noting Frequent elsewhere in Shakespeare. Palmer (1946, p. 106) comments on the Duke’s commendation of tongue-tied simplicity when he is about to encounter Bottom, who was never at a loss for the right word as among the accidental felicities of the play. Cf. 1020.

1902 In…most]
The Clarkes (1879, p. 24) call attention to the expressive use of antithesis. Scott (ed. 1898): The expression is epigrammatic. Cf. the Latin phrase multum in parvo. Kittredge (ed. 1939): In saying least. The less they are able to say, the more they really express.

1902 to my capacity]
Thirlby (MS 1725–33): I suppose for thinking, i.e. in my judgment. Thirlby (MS 1747–53): Understanding is the meaning. Capacity may sometimes be used for it. Franz (§528): So far as I am able to understand. See OED Capacity, 4.

1903 Philost.]
See n. 1792.

1903 So…Grace]
OED (Please, v. 3): Formerly usual in deferential phrases of address; see 3c for examples. See also Blake 4.4.1 (e), referring to its use as a discourse marker.

1903 Prologue]
Verity (ed. 1893): The speaker of the prologue; he compares Cotgrave (1611, Avant-joüeur): A Prologue, he that beginneth, or playeth before, the game, Enterlude, or Commedie. See OED, n. 2.

1903 addrest]
Steevens (ed. 1773): That is, ready. See OED, Address, v. 4a. Obs. Halliwell (ed. 1856) cites Thomas Heywood’s The Four Prentises of London: The stage direction is enter three in blacke clokes at three doores, and the first gives the following description of his character,—What meane you, my maisters, to appeare thus before your times? Doe you not know that I am the Prologue? Do you not see this long black velvet cloke upon my backe? Have you not sounded thrice? Do I not looke pale as fearing to bee out in my speech? Nay, have I not all the signes of a Prologue about me? (1615, A4r).

1904 Duk.]
This is the first change in the SP from the normal The. SPs revert to The. at 1916, 1922, 1952, 1966, 1970, 1984, then become Duk. or Duke for the rest of the scene. Fleay (1886, pp. 182–3) believes these variations to indicate alteration (see n. 1509–50). Brooks (ed. 1979, p. xxiii) believes that such variations indicate foul-paper copy, and asks: Did Shakespeare, beginning the Burlesque, foresee possible confusion between The. or Thes, and This., revert to his normal prefix while no confusion could arise, and only change again to Duk.…when This. had started to occur? However, Kennedy (1998, pp. 182–6) demonstrates that the variation occurs because of type shortage. See also .

1904 [Flor. Trum.]]
See textual n. Steevens (ed. 1778): It appears from the Guls Hornbook, by [Thomas Dekker], 1609, that the prologue was anciently usher’d in by trumpets: Present not yourself on the stage (especially at a new play) untill the quaking prologue hath (by rubbing) got cullor into his cheekes, and is ready to give the trumpets their Cue that hees upon point to enter [E3v]. The entry for trumpet in Dessen & Thomson (1999, pp. 237–8) indicates that most SDs for the sound or a flourish of trumpets occur when important figures enter and/or for military business. Trumpets in Ham. Q2 (hoboyes in F1) introduce the dumbshow for The Mousetrap (Ham. 3.2.135+1 [1990]), but possibly their appearance here and at 1924 serves to increase the comic effect of the incongruously tragic interlude that follows.

1905 Prologue]
Staunton (ed. 1857): The prologue was evidently spoken by Peter Quince, as in the folio, after the direction, Enter the Prologue, is Quince. White (ed. 1858): Peter Quince is plainly the author of this interlude; and as he had no part in the performance, the assignment of the Prologue to him is highly appropriate. Quoting J. Payne Collier: In the earlier period of our drama the prologue-speaker was either the author in person or his representative (The History of Dramatic Literature, 3 vols, 2nd ed. 1879, 3:245), Furness (ed. 1895) avers: In the light of this statement by Collier, the appearance here in the Folio of Quince is noteworthy as an indication that the Duke was to accept Quince as the author of the play. Porter & Clarke (ed. 1903): Is [Quince’s] way of delivering his introduction a gibe at the usual failure of poets to deliver the best sense of their own work, the histrionic faculty being a distinct ability? Phillips (ed. 1969): Should we look upon Quince as the author of the interlude (his authorship perhaps lying in the corrupting of a more scholarly work)? And his trials with his cast as a parody of a dramatist’s sufferings in hearing his words ill-treated? Shakespeare must have known all about that. See also n. 895–917.

1906–15 If…to knowe]
1. Punctuation: Gentleman (ed. 1774): The humour of this prologue is artfully contrived; for by taking away the misplaced periods, and that which appears a matter abrupt and ignorant, is not without meaning and complaisance: a satirical stroke is levelled at those who, in verse, always pause at the end of a line, which frequently we hear from unskilled readers and speakers. Capell (1783, 2.3:115–16): In it’s Prologue, a gentle rub upon players (country ones, we’ll suppose) seems to have been intended; whose deep knowledge of what is rehears’d by them, is most curiously mark’d in the pointing of this prologue: upon which must have been taken some pains by the Poet himself, when it pass’d the press; for it’s punctuation, which is that of his first quarto, can be mended by no body: In reading it, we apprehend we see something, and so there is; for it is just possible to point it into meaning, (not sense) and that’s all, an experiment which we shall leave to the reader. Staunton (ed. 1857, p. 385), citing Udall’s Ralph Roister Doister [?1553, printed ?1566, 3.4.36–70, C2v–3r]: The humour of distorting the meaning of a passage by mispunctuation was a favourite one formerly. Neil (ed. 1878) notes that the Roister Doister letter was included in Thomas Wilson’s Rule of Reason (1553/4, S2v–3v; ed. Richard S. Sprague, 1972, pp. 166–7). Kittredge (ed. 1939): For a similar trick with disordered punctuation see Riche his Farewell to Military Profession, 1581 (R1v–2r; ed. Donald Beecher, 1992, pp. 242–3). Levitt (1983, p. 21) gives earlier examples of the use of the device from R. H. Robbins Secular Lyrics of the XIVth and XVth Centuries (2nd. ed. 1954), quoting No. 111, and noting that it was quite popular, being found scribbled on the fly-leaves of no fewer than four manuscripts. See also Parkes (1992, pp. 107–8, 151), who discusses such poems as part of a growing interest in the role of punctuation in verse during the late fifteenth and sixteenth century. See also n. 1911–12.
Miriam Joseph (1947, pp. 66–7) quotes this passage as an example of amphibology: Ambiguity of grammatical structure, often occasioned by mispunctuation. Blake (7.6) discusses it in the same context.
Editors who have attempted to give a corrected version of the passage include Knight (ed. 1839), Hudson (ed. 1851), Hunter (ed. 1870), Deighton (ed. 1891), Johnson (ed. 1894), Chambers (ed. 1897), Porter (ed. 1903), Watt (ed. 1906), Kittredge (ed. 1939), Brooks (ed. 1979). Sisson (1956, 1:29) in a discussion of the authority of the punctuation in early printed texts, cites this passage as an instance where the author’s punctuation was essential in the manuscript, in the acting, and in the print, not to be departed from without ruin.
2. Versification: Thiselton (1903, p. 71): One might be inclined to regard this Prologue as a Shakespearian Sonnet, with the third quatrain omitted owing to Quince’s nervousness. Since, however, Theseus describes it as nothing impaired [1922–3], it was clearly not intended to make a point of the fact that as a sonnet it was deficient. Cunliffe (1909, p. 233): Mr Dover Wilson has suggested that in the prologue to Pyramus and Thisbe Shakspere was parodying Gascoigne’s prologue to The Glasse of Governement [1575, A3v]. It is in the same measure…with the same arrangement of alternate rhymes ending with a couplet; and there is possibly an intentional echo of Gascoigne’s unhappy phrases. Riess & Williams (1992, p. 215) argue that the metric shift to iambic pentameter rather than the 8 and 6 or 8 and 8 suggested in 834–7 is done to make the Prologue conform to the metrics of the Rom. prologue.
3. Delivery: Foss (1932, p. 128): It is peculiar that Quince, the supposed author of the interlude, should be made to speak the Prologue without any regard to punctuation and convey the exact opposite of his meaning; but if he enters with many scrolls of the various speeches he has introduced and drops one at every misplaced full-stop, it would distract him and make him pause in these wrong places. Walter (ed. 1964): In a recent production Quince’s scroll refused to remain unrolled, which supplied him with additional grounds for his mispunctuated reading. Mowat & Werstine (ed. 1993): The comic effect of this prologue depends on its being delivered with the major pauses in the wrong places, in just the way that Theseus had earlier described. Holland (ed. 1994): Quinces have varied between non-stop panic, an increasing panic inducing the mistakes after a good start, and horrified amazement at the meaningless speech he finds himself making.
4. Interpretation: Leinwand (1986, p. 22): The meaning of the prologue is rendered ambivalent by its mispunctuation in delivery. Like Elizabethan artisans who rioted not to offend, but with good will, the prologue teeters back and forth between deference and offensiveness. Marcus (1988, p. 48): The mispunctuation speaks hostility toward the auditory:…However, these possible meanings have been evacuated in advance by Duke Theseus’s overriding interpretation of what the Mechanicals will intend.…The duke’s insulating paternalism protects both audience and performers. Plasse (1992, p. 36) finds a suggestion of willfulness and aggression on the part of a group of players who are subject (in more ways than one) to the power of their courtly audience. Montrose (1996, p. 195): The Prologue reads as a potential act of impudent effrontery—but one that is rendered excusable and enjoyable by its construal as a well-intentioned instance of vulgar error.

1906–8 If…good will]
Hapgood (1988, p. 27): The repetition of good will may be significant. Is there a suggestion of good Will Shakespeare in it? Did he perhaps play the role of Quince? See also n. 2207.

1907–9 we come…end]
Litt (1989, p. 10) arguing that the prologue serves as epilogue in its apologetic function: Our end may be read as goal, but also as placement, or more precisely, displacement, at the top instead of the bottom of their play, thereby hinting at the central function of an epilogue as closure.

1910 in despight]
Brooks (ed. 1979): To be vexatious. Gill (ed. 1981): To annoy you. Holland (ed. 1994): In contempt (Quince); to vex you (correctly).

1911–13 We doe…repent you]
Theobald (ed. 1733), quoting Pope’s text (see textual n.): Thus the late accurate Editor, deviating from all the Old Copies, has, by a certain peculiar Fatality, pointed this Passage. The whole Glee and Humour of the Prologue is in the Actor’s making false Rests, and so turning every Member of the Sentences into flagrant Nonsense. And Mr. Pope seems very cruel to our Author, (considering, how many Passages, which should have been pointed right, he has pointed wrong;) that here, when he should point wrong, with a strange Perverseness, and unusual Appetite for Sense, he will point right.

1911–12 content…intent]
Cunliffe (1909, p. 233): [These lines] reproduced the very words of Gascoigne’s prologue [to The Glasse of Governement, 1575, A3v] Content you then (my Lordes) with good intent, though the resemblance may be merely accidental. Sir John Harington similarly plays on the intent to please and not offend in his epigrams Of writing with double pointing (1618, 1.33–5, C1r-v).

1911 minding]
Schmidt (1875, Mind, vb. 4: To intend, to mean. The Clarkes (1879, p. 568): Wishing, being in the mind. See OED, v. 6b.

1913 repent you]
Spevack (ed. Ant. 1990, p. 164, n. 1672): For the reflexive pronoun in the dative case, see Franz §307.

1914–15 by…knowe]
Wilson (ed. 1924): These words announce the dumb-show to follow, and give us Shakespeare’s opinion of dumb-shows and presenters in general; he compares Ham. 3.2.141–2 (2008–9): We shall knowe by these Fellowes: the Players cannot keepe counsell, they’l tell all. Mehl (Dumb Show, 1965, p. 109): This could of course refer to a mere procession round the stage in the manner of popular drama, but the following prologue sounds in places very much like a description of a pantomime performed at the same time. Serio (1978–9, pp. 22–3) sees a double meaning: By viewing Pyramus and Thisby, by beholding an image of love that is selfless, inviolable, enduring, the spectators shall know all, that [they] are like to know about the nature of true love. Parker (Othello, 1994, p. 133), discussing show in Ham. 3.2.139–46 (2006–13): Quince’s prologue promises his players’ similarly doubled audience that by this show, / You shall know all, that you are like to know…, before a performance that features obscene play on the little o of the crannied hole or chink of a Wall suggestive of the hymeneal one.

1914 showe]
A normal abbreviation for dumb show: see OED, Show, n1 13a; Dessen & Thomson, 1999, p. 80.

1915 like]
OED (adv. 8): Likely.

1915 knowe,]
Wilson (ed. 1924): The final comma…delightfully suggests the rising tone on which the stage-frighted Quince concludes. See also n. 66 (looke,).

1916–23 This…disordered]
Crane (1951, pp. 76–7): The mingling of prose and verse in the last scene is, for delicacy and skill, unmatched in Shakespeare’s work.…[After Quince’s prologue], Theseus and the rest…begin a barrage of quips and puns in prose. Their remarks are witty; but, after all, they have a splendid text to annotate.…Technically, at least, this is critical prose; and the form is doubly appropriate. The speakers have not hitherto dealt with such a theme; and their remarks offer a delightful contrast with the extraordinary verse. The prose follows a quite rigid form; any of the characters…might speak any of the lines. One may here confound Theseus’ prose with Lysander’s, although their verse is clearly distinguishable. Mahood (1957, pp. 31, 166): Puns at the expense of a third person are socially exclusive.…This is never an attractive form of wit.…Shakespeare distinguishes the clowns from the courtiers by one group’s linguistic abuses and the punning virtuosity of the other. Berry (1988, pp. 36–7): The great point about the courtiers’ running commentary is that it is to one another, semi-private, and not hurled at the actors; but see n. 1984–90. Andrews (ed. 1989): From this point on during the playlet, the members of the audience speak in prose, condescending to the normal discourse of the Mechanicals and thereby heightening the ludicrousness of the verse in the play the Mechanicals are performing. The Sokols (2000, p. 255), in the entry pardon/clemency: Many in the noble audiences of meta-theatrical spectacle are sharply judgemental, and express this in an unbridled way during enactments (LLL 5.2 and MND 5.1); see further n. 2214.

1916 stand…points]
OED (Point, n.1 3): A punctuation-mark; and (5b), citing this instance: To be punctilious or scrupulous. Delius (ed. 1859): = to be meticulous, and = to respect punctuation (Ger.). Smith (ed. 1925): In two senses—mind his stops, and trouble about small politenesses of behaviour. Andrews (ed. 1993): Theseus is probably jesting only about Peter Quince’s enunciation. But stand upon Points can also refer to the erection of a man’s Good Will, an unintended sense here reinforced by the repetition of such words as come and know. See also n. 149.

1916 stand vpon]
OED (Stand, v. 78j): To attach importance to, treat as important.

1917–18 He…stoppe]
Deighton (ed. 1891): His prologue goes with the paces of an unbroken colt that pays no regard to the check of its rider. Thiselton (1903, pp. 71–2), citing Madden, Diary of Master William Silence (1897, pp. 296–8): Manifestly Quince is the rough Colt.…If I am right in the view that the comma at the end of the Prologue [see 1906] in [Q1] indicates a precipitate withdrawal on the part of Quince, we may imagine that he ought, instead of so withdrawing, to have waited to make a leg, which would have sufficiently resembled the attitude to which a horse was brought at the stop in the career (here the Prologue), to give an added force to Lysander’s wit. See also Sieveking (1916, 2:414).

1917 rid]
OED (Ride, v. 15a), quoting this instance first in figurative use: To manage or control while seated on. For the form of the participle see Franz §168. Thiselton (1903, p. 71): Rid is not from the verb to ride, but from the verb to rid. Watt (ed. 1906) sees a pun on Got through, and ridden; so also Wilson (ed. 1924, Glossary): (a) ridden, (b) rid himself of, discharged.

1917 rough]
OED (7c), quoting this instance first: Of horses: Not properly broken in. rare.

1918 stoppe]
OED (Stop, n.2 17a): A mark or point of punctuation; and (21a), quoting this instance: In the manège. A sudden check in a horse’s career.

1919 speake true]
Delius (1859): = Speak correctly; and = tell the truth (Ger.).

1921 Recorder]
Steevens (ed. 1773): A kind of flute. Shakespeare introduced it in Hamlet. See Ham. 3.2.357–60 (2228–31): Gouerne these Ventages with your finger and thumbe, giue it breath with your mouth, and it will discourse most excellent Musicke. Looke you, these are the stoppes. Brooks (ed. 1979): Pipe of the whistle family: see Edgar Hunt, The Recorder and its Music. Renaissance recorders are described and illustrated by David Munrow, Instruments of the Middle Ages and Renaissance (pp. 56–8 of the book accompanying the discs, which exemplify the music on Side III, No. 10).

1921 not in gouernement]
Steevens (ed. 1773): That is, not regularly according to the tune. Mason (1785, p. 72), quoting Ham. 3.2.357–8 (2228–30), see previous note: This explains the meaning of government, in this passage. Onions (1916, 2:39): Govern. To regulate an instrument by means of its stops.…Government is similarly used.

1921 in gouernement]
Johnson (1755, 7): [In grammar.] Influence with regard to construction. Brooks (ed. 1979): Under control. Revived by Arnold Dolmetsch, the recorder, in simplified forms, is now often governed by schoolchildren. See OED, n.2 1a.

1922–3 tangled…disordered]
Andrews (ed. 1989): All the links are in good condition, but the relationships among them are in complete disarray. Forey (1998, p. 328) compares Golding’s Preface to the Reader, 205–6 (see ), finding in the simile a conscious, ironic reference to Golding’s Preface, and…an acknowledgement of Shakespeare’s irreverent use of it, which has implications for the play’s use of parody. Weimann (2000, p. 86) examines the contrarious ideas of tangled, nothing impaired, disordered in arguing that [o]n a verbal level, deformation is at work.

1922–3 tangled]
OED (ppl. a.), quoting this instance first: Mixed up confusedly.

1922–3 nothing impaired]
Evans (ed. 1974): I.e. still unbroken (nothing is here, as often, adverbial, meaning in no respect, not at all).

[1924] Tawyer…trumpet]
Introduced in F1: see textual n. Collier (ed. 1842): Possibly Tawyer was the name of the trumpeter. Halliwell (1885, p. 500): Tawyer was a subordinate in the pay of Hemmings, his burial at St Saviour’s in June, 1625, being thus noticed in the sexton’s MS. notebook,—William Tawier, Mr. Heminges man, gr. and cl., xvj.d. On the trumpet, see n. 1904.
Collier (185, pp. 108–9 and ed. 1858) claimed that in his corrected folio of 1632 Tawyer was called the Presenter, and that the SP at 1926 was altered to Pres.; this specious claim appealed to some later editors, since it meant that the correctly punctuated speech 1926–50 was not delivered by the same character who had mangled 1906–15.

1925 Lyon]
Theobald (ed. 1733) added as in dumb show; see textual n. Thirlby (MS 1733–47) draws attention to the parallel in Ham. 3.2.135+1 (1990): The dumbe shew enters [F1]. See n. 1914–15.

1926 Prologue]
Wilson (ed. 1924): Q. heads this Prologue, and it was certainly Quince’s speech; but here he is playing the part of Presenter to explain the dumb-show which has just entered. He explains everything, at great length, and when he has finished, the play itself is seen to be superfluous.

1926–40 Gentles…affright]
Riess & Williams (1992, pp. 215–6) argue that 1926–40 form a sonnet like the Prologue in Rom., with the exception of the extra unrhymed line 1938, inserted on behalf of the actor playing Lyon.

1926 Gentles]
Schmidt (1874, Gentle, subst.): In the plural = gentlefolks.…Mostly used as a familiar compellation.…Especially in addressing an audience. Anon (ed. 1887): Common gender. We now say Ladies and Gentlemen. See OED, Gentle, n. 1a and c.

1927 truthe…plaine]
Proverbial; quoted by Tilley (T591): Truth will come to light. Smith (1963, p. 121) cites Tilley (T593): Truth’s tale is simple (Truth is plain) and many other parallels.

1929 certaine]
OED (adv. 1): Certainly, assuredly. Steevens (ed. 1793), giving various examples: A burlesque was here intended on the frequent recurrence of certain as a bungling rhime in poetry more ancient than the age of Shakspeare. Wright (ed. 1877): A most convenient word for filling up a line and at the same time conveying no meaning. Donaldson (1985, pp. 13–14): In neither Middle nor Elizabethan English is the rhyme -ain a hard one, but the word certain, with the stress wrenched to the second syllable, often appears in rhyme in lesser poets of both eras,…The result is apt to be an earnest asseveration of a fact not really subject to dispute, as when the narrator, needing a rhyme for grain, remarks of Sir Thopas: And I you tell in good certaine He had a semely nose [728–9; see ], or the result may be an emphatic revelation of the obvious, as in Quince’s Prologue’s announcement, when he needs a rhyme for plain,…Thisbe’s identity, if not her beauteousness, is hardly open to question.…[I]t was surely not J. Thomson that Shakespeare was making fun of [as Muir 1954, p. 145 suggests], but—like Chaucer—all the lazy versifiers who reject rhyme’s challenge to make them think.

1930–1 This…Wall]
Wickham (1959, 1:101, 371 n. 78) says of medieval pageant stages that some degree of realism was aimed at; he quotes a description of battlements of timber leyed ov(e)r wt canvas empeynted like frestone and whight lyme, so that the semys of the stone were p(er)ceyved like as mortur or sement had ben betwene, pointing out that Chaucer’s tregetours (magicians) use lime and stone, the same materials enjoined by Shakespeare for the costume of Wall, in creating the illusion of a castle (FranT, 1149). However, later (1963, 2.1:298) he believes the Mechanicals’ Wall to be a painted cloth made ridiculous by being held in position by one of the actors. Brooks (ed. 1979): Lime and stone [1962] here and in [1966] and [1994] are from Chaucer’s [LGW 765, see ]. The practical problems of dramatic presentation are stressed in the frequent reference during the performance of the interlude to the wall’s composition, unusual in the narrative analogues. See also nn. 878–82, 1962.

1930 lyme]
Thirlby (MS 1725–33) conjectured loam, citing 879 (see textual n.). See also 1962, 1966.

1930 roughcast]
See n. 879–80; see also 1962.

1931 Wall…sunder]
Hitchcock (1865, pp. 90–2, 98–9) argues that the fable of the Interlude mystically shows the doctrine of the unity of the spirit of man…with the spirit of nature between which the body stands like a wall of separation; hence here: Through this wall (the dull substance of the flesh), the lovers may indeed communicate, but only by a whisper, very secretly. Rickert (1923, pp. 73–4) believes that the wall of the Piramus and Thisbe analogy in a poem by King James VI and I is Hadrian’s Wall, which separates the Scottish king from the English queen Elizabeth whom he is wooing. Richmond (1971, p. 113): It is an archetypal image for the obstacle which passion unconsciously solicits as a prerequisite for full ardor. Not only does it provide a concrete analogue to the legal and emotional barriers that create the intensity of all the lovers in the play; it is also a wry distortion of the famous balcony which divides Romeo from his beloved. Hinely (1987, p. 129): The thwarted sexual development and frustration of the lovers blocked by this recalcitrant wall is bawdily suggested by a hodgepodge of vaginal, phallic, and anal allusions. (Goldstein 1973, [pp. 193–4]; Gui 1952, [p. 280]). The wall suggests both the female and male sexual organs. Cf. Golding’s Ovid, 4.91; see . For other examples of critical ingenuity exercised in interpreting Wall’s significance, see n. 878–82.

1932 poore soules]
See n. 729.

1933 At…wonder]
Forey (1998, p. 326): Golding’s propitiatory and somewhat ingenuous tone has found its way into the characterization of the mechanicals; his defensive plea Let no man marvell at the same (Epistle, l. 310) shares very much the tone of the mechanicals’ prologue.…Both fear, though for different reasons, that their work may be wrongly taken at face value. See .

1933 the which]
Blake (3.3.2.6 (d), quoting this instance: May be used instead of simple which in all grammatical positions.

1934–5 This…moone-shine]
Wiles (1993, p. 58) : In lieu of the conventional chaste/fertile female moon, Quince offers his audience a male moon. Of [the moon-goddess] Cynthia’s traditional iconographic attributes, the one that remains is the dog, the dog of Diana the huntress. [The lantern and thorn bush] could be seen as a parodic substitute for the flaming torch that is the invariable attribute of Hymen.…Starveling represents the negation of both Cynthia and Hymen, the two figures who should be preeminent in a wedding masque. Gill (ed. 1981): The dog…seems to be Shakespeare’s own invention; but Halliwell (ed. 1856 at 2045) reproduces a seal affixed to a deed dated 1335 which shows the man in the moon accompanied by his dog; see also Emerson (1906, p. 842), and Wiles (1993, p. 58 n. 38). Holland (ed. 1994): Starveling’s dog may have been and usually is a stuffed animal though Lance’s dog Crab was real; see TGV 2.3 and 4.4. Griffiths (ed. 1996, p. 203): Kean’s Moonshine had a real dog, a clever little Scotch terrier who barked pleasantly at the lion (Press, 12 February 1859); Ray Llewellyn’s real dog at Birmingham Town Hall in 1973 caused havoc among the members of the City of Birmingham Symphony Orchestra on stage to play Mendelssohn’s music; Lepage in 1992 also used a real dog. For the man in the moon see n. 870–2.

1935 Presenteth]
See n. 871–2.

1936 By moone-shine]
See 858–64, and nn. Cf. Tit. 2.3.231–2 (985–6): So pale did shine the Moone on Pyramus, When he by night lay bath’d in Maiden blood.

1936 thinke no scorne]
Did not disdain. See Schmidt (1875, Scorn subst. 1). Deighton (ed. 1891): Were not ashamed.

1937 To…wooe]
Steevens (ed. 1778) notes the parallel lines in Chaucer (LGW 784–5; see ). Wright (ed. 1877) adds the corresponding line in Golding’s Ovid (4.108; see ).

1937 Ninus tombe]
See nn. 910, 911.

1937 wooe]
White (ed. 1858): It may be remarked here upon the rhyme of woo with know, that the former word seems to have had the pure vowel sound of o. It was spelled wooe or woe, and as often in the latter way as the former. Cercignani (1981, p. 197): If actually intended,…the instance know : woo…would have to be explained as based on lME ɚu in know…as well as in woo, which in Spenser occurs in wowed : vowed (OF vuer)—but cf. Smith’s ŏ in wooëd (1568, 43r), which appears to be by shortening of ME ou (Dobson, § 40).

1938 grizly]
Schmidt (1874, Grisly 2): Grim, full of terror. Cf. OED, Grisly, a. 1. See also OED, Grisy, a.1 Obs., quoting the F variant of this instance. See textual n.

1938 Lyon…name]
Theobald (ed. 1733) transposing Lyon hight with by name: As all the other Parts of this Speech are in alternate Rhyme, excepting that it closes with a Couplet; and as no Rhyme is left to, name; we must conclude, either a Verse is slipt out, which cannot now be retriev’d: or, by a Transposition of the Words, as I have placed them, the Poet intended a Triplet. See textual n. The Clarkes (1864, p. 674): We believe that the defective rhyming was intentional…to denote the slipshod style of the doggerel that forms the dialogue in the Interlude…which we have always cherished a convistion Shakespeare intended to be taken as written by Peter Quince himself citing 1740–1 and F1 SD Enter the Prologue. Quince. Wells & Taylor (1987, p. 285): The formal ineptitude probably results from textual corruption, but may be deliberately comic; no one has ventured to invent a line to rhyme with name. See also n. 1926–40. Muir (1954, p. 145): The anonymous author [of the Pyramus story in A gorgious Gallery, 1578] uses the elegant variation name and hight in successive lines (N4r). On the lion’s gender, see n. 327.

1938 hight]
Malone (ed. 1790): Hight, in old English signifies—is called. See OED, v.1 4. Scott (ed. 1898): The word was practically obsolete in Shakespeare’s time, but occurs frequently in Spenser’s Fairy Queen (1590). It is possible that Shakespeare is thus ridiculing Spenser’s affectation of antique words and forms. Blake (4.2.9 (f)), quoting this instance: A survival of an old passive form. See Franz §184.

1940 Did…did]
Muir (1954, p. 143) suggests that the excessive use as padding of the auxiliary did glances at Golding’s similar habit in translating Ovid. Taylor (1990, pp. 59–60) demurs, citing Cooper’s Thesaurus in the Dictionarum Historicum & Poeticum, where Thisbe for haste dyd lette fall hir upper garments, which the beaste dyd rente and teare in peeces, and adding: Elizabethans were generally fonder of the pleonastic did than we are…Shakespeare’s lines are thus a slightly comic elaboration of a stock Elizabethan habit which arises because of Quince’s verbose rhetoric.

1940 Did scarre]
Phillips (ed. 1969): I.e. the lion scared Thisby. Strained syntax is a feature of the clowns’ play.

1940 scarre away]
OED (Scare, v. 1.c): To frighten away, drive off. Now chiefly with adv., exc. with reference to keeping off birds from corn, etc.; the first example given with the adv. is 1660.

1940 scarre…affright]
Barber (1959, pp. 153–4 n.) cites this as an example of the many redundant synonyms characteristic of the style of the interlude. Donaldson (1985, p. 16): Perhaps scare away, or rather…affright should be considered an example of correctio, but it is a curious example because the contrast between scare away and affright exists nowhere but in the speaker’s mind. Possibly he thought it more ladylike to be affrighted than to be scared away.

1941 And…fall]
Steevens (ed. 1778) compares Chaucer (LGW 813) and (ed. 1785) Golding’s Ovid (4.125); see , .

1941 mantle]
Muir (1954, p. 143): Both Golding and Shakespeare say that Thisbe left her mantle behind, and Thomson also calls it a mantle. In Ovid it is velamina, and this becomes a scarf (Mouffet), a kerchief ([A gorgious Gallery, 1578, O4v]), and a wimpel (Chaucer and Gower).

1941 did fall]
Franz (§630c): = dropped (OED, v. 49a). Steevens (ed. 1773): The modern editions read,—she let fall, unnecessarily. Capell (1783, 2.3:116): There is more comic expression in did fall than let fall. See textual n.

1942, 1945, 1947 Lyon…blade…Mulberry]
Abbott §82: Shakespeare rarely indulges in this archaism [of omitting A and The] except to ridicule it. See also 2025.

1942 Lyon]
Muir (1954, p. 150): Sources are divided about the sex of the lion. Taylor (1990, p. 59) claims that the use of Lyon here shows Shakespeare is recalling Thomson [in A handefull of plesant delites] and not Golding at this point. See , and Kennedy (2005, p. 18). See also n. 327.

1942 Lyon vile]
Kranendonk (1932, p. 214) gives examples from Spenser of similar archaic transposition of the adjective: As Lyon fierce, a dragon fell, A monster vile, (FQ 1.1.17, 1.2.10, 1.1.13). Hammerle (1958, pp. 56–7) cites such inversions as one of the characteristics of Quince’s grammatical style, and gives more examples from Spenser, especially those involving vile (Ger.).

1942 with…staine]
Verity (ed. 1912): It had just killed some oxen (Ovid, Met. 4.96–7; cf. Golding, 4.129 bloudie teeth; see ). Muir (1954, p. 146): A phrase which occurs in two of Chaucer’s lines [LGW 807, 820; see ]…doubtless gave Quince his phrase with bloody mouth. Taylor (1989, p. 318) claims Chaucer and Sh. are independently translating Ovid’s ore cruentato (4.104). Kranendonk (1932, p. 213 n.) compares the bloudy mouth of the slain Sans Foy (FQ 1.2.19). Cf. A gorgious Gallery (1578, O4r), with his mouth…it staynes with blood.

1943 Anon]
See n. 387.

1943 tall]
Johnson (1755, 3): Sturdy; lusty. OED (2b): Comely, handsome; (3): Brave, valiant. Schmidt (1875, 3) makes the point that in such senses the word is almost always used either with irony, or, as here, put in the mouth of mean persons.

1944 trusty]
Halliwell (ed. 1856): The epithet trusty, which occurs in [Qq], is accidentally omitted in ed. 1623, and the editor of the second folio, observing that a disyllable had been left out, arbitrarily supplies it with the word gentle. See textual n. Thiselton (1903, p. 72), conjecturing that the line has been garbled by the prologiser and should have read And finds the Mantle of his Thisbie slaine, argues that it is by no means certain that the Quartos should be followed.

1944 slaine]
OED (Slay, v.1 5a): Kill; noting In this sense, slay is now mainly confined to literary and rhetorical language. Rann (ed. 1787): Torn; possibly drawing on Chaucer, LGW 820 (see n. 1942) or Golding 4.129 in pieces she did tear (see ). Becket (1815, 1:278): Should be printed sleyne, i.e. torn into threads, or to pieces, possibly having in mind a weaving term; see OED (Slay, sley, n.1 1): An instrument used in weaving to beat up the weft.

1945–6 with blade…breast]
Upton (1746, p. 254): But whatever beauty this alliteration might have, yet the affectation of it must appear ridiculous; for poets are not made by mechanical rules: and it was ridiculed as long ago as the times of old Ennius: O Tite tute Tati tibi tante tyranne tulisti [Annales 104 Skutsch]. And by Shakespeare in [1945–6]. Johnson (ed. 1765): Mr. Upton…might have remarked the same of The raging Rocks And shivering Shocks. Gascoigne, contemporary with our poet, remarks and blames the same affectation (see Elizabethan Critical Essays, ed. G. Gregory Smith, Oxford, 1904, 1:47). Examples of 16th-c. excessive use of alliteration are given by Steevens (edd. 1773, 1778, 1793). Halliwell (ed. 1856) quotes Puttenham (1589; 1936, p. 254) on Tautologia: Ye haue another manner of composing your metre nothing commendable, specially if it be too much vsed, and is when our maker takes too much delight to fill his verse with wordes all beginning with a letter. Wright (ed. 1877): Shakespeare ridicules the alliteration which the poetasters of his day affected. It was an exaggeration of the principle upon which Anglo-Saxon verse was constructed, and comes again under his lash in [LLL 4.2.55–7 (1213–18)]. Verity (ed. 1893) suggests that in this speech of Holofernes in LLL, as well as here in MND, Sh. is satirizing Euphuism and contemporary affectations. Lees (ed. 1902) disagrees, citing rather the extravagant rant of some of the inferior dramatists of the day, or Thomson’s poem in A Handefull, And then the beast with his bright blade / he slew certaine (ll. 51–2). Koeppel (1911, p. 191) sees parody of Seneca’s translators, especially John Studley (Ger.). Kranendonk (1932, p. 213) suggests Sh. is ridiculing Spenser’s excessive use of alliteration, and gives several examples including FQ 2.2.32, boyling brests.
Walker (1860, 1:154) cites Ovid, Met. 4.119–20. Rudd (1979, p. 185) quotes the same passage in suggesting that Sh. had read the original Latin; for Ovid writes: demisit in ilia ferrum, nec mora, ferventi moriens e uulnere traxit; He plunged the blade into his entrails, and immediately, as he died, he drew it from the boiling wound. Golding merely says bleeding wound [4.146; see ]. Brooks (ed. 1979) compares the boyling brest of the Calydonian boar in Golding, 8.478 (see ), but Holland (ed. 1994) believes Sh’s joke depends on his having read the Latin lines quoted by Walker. Taylor (1990, p. 59) points out that Golding and Ovid locate the wound in the stomach: But in Thomson, as in Shakespeare, one finds the weapon described as a blade and the wound…in the hart. Bush (1931, p. 145) does not find the use of the single word blade in Moffet’s or other versions of the Pyramus and Thisbe story evidence of a significant link with Sh., but in A gorgious Gallery (1578, P1v) one usage is combined with intrusive alliteration: his naked blade hee fiersly from his side Out drew, & through his brest, it forst with mortal wound to glide, The streames of gory blood out glush [sic: a nonce word?]. Cf. Brutus preparing for suicide in Whitney’s Choice of Emblemes (1586; ed. Henry Green, 1886, p. 70): And bending then to blade, his bared brest.

1946 brauely]
OED (1), quoting this instance first: Valiantly, fearlessly.

1946 broacht]
Steevens (MS 1773–8): Spitted, stabb’d. Evans (ed. 1887): According to Schmidt tapped like a cask; but may it not mean pierced as with a broach or spit? Mowat & Werstine (ed. 1993): Stabbed (with a comic allusion to broaching [i.e., tapping] a keg of beer or wine). See OED, Broach, v.1 1 and 4.

1947 tarying…shade]
In Golding’s Ovid, the lovers arrange to tarie (i.e. wait) under a mulberry tree (4.109, see ). Anon. (1924, p. 857): The sublime ridiculousness of that tarie obviously struck Shakespeare. Parker (2001, p. 55): A line that deftly catches Ovid’s own punning on mora [Met. 4.120, 127] as both mulberries and delay.

1947 Mulberry shade]
See n. 985. This is the only mention in MND of the tree whose fruit, in Ovid Met. 4. 125–7, is metamorphosed from white to purple by Pyramus’ blood, but see 544 and n. The mulberry is entirely omitted by Chaucer, Gower, and the author of A Handefull. In A gorgious Gallery the lovers agree to meet at the Tombe which A mulbery, with braunches making shade overgrows (l. 286). In Ovid they arrange to meet sub umbra arboris (4.88–9); Golding has no mention of shade.

1948 For…rest]
Deighton (ed. 1891): As for the rest of the story.

1950 At large discourse]
Andrews (ed. 1989): Speak at greater length.

[1951] F1: Exit all but Wall]
Thiselton (1903, pp. 72–3): This direction is not in the Quartos. It is clearly a correction of the direction Exit Lyon, Thisbie, and Mooneshine, which the Quartos place after Demetrius’ next speech [1955], and which the Folio carelessly retains notwithstanding the correction. See textual n. However, this addition, together with the added re-entry for Pyramus at 1971, may represent a lack of understanding of staging intention on the part of the F compositor. There is no reason why Pyramus should not remain on stage; the expression in 1970 Pyramus drawes neere the Wall indicates change of place on stage rather than re-entry.

1952 be to]
Doran (ed. 1959): Is going to. For the construction, see n. 1775.

1953 No wonder]
Deighton (ed. 1891): It would be nothing wonderful if he had to, etc.

1953–4 One…doe]
Grey (1754, 1:75) suggests an allusion to Balaam’s ass (Num 22:21–3), as humorously applied by a Divine to a stammering Lord who was no friend to the clergy, and had a mind to set it in a ridiculous light at his table, observing, that Balaam’s ass spoke be—be—because he was prie—prie—prie—st—Priest—rid, Sir, said a valet de chambre (who stood behind his chair) my lord would say; No friend, replied the clergyman, Balaam could not speak for himself, and so his ass spoke for him. Scott (ed. 1898): A suggestion that biped asses are numerous. Baldwin (1944, 2:346–7) discusses connections between Erasmus’s explanations of the origin of the proverb Induitis me leonis exuuium (Collected Works, Toronto, 1982, 32:290–1) and the lion’s skin/ass passage in Jn. 2.1.134–46 (434–46); in a footnote he adds: This lion’s skin probably did duty also for the lion in [MND]. The apposition of lions and asses there looks suspiciously like an echo of our proverb. See also Barkan (1986) at n. 2104.

1956 enterlude]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Properly something played in the intervals of a festivity. Gaubert (1945, p. 202) points out that although the artisans’ play is complete in itself, and called by Quince a lamentable comedy (279–80) as well as our Enterlude (273–4), it is no more than an interlude in the context of the wedding festivies and of Sh.’s play (Fr.). See also n. 273–4.

1957 That…wall]
Robinson (1964, p. 200): This and the other brief self-identifications, spoken by the personifications…are clearly exaggerated imitations of a brief style of self-identification extensively employed for the personified characters in the moral interludes and hybrid plays: Christianity I doe represent, vor we Ignoram people whom itche doe perzente, I represent a vertue called Pacience, Commons complaint I represent,35 and a host of others. (Wapull, The Tide Tarrieth No Man [1576; Tudor Facsimile Texts 1910], F2v; Respublica [1553; EETS 226, 1952], 3.3.648; Phillip, Patient Grissel [1558–61; MSR, 1909], I. 1787; Preston, Cambises [c. 1558–69], CFv.)

1957 Flute]
All editors except Pope (ed. 1725) correct to Snout; see textual n. Brooks (ed. 1979): Probably Shakespeare’s error, perhaps because Thisbe, whom he has just brought on, is in his mind. Cf. Cobweb for Peaseblossom, (1533). Foakes (ed. 1984): An error difficult to explain as a misreading (though not impossible if a long s was read as f), and may be Shakespeare’s own confusion of names; he instances 1994 as another puzzling error in Q.

1959, 1964 cranied…cranie]
Capell (1783, 2.3:116): The reciter who would give [comic] expression to crany’d, and crany, in Wall’s speech, must make both vowels long: his crany is his left hand held up, the hand turn’d and a finger seperated. Furness (ed. 1895) quoting Capell, comments: [He], as an actor, was, I fear, a case of arrested development.

1959, 1964 cranied]
Johnson (1755, Crannied), quoting this instance: Full of chinks.

1959 hole or chinke]
Anon. (1900?, n.p.): The chink or hole in the wall has an obvious tendency to the ludicrous and the farcical. Accordingly it has been annexed by farce, and will ever with a mural surprise of the right kind excite the risibility of the amusement-loving public. Andrews (ed. 1993): This phrase is redundant, and it unintentionally calls attention to the Person (IV.ii.11 [1757]), the anatomy, of the actor playing Wall. Parker (Teaching, 1994, pp. 205–6): The Wall first presents its crannied hole or chink.…There then follows an extended series of double entendres, which, beginning with Pyramus’s insistent O (O wall! O sweet, O lovely wall), start to give an obscene sense to the hole or chink between the lovers, in a passage that harps repeatedly on stones and ends with the following exchange: [quotes 2003–13]. McDonald (ed. 2000): Chink (a slang term for vagina or anus, and part of a network of obscene jokes running through the mechanicals’ play). See also nn. 875, 2003–4.

1960–1 Thisby…secretly]
Walker (1860, 1:114), discussing the peculiar mode of rhyming: This is of a piece with the purposely incondite composition of this dramaticle.…We might indeed scan: Pyram | us and | Thisby; but this is not likely. Furness (ed. 1895) finds the rhyme particularly confusing because he is following F’s spelling Thisbie. Barber (1959, p. 153 n.): To fill out a line, or to make a rhyme as false as Thisby…secretly, the mother wits often elaborate redundancies, so that technical ineptitude results in a most ineffective and unpoetical factuality.

1962–3 This…wall]
See n. 1930–1.

1962 This…this…this]
Gerstner-Hirzel (1957, p. 47): The three successive demonstrative pronouns obviously refer to deictic gestures; he speculates on possible costuming, with two paste-boards or cloak, and on what gestures might be used.

1962 lome]
See n. 879. Collier (ed. 1842) justified the emendation to lime by referring to 1930 and 1966 (see also 1994). See textual n.

1962 roughcast]
See n. 879–80.

1962 stone]
Foakes (ed. 1984): Compare [1993–4]; stone is an addition to the lime or loam and rough-cast mentioned at [1930] and at [879–80]; like Moonshine with his dog…, Wall is perhaps intentionally overburdened with his attributes, for comic effect.

1964–5 And…whisper]
Ritson (in Steevens, ed. 1793): So, in Golding’s Ovid, 1567: [quotes 4.83–8; see ].

1964 this…sinister]
Johnson (ed. 1894): Holding up one hand with two fingers spread wide apart. He must have put them down again. See [1979]. Thiselton (1903, p. 73): Snowt shows the cranny with both his right and his left (right and sinister) hands, indicating the cranny’s opening at each side of the wall. Doran (ed. 1959): I.e. running right and left, horizontal. Clayton (1974, p. 108): While use of the fingers…is reasonable, it is within the prospect of reasonable inference that the chink or cranny is the space between Wall’s (not yet spread) legs; such a vertical aperture is no less appropriate…than a horizontal one (or perhaps a bilateral pair) to be described in this homespun play as right and sinister: right and proper is wanted, but sinister is a necessity for what New Cambridge aptly refers to as an exquisite rhyme! (Wilson, ed. 1924, p. 145). Right is forced by the primary Latin sense of sinister (left) to take on a passing—and I think irrelevant—spatial sense. But right and sinister together also make the pointedly oxymoronic jar of simultaneous upright and base. Brooks (ed. 1979): Echoing, in farcical vein, Chaucer’s Upon that o syde of the wal stod he, / And on that other side stod Thesbe [LGW 750–1; see ].

1964 sinister]
Delius (ed. 1859): = left; in farfetched and affected language (Ger.). Harrison (ed. 1937): With the double meaning of ominous and left hand. For the accent on the penultimate syllable, usual in Sh., see Abbott §490. Cercignani (1981, p. 41) considers this accentuation probably due to the continued influence of L[atin] sinister.

1965 fearefull]
Baker (ed. 1896): Timorous. Kittredge (ed. 1939) adds: Timid. See OED 3a.

1965 whisper]
Wells (ed. 1967): The inexactness of the rhyme is of course part of the parody. An actor has been known to show Snout realizing the fault, and confusedly pronouncing whipister. The effect was amusing.

1966–7 Would…better]
Thiselton (1903, p. 73): As our Wall had a cranny in it, and Snowt has only mentioned loame roughcast and stone, Theseus probably means that even a wall of superior constituents could not speak better. Thisbe’s subsequent mention of Lime and Haire [1994] will not militate against this view, for the existence of the cranny is due to the lime and hair not having been duly mixed and tempered.

1966 lime and haire]
See OED, Lime, n.1 3c: lime and hair: a kind of plasterer’s cement to which hair is added to bind the mixture closely together; giving 1626 as the earliest instance. Cf. Dekker, Satiromastix (1602), whooreson poore lyme and hayre-rascal (1.2.281; ed. Fredson Bowers, 1953, 1:324).

1968–9 The…discourse]
Farmer (in Steevens, ed. 1773, 10:App.2) was the first to call attention to the pun on partition: Alluding to the many stupid partitions in the argumentative writings of the time. Baldwin (1944, 2:110): Apparently the pun grows directly out of Cicero’s definition in Topica [4.24–7], when he uses parietem as an illustration of something which exists, and therefore can be partitioned into parts. Just preceding this definition there had also been a long discussion of the rights and wrongs of a common wall, such as Shakspere’s Wall was. So in Topica wall was already connected both directly and by association with partition in discourse. Miriam Joseph (1947, p. 112): Merismus, or partitio, divides a whole into its parts.
The Clarkes (ed. 1864), questionably: By the way in which we find the expressions partition and partition wall employed by Hooker and other controversialists, it seems probable that the word was used much in the same sense that we now use sect; and thus Demetrius’s punning rejoinder would be comprehensible, as meaning—This is the most ingenious propounder of doctrine that ever I heard hold forth.

1968–9 wittiest]
Kittredge (ed. 1939): Most intelligent.

note.p: For the SD in F, see textual n. and [1951].

div.note:
1972–83 O grim…mee]
McFarland (1972, pp. 86–7): Pyramus’s entrance mocks Hieronimo’s O eyes! no eyes, but fountains fraught with tears; / O life! no life, but lively form of death; / O world! no world, but mass of public wrongs…, and, indeed, the idiosyncratically repetitive technique that runs all through Kyd’s tragic prosody: [quotes 1972–9]. The passage is wickedly funny. The threefold alack, so as to rhyme with black, shows an unabashed paucity of invention; the witless fixing of night as when day is not; the rhyme of mine and eyne, thrown askew by the adjectival repetition of mine; the improperly placed rhyme of chink and blink; and most of all, the inspired personification of Wall—all these ludicrous elements are masterfully realized.

1972–5 O grim…forgot]
Evans (ed. 1887): Cf. the Nurse’s lamentation over the supposed dead body of Juliet [Rom. 4.5.49–54 (2629–34)]. In both places Shakspere may have intended to ridicule the bombast of such plays as Kyd’s Spanish Tragedy. Halliwell (ed. 1856) compares Gascoigne’s translation of Euripides’ Jocasta (1566); see n. 1976. Rushton (1909, p. 93) quotes Puttenham (1589; 1936, p. 212, Ecphonesis): The figure of exclamation, I call him [the outcrie] because it vtters our minde by all such words as do shew any extreme passion, whether it be by way of exclamation or crying out, admiration or wondering, imprecation or cursing, obtestation or taking God and the world to witnes, or any such like as declare an impotent affection. Arnold (1911, p. 114): From Aristophanes to Sheridan, the apostrophe is a favorite device of the burlesque soliloquy, but nowhere as in Shakespeare is its absurdity so effectively enhanced by frequent repetition. The soliloquies of the play within the play of [MND] depend largely on this rhetorical trick. Bradbrook (1951, p. 114) compares Luc. 764–70. Walter (ed. 1964): A burlesque of some popular verse writing with its frequent apostrophes. See Phoenix Nest, 1593, O night, o jealous night, etc. (N3v; ed. Hyder E. Rollins, Cambridge, Mass., 1931, p. 102). Elam (1984, p. 64) takes issue with the claim of Stamm (1954, p. 33) that here Sh. is parodying his own use of word-scenery: Ingenuous scene-painting for purely expository purposes has little to do with actual Shakespearean practice. Calderwood (1992, pp. 129–30) explores O, making some ingenious if farfetched connections with Lr. and Wiv.

1972 grim lookt]
Rolfe (ed. 1877): Grim-looking. See Abbott §374. Cf. our well-spoken=well-speaking. On the formation of such compounds, see Franz §138.

1972 night]
Rudd (1979, p. 184): Night, which is mentioned several times by Ovid, is not brought on stage by Shakespeare, but in [1972 ff.] it does receive from the hero a passionate apostrophe:…This is rather odd. In Ovid it is morning when the lovers plan their elopement [4.81 f.; cf. Golding, 4.101 f.; see ].

1974 O…alacke]
Phillips (ed. 1969): It is tempting to imagine that Pyramus has forgotten his words.

1974 alacke]
See n. 808.

1975 I…forgot]
Pyramus’s doubt is not paralleled in the sources and analogues listed , but seems to echo Lysander’s need for reassurance. See n. 190.

1976, 1978 thou…Thou]
OED (pers. pron. 1b), quoting 1978. Here used in poetic entreaty; see OED 1. headnote. OED is misleading in placing this quotation among examples of reproach or contempt. Contrast 36 and n.

1976 ô sweete…wall]
Halliwell (ed. 1856), preferring Qq to F (see textual n.): The repetition of the vocative case is of frequent occurrence in Elizabethan writers. Thus Gascoigne, in his translation of the Jocasta of Euripides, 1566 [A Hundreth Sundrie Flowres, 1573, X2r-v] paraphrases this brief sentence of the original,—O mother, O wife most wretched, into,—O wife, O mother, O both wofull names, O wofull mother, and O wofull wyfe! O woulde to God, alas! O woulde to God, Thou nere had bene my mother, nor my wyfe! Arnold (1911, p. 37): It does not seem unlikely that the burlesque apostrophes to the wall…were suggested by Golding’s translation of Ovid [4.91; see ]; see also n. 2074–85, 2089–99, 2116–31. Cf. A gorgious Gallery (1578, O2r): (O happy wall) mayst thou so blist be made.

1976 wall]
Goldstein (1973, pp. 193–4) considers wall to signify female genitalia and to be the major symbolic word in the interlude. See also Parker at n. 1959.

1977 standst]
See textual n. Thiselton (1903, p. 73): For stands see Abbott §340, which might well be extended to cover instances like the present. The difficulty of enunciating Q1’s standst may contribute to a comic effect.

1979–83 Showe…mee]
Frame (1999, p. 58), arguing that scopophilia is present in the play, suggests that in these lines Sh. equates the wall’s hole to the voyeur’s camera.

1979 Showe…chinke]
Gerstner-Hirzel (1957, p. 47): Snout probably puts his one arm akimbo…and Pyramus stoops to look through. Leech (1970, p. 1516), in trying to establish a pun on chincks in Rom. 1.5.117 (698), quotes MND 1932–3, 1958–61, 1978–9, and 1991–5 as evidence of bawdy meaning; drawing on his argument, Shand (1971, pp. 126–7) argues, despite 880–1, that the gesture called for here is not with the fingers, but with spread legs, thus giving added point to the other bawdy puns in the passages cited by Leech. Others who have elaborated on this suggestion, usually without reference to Shand, include Clayton (1974, pp. 101–13), Foster (1984, pp. 14–15), Meagher (1997, p. 53; see reference to Shand p. 220 n.) See also nn. 880, 880–1, 2003–4.

1979 blink]
Johnson (1755, 2), quoting this instance: To see obscurely. Deighton (ed. 1891): Peep through. See OED, v. 4, quoting this instance first. Muir (1954, p. 145) finds an echo of A gorgious Gallery (1578, O2r): And scarcely then her pearcing looke, one blinke therof had got, But that firme hope of good successe, within her fancy shot.

1979 eyne]
Variant spelling of archaic plural of eye. See n. 256.

1980 Thankes]
Holland (ed. 1994): The extent of Bottom’s gratitude…will depend on how quickly Wall remembers to show his chink.

1980 curteous wall]
Muir (1954, p. 143) quotes Golding’s Ovid 4.95–7, where the lovers thank the wall for this same piece of courtesie; see .

1980 Ioue…thee]
See n. 841.

1982 wicked wall]
Steevens (ed. 1778) compares Chaucer, LGW 756: Alas thou wicked wal; see . Muir (1954, p. 146 n. 11) notes the phrase wicked cruell wall also occurs in A gorgious Gallery (1578, O3v). Taylor (1989, p. 318) asserts that the passage in A gorgious Gallery derives from Chaucer, and that Sh. is indebted to the later poem, not to Chaucer; his argument is contested by Lynch (1999, p. 111).

1983 Curst…stones]
Andrews (ed. 1989): This phrase unintentionally calls the audience’s attention to the stones (testicles) of the actor playing Wall. OED, Stone, n. 11a. See nn. 1993, 1994. In A gorgious Gallery (1578, O3v) Pyramus addresses the wall’s cursed scyte [i.e. site]. See also nn. 1993, 2003–4.

1984–90 The.…comes]
Willcock (1960 [1934], pp. 120–1): The joking comments of the polite audience are not asides conventionally inaudible to the victims; they are heard and sometimes replied to.

1984 mee thinkes]
See n. 6.

1984 sensible]
Schmidt (1875, 2): Capable of perception, endowed with feeling. Andrews (ed. 1989): Theseus puns on two meanings: (a) being sentient (able to feel, as if it were a living thing), and (b) being intelligent and responsive.

1984 should]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Ought, might be expected to.

1985 again]
Franz (§433): In return. Blake (5.1.3.5 (i)): In reply.

1986–90 No…comes]
No commentator has attempted to explain the verse lineation in Q1–Rowe; all editors silently follow Pope (ed. 1725). See textual n. Irving & Marshall (ed. 1888): This speech of Bottom’s, in which he forgets all about his assumed character, and answers directly the critical observation of Theseus, is a very humorous touch; his intense self-consciousness will not let him be quiet. Vickers (1968, p. 66): Bottom puts aside the tortured rhetoric of his Pyramus and answers with his own quite personal blend of simplicity and unshakeable confidence.

1986 he…not]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Bottom, taking Theseus’ words seriously, replies, No, that is not in his part.

1988, 1990 fall…you]
Thiselton (1903, p. 73), discussing the period after fall in F (see textual n.): Pat as I told you is certainly better disjoined from fall, as, so treated, it imports a don’t-I-know-all-about-it air, which is quite in keeping with Bottom’s characteristic vanity.

1990 Pat]
See n. 815.

1990 yonder]
See n. 1084.

1991 full…mones]
Brooks (ed. 1979) compares Golding’s Ovid 4.104, after much complaint and mone; see .

1992 For parting]
Deighton (ed. 1891): On account of your separating. Baker (ed. 1896): Because you have parted.

1993–4 My…knit]
Brooks (ed. 1979) compares Chaucer LGW 768, 760–1; see . Farrell (1989, p. 177), equating the wall with patriarchal opposition: The obscene joke insinuates the daughter’s submissive love for the father. Webb (1989, p. 69): Possibly—stones/testicles; hair = pubic; lime/semen. The extent of the bawdy in the burlesque scene however is uncertain.See also nn. 1931, 1983, 2003–4.

1993 cherry lips]
See n. 1165. OED, Cherry, n. 5a quotes as the first figurative instance Sidney’s Arcadia: She spake to us all, opening the cherry of her lips (ed. Evans, 1977, p. 62). Cf. n. 2120. There may be a suggestion of the game of cherry-pit, throwing a stone into a hole, sometimes used in analogy of sexual intercourse: see Webb (1991, pp. 84–5).

1993 stones]
