Johnson (1755, Stone 7): Testicle (OED 11a). For this and other Shn. puns on stones see Williams (Glossary, 1997), on stone, chink, and nose.

1994 knit]
OED (1a): To bind, attach, join; (5a): To make compact or firm. See also nn. 183, 699. Clayton (1979, p. 7) implausibly suggests a connection with knit as a method of gelding rams by tying the scrotum (see OED 1c).

1994 knit now againe]
See textual n. Collier (ed. 1858): Knit UP IN THEE…is the preferable reading of the folio: the quartos have knit now again, which does not preserve the intended rhyme. Most editors have agreed, and have not attempted alternative emendations. White (ed. 1858) finds evidence in the variant that the copy of Q2 furnished as copy to the printers of the folio, had been corrected either by Shakespeare or some one else in his theatre. Wells & Taylor (1987, p. 285): Perhaps againe is a misreading of a word ending -nie, -mie, or -rie. Possibly knit now awrie would provide a sufficient near-rhyme and almost-sense.

1995–6 I see…face]
Theobald (1909, pp. 259–60): Figurative transposition of the function of the senses is classically derived,—I hear Leander swimming and the lamp (burning). [Musæus], De Herone et Leandro 5). This somewhat strained metaphor must not be confounded with a similar extravagance put into the mouth of Bottom…, where it simply is due to the rustic ignorance of the speaker,…That the passage in Musæus was, however, present to the writer’s mind is, I think, evident, from the following words of Bottom [quotes 1958–9].…It is conceivable, too, that in the word Limander we have an indication that Bottom (the writer that is to say) recollected that in the poem of Musæus the first syllable of the name is indifferently short, or long, being in the latter case spelt Leiander: At length she addressed her sweet voice to Leiander. (Line 172.) Another instance of this inverted metaphor may be quoted from Musæus,—For the tongue of men is fond of mocking, and hears, where three roads meet, what anyone in silence does. (Line 183.) The explanation of this curious expression the tongue hears, seems to be that the tongue is personified, and, as such, is only capable of delivering what it has seen or heard. Langley (1991, p. 122–3), quoting these lines, comments that Sh. puts the figure of Hypallage called by Puttenham (1589; 1936, pp. 171–3) the Changeling frequently into Bottom’s mouth: the mouth, that is, of someone who is a changeling himself, and the changeling for a changeling in the shape of the little changeling boy [495]. O’Connell (2000, p. 132): Bottom’s synesthesia extends into the performance of Pyramus and Thisbe: [quotes 1995–6, 1999, 2135–6]. To object to Bottom’s verbs would be to align oneself with the lame inability of the court to enter into theatrical play and, more importantly, to misunderstand the way they define precisely the phenomenon of theater. Bicks (2003, p. 87), with depressing earnestness: Pyramus encounters profound sensual confusion at the hole…The chink was a common early modern term for the space behind the hymen.…To see through the chink is to have an unobstructed view of the space beyond the unreliable hymen; in the tale of Pyramus and Thisbe, however, the chink and the Wall that reveals it confound rather than clarify male access to the text of the female body. Cf. nn. 1236–7, 1737–40.

1995 will I]
For ellipsis after will when followed by a preposition of motion, see Abbott §405.

1996 and]
OED (conj.1 C.4): If, whether, quoting this use first. See also n. 313.

1997–2002 My…you]
Carroll (1985, p. 164): Each pair of lovers…represents a different stage of sexual completion: Pyramus and Thisbe never sexually unite, but die together; Hero and Leander, if that is who Helen and Limander are, die after their union; and Cephalus and Procris were married, but jealousy destroyed them.

1997 My…thinke.]
Evans (ed. 1974): The Q1 punctuation is here retained, although it doth not stand upon points. Holland (ed. 1994): Q1’s punctuation…may represent another of the workmen having trouble with punctuation. See textual n.

1998 Thinke…wilt]
Brooks (ed. 1979), citing Tilley (T27, Take it as you will): A cliché. Holland (ed. 1994) cites also Tilley (T28): Take it or leave it. See also Dent, questioningly, (T221.1): Think what you will.

1998 thy…Grace]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Your graceful lover. Kittredge (ed. 1939): Thy lover’s beautiful self. Brooks (ed. 1979): Comic version of an honorific title: a duke might be styled my lord’s grace; see n. 47, but see also OED, Grace, n. 16a. Cf. nn. 219, 744.

1999–2000 Limander…Helen]
Thirlby (MS 1725–33): An affected blunder for Leander, & in the next line Helen the same for Hero. Capell (1783, 2.3:116): This Limander should be Paris, by the lady he is coupl’d with: and he is call’d by his other name, Alexander; corrupted into Alisander, (as in [LLL 5.2.564 (2515)]) and Lisander, which master Bottom may be allow’d to make Limander of. Blackstone (in Halliwell, ed. 1856): Limander stands evidently for Leander, but how came Helen to be coupled with him? Might it not have originally bee wrote Heren, which is as ridiculous a corruption of Hero, as the other is of her lover. Neil (ed. 1878): Marlowe’s paraphrase of the Greek of [the pseudo?] Musæus, left unfinished at his death, 1st June 1593, was completed by George Chapman, and published by Edward Blunt in 1598. It is probable, however, that Shakespeare had seen the story in a native dress before Marlowe’s version was published; for Abraham Fleming, in his translation of Virgil’s Georgics [Bucoliks] (1589), states in a marginal note [G1r] that a dozen years ago (1577) he had issued an English version of a Historie of Leander and Hero, in which title we have the order of the names employed here. Flügel (1889, p. 16) notes that Hero’s story follows Tysbe’s in Christine de Pisan’s Cyte of ladyes (tr. B. Anslay, 1521); he claims that her lover is here called Ilander, but he may have misread the text’s no less odd name Hander (Q1r-Q2v) (Ger.). Rudd (1979, p. 181) refers to the story of Leander in Ovid’s Heroides 18 and 19; (p. 238 n. 18) he dismisses the connection Limander/Alexander/Paris as too arcane. Clayton (1979, p. 9): Has Quince, in frantic efforts to correct error, been desperately putting Thisbe right with lime and hair, only to have Pyramus pick this up and make Leander Limander? Parker (1982, p. 101) sees irony in the mistaken reference to the unfaithful Helen of Troy and suggests an unconscious reference to the inconstant Lysander and the faithful Helena. See also Theobald at n. 1995–6.

1999 still]
See n. 206.

2000 Helen]
Holzknecht & McClure (ed. 1937): Hardly a model of constancy and fidelity. Kittredge (ed. 1939): Helen…was far from trusty; but the speaker means Hero.

2000 fates]
See OED, Fate, n. 2b, quoting this instance first. See nn. 300, 2084.

2001 Shafalus…Procrus]
Thirlby (MS 1725–33): For Cephalus and Procris. Steevens (ed. 1778) first noticed A booke entitled, Procris & Cephalus, divided into four parts, entered on the Stationer’s Register Oct 22, 1593, which he believed might be a play ridiculed by Sh. Malone (ed. 1790, 10:580) points out that this is a poem, but not dramatick, as has been suggested. It was not, however by Henry Chute, as Malone believed, but by Thomas Edwards, printed in 1595 (see Elizabeth Story Donno, Elizabethan Minor Epics, 1963, pp. 12, 155–79). Halliwell (ed. 1856) notes that the poem is alluded to in Nash’s Have with You to Saffron Walden, 1596 [O2r-v], where it is attributed to Chute. Neil (ed. 1878): The legend of these lovers true is contained in varying forms in the 25th of the Fragments of Pherecydes [Atheniensis], in the Bibliotheca of Apollodorus of Athens, iii, 15, and in Ovid’s Metamorphoses, [7.665–865]; cf. Golding, 7.853–1117, summarized . Bush (1932, p. 59) instances Howell’s The lamentable historie of Sephalus with the Unfortunat end of Procris. To the tune of Appelles (Newe Sonets, 2nd ed. 1570, F1v–3r). Blits (2003, p. 182): Unlike Helen and Paris, Cephalus and Procris were faithful, but they falsely and, finally, fatally distrusted each other. Pyramus and Thisbe mean them as a model of trustiness…but their story is, above all, a cautionary tale of lovers’ distrust.

2003–4 O…all]
Ritson (in Steevens, ed. 1793) compares Golding’s Ovid 4.99–100; see . Brooks (ed. 1979) compares also Chaucer, LGW 760–1, 768; see . Bristol (1985, p. 177): Bottom (as Pyramus) and Flute (dressed as a woman—Thisby) mime the attitudes of romantic love and restate its idealizations in coarse bodily language. Engle (1993, p. 141) sees in these lines a staging of the bawdy climax of Chaucer’s Miller’s Tale, 3732–5. Glasgow (1995, p. 126) compares to the emphatically bawdy treatment of the episode in Gryphius’s Peter Squenz, which positively relishes the crass ambiguity of rods, shafts and orifices (quotes from Act 3; cf. Brennecke, 1964, pp. 90–1).

2003–4 hole…walles hole]
Colman (1974, p. 28) believes hole is chosen by Sh. for deliberate bawdy effect, but resists the suggestion that they kiss through Wall’s spread legs (see n. 1979): Flute’s line about kissing the wall’s hole surely works best as a verbal infelicity, unintended by its speaker. Cf. OED, Hole, n. 8; also Florio (1598, Malforo): A mischievous hole, taken for a womans priuities; and (Forame): A hole. Also taken for the arse. See also n. 1959.

2003–4 vilde]
See n. 2090.

2005–7 Wilt…delay]
Ritson (in Steevens ed. 1793) compares Golding’s Ovid, 4.108; see . See also n. 910.

2005 Ninnies tombe]
Cf. 910, 911. Despite the Prologue’s correct pronunciation at 1937, Bottom prefers Thisby’s, which she repeats at 2063. Wilson (ed. 1924): The tomb contains two ninnies before the end of the scene.

2005–6 straight way]
See n. 1056.

2007 Tide]
OED (v.1 1): To happen, befall = BETIDE v. Delius (ed. 1859): May life or death befall me (Ger.). Foakes (ed. 1984): Compare the common proverb Come what come may (Tilley C529).

2008–9 Thus…goe]
Hasler (1974, pp. 89–90): His exit is prompted by a perfectly superfluous couplet in the best illusion-destroying vein:…He is obviously glad that his task is accomplished and he is allowed to withdraw. Only the last thus is a text-direction for the walking and speaking Wall, and draws our attention to these his incongruous faculties once more. The first line, with its abstract Thus duplicated by the redundant rhymeword so, is one deep sigh of relief.

2008 my…so]
Wiles (1993, p. 59) argues unconvincingly that these words confirm by innuendo the phallic implications of Snout’s name [= nozzle; see DP n. 14]. For a similar interpretation, see Mousley (2000, p. 86).

2008 discharged]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Enacted; see also n. 354.

2009 being done]
Deighton (ed. 1891): The part being played. Baker (ed. 1896): Now that the part has been performed.

2009 [Exit Clow.]
See textual n. Wells & Taylor (1987, p. 285): F’s Clow. presumably stands for all three actors, even though Bottom and Flute almost certainly begin their exit before Snout. Holland (ed. 1994), agreeing, gives an exit for Bottom and Flute after 2007.

2010 Moon vsed]
Johnson (ed. 1888, p. xvi): The agreement of [the Qq] gives a strong presumption in favour of the correctness of a reading. Something besides can be said for the reasonableness of this passage.…The Prologue had announced [quotes 1934–7]. The Enterlude then proceeded as far as this agreement between Pyramus and Thisbe to meet at the tomb, and Wall, who had served between the two neighbors, makes his explanation and leaves the stage. Thereupon the Duke says that now, in accordance with the statement of the Prologue, the Moon will be used between the two neighbors, probably in some such ingenuous way as the Wall had been. Watt (ed. 1906): This, though awkward, gives sense. Theseus, on seeing the Wall go off, remarks that Moonshine must now take up the part of confidant between the neighbours. Demetrius replies that this is inevitable, because this wall has proved that it has ears by its sudden response either to Pyramus, or to a call from the prompter to tell it to go off, and can no longer be trusted. Wilson (ed. 1924): Cf. [1936–7] By moonshine did these lovers think no scorn To meet at Ninus’ tomb, there, there to woo. Has Theseus some other meaning also? Demetrius’ words seem to be a reply to a jest of some kind. Ridley (ed. 1934): I prefer to leave the Q reading, little sense though it makes, partly because Demetrius’ answer, with its natural emphasis on walls, suggests that Theseus was talking of something else. Phillips (ed. 1969): An obscure phrase. Perhaps Theseus means that the moonlight is all they have for a partition now—he knows Moonshine is to appear in the action. Farrow (ed. 1972): The Moon is the only go-between left. Evans (ed. 1974): Moonshine, Wall being down, will now come into play. Hyland (ed. 1989): Theseus jokes that the moon has been used up. Wiles (1993, p. 59), questionably, argues that the Qq reading makes perfect sense in this homosexual context [of phallic and anal puns]. There appears to have been a stock joke about the Man in the Moon being sexually in the moon. The kiss through the wall becomes an image of homosexual sterility, a comic inversion of the fruitful heterosexual love of the real bride and groom.
F’s morall downe (see textual n.) has dissatisfied editors and commentators nearly as much as the Qq reading, but some variant on it has been more often defended and explicated. The most fruitful suggestion was mure all down from Theobald (1726, pp. 141–2), rapidly merged with the conjecture mural from Pope (ed. 1725). Capell (1783, 2.3:116) argues in support, but Collier (ed. 1852, p. 100) objects no instance has been adduced of the employment of mural as a substantive; Singer (1853, p. 31) counters the same objection might be made to several other words peculiar to Shakespeare, but see OED, Mural, n. 1 Obs., with examples ranging from 1471–1555, and dismissing the MND conjecture as doubtful. White (ed. 1858) supported moral down because Shakespeare evidently thought that it would be plainer if the wall were represented both as the restraint upon the passions of the lovers, and as a pander to them; but also considered a possible pun on mural, and in the supplementary notes to vol. 12 (1866) retracted: The Note upon this passage assumes too subtle a meaning. Mural is probably right; or perhaps moral is a misprint for wall. (cf. Collier, ed. 1858, pointing to his old corrector’s insertion of wall). Simrock (1850, p. 10) says of the wall in the Pyramus and Thisbe story: With the ancients, a moral obstacle is invariably concealed by the material one presented (quoted Neil, ed. 1878). Thiselton (1903, p. 74), citing Lysander’s remark at 1918–19 and Bottom’s unconscious hint that the wall will fall in 1989, and suggesting wordplay on through/true and down/done: The morall is done, because the prophecy has reached fulfilment: it is also at an end, for, the mural being down, there is nothing left to speak through, or true. Possibly the significance of Theseus’ words has not yet been exhausted if to do the moral had a special meaning. Nor is White’s first notion of the wall being restraint on the passions of the lovers to be lightly rejected: cf. what Sidney wrote of Valour in Valour anatomised in a Fancie, Nothing draws a Woman like to it. Nothing is more behoveful for that Sex: for from it they receive Protection, and in a free way too, without any danger. Nothing makes a shorter cut to obtaining: for a Man of Arms is alwayes void of Ceremony, which is the Wall that stands betwixt Piramus and Thisby, that is Man, and Woman (see The Prose Works, ed. Albert Feuillerat, Cambridge, 1912. Rpt. 1963, 3.308). Ellis (1973, pp. 164–5) finds a pun on the fungus morel which resembles the male member closely enough to have caused botanists to classify it among the phallales; and hence feels little doubt that the moral which is down between the two neighbors is a phallic-metaphorical morel. Andrews (ed. 1989): This phrase…seems to be a pun on moral and mural (wall), and thus a witty Partition of the Duke’s (see [1968]). Parker (Teaching, 1994, pp. 206–14) explores the possible plays on words in moral/mural, seeing links to the Hymeneal wall; Latin mora delay; morus, mora, and moriens in Ovid compared with Golding; Ovid moralisé; biblical echoes; Spenser’s FQ. The discussion is richly wide-ranging, but sometimes farfetched: see further Parker (1996, p. 275, and 2001, pp. 55–6).
Some attempt bibliographic explanations of the textual problems. Wright (ed. 1877) speculates that Qq offers a corrupted stage direction, and that the F reading results from imperfect MS correction. Sisson (1956, 1:132) suggests that vsed is a misreading by the compositor of to see. Brooks (1970, pp. 125–7; ed. 1979) argues that the autograph copy read mure rased, misread by the compositor of Q1 as Moon vsed; the correction in prompt copy of Q2 to wall downe was then misread by the compositor of F to intend morall downe.

2012–13 No…warning]
Kenrick (1765, p. 22): That is, so wilful as to take their cue before it be given them. Farmer (in Steevens, ed. 1773, 10:Oo8v-Pp1): Alludes to the proverb, Walls have ears [Tilley (W19)]. A wall between almost any two neighbours would soon be down, were it to exercise this faculty without previous warning. Deighton (ed. 1891): Nothing else could be done than to throw walls down when they take to overhearing in this clandestine way, without giving any warning of their presence as an honourable person would do rather than overhear a secret conversation; an allusion to the proverb Walls have ears, i.e. it is not safe to tell a secret when some one may be concealed behind a wall and overhear it. Thiselton (1903, p. 74): Wall…makes off incontinently, leaving the lovers standing for a moment together without any partition between them. Kittredge (ed. 1939): Demetrius intends to suggest that it was Wall’s duty to warn the lovers’ parents of the meeting planned at the tomb. Garrison (1994, p. 153) sees the abundant alliteration as part of the commentary by the aristocratic audience on the rhetorical artifices of the interlude.

2012–13 so…to]
For the omission of as see Abbott §281.

2012–13 wilfull]
OED (a.1 3), quoting this instance: Willing; consenting; ready to comply. Obs.; but see (1a): Obstinately self-willed or perverse. Brooks (ed. 1979): Forward.

2013 heare]
See textual n. and conj. . Warburton (ed. 1747) was supported by Grey (1754, 1:75–6) by reference to an anecdote when a wall was built overnight on a hint from Queen Elizabeth. Wells & Taylor (1987, p. 286) find leave, conjectured by Gould (1884, p. 16), palaeographically attractive, but not entirely convincing.

2014 Dutch.]
For the shift in SPs because of type-shortage, see Kennedy (1998, p. 186).

2014–19 This…men]
Mackenzie (1924, p. 34): Hippolyta’s rather sef-consciously superior boredom over the joyous tragedy…contrasts…with the amused and tolerant courtesy of her bridegroom, and smacks a little of a Girtonian of the earnest nineties. Berry (Explorations, 1972, p. 109): In the immediate context, Theseus is deftly winning an argument by turning Hippolyta’s own words against her. But the words gather to themselves an easy, symbolic weight. Shadows brings all together; it means actors, but it merges with night and dreams. It touches, perhaps, the softly-iterated nothings that measure the final scene: [quotes 1875, 1885–6, 2101–2].

2014 This…heard]
Seiden (1959, p. 86): What Hippolyta says of the mechanics’ mutilated mummery is precisely what any groundling, earth-bound by an overly literal mind, might say of Shakespeare’s play.…Shakespeare’s rhetoric is of course ad feminam: Hippolyta is made to seem as silly as the play she scorns. Spisak (1983, p. 94), however, believes she is made nervous by what she sees, try though she does to dismiss it as silly. The discomfort she feels is a sure guide to the audience’s final response.

2015–19 The…men]
Maginn (1837, p. 371): Theseus’s remarks were intended [by Sh.] as an excuse for the want of effect upon the stage of some of the finer touches of such dramatists as himself, and an appeal to all true judges of poetry to bring it before the tribunal of their own imagination; making but a matter of secondary inquiry how it appears in a theatre, as delivered by those who, whatever others may think of them, would, if taken at their own estimation, pass for excellent men. Dowden (1875, p. 70) feels that Maginn has missed the dramatic appropriateness: To Theseus, the great man of action, the worst and the best of these shadowy representations are all one. He graciously lends himself to be amused; Sh. does not endorse this attitude, but does agree that it is the business of the dramatist to set the spectator’s imagination to work,…and that the co-operation of the spectator with the poet is necessary. For the method of Bottom and his company is precisely the reverse,….They are determined to leave nothing to be supplied by the imagination.

2015–16 The…them]
Ruskin (The Eagle’s Nest 1887, Works 22:152): That is Shakespeare’s judgment of his own art.…I hope…you will learn this entire sentence by heart, being, as it is, a faultless and complete epitome of the laws of mimetic art. Irving & Marshall (ed. 1888): This speech of Theseus should serve as a motto, and as a philosophical consolation, to those who, in search of amusement, are induced to witness a more or less inadequate performance. Verity (ed. 1893) compares the apology for inadequacies of the stage in the Prologue of H5. Chesterton (1920, p. 136) calls the comment a noble scrap of sceptical magnanimity…of some kindly but cultivated squire, who will not pretend to his guests that the play is good, but who will not let the actors see he thinks it bad. Wilson (ed. 1924): With this reference to players, cf. the fling at poets [1799–1800, 1804–9]. Both are placed in the mouth of Theseus the practical man. Both are probably 1598 additions to the text. Brown (1949, p. 241): Theseus speaks for William Shakespeare’s occasional distrust of plays and players when these are matched with the great actualities of public life and of real action in the forum of the world. Holland (ed. 1994): While the description of performers as shadows is conventional, the discussion between Truth and Poetry in the prologue to The True Tragedy of Richard the Third (1594) seems particularly potent here: Truth.…what makes thou upon a stage? Poetry. Shadows. Truth. Then will I add bodies to the shadows. Therefore depart and give Truth leave To show her pageant (ed. W. W. Greg (Malone Society; Oxford, 1929), ll. 8–12).

2015 in…kinde]
Rann (ed. 1787): Of actors. Deighton (ed. 1891): I.e. dramatic representations. Kittredge (ed. 1939): In this genre—i.e., the best plays. Evans (ed. 1974): Of this profession, i.e. actors.

2015 shadowes]
OED (6b), citing this instance and 2207: Applied rhetorically…to an actor or a play in contrast with the reality represented. Hense (1851, p. 38 n.) considered the appropriate German translation to be Schattenspiel, shadowplay (Ger.). Holzknecht & McClure (ed. 1937): With a possible allusion to shadow puppets. Andrews (ed. 1989): Illusions, insubstantial things. Greenblatt (ed. 1997): Mere likenesses without substance. See also 1388, 2207 and nn.

2016 amend them]
Baker (ed. 1896): Aid them, supply their deficiencies.

2017 It…theirs]
Kittredge (ed. 1939): Thus Hippolyta interprets the words of Theseus—she does not contradict them. He might have answered: That is just what I mean—the imagination of the spectator. Frye (1983, p. 79): Here imagination is approaching its more modern meaning of creative power which Hippolyta ascribes in this creative sense to the audience and not to the dramatist.

2018–19 If…men]
Leinwand (1986, p. 23): Their pride in their manhood, as well as their anxiety, is merely patronized by Theseus.…For Theseus, their manhood, like their childishness, is contingent upon him.

2019 passe for]
Schmidt (1875, vb. 5 Fig.c.): To be current, to be regarded or estimated.

2019–20 Here…Lyon]
Most editorial comment concerns the placement of the comma, and whether man should be emended; see textual n., and conj. .

2019–20 Here…in]
Most editors agree with Heath (1765, p. 58): The sense requires a comma after the particle, in; for the construction is, Here come in two noble beasts. See textual n. Malone (1783, p. 11) differs: I believe our author wrote Here come two noble beasts: e’en, &c. So, in As you like it: Here comes a pair of very strange beasts, &c. not—here comes in—. Without finding emendation necessary, Wright (ed. 1877) substantially agrees with Malone: In here signifies in the character of: see [1770]. Similarly, Sisson (1956, 1:132): In…in effect means to wit or viz.

2019–20 man]
Theobald (ed. 1733) first proposed to read Moon, on the basis of the immediately following SD (see textual n.). Harness (ed. 1825; 1830) objected that Theseus could not yet have identified Moon, but Scott (ed. 1898) and Thiselton (1903, p. 75) point out that Moon had already been identified by his attributes in 1934–5. Malone (ed. 1790) considers the change unnecessary: The conceit is furnished by the person who represents the lion, and enters covered with the hide of that beast; and Theseus only means to say, that the man who represented the moon, and came in at the same time, with a lantern in his hand, and a bush of thorns at his back, was as much a beast as he who performed the part of the lion. Wilson (ed. 1924): Mod. edd. have hesitated to adopt Theobald’s emendation, but it effects so great an improvement in the text that it seems to us certain;…Theseus has just remarked that Quince’s company of actors may pass for excellent men, and the jest in the sudden transition from this to here come two noble beasts in would be blunted if we read a man and a lion. Sisson (1956, 1:132): To read man involves us in accepting that Theseus means the man-in-the-moon, and that the whole line means, here come two noble beasts, two men, one in a man and one in a lion’s skin, which seems very weak indeed. It is surely more amusing, and more probable, that Theseus links up lion and moon as two noble beasts.
Furness (ed. 1895): Possibly the choice between man and moon will lie in the degree of absurdity which strikes us in calling either the one or the other a beast. Scott (ed. 1898): No one has remarked on the fact that a dog, as well as a lion, enters.

2022 Ladies…feare]
Bates (ed. 1895): And one of these ladies has been the Queen of the Amazons. Chambers (ed. 1897): The lion’s part is after all more than roaring…But this was necessitated by Bottom’s proposal in [847]. See 838–56 and nn.

2023 smallest monstrous]
Deighton (ed. 1891): An intentional contradiction in terms. Brooks (ed. 1979): Monstrous = (1) enormous; yet to the ladies the smallest mouse is (2) like a terrifying monster. See n. 314.

2025 Lyon]
For omission of the article, see n. 1942, 1945, 1947.

note.p:
These lines elicited varying interpretations and emendations. See textual n. and conj., . The two most frequent are:
(1) as [I am] Snug the joyner [I] am [neither] a Lyon fell, nor else no Lyon’s damme. Cf. Ritson (1783, p. 49): I am neither lion nor lioness, I am Snug the joiner, and Malone (ed. 1790): I am Snug the joiner, and neither a lion, nor a lion’s dam; both Ritson and Malone read F’s one for Q’s as. The variant does not substantially change the interpretation offered, which depends on recognizing what Ritson observed: The use of the single negative with a double aspect is common with our author (Abbott §396 Ellipsis of Neither before Nor. Blake 5.3.1. (d) (iv) (e).).
(2) Sisson (1956, 1:133): It is only as Snug the joiner playing his part that I am a terrible lion, and not otherwise. Doran (ed. 1959): Only as Snug the joiner am I either a fierce lion or a lioness. Brooks (ed. 1979): It is as Snug that he is a lion, and in no other way is he a lion’s dam. All adhere to Q, but appear influenced by Field (1845, pp. 60–1) who proposes taking fell as a substantive meaning skin and reading either lion’s fell or lion-fell. Field paraphrases: I, Snug the joiner, am only a lion’s skin; nor any otherwise than as a lion’s skin may be said to be pregnant with a lion, am I the mother of one; he is enthusiastically supported by Furness (ed. 1895): For Snug to say that he is neither a lion nor a lioness is, to me, pointless, but all is changed if we suppose him to say that he is a lion’s skin, and only because, as such, he encloses a lion, can he be a lioness. Cuningham (ed. 1905): No doubt Shakespeare intended a quibble between fell skin and fell fierce. Evans (ed. 1974) sees in the quibble an unintentionally humorous reference to Snug’s costume.

White (1993, p. 92), discussing Karl Marx’s reference to these lines, believes that he shows an understanding of Sh.’s simultaneous awareness of actor and role which sometimes becomes deliberately discrepant. Cf. 847–56 and nn.

div.note:
2027 Lyon fell]
If fell is taken to be a substantive, then Snug in a lion’s fell, or skin, is like the proverb An ass in a lion’s skin [Tilley (A351)], the title of one of Aesop’s fables. If adjective, then compare La conusaunce damours (1528?, b2v) where Pyramus calls on ye moost cruell and rabbysshe lions fell to come and teare his corps. For noun compounds, such as this may be, see n. 184.

2027 fell]
Johnson (1755): 1. Cruel; barbarous; inhuman. 2. Savage; ravenous; bloody. See also n. 390.

2027 Lyons damme]
Thiselton (1903, p. 75) suggests a reminiscence of the Ovidian lioness that scares Thisbe (Met. 4.97; Golding 4.120; see ). Franke (1979, pp. 282–4) questioning the emphasis on male rather than female, pursues a pun on snug/snag to formulate the hypothesis that…Shakespeare has Snug protest to the ladies that he is not really a lion, but only playing one, while at the same time he has him unconsciously assure them of his sexual prowess as a male. The apparent confusion may have been suggested by the shifting gender of the beast in earlier versions: see n. 327. There may also be a jesting pun on lion’s dam = lioness = whore: cf. Honigmann (ed. Jn., 1954, at 2.1.291 [598]).

2028–30 For…conscience]
Ruskin (St. Mark’s Rest 1877, Works 24:346) compares the fanciful spirit of these lines with Carpaccio’s St. Jerome and the Lion, where the painter drew his delicately smiling lion with his head on one side like a Perugino’s saint, and his left paw raised, partly to show the thorn wound, partly in deprecation.

2028 in strife]
OED (Strife 2a), quoting this instance last: In a state of discord or contention. Obs.

2029 ’twere…life]
Delius (ed. 1859): Perhaps should not be connected with pity, but should be taken as a protestation: by my life (Ger.). Gill (ed. 1981): I would have to ask you to have pity on my life (because he would have deserved punishment). McDonald (ed. 2000): I’d sooner die than frighten you into thinking I am really a lion. Cf. 853 and n.

2030 gentle]
Kittredge (ed. 1939): Gentlemanly, polite. Cf. n. 954.

2030 conscience]
Baker (ed. 1896): Shown by his letting the audience know that they need not fear him. Andrews (ed. 1993): Both (a) moral sensibility, and (b) consciousness, intellect.

2031 best…beast]
White (ed. 1858): From the nature of this speech, it is plain that best and beast were pronounced alike. Furness (ed. 1895) demurs: This is stated…too strongly in a matter which is difficult of proof. Kittredge (ed. 1939): A desperate pun. Sisson (1956, 1:133): I have…found an Elizabethan witness in Chancery deposing in 1595 that a certain Captain Thomas Best or Beast was well named, being a beast both by name and by nature: …as Best was called Best so he had played the beast in his vsage in that voyage [C24/245/20]. Similarly Thomas Nest, a gentleman of Worcester, in 1614 signs his name as Thomas Neast [C24/414/63]. Andrews (ed. 1989): Demetrius puns on bestial. Cercignani (1981, p. 168) asserts that here and in MV 1.2.88–9 (278–9) the word-play depends on antithesis rather than identity.

2032–5 This…the goose]
Wright (ed. 1877): Valour and discretion are associated as in the proverb [1H4 5.4.119–20 (3085)]: The better part of valour is discretion [Tilley (D354)]. Foakes (ed. 1984) on the attributes of lion, fox and goose cites Tilley (L308): As fierce (valiant) as a lion, (F645): He that will deceive the fox must rise betimes, (F647): An old fox cannot be taken by a snare, (F659): The fox’s wiles will never enter into the lion’s head, (G346): As dizzy as a goose, (G348): As wise as a goose. Holland (ed. 1994), comparing also Tilley (L319, If the lion’s skin cannot the fox’s shall): The joke is complicated, confusing and laboured; it is abandoned by Theseus at [2037–8]. Cf. Cor. 1.1.171–2 (182–3): Where he should finde you Lyons, findes you Hares: Where Foxes, Geese you are.

2032 very]
See n. 419 (very).

2032 fox…valour]
Kittredge (ed. 1939): As to valour, he is more crafty than brave (since he is afraid of giving offence). Brooks (ed. 1979): This lion is found wanting as a king of beasts: princes, wrote Machiavelli, should combine the qualities of lion and fox. For this criterion in Shakespeare, see Tillyard, Shakespeare’s History Plays [1944], pp. 186, 227.

2033 discretion]
OED (2): Discrimination. Obs.; (3): Discernment. Obs. See n. 343. Hillman (1996, pp. 82–3) explores the social and political implications of the word as used here, linking it with disfigure; cf. 59, 871 and nn.

2034–5 Not…goose]
Deighton (ed. 1891): That simile will not hold, for if he were like a fox in point of valour, and a goose in point of discretion, his valour ought to be able to carry his discretion, as the fox carries the goose; carry in the former case being used in the sense of be equal to the burden of, in the latter of bear away his prey.

2036–7 His…fox]
Deighton (ed. 1891): If his discretion, as you say, is too much for his valour (i.e. if his discretion prevents him from exhibiting his valour), I am sure on the other hand that his valour is too much for his discretion (i.e. will not allow his discretion to show itself), for, as we all know, it is not the goose that carries the fox.

2038 listen]
No editor has attempted to explain Q2’s change to hearken (see textual n.); was it perhaps made to avoid excessive alliteration in the Duke’s speech?

2039–40 This…present]
Gerstner-Hirzel (1957, p. 48) notes that a gesture is clearly called for here; he speculates on how the actor deals with the interruption, and whether the gesture is repeated at 2044–5.

2039–40 present]
See n. 871–2.

2039 lanthorne]
A late variant spelling of lantern which the OED considers prob. due to popular etymology, lanterns having formerly been almost always made of horn; used 2039–60 by all speakers, to carry the quibbling references to cuckoldry in horned and hornes, but spelled lãtern at 871 and lanterne at 1934. Q2, followed by F1, spells lanthorne throughout. For similar punning, cf. 2H4 1.2.48 (321).

2039 horned]
Douce (1807, 1:196): He evidently refers to the materials of which the lantern was made. Delius (ed. 1859) cites Cor. 1.1.213 (226): Hang them on the hornes a’th Moone. West (1865, p. 17) compares Sidney’s Arcadia: Like to the moon…Her horned head abroad doth range (ed. Evans, 1977, p. 287). Deighton (ed. 1891): The crescent moon. Evans (ed. 1974) notes the wordplay on the material of the Lanthorn and on the crescent moon, and on the horns of the cuckold in the next line.

2041 worne…head]
Deighton (ed. 1891): To show that he is a cuckold. Brooks (ed. 1979) cites Tilley (H625): He wears the horns.

2042–3 no…circumference]
Rolfe (ed. 1877): Not a waxing moon, but in the wane, as Theseus says just below. Deighton (ed. 1891): If he represented the crescent moon, his horns would show; but he represents the full moon, and therefore his horns are hidden like those of the moon when at the full. Moberly (ed. 1881) suggests: Too stout to represent a crescent moon, however he might do for a full moon; but Brooks (ed. 1979), referring to the probability that Starveling/Mooneshine was a thin man role played by Sincklo (see DP n. 15), sees rather a pun on his want of growth.

2044–5 This…present]
See n. 2039–40.

2045 my selfe]
See n. 126 (your selfe).

2045 man…Moone]
See n. 870–2.

2046–7 This…lanthorne]
Deighton (ed. 1891): If, as he says, the lanthorn represents the moon, then he, in not being inside the lanthorn, is guilty of a greater blunder in acting than any of the others.

2046–7 man…lanthorne]
Brooks (ed. 1979): The quip is the more apt because Starveling is a thin-man part.

2046 greatest…rest]
For the construction, see n. 1375–6 (most).

2047–8 How…Moone]
Deighton (ed. 1891): A question of appeal equivalent to in no other way can he be the man in the moon.

2049–50 He…snuffe]
Fleay (1886, p. 186): May allude to the offence given at Court by Lyly’s Endymion in 1588. Andrews (ed. 1989): Demetrius says that the actor playing Moon is afraid to put himself into the lantern because of the candle, which is already in danger of being snuffed out. Holland (ed. 1994): Starvelings often appear nervous but the comment suggests that he is angry and eventually gives up in furious disgust.

2049 for the candle]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Because of the candle; for fear of being burnt by the candle; see Abbott §150. See Franz §479.

2050 in snuff]
Johnson (ed. 1765): An equivocation. Snuff signifies both the cinder of a candle, and hasty anger. Watt (ed. 1906): Angry. Possibly Starveling was annoyed and forgot his words. He has all through played a feeble part. Holzknecht & McClure (ed. 1937): (a) on the wane, (b) in a huff, flustered. Brooks (ed. 1979): (1) needing to be snuffed; to have the burnt-out part of the wick removed; but (2) to take in snuff was an idiom for to resent, to take in anger; Tilley, S598. See OED, Snuff, n.1 I.1a; II.4.

2051–2 I…change]
Phillips (ed. 1969): An odd reminder of the opening words of the play. Andrews (ed. 1989): Hippolyta’s wish for a change in the phase of the Moon (here an impatient desire to see the actor playing Moon move on) echoes Theseus’ opening lines of the play [5–7].

2051 aweary]
Schmidt (1874): Weary, fatigued.…Followed by of, = tired of. For the form of the adverb, see Franz §240.

2051 Would]
See n. 431.

2053–5 It…time]
Baker (ed. 1896): Theseus puns here on the faintness of the light in the lantern and the slight discernment and judgment with which Moon gives his lines. Cox (1982, p. 178) sees in the speech a complex series of puns on light, discretion, wane, courtesy, (one of the meanings of discretion), reason, and time.…Shakespeare makes the two most political characters of his play engage in a brief exchange that dwells, however playfully, on the intrinsic limitation of the artisan who plays Moonshine—on, that is, his weak light of discretion. Yet Shakespeare also makes Theseus, however playfully, indicate that in all civility, yes, even in all reason, they must accept the presence of that artisan: his light, it seems, dim though it is, is required by the city.

2053–4 It…wane]
Deighton (ed. 1891): It appears, so far as we can judge by the small amount of sense in his words, that he will soon be no longer visible, i.e. that he will soon leave the stage; we should now say on the wane. See also 7. Dent compares the proverb His wit is in the wane (W555).

2054 in curtesie]
In the general sense of politeness or good manners (see n. 1172, and cf. 708, 730, 982, 1980), but with overtones of OED (2a.): Nobleness, generosity, benevolence.

2054 in all reason]
Baker (ed. 1896): For every reason; every reason obliges us to wait until he goes. Brooks (ed. 1979): As is only reasonable. Clayton (1999, p. 70): For this function of reason Theseus deserves credit that he is now seldom accorded by critics.

2055 stay]
Schmidt (1875, vb. 2g): Wait for. On Sh.’s turning intransitive verbs into transitive ones, see Blake 4.4.3 (b), Franz §630 (a), Abbott §200. See also n. 614.

2055 the time]
Deighton (ed. 1891): I.e. the time fixed for his disappearance from the scene.

2057–9 All…my dogge]
Simpson (1916, 2:278) quotes from Jonson’s The Case is Altered Valentine’s strictures on the behaviour of Caprichious gallants at a play (2.7.40–83; Works, 3:136–7), and adds: Shakespeare’s kindlier temper made him incapable of such an outburst, but even he suggests that there is an element of truth in the accusation. The courtiers’ banter of the Nine Worthies in Love’s Labour’s Lost and of Quince’s company in A Midsummer Night’s Dream has no spite in it, but it is equally disturbing to the players. Black (1965, p. 59): Confused by the interruptions, Starveling abandons his versified lines and speaks plain prose. Wells (ed. 1967): Moonshine, in exasperation, lapses into prose.

2059 thorne…dogge]
See n. 1934–5.

2060–1 for…Moone]
Deighton (ed. 1891): For we see all these in the moon; the mountains in the moon having been likened to these objects.

2063 ould]
Cf. 426 hould and n.

2063 Ninies]
Verity (ed. 1893): Thisbe forgets Quince’s correction, [911]. See nn. 911, 2005.

2063 tumbe]
OED: Obs. f. tomb. On the pronunciation, see n. 2118. See also n. 910. Verity (ed. 1912): The scene has changed, and the change would be indicated by bringing on the stage some piece of furniture more or less suggestive of a tomb. Wells (ed. 1967) suggests that the speech itself is indicating a change of scene. No further indication is necessary, though producers have been known to employ a tomb inscribed Hic iacet Ninus.

2064 Oh]
Andrews (ed. 1993): This word represents the Lion’s roar. F1 specifies in the SD [2065]: The Lion roares, Thisby runs off.

2069 with…grace]
Creditably (OED, Grace, n. 1b).

2070 mouz’d]
Malone (ed. 1790, 10:580): To tear in pieces, as a cat tears a mouse (OED, v. 3. trans., quoting Jn. 2.1.354 [668]). Seymour (1805, 1:52): This refers to the action of the lion, in pouncing on the garment, as a cat would on a mouse, quoting Mac. 2.4.12–13 (938–9). Schmidt (1875, Mouse, vb.): Jocularly applied to a lion, quoting this instance. Foakes (ed. 1984): Theseus’s comment also suggests that Lion squeaks rather than roars, and is more pitiful than fearsome in performance. Garrison (1994, p. 153): Theseus…contributes once again to the play’s fundamental resource of paradoxes by making up another one when he says Well moused, Lion. Mason’s (1785, p. 72) conjecture mouth’d alluding either to his roaring, or to his tearing with his mouth the mantle of Thisbe, found some favor (see textual n.). Steevens (ed. 1785), similarly, understood mouz’d as musled (OED, Muzzle, v.11a), meaning the lion well tumbled and bloody’d the veil of Thisbe.

2071–2 And…vanisht]
QqFf do not mark an exit for the lion, editors placing it variously from 2069–73 (see textual n.). Hailes (1786, p. 361): The lion…goes out upon the entering of Pyramus. Delius (ed. 1859): Demetrius makes his remark about what is to come at the performance only in the past tense, as if he were telling the story of Pyramus and Thisbe, and Lysander joins in the same tone (Ger.). Thiselton (1903, p. 76) finds the departure of the lion at the approach of Pyramus a humorous travesty of nature. Andrews (ed. 1993), implausibly: [Lion] needs to remain visible to the audience so that he can speak [2134–7], which F1 gives to Bottom. The unnecessary proposals to replace Lyon with Moon, and to transpose 2071–2 (see textual nn.) caused considerable discussion in the 18th and 19th c.: see Furness (ed. 1895).

2073 Enter Pyramus]
Dyce (ed. 1857) observes that here, as in Ovid, Pyramus appears to have been somewhat slow in keeping the assignation, Serius egressus, [4.105] &c.; cf. Golding, Ovid 4.127–30 (see ). He compares also A gorgious Gallery (1578, O4v), where Pyramus’s delay is explained because he could not earst, the wrathfull house forgo.

2074–85, 2089–99, 2116–31 Sweete…quell…O…dy…A…adieu]
Arnold (1911, pp. 114–15): The burlesque by Pyramus of the conventional apostrophe to the dead includes apostrophes to the moon and to the fates and furies, as well as to the deceased sweetheart.…It is no mere rhetorical trick which places Pyramus and Thisbe, mock hero and heroine, in the forefront of fantastic and burlesque figures. Each reveals his amorous condition by a maudlin admiration for the objects of nature, each exhausts the stage vocabulary of the bereaved lover, and each, with an exaggeration of the requirements of the suicide soliloquy, doubly redoubles the announcement of the stabbing and of the expiration; earlier (p. 37) he notes: The mock heroic suicide soliloquies…might easily have been inspired by the corresponding soliloquies in the pseudo-heroic style of Golding’s Ovid [4.133–40, 172–95; see ]. Cf. n. 1976. Starkie (1916, p. 216), citing 2074–85 and especially 2118–22: Laughter is caused when an aggravated style is employed in embellishing a mean subject whether by language or meter, the latter yielding here a very Aristophanic parody. Apart from the cross-rhymed quatrain beginning each of Pyramus’s speeches (2074–7, 2089–92), Brooks (ed. 1979) notes: The stanza-form of the laments is a version, with internal rhyme, of eight and six (cf. [834]). It is likely to have been suggested by Thomson’s use of it [see ]. Levi (1988, pp. 136–7): Some of the mixed metre is parody—Pyramus’ lament comes from Lamphedon and Clarisia in Common Conditions (1514–23) and follows its metre—but most is lyrical. Acheson (1922, pp. 206–7) sees the nonsense of these verses, with…their extravagant alliteration as a travesty of Matthew Roydon’s Elegie on Sidney (in The Phoenix Nest, 1593, B1r–4v). Wallace (1997, p. 120) finds these lines strongly reminiscent of the tail-rhyme of Sir Thopas. See .

2074–6 Sweete…beames]
This apostrophe to the Moon may glance at Medea’s soliloquy (in Ovid, Met. 7.192–3) to night and the golden stars, whose light Doth jointly with the moon succeed the beams that blaze by day (Golding, Met. 7.258–60; see ). Lunar beams appear also at 539 where the moon has chast beames and at 991, Moone-beames.

2074 Sweete…beams]
Brooks (ed. 1979): This absurdity, except for being unconscious, resembles the one Petruchio makes Kate accept, [Shr. 4.5.2 (2297) ff.] Donaldson (1985, pp. 10–11), pointing out that both Chaucer and Sh. are parodying bad poets, compares Sir Thopas (879–80; see ). Taylor (1989, p. 320) finds the parallel unconvincing, claiming that it rather serves to highlight the considerable distance in tone and intention between Chaucer’s sly, ironic, and rather delicate poetic parody and Shakespeare’s robust and uninhibited dramatic Ovidian burlesque. Taylor (1990, pp. 61–2) sees sunny beams as a mistranslation of Ovid, Met. 4.99, lunae radios (the rays of the moon), Quince being misled by a stock dictionary definition of radius as A beame of the sunne. Cf. A gorgious Gallery (1578, O4r), where Thisbie is grateful to have as guide the gladsom seeming cheare of Phebe fayre.

2075–7 I…sight]
Brooks (ed. 1979): Cf. Chaucer’s account: The mone shon, and he myghte wel yse [LGW 825; see ].

2076 For…beames]
Capell (1783, 2.3:116–17), one of many to object to the repetition of the rhyme word of 2074, endorses F2’s streames and suggests the change ought to be ascribed to the Author himself; he supports the reading by quoting Sackvile’s in the Mirror for Magistrates (ed. Lily B. Campbell, [1938], rpt. New York 1960, p. 300, ll. 59–60). The other chief candidate for substitution is gleams (see textual n.). Furness (ed. 1895) rehearses earlier arguments, and concludes: The unanimity of [Qq] and [F1] cannot be lightly whistled down the wind. The fact that beams is wrong and streams or gleams manifestly right, seems to me the very reason why it should be retained in the speech of one whose eye had not heard, nor his ear seen, nor his hand tasted a dream which he had in the wood where he had gone to rehearse obscenely. However, Brooks (ed. 1979) enters a belated argument in favor of gleams. Sisson (1956, 1:134): I prefer to read the line with an embarrassed pause after glittering and a defiant emphasis on beams, the more effective precisely because it does not alliterate and is an anticlimax after gracious, golden, glittering. Wells (ed. 1967) agrees: Beams may be a deliberate comic touch.

2076 gratious]
OED (3): Benevolent, courteous. Cf. 1744.

2077 take]
Many editors follow F1’s reading taste, agreeing with Delius (ed. 1859) that it shows characteristic absurdity (see textual n.). But Brooks (ed. 1979): [F1]’s taste might conceivably be the true reading, one more instance of the comic confusion of the senses. But to adopt it would not be justified: it may just as well be an assimilation by the compositor to trust and truest; and Q1 yields good sense. Deighton (ed. 1891) glosses take: For catch, for the sake of the alliteration.

2077 ô spight]
See n. 148.

2077 marke]
See n. 949.

2077 poore knight]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Apostrophizing himself. Muir (1954, p. 144): Like Thomson [see ] Shakespeare refers to Pyramus as a knight…, and the joke is pointed by [307].

2079 dreadfull dole]
Cf. the deadly dole of Thisbie in A gorgious Gallery (1578, P2r).

2080 Eyes…bee]
Bicks (2003, p. 88) cites Pyramus’s mistaken interpretation of the bloody cloak as an example of the unreliable nature of anatomical sights and signs in MND.

2081 O…deare]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Apostrophizing Thisbe.

2081 dainty duck]
Possibly proverbial; Dent (D630.1).

2081 dainty]
Johnson (1755, 1; 2): 1. Delicious.…2. Delicate. Schmidt (1874, 2) adds enticing, lovely. Cf. Tit. 2.1.117 (680), dainty doe.

2081 duck]
Johnson (1755, 2): A word of endearment, or fondness (OED, n.1 3a, quoting this instance first). Johnson quotes WT 4.4.317 (2140), My dainty Ducke, my deere-a. Webb (1988, p. 17): Sexual prey; a lover.

2081 deare]
F1’s Deere at this line and 2090 (see textual n.) encourages the reader to understand the animal, as does the parallel with Ducke for the auditor. For a similar pun, see Tit. 3.1.88–91 (1228–31).

2082 Thy…blood]
Hammerle (Laubenmotiv, 1953, p. 311) parallels Spenser’s Argante, who suffred beasts her body to deflowre (FQ 3.7.49; one rhyme word is deuoure) (Ger.). Wiles (1993, p. 60), questionably: Thisbe’s bloodstained mantle…becomes a hymeneal emblem.…The substitution of deflower’d for devour’d obliges the audience to relate the bloodstain to the context of the wedding night. See n. 2090.

2082 good]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Fine; for the sake of the rhyme.

2082 what,]
For the construction and tone, see n. 436.

2083–4 Approach…come]
Many parallels are adduced, e.g.—Malone (ed. 1790, 10:580), prompted by Farmer (see Boswell, ed. 1821), compares Richard Edwards’s Damon and Pythias (1571, D1v): Ye furies, all at once On me your torments trie. Steevens (ed. 1793) suggests our poet might possibly have recollected La Conusaunce damours (1528?, b2v), O ye moost cruell and rabbysshe lions fell, Come nowe and teare the corps of Pyramus. Wright (ed. 1877, p. xx) compares Appius and Virginia, 1575. Root (1903, p. 64) points to Vergil’s Aeneid 6.[250, 280, 375]. Fripp (1930, p. 101) parallels Furies fell with Ovid’s grave et implacabile numen [divinities deadly and implacable] (Met. [4.]452). Doran (ed. 1959): Invocations of the Furies were common in Senecan drama. Brooks (ed. 1979) specifies Seneca’s Medea, ll. 13–15, Hercules Furens 86–8, Agamemnon 759–65, etc. Holland (ed. 1994) finds a suggestion of the furie infernal that fatally startles Arcite’s horse, but in the printed texts of Chaucer’s works most probably used by Sh. the furie is a fyre (KnT 2684; see ). Clemen (1955, p. 246): That Shakespeare himself was well aware how entirely the conventional invocation to the Furies lost its force when it became a mere rhetorical adornment in association with other formal devices of the dramatic lament is evidenced by his parody of this kind of tragic rhetoric in the Pyramus and Thisbe episode in [MND]. There we find the whole paraphernalia of the formal apostrophe turned to comic purposes.

2083 Furies]
Johnson (1755, Fury 4): One of the deities of vengeance (OED, Fury, n. 5, adding, dread goddesses with snakes twined in their hair, sent from Tartarus to avenge wrong and punish crime: in later accounts, three in number (Tisiphone, Megæra, Alecto).

2083 fell]
See n. 390.

2084–5 O…quell]
Richmond (2002, p. 26) quotes these lines to illustrate that Sh’s heavy use of alliteration is often comically associated with provincial or archaic verse.

2084 O…thrumme]
Cf. Golding, Ovid 2.819, The fatal dames shall break thy thread. Cf. A gorgious Gallery (1578, N4v-O1r), where Pyramus and Thisbie on happy death they call, Come death come wished death at once, and rid vs life and all, and Pyramus expects his feeble thread to knap in two.

2084 fates]
See n. 300.

2084 cut…thrumme]
Nares (1822; 1905, Thread and Thrum): An expression borrowed from weaving, the thread being the substance of the warp; the thrum, the small tuft beyond, where it is tied. Hence, metaphorically, the good and bad together (OED, Thread, n. 2c, quoting this instance first). Capell (1783, 2.3:117): A line of singular fitness in the mouth of master Bottom. Brooks (ed. 1979): [The expression’s] aptness to Bottom’s trade turns to burlesque the classical thread of life ( OED, Thread, n. 6a).

2085 Quaile]
Steevens (MS 1773–8): To languish, sink in dejection (OED, v.1 1). Rann (ed. 1787): Vanquish. Deighton (ed. 1891), quoting Ant. 5.2.85 (3303): Crush, destroy (OED, v.1 4, quoting this instance).

2085 conclude]
Alone, Page (ed. 1897): Finish, make an end, destroy (OED, v. 7 intr)

2085 quell]
Steevens (MS 1773–8): To murther, to destroy (OED, v.1 1, quoting this instance). Caldecott (MS 1813–33) compares 2H4 2.1.53 (660) where Man-queller means man-killer.

2086–7 This…sad]
White (1853, p. 393): The humor…consists in coupling the ridiculous fustian of the clown’s assumed passion, with an event which would, in itself, make a man look sad. Hunter (ed. 1870): This expression of emotion, along with the death of a dear friend. The language is satirical, implying that the passionate speech counts for nothing.

2086 passion]
Johnson (1755, 2): Violent commotion of the mind. OED (n. 6d), quoting the instance at 2109: A poem, literary composition, or passage marked by deep or strong emotion; a passionate speech or outburst. Kittredge (ed. 1939): Paroxysm of grief. Mowat & Werstine (ed. 1993): Staging of strong emotion. Greenblatt (ed. 1997): Suffering; extravagant speech. Wright (ed. 1877) compares Ham. 2.2.561 (1601). See 2109. See also nn. 1097, 1866.

2088 Beshrewe…but]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Bad luck to me if I do not. See n. 706.

2088 but]
Abbott §126: But…means if not after beshrew, quoting Jn. 5.4.49–50 (2510–11). See OED (But, prep. 11c), quoting MV 2.6.52 (953).

2088 I…man]
Beiner (1985, p. 108): Hippolyta…responds for a moment to the tragedy and not to the artisans’ lamentable performance.…But even she has no insight into the significance of Pyramus and Thisbe in connection with the story of the night.

2089–99 O…dy]
Gentleman (ed. 1774): Stage-deaths, as well as many other tragic incidents, are sometimes liable to ridicule. Our author unquestionably meant this as a satire upon the abrupt, and sometimes absurd, introduction of such events. Harrison (ed. 1937) finds a parody of the histrionic style of Edward Alleyne, as Hieronimo lamenting over the dead body of Horatio in Thomas Kyd’s The Spanish Tragedy (1592, D3r; 2.5.14–33). Cf. Young (1966, p. 41). Bush (1956, p. 26): Bottom, in the person of Pyramus, utters the cri du coeur of the tragic hero at the discovery of natural evil: [quotes 2089] But the lions of the comedies are harmless, their part is nothing but roaring, and Bottom rises from the dead to inquire: Will it please you to see the Epilogue? [2135]. Hibbard (1981, p. 146) finds Shn. self-parody, citing Tit. 4.1.59–60 (1603–4) and Rom. 4.5.35–7 (2615–17). Cf. also the language of defloration in Tit. 2.3.191 (940) and 2.4.26 (1099). Garber (1981, p. 145): The contiguity—even interchangeability—of defloration and dying carries with it not only the force of the usual Renaissance pun, but also a somewhat different meaning, increasingly familiar to the Shakespearean audience, in which death becomes an alternative or substitute for love. See also n. 2093–9, 2116–31.

2089 frame]
Herbert (1977, p. 56) compares Blake’s Tyger, What…Could frame thy fearful symmetry. In A gorgious Gallery (1578, N4r) it pleased Nature…to frame Pyramus and Thisbie.

2090 vilde]
OED gives a separate entry for Vild a., as a Variant of vile a. with excrescent -d, with the same meanings, noting that the form is extremely common from c1580 to 1650. There is reason to keep the less familiar form, because it may be another of Bottom’s blunders, for wild, a word associated with lion at 842–3 and 2025. Three texts, mistakenly or deliberately, change to wild (see textual n.). Or perhaps it is just Bottom’s pronunciation, for he uses the word only twice (here and at 2003), both times changing the original vile in Quince’s Prologue (at 1931—vile wall, and 1942—Lyon vile) to vilde.

2090 deflour’d]
Thirlby (MS 1725–33) and Delius (ed. 1859) see deflour’d as a blunder for devour’d. Deighton (ed. 1891): Misused by Bottom in the sense of cutting off a flower in its bloom (OED, Deflower, v. 2). Black (1965, p. 59): Literally, ravished. Bottom presumably means destroyed. Flint (1991, p. 16): A malapropism which reminds us of the potential closeness of human and animal sexual relations. Holland (ed. 1994): In the Reinhardt-Dieterle film (1935), Joe E. Brown as Flute carried an enormous fake flower so that he could be de-flowered. Audiences familiar with other versions of the story would expect devoured or an equivalent; see Kennedy (2005, p. 18).

2090 deare]
See n. 2081.

2091 no…was]
Holland (ed. 1994): Sometimes played as Bottom prompting himself without Quince’s help but it is perhaps better as Pyramus’ aghast realization that Thisbe is no more.

2091 dame]
OED (n. 7a), quoting this instance: The wife or daughter of a lord; a woman of rank, a lady.

2092 That…cheere]
Bush (1931, p. 145) compares A gorgious Gallery (1578, P2r) where Thisbie pleads with the dying Pyramus not to deny a final look To her with thee that liu’d and lou’d, and eke with thee will dye.

2092 cheere]
Most, beginning with Steevens (ed. 1793), gloss countenance, as at 1119. However, Schmidt (1874, 1): Cheerfulness, high spirits (OED, n.1 4). Foakes (ed. 1984): Perhaps = face as at [1119], but suggesting also cheerfulness.

2093–9, 2116–31 Come…dy…Asleepe…adieu]
Arkwright (1906, p. 401), commenting on death songs written for two lost plays in an MS dated 1581, suggests that they represent a convention which may be parodied in Pyramus’s and Thisby’s laments: I take it for granted that both Pyramus and Thisbe are intended to sing their death songs. When it is recognized that this was the practice in the old plays [in fn. he instances Cambises], the change of metre in Pyramus and Thisbe seems plainly to indicate that these passages should be sung.

2093 Come…wound]
Scott (ed. 1898): The apostrophe to tears as if they could confound or kill the man is ludicrous, but part of the show is to produce tears so as to render the self-destroying valour grotesque.

2093 confound…wound]
For the pronunciation see n. 755–6.

2093 confound]
Johnson (1755, 5): To destroy; to overthrow (OED, v. 1). Ritson in Steevens (ed. 1793) compares Golding, Ovid 4.133; see . Wright (ed. 1877) compares Mac. 2.2.11 (661). Deighton (ed. 1891): Throw me into a state of distraction; if, indeed, Bottom is to be flattered by supposing him to have a definite meaning (OED, v. 4). Black (1965, p. 59), somewhat despairingly: It is useless to try to make sense of all of Quince’s riming verse. See n. 1116.

2093 out]
OED (adv. 13a): With ellipsis of intr. vb. (go, come, etc.); hence functioning as a verb without inflexion.

2094–5 pappe…pappe]
OED (n.1 1): A teat or nipple: a. of a woman’s breast (now arch. or northern);…c. the corresponding part of a man, the mamilla (chiefly literary, somewhat arch.). Bush (1931, p. 145): The Thisbe of the History stabs herself beneath her pap, (A gorgious Gallery, 1578, P2v). Muir (1954, p. 146): Sh. borrows the pap and makes it more ludicrous by giving it to Pyramus. Brooks (ed. 1979): Muir is probably right, though Yong uses it of a knight in a serious context (Diana, Kennedy, p. 238). Foakes (ed. 1984) compares Chapman’s translation of Homer’s Iliad 4.517, and LLL 4.3.23–4 (1355–6). Donaldson (1985, p. 12) compares Chaucer’s reference to Sir Thopas’s sides smale (836; see ) as a joke on types of anatomy usually associated with females. See also n. 906–7.

2095 that…hoppe]
Steevens (ed. 1793) compares Juvenal, Satires 7.159–60, laevae parte mamillae, nil salit [in his left breast nothing hops]. Taylor (1987, pp. 207–8) claims Sh. is ridiculing the style of Thomas Phaer’s translation of Vergil’s Aeneid 5.133, Their harts for ioy doth hop (1558, M2v). Bowers (1994, pp. 33–5) demurs, because Phaer’s reputation was high, and his translation published too long ago for the audience to recognize a parody. Taylor (1995, p. 314) responded that Sh.’s mockery of the early translators of the classics was generally good-humored. Holland (ed. 1994): Hop sounds ridiculous but for its strong sense of dance (OED v.[1] 2) here compare Herod, O my harte hoppis for joie, in The York Plays, ed. Richard Beadle (1982), p. 275. On the rhyme with pappe, see n. 419–20.

2095 left]
Grey (1754, 1:76): ’Twas the vulgar notion that the heart laid on the left side of the thorax, or breast, but it is situated in the middle of the thorax. Bucknill (1860, p. 91): Every one of course knows on which side the heart beats, but here is authority.

2096–9 Now…dy]
Kranendonk (1932, p. 216) finds almost equally ludicrous the 14-fold repetition of dye and dying in Spenser’s FQ 3.5.45–7. Knutson (1991, p. 219) compares Marlowe’s Jew of Malta: Dye life, flye soule, tongue curse thy fill and dye (5.5.89; 1:334). Mangan (1991, pp. 73–4): Pyramus’s speech…is a remorseless parody of [the dying speech].…There are thirteen…opportunities for the actor to die on stage and then come back to life. Taylor (2002, p. 48): And die he does. And as he does so, if he is to be believed, he, or his soul, ascends into the sky.…This translation is underscored by the presence in the next line, Tongue lose thy light (which illogically combines tongue, lose thy voice and Eyes lose thy light), of the figure of speech known in classical rhetoric as hypallage, called also the Changeling, which is characterized by George Puttenham [1589; 1936, p. 171] as words changing their true construction and application, whereby the sense is quite perverted and made very absurd.

2096 Thus…thus]
Collier (1853, p. 110) attributes to his old corrector this singular manuscript stage-direction…, Stab himself as often; that is, as often as he exclaims, thus, thus, thus! Most editors provide a similar SD (see textual n.). R. (1780, pp. 520) compares Vergil’s Aeneid 4.660, sic, sic iuvat ire sub umbras, [Thus, thus I go gladly into the dark], Dido’s last words. Hasler (1974, p. 88–9) compares Kyd’s Spanish Tragedy 2.4.54, and Oth. 5.2.356 (3667), adding: On the face of it, the death of Pyramus ought to resemble the heroic act of the Moor. But Othello’s last word is here irresistibly comic by virtue of its emphatic repetition, which Bottom is compelled to enact. His improbable feat in stabbing himself four times over is crowned by the impossible mirror line Now am I dead. Riess & Williams (1992, p. 216) compare this and 2099 with Rom. 5.3.120 (2977). See 2130.

2097 sky]
OED (n.1 4a), quoting this instance first: Heaven. Hassel (2005, p. 329): The singular sky, though more often referring to the natural than the supernatural canopy, can also imply the abode of the gods and of departed souls, [quotes Berowne LLL 4.3.77–8 (1413–14)]…When Pyramus dies with the words [quotes 2097], he also speaks (prematurely to be sure) of going to heaven.

2098 Tongue…flight]
Thirlby (MS 1725–33) conjectures eye for Tongue. Capell (1783, 2.3:117): Tongue, instead of Sunne or Sun…is a very choice blunder. See textual n. and . Halliwell (ed. 1856): Although Bottom is a great blunderer, the present error of tongue for sun appears too absurd to be humorous, and it may well be questioned whether it be not a misprint. Deighton (ed. 1891): Surely the nonsense is intentional, and Pyramus, if made to talk sense, would have said, Breath, take thy flight; Moon, lose thy light. Scott (ed. 1898) appears determined to make Bottom talk sense: Moon and tongue are transposed as see and hear below [2135–6]. On this view Bottom says Moon lose thy light, thus instructing Moonshine to disappear or to put out the candle; then he adds Tongue take thy flight, by which he means that he will speak no more.

2098 loose]
See n. 123.

2100–4 No…Asse]
Cf. the similar punning in LLL 5.2.628–31 (2580–6). Craig (ed. 1903) suggests possible quibbles between ace in cards (then pronounced ass), as, a Roman copper coin (spelt asse in Holland’s Plinie, ii., 463), and ass a simpleton. But Cercignani (1981, p. 179): The quibble ace (OF as)—ass MND [2100–4] cannot be held to show (despite Kökeritz, 89) either ă in ace or a distorted realization of ass, since the word-play obviously rests on antithesis rather than identity.

2100–2 No…nothing]
Cf. Dent (O50.1): Man (Life) is but a figure of One.

2100 No…one]
While Delius (ed. 1859) is the first to explain the puns here, Neil (ed. 1878) is the clearest: The first pun is on die, to expire, as used by Bottom, and die, a cube, spotted with dots from one to six, used in gaming; and ace, the die having the lowest mark, one,…and ass, used as a synonym for stupid fellow. Watt (ed. 1906): Pyramus had, so to speak, thrown five [i.e., uttered five dies at 2099]: whereas, being one man, he was only entitled to an ace, as he could only die once. Kittredge (ed. 1939): He is not a whole die, only a one-spot. A very feeble pun, but Theseus does not disdain to cap it with his play on ace and ass. George Peele also uses the pun on ace/ass in Edward I (1.151–2; ed. Frank S. Hook, 1961, p. 77 and p. 174 n.) Steevens (ed. 1773) also here reprimands Theseus who in support of his pretensions [to wit] never rises higher than a pun, and frequently sinks as low as a quibble. Doran (ed. 1959) sees a further quibble in one, on one person and unique. Capell (1783, 2.3:117), mistakenly: To make even a lame conundrum of this, you are to suppose—that die implies, two; as if it came from—duo. Cuningham (ed. 1905), unaptly, quotes from OED, Ace, n. 2. fig., Ace was frequently used for bad luck, misfortune, loss.

2103–4 With…Asse]
Brooks (ed. 1979) and Friedenreich (1979, pp. 50–1) see a possible link with the doctor who resuscitates people in the Mummers’ plays of St. George and Netley Abbey. See also n. 2134. Commentators have not found a real-life parallel in the 16th c., but John Evelyn in his diary entry for 22 March 1675 tells of Sir William Petty’s fame for having resuscitated a poor wench that had been hanged for felony. Grey (1754, 1:77) also provides a late anecdote: A celebrated anatomist, who had a body deliver’d to him after execution,…[with] some signs of life; [said] that if he could recover him, he would prove no better than an idiot.

2104 yet]
Brooks (ed. 1979) : Q1’s yet is caught from yet recover (see textual n.).

2104 prooue…Asse]
Verity (ed. 1893): There is a point, an irony, in this remark that Theseus is not aware of: Bottom has already proved an ass. Barkan (1986, p. 269), commenting on metaphor and metamorphosis in these lines: Ass is such a familiar term for a fool that one almost forgets it is a metaphor.

2105–8 How…starre-light]
Blits (2003, p. 189) notes that Pyramus and Thisbe are deprived of moonlight, as the young lovers in the wood had been; see n. 222–3.

2105–6 How…louer]
Bijvoet (1988, p. 83): [W]hen Pyramus, dying or already dead, urges Moon, take thy flight, Starveling obeys, which, according to the dramatic conventions we subscribe to, leaves the stage in darkness, too early. The actors fail to realize this, but their Athenian audience naturally notices the mistake [quoting 2105–7.].

2105 How chance]
For an explanation of this phrase, see n. 139.

note.p: F1 adds a SD here. Furness (ed. 1895): In this command to the actor to be ready to enter before he has to make his actual appearance on the stage, we have another proof that [F1] was printed from a stage-copy. Many editors move the SD later. See textual n.

div.note:
2108–9 Shee…Play]
The lineation change made by F1 (see textual n.) seems to be an attempt to turn prose to verse, as earlier at 2068–9. See Werstine (1984, p. 85). Wells & Taylor (1987, p. 286), suggest a reason for the line division: perhaps an annotator’s mark, indicating where to place the stage direction [2107], was misinterpreted as a line-break.

2109 Here…Play]
Thirlby (MS 1725–33) conjectures this line may belong to Philostrate. Theobald (1729, in Nichols, 1817, 2:240) suggests the same for [he] had seen the interlude rehearsed and consequently knew how it ended. He did not, however, adopt this in his text, because, Furness (ed. 1895) says, He probably remembered that Theseus had seen the Dumb-show.

2109 passion]
See n. 2086.

2110 Me thinkes]
See n. 6.

2112–13 A…better]
Deighton (ed. 1891): The very smallest atom will be enough to turn the balance between them, they being so evenly matched, and show which of the two is the better actor.

2112–13 which…better]
Abbott §273 asserts that the sentence is hard to explain…unless which is used for the kindred whether. Deighton (ed. 1891): Apparently a confusion between (1) which of the two, Pyramus and Thisbe, is the better, and (2) whether Pyramus or Thisbe is the better of the two.

2112 moth]
Johnson (1755, Mote): A small particle of matter; any thing proverbially little (OED, Mote, n.1 1c). Most commentators repeat Malone (ed. 1790, 10:580): Moth was the old mode of spelling this word. See textual n. Caldecott (MS 1813–33) compares Ham. 1.1.112 (Q2 B2v), Wright (ed. 1877) LLL 4.3.159 (1498). Brooks (ed. 1979) cites Matt. 7:3. See also Moth the Fairy in DP.

2112 turne]
Johnson (1755, 4): To change the state of the ballance, quoting MM 4.2.30–1 (1883–4), a feather will turne the Scale. OED (v. 49), quoting this instance first: To preponderate. Cf. Ham. 4.5.158 (2910).

2112 ballance]
Johnson (1755, 2): A pair of scales. OED (n. 8a), quoting this instance: The metaphorical balance of justice, reason, opinion, by which actions and principles are weighed or estimated.

2113–13+1 he…vs]
Johnson (ed. 1765) on the omitted lines (see textual n.): Q1 makes this speech a little longer but not better. Collier (ed. 1842): This passage…was omitted in [F1], possibly on account of the statute against using the name of the Creator, &c., on the stage, 1 Jac. 1. ch. 21. Staunton (ed. 1857) paraphrases: From such a man, God defend us; from such a woman, God save us. Baker (ed. 1896): The ejaculations…suggest a shrugging of the shoulders and a look which say: They are so bad that words fail me. Black (1965, p. 60): Heaven help us if Pyramus as played by Bottom, and Thisby as played by Flute, represent mankind.

2113 warnd]
Almost all editors from Collier (ed. 1842) on alter warnd to warrant (see textual n.), meaning save, defend, preserve. But see Schmidt (1875, Warn 1): God warn us = God guard us, God forbid instancing AYL 4.1.77 (1989); under Warrant (4) he suggests that Audrey’s Lord warrant us in AYL 3.3.5 (1618) has seduced modern editors to change warnd in MND to warrant. Kittredge (ed. 1939): This exclamation (like God bless us!) calls attention to the absurdity of the performance, as if we needed God’s help to save us from the ill omen of such monstrosities. Cf. [841, 1759–60].

2114 She…eyes]
Furness (ed. 1895): Does not this remark of Lysander’s give us an insight of the way in which Thisbe, like any amateur actor, ran at once to Pyramus’s body, without looking to the right or left?

2115 meanes]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Means to say. Kittredge (ed. 1939): The most obvious sense is, Thus she expresses her sentiments,… [OED, v.1 6b]. But mean (Anglo-Saxon mænan) is an old word for to lament (especially for the dead) [OED, v.2 2].…These two senses may be combined here.…The difficulty is, that mean in the first of these senses appears in Shakespeare’s time to have been almost—and, in the second sense, quite—restricted to the Scottish dialect. Perhaps Theobald’s [ed. 1733] emendation moans (cf. [1991]) is what Shakespeare wrote. So in [Son. 30.5–8]. See textual n.

2115 videlicet]
Johnson (1755): (Latin.) To wit; that is. This word is generally written viz. (OED, adv., adding namely). Evans (ed. 1974): as follows.

2115 videlicet;]
Demetrius’ short speech ends with a semicolon, which probably indicates an interruption of his speech by Thisby. Cf. . Or it may signal a gesture, a pointing of the arm to Thisby to introduce her speech. Cf. 1678 and n.

2116–31 A…adieu]
Many comment on parody in the speech as a whole and in various parts, adducing parallels with MND itself, other Shn. plays, and a wide range of classical, medieval, and Renaissance literature and art. Wells (ed. 1967): The parodic derangement of epithets here [esp. 2120–3] recalls the confusions of the senses in earlier scenes. Zesmer (1976, p. 111) compares 755–7. Andrews (ed. 1993) and Bate (1995, p. 182) are reminded of the deaths of Juliet in Rom. and of Bassianus in Tit. Lanier (1902, 2:284–5) compares the eclogue of Carmela and Doron in Robert Greene’s Menaphon (1589, K3v–4v). Bradbrook (1951, p. 256) compares the Mother’s lament over her slain child in Thomas Preston’s Cambyses (1570, C4v). Salingar (1974, p. 70) compares Neronis’s speech in Clyomon and Clamydes as she prepares to kill herself with a sword over what she believes to be Clyomon’s corpse (1599, F4v). Goy-Blanquet (1989, p. 95) sees Pyramus’s exquisite corpse as a still-life by Arcimboldo (Fr.). See also Donaldson (1985, pp. 11–12); Wallace (1997, p. 120). Suzuki (1990, pp. 110–11) compares Phaedra’s lament over Hippolytus’s dismembered body (Seneca, Hippolytus, tr. Newton, p. 179 [1168–72]). Brooks (ed. 1979) compares Chaucer’s Tisbe (LGW 880; see ).

2116 A sleepe]
See n. 555.

2116 What,]
For the construction and tone, see n. 436.

2116 doue]
OED (2d): An appellation of tender affection, quoting Chaucer, MerT 2138–9 (see ). Webb (1996, p. 57) sees this as a burlesque of the common endearment, comparing Ham. 4.5.168 (2920). See n. 182.

2117 arise]
Andrews (ed. 1993) finds erotic wordplay here.

2118–22 Speake…mone]
Rushton (1867, p. 219), quoting 2121–2, observes that Sh. is using a form of repetition called Epizeuxis, or the underlay or Coocko-spell, quoting Puttenham (1589; 1936, p. 201).

2118 dumbe]
Johnson (1755, 1; 4): 1. Incapable of speech.…4. Silent.; so Mowat & Werstine, ed. 1993.

2118 tumbe]
OED: Obs. f. tomb. Wells (ed. 1967): At this date, a true rhyme with dumb. Cercignani (1981, p. 199): lME ŭ in tomb would seem to be attested by the rhymes dumb (OE dumb) : tomb; he cites this example and AWW 2.3.139–40 (1041–2), with several others. Tumbe is spelled thus twice (2063 and here); as toumbe twice (910, 911); as tombe twice (1937, 2005). Brooks (ed. 1979) compares Golding, Met. 4.108, 4.117, 4.201; see .

2120–5 These…milke]
Bond (1902, 3:517) compares Lyly, Endimion 5.2.95–100 (3:70–71). Donaldson (1985, p. 12): Thisbe lavishes on [Pyramus] a number of epithets usually considered a female property.…The lilies and cherries are, by long tradition, part of her own fair femininity.…I’m uncertain of the rhetorical gender of yellow cowslip cheeks or eyes as green as leeks…: perhaps they are produced by a democratic wish to give as much poetic credit to lesser forms of vegetation…as to the somewhat over-publicized roses and lilies. See also n. 906-7.

2120 lippes…nose]
For editorial efforts to correct the lack of rhyme, see textual n. and . Cherry lips are a commonplace of Renaissance lyric poetry. For ridiculous reversals see n. 2116–31, esp. Wells and Wallace. Cf. also n. 1162–9; 2123 and n.

2121 yellow]
Webb (1991, p. 154): Derisively, of the face, comparing Err. 4.4.61 (1345)—saffron face—and here. More specifically, used of withered old age as in 2H4 1.2.181 (440–1) where one of the characters of age is a yellow cheek (OED, a. 1b). And in Chaucer, Romaunt of the Rose, 308, 310: Sorowe, thought, and gret distresse,…Made hir [Sorowe] ful yelow.

2121 cowslippe]
OED (3. attrib.), quotes this instance alone. See 385.

2122 make mone]
OED (Moan, n. 1a): Complaint, lamentation.…Chiefly in phr. to make (one’s) moan.

2123 His…leekes]
Furness (ed. 1895): Quoting from a private letter about a woman in Wales who heard an old woman praising the beautiful blue eyes of a child in these words, mae nhw’n lâs fel y cenin, i.e. they are as green as leeks, green and blue having the same word (glas, from the same root as our glaucous) in Welsh. So Thisbe must have borrowed her phrase from Welsh.

2123 eyes…greene]
Rann (ed. 1787) compares Rom. 3.5.220 (2269): so green, so quick, so fair an eye. Spalding (1833, p. 50 n.) quotes Don Quixote, Pt. 2 ch. 11: Los ojos de Dulcinea…verdes esmeraldas [The eyes of Dulcinea…emerald green]. Dyer (1883; 1966, p. 493) Green eyes have been praised by poets of nearly every land, and according to Armado, in LLL 1.2.86 (391): Green indeed is the colour of lovers. Verity (ed. 1893) quotes TNK 5.1.144: thy rare green eye, and adds Skeats’s note on Dante’s use of smeraldi (emeralds) to describe Beatrice’s eyes (Purgatorio, 31.116). Kittredge (ed. 1939), alone, alleges that green eyes are bright hazel eyes, but OED (Hazel1 3a): The reddish brown colour of a ripe hazel-nut adding used esp. of eyes.

2123 greene…leekes]
Proverbial; Tilley L176. Cf. Chaucer’s Romaunt of the Rose 211–12: Ful fade and caytif was she eek, | And also grene as ony leek.

2123 leekes]
OED (Leek 1): A culinary herb…allied to the onion, with broad leaves. Ellacombe (1884, pp. 138–9) discusses the very high value placed on the leek by the ancient Egyptians, Israelites, and Greeks. It was so important in Anglo-Saxon times that its name commonly designated kitchen garden, and gardener. Its popularity still continues among the Welsh,…but it does not seem to have been much valued in Shakespeare’s time, for Gerard [Herball, 1597] has but little to say of its virtues, but much of its hurts.

2124 sisters three]
OED (Sister, n. 4b): The Fates or Parcae. Proverbial (Tilley S490, quoting this instance). Malone (ed. 1790, 10:580) compares Richard Edwards’s Damon and Pythias (1571, D1v); cf. n. 2083–4. Fripp (1930, p. 125) quotes Ovid, Met. 8.452 and 15.781. Bradbrook (1951, p. 256) compares Thomas Preston’s Cambyses (1570, A2r). Brooks (ed. 1979): Cf. Launcelot Gobbo’s bantering reference and Pistol’s bombastic one; [MV 2.2.63 (625), 2H4 2.4.199 (1220)].

2125 as…milke]
See n. 544 (milke white).

2126–7 Lay…silke]
Capell (1783, 2.3:117) finds the weaving terms here singularly proper applied to Bottom. Cf. 2084 and n.

2126 Lay]
OED (v. 8a): Place. On Theobald’s (ed. 1733) emendation Steevens (ed. 1773) remarks that he has no note to justify or account for the alteration. See textual n.

2126 gore]
OED (n.1 2): In poetical language often: Blood shed in carnage.

2126 shore]
Cut. Johnson (1755), quoting Oth. 5.2.205–6 (3494–5): The preterit of shear (OED, v. 1a). Wright (ed. 1877): For shorn. Foakes (ed. 1984): Providing a comic rhyming effect.

2127 sheeres]
OED (Shear, n.1 1f), quoting this instance first: [Scissors] fig. esp. as attributed to the Fates.

2127 threede]
See n. 2084.

2128 sword]
