Halliwell-Phillipps (1879 [1855], p. 35) first called attention to the reference in Edward Sharpham’s The Fleire: Like Thisbe in the play, ’a has almost kil’d himselfe with the scabberd (1607, E1v); he infers that in the original performances of MND, the interlude was conducted in the extreme of burlesque, and the actor who represented Thisbe, when he pretends to kill himself, falling upon the scabbard instead of upon the sword.

2129 imbrew]
OED (Imbrue, v. 2): To stain, dye (one’s hand, sword, etc.) in or with (blood, slaughter, etc.); or (3): In pregnant sense (with blood understood): a. said of a person; also absol.; b. of a weapon piercing a part;…Obs. Schmidt (1874, 2 tr.): To shed the blood of. Wright (ed. 1877): Make bloody, stain with blood. The word is evidently used for purposes of alliteration. Watt (ed. 1906): It was evidently a highflown tragic word. Thirlby (MS 1747–53) compares 2H4 2.4.196 (1217–18). Cf. Golding, Met. 12.118 where Achilles boasts that he did Thebe with her proper blood embrew. Hibbard (1981, p. 145) quotes Appius and Virginia (c. 1564; ed. Farmer, Five Anonymous Plays, 1908, pp. 36, 39) for two splendid uses of imbrue. In A gorgious Gallery (1578, O4v) the lion’s muzzle is all embrude with blood. Cf. also Sylvester’s translation of DuBartas, (1605) Weekes 1.6.76: [Factious French-men] In their own brests their furious blades t’imbrew.

2130 farewell friends]
Arnold (1911, p. 106): There are surprisingly few direct appeals to the audience in Shakespeare.…Thisbe’s farewell to her friends…may be interpreted as referring to the onlookers, but, if so, it is excusable as a burlesque of such absurdities as the exhortation of the audience by the dying Cambises (1570, F2r-v).

2130 thus]
For the stage action accompanying this word see n. 2096. Cf. A gorgious Gallery (1578, P2v).

2132–3 Moone-shine…Lyon.…Wall]
Wilson (ed. 1924) has these three characters re-enter at this point (see textual n.) because they are needed…(i) to bury the dead by drawing the traverse in front of Ninny’s tomb (the inner-stage), so as to allow Pyramus and Thisby to make their exit unseen; and (ii) to offer the Epilogue and dance the Bergomask. Hasler (1974, pp. 94–5) posits that Moonshine and Lion may remain on stage, though he admits that 886–7 and 2021 indicate that they do not.

2132 left]
Kittredge (ed. 1939): Left alive; survive the tragic catastrophe. Brooks (ed. 1979) adds Not (pace [Wilson (ed. 1924)]), as present on the stage.

2133 I]
=Aye, ay (yes). See n. 617.

2133 to]
=Too (also). See n. 313.

2134 Lyon]
See textual n. Warner (in Steevens, ed. 1773): Q1 gives this speech to Lion, but see an epilogue and hear a dance is so much in Bottom’s character, that the modern editors are justified in restoring it to him. Collier (ed. 1858) suggests the change in Q2 was to excite laughter on the resuscitation of Bottom. Barber (1959, p. 154 n.): Perhaps when Bottom starts up,…his comic resurrection owes something, directly or via the jig, to the folk play. When the St George, or Fool, or whoever, starts up, alive again, after the miraculous cure, the reversal must have been played as a moment of comical triumph, an upset, more or less grotesque or absurd, no doubt, but still exhilarating—to come back alive is the ultimate turning of the tables on whatever is an enemy of life. Brooks (ed. 1979): Q1’s Lion was evidently induced by the mention in [2132]. The speech can never have been meant for Snug. Foakes (ed. 1984): Lion (Snug) vanished offstage at [2072]. Wells & Taylor (1987, p. 286): [F1]’s reading could not have been supplied without reference to a manuscript, for there is nothing self-evidently wrong with Q[1].

2134–5 the…fathers]
Deighton (ed. 1891): i.e. and therefore cannot assist in burying the dead. However, Stewart (1908, p. 115) believes this to be a half symbolic statement, reminding us of the last scene in [Rom.]. Schanzer (Midsummer, 1955, p. 13): [This sentence] surely refers to a metaphorical wall, to the reconciliation of the parents, who were formerly at enmity, through the tragic death of their children. Walter (ed. 1964): I.e. their fathers are reconciled. Mr B. M. Forrest drew my attention to a similar breaking down of a wall as a symbol of reconciliation in Terence’s Adelphi, ll. 908–9. See Ephesians, ii.14. van Emden (1975, p. 203): This remark, all the more significant for being entirely gratuitous in the context of the Pyramus play, shows that the fathers were conceived as quarrelling, and so contributing to the tragedy of their children; this conception…is present only in [A Gorgeous Gallery] among Shakespeare’s sources; both he and Riess & Williams (1992, p. 215) note the similarity with Rom. (see ).

2135–6 to see…daunce]
Wells (ed. 1967): A last touch of Bottom’s characteristic verbal confusion. cf. n. 1995–6.

2135 Epilogue]
OED (n. 3), quoting the instance at 2138 first: A speech or short poem addressed to the spectators by one of the actors after the conclusion of the play. Delius (ed. 1859): It is the office of the Epilogue to ask the public for indulgence with the enacted drama and to excuse its shortcomings (Ger.). Baker (ed. 1896): Such speeches were usually apologetic in tone. This fact explains the words of Theseus in [2138–9]. Wright (ed. 1877) compares the epilogues ending Tmp., AYL, AWW, 2H4, H5, H8.

2136 Bergomaske]
Hanmer (ed. 1744, 6:Glossary): A Dance after the manner of the Peasants of Bergomasco a Country in Italy belonging to the Venetians. All the buffoons in Italy affect to imitate the ridiculous jargon of that People, and from thence it became a Custom to mimick also their manner of dancing. Heron (1785, p. 112) recommends Riccoboni’s Histoire du theatre Italien (1730, pp. 51–2) for details of the dance, adding Harlequin, I believe, was a native of the whimsical country above Bergamo. Wilson (ed. 1924): The Bergomask is the antimasque, introductory to the dance and song of the fairies with which the play closes. Long (1955, p. 96) suggests the dance, probably a jig, is…what Bottom proclaims it to be—a part of the entertainment planned by the rustics—an alternative afterpiece to their play. Sorell (1957, p. 383): The Bergomask dance was well known in England as an Italian country dance.…It was a round dance of couples in which the dancers execute little entrechats after every sixth or ninth step. Since William Kempe created the role of Bottom, and was an excellent dancer…he probably put in the Bergomask acrobatic and clownish features. Phillips (ed. 1969): The Bergomask, because it is wordless foolery, lowers the comic tone and prepares the audience for the quiet withdrawal of the Duke’s party and the hushed final masque of the fairies. Brissenden (1972, p. 91) suggests the dance may well have been heard, for it could have been done with stamping of feet, perhaps accompanied by the tongs and the bones [1540].…Their rustic dance…is the extreme contrast in the play between the mortal and the fairy worlds. Sabol (1978, pp. 15, 594 #217) compares the morris dance which celebrates a wedding in the last act of Munday’s John a Kent, noting that the music for both rustic dances is technically similar. Franke (1979, p. 293): The dance is just as lascivious as it is burlesque. Cf. Williams (Moonlight, 1997, p. 26). McRae (1988, p. 183): The dance is a vital contribution to the dramatic unity of the play, a careful complement rather than a contrast to the courtly dances before and after. Howard (1993, pp. 336–7): The bergomask…is much more than humor at the expense of the lower classes or simple opposition of the grotesque and the graceful [quoting Brooks, ed. 1979, p. cxx].…[It] offers an alternative model of gender relations, one patently in opposition to Oberon’s dance of amity [1605]; for where the courtly couple is isolated, enclosed, regulated, and heterosexual, the popular couple is gregarious, intimate, improvisational, and often ungendered. See also .

2136–7 cōpany]
See n. 269.

2139 no excuse]
No apology (OED, n. 1a). See n. 2135.

2139–40 Neuer…deade]
Halliwell (ed. 1856): A satirical allusion, perhaps, to the ending of some of the dramas of the time, such as Locrine; he lists the ten dead from that play.

2140 there…blamed]
Deighton (ed. 1891): None can come in for blame.

2140 Mary]
See n. 279.

2140–2 if…Tragedy]
Granville-Barker (ed. 1924, p. xli): Can we detect, too, Shakespeare’s own mock apology for the imperfections of his work, put into the mouth of Theseus? Did Shakespeare, by any remote chance, play the part himself? It was in his line, according to tradition, and, if so, the joke would have been a good one indeed.

2141 writ]
Wright (ed. 1877): The common form of the preterite in Shakespeare, who seldom uses wrote (OED, Write, v. A. 2. pa. tense a., γ). See Blake 8.3.8. See n. 121 (spoke).

2141–2 hangd…garter]
Proverbial; see Tilley (G42): He may go hang himself in his own Garters. For other instances of this proverbial turn of phrase see Tilley (1927, p. 307).

2143 notably]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Finely. Gill (ed. 1981): Commendably. Boas (1896, p. 184): A courteous double-entendre.

2143 discharg’d]
See n. 354.

2144 Burgomaske]
See n. 2136.

2144 alone]
Although Qq and Ff have no SD here, Theseus calls for the dance, and as early as 1661 Cox included it. See textual n. Wilson (ed. 1924): The pause for the dance is marked by the change from prose to verse in Theseus’ speech [2145]. Wells & Taylor (1987, p. 286): Most editors since Rowe [or Cox] have all the clowns dance, but Bottom specifies two [2136], and only two are on the stage—himself and Flute. Tucker & Holden (ed. 1990) suggest, questionably, that Theseus rather than suffer the dance,…hears the clock strike and immediately launches into a poetic speech to avoid the dance, exiting before it can take place.
Neil (ed. 1878) compares Jonson’s Love’s Welcome at Bolsover and the Dance of Mechanickes (7:809, 36–7). Vitruvius, the supervisor of the crew, uses some of the same phrases as Quince does when he addresses his mechanicals—every Mothers Sonne at 39 (7:809) and Pumpes and Ribbands at 64–5 (7:810) as Quince at 340 and 884, and 1781 (new ribands to your pumpes).

2145 tongue]
OED (Tongue 14d, quoting this instance): The clapper of a bell.

2145 tolde]
Theobald (MS 1723–33): toll’d. Deighton (ed. 1891): Counted, numbered. Page (ed. 1897) adds proclaimed.…There may also be a reference to tolled. Wilson (ed. 1924): The quibble on tolled is obvious.

2146 Louers…time]
Walter (ed. 1964): Earlier [at 1795] Theseus rejected fairy toys. Perhaps this is said jestingly to tease the lovers. Leggatt (1974, p. 101): At the end of the scene, the rational sceptic makes an interesting slip: [quotes 2146]. It may be just a casual turn of phrase; or it may be an involuntary acknowledgement of a truth that in his more guarded moments Theseus would deny (like Chesterton’s My God, said the atheist). Donaldson (1985, p. 33): It is a delightful irony…that the skeptical Theseus begins his last speech in the play by mentioning the fairies in whom he disbelieves—[quotes 2145–6] and upon ending it abandons the stage to the whole troop of presumably nonexistent fairies. Chaucer’s Theseus argues for the existence of an unseen benevolent power; Shakespeare’s Theseus argues against the existence of a power we can all see. See KnT 1987 ff.; . Horn (1986, p. 68): Shakespeare intends Theseus to speak his last four words in a mildly derisive tone of voice—he is mocking the lovers for their belief in their own story and for their talk of possible fairy intervention—and he also wants us to perceive the following: that Theseus is utterly in the wrong on both counts and that Hippolyta’s reply to his speech on the imagination has had no beneficial effect on him.

2146 Fairy time]
Irving & Marshall (ed. 1888): Midnight. Deighton (ed. 1891): Time for the fairies to be at their sports. Littledale (1916, 1:536): Begins after midnight, and closes at the rising of the morning star, but may extend later, referring to 1429–34.

2147–8 we…ouerwatcht]
We shall sleep as late this morning as we have stayed up late tonight.

2147 outsleepe]
Johnson (1755), quoting this instance: To sleep beyond (OED, v. 1, quoting this instance first).

2148 ouerwatcht]
OED (v. 2), quoting this instance first: To watch all through (a night). More accurately, Brooks (ed. 1979): Stayed awake after normal hours.

2149 palpable grosse]
Baker (ed. 1896): Evidently dull and rough. Page (ed. 1897): So gross as to be evident as it were to the touch, comparing 1H4 2.4.225–6 (1183–4). Holzknecht & McClure (ed. 1937): Obviously stupid. See Abbott §§1, 2.

2149 grosse]
The Clarkes (ed. 1864): Here used in the sense of clumsy, lumbering; which accords well with the heavy gait of night,—Shakespeare sometimes using the word heavy to express slow-paced, tardy.

2149–50 beguil’d…night]
Baker (ed. 1896): Which has made the hours of the night, that otherwise would have moved so slowly for us, pass so pleasantly.

2149 beguil’d]
See n. 1837.

2150 heauie]
OED (a.1 19b), quoting this instance first: Slow, sluggish. See also nn. 1107, 2156.

2150 gate]
Gait, pace. See n. 506. Halliwell (ed. 1856) compares H5 4.Chor.20 (1809), cripple-tardy-gated Night. See also 2200.

2151–2 A…iollity]
Ness (1941, p. 129): [This] is actually a scene-end couplet, for the remainder of the play is concerned with the masque of the fairies. Gentleman (ed. 1774): The Play in reality ends here, but loth to forget his Fairies, Shakespeare has brought them on by way of Epilogue; and what he gives them to say or sing is very poetical. He is most inimitably happy in painting these children of romantic fancy.

2151 solemnitie]
See n. 14.

2152 Reuels]
See n. 23 (reuelling).

2152 Exeunt]
Many editors mark a new scene after this exit (see textual n.). Andrews (ed. 1993) defends this: The stage is cleared with the Exeunt at [2152] and a vacated stage [is] the standard criterion for a scene change, so there should be a new scene designation. Walter (ed. 1964) agrees: A fully ceremonial departure emptying the stage. But Holland (ed. 1994): The action is self-evidently continuous and any hint of a break would ruin the effect of Robin’s entrance.

2153 Pucke]
See textual n. Collier (1853, p. 110) explains his old corrector’s addition to the SD as embodying the very way in which [Puck] is represented on the title-page of the old tract of Robin Goodfellow, his Mad Pranks [1628]; he refers to 2172–3. Brooks (ed. 1979, at 2172) points to Jonson’s masque Love Restored, where there are two references to Robin’s broom, at 57–8 (7:378) and 138 (7:381); he adds: this was an attribute…, like those which identify saints, e.g. Catherine’s wheel.

2154–73 Now…dore]
Duff (1770, pp. 162–3): The…passage presents us with a strangely fanciful assemblage of ideas, exhibited in solemn, striking, and vivid description.…This description is wild and irregular, but the objects represented in it are perfectly congenial to the occupations, time, and manner of appearance of the Fairy species with which they are connected. Coleridge’s (1960 [1811], 1:92) praise of these lines is often quoted: Anacreon in the perfectness of the lines—proportion, grace, spontaneity! So far it is Greek. But then add to it, O what wealth, what wild luxuriance, and yet what compression and condensation of English fancy. There is nothing in Anacreon more perfect…, or half so rich and imaginative; a diamond speckless! Chambers (ed. 1897): The exquisite poetry of this passage comes in striking contrast to the rude mirth of the burlesque that has preceded it. Bradby (1926, p. 70): This is not the attitude towards night which we should expect from one of the fairies, who are always fleeing from the presence of the sun and chasing [Following] darkness like a dream [2168–9], it is rather a summary of the night-fears of childhood. Quiller-Couch (in Wilson, ed. 1924, pp. x–xi) quotes Spenser’s Epithalamion 338–52, claiming that Sh. intended Puck’s speech as a merry κάθαρσις, a pretty purgation, of those same goblin terrors which Spenser would exorcise from the bridal chamber. Merchant (1961, p. 165): The permanent possibility that the forces of evil irrationality will burst upon the seemly dance of matrimony is sustained to the end of the play. At the faintly ominous afterthought of Theseus, [quotes 2146], Puck catalogues the terrors of the night. See also Traversi (1968, 1:158–9); Belsey (1993, p. 190). Krieger (1979, p. 68): Puck’s speech evokes not only the real dangers of nature—real lions and wolves—but also the real hardships of physical labour. This emphasis on physical danger and hardship suggests a world outside of or beyond the palace threshold, a world of nature that the aristocratic imagination cannot thoroughly subordinate. See also Montrose (1996, p. 145). Rudd (1979, p. 193): Noting the references to lion, moon, night, graves (implying death), fairies, Hecate (or Titania), darkness, and dream, it is hard not to think that Puck is in some way glancing back over the play. If there is anything in this notion, it is clear that the story of Pyramus and Thisbe is included in his summary. Leggatt (1999, p. 64), also linking the lion to Pyramus and Thisbe: An invasion by one kind of shadows, the actors, leads to an invasion by another kind, the fairies. Ibid (2004, p. 197): In an unlocalized setting,…[w]here the fairies are, there the wood is also. And so at the end when the fairies enter, the palace dissolves into the wood, bedchambers and all; it has been transported, or (like Bottom) translated. The wall has not just been breached; like its equivalent in Pyramus and Thisbe it has disappeared. (But see Smith, n. 2171). Cf. nn. 226 (gates), 2134–5.

2154 hungry Lyons]
All subsequent editors follow without comment Rowe’s reduction to a single lion; no editor is moved to follow Grey (1754, 1:78) in substituting for the beast’s appetite his nationality: Hungarian.

2154–5 Lyons…wolfe]
Malone (ed. 1790, 10:580–1) attributes to an anonymous writer the contention that Sh. would never have thought of intermixing the exotick idea of the hungry lion roaring with more familiar night-time sights and sounds, if he had not read Ps. 104:20–1. Steevens (ed. 1793) suggests Sh. might have found the midnight roar of the Lion associated with the howl of the Wolf, in Phaer’s translation of Vergil, Aen. 7:15–18, but Vergil’s Latin (sub nocte rudentum and ululare) is closer to Sh. than Phaer’s translation, where the lions grone and the wolves wawle (1558, S2v). Steevens adds: Puck, who could encircle the earth in forty minutes [552–3], like his fairy mistress, might have snuffed the spiced Indian air [500]; and consequently an image, foreign to Europeans, might have been obvious to him. Pye (1807, p. 59): Sh. neither knew or cared whether there were any lions in Attica, for there, and not England,…is the scene laid. Most pertinently Fairchild (1937, p. 36) refers to the 1592 diary of the Duke of Wirtemberg who visited the Tower of London, where are kept six lions and lionesses.…Not far from these is also a lean, ugly wolf, which is the only one in England (William Brenchley Rye, England as Seen by Foreigners, 1865, pp. 19–20); Fairchild adds: That Shakespeare had seen [the wolf] and heard its howl is suggested in [Ven. 459 and Mac. 2.1.53–4 (633–4)]; and that he sometimes thought of the lions and the wolf together, in Puck’s [2154–5 and TN 3.1.129 (1343)].

2155 beholds]
See textual n. Most editors accept the emendation of Warburton (in Theobald, ed. 1733): As ’tis the Design of these Lines to characterize the Animals, as they present themselves at the Hour of Midnight; and as the Wolf is not justly characteriz’d by saying he beholds the Moon; which all other Beasts of Prey, then awake, do: and as the Sounds these Animals make at that Season, seem also intended to be represented; I make no Question but the Poet wrote; And the Wolfe behowls the Moon. For so the Wolf is exactly characteriz’d, it being his peculiar Property to howl at the Moon. (Behowl, as bemoan, beseem, betrim, and an hundred others. Malone (in Steevens, ed. 1778): The word behold was in the time of Shakespeare frequently written behould (as I suppose it was then pronounced)—which probably occasioned the mistake. Idem (in Boswell, ed. 1821) mentions the spelling could for cold in Luc. 177. Walker (1860, 2:66) explains the putative error as the confusion of final d and e. Furness (ed. 1895): If it be assumed that the compositors set up at dictation, the mishearing of beholds for behowls is not difficult of comprehension. Cercignani (1981, pp. 15, 225–6) apparently accepts the linguistic possibility of the emendation. Brooks (ed. 1979): Although the word does not occur elsewhere in Shakespeare, he has at least thirty be- compounds: cf. be-rhyme, Rom. [2.4.41 (1145)]. Many parallels are adduced in support of the emendation; most appositely Theobald (ed. 1733) cites AYL 5.2.109–10 (2516–17). Steevens (ed. 1778, 1793) accumulates parallels from Lyly, Marston, Massinger, Lodge, Spenser, and others. See also Dodd (1752, 1:89). Brooks (ed. 1979) gives several more parallels from Lyly. See also Tilley SD449.
A few support Q1, if tentatively. Johnson in Steevens (ed. 1773): The alteration is better than the original reading; but perhaps the author meant only to say, that the wolf gazes at the moon. Caldecott (MS 1813–33) restores beholds, glossing gazes, steadily & earnestly looks, upon. Herbert (1977, pp. 61–2): [Puck] recapitulates…the proper mood of the mechanicals’ dreadfully constructed Babylonian world. [Quotes 2154–5]. The image recalls Hippolyta’s first speech, promising that the moon will beholde the night / Of our solemnities.…Robin’s picture of the silent, static predatory wolf looking up at the dead bright satellite in a moment before he makes his anguished noise brings to Athens an instant of Babylonian desolation more chilling than Warburton’s clever emendation behowls the moon. Andrews (ed. 1989): Most editors emend to behowls; but since this idea is implicit in the manner of beholding associated with wolves, no alteration in the text is necessary. On the use of the prefix be see Abbott §438.

2156 heauie]
OED (a.1 28): Weighed down by sleep, weariness. Gill (ed. 1981): Exhausted. Mowat & Werstine (ed. 1993): Sleepy. Cf. 1107.

2157 foredoone]
Johnson (1755, Foredo v. a. 2), quoting this instance: To overdo; to weary (OED, Fordo, v. 7; Abbott §441, citing this instance). Steevens (ed. 1773): Overcome. Andrews (ed. 1989): Completely done. The Quarto spelling…shows that this word is meant to rime with Moon. See Cercignani (1981, p. 133).

2158 wasted]
OED (ppl. a. 2a), quoting this instance: Diminished or reduced in substance;…worn.Deighton (ed. 1891): Partly burnt out.

2158 brands]
Rann (ed. 1787): Embers OED (2): A piece of wood…burning on the hearth.

2159 scriech-owle]
Johnson (1755, Screechowl): An owl that hoots in the night, and whose voice is supposed to betoken danger, misery, or death (OED, Screech-owl 1a). Harting (1871; 1965, pp. 83–6): The owl has usually been regarded as a bird of ill omen, and superstitiously considered a messenger of woe; he quotes Ovid, Horace, and owls of omen from Sh.’s plays. Fripp (1930, p. 110) quotes from Ovid’s Metamorphoses as a source for the ominous bird in Sh’s plays. See e.g. Golding’s Ovid for the story of Ascalaphus (5.669–82) and the ill omens at the wedding of Tereus and Procne (6.553–4); see , . Brooks (ed. 1979) compares Scot (1584, 7:15; see ), shreeke owles. Garrison (1994, p. 147): While the owl haunts Thisbe in Ovide moralisé on her trip to Ninus’s Tomb, in Shakespeare the owl is only heard in the dark of night when the lovers are all safe.…Instead of evil to come, the owl represents evil from which the characters in the play have been protected. Cf. 2H6 3.2.327 (2042); Spenser, Epithalamion 345–7; Dent R33.

2159–61 scrieching…shrowde]
Delius (ed. 1859): The ill wretch, wakeful on his painful bed, holds the screeching of the night owl for an omen of his approaching death (Ger.).

2163 That]
See n. 1657.

2164 spright]
See n. 403 (sprite).

2165 Churchway]
OED (1. attrib.), quoting this instance: The public way leading to a church.

2166 wee]
Wells & Taylor (1987, p. 286): Modern wee [little] might be intended; but OED’s only Shakespearian example [Wiv. 1.4.22 (419)] is dubious, and the metrical pattern discourages any special emphasis on the adjective here. [Q1] uses this spelling for the pronoun 26 times elsewhere. Only Q1 has this spelling.

2167 triple Hecates]
The Clarkes (ed. 1864): So-called, because she bore three names: Luna, in heaven; Diana, on earth; and Hecate, in the infernal regions. Hense (1851, p. 113, n. 2): The Hecate named in this verse is the nocturnal goddess, whom Vergil describes in Aen. 4.609 as nocturnis Hecate triviis ululata per urbes (compare Theocrit. 2, 15). Shakespeare makes Hecate the leader of the witches. Compare [Mac. 3.5.1–36 (1429–69); 1H6 3.2.64 (1500); Lr. 1.1.110 (117)]; Shakespeare likes to refer to Hecate in order to connote the ghostly and horrible aspects of the night that instill fear. Compare [Mac. 2.1.52 (632) and 3.2.41 (1199)] (Ger.). Hunter (ed. 1870) adds the names Cynthia in heaven and Proserpine in hell. Wright (ed. 1877) cites Golding’s use of triple Hecate (7.136; see ). Root (1903, p. 54) shows from Ovid (Met. 14.403–5) that sometimes Hecate is thought of merely as representative of darkness or night, as here. Cf. n. 1420.

2167 Hecates]
OED (Hecate), quoting this instance: Always disyllabic…in Shakes., except in 1H6 3.2.64. Identified b. with the moon;…c. regarded as presiding over witchcraft and magical rites. Douce (1807, 1:385) observes that she was called the queen of fairies.

2167 teame]
Douce (1807, 1:197): The chariot of the moon, said to be drawn by two horses, the one black, the other white; he thinks Sh. may have consulted Boccaccio’s Genealogy of the Gods. More probably the team consists of two dragons (cf. 1420), as Root (1903, p. 56) suggests: In Met. 7.219 (as in Seneca, Med. 1023; cf. Euripides, Med. 1321)…Medea’s prayer to Hecate is answered by the descent of a dragon-drawn car in which Medea is carried aloft (Golding 7.287–92; see ).

2169 Following…dreame,]
Following Hecate, here and elsewhere in Sh. identified with Nox, or Night, thus darkness, as Root (1903, pp. 54–5) has shown. See also 1614 and n. Rann (ed. 1787), presumably referring to dreams: As constant concomitants. Similarly, Deighton (ed. 1891): As the events of the day follow a man in sleep.

2170 frollick]
OED (Frolic, a. 1): In early use: Joyous, merry, mirthful. In later use with sense derived from the vb.: Frolicsome, sportive, full of merry pranks. Rolfe (ed. 1877): Used by S.…nowhere else except in [Shr. 4.3.182 (2165)].

2170–1 not…house]
Proverbial; Dent (M1236.1). Page (ed. 1897) compares Ham. 1.1.10 (15): Not a mouse stirring.

2170–1 mouse]
Morris (1985, pp. 253–4) calls attention to Panofsky’s (1936, p. 233) remark that the mouse is a time-honoured and very well-known symbol for all-devouring time.

2171 hallowed]
Moore (2001, p. 117): The ominous tone of the walking-ghost imagery [is] superceded…[when] Puck calls the house hallowed not haunted.

2171 house]
Smith (1975, p. 77) comments on the relatively few lines in Sh. (fifty-eight compared to the 256 connoting exteriors) that establish the setting as an interior.…Very rare indeed are the four successive identifications of the interior of a dwelling,…where Puck and Oberon between them use the word house thrice and palace once. Possibly the dramatist was attempting to dissociate the entering fairies from the woodland setting in which they had invariably appeared up to this point in the action.

2172 I…broome]
See n. 2153.

2173 sweep…dore]
Johnson (ed. 1765): Cleanliness was always necessary to invite the residence and favour of Fairies, quoting Drayton’s Nimphidia, ll. 65–8. Douce (1807, 1:198): In confirmation of Dr. Johnson’s remark quotes two poems printed in Percy’s Ancient ballads, the Fairy Queen and the Fairies Farewell, (1775, 3:207, 210). Malone (in Boswell, ed. 1821) refers to Wiv.; see esp. 5.5.44–6 (2526–8). Halliwell (ed. 1856) quotes several more works to demonstrate the cleanliness of fairies, including Scot (1584), where Robin goodfellow is described as sweeping the house at midnight (see ). These parallels all support understanding Robin’s action as sweeping dust from behind the door, as Pye (1807, p. 60) first clarifies. Kott (1981, p. 142) comments: Sweeping away recurs in all carnival and spring rituals in England, France, Italy, Germany, and Poland. The symbolism of sweeping is rich and complex. A broom is a polysemic sign. But invariably sweeping away is a symbol of the end [and] of the beginning of a new cycle. One sweeps rooms away after a death and before a wedding. Cf. Garber (1981, p. 9) and Calderwood (1992, p. 93).
However, Farmer (in Steevens, ed. 1773, 10:2P1r): To sweep the dust behind the door is a common expression, and a common practice in large, old houses; where the doors of halls and galleries are thrown backward, and seldom or never shut. Adopting this view, Halio (1976, p. 35) enlarges on its implications for the play as a whole (see ).

2173 dust]
Holland (ed. 1994): There is a superstitious association of dirt with wealth that may be echoed here; Holinshed records a story of Alice Kettle sweeping the dirt towards her son’s house with the words To the house of William my Sonne, Hie all the wealth of Kilkennie towne (quoted by Iona Opie and Moira Tatem, eds., A Dictionary of Superstitions (Oxford, 1989), s.v. Dirt). But Robin may just be helpfully cleaning up.

2174 traine]
Foakes (ed. 1984): The whole train of fairies would include at least Puck, the four named fairies, and the unnamed Fairy who speaks in 2.1 [376–431], a total of six in all. Stage tradition has often swollen the number who enter here into a large troop, played sometimes by women, and sometimes by children.

2175–2206 Through…day]
Most commentary is concerned with what is sung, what is spoken, and by whom. Much turns upon F1’s printing 2185–206 centered and italicized, inserting a heading The Song, and omitting the SP Ob. (see textual nn. 2184, 2185). Johnson (ed. 1765), believing all the text spoken, restores Q’s arrangement, explaining: After the speech of Puck, Oberon enters, and calls his Fairies to a song, which song is apparently wanting in all the copies. Next Titania leads another song which is indeed lost like the former, though the Editors have endeavoured to find it. Then Oberon dismisses his Fairies to the despatch of the ceremonies. The songs, I suppose, were lost, because they were not inserted in the players parts, from which the drama was printed. Capell (1783, 2.3:117–18) similarly believes that from the measure, the matter, and very air of every part of it 2185–206 cannot be a song; he rejects the notion that the exchange 2175–83 can be song or even recitative, but since Now at 2185 seems to argue a song preceding, he claims that Sh. originally wrote a song which, because it dissatisfied him, he deleted, the F arrangement signifying an injudicious attempt of the players to supply one. Wilson (1873, pp. 257–8) differs from most 19th-c. commentators in accepting 2185–206 as a song, but believes it should be divided among Oberon, Titania, and the fairies. Similarly Noble (1923, pp. 56–7) argues that Oberon leads the song solo, the fairies join in for 2187–98, and Oberon concludes with his final instructions; he also believes (p. 51) that 2175–83 are sung or intoned. Granville-Barker (ed. 1924, pp. xxiii–v) demurs, with arguments similar to Capell’s on meter and the force of Now; he suggests that Hymen’s song (TNK 1.1.1–24 ) would be suitable for MND. Long (1955, p. 99), assigning 2175–9 to Oberon, 2180–6 to Titania, 2187–98 to the Fairies, 2199–206 to Oberon: The entire song will consist of five six-line stanzas and a brisk closing couplet. These stanzas perform two functions; the first two contain directions given by Oberon and Titania to their attendants, that is, they explain what is to be done.…In the third and fourth stanzas, the fairies describe the nature of their blessing. In the fifth stanza, Oberon repeats his commands and concludes the song; he entertains the possibility of some of Oberon and Titania’s lines being intoned rather than sung. Brooks (ed. 1979, pp. cxxiii–iv) vehemently disagrees with Noble and Long, endorsing rather Granville-Barker’s views. For musical settings, see .

2175–9 Through…trippingly]
Confusion over whether the light is emitted by the fairies or by the fire has arisen partly because Capell, followed by many others, replaced QqFf comma after fier with a colon (see textual n.) The heavier stop encouraged commentators to interpret By as By means of (see Hudson, ed. 1880 and Furness, ed. 1895). Puzzling over how the dead fire could give even a glimmering light, or be transported by the fairies, elicited many emendations and conjectures (see textual n. and ). QqFf’s light punctuation makes the parallel structure of Oberon’s directives clearer: The elves and fairies are to give light through the house past the fire, to hop lightly, and to sing and dance (OED, By, prep.: Into the vicinity of and on beyond, past.). But Kittredge (ed. 1939), alone: Give is not addressed to the Fairies, but (as in a charm) to whatever powers govern such things.
Porter & Clarke (ed. 1903): It is obvious enough that Shakespeare’s stage-fairies would here enter carrying tiny lights. Wilson (ed. 1924) cites Wiv. 4.4.51 (2172–3) where, for the trick on Falstaff, Mistress Page plans to dress some children Like Vrchins, Ouphes, and Fairies…With rounds of waxen Tapers on their heads; he notes that the lights are not carried in the hand, as is clear from the reference to dancing hand in hand [2182]. Partly because of 2182, Wiles’s (1993, pp. 115–16) argument that the fairies are to be seen as hymeneal torchbearers for an epithalamium is unconvincing.

2175 glimmering]
Gill (ed. 1981): Flickering. See n. 452. Holland (ed. 1994): Glimmering has appeared twice before in [MND] (at [452] and [1084]); Shakespeare uses the word nowhere else. The glimmering night which has accompanied Theseus’s previous desertions has now become the glimmering light that blesses his marriage bed; he credits Adrian Poole for the observation.

2177–83 Euery…place]
Barber (1959, p. 138) conceives two dances: The first a processional dance led by the king and the second a round led by the queen.…This combination of processional and round dances is the obvious one for the occasion: to get the fairies in and give them something to do.…[T]hese two forms of dance are associated in origin with just the sort of festival use of them which Shakespeare is making. Wiles (1993, p. 117) also envisages two dances, but believes Oberon cues a lively dance…that might be a galliard or corranto.…Titania then seems to introduce not the same but a different dance…[quoting 2180–3]. The song is slower (warbling) and formal (by rote), and fulfils Oberon’s earlier promise that the fairies would at midnight solemnly dance in Theseus’ house [1606–7]. Brissenden (1972, p. 92; see also 1981, p. 45): The words of Oberon and Titania again draw attention to the lightness of the fairies. [The dance] Hand in hand…is a return to one of the oldest forms of dance, the carole, a linked dance, for which the music is sung, usually by the dancers themselves [referring to M. Wood, Some Historical Dances, London, 1952, pp. 11 ff.]; he, like Brooks (ed. 1979, p. cxxiv) sees the dance as a symbol of harmony and concord. Leimberg (1987, p. 126) finds an unmistakable allusion to the dance of the graces. Howard (1993, pp. 327–8, 341) objects to what she sees as a bland celebration of harmony…[which] effaces the difference between courtly and popular cultures, claiming It is the linked dance of the fairies and not a cosmic dance at all that brings the play full circle; her later assertion that Oberon’s words 2187–8 and 2201–4 restore masculine surveillance and control and the hierarchy and authority of the cosmic dance seems contradictory, but she claims that Puck’s epilogue, by defining as illusory the play’s conflicts between cultures and genders, restores balance. See also nn. 516, 1603–10.

2177 Elfe]
See n. 387.

2177 spright]
See n. 375 (spirit).

2178 Hop]
OED (v.1 1b), quoting this instance: To move by leaps with both or all the feet at once…: said esp. of small birds. See nn. 983, 2095.

2178 as…brier]
Proverbial. See Tilley B359 and The Oxford Dictionary of English Proverbs (1970, p. 60).

2178 brier]
See n. 376.

2179 daunce it]
Delius (ed. 1859): To dance gaily, instancing Tmp. 1.2.379 (524), Foote it. For this use of it as an indefinite object see OED (pron. 9). See also Abbott §226; Franz §295.

2179 trippingly]
Moving lightly and nimbly on the feet; skipping, dancing (OED, adv., quoting this instance first). Knight (ed. 1839): The trip was the fairy pace, quoting Tmp. 4.1.46–7 (1701–2), Ven. 146, and Wiv. 5.5.91–2 (2575–6). See n. 1614 (Trippe). Cf. Sh.’s only other use of the adverb in Ham. 3.2.1–2 (1849–50) Speak the speech…trippingly on the tongue.

2180 rehearse…rote]
Recite from memory (OED, Rehearse, v. 1a, quoting this instance, and Rote, n.2 2). Understanding rehearse as practise (OED 5a; cf. 363), Thiselton (1903, p. 78) claims: Titania bespeaks a rehearsal of [the song] with the professed object that the Fairies may be word-perfect and also know what they have to do. Holland (ed. 1994): John Caird’s production (RSC, Stratford, 1989) made a great deal of comic business out of taking rehearse in [this] sense with the fairies holding a rehearsal that was all too necessary.

2180 your]
Phillips (ed. 1969): Presumably addressed to Oberon, as the others are to sing after him. Q2’s this (see textual n.) possibly indicates some early shift in the disposition of songs and dances?

2181 warbling]
Johnson (1755, To warble 2): To be uttered melodiously (OED, ppl. a.1). See n. 1233.

2182–3 Hand…place]
The harmony between Oberon and Titania that had been disturbed (434–521) so that the Fairies cannot daunce (461) is restored when Oberon says to his Queen take hands with me (1603) and he promises they will to morrow midnight…Daunce, in Duke Theseus house…And blesse it (1606–8). See also nn. 1603–10, 2177–83.

2186 each…stray]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Let each fairy stray.

2187–8 To…be]
Steevens (ed. 1793): We learn from Articles ordained by K.Henry VII for the Regulation of his Household, that this ceremony was observed at the Marriage of a Princess. —All men at her comming in to bee voided, except woemen, till shee bee brought to her bedd;…Then the Bishoppe, with the Chaplaines, to come in, and blesse the bedd:… p. 129 (see A Collection of Ordinances and Regulations for the Government of the Royal Household, 1790). Idem (MS 1793–1800) quotes Chaucer, MerT 1819 (see ). Douce (1807, 1:199–200) quotes the Latin from the Manual for the Use of Salisbury [or Sarum] to show that the bed blessing was observed at all marriages. See also Crehan (1964, pp. 47–50). See n. 2199–201.

2187 best…bed]
Wiles (1993, pp. 118–19) argues unconvincingly that this refers to the bed of a real bridal pair; he believes 2191–2 indicates that the three couples of the play are differentiated from the occupants of the best bride-bed.

2187 will wee]
For ellipsis of go see n. 522 (Thou…groue).

2187 wee]
Deighton (ed. 1891): I.e. Titania and himself, as king and queen of the fairies, will make our way to the chief bed of the house occupied by Hippolyta and Theseus. Howard (1993, p. 341) apparently reads Oberon’s usage as the royal we, excluding Titania. There seems little justification for her insistence that Oberon will go himself to the best bride-bed, thus asserting masculine dominance.

2189 the issue]
Verity (ed. 1893): The only issue of Theseus and Hippolyta known to mythology was Hippolytus, who was far from fortunate. Pearson (1974, pp. 296–7) also finds this ironic, and argues that Sh. chose to call Theseus’s Amazon bride Hippolyta rather than Antiopa in order to reinforce the connection with Hippolytus.

2189 create]
OED (ppl. a. †1. as pa. pple. Obs., quoting this instance last): Created. For the omission of d in the participle see n. 1235.

2193–8 And…be]
Wright (ed. 1877): These blemishes were attributed to malignant fairies. Verity (ed. 1893): The notion of fairies presenting a child at nativity (=birth) with good or evil gifts and personal qualities, e.g. beauty or ugliness and the like, occurs in many fairy-tales. Thus in the romance Huon of Bordeaux [see ], which introduced Oberon into English fairy-lore, Oberon himself is represented as having received at his birth all sorts of good qualities and possessions from the fairies; but there was a single malicious fairy present, who said that he should never be more than three feet in height, and, according to the romance, he never was. Cressy (2000, p. 30): Alongside popular broadsheets announcing monstrous births, medical texts displayed a gallery of horrors that compounded the normal terrors of childbirth. To ward off these horrors churchmen offered prayers that may have been common on expectant parents’ lips.…Daniel Featley…prayed, that the notes of the parents’ sin be not seen in the marks, maims, and defects of the child. Oberon’s consecration of the lovers…invoked this tradition.…The counterpoint to this was the womb-invading curse, mobilized by Lear against Goneril (Lr. 1.4.281–3 [795–7]).

2193–6 blots…prodigious]
Wright (ed. 1877) compares Jn. 3.1.44–7 (965–8).

2193 blots…hand]
Root (1903, p. 89): Shakespeare’s Nature is merely a personification of a philosophical abstraction, properly not mythological at all.…It is she who frames the human body, determining its physical appearance. Bush (1956, p. 26): If the Goddess Nature can blot her writing, what is it that jiggles her arm? Montrose (1986, pp. 86–7): Oberon…metaphorizes the engendering of their offspring as an act of writing. Lecercle (1989, p. 153): Oberon’s last act is scriptuary. In this pagan version of the Immaculate Conception, which annuls the blighted natural progeny of the opening scenes of the play, Oberon guides the hand of Nature if he is not its scribe.

2194 stand]
OED (v. 72h): To reside or inhere in; to be an attribute of. Deighton (ed. 1891): Be found. Bertram, Roberts & Atwan (ed. 1997): Continue (i.e. be inherited).

2195 mole]
Dyer (1883; 1966, pp. 463–4): These have from time immemorial been regarded as ominous, quoting this use, and referring to Brand, Observations on the Popular Antiquities, (1848–9 [1970], 3:252–5). Cf. Ham. 1.4.24 (Q2 Dr), vicious mole of nature.

2195 hare-lippe]
Steevens (ed. 1793): This defect in children seems to have been so much dreaded, that numerous were the charms applied for its prevention. The following might be as efficacious as any of the rest. If a woman with chylde have her smocke slyt at the neather ende or skyrt thereof, &c. the same chylde that she then goeth withall, shall be safe from having a cloven or hare lippe. Thomas Lupton’s Fourth book of Notable Thinges (1579, Mr). Rolfe (ed. 1877) compares Sh.’s only other use in Lr. 3.4.118 (1897) where the foul fiend gives the defect.

2196 marke prodigious]
Doran (ed. 1959): Ominous birthmark.

2196 prodigious]
Johnson (1755): Such as may seem a prodigy; portentous; monstrous (OED, a. 1 Obs., quoting this instance). Evans (ed. 1974): Abnormal (OED, a. 2).

2197 Despised…natiuitie]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Looked upon with horror, regarded as hateful, in a new-born child.

2199–201 With…blesse]
Thirlby (MS 1725–33; 1747–53): Holy water; adds Fairy. Steevens (ed. 1793): The same superstitious kind of benediction occurs in Chaucer’s Miller’s Tale [3479–85]. Douce (1807, 1:204–5): There seems to be a covert satire against holy water. Whilst the popular confidence in the power of fairies existed, they had obtained the credit of occasionally performing much good service to mankind; and the great influence which they possessed gave so much offence to the holy monks and friars, that they determined to exert all their power to expel the above imaginary beings from the minds of the people, by taking the office of the fairies’ benedictions entirely into their own hands. Of this we have a curious proof in [Chaucer, WBT 863–74; see ]. The other quotation from Chaucer, which Mr. Steevens has given, is not to the present purpose. The fairies’ blessing was to bring peace upon the house of Theseus; the night-spell in the Miller’s tale, is pronounced against the influence of elves, and those demons, or evil spirits, that were supposed to occasion the night-mare, and other nocturnal illusions. Dobbs ([1948], pp. 159–60): MND is, so to speak, dripping with dew, taking this word to mean inspiration from the Greek.…Dew would therefore appear to refer particularly to the classical idea of the supernatural, which is so intimately woven into the fantasy of the Athens wood. Barber (1959, p. 139): The fairies enact a lustration. Fertilizing and beneficent virtue are in festival custom persistently attributed to dew gathered on May mornings; referring to clerical blessing of bridal beds (cf. n. 2187–8), he suggests that this may itself be an ecclesiastical adaptation of a more primitive bridal lustration, a water charm of which dew-gathering on May Day is one variant. Vlasopolos (1978, p. 24), connecting summer solstice saturnalia practices with curative waters associated with St. John’s Day: The pervading moonlight and its associations with dew in [MND] become relevant in terms of the magical properties of water at Midsummer. The moon, as the celestial body governing not only the tides, but all other waters, and believed to be instrumental in the formation of dew,…must be present in order to affect Midsummer waters and bring forth the dew. The field dew consecrate…used by the fairies to bless the wedded couples and their issue, though technically applied after Saint John’s Day (because applied after midnight), belongs to these magical waters by virtue of its property to ward off evils and bodily imperfection. Foakes (ed. 1984): For the association of dew with benediction, compare Cym. [5.5.350–1 (3662–3)]. Wiles (1993, p. 119) compares the similar association in H8 4.2.133 (2724).

2199 consecrate]
Johnson (1755): Consecrated; sacred (OED, ppl. a. 1b). For the omission of d in the participle see n. 1235.

2200 take his gate]
Steevens (ed. 1778): Take his way, or direct his steps (OED, Gate, n.2 2).

2201 seuerall]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Separate; individual (OED, a. 3a).

2203–4 Euer…blest]
The transposition of these lines, first adopted by Staunton (ed. 1857) but first conjectured by Thirlby (MS 1747–53), is now generally accepted. Many earlier conjectures and emendations attempted to make sense of the untransposed text. See textual n. and .

2205 Trippe away]
See n. 1614 (Trippe).

2205 make no stay]
Baker (ed. 1896): Delay not (OED, Stay, n.3 3, quoting this instance). Cf. 1110.

2206 Exeunt]
Thiselton (1903, p. 79) believes the Q SD incorrect, as Robin Goodfellow, at least, remains on the Stage. Why should not also the other Fairies so remain with Robin as spokesman (cf. we in [2207, 2114, 2116, 2118], which perhaps suggests their continued presence)? See textual n.

2207–22 If…amends]
Gentleman (ed. 1774, p. 202 ): Puck’s address to the Audience is a politic and plausible bait for applause; it contains too a modest sensible apology for the visionary circumstances which are frequently met with in this Dramatic Dream. Hunter (1845, 1:298): This simple epilogue forms a graceful close to this beautiful drama;….What the poet had put into the mouth of one of the characters in respect of the poor attempts of the Athenian clowns [2015–16], he now by the repetition of the word shadows, in effect says for himself and his companions. Fender (1968, p. 58): But Puck’s use of shadows [is] an ironic reflection of Theseus’s.…Our attention is drawn not to the similarity, but to a potential difference, between our response and Theseus’s. Cf. Holland (ed. 1994). Cecil (1957, p. 41): Puck remains to utter a final apostrophe to the audience on behalf of the play of which he, not rational Theseus, is the significant and presiding genius. Coghill (1964, p. 59): Puck…tells the audience to think the whole dream was theirs: this is not stage-craft but audience-craft. Nuttall (1967, p. 148): Puck’s speech is ingenious, delightful and undisturbing. If anything, it is reassuring. Common sense is not unseated by this play with reality and unreality, for the simple reason that the normal scope of the terms has suffered no metaphysical revision. It is the players who are shadows, the play which is a dream. The audience is allowed to be utterly real. Cf. Martz (1971, p. 64). Fawcett (1973, p. 27): Puck’s words are an explicit inclusion of the spectators into the dreamlike nature of the play, and therefore a direct challenge to the kind of seeing the audience might think it has been doing. Brunel (ed. 1974, p. 107): Puck’s final speech draws the spectators into the concluding propitiatory ceremony which has consisted of exorcism, perlustration, and catharsis (Fr.). Leggatt (1974, p. 115): Just as Bottom is the only mortal to speak to the fairies, Puck is the only character to speak to the audience; and this gives him, like Bottom, some identification with the dramatist. Graham (1987, p. 49): Puck’s epilogue is not a throwaway confession of absolute fictionality, or a wry post-modernist manifesto about the self-consuming unreality of words, things, and perceptions. The ironic invitation Puck offers is that we should make ourselves resemble the very Theseus whose inadequacies we have just registered. We are invited to dismiss as shadows what we have seen to be perfectly real in terms of the play, and like the four lovers at the end to scorn as mere delusion a performance which has been no less than a stylized rendering of how we live. For the interplay of dream and shadow, cf. Ham. 2.2.254–64 (1300–10).
For comment on whether this speech was composed for a particular performance, see esp. Fleay (1886, pp. 182–3), and Wilson (ed. 1924, pp. 87–8).

2207 shadowes]
Kittredge (ed. 1939): This refers not only to the nature of the fairies (cf. [1388]), but also to the idea that the characters in any drama are mere shadows of reality. See n. 2015.

2208–12 Thinke…dreame]
Deighton (ed. 1891): All you have to do is to imagine that you were asleep when these visions appeared to you, and then everything will be well. Delius (ed. 1859) sees these lines relating to and explaining the play’s title (Ger.). The Clarkes (ed. 1864): Even to the epilogue does Shakespeare maintain the dream-like character of this enchanting play, which he has so well entitled. Casey (1994, p. 33): We are left with the assertion that theater is by its very nature elusive and illusory, music-like in its temporality. Moffatt (2004, p. 186): Puck…alludes to the idea of dream as truth when he equates dream with vision. Thirlby (MS 1725–33) compares 1411–12. Cf. also 1583–4. Furness (ed. 1895) compares Lyly’s Prologue to The Woman in the Moon (3:241). Rickert (1923, p. 147) also compares the Prologue to the Court of Lyly’s Sapho and Phao (2:371). See also Brown (1972, pp. 10–11).

2208 mended]
Cox (1991, p. 149): Chaucer’s use of amend in WBT 1097–9 seems to have captured Shakespeare’s imagination: Puck uses various forms of the word four times (all but one of those times in a rhyme position) in [2208–22],…echoing Theseus’s advice to Hippolyta (and, by implication, to the audience)…[2016] and Oberon’s to Titania…[493].

2210 visions]
Andrews (ed. 1989): Puck means (a) dreams, (b) special insights or revelations, and (c) the entire play.

2211 idle]
OED (a. 2a): Worthless, trifling. Doran (ed. 1959): Foolish (OED, a. 2b). Mowat & Werstine (ed. 1993): Trivial. Cf. nn. 1193, 1692.

2211 theame]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Subject (OED 1a). Possibly also OED (4. Mus.): A simple tune on which variations are constructed; see Casey at n. 2208–12.

2212 No…but]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Nothing more than. Johnson (ed. 1894): Meaning no more. Watt (ed. 1906): Producing nothing. For but = than see OED, conj. 5. See also n. 343.

2213 reprehend]
Wright (ed. 1877): Censure, blame (OED, v. 1c, quoting this instance first).

2214 mend]
Deighton (ed. 1891): Improve in our behaviour (OED, v. 2b). Kittredge (ed. 1939): Do better next time. A regular apology and promise, conventional in epilogues. Brooks (ed. 1979), questionably: The promise could only be addressed to the company’s regular audience in the public theatre, for whom it is plain the epilogue was written.

2215 I am]
Wilson (ed. 1924): The contracted form I’m is not found in Shakespeare: it does not follow that he did not wish it to be so pronounced. See textual n.

2215 honest]
Collier (ed. 1842): Sh.’s fairy messenger [needs] to assert his honesty, and to clear himself from any association with the helle Pouke. Furness (ed. 1895): Honest here refers merely to his veracity, as is shown by [2219]. See also n. 410.

2216 If…luck]
Steevens (ed. 1773): If we have better fortune than we have deserved.

2216 vnearned]
OED (ppl. a. 1), quoting this instance: Not earned by merit or desert.

2217 Now…tongue]
Johnson (ed. 1765): If we be dismiss’d without hisses. OED, Serpent, n. 1d, quotes the Serpents tongue from here as a nonce-use for hissing. Schmidt (1875, Serpent) interprets the line as not to be hissed, and in (1874, Hiss) cites LLL 5.1.137–9 (1869–71). See also JC 1.2.259–61 (362–5), WT 1.2.187–9 (269–71). Holland (ed. 1994): See also the serpent of Hermia’s dream [800–5] and her description of Demetrius [1095–6].

2217 scape]
Escape: see n. 1767.

2218 amends]
Johnson (1755): Recompense; compensation; attonement (OED 2a). Verity (ed. 1893), citing the Epilogue to 2H4, thinks Sh. perhaps had another play ready. Black (1965, p. 61) cites the dedication to Ven.

2219 Else]
Deighton (ed. 1891): If I do not keep my promise. Baker (ed. 1896): If this be not true.

2221 Giue…hands]
Johnson (ed. 1765): Clap your hands. Give us your applause (OED, Hand, n. 15, quoting this instance first). Verplanck (ed. 1845): Playfully intended to convey two analogous senses—the giving and joining hands of friends, and the clapping hands of theatrical applause. Verity (ed. 1893): The Latin comedies of Plautus and Terence end with the appeal to the audience Plaudite or an equivalent. Cf. AYL (ed. Knowles, 1977), Epil. (5.4.22–3 [2796]), and n. Howard (1993, p. 342) suggests that at first Puck seems to be proposing that the spectators join hands in the terpsichorean fellowship of the fairies’ round, but actually he is asking for applause. Holland (ed. 1994): In Peter Brook’s production the cast left the stage to shake hands with the audience.

2222 restore amends]
Rolfe (ed. 1877): A phrase not elsewhere used by S., his ordinary expression being make amends. We have restore=make amends for, in [Son. 30]. Deighton (ed. 1891): In return show you marks of our friendship. Kittredge (ed. 1939): Provide for such improvement in the future as shall make up for all the defects in this play. See n. 2218.
